lines.text: Quis homo te exuperauit usquam gentium inpudentia?
Quis autem malitia te?
Epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: Watch out, if you wish your wrath to compare with the wrath of Liber.
doc.differences:
TrRF: 33 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 17/24)

Warmington: 48 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 17/25)

Marmorale: p.195 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 13/24)

Klotz: 38 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 13/24)

Ribbeck2: 39 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 13/24)

Ribbeck3: 36 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 13/24)

All editors place this fragment towards the end of the play, as it requires some sort of action to have taken place. We can safely suppose that already Lycurgus has opposed to the bacchic rites in the previous acts, resulting in the uttering of this phrase, either by Liber himself or by others towards the king. This is of course before the final punishment by the god.
doc.apparatus:
contendas iram] contendas iram L3 : contenda syram α L2

Possible confusion amongst the copyists with the word Syra, that is found in the second previous example by Nonius in this lemma.
doc.commentary:
contendere significat conparare] This explanation by Nonius points to the verb comparare, composed from par (and not parare), that means ‘oppose to’, ‘put in rivalry’, cf Accius 644 Wa., also quoted by Nonius (Spaltenstein 1364).

sis: si vis] The word can be associated with comic and everyday speech, often following cave. cf Terence, Eunuch, 798 and Plautus, Amphitryo, 845. (Lattanzi 1994)

iram] the emendation is correct, as syram seems obscure.

cave...contendas…contra cum] alliteration of c, as four out of nine words of the fragment begin with this letter. Contendas and contra also share the first four letters.
doc.reconstruction:
It is safe to assume two parties in this fragment: the speaker (or speakers) and the addressee. The latter is beyond doubt Lycurgus, who with his furious actions (tuam iram) wishes to confront with the wrath of Liber. Warmington (1936, p. 133), Marmorale (1950, p. 195) and Traglia (1986, p. 203) suggest that in this scene of dispute it is Liber who speaks to Lycurgus. Klussmann (1843, p. 115) is not sure about the speaker but points out to similar reasoning from Teiresias in Euripides’ Bacchae (325). Marmorale also considers whether the king is being advised by one of his satellites or even from the Bacchae. Spaltenstein (§1361) tends to agree, as known dialogues between chorus and characters in choral meters.

In general Liber doesn’t reveal his identity straight away (cf. fr. 37), so we can’t be sure how the speaker assumes ira Liberi. In Euripides the identity and the name of the god were known to Pentheus and his comrades, but they didn’t identify him as the captive. This could also be the case in Naevius so whoever speaks knows about Liber but ignores that the god is present in the uttering of those words (Spaltenstein §1362).

Duentzer (1837, p. 439) places this fragment exactly before the fr. 26, which holds as a natural continuation of the dispute here. Fragment 21 is thought to be the response of Lycurgus to this exhortation as the theme of soul’s fierceness is repeated. In that case, ille there would most definitely mean Liber himself and the abovementioned suggestions by Marmorale would seem more plausible. Spaltenstein (§1359) states that this dispute leads to the victory of the god in fr. 24.

The motif of angrily (θυμούμενος) going against Bacchus can be found also in Euripides’ Bacchae (794), where Dionysus, with the form of the introducer of the worship, states that he would gladly sacrifice than oppose to him. In the same play (505), Pentheus expresses his opinion that he is above the disguised Dionysus (κυριώτερος σέθεν).

Bibliography:

Duentzer, H. (1837) ‘De Naevii Lycurgo et Ennii Iphigenia’, RhM 5, 433–446.

Euripides. Bacchae. Iphigenia at Aulis. Rhesus. Edited and translated by David Kovacs. Loeb Classical Library 495. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 2003.

Klotz, A. (1953) Scaenicorum Romanorum fragmenta: Volumen primus: Tragicorum fragmenta. München.

Klussmann, E. C. Chr. (ed.). (1843) Naevii Vita, carminum reliquiae, poesis ratio. Jena.

Lattanzi, L. (1994) ‘Il Lucurgus di Nevio’, Aevum 7, 191–265.

Livius Andronicus, Naevius, Pacuvius, Accius. Remains of Old Latin, Volume II: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Pacuvius. Accius. Translated by E. H. Warmington. Loeb Classical Library 314. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 1936.

Marmorale, E. V. (1950) Naevius Poeta: Introduzione biobibliografica, testo dei frammenti e comment. Florence.

Plautus. Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives. Edited and translated by Wolfgang de Melo. Loeb Classical Library 60. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press, 2011.

Ribbeck2, O. (1871) Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Ribbeck3, O. (1897) Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Spaltenstein, S. (1939) Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Naevius. Brussel.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
This line is quoted by Nonius Marcellus in his dictionary De compediosa doctrina to demonstrate that contendere can take the meaning conparare.

contendere significat conparare...Naevius in Lycurgo:

cave sis tuam contendas iram contra cum ira Liberi

(Non. pp. 258,38-259,6 Merc.=395 Li.)

lines.text: cave sis tuam contendas iram contra cum ira Liberi
Iove propagatus est ut perhibent Tantalus,
Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus
Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: With cloaks, with gold borders, with supple saffron-coloured dresses, with grave (clothes)
doc.differences:
TrRF: 40 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 24/24

Ribbeck2: 46 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 18/24)

Ribbeck3: 43 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 18/24)

Warmington: 39 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 10/25)

Marmorale: p. 197 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 18/24)

Klotz: 43 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 18/24)

Ribbeck3 places this fragment (p.14, 43 Ri.3) in the middle of the play, as part of the description of the capture of the Bacchants. Marmorale (p. 197 Marm.) and Klotz (frg. 43 Kl.) follow Ribbeck in his ordering of the fragments, whereas Warmington, on the other hand, places this fragment in the same context of the description of the Bacchants’ capture, but orders the other fragments of the play in a different way.
doc.apparatus:
patagiis] patagis codd. (Non.)

malacis] malacis α γ L (Non. p. 540,3-7) : malicis β G H2 (Non. p. 540,3-7) : malaciis Bamb. γ (Non. p. 548,24-32) : malatiis β G H L (Non. p. 548,24-32) : malitiis α (Non. p. 548,24-32) : manicis coni. Bothe (1837)

mortualibus] mortalibus codd. (Non.)

In general, there are few textual problems for this fragment. The only word which could pose a problem is malacis. This reading malacis is found in manuscripts α, γ, and L, but other manuscripts provide variations that are difficult to make sense of within the context of the line, such as malitiis ‘with malice’ in α, or that could be scribal errors, such as malicis in β G H2. Bothe has suggested the conjecture manicis, which would be a form of maniacus ‘maniacal’, according to the DMLBS. This would fit a context of Bacchants nicely. However, this word maniacus is only found in mediaeval and later Latin texts, which would make it difficult to accept as a reading in a text of Naevius. As such, malacis is the most readily accepted reading.
doc.commentary:
pallis] A palla was a long, flowing garment, typically worn by women, as in Plautus’ Asinaria 939, and in his Menaechmi 420 and 540.

patagiis] A patagium was a gold edging or border, typically attached to women’s clothing. Cf. Festus p. 221 (edition by Müller).

crocotis malacis] A crocota, sc. vestis (from the Greek ὁ κροκωτός, sc. χιτών) was a saffron-coloured garment for special occasions. Marmorale asserts that this garment was worn by both women and effeminate men, which is alluded to in Cicero’s De haruspicum responsis 21, 44: P. Clodius a crocota, a mitra, a muliebribus soleis […]. The word malacus is also a Greek loanword, from μαλακός, meaning ‘soft’.

mortualibus] It is probable that vestibus should be supplied. Warmington suggests that these clothes need not be funerary clothes, as that would be an unexpected ending to the list of clothing, but could also be grey or dark garments. Marmorale translates mortualibus as nere ‘black’, but adds the note that these still are funerary clothes. In my opinion, the contrast between saffron-coloured garments and dark clothes is quite stark and would not be necessary if we do interpret mortualibus as having to do with death. This is not unthinkable when dealing with Bacchants but would not necessarily imply a dark colour. For more on this, see the fragment reconstruction below.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck (1875) 59, Warmington and Marmorale all suggest this fragment is part of a story by a satelles, who recounts how he and the other satellites had captured the Bacchants and consequently describes their attire. This seems like very sensible interpretation.

If, as suggested above, mortualibus would be interpreted as ‘funerary, grave, having to do with death’, we could read here a reference to the eschatological connotations of the Bacchic cult; cf. Graf (2018). Bothe (1837) 271 suggests that here the image of a field covered with the abandoned clothes of the fleeing Bacchants is evoked and that the clothes being mortualibus signals the impending death of Lycurgus.

Following Scaliger (1565), Klussmann (1843) 116 and Duentzer (1837) 435 both propose that this fragment should be placed directly after TrRF 43, in which someone’s clothes are described, possibly Bacchus’, wearing diabathra on the feet and an epicrocum, a fine, transparent garment typically worn by women. If this is the case, TrRF 40 could apply to Bacchus, which is somewhat difficult due to the plurality of clothes in this fragment. The interpretation in which TrRF 40 refers to the Bacchants seems more plausible.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted two times by Nonius in De compendiosa doctrina:

The first time Nonius quotes this line is in the context of an explanation of the word patagium:

patagium, aureus clavus, qui pretiosis vestibus inmitti solet. Plautus Epidico (231): indusiatam, patagiatam[=Lindsay 231]. Naevius Lycurgo (43): pallis, patagiis, crocotis, malacis mortualibus. (Non. p. 540,3-7)

The second time Nonius quotes the Naevian line he is illustrating the words caltulam and crocotulam, which he determines to have been derived from flower names, calta and croco:

caltulam et crocotulam. utrumque a generibus florum translatum, a calta et a croco. Vergilius in Bucolicis: pinguia luteola pingit vaccinia calta[=Verg. Ecl. 2. 50]. Plautus in Epidico (231): intusiatam, patagiatam, caltulam aut crocotulam[=Lindsay 231]. Novius Paedio: molucinam, crocotam, cheridotam, ricam, ricinum[=Nov.Paedius 71]. Naevius Lycurgo (43): pallis, patagiis, crocotis, malacis mortualibus. (Non. p. 548,24-32)

lines.text: pallis patagiis crocotis malacis mortualibus

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: proinde ita parent se in vita, ut vinci nesciant.

doc.translation: So may they prepare themselves in life so as never to know defeat.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. LX, v. 112:

[ATREUS]:

112: Proinde ita parent se in uita, ut uinci nesciant.

Warmington:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. 72:

72: Proinde ita parent se in vita ut vinci nesciant.

Then let them so in life prepare themselves

That they know not defeat.

Franchella:

Fragment 7 (7/22), 363:

363: proinde ita parent se in uita, ut uinci nesciant.

Dunque si preparino cosí da ignorare, finché vivono, che cosa sia la sconfitta.

Not in D’Antò.

Not in Dangel.
doc.apparatus: No textual issues to report.
doc.commentary: proinde ita: Proinde (or proin) is attested from Naevius onwards (Lycurgus F 23 TRF, proinde huc Dryante regem prognatum patre, | Lycurgum cette, ‘then bring here the king, born of his father Dryas, Lycurgus’, transl. Maltby–Slater, FRL VI.282). The adverb proinde (‘according to the manner or degree (in which)’; ‘in the same way or degree (as)’, OLD s.v.) can either be used on its own or in correlation with ut. Here it seems to be used as an adverb on its own: the verb in the jussive subjunctive (parent) is governed only by ita, which itself is correlated with a consecutive clause introduced by ut (OLD s.v. ita 18).  So far as I can see, proinde ita occurs elsewhere only once, at Cic. Att. 2.14.1 (proinde ita fac uenias <ut> ad sitientis auris ‘so come now expecting greedy ears’, transl. Shackleton Bailey 1965-1970, I.233, where ut is introduced by conjecture), ita being introduced by conjecture at Plaut. Capt. 794 [ERGASILUS, parasite]: proinde ita [ut codd.] omnes itinera insistant sua, ‘so all should stick to their own paths’, transl. De Melo 2011, I.587).
doc.reconstruction:
Bothe (I.184) includes this fragment as F 3 (3/19) of Accius’ Atreus; cf. Welcker 1839-1841, I.363, printing: proin ita parent se in vita, ut vinci nesciant. Ribbeck prints the fragment as an adespoton (inc. inc. fab. LX, v. 112 Ribbeck3), but, in his second and third editions, reports Welcker’s attribution of it to Accius’ Atreus. Franchella (227), like Bothe, prints it as an actual fragment (certum) of Accius’ Atreus (F 7 (7/22), apparently no line number attributed). This adespoton is presented by Cicero as ‘the precept of Atreus’ (praeceptum illud Atrei, Cic. Tusc. 5.52), but it is uncertain whether Cicero is talking about the character of Atreus as depicted in the Atreus of Accius, or about another play (Livius Andronicus? Ennius?).

﻿ Lana (1959, 302) argues that this fragment is closely connected with Atreus’ argument, expressed in F 104.8-10, that, for a man with supreme power, the adultery of a spouse constitutes the most terrible danger: (quod re in summa summum esse arbitror | periclum matres conquinari regias, | contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus). Indeed, if his wife, the queen, gives birth to illegitimate children, this would put the proper royal line of succession at risk by giving rise to potential confusion between legitimate heirs and illegitimate heirs. Lana (p. 302) argues that F Adesp. 65 (proinde ita parent se in vita, ut vinci nesciant) constitutes Atreus’ solution to the potential problem of succession that might arise from his wife’s infidelity with Thyestes: he must raise Agamemnon and Menelaus in such a way as to instill in them the mindset that they cannot be beaten. He argues that the fact that this fragment is phrased in the third person indicates that this educational precept (cf. Cic. Tusc. 5.52 praeceptum illud Atrei) was formulated not to the children themselves, but to another character such as the attendant.
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero, Tusculan Disputations 5.52: nam in quem cadit aegritudo, in eundem metum cadere necesse est (est enim metus futurae aegritudinis sollicita expectatio); in quem autem metus, in eundem formido timiditas pavor ignavia; ergo, ut idem vincatur interdum nec putet ad se praeceptum illud Atrei pertinere: ‘Proinde ita parent se parent in uita, ut uinci nesciant’ (= Adesp. F 65 TRF); hic autem uincetur, ut dixi, nec modo uincetur, sed etiam seruiat. At nos †autem uirtutem semper liberam uolumus, semper inuictam; quae nisi sunt, sublata uirtus est.

The man who can be affected by distress must also be affected by fear, for fear is the anxious expectation of future distress. The man affected by fear must also be affected by dread, nervousness, panic and cowardice, so that in consequence he is sometimes conquered, and does not think Atreus’ advice applies to him: ‘Let them then so prepare themselves in life that they know not how to be conquered’ (= Adesp. F 65 TRF). But he will be conquered, as I said, and not only conquered but even enslaved. But we claim that Virtue is ever free, ever invincible. Unless this is so, Virtue is destroyed.

(Text: Giusta; transl. Douglas 1985-1990, II.107-109).

The fragment is preserved in a philosophical treatise in five books by Cicero entitled Tusculan Disputations, which was probably written during the summer of 45 BCE (on the dating, see Marinone–Malaspina 20042, 215). In this treatise, which is addressed to Brutus the assassin of Caesar (RE ‘Iunius 53’), Cicero aims to present to a Roman audience Greek philosophy of the Hellenistic period on a range of topics. This fragment is quoted in a section of Book 5 of the treatise, which deals with the question of whether virtue is sufficient to ensure a happy life. In this section, ‘Cicero’ discusses the position of Xenocrates (Davies 1709, 324 suggests that the Cicero’s passage possibly draws on Xenocrates’ περὶ ἀρετῆς in two books, cf. Diog. Laert. 4.12). Atreus’ words are presented as an example of the wrong sort of thing to say to oneself when one is experiencing fear.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: scibam hanc mihi supremam lucem et serviti[is] finem dari

doc.translation: I knew that this day would be given as my last and as the end of my slavery.
doc.differences:
TRF: 142 (Accius' Clytemnestra 5/10)

Ribbeck: 37 (Accius' Clytemnestra 7/10)

Warmington: 243 (Accius' Clytemnestra 7/11)

Franchella: 396 (Accius' Clytemnestra 7/10)

D'Antò: 37 (Accius' Clytemnestra 7/10)

Dangel: 296 (Accius' Clytemnestra 6/10)


Warmington:

I always knew this was the day ordained | To be my last, the end of slavery.

Franchella:

scibam hanc mihi supremam lucem et seruiti finem dari.

Sapevo che questo giorno mi era stato dato come l’ultimo ⟨della mia vita⟩ e come fine della mia schiavitú.

D'Antò:

scibam hanc mihi supremam lucem et seruiti finem dari.

Sapevo che questo giorno mi era concesso come ultimo e come fine della schiavitú.

Dangel: Je savais que m’étaient donnés ce jour suprême et la fin de ma servitude.
doc.apparatus:
scibam] scibam LGH2: scibam H1EF

seruiti] seruiti Bothe: seruitas L1: seruitutis L2HEGF, ‘fort. melius’ GMS: seruitii Vossius: seruitei Müller 1888, 340

Del Rio’s (Delrius') edition (Syntagma I.134) reads fidem instead of finem. Perhaps a misprint?

The lemma under which this fragment of Accius is quoted has been lost (see Citation context). The lemma preceding the one that concerns us is suasiones (226.8 M = 335.9 L), after which editors posit a lacuna. The text then resumes: ‘generis feminini. Neutri. Accius Clytaemestra: scibam…’. Lindsay (1901, 97) explains that the lemma for suasiones has been lost owing to homoeoteleuton: the words generis feminini probably appeared both in the suasiones lemma and in the lemma that followed it, that is, the lemma that concerns us. This repetition caused a copyist inadvertently to omit the contents of the suasiones lemma, of which, Lindsay suspects, the illustrative quotation was likely another Accian fragment, and to jump straight to the following one. Based on the contents of our Accian fragment, Lindsay suggests that it was originally quoted within the lemma seruitus, a feminine noun (seruitus, -tutis, f.). But the reason why Nonius quotes our fragment is because it apparently contains not the feminine noun seruitus, but the neuter noun seruitium. We would therefore expect to see the neuter noun seruitium in our fragment.

The original reading (L1) of the L manuscript of Nonius (= Leiden Universteitsbibliotheek, VLF 73, f. 114v) seems to be either seruitiis, the dative or ablative form of the neuter noun seruitium, or the meaningless seruitas, as reported by L. Müller (1888, 340): having inspected the manuscript myself, seruitas does indeed seem to be what is written. But another family of the manuscript tradition, the ‘second family’ (Lindsay’s BA) containing (to use L.D. Reynolds’ terminology) the ‘doctored’ text of Nonius (19862, 249), has the reading seruitutis, the genitive form of the feminine noun seruitus. The second, correcting hand of the L manuscript (L2), which is correcting L manuscript with readings drawn from the ‘second family’ of the tradition, inserts the reading seruitutis. Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 146) conjectured seruitii, genitive of seruitium, and, following this line of thought, Bothe (I.195) later conjectured the contracted form of this word, seruiti, which is accepted by virtually all scholars. Bothe’s conjecture is plausible, as the contracted form -ī of the genitive in *-iī is widely attested in early Republican verse: in fact, the genitive in -iī is not attested securely before Lucretius (e.g. 1.832 patriī): see Weiss 20202, 240. It is worth noting that the most recent editor of this passage of Nonius, Mazzacane (in GMS I.395), writes in her critical apparatus that seruitutis is ‘perhaps better’ (‘fort. melius’) than the reading she prints in her text, seruitii. But if Accius wrote seruitutis, then we would have to posit that a corruption occurred before Nonius, because the text to which Nonius had access contained a neuter noun.
doc.commentary:
scibam: the normal form, cf. Enn. Telamo F 118 TRF = v. 272 Jocelyn, scibas natum ingenuum Aiacam cui tu obsidionem paras, ‘you knew that Aiax was born in wedlock, to whom you present a blockade [i.e., trouble]’, tr. Goldberg–Manuwald.

...hanc mihi supremam lucem et seruiti finem dari: dare OLD s.v. 13b ‘to appoint (a day or time)’. Supremam is used predicatively (i.e. ‘...that this day was given to me as my last...’, cf. Franchella 245: ‘sapevo che questo giorno mi era stato dato come l’ultimo e come fine della mia schiavità’). For a similar clause, with dari in same sedes, see Ter. Phorm. 560 sed opus est mihi Phormionem ad hanc rem adiutorem dari ‘but I need to find Phormio to help me in this scheme’ (tr. Maltby). Cf. also Pacuvius Dulorestes F 101 TRF = 89 Schierl gnatam despondit, nuptiis hanc dat diem (‘he/she has promised his/her daughter in marriage, he has set a day for the wedding’). Dare finem is found at Sen. Suas. 4.3, where it seems to refer to astrologers assigning to some people an imminent death (aliis dedere finem propinquum).

seruiti: the idea that death is to be preferred to slavery, which is presented as typically Roman, is often found in Cicero’s Philippics (2.113, 3.29, 11.24, 12.2; cf. Fam. 10.27.2; and Quint. Curt. 8.7.14 …aut, quod tristius morte est, in seruitute uiuendum, ‘...or, what is more wretched than death, to live in slavery’). Death and slavery are occasionally compared in legal contexts: Iustin. Dig. 24.1.32 (quamuis morti seruitus comparetur ‘although slavery is comparable to death’), 35.1.59 (quia seruitus morti adsimulatur ‘for slavery is equated with death’). The neuter seruitium seems to have a certain archaic flavour, as it is not found in Cicero, who prefers the feminine noun seruitus.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker is universally agreed to be Cassandra (cf. Del Rio I.179), likely shortly before her assassination, possibly in a scene involving Cassandra, Aegisthus, Clytemnestra, and Electra all on stage together, like at the end of Seneca’s Agamemnon (Aricò 2002, 12).

Bothe (I.195) believed that the line should be written scibam, hanc mi aut supremam lucem aut seruitî finem dari (because no character in the drama could say that the same day would see them die and their slavery be brought to an end). He thinks the line was spoken by Electra after having killed Clytemnestra and Aegisthus: if she had not killed them, she knows that she herself would have been murdered (the character of Electra often complains, in Greek and Roman tragedy, about being enslaved to her mother following Agamemnon’s death). (‘Electra haec, occisis Clytaemnestra et Aegistho, qui si mortem effugissent, consciam mactaturi erant. Servitium suum apud matrem post caedem Agamemnonis non semel virgo queritur apud tragicos’). Franchella 245 provides a convenient list of parallels, many of which had already been adduced before: Aesch. Ag. 1301 (Cassandra: ἥκει τόδ᾿ ἦμαρ [i.e. of her death]· σμικρὰ κερδανῶ φυγῇ ‘That day has come. I shall gain little by running away’, tr. Sommerstein); 1323-6 (Cassandra: ἡλίου δ᾿ ἐπεύχομαι | πρὸς ὕστατον φῶς, <δεσπότου> τιμαόροις | ἐχθροὺς φόνευσιν τὴν ἐμὴν τίνειν ὁμοῦ, | δούλης θανούσης, εὐμαροῦς χειρώματος ‘Looking on my last sunlight, I pray that my enemies may pay to my master’s avengers the penalty for my murder as well—for the death of a slave, an easy victim’, tr. Sommerstein); 1136-8 (Cassandra: ἰὼ ἰὼ ταλαίνας κακόποτμοι τύχαι· τὸ γὰρ ἐμὸν θροῶ πάθος | ἐπεγχύδαν. ποῖ δή με δεῦρο τὴν τάλαιναν ἤγαγες; οὐδέν ποτ᾿ εἰ | μὴ ξυνθανουμένην· τί γάρ; ‘Ió ió, my evil fate, my wretched fortune! I cry out my own sufferings, pouring them on top of these. Why have you brought me here in my misery? For no reason on earth, if not to die with him—what else?’, tr. Sommerstein) 1149 (Cassandra: ἐμοὶ δὲ μίμνει σχισμὸς ἀμφήκει δορί ‘while what awaits me is to be cloven by a two-edged weapon’, tr. Sommerstein); 1160-1 (Cassandra: νῦν δ᾿ ἀμφὶ Κωκυτόν τε κἀχερουσίους | ὄχθους ἔοικα θεσπιῳδήσειν τάχα ‘now it seems I shall soon be prophesying beside Cocytus and the steep banks of Acheron’ tr. Sommerstein); 1258-60 (Cassandra: αὕτη δίπους λέαινα, συγκοιμωμένη| λύκῳ λέοντος εὐγενοῦς ἀπουσίᾳ, | κτενεῖ με τὴν τάλαιναν ‘This is the two-footed lioness, sleeping with a wolf while the noble lion was away, who will kill me, wretched that I am’, tr. Sommerstein), 1289, 1291 (Cassandra: ἰοῦσα †πράξω† τλήσομαι τὸ κατθανεῖν· | Ἅιδου πύλας δὲ τάσδ᾿ ἐγὼ προσεννέπω ‘ I too shall go and have the courage to face death. I address these gates as the gates of Hades’, tr. Sommerstein); Sen. Ag. 750-2 (iuuat per ipsos ingredi Stygios lacus, | iuuat uidere Tartari saeuum canem | auidique regna Ditis! ‘What joy to embark on the very pools of Styx, what joy to see the savage hound of Tartarus and the realms of greedy Dis!’, tr. Fitch); 796-7 (Ag. ne metue dominam famula. Ca. Libertas adest. | Ag. Secura uiue. Ca. Mihi mori est securitas; ‘Ag. Do not fear your mistress, though a slave. Ca. Freedom is close. Ag. Live in security! Ca. For me death is security’, tr. Fitch). Franchella further suggests (p. 248 n. 3) that this fragment might be connected with Accius inc. fab. F 406 TRF = inc. fab. v. 655, F V Ribbeck3 = inc. 33 Warmington = inc. 700 Dangel (uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum, Cic. Orat. 156), in which Cassandra has a prophetic vision of two tombs, presumably her own and Agamemnon’s. For a different interpretation of that incertum, see Fragment reconstruction, Accius Aegisthus F 7 TRF = v. 23 Ribbeck3 = 12 Warmington = 310 Dangel.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Stasiastae
doc.translation: You have dared to strike <them> dead from the back!
doc.differences:
Dangel: 1 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 1/4)

D’Anto: 1 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 1/4)

Ribbeck1: 1 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 1/4)

Ribbeck2: 1 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 1/4)

Ribbeck3: 1 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 1/4)

Warmington: 610 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 2/4)

Corporare abs tergo es ausus! - x - x - ◡ ◡

D’Anto: Corporare abs tergo es ausus! - ◡ x

This fragment is always placed near the beginning of the play, mostly as the first fragment. Dangel describes the line as a taunt aimed at Lycurgus, most likely spoken by Liber. She placed it first because the other fragments can be seen as results from this taunt. The story would then go as follows: The king of Thrace, Lycurgus, does not like the new god Liber or his followers very much. Still, he grants them passage through his kingdom when they ask for it. In the night, when the Maenads and Liber are asleep, Lycurgus sneaks up on them and kills one of them. When this is found out, Liber accuses Lycurgus, (fr. 1), who will presumably deny these accusations. However, his actions have already led to an uprising within the group of Maenads (fr. 2). Liber is not fooled either and promises Lycurgus that he will take revenge. A description of this punishment is given in fr. 3. Finally, fr. 4 describes the dead body of Lycurgus.

Most other editors are in agreement with Dangel that this fragment should be placed first. Warmington is the only editor who diverges on this (fr. 610 Warmington). Instead, he thinks it should be second, following the fragment which Dangel places third. That fragment would then serve as a warning to Lycurgus, that his rebellious behaviour towards Liber is getting out of hand, and that his actions cause an uprising with the Maenads. This fragment would follow, either as an accusation after Lycurgus’ attack on the Maenads, or as a taunt after Lycurgus had killed his own son Dryas in madness. According to Warmnington, the play supposedly would have dealt with Lycurgus’ hostility towards Liber and his followers as a central theme, followed by Lycurgus’ downfall.
doc.apparatus: corporare] torporare Bergkius
doc.commentary:
abs] Abs means the same as ab or a, but can only be used in fixed constructions. Here, it is the preposition to tergo, forming the combined meaning of ‘from behind’. According to ancient battle customs, it was mostly not done to kill an opponent from behind. Rather, it showed cowardice and an unwillingness to face said opponent tête à tête. For instance, in the Iliad, Homer often followed a fixed pattern when describing sword fights, and he usually named the two fighters involved. While the type of attack and the outcome may vary, the structure seems fixed. There are no third parties involved, nor does one fighter attack the other from behind (Saunders 2006, 279). In this line, the addressee is explicitly accused of having done just that. In other words, the speaker of this verse calls the addressee too cowardly, weak, or simply not manly enough to look at his opponent when he kills them.

ausus es] The verb audere normally carries the meaning to be bold, or to venture. Here, the tone is less positive, if not outright accusing. Therefore, it should be translated as ‘you have the audacity’, with the negative undertone that the addressee really should regret what he has done, since he will not like the consequences of his actions.
doc.reconstruction:
This line is most likely direct speech, since the speaker uses the verb es to speak about the addressee in second person singular. This indicates that there is at least one other person present, to whom the speaker of this fragment speaks. The addressee is male, as indicated by the masculine form ausus.

Dangel thinks that this fragment should be read in the context of a dialogue between Liber and Lycurgus, and that this line is spoken by Liber as a reproach to Lycurgus. This conversation would take place after Lycurgus has killed one of Liber’s followers, the Maenads, in a rather cowardly way (abs tergo). Liber claims that Lycurgus had allowed himself and the Maenads passage through the latter’s territory, in order to sneakily and treacherously kill them at night. (Dangel 1995: 339).

Warmington agrees that this line is spoken by Liber to Lycurgus after an attack by the latter, and adds the possibility that this verse relates to the murder of Lycurgus’ son Dryas, who has been killed by Lycurgus in a fit of madness. There is an overall agreement that this accusation, as well as the crime it implies, set the punishment of Lycurgus in motion.

The overall plot of the play is uncertain. Warmington thinks that the play focuses on the hostility of Lycurgus towards Liber and his followers, followed by his downfall and death. The possibility of a parallel with the plots of Naevius’ Lycurgus and Euripides’ Bacchae is logical.

The metre of fragment is not collectively agreed upon either. It is, however, clear that this verse is not complete. Most editors have chosen to leave the rest of the verse blank, without assuming whether the missing part should go before or after the quoted passage. Two editors, Dangel and D’Anto, have filled in the rest of the verse as they see fit. They do not agree on the metre, however. Dangel thinks it should have been a trochaic septenarius, whereas D’Anto thinks it was an iambic senarius. Neither of these two options affect the word order or the overall meaning of the fragment.

Bibliography: @article{Saunders2006, author = {Saunders, K.B}, title = {Sword-Fighting in the Iliad: A Note on Elaúno}, journal = {Classical Quarterly}, year = {2006}, number = {56}, pages = {279-284}, month = {5}, volume = {1}, }

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes this line from Stasiastae to explain the meaning of the verb corporare, which means 'to kill and leave behind without a soul'.

‘Corporare’ est interficere et quasi corpus solum sine anima relinquere. Ennius (...) (21) Accius, Stasiastis vel Tropaeo Liberi. corporare abs tergo es ausus. (Non. I.18-22).

lines.text: Corporare abs tergo es ausus! - x - x - ◡ ◡
Id quod multi invideant multique expetant inscitia est
postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas.
Video sepulcra duo duorum corporum.
quem metuunt oderunt, quem quisque odit periisse expetit

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: O nurturing Faith, apparelled with wings, and the oath of Jupiter
doc.differences:
TrRF: 165 (frg. inc. )
Jocelyn: 350 (frg. inc. )
Warmington: 389 (frg. inc. )
Vahlen2: 403 (frg. inc. )
Ribbeck2/3: 380 (frg. inc.)
doc.apparatus: pinnis] pennis det. codd.
doc.commentary:
Fides] Fides was an important Roman deity, the personification of ‘good faith’ in private and public life. A temple, properly to Fides Publica Populi Romani, was erected to her somewhere between 254BC and 250BC on the Capitoline, near to the temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the most important temple in Rome. The centrality of Fides, and the connection between religiosity and the upholding of oaths, is the context of Cicero’s quotation of the Ennian line in De Officiis.

alma] Statius (Thebais 11.98) and Silius Italicus (Punica 6.132) both refer to Fides alma. In the case of Silius Italicus, it is in the context of the exemplum of Regulus, and draws on the distinctively Roman character of Fides. The instance from Statius is when the Furies are inciting the brothers Polyneices and Eteocles to fight – if the Ennian fragment does come from Thyestes, this could be a Statian nod to this particular line.

pinnis] the forms pinna and penna were already being used interchangeably in Ennius’ time (e.g. Plaut. As. 80). The better manuscripts have pinna, but then it is worth remembering this is as Cicero quotes the line. And Cicero uses the form pinna more often than penna. Fides does not appear in art as winged. Nor is Themis described as a winged goddess. Other winged personifications in Latin literature famously include Virgil’s Fama.

ius iurandum Iovis] Zeus in Greece and Jupiter in Rome were very often the deities oaths were sworn by. As Kovacs has argued in his commentary on Euripides’ Medea, Zeus, as the keeper of oaths (Zeus Horkios), is responsible for the events of the plot, even if Euripides’ Medeahas often been seen as a play where the gods are absent. In the myth of Thyestes and Atreus, the brothers swear a number of vows: one vow made is to cede the throne to the holder of the golden fleece (Atreus believed it was himself, but Aerope had secretly given it to Thyestes); another is sworn by Thyestes that he will give up the throne to Atreus when the sun reverses course. What is meant as an adynaton (i.e.: “I shall never give up th throne to Atreus”), however, will actually come true after Thyestes has eaten his own children (cena Thyestea): the sun turns away in disgust and Atreus proceeds to banish him. The deity responsible for the sun’s reversed course is Zeus. So in a Roman adaptation of the Thyestes and Medea myths, oaths sworn to Jupiter could easily feature.
doc.reconstruction:
It is hard to say which of Ennius’ plays this line might have come from. Ribbeck suggested, confidently in his first edition, then hesitatingly in his second and third editions, that it might be the Thyestes. The reason for this can be divined from his comments on Accius’ Atreus in his 1875 monograph: Ribbeck highlights that in the wake of the feast, both Accius’ and Seneca’s Thyestes complain about Atreus’ betrayal of their trust, using the keyword fides. While Ribbeck is not explicit, he presumably thought that Ennius’ ThyestesThyestes might also have this focus on fides and broken promises and that this line might belong to a speech of Thyestes’ after the dinner.

Meanwhile, Vahlen suggested Medea on the basis of a couple of parallels with Euripides’ MedeaMedea: κλύεθ᾽ οἷα λέγει κἀπιβοᾶται / Θέμιν εὐκταίαν Ζηνός, ὃς ὅρκων / θνητοῖς ταμίας νενόμισται (168-170); and θεοκλυτεῖ δ᾽ ἄδικα <πάθη> / παθοῦσα τὰν Ζηνὸς ὁρ- / κίαν Θέμιν (208-210). In both cases the chorus describes Medea praying to Themis, the Greek goddess of oaths. Vahlen does not quote Medea’s actual words, which are: ὦ μεγάλα Θέμι καὶ πότνι᾽ Ἄρτεμι, / λεύσσεθ᾽ ἃ πάσχω, μεγάλοις ὅρκοις / ἐνδησαμένα τὸν κατάρατον / πόσιν; (160-164). In the Ennian fragment, it is Zeus who the oath was sworn to; in Euripides’ MedeaMedea, the chorus mentions Zeus as the father of Themis, but Medea herself in her prayer addresses Artemis, not Zeus, along with Themis. The Ennian line is also in trochaic septenarii, which means that if it does belong to a MedeaMedea, it would be spoken by Medea, not the chorus, which means Ennius has heavily adapted Medea’s words.

The passage is quoted by Cicero, and Cicero often quotes from Ennius’ well-known plays without attribution to a specific title. He does so with the Medea (TrRF 89, 90) and Thyestes Thyestes (TrRF 134). An attribution to either Medea or ThyestesThyestes is plausible, although Fides was not a deity at any time syncretic with Themis, so either way Ennius is explicitly Romanizing.

lines.text: o Fides alma apta pinnis et ius iurandum Iovis

doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: pervade polum splendida mundi
sidera bigis continuis sex
†cepit† signis

doc.translation: With your two-horse chariot, traverse the sky, the shining celestial bodies of the universe, †…† with six continuous constellations.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck2-3:

Fragment inc. fab. XXII, v. 678-680:

678: Peruade polum, splendida mundi

679: Sidera binis continuis sex

680: Picti spoliis.

Ribbeck1: 

Fragment inc. fab. XXII, v. 678-680:

678: Peruade polum, splendida mundi

679: Sidera binis continuis sex

D’Antò:

679: sidera binis continuis sex

Dangel:

doc.apparatus:
2: bigis ] bigis F: binis Bothe I.249, iam Popma 1788, 173: bis qui Nosarti 1976, 74: bigis bis Kent 1936, 75

2: continuis sex ] continuis sex Scaliger 1565, 129: continui se F

3: †cepit† ] †cepit† F: picti Ribbeck2, 224: saepti Ribbeck1, 191: apti Scaliger 1565, 129: addita Müller 1833, 123: sepit uel saepit Nosarti 1976, 74: repit Cazzaniga 1971, 58: expicti Kent 1936, 75

3: signis ] signis Scaliger 1565, 129: spoliis F: polus Cazzaniga 1971, 58: spatiis Dangel 1995, 246

3: †cepit† signis ] †cepit† signis: sesepit soliis Popma 1788, 173

Scaliger (1565, 129) rewrites the passage in prose (‘peruade polum bigis, appositiue, splendida sidera mundi apti sex signis’), compares it to Vergil’s description of Atlas (Aen. 4.482 = 6.797 axem umero torquet stellis ardentibus [fulgentibus DS ad Aen. 1.108] aptum), and explains it as follows: ‘mundum ergo aptum sex signis intellegit hoc superius hemisphaerium: nam sex altera signa sunt in inferiore’). His correction of the manuscript’s spoliis (an error probably introduced on account of dittography arising from polus, cf. Popma 1788, 173) to signis is almost certainly correct, because the passage that follows shows that Varro read the word signum (signa dicuntur et eadem et sidera. signa quod aliquid significent, ut libra aequinoctium…, ‘Signa “signs of the zodiac” means the same as sīdera “constellations”. The signa got their name because they significant “indicate” something, as the Libra indicates the equinox…’, transl. De Melo 2019, I.427), cf. OLD s.v. 11b ‘(spec.) a zodiacal constellation, a sign of the zodiac (or the division of the heavens in which it lies)’. Scaliger seems to understand bigis as a noun (i.e. ‘in a two-horse chariot’) and the rest of the sentence in apposition to polum. Popma (1788, 173) suggests emending what in the F manuscript reads continui se cepit spoliis to binis continuis sesepit soliis, arguing that bina solia refers to both northern and southern hemispheres; he glosses sesepit as seorsum munit. Goetz–Schoell, in their edition of Varro’s De Lingua Latina (1910, 96), are manifestly unpersuaded by Scaliger’s signis, because they print the paradosis spoliis and obelise bigis (†bigis…spoliis). In their notes (p. 271-2), they point out that the reading binis continuis sex apti signis (a combination of Scaliger’s and Popma’s emendations) is problematic because binis sex is unattested, the usual expression being bis senis.  Goetz–Schoell argue (following Popma) that the apparent allusion to our fragment in a Menippean Satire entitled Dolium aut seria by Varro secures the reading bigis. Furthermore, they posit a lacuna between bigis and continuis, proposing to supply: bigis <Luna ut limbum cum signis bis> continuis sex cepit ‘...on a two-horse chariot, like the Moon, whenever she takes the Belt with its twelve constellations/has taken up her position (OLD s.v. 7 ‘to enter or take up (a position)’) in the zodiac with its twelve continuous (or successive?) constellations’.

The text that Baldarelli (2004, 249) prints is the same as the TRF, but with the following punctuation:

pervade polum splendida mundi

sidera, bigis; continuis sex 

†cepit† signis

De Melo’s text and translation are as follows (2019, 426-7), printing Kent’s conjectures bigis <bis> and expicti (cf. Kent 1936, 75):

Peruade polum, splendida mundi

sidera, bigis, bis continuis

sex expicti signis

With thy team do thou go through the sky, through the bright constellations aloft, which the universe holds, adorned with its twice six continuous signs. (Kent’s own translation, 1951, I.283).

With your chariot go through the sky, the bright constellations of the universe painted with twice continuous signs of the zodiac. (De Melo’s translation, adapted).

For a defence of the reading bigis continuis sex, printed by TRF, see ‘Fragment commentary’.
doc.commentary:
1-3: parallels that have been suggested include [Aesch.] PV 91, Eur. Or. 1685, Ion 1148-1157, F 779.90 TrGF, F 839.8-11 TrGF, Ennius Andromeda F 34 TRF, Ennius Hecuba F 72 TRF, Varro, Men. 92-96 B.-A., Ov. Met. 2.129-133, Manilius 2.218-220.

1 bigis: often used of deified personifications such Moon, Dawn, or Night (TLL II.1982.50-66), cf. Ennius Andromeda F 34 TRF: <sacra nox> [Bücheler] quae caua caeli | signitenentibus conficis bigis, ‘you who traverse the hollows of the sky to the end on your star-bearing chariot’, (transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL I.43), also preserved in Varro’s De Lingua Latina (5.19), and Varro Men. F 92 Bücheler–Astbury (= [Prob.] Verg. Ecl. 6.31): mundus domus est maxima homulli | quam quinque altitonae †fragmine† zonae | cingunt, per quam limbus pictus | bis sex signis stellumicantibus | altus in obliquo aethere Lunae | bigas acceptat, ‘L’Univers est l’immense demeure du pauvre petit homme; par découpage du ciel, cinq zones l’entourent; à travers elle, un bandeau brodé de douze constellations brillantes d’étoiles reçoit, haut dans le firmament oblique, le bige de la Lune’ (transl. Cèbe 1972-1999, III.408).

2-3 continuis sex…signis: for the division of the constellations of the Zodiac into two parts, diurnal and nocturnal, Pierini (1980, 101-2) compares Cicero Aratea 334-340 hic quantus terris consectus pellitur orbis, | tantundem pandens supera mortalibus edit: | sex omni semper cedunt labentia nocte, | tot caelum rursus fugientia signa reuisunt. | hoc spatium tranans caecis nox conficit umbris, | quod supera terras prima de nocte relictum est | signifero ex orbi, sex signorum ordine fultum, ‘Ce cercle est coupé en deux parties égales, dont l’une est chassée sous la terre, et l’autre au-dessus de l’horizon vient s’offrir aux regards des mortels. Chaque nuit, toujours, six signes vont déclinant, et six, inversement, s’évadent pour venir revoir le ciel. La nuit passe et fait peser ses sombres ténébreuses sur la portion du Zodiaque que le crépuscule a laissée au-dessus de la terre et que soutient la succession de six signes’ (transl. Soubiran 20022, 185) and Manilius 2.218-220 quidam etiam sex continuis dixere diurnas | esse vices astris, quae sunt a principe signo | Lanigeri, sex a Libra nocturna videri, ‘Some have also asserted that the diurnal stations belong to the six consecutive stars which begin with the Ram and that the six from the Balance count as nocturnal’ (transl. Goold 1977, 99-101), compared by Pierini 1980, 101. Pierini points out that Manilius’ use of the expression sex continuis…astris to describe the diurnal constellations seems to echo Accius’ continuis sex…signis (assuming that this is the correct reading). She thus suggests that, in the Accian passage, the goddess Night is addressed, and that sex continuis…signis refers only to the six constellations visible during the night, and not the twelve constellations in total. On this account, attempts to reconstruct, by means of conjectural emendation, a reference to ‘twelve’ constellations (e.g. binis sex (Scaliger, Bothe), binis soliis (Popma, with solium = one hemisphere containing six constellations, or bis sex (Goetz–Schoell, Kent)) are erroneous.
doc.reconstruction: Baldarelli (2004, 249-50) suggests that this fragment might belong to Accius’ Atreus. She connects it with F 111 TRF (sed quid? tonitru…), in which the chorus describe an earthquake accompanied by thunder, and suggests that this fragment might belong to a sequence of the choral ode in which the chorus prays to the moon to shine its light following the disappearance of the sun. Bothe (I.249) thought that these were the words of the Sun to Phaethon [does anyone before Bothe suggest this?]; Ribbeck (1852, 301), comparing [Aesch.] PV 91-92, suggested that this fragment belongs to the Prometheus; Cazzaniga (1971, 59) thought it might relate to the death of Hercules; Degl’Innocenti Pierini (1980, 102) that the addressee is either the Moon or Night.
doc.metrical_analysis: Anapaestic dimeters, or quaternarii (an4).

context.author: Varro
context.text:
Varro, De Lingua Latina 7.14:

Quod est apud Accium:

Peruade polum, splendida mundi

sidera, bigis, bis sex continuis

sex expicti signis,... (De Melo’s text of the fragment)

pervade polum splendida mundi

sidera bigis continuis sex

†cepit† signis (TRF’s text of the fragment)

polus Graecum, id significat ‘circum caeli’. Quare quod est peruade polum ualet ‘uade περὶ πόλον’. Signa dicuntur eadem et sidera. Signa quod aliquid significent, ut libra aequinoctium; sidera quae quasi insidunt atque ita significant aliquid in terris perurendo aliaue qua re; ut signum candens in pecore.

As for what is in Accius:

With your chariot go through the polus ‘sky’, the bright sīdera ‘constellations’ of the universe painted with twice six continuous signa ‘signs of the zodiac’ (translation of De Melo’s text of the fragment)

With your two-horse chariot, traverse the sky, the shining celestial bodies of the universe, †…† with six continuous constellations (translation of TRF’s text of the fragment)

polus ‘sky’ is Greek, and it means the circus caelī ‘circle of the sky’. Therefore the phrase peruāde polum ‘go through the sky’ means uāde περὶ πόλον ‘go around the πόλος’. Signa ‘signs of the zodiac’ means the same as sīdera ‘constellations’. The signa got their name because the significant ‘indicate’ something, as the Libra indicates the equinox; sīdera are those which, as it were, īnsīdunt ‘settle down’ and in this way indicate something on earth by burning or in some other way; as for example the signum candēns ‘scorching sign’ in cattle.

(Text and transl. De Melo 2019, I.426-7).

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: He wore slippers on his feet, he was cloaked in a thin garment
doc.differences:
TrRF: 43 (Naevius’ Incerta 3/12)

Warmington: 43 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 20/36)

Ribbeck2: 57 (Naevius’ Incerta 4/12)

Ribbeck3: 54 (Naevius’ Incerta 4/12)

Marmorale: 4 (Naevius’ Incerta 4/12)

Klotz: 54 (Naevius’ Incerta 4/12)

There are no textual differences between the editors, only their ordering differs. Warmington assigns the fragment to the Lycurgus and places it within the report of the guard on how they captured the Maenads, just before the fragment on how Bacchus was captured (TrRF. 37). Ribbeck, Marmorale and Klotz all place the fragment among the incerta of Naevius.
doc.apparatus:
diabathra] dyabathra F

pedibus] pecudibus F codd.

epicroco] epycroco F

erat] erat F G H a : et erat b : om. rell.
doc.commentary:
diabathra] These are a type of sandals seen as typically Greek. Cf. Paul. ex. Fest. p.65: Diabathra genus solearum Graecanicarum.

epicroco] This is a fine, yellow cloak, which is transparent. Cf. Paul ex. Fest. p. 72: Epicrocum amiculi croco tinctum, tenue et perlucidum.
doc.reconstruction:
Most of the difficulties in interpreting this fragment arise from the fact that Varro did not mention from which work of Naevius he cited this line. Marmorale and Klotz propose that these words are said about Bacchus in the Lycurgus. Bothe and Warmington place the fragment in the Lycurgus as well, thinking that these words are said by the attendants in the description of the capture of the Maenads.

Bothe, following Scaliger, even thinks that this line should follow directly after the other fragment of Naevius which is quoted by Varro in the same paragraph: risi egomet, casabundum ire ebrium. Duentzer, Klussmann and Bergk think that another fragment of Naevius about clothing, TrRF. 40, pallis patagiis crocotis malacis mortualibus should follow directly after this fragment.

All these reconstructions could be possible, but the uncertainty whether the fragment really belongs to the Lycurgus remains. The fragment might as well belong to a different tragedy of Naevius which is not transmitted in which Bacchus is a character as well. Traglia even proposes that this could be a fragment from a comedy of Naevius, which is a possibility as well.

there is consensus among the editors that the metre is a septenarius trochaicus. Ribbeck however proposes to change the word order of the beginning of the fragment in pedibus diabathra habebat instead of diabathra in pedibus habebat so the metre would fit better.
