context.author: Varro
context.text:
Varro quotes this line once in De Lingua Latina for the two Greek loanwords the line contains: diabathra and epicroco:

Apud Naevium: Risi egomet mecum cassabundum ire ebrium. [Naev. com. 120 Ri.]: Cassabundum a cadendo. Idem: Diabathra in pedibus habebat, erat amictus epicroco. Utrumque vocabulum Graecum. (Varro, Ling. 7.53)

lines.text: diabathra in pedibus habebat, erat amictus epicroco
Ipsus se in terram saucius fligit cadens.
Solemnitusque †adeo ditali laudet lubens.
........ Celebri gradu
gressum adcelerasse decet
Cur fugit fratrem? Scit ipse.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: "Hail to your strenght! I regard your deeds and omen as good".
doc.commentary: macte esto virtute is used as an expression of applause or gratulation of something that needs to be done or has been done. Schierl assumes a change of speaker between operaque and omenque, because virtute operaque can be an idiomatic phrase (cf. Liv. 10,40,11). Some editors regard the double -que as a dittography. In my opinion, a change of speaker is not necessary: it is possible to regard opera and omen as accusatives by approbo.
doc.reconstruction: The persons involved may be Electra who encourages Orestes and gives her approval of the killing of Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus.

lines.text: macte esto virtute operaque! :: omenque approbo
quem metuunt oderunt; quem quisque odit periisse expetit.
... melius quam viri
callent mulieres.

doc.author: Pomponius Secundus
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: [...] nunc te obsecro,
stirpem ut evolvas meorum <rem>que notifices mihi.

doc.translation: Now I beseech you to unfold my lineage and make the matter known to me.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck2-3:

Fragment Pomponius Secundus Atreus I, v. 1-2:

[ATREUS]:

1: nunc te obsecro,

2: Stirpem ut euoluas meorum <me>que notifices mihi.

Ribbeck1:

Fragment Pomponius Secundus Atreus I, v. 3-4:

3: nunc te obsecro,

4: Stirpem ut euoluas meorum <nomen> notifices mihi.

Not in Warmington.

Franchella:

Fragment 14 (14/22), 375-376:

375: nunc te obsecro

376: stirpem ut euoluas meorum <rem>que notifices mihi

Ti scongiuro di parlarmi dei miei figli e di farmi conoscere la cosa.

Not in D’Antò.

Not in Dangel.
doc.apparatus:
2: meorum <rem>que ] meorum <rem>que Müller 1888, I.208: meorumque codd.: meorum <quaeque> Bothe I.273: meorumque <nomen> Ribbeck1, 197: meorum <iamque> Schmidt 1861, 597

The paradosis of the second line does not scan because it lacks a syllable (stirpem ut euoluas meorumque notifices mihi). The text printed by the TRF (as well as Lindsay) is remque, a conjecture by Lucian Müller (1888, I.208), which can be taken to mean ‘the matter’.
doc.commentary:
A character wishes to know about his offspring, and asks his interlocutor for information.

2 euoluas: the verb euoluo, which has the primary sense of ‘to eject with a rolling or coiling motion’ (OLD s.v. 1), here means ‘to make known by narrative, exposition, etc., unfold’ (OLD s.v. 7, cf. TLL V.2.1069.35-56).

2 remque notifices mihi: ‘to make the matter known to me’.
doc.reconstruction: Franchella (230) attributes this fragment of Pomponius Secundus’ Atreus to the play of the same name by Accius (F. 14 (14/22), 375-376). He explains: ‘Il Lindsay colloca questo frammento, con esitazione, tra quelli dell’Atreus di Accio. A me sembra che, sia per ragioni metriche sia per il contenuto, possa essere di Accio. D’altronde nulla vieta di pensare che Pomponio abbia seguito l’Atreus di Accio nel riportare la cena tiestea nella sua tragedia’. Lana (1959, 334-5), who believes that this fragment belongs to Pomponius Secundus’ Atreus, interprets it as belonging to a dialogue between Atreus, who is the speaker, and his wife Aerope, which takes place after Atreus discovers Aerope’s adulterous relationship with Thyestes. Atreus wishes to know which of Aerope’s children are legitimate (fathered by him) and which are illegitimate (fathered by Thyestes). Acknowledging that the tone of the request (nunc te obsecro…) might make it difficult to imagine that these could be the words of a husband who has just discovered his wife’s infidelity, Lana suggests that the fragment might also be interpreted as the words of Thyestes, having finished his meal at the banquet and asking Atreus where his children are (comparing the use of stirps in the sense of ‘children’ at Ennius inc. F 171 TRF, ut tibi Titanis Trivia dederit stirpem liberum (‘so that [or: as soon as] Trivia, of Titan’s family, gave [or: will have given] you a line of children, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL II.181) and inc. F 152 TRF, neque tuum umquam in gremium extollas liberorum ex te genus (‘and you may never lift into your lap offspring of children from you’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL II.161)). The notion that this fragment is from an (otherwise unattested) fabula Attelana entitled Atreus by the playwright L. Pomponius of Bononia, i.e. modern Bologna (fl. 89 BCE), entertained by Hertz (1869, 4 n. 3), has been restated by Welsh (2013, 276). Welsh argues, on the grounds that the diction of this fragment is similar to that found in other fragments of Attelana, both by Pomponius (Armorum iudicium fr. 91-2 Ribbeck3: tum prae se portant ascendibilem semitam | quam scalam uocitant, ‘at that point they carry in front of them a climbable path which they call a “ladder”’; Agamemno Suppositus fr. 4-5 Ribbeck3: ne quis miretur, cum tam clare tonuerit, | ut, si quis dormitaret, expergisceret, ‘let nobody be astonished, when it thunders so loudly, that, if someone is asleep, they are awakened’) and by Novius (Andromache fr. 4a-b Ribbeck3: quod tu, mi gnate, quaeso ut in pectus tuum | demittas tamquam in fiscinam uindemitor, ‘I ask of you, my son, that you cast this down into your heart like a grape-gatherer into a basket’), that the fragment should be ascribed to ‘a farcical script entitled Atreus’ by Pomponius of Bononia rather than to Pomponius Secundus’ Atreus: Welsh’s argument is endorsed by Panayotakis 2023, 419. Haley (2021b) suggests that F Adesp. 83 TRF (ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam) also belongs to Pomponius of Bononia’s Atreus.
doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarii.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius De compendiosa doctrina p. 144.21-23 Mercier = 210.21-23 Lindsay

NOTIFICEM, notam faciam. Pomponius Atreo:

nunc te obsecro 

stirpem ut evolvas meorum remque notifices mihi.

This fragment of Pomponius Secundus’ Atreus is preserved in Book 2 Nonius’ De compendiosa doctrina, entitled ‘De honestis et nove veterum dictis’. It is cited as an example of the use of the verb notifico ‘to make known’. Lindsay (1901, 51) suggests that the fragment is quoted from an anonymous glossary known as ‘Alph. Verb.’ or ‘source 27’, which contains a list of verbs arranged in alphabetical order. Lindsay reports the fragment as belonging to Accius’ Atreus.

lines.text: [ATREUS]: Iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum aduenit,
Iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat:
Maior mihi moles, maius miscendumst malum,
Qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam.
[THYESTES]: – x – x – x – x – x fregistin fidem !
[ATREUS]: Neque dedi, neque do infideli cuiquam ! – x – u x
quin, quod parere <mihi> vos maiestas mea
procat, toleratis temploque hanc deducitis?
ibi quid agat, secum cogitat, parat, putat.
[THYESTES]: <20> fregisti fidem.
[ATREUS]: Neque dedi neque do infideli cuiquam .  .  .  .  .

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: matrem [e] ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas!

doc.translation: You criticize your mother for a lawful deed but approve of your unlawful father!
doc.differences:
TRF: 139 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/10)

Ribbeck: 41 (Accius' Clytemnestra 10/10)

Warmington: 245 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/9)

Franchella: 400 (Accius' Clytemnestra 10/10)

D'Antò: 41 (Accius' Clytemnestra 10/10)

Dangel: 300 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/10)


Warmington:

Your mother for a righteous deed you blame, | Your father, all unrighteous, you acclaim.

Franchella:

matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas?

Tu rimproveri la madre per avere agito secondo giustizia ⟨ed⟩ approvi l’ingiusto padre?

D'Antò:

matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas

La madre biasimi per un’azione giusta, il padre ingiusto approvi.

Dangel:

Tu invectives ta mère pour un acte de justice, mais tu approuves l’injustice de ton père !
doc.apparatus:
matrem ob] matrem ob Grotius: matre meo LFHEGP2: matere meo P1: mater meo Jenson, ed. Ven. 1476, f. xlvi r: mater nec uel mero Fruterius (apud Meyer 1878, 242): mater mea Delrius: matrem me ob Bergk 1835, 82

incilas] incilas codd.: incilans Jenson, ed. Ven. 1476, f. xlvi r: incitas Bergk 1842, 238

matrem ob iure factum incilas] matrem ob iure factum incilas Grotius: mater, me eiuras; astu incilas Palmerius apud Gruterum 1602-1634 [1604], IV.769, cf. Ter. Eun. 987 an in astu uenit?: matrem ob iure factum incilans Hermann

The paradosis matre meo iure factum incilas is ungrammatical (matre meo iure cannot stand as a clause in the ablative absolute). Grotius’ conjecture matrem ob iure solves the problem, making matrem object of the verb incilas and iure factum dependant on the preposition ob (‘on account of, for, because of, by reason of, etc.’, cf. e.g. Ter. An. 610 ego pretium ob stultitiam fero ‘I’m paying the price for my stupidity’). Two separate series of conjectures by Grotius are to be distinguished (Nellen 2007, 301 n. 142): the present conjecture is reported by Vossius in Scriverius’ Collectanea of 1620. It was thus made before the publication of Scriverius and Vossius’ 1620 edition, and thus belongs to the first series of conjectures, and not to the second, which Grotius made in his copy of Scriverius’ and Vossius’ edition after it had been published. Although Grotius’ copy of this edition is now lost, the emendations he made in the margins are preserved in a manuscript by Pieter Burman (1668-1741, UBL Shelfmark Per Q 41:2). Burman’s manuscript is printed by Peerlkamp in 1828: the conjectural emendations on the fragments of Republican tragedies are attributed dubiously to Hugo Grotius (i.e. uiri eruditi [fort. Hugonis Grotii] emendationes...). In her critical apparatus (p. 168), Dangel reports that Nicolaus Jenson’s edition of Nonius (Venice 1476, ISTC in00265000) has the reading mater meo: this reading is only found in some copies (e.g. the one now in Bayerische Staatsbibliothek, Munich; the copies now held in Biblioteca Nazionale Marciana in Venice and in Universiteitsbibliotheek Leiden read the paradosis matre meo). It is unclear whether mater meo is a genuine attempt by Jenson to emend the text or whether it was deemed a misprint and therefore subsequently ‘corrected’ to the paradosis matre meo (perhaps as a result of a misunderstanding between Jenson and the printer?). In any case, unlike matre meo, mater meo does actually produce meaningful Latin if mater were to be understood as a vocative: mater, meo iure factum incilas... could be translated as ‘mother, you criticize a deed done according to my rights/committed in accordance with my decree...’. This however would not square with the rest of the line, as Electra could hardly say that Clytemnestra considers Agamemnon’s actions to be lawful. Del Rio’s (i.e. Delrius') attempt to emend the text by positing a vocative mater mea is to be rejected on the same grounds. Bergk’s attempt to improve Grotius’ conjecture, matrem me ob iure factum incilas, does not scan as a single iambic senarius. It could be set as two truncated septenarii (- ˘ - ˘ - ˘ - ˘ matrem me ob iure factum | incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas - ˘ - ˘ -), but this would disrupt the effective structure of a single line containing two clauses in adversative asyndeton, one with a verb before the main caesura, the other with a verb at line-end. This verse structure is well paralleled in Accius and elsewhere in Republican verse (see ‘Fragment commentary’). In Jenson’s 1476 edition of Nonius, the present participle incilans is found instead of the second-person singular incilas: while this reading won the approval of Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xiii), it too is undesirable on stylistic grounds because it would remove the balanced antithesis created by the two second-person verbs (incilas…adprobas, on which see ‘Fragment commentary’). Jan Meller Palmerius (apud Gruter 1602-1634 [1604], IV.769) suggests the following text: mater, me eiuras; astu incilas; genitorem iniustum adprobas, with astu in the Greek sense of ‘city’, attested only at Ter. Eun. 987. It is a text I cannot make sense of.
doc.commentary:
ob iure...iniustum: Latin ius can mean both ‘justice’ and ‘law’ (and, further, ‘divine law’, ‘natural law’, and ‘law by convention’). The issue of whether Clytemnestra acted justly, and indeed of whether a murder can be ‘just’, is at the heart of the agones between Clytemnestra and Electra in both Sophocles’ and Euripides’ Electra. Cf. e.g. Soph. El. 523 (Cl.) ἐγὼ δ᾿ὕβριν μὲν οὐκ ἔχω (‘I do no violence’), 528 (Cl.) ἡ γὰρ Δίκη νιν [sc. Agamemnon] εἷλεν (‘Yes, Justice was his killer’, tr. Lloyd-Jones). Ius here has the sense ‘strictius fere i. q. legitime, iuste’, TLL VII.2.698.47-698.58.

D’Antò 204 notes that antithesis between two propositions in asyndeton is common (‘antithetische parallel gebaute Kola’, Leo 1913, 403 n. 4): Ennius Achilles F 6 TRF: serua ciues, defende hostes... ‘save the citizens, ward off the enemy…’; Alcmeo F 13.4 TRF: fer mi auxilium, pestem abige a me ‘bring me help, drive away this plague from me’; Medea (exul): F 92 TRF: tu me amoris magis quam honoris seruauisti gratia ‘you have saved me for the sake of love rather than for the sake of honor’; Incerta F 151.7 TRF tui me miseret, mei piget ‘I have pity for you, I am vexed at myself’; Adesp. F 124.2 TRF Helena redeat, uirgo pereat…? ‘should Helen return, should a young girl die…?’; Naevius Tarentilla F 72.3 FRL: alii adnutat, alii adnictat ‘To one she nods, at another she winks’; Pl. Amph. 683 sic salutas atque appellas ‘...greeting and addressing me like this…’, 1013 medicinis in tonstrinis ‘at the doctors’, at the barbers’’; Bacch. 64, Curc. 179, 285; 286; 297; Epid. 198, 230; 231; Men. 403, 1015; Merc. 493, 833; Mil. 331; Pseud. 695; Truc. 824.

incilas: a rare verb, the only attestation of which (outside the fragments preserved in the Nonian lemma) is at Lucr. 3.963 iure increpet inciletque 'she rightly upbraids and rebukes you' (i.e. Nature upbraiding the person who refuses to die).
doc.reconstruction:
Scholars understand these as the words of either Aegisthus or Clytemnestra to Electra. They probably formed part of a dialogue between Clytemnestra and Electra (and perhaps also Aegisthus) similar to the ones in Sophocles’ Electra (516-659), Euripides’ Electra (998-1146), and Seneca’s Agamemnon (953-1000). It has been suggested (Venini 1954, 327) that similar scenes potentially occurred in Livius Andronicus’ Aegisthus (F 2 and possibly 5 TRF) and in Pacuvius’ Dulorestes (F 84 TRF = 94 Schierl). Franchella (251) imagines this fragment to belong to the same dialogue as Accius Clytemnestra F 138 TRF = v. 40 Ribbeck3 = 247 Warmington = 297 Dangel (seras potiuntur plagas). He thinks that these are the words of Clytemnestra to Electra in the course of an exchange during which Clytemnestra (or Aegisthus) previously had expressed their satisfaction that Agamemnon and Cassandra had finally received punishment, to which Electra responded reproachfully. Ribbeck1, 300 compares Clytemnestra’s words to the chorus at Aesch. Ag. 1412-14 (νῦν μὲν δικάζεις ἐκ πόλεως φυγὴν ἐμοὶ | καὶ μῖσος ἀστῶν δημόθρους τ᾿ ἔχειν ἀράς, | οὐδὲν τότ᾿ ἀνδρὶ τῷδ᾿ ἐναντίον φέρων; ‘Now you judge me to have incurred exile from the city, the hatred of the community, and loud public curses; but you didn’t show any opposition at all to this man at that former time…’, tr. Sommerstein) and her words to Orestes at Cho. 918 (μἀλλ᾿ εἴφ᾿ ὁμοίως καὶ πατρὸς τοῦ σοῦ μάτας, ‘No, you should speak equally of your father’s lustful follies’, tr. Sommerstein), while Di Benedetto 1983, 168ff. (cited in Aricò 2002, 11) compares Soph. El. 287-91: αὕτη γὰρ ἡ λόγοισι γενναία γυνὴ | φωνοῦσα τοιάδ᾿ ἐξονειδίζει κακά, | ‘ὦ δύσθεον μίσημα, σοὶ μόνῃ πατὴρ | τέθνηκεν; ἄλλος δ᾿ οὔτις ἐν πένθει βροτῶν; | κακῶς ὄλοιο [...]’, ‘Yes, this woman, who is said to be so noble, gives tongue and utters insults such as these: “Accursed, hateful creature, are you the only one that has lost a father? Does no other mortal mourn a loss? May you perish miserably…”’ (tr. Lloyd-Jones). Ribbeck also connects this fragment to a number of fragments of uncertain play or authorship:

(i) Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF = v. 656, inc. fab. F VI Ribbeck3 = Inc. 34 Warmington = Inc. 701 Dangel.

(ii) Accius inc. fab. F 413 TRF = v. 651, inc. fab. F I Ribbeck3 = Inc. 18 Warmington = Inc. 692 Dangel.

(iii) Adesp. F 24 TRF = Inc. inc. fab. v. 125-126, F LXVII Ribbeck3 = Inc. inc. (Accius?) 23-4 Warmington, not in Dangel.

(iv) Adesp. F 84 TRF = Inc. inc. fab. v. 127-128, F LXVIII Ribbeck3 = Inc. Inc. 131-2 Warmington, not in Dangel.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 124.32-125.7 Mercier = 180.36-181.8 Lindsay:

incilare est increpare vel inprobare. Accius Meleagro: quis erit qui non me spernens, incilans probris, | sermone indecorans turpi fama differet? idem Clytaemestra: matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas? Pacuvius Duloreste: siquis hac me oratione incilet, quid respondeam? Lucilius lib. XXX: nunc, Gai, quoniam inclines nos laedi’ vicissim.

Nonius quotes this fragment to illustrate the meaning of the verb incilare ‘to revile, abuse’.

lines.text: Iuppiter Dardanum genuit, Dardanus Troum, Trous Assaracum et Ilum Ganymedemque; Capys ex Assaraco satus; qui statuit Pergamum, Laomedon Priamum et Capys Anchisen edidit.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: mulier una duum virum

doc.translation: One wife of/for two husbands
doc.differences:
TRF: 406 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 655 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 33 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 656 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 655 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 700 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

I see, of corpses two, two sepulchres.

Franchella:

uideo sepulchra duo duorum corporum.

Vedo due sepolcri di due cadaveri.

D'Antò:

uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum.

Vedo due sepolcri per due cadaveri.

Dangel:

Je vois deux tombeaux pour deux corps.
doc.apparatus:
duum uirum] duum uirum codd.: duum uirorum Ribbeck1-2

duum] duum LAμ.: apud duum BNQ: duom Heerdegen 1884

In his first and second editions, Ribbeck prints his own conjecture, duum uirorum, in which changes the paradosis uirum, archaic form of the genitive plural, to uirorum, the uncontracted form of the genitive plural that was more common in Cicero’s day. Ribbeck presumably thought that a scribe had mistakenly written uirum, the archaic form of the genitive plural of uir, instead of uirorum, the uncontracted genitive form, likely because of the similarity of the form uirum with the immediately preceding word duum. Warmington prints Ribbeck’s uirorum, without however mentioning in his critical apparatus that the reading is a conjecture by Ribbeck. In his third edition, Ribbeck restores the transmitted reading uirum and, further, adopts the emendation of duom (archaic form of duum) proposed by Heerdegen in his edition of the Orator in 1884. The critical apparatus above is based mainly on that of Westman in his edition of Cicero’s Orator (20022, 50). For a full list of manuscripts and their respective sigla, see Westman 20022, viii-xvii. For a succinct account of the textual tradition of Cicero's Orator, see Winterbottom–Rouse–Reeve apud Reynolds 19862, 103-109.
doc.commentary: duorum corporum: a genitive of possession (Woodcock §72) depending on sepulcra, ‘tombs’. Translated either as ‘for two corpses’ (D’Antò, Dangel) or ‘of two corpses’ (Warmington, Franchella).

context.author: Cicero
context.text: This fragment is quoted, alongside Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (video sepulcra dua duorum corporum), at Cic. Orat. 156, in a section of the treatise in which the phenomena of hiatus, elision, and related sonic effects are discussed (Orat. 149-64: cf. Kaster 2020, 213-23). At Orat. 155-6, Cicero presents the view of some contemporary critics who believe that forms such as the archaic genitive plural deum, the contracted form of deorum which was more in use in Cicero’s day, should not be used and that the uncontracted form should be preferred. Cicero cites several examples from Republican tragedy in which the contracted form is used, among which notably our two fragments of Accius (see above) and Fragmenta ex certis incertorum tragoediis F 1.1-3 TRF = Pacuvius Chryses F 76 Schierl. The fragments of Accius are quoted to show that the poet uses the genitive plural forms duorum and duum indifferently.

doc.metrical_analysis: As printed by the TRF edition (i.e. quae mulier una | usurpat duplex cubile), the metre would seem to be the trochaic octonarius (Raven §53B) because in this metre the penultimate syllable of the line is heavy (ūna). In his first edition, Ribbeck includes a note about the metre of this fragment (Ribbeck1, 216) in which he suggests that, if the text is read as a single line (quae mulier una usurpat duplex cubile), it could be the final part of an iambic septenarius (Raven §44B). Ribbeck notes, however, that there is no certain attestation of this metre being used in tragedy, comparing the dubious examples at Enn. inc. F 205 TRF = 346 Jocelyn (aliquot somnia uera…sed omnia non necesse est, = Ennius inc. fab. F LVI, v. 401, Ribbeck1) and at Accius inc. fab. F 435 (cum illud oculi⟨s⟩ violave⟨rat⟩ [de Melo: oblivo lavat F] qui invidit [de Melo: incidit F] invidendum = Acc. inc. fab. 697, F XXXV Ribbeck1 = Varro, L.L. 6.80): see De Melo II.880 on the latter fragment, and Moore 2012, 184: ‘the iambic septenarius appears to have had a special association with comedy: its Greek equivalent, the iambic tetrameter catalectic, is never used in extant tragedy’. If Bergk’s combination of this fragment with Accius inc. fab. F 407 (see ‘Fragment reconstruction’) were accepted, i.e. quae mulier una duum uirum | usurpat duplex cubile…, then the metre would be the trochaic septenarius.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: erras erras: nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi
repriment ualidae legum habenae atque imperi insistent iugo

doc.translation: You are mistaken, you are mistaken. You are unrestrained and overly self-confident: the powerful reins of the law will keep you in check and stand you in the yoke of authority.
doc.differences:
TRF: 24 (Adespoton)

Ribbeck: 125-126 (Adespoton)

Warmington: 23-24 (Adespoton)

Franchella: 35 (Adespoton)

Not in D'Antò's edition.

Not in Dangel's edition.

Warmington:

You err, you err; for though you do exult, | And in yourself are overconfident,

The sturdy reins of laws will pull you back, | And stand you firm in yoke of government.

Franchella:

erras erras: nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi

repriment ualidae legum habenae atque imperi inistent iugo

‘Sbagli sbagli: poiché le forti briglie delle leggi ti freneranno e ti peseranno

addosso col giogo del comando, mentre esulti e confidi troppo in te’.
doc.apparatus:
erras, erras] erras, erras L: erras M

nam] nam C: non U

nam exultantem te] nam exultantem te L: nam et uidente te A2: nam et uidente me H: nam et uiolentem Buecheler

praefidentem] praefidentem VOVU: praesidentem OPR: praefidente M

repriment] repriment C(OV): reprimunt O1

ualidae] ualidae MR: ualide VOPUHL: ualid(a)e A2

legum habenae] legum habenae M, om. VV (suppl. Vm)O (suppl. Oal) P1 (suppl. P2) R ‘legum habenae’ non est in veteri RV

atque] atque C: et Iul. Vict.

imperi] imperi MA2ac: -ii LApc

insistent] insistent codd.: sistent Kiessling﻿

The reading of L, the best manuscript of Cicero's De Oratore, was exultantem: Bücheler (apud Ribbeck2-3) conjectured uiolentem, thinking that this is the reading that laid behind the corrupt reading uidente me. The purpose of Kiessling’s conjecture of sistent for insistent (reported in Ribbeck2) seems to be to remove a perceived awkwardness in the use of insisto with iugo (i.e. sistent iugo = ‘stop you with a yoke’), but Kröner (apud TLL VII.1.1923.10-11, 45-6), understanding insisto in our passage as meaning ‘consistere in aliquo loco’, defends the manuscript reading by adducing Caes. Gall. 4.33.3 per temonem percurrere et in iugo insistere (where insistere iugo literally means ‘standing on the yoke’.

The critical apparatus supplied above is essentially that of Kumaniecki (19952, 329). It also contains additions made by Leeman–Pinkster V.xv. For a full list of manuscripts and their respective sigla, see Kumaniecki 19952, v-xxiv and xxxviii-xl, and, more succinctly, Winterbottom–Rouse–Reeve apud Reynolds 19862, 103-109.
doc.commentary:
erras, erras: Gaertner (2015, 439 n. 57), comparing the use of erras to suggest an introduction into a new way of life (‘Einleitung einer Lebensweisheit’) at Pl. Mos. 188-90, Trin. 666, Ter. Ad. 65-7, detects a possible comic undertone in this fragment, although as he himself suggests, the word may also be at home in tragedy: cf. Sen. Thy. 306-7 erras: malorum sensus accrescit die. | leue est miserias ferre, perferre est graue, ‘You are wrong. The sense of hardship accumulates day by day. To bear wretchedness is a light thing; to keep bearing it is heavy’ (tr. Fitch).

exultantem: or exsu-: the primary meaning is ‘to spring up, leap about, dance’ (OLD s.v. 1), and in an extended sense can mean to ‘behave in an unrestrained manner’ (OLD s.v. 2). It is glossed by Nonius as gloriari cum iactantia (300.31 M = 467.38 L), which seems to correspond to the sense here.

legum habenae: conveys the image of the law as a bridle restraining a wild or excited horse (for a similarly bold metaphor, cf. Cic. Cluent. 150 laqueos legum). The image is reminiscent of Aegisthus’ threat to subdue the chorus leader at Aesch. Ag. 1639-40: see further ‘Fragment reconstruction’.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck (Ribbeck2-3; 1875, 462) attributes these lines to Accius’ Clytemnestra, comparing Aesch. Ag. 1639-40 (Aegisthus to the chorus: τὸν δὲ μὴ πειθάνορα | ζεύξω βαρείαις, οὔ τι μὴ σειραφόρον | κριθῶντα πῶλον ‘Anyone who will not obey his master I will yoke with heavy straps—he certainly won’t be a young trace-horse high on barley’, tr. Sommerstein) as well as (cf. Ribbeck3, 293) Sen. Ag. 964-5 (Clytemnestra to Electra, indomita posthac virginis verba impiae | regina frangam ‘These unbridled words from an undutiful virgin – I shall break them later as queen’, tr. Fitch). Warmington (II.605) suggests that these could be the words of Aegisthus to Electra in Clytemnestra.

There seems to be no reason why this fragment could not belong to Accius’ Aegisthus: it could have for example belonged to the same scene as Accius Aegisthus F 6 TRF (neque fera hominum pectora | fragescunt, donec uim persensere imperi), with which it seemingly shares the common themes of tyranny and punishment.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
This fragment is preserved in Cicero’s De Oratore, a work which contains a large amount of references to or quotations of poetry (eighty-four in total, by Čulík-Baird’s reckoning, p. 238-40). It is cited at De Or. 3.166, a passage which falls within the long technical discussion by L. Licinius Crassus (RE Licinius 55) of ornatus with a focus on individual words (De or. 3.148-70: cf. Leeman–Pinkster V.176-231). More specifically, our fragment is cited in a section devoted to metaphor (translatio) and related devices (De or. 3.155-69), in order to illustrate an example of a metaphor developed over several continuous words whereby one says one thing and means another, a device which in Greek is known as ἀλληγορία ‘allegory’. The full two lines are preserved in Cicero, and the words ‘imperi insistent iugo’ are quoted by the fourth-century CE writer on rhetoric Iulius Victor (Ars rhetorica p. 84.2 Giomini–Celentano = 432.18 Halm).

In Cicero's De Oratore, this fragment is transmitted alongside another, Adesp. F 23 TRF (neque me patiar | iterum ad unum scopulum ut olim classem Achiuom offenderem), which Ribbeck in his first edition (p. 284) compares with two fragments of Pacuvius’ Chryses 58 (adiutamini et defendite) and 57 (est ibi sub eo saxo penitus strata harena ingens specus) TRF = F 68 and 66 Schierl = v. 98-99, F XI and XII Ribbeck1) and considers close to some lines of comedy, e.g. Pl. Most. 677, Merc. 197, Ter. Phorm. 689.

doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarii (tr7).

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: proin demet abs te regimen Argos, dum est potestas consili.

doc.translation: And so Argos will take the command away from you, while it has the power to decide.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. XVI, v. 29 Ribbeck3:

29: Proin demet abs te regimen Argos, dum est potestas consili.

Warmington:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. 51 Warmington:

51: Proin demet abs te regimen Argos dum est potestas consili.

And so Argos, while it has power to fulfil the intent, will take from you the government.
doc.apparatus:
1: proin demet abs te regimen Argos ] proin demet abs te regimen Argos Lachmann apud Jahn 1845, 95: proinde et abs te codd. plerique: proinde abs te R (= Rossianus Latinus 1050, s. XV): proin det ab se regimen Argi Bothe I.285

1: consili ] consili Lachmann apud Jahn 1845, 95: consulendi C2: consolendi B

Lachmann, seeing that the sentence needed a main verb, conjectured the corrupt paradosis proinde et to proin demet, demet being the third-person present subjunctive of the verb demo (‘to remove, take away’). Reading Bothe’s conjecture proin det ab se regimen Argi, the sense would be ‘and so may he give away from himself, i.e. abdicate, the rule of Argos, while he has the faculty to make that decision’.
doc.commentary: abs: abs, or aps or af  (OLD s.v. ab) is an orthographical variant of ab, found for example at Plaut. Mil. 974 aps te (‘(away) from you’).
doc.reconstruction: La Penna (1972, 363-4) suggests that this fragment might belong to Accius’ Atreus: Thyestes could be predicting that the people of Argos will rise up and rebel against Atreus’ tyranny (possibly in a canticum, accompanied with music?). Ribbeck1, 264 suggested that the fragment perhaps belongs to Ennius’ Telephus (the words of Agamemnon to Menelaus during an argument between the two brothers).
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic octonarius (see ‘Citation context’). With Lachmann’s conjecture, the first foot is a trochee: proin scans in synizesis as a single syllable, and the first syllable of demet is heavy.

context.author: Pseudo-Censorinus
context.text:
Pseudo-Censorinus Fragmentum de metris (Anonymi cuiusdam epistola, fragmentum Censorini quod aiunt) F 14.6.20-14.17.3 Sallmann = VI.613.9-613.13 GLK:

octonarius iambus ‘proin demet abs te regimen Argos, dum est potestas consili’ (= Adesp. F 120 TRF)

The fragment is quoted as an example of the iambic octonarius in an anonymous treatise on metres (De metris), sometimes referred to as Fragmentum Censorini, preserved in the manuscripts of the third-century CE grammarian Censorinus’ work ‘On Birthdays’ (De die natali).

lines.text: [THYESTES]: <18> Fregisti fidem.
[ATREUS]: Neque dedi neque do infideli cuiquam.  .  .  .
x – x – x – x – x concoquit
partem uapore flammae ; tribuit in foco
ueribus lacerta. – x – x – u x
Sed sonitus auris meas pedum pulsu increpat.
en impero Argis, sceptra mihi liquit Pelops,
qua ponto ab Helles atque ab Ionio mari
urgetur Isthmus

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: [THYESTES]: ipsus hortatur me frater, ut meos malis miser
manderem natos
[ATREUS]: iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum advenit;
iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat.
maior mihi moles, maius miscendum est malum,
qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam.
qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum
quod re in summa summum esse arbitror
periclum matres coinquinari regias,
contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus
addo huc quod mihi portento caelestum pater
prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei,
agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma,
quem clam Thyestes clepere ausum esse e regia;
qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi!

doc.translation:
(I)

My brother himself incites me, in my misery, to chew my own sons with my jaws.

(II)

Once again Thyestes comes to lay his hand on Atreus; once again he approaches me and disturbs my rest. A greater undertaking, a greater evil must be stirred up, by means of which I may crush and crunch his cruel heart.

Who was not satisfied with enticing my wife to commit adultery.

What I consider to be the greatest danger in affairs of state, is for royal mothers to be defiled, for the line of descent to be contaminated, for family to be mixed together.

I add to this that the father sent as a portent a prodigy from the sky, a support for my rule, a lamb shining amongst the flock with a golden fleece, which Thyestes in stealth dared to steal from the palace: and in this matter he enlisted my wife as his accomplice!

[ATREUS]:

… Again Thyestes comes

At Atreus to grabble, now again

Approaches me to rouse me from my calm.

More moil for me! A bigger bane to brew,

That I may crush and crunch his grievous soul!

Fragment 169-77 (5/17):

169: qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum;

[...]

172: contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus.

[...]

177: qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi.

[ATREUS]:

Who was not satisfied when he allured

My wife into debauchery; [...]

… a thing I hold to be

In matters of high state the height of danger –

When mothers of the royal house are polluted,

Their stock defiled, their lineage confused.

[...]

Thereto withal the lord of heaven by portent

Sent me a prodigy, for my realm a stay, –

A ram of golden fleece among my sheep

Shone brilliant; him Thyestes durst by stealth

Purloin from out the palace; and in this deed

He took my wedded queen for his accomplice.

[THYESTES]:

To chew my children with my jaws – poor me!

Will some one pay heed to this? Bind ye him!
