Derechef, Thyeste s’approche d’Atrée pour l’attaquer ; derechef, voilà qu’il m’assaille et m’arrache à ma quiétude. Plus massif est le montage, plus massif est le mal qu’il me faut machiner pour écraser et étouffer la cruauté de son cœur.

Fragment 3 (3/21), 33:

33: qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum

qui ne s’est pas contenté de fourvoyer mon épouse dans le stupre

Fragment 4 (4/21), 34-36:

34: x – x Quod re in summa summum esse arbitror

35: periclum matres coinquinari regias,

36: contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus.

Ce qui, de mon point de vue, est au rang suprême le suprême péril, c’est que les mères des rois soient souillées, la souche polluée, la race abâtardie.

Fragment 5 (5/21), 37-41:

37: Addo huc quod mihi portento caelestum pater

38: prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei,

39: agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma ; 

40: quem clam Thyestes clepere ausum esse e regia !

41: qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi !

J’ajoute à cela la merveille que, dans un miracle, le père des dieux m’a envoyée, pour asseoir mon règne, un agneau resplendissant, au milieu de mes troupeaux, par sa toison d’or. Et dire que Thyeste a osé le subtiliser subrepticement dans mon palais ! Et, dans l’affaire, il s’acquit la complicité de mon épouse !

Fragment 16 (16/21), 58-59:

58: Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser

59: manderem natos ! x – x – x – x – u x

C’est lui, mon frère, qui m’invite, dans mon malheur, à dévorer à pleines dents mes propres fils !
doc.apparatus:
104.1: ipsus ] ipsus ‘Lag 69 Rom’ [De orat.]: impius Lambinus 1583, 227: ipsius M [De orat.]: ipse L [De orat.]: ipsum X [Tusc.]

104.2: manderem ] manderem codd.: mandem Lambinus 1583, 227

104.3: Atreum ] Atreum ML [De orat.]: mecum Lambinus

104.3: adtractatum ] adtractatum AE: altercatum Lambinus: attrectatum Ellendt

104.3 advenit ] advenit MVmP: venit VvOPa1’R om. U

104.5 miscendum est ] miscendum est EL [De orat.]: miscendumst H [De orat.]: miscendum X (= GKRV) [Tusc.]

104.6 qui ] qui M [De orat.]: quin L [De orat.]

104.6: comprimam ] comprimam codd.: conteram Welcker?

104.8 quod re ] quod re codd.: <propterea> quod re Brakman 1935, 58

104.9 periclum ] periclum A ante correctionem: periculum A post correctionem, B

104.9 coinquinari ] coinquinari uel quoinquinari AB: conquinari Ribbeck1, 137

104.10 admisceri ] admisceri codd.: ac misceri Ribbeck1, 137

104.11 addo ] addo codd.: adde Ribbeck1, 137

104.14: quem clam ] quem clam A2: quondam Ald.: em clam Ribbeck 1871, liii-liv: quem dan Λ1V: quendam HB2: quam dan B1

104.14 ausum esse ] ausum esse AB: esse ausum ‘Orelliana’ (apud Ribbeck2, 163)

104.15 qua ] qua A: a qua B

104.1-2: apparatus based on Kumaniecki 19952 on Cic. De orat. 3.217-219, Giusta 1984 on Cic. Tusc. 4.77.

104.3-4: apparatus based on Kumaniecki 19952 on Cic. De orat. 3.217-219.

104.5-6: apparatus based on Kumaniecki 19952 on Cic. De orat. 3.217-219, Giusta 1984 on Cic. Tusc. 4.77, and Plasberg–Ax 19332 on Cic. Nat. D. 3.68-9.

104.7-15: apparatus based on Plasberg–Ax 19332 on Cic. Nat. D. 3.68-9.

104.1 ipsus: this reading is attested in a manuscript known as ‘Lagomarsinianus 69’: the so-called codices Lagomarsiniani owe their name to the Jesuit scholar Girolamo Lagomarsini (1698-1773) who collated an important number of manuscripts of Cicero. These collations were consulted by several scholars in the early nineteenth century but were subsequently lost and quoted by later scholars at second hand, before being rediscovered in the Vatican Library by Laurand in 1927 (cf. Laurand, Cicéron, Volume complémentaire, 1934, p. 230-42). They now bear the shelfmark Vat. Lat. 11616-11696 (the collations of Cicero are numbers 11649-11696), and the key which Lagomarsini used to designate these manuscripts (e.g. Lag. 36 = Florence, Laur. 50, 34) was found by Laurand in a manuscript housed in the Biblioteca Marucelliana in Florence (shelfmark B. III. 44). Kumaniecki explains (praef. p. xxii-xxiii) that he has consulted some, but not all of these collations himself: Lag. 69 is among those he has not consulted himself, and so here relies on the critical apparatus of Ellendt (1840, cf. I.572), who had access to Lagomarsini’s collations (praef. I.viii). The reading ipsus had already been printed in the editio princeps of De oratore by C. Sweynheym and A. Pannartz at Rome in 1469 (f. 107v). In Giusta’s critical apparatus of Cic. Tusc. 4.77 (1984, 260), the reading is attributed to Baiter (Orelli–Baiter 18452 [1826], M. Tullius Ciceronis opera quae supersunt omnia… Volumen primum…, Zürich, praef. vi), who himself draws on the apparatus of Ellendt. Giusta attributes the reading impius to Erasmus. The readings of the MSS of De oratore are ipsius and ipse, that of the Tusculan Disputations is ipsum.

104.2 manderem: Lambinus (1583, 227) conjectures mandem for manderem, presumably on the grounds that, given that the verb in the main clause is in the present indicative (hortatur), a present subjunctive (mandem) is felt to be more appropriate than an imperfect subjunctive (manderem).

104.3 Atreum: self-naming is a feature which is developed in Senecan tragedy.

104.3 adtractatum: the reading of manuscripts A and E of De oratore, both descended from the lost but excellent manuscript M. Whether one opts for adtractatum or for Ellendt’s attrectatum seems essentially to be a question of orthography (TLL II.1161.56-8). Lambinus’ altercatum (cf. OLD s.v. altercor ‘to dispute, esp. angrily, argue, wrangle’) is certainly possible: the active form alterco is attested in Republican writers (Ter. An. 653 cum patre altercasti dudum ‘you were arguing with your father all this time’; Pacuvius Iliona F 141 TRF, mecum altercas? tace! ‘are you arguing with me? Be silent!’). But there seems here to be no reason not to accept the paradosis, which is also appealing for other reasons (see ‘Fragment commentary’).

104.3 advenit: the two best manuscripts of De oratore, M and L, which are lost but whose readings can be reconstructed by collations in later manuscripts (see Kumaniecki’s preface), are divided: M had aduenit and L had uenit. Both variants are metrically possible, though aduenit seems to be preferable as it is arguably easier to account for the disappearance of the prefix ad- than for its insertion.

104.5 miscendum est: whether one reads miscendum est (so TRF, Dangel) or miscendumst (with contraction of esse, so Ribbeck, Warmington) ultimately makes little difference. The L manuscript of De oratore, as well as manuscript E which is descended from the best representative of the other branch of the tradition M, both read miscendum est; manuscript H, which like E is also descended from M, reads miscendumst. It follows that the reading of the archetype was miscendum est (though Kumaniecki prints miscendumst). The best manuscripts of the Tusculan Disputations read miscendum. Plasberg–Ax in their edition of De natura deorum (3.68) print miscendumst without mentioning any variants in their critical apparatus.

104.6 qui: on qui, see ‘Fragment commentary’. With the reading of the L manuscript of De oratore, quin, the meaning of the word could be ‘indeed’ (OLD s.v. quin A2). Alternatively, the line could be formulated as a question introduced by quin: quin illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam?, i.e. ‘why should I not crush and crunch his grievous soul?’ For quin as interrogative pronoun followed by a verb in the subjunctive, see OLD s.v. A1 and e.g. Pl. Mil. 426 quin ego hoc rogem quod nesciam? ‘Why shouldn’t I ask what I don’t know?’ In the two other manuscript traditions that preserve this fragment, Tusculan Disputations and De natura deorum, the reading qui seems unanimous.

104.6 comprimam: Welcker (1839-1841, I.363), in his discussion of this fragment, prints conteram instead of comprimam. It is unclear whether this is actually meant as a conjectural emendation.

104.8-10: Davies (1733, 363) suggested that these lines should be rewritten as follows:

quod in re summa summum esse periclum arbitror

matres coinquinari regum; regiam

contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus.

These suggestions have won no approval and so I do not discuss them.

104.8 quod re: Brakman (1935, 58) proposes adding propterea (‘therefore’) before these words so as to form a complete iambic senarius, suggesting that the word might easily have been omitted by an excerptor. 

104.9 periclum: the reading of the A manuscript (= Leiden Universiteitsbibliotheek, VLF 84, f. 33v) of De natura deorum before correction. The unmetrical reading periculum is by a later hand. The B manuscript (= Leiden Universiteitsbibliotheek, VLF 86, f. 54r) also reads periculum. Periclum is explained by Reineke (apud TLL X.1.1457.63-4) as an archaic form of periculum: the latter form is found in the manuscripts at Plaut. Persa 524, 715 and Poen. 878 where it is also unmetrical.

104.9 coinquinari: this is the spelling found in manuscripts A and B of De natura deorum (A has the orthographical variant quoi-). On the basis of a passage of fourth-century CE grammarian Marius Victorinus’ Ars grammatica (VI.22.7 GLK, conquinat, non coinquinat), Ribbeck prints conquinat. In any case, the word must be quadrisyllabic on metrical grounds, so -oi- is to be pronounced in synizesis. 

104.10 admisceri: Ribbeck (in all three editions) prints ac misceri, which is apparently his own conjecture.

104.11 addo: the second-person imperative form adde is a conjecture proposed apparently by Ribbeck in his first edition (p. 137).

104.14 quem clam: Plasberg–Ax, in their edition (p. 148), report that quem clam is the reading of ‘A2’: they explain in the preface (p. vi) that they note with the siglum A2 a correction by one of two hands whose notations are scarcely visible. But having inspected the manuscript (f. 33v) it seems to me that quem clam is not a correction, but the text of manuscript A itself, before correction. A variant reading is quendam, which is corrected in the 1523 Aldine edition to quondam (the reading printed by Scriverius, Bothe, Ribbeck in his first and second editions, among others). While quondam produces good sense (i.e. Thyestes stole the golden lamb ‘long ago’), quem clam is arguably the more difficult reading, and a scribe might easily have confused -cl- with -d- and thus written quondam instead of quem clam. Ribbeck (‘Corollarium’, 1871, liii-liv) conjectured em clam, the interjection em being common in Republican comedy, arguing that the letters qu- were mistakenly introduced by a scribe owing to qua at the beginning of the following line. Fulvio Orsini (i.e. Ursinus, 1581, 51) reports that he saw the reading quem de ara Thyestem… (i.e. ‘which [sc. the lamb] Thyestes [stole] from the altar…’) in a very old manuscript (‘in vetustissimo codice’).

104.14 ausum esse: the reading of A and B, two of the Leiden manuscripts of De natura deorum. [...] The reading esse ausum is printed by Ribbeck in his first edition, and ausum esse is attributed to ‘Erl. [i.e. Erlangensis 847, s. XV], Nonius’, despite this in fact being the reading of the earlier manuscripts, A and B, and that this line is not cited by Nonius. Ribbeck corrects his error in his second edition, printing ausum esse and attributing the reading esse ausum to ‘Orelliana’. It thus seems to be a conjecture introduced into the text by Orelli in his 1828 edition of De natura deorum (IV.2.113): in the 1861 second edition revised by Baiter, the printed text reads ausum esse, and esse ausum is attributed in the critical apparatus to ‘O’, that is, ‘ed. Orelliana’ (IV.469).

104.5 qua: the B manuscript of De natura deorum has a qua but the a cannot stand on metrical grounds.
doc.commentary:
104.1 ipsus: equivalent to ipse. Ipsus is found often in Early Latin from Livius Andronicus onwards (Aegisthus F 3 ipsus se in terram saucius fligit cadens, ‘he dashed himself to the ground, wounded and falling’, transl. Maltby–Slater, FRL VI.45). Dangel (1995, 281) suggests that ipsus here is synonymous with dominus, but, in such cases, ipsus/ipse is used as a noun, whereas in this case it seems to be used as an adjective qualifying frater: cf. TLL VII.2.344.13-33, pointing out in instances where ipsus = dominus, the speaker is normally a slave, e.g. Pl. Aul. 356 (at iam afferetur, si a foro ipsus redierit, ‘But some [alcohol] will be brought in a moment when master himself returns from the market’, transl. De Melo 2011, I.297, the slave Strobilus speaking about his master Megadorus); and OLD s.v. 12 ‘[as noun] the master, esp. the master of the house, “himself”’ and also s.v. ipsimus, -i, m. ‘the master of a household’.

104.1-2 meos malis miser | manderem: Lana (1959, 309 n. 3) suggests that the m alliteration mimics the act of chewing. Lambinus (1583, 227) compared Lucr. 2.638 ne Saturnus eum malis mandaret adeptus (‘that Saturn might not catch him [sc. Jupiter] and cast him into his jaws’, transl. Rouse–Smith 19922, 145).

104.2 manderem: the verb mandere (OLD s.v. mando2 ‘to crush with the teeth, chew, bite’) is synonymous with, though more expressive than, edo (Ernout 1954, 155 n. 1). Livius Andronicus uses the verb at Odyssia F 4 FRL (cum socios nostros mandisset impius Cyclops, ‘when the wicked Cyclops had devoured our comrades’, transl. Maltby–Slater, FRL VI.123), intensifying Homer’s ἤσθιε, and it also occurs in Ennius’ Annales (125 Skutsch uolturus in †spineto† miserum mandebat homonem, ‘a vulture devoured the wretched man [hanging?]’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL I.171).

104.3-4: iterum…iterum: the repetition emphatically evokes the humiliation suffered by Atreus at the hands of Thyestes, who committed adultery with Atreus’ wife Aerope (cf. F 104.7 TRF qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum) and exploited her to gain access to the golden ram, symbol of Atreus’ rule (F 104.11-15: Lana 1959, 300). Lana (1959, 316-17) considers the Senecan Atreus’ exhortative self-address to his animus at Sen. Thy. 191-200 (age, anime,...) to be an amplification of these two Accian lines. It is unclear whether Thyestes is actually returning  with a plan to exact vengeance against his brother (as Jocelyn (1969, 414 with n. 1) suggests), or whether rather he is returning as a suppliant with the hope of being given hospitality by Atreus, like in Aegisthus’ account of the story in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon (1587-90, a passage cited as a model of this fragment by Franchella 1968, 224). It should be noted that it cannot be ruled out that both Accius’ Atreus and Aeschylus’ Aegisthus may be misrepresenting or twisting the facts of the matter to suit each of their own interests and motivations, which are completely opposed. Indeed, it seems unlikely that Atreus’ words here are to be taken entirely at face value (cf. La Penna 1972, 359-60). Indeed, as Di Benedetto Zimbone suggests (1973, 267-268), if Atreus really did fear that he was at risk of being supplanted as king by Thyestes, then he would not have let his brother into the palace in the first place, and furthermore he would likely have made plans to kill not only his brother’s children, but his brother himself, too. Hartung (1843-1844, I.171), who believes that Accius’ play is a Latin version of Euripides’ Cressae, seems to suggest that Accius’ use of iterum serves to express, in a manner which scholars today might describe as self-conscious, the fact that an account of the same story had already been told in Euripides’ play. He interprets the word quondam (a variant reading of quem clam at F 104.14 TRF) in a similar fashion.

104.3 adtractatum: the verb adtractare (or adtrectare) often seems to carry connotations of pollution or contamination, or sexual connotations (TLL II.1161.1151-8: cf. e.g. Pl. Rud. 420 (sexual), Cic. Cael. 20 (sexual), Har. resp. 26 (religious contamination)). Lana (1959, 300) suggests that the verb here carries a double meaning, both ‘to caress shamelessly’ (‘carezzare impudicamente’ Lana; ‘qui a été caressé avec impudicité’ Dangel 277) and ‘to extend one’s hands to seize something’ (‘allungare le mani per prendere qualcosa’ Lana; ‘ce sur quoi on a porté la main pour s’en emparer’ Dangel 277), and thus connotes both of the outrages committed by Thyestes against Atreus, that is, sleeping with his wife and coveting his throne. The supine form in the accusative case expresses purpose (see Woodcock §152: ‘the accusative form [sc. of the supine] is used only as an Accusative of the Goal of Motion, without preposition [...]. It therefore expresses end, aim, or purpose, and is found only after verbs of motion or verbs implying motion’).

104.5-6 maior mihi moles, maius miscendum est malum…: having just recalled the wrongs committed by Thyestes against him in F 104.2-3, Atreus now plots his revenge with particular determination and fervour, which is expressed in part by the alliteration in m (Lana 1959, 300). The comparative adjectives maior and maius indicate that Atreus wants his revenge to outdo the wrongs committed by Thyestes, and also to outstrip the past punishments he himself inflicted on Thyestes, that is, exile and poverty. Marchesi (1908, 86) identifies an echo of these lines at Sen. Thy. 199-200 (noui ego ingenium uiri | indocile: flecti non potest – frangi potest, ‘I know the man’s intractable nature: he cannot be bent, but he can be broken’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.247).

104.5 miscendum: D’Antò (1980, 284) notes that the sense of misceri here is close to that of moliri, cf. Cic. Cat. 4.6 ego magnum in re publica versari furorem et nova quaedam misceri et concitari mala iam pridem videbam, ‘It had long been observed by me that a dangerous madness was abroad and that evils as yet unknown were seething and welling up in the Republic’ (transl. Macdonald 1977, 141). OLD s.v. 13 explains it as meaning ‘to produce, stir up (a confused activity, turbulent emotion, etc.)’.

104.6 qui: an old ablative form probably to be taken as a final conjunction (Pinkster §16.52 ‘Purpose clauses with quī, adjuncts’, so Warmington’s ‘that I may crush’) rather than dependent on malum: see Fantham apud Leeman–Pinkster 1981-2008, V.361.

104.6 quietum suscitat: Lana (1959, 317) compares Sen. Thy. 201-2 proinde antequam se firmat aut uires parat, | petatur ultro, ne quiescentem petat, ‘So, before he strengthens himself or marshals his powers, he must be attacked first, lest he attack me at rest’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.247). Seneca interestingly develops the idea, already found in Accius, of Atreus’ sense of weariness and exhaustion at having to face his brother yet again, by making Accius think it imperative that he should act: if he does not rouse himself to action, then Thyestes will be the one to take advantage of his passivity and act first. It seems as if, by some kind of bizarre logic, Atreus thinks that Thyestes will gain strength from his, Atreus’, state of passivity (quietus).

104.6 qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam: what exactly is the meaning of the word acerbum? The OLD (s.v. acerbus 3a) explains it as: ‘Bitterly hostile, pitiless, cruel, harsh: (of persons, etc.)’. Interestingly, the word also occurs in Aulus Gellius’ account of Accius’ response to Pacuvius’ criticism of his ‘immature’ tragic style (NA 13.2.4-6, ‘ita est’ inquit Accius’, uti dicis; neque id me sane paenitet; meliora enim fore spero quae deinceps scribam. Nam quod in pomis est, itidem’ inquit ‘esse aiunt in ingeniis; quae dura et acerba nascuntur, post fiunt mitia et iucunda; sed quae gignuntur statim uieta et mollia atque in principio sunt uuida, non matura mox fiunt, sed putria. Relinquendum igitur uisum est in ingenio, quod dies atque aetas mitificet’, ‘“What you say is true,” replied Accius, “and I do not greatly regret it; for it gives me hope that what I write hereafter will be better. For they say it is with the mind as it is with fruits; those which are at first harsh and bitter, later become mild and sweet; but those which at once grow mellow and soft, and are juicy in the beginning, presently become, not ripe, but decayed. Accordingly, it has seemed to me that something should be left in the products of the intellect for time and age to mellow.”’, transl. Rolfe 1946-19522, II.419): is Gellius’ Accius using his own tragic language metaphorically to describe his own style?

104.7 inlexe: = illexisse, the contracted form of the perfect infinitive of the verb illicio (or inlicio): OLD s.v. 2 ‘to lure, entice (usu. into a course of action to which one is normally or naturally disinclined’. Cf. Pl. Merc. 53 amorem multos illexe [Camerarius: illexisse Pylades: inlexit B: illexit rel.] in dispendium, ‘Love had enticed many to waste’ (transl. de Melo 2011, III.21). Enk (II.21, ad Pl. Merc. 53) cites a list of parallels of similar syncopated forms: aduexe, Pl. Merc. 333; adduxe, Rud. 1047; detraxe, Trin. 743; dixe, Poen. 961; despexe, Mil. 552; circumspexe, Varro apud Nonius p. 106.16 M = 151.15 L; produxe, Ter. Ad. 561; surrexe, Hor. Sat. 1.9.73; duxe, Varro, Sat. Men 329 Bücheler–Astbury. Illicio seems to be a somewhat archaic verb, found, outside Republican drama, mainly in prose. Some late Republican usages include Cic. Inv. 2.157, Varro, Rust. 1.17.6, Sall. Catil. 16.1, Iug. 97.3, Liv. 39.8.5; Early Imperial: Vell. Pat. 2.13.2, Columella, Rust. 2.1.2, Tac. Hist. 1.53, 4.57). An exception is possibly Lucr. 4.1145, a humanist conjecture (which can possibly be attributed to Michael Marullus, cf. Deufert 2019, xix) for the manuscripts’ inligniaris.

104.7 inlexe in stuprum: the Latin word stuprum can denote a wide range of disgraceful acts, most often of a sexual nature (cf. OLD s.v. 2 ‘Illicit sexual intercourse in any form (whether forced or not) or an instance of it’), but here seems specifically to refer to adultery (so Flores 1978, 49, though strictly speaking stuprum was distinct from adulterium: Papinian De Adulteriis I and Modestinus Liber Regularum I, quoted in Fantham 1991, 275). On the adultery of Aerope, Dangel (1995, 277) comments suggests that it would have seemed particularly scandalous to a Roman audience because ‘ceux-ci [sc. les Romains] punissaient de mort la coupable’. According to Plutarch, Romulus promulgated a law according to which a man could divorce (ἐκβάλλειν, LSJ s.v. A4) his wife if he found that she had committed adultery (cf. Plutarch, Vit. Rom. 22, which reads: Ἔθηκε δὲ καὶ νόμους τινάς, ὧν σφοδρὸς μέν ἐστιν ὁ γυναικὶ μὴ διδοὺς ἀπολείπειν ἄνδρα, γυναῖκα δὲ διδοὺς ἐκβάλλειν ἐπὶ φαρμακείᾳ καὶ τέκνων ὑποβολῇ καὶ μοιχευθεῖσαν, ‘He [sc. Romulus] also enacted certain laws, and among them one of severity, which forbids a wife to leave her husband, but permits a husband to put away his wife for using poisons, for substituting children, and for adultery’, transl. Perrin I.163). A fragment from a speech entitled On Dowry (De dote) by Cato the Elder (234-149 BCE), however, suggests that in the mid-Republic it was essentially legal for a man to kill his wife if she committed adultery (F 222 ORF4 = Gell. NA 10.23.5, ‘in adulterio uxorem tuam si prehendisses, sine iudicio inpune necares; illa te, si adulterares siue tu adulterarere, digito non auderet contingere, neque ius est’, ‘“If you should take your wife in adultery, you may with impunity put her to death without a trial; but if you should commit adultery or indecency, she must not presume to lay a finger on you, nor does the law allow it”’, transl. Rolfe 1946-19522, II.279). The lex Iulia de adulteriis, promulgated by Augustus in 18/17 BCE, seems to have abolished a husband’s right to kill his adulterous wife (RS 60, II.781-6). Baldarelli (2004, 215, 219), however, interprets the expression differently: she identifies a comic undertone in illexisse in stuprum which may have made an audience think of Thyestes and Aerope behaving with obscene indecency, like characters from comedy might do. She points out that the construction of illicio with in followed by accusative is otherwise attested only in comedy (Pl. Merc. 53, Mil. 1435, Truc. 298, Ter. An. 911). Furthermore, she suggests that Lucilius v. 633 Krenkel coniugem infidamque, pathicam familiam, impuram domum may allude, in satirical vein, to this line of Accius.

104.8 re in summa summum… | periclum: cf. Atreus’ first words in Seneca’s play (Sen. Thy. 176-8): ignaue, iners, eneruis et (quod maximum | probrum tyranno rebus in summis reor) | inulte…, ‘Idle, inert, impotent, and (what I count the greatest reproach for a tyrant in crises) unavenged’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.245). Both passages contain the expression res summa (OLD s.v. summus 6, ‘(of a moment, etc.) Marking a critical point in one’s fortunes, crucial; [...] a critical situation’), which elsewhere in Republican Latin seems often to refer to the state (e.g. Accius Aeneadae uel/aut Decius F 2 TRF, quibus rem summam et patriam nostram quondam adauctavit pater; Enn. Ann. 97 Skutsch = F 48 FRL, astu non vi sum summam servare decet rem, ‘it is right that he guide important affairs of state by cleverness, not force’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL I.157). While Seneca may be drawing on the Accian Atreus’ language (cf. Tarrant 1985, 117), notice how the focus is in each case slightly different: whereas Accius’ Atreus identifies the unfaithfulness of a king’s spouse, and thus the possibility of illegitimate pretendants to the throne, as the greatest threat to the permanency of his power, Seneca’s version of the character is concerned with the very fact that he is unavenged (cf. Tarrant 1985, 117: ‘Seneca’s character thinks above all not of the practical dangers of confused descent [...], but of the mere fact of being unavenged’). While the legitimacy of his sons is something that concerns the Senecan Atreus (cf. esp. 1098-9 liberos nasci mihi | nunc credo, castis nunc fidem reddi toris, ‘Now I believe that the children are mine, and that my bed is faithful and chaste once more!’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.325), he is overall less preoccupied specifically by the matter of his wife’s infidelity, something which, judging albeit solely on the basis of the surviving fragments, seems to have troubled the Accian Atreus more (cf. this fragment and F 104.7 qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum). Moreover, Seneca’s Atreus is talking to himself in an attempt to rouse himself from his passiveness and idleness and to set into motion his revenge: whom was Accius’ Atreus addressing?

104.9-10:  …matres conquinari regias, | contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus: Marchesi (1908, 86) sees an echo of these lines in the speech of the Fury in Seneca’s Thyestes (52 misce penates, odia caedes funera, ‘Bring havoc on the house gods, summon hatred, slaughter, death’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.237).

104.9 coinquinari: see Pease 1955, II.1149 ad Cic. Nat. D. 3.68: ‘the earliest occurrence of this word, here to be read as a quadrisyllable. The danger involved is one affecting the purity of the royal blood, even though in this case it is by mixture with another of the same family rather than with one of lower social rank. In either case, however, the throne might pass into the possession of a usurper (qui regnum adulterio quaereret) or his descendants’.

104.11-15: cf. Eur. Or. 996-1000 [Electra], describing the curse that has affected the house of Pelops since Pelops threw the charioteer Myrtilus into the sea: ὅθεν δόμοισι τοῖς ἐμοῖς | ἦλθ᾿ ἀρὰ πολύστονος, | λόχευμα ποιμνίοισι Μαιάδος τόκου, |  τὸ χρυσόμαλλον ἀρνὸς ὁπότ᾿ | ἐγένετο τέρας ὀλοὸν Ἀτρέος | <ἀγροῖς ἐν> ἱπποβώτα, ‘From this deed for my house came a curse laden with groaning, when, brought to birth in the flocks of the son of Maia, there came the famous lamb with fleece of gold, a portent of ruin <in the fields> of horse-pasturing Atreus’ (transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, V.523). For the lamb’s golden fleece as a divine endorsement of Atreus’ kingship, see schol. Thom. Eur. Or. 812: καὶ ἑκάτερος αὐτῶν τῆς βασιλείας ἀντεποιεῖτο, Ἀτρεὺς μὲν, ὡς πρῶτος ὢν κατὰ νόμους, Θυέστης δὲ τοῖς νόμοις ἐναντιούμενος. συνέθεντο οὖν ὡς, εἴ τις αὐτῶν δείξει τι τέρας, κύριος ἔσεσθαι τῆς ἀρχῆς, ‘and each of them laid claim to the kingship, Atreus as being first (in line) according to laws/customs, but Thyestes acting against laws/customs. So they made a compact that if any of them will display some portent, he will be empowered with the rule’, transl. Mastronarde; schol. vet. Eur. Or. 999: ὁ δὲ Ἀτρεὺς βουλόμενος δεῖζαι ὅτι αὐτοῦ ἐστιν ἡ βασιλεία, ἔφη δείζειν ὅτι χρυσόμαλλος ἀρνειὸς αὐτῷ ἐτέχθη, ‘And Atreus, wanting to show that the kingship belongs to him, said he would show that a golden-fleeced lamb had been born for him’, transl. Mastronarde; Sen. Thy. 223 specimen antiquum imperi, ‘our ancient symbol of power’, 230-1 possessor huius regnat, hunc tantae domus | fortuna sequitur, ‘The one who possesses him reigns, the fortune of this great house follows him’, transl. Fitch. (Passages adduced by Welcker 1839-1941, I.360 n. 3 on this passage of Accius).

104-11: caelestum pater: it is to Jupiter, referred to as deum regnator at Accius Clytemnestra F 146 TRF, that Atreus attributes the act of having sent down the golden lamb to symbolise his endorsement of Atreus’ rule. As Dangel (1995, 277-8) acutely observes, however, it cannot be ruled out that, elsewhere in the play, Accius lent emphasis to the role of Mercury, whose attitude towards the sons of Pelops might be described as potentially ambivalent. Indeed, according to the genealogy apparently adopted by Accius in this play (T 25 TRF), Mercury is at once the son of Maia and thus nephew of Oenomaus, Atreus’ grandfather on his mother Hippodamia’s side, and the father of Myrtilus, the charioteer killed by Atreus’ father Pelops. Cf. too Baldarelli 2004, 163.

104.11-13: …mihi portento caelestum pater | prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei, | agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma: Hartung 1843-1844, I.173 (cf. too Enk 1962, 109) compares schol. vet. Eur. Orest. 999: φιλονεικίας γὰρ οὔσης αὐτοῖς περὶ τῆς βασιλείας παρελθὼν ὁ Ἀτρεὺς ἐπηγγέλλετο δεῖξαι πρὸς τὸ βεβαιωθῆναι αὐτῷ τὴν βασιλείαν χρυσόμαλλον ἐν τοῖς ποιμνίοις αὐτοῦ ἄρνα· ἣν ὁ Θυέστης λάθρα παρὰ τῆς Ἀερόπης αἰτήσας ἔλαβεν, ‘When there was rivalry between them over the kingship, Atreus came forward and undertook to show, for the securing of the kingship for himself, a golden-fleeced lamb in his flocks. This (lamb) Thyestes secretly asked for and received from Aerope’ (transl. Mastronarde). Compare esp. πρὸς τὸ βεβαιωθῆναι αὐτῷ τὴν βασιλείαν with Accius’ regni stabilimen mei: Hartung suggests that the Euripidean scholia are drawing on the text of Euripides’ lost Cretan Women (‘[Scholiasta] Euripides verba accuratissime reddidisse videtur’) and suggests that Accius is alluding to that lost Euripidean play.

104.11-12: portento…prodigium: for a different perspective on the lamb, see that of Electra in Euripides’ Orestes, who sees it as a τέρας ὀλοόν ‘portent of ruin’. Might Accius’ portento…prodigium enclose wordplay? Some ancient etymologists believed both words to derive in part from the adverb porro ‘straight on, onward, forward, ahead’ (Maltby, LALE s.vv.). On portentum, see DS Aen. 7.256 portendi porro tendi, cf. Festus p. 245.16-17 Müller = 284.6-7 L portenta, quae quid porro tendatur [Qu. XII.17: tendeatur F], dicent. On prodigium, see Varro apud DS Aen. 3.366 = F 440 Funaioli: Varro sane haec ita definit: … prodigium, quod porro dicit, and Nonius p. 44.17 M = 64-17 L prodigia dicta sunt porro adigenda, with a quotation of Pl. Amph. 739. Note however that Cicero derives prodigium from praedicere (Div. 1.93).

104.14 quem clam Thyestes clepere ausum esse e regia: for Thyestes’ daring (ausum) in committing the outrageous deed of stealing the golden ram and for committing adultery with his brother’s wife, cf. Sen. Thy. 234-5 hunc facinus ingens ausus assumpta in scelus | consorte nostri perfidus thalami auehit, ‘Risking a flagrant crime, and taking the partner of my bed as accomplice, that betrayer carried him off’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.251).
doc.reconstruction:
104.1-2 ipsus…natos: Scriverius–Vossius (1620, 105) imagine Thyestes to be the speaker of these lines, reacting in shock and horror to the moles that Atreus has prepared and enacted (cf. F 104.5-6 maior mihi moles, maius miscendum est malum, | qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam). Ribbeck’s interpretation (1875, 455) is also along these lines: he imagines the fragment to belong to a monologue delivered as a canticum in which Thyestes expresses his pain and grief after having told Atreus that he has broken faith (F 106 TRF, fregisti fidem | :: neque dedi, neque do infideli cuiquam). Lana (1959, 309) suggests that Thyestes addresses Atreus in the third person to stress the outrage and indignity of the situation. He also argues that none of the surviving fragments thought to belong to this scene contains a lamentation by Thyestes for his dead children (cf. F 106 TRF, fregisti fidem…, and F 109 TRF, egone Argivum…). He deduces that Thyestes was depicted by Accius as being more troubled by the means with which Atreus took revenge than by the death of his children. But such an extreme interpretation is surely unwarranted: it cannot be ruled out that the play did contain a lamentation by Thyestes, but that it is not preserved among the fragments that have come down to us. Jocelyn (1969, 416) considers the possibility that this fragment might not belong to Accius’ Atreus, but rather to Ennius’ Thyestes. He does so on the grounds that the emotion of anger, which Cicero attributes to the speaker of these lines at De orat. 3.217 (see ‘Citation context’), would be more suited to Thyestes’ reminiscing about the awfulness of having eaten his children some time after the event, rather than to an immediate reaction, in which Jocelyn expects disgust would be the leading emotion. It is important to point out that Jocelyn, unlike earlier scholars such as Welcker and Ribbeck, considers the plot of Ennius’ Thyestes to take place at the court of Thesprotus quite some time after the events depicted in Accius’ Atreus.

104.3-6 Iterum…: Ribbeck (1875, 448) argues that these lines belong to a monologue by Atreus after the first choral ode which mentioned Pelops’s victory in the chariot race at Pisa. Atreus’ monologue, Ribbeck believes, was followed by a scene in which Atreus discusses his plans for revenge against Thyestes with a confidant. The general outline of the scene is seen by Ribbeck as essentially similar to Atreus’ monologue followed by his conversation with the satelles in Seneca’s Thyestes (Sen. Thy. 176-335). More specifically, Ribbeck compares this Accian fragment with Sen. Thy. 192-5:

Age, anime, fac quod nulla posteritas probet,

sed nulla taceat. aliquod audendum est nefas

atrox, cruentum, tale quod frater meus

suum esse mallet.

Come, my spirit, do what no future age will endorse, but none fail to talk about. I must dare some fierce, bloody outrage, such as my brother would have wished his own. (Transl. Fitch 20182, II.247)

And with Sen. Thy. 199-202:

noui ego ingenium uiri

indocile: flecti non potest – frangi potest.

proinde antequam se firmat aut uires parat,

petatur ultro, ne quiescentem petat.

I know the man’s intractable nature: he cannot be bent, but he can be broken. So, before he strengthens himself or marshals his powers, he must be attacked first, lest he attack me at rest. (Transl. Fitch 20182, II.247)

Ribbeck imagines the scene between Atreus and his confidant culminating with Atreus saying his famous line oderint, dum metuant (F 103.4 TRF). He identifies in Accius’ Atreus ‘das Urbild des rücksichtslosen Tyrannen’ (1875, 449). As Ribbeck further points out (1875, 448-9), Accius’ version corresponds to that found in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon (1577-1611), according to which Thyestes, having been exiled by his brother, returns to his brother’s palace of his own accord. See especially Aesch. Ag. 1587-90:

καὶ προστρόπαιος ἑστίας μολὼν πάλιν

τλήμων Θυέστης μοῖραν ηὕρετ᾿ ἀσφαλῆ,

τὸ μὴ θανὼν πατρῷον αἱμάξαι πέδον

αὐτός·

Later the unhappy Thyestes came back as a suppliant to his hearth and secured himself safety from death and from staining his ancestral soil with his own blood. (Transl. Sommerstein 2008, II.193).

Here, Aegisthus’ claim that his father acted as a suppliant (προστρόπαιος) and that Atreus tricked Thyestes by pretending to offer him hospitality (ξένια, 1590) presents Atreus’ crime as even more transgressive and outrageous. Ribbeck suggests that this version was also possibly adopted by Euripides in his Thyestes or Cretan Women. In other accounts such as those of Hyginus (Fab. 88.8) and Seneca’s Thyestes, however, Atreus invites Thyestes to return to the royal palace, and the latter, after some hesitation, accepts. In Lana’s reconstruction (1959, 299-302) this fragment belongs to the first scene of the play after the prologue (and first choral ode?): Atreus shares the stage with an attendant and recalls the past wrongs committed by Thyestes against him and plots his revenge. According to Lana, a further five fragments belong to this scene: F 107 TRF (ego incipio; conata exequar); F 103.4 (oderint, dum metuant); F 104.7 (qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum); F 104.8-10 (quod re in summa summum esse arbitror | periclum matres conquinari regias, | contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus); and F Adesp. 65 TRF (proinde ita parent se in vita, ut vinci nesciant). Lana believes (p. 301) that at this stage of the play, Atreus does not yet know what exact form his revenge will take: he suggests that the details of the plan (i.e. the idea of killing Thyestes’ children and serving their remains to him during a banquet) takes shape in his mind as the conversation with his attendant progresses. Interestingly, Lana (1959, 364) rejects the traditional view that, in Accius’ version of the myth, Thyestes returns of his own accord. He suggests rather that, like in Seneca’s Thyestes, Atreus invited Thyestes back. Lana deduces this from the attitude of Thyestes’ sons, who, based on how Thyestes speaks to them, seem to have reason to hope that their father could claim the throne. For them to have such a hope, Lana argues, presupposes that Atreus invited Thyestes back, and that this invitation led the children to believe that Atreus might hand the throne over to Thyestes. Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 268) and D’Antò (1980, 279) endorse Lana’s interpretation. D’Antò (1980, 280) makes the additional observation that, if it is believed that Thyestes returned of his own accord and not after having been invited to do so by Atreus, then the expression fregisti fidem in F 106 TRF arguably becomes more difficult to interpret. It is indeed more straightforward to imagine that, in order to convince Thyestes to return and allay any suspicions his brother might have, Atreus gave his word (fides) that he would abandon any intention to take revenge. Baldarelli (2004, 209-212) rejects Lana’s view on the grounds that the wording of F 104.3-4 TRF (iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum aduenit; |  iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat) suggests that Thyestes’ return is not expected by Atreus. She instead suggests that, in these lines, Atreus is describing how his brother Thyestes continuously haunts him in recurring dreams: this phenomenon would also account for the seemingly obsessive sense of immediacy that spurs Atreus to take revenge on his brother for offenses which took place quite some time in the past.

104.6: how far, if at all, can Aulus Gellius’ account (NA 13.2) of Accius’ recitatio of his Atreus in front of Pacuvius provide insight into the play?

104. 7 qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum: refers almost certainly to Thyestes’ affair with Atreus’ wife Aerope. Ribbeck (1875, 450) identifies Atreus as the speaker and suggests that the fragment belongs to the same scene as F 104.3-6 TRF on the basis of the similarities with the scene in Seneca’s Thyestes in which Atreus speaks with the satelles: cf. esp. Sen. Thy. 222-3 coniugem stupro abstulit | regnumque furto, ‘He [sc. Thyestes] stole my wife by adultery and my kingdom by theft’ and 234-5 hunc facinus ingens ausus assumpta in scelus | consorte nostri perfidus thalami auehit, ‘Risking a flagrant crime, and taking the partner of my bed as accomplice, that betrayer carried him off [sc. the golden ram, symbolic proof of Atreus’ right to the throne]’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.251). Lana (1959, 301) suggests that this fragment follows the famous oderint dum metuant (F 103.4 TRF) and belongs to a list by Atreus of the wrongs Thyestes has committed against him, which includes sleeping with his wife. Also belonging to this list is F 104.8-10 (quod re in summa summum esse arbitror…) and F 104.11-15 (addo huc…).

104.8-10: quod re in summa summum esse arbitror…: Lana (1959, 301-2) sees this fragment as closely connected with F 104.7 (qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum). For a man with supreme power, the adultery of a spouse constitutes the most terrible danger: if his wife, the queen, gives birth to illegitimate children, this would put the proper royal line of succession at risk by giving rise to potential confusion between legitimate heirs and illegitimate heirs. Atreus expresses his fear that this might indeed happen in F 104.8-10 (quod re in summa summum esse arbitror | periclum matres conquinari regias, | contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus). Lana (p. 302) argues that F Adesp. 65 (proinde ita parent se in vita, ut vinci nesciant) constitutes Atreus’ solution to the potential problem of succession that might arise from his wife’s infidelity with Thyestes: he must raise Agamemnon and Menelaus in such a way as to instill in them the mindset that they cannot be beaten. 

104.8-15 quod re in summa…cepit sibi!: these lines, quoted at Cic. ND 3.68, seem first to have been attributed to Accius’ Atreus by Columna in his edition of Ennius (1590, 419).

104.11-15: Lana (1959, 302) sees this fragment as belonging to a speech by Atreus in which he lists the wrongs committed against him by Thyestes. Also belonging to this list are F 104.7 (qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum) and F 104.8-10 (quod re in summa…). Lana suggests that Atreus may have been prompted to list all these wrongs in order to justify his revenge after the attendant urged him to reconsider (see further ‘Fragment reconstruction’ F 103. 4 oderint, dum metuant).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De Oratore 3.217-219:

nullum est enim horum [sc. sonorum] generum, quod non arte ac moderatione tractetur. hi sunt actori, ut pictori, expositi ad variandum colores. aliud enim vocis genus iracundia sibi sumat, acutum, incitatum, crebro incidens:

Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser

Manderem natos . . . . . . . . . . . . . (= Accius Atreus F 104.1-2 TRF)

et ea, quae tu dudum, Antoni, protulisti:

Segregare abs te ausus . . . . . (= Pacuvius F inc. 226.1 TRF, possibly from Teucer)

et:

Ecquis hoc animadvortet? Vincite . . . . . (= Accius F inc. 405 TRF)

et Atreus fere totus. aliud miseratio ac maeror, flexibile, plenum, interruptum, flebili voce:

Quo nunc me vortam? quod iter incipiam ingredi?

domum paternamne? anne ad Peliae filias? (= Adesp. F 25 TRF, possibly from Ennius’ Medea/ Medea exul)

et illa:

O pater, o patria, o Priami domus! (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.10 TRF)

et quae sequuntur:

Haec omnia videi inflammarei,

Priamo vi vitam evitarei. (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.15-16 TRF)

aliud metus, demissum et haesitans et abiectum:

Multis sum modis circumventus, morbo, exilio atque inopia

tum pavor sapientiam omnem mi exanimato expectorat;

mater terribilem minatur vitae cruciatum et necem,

quae nemo est tam firmo ingenio et tanta confidentia

quin refugiat timido sanguen atque exalbescat metu. (= Ennius Alcmeo F 12 TRF)

aliud vis, contentum, vehemens, imminens quadam incitatione gravitatis:

Iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum advenit

iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat.

maior mihi moles, maius miscendumst malum

qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam. (= Accius Atreus F 104.3-6 TRF)

The employment of each of these kinds [of sounds] falls under the regulation of art. They are at our disposal to be varied at will in delivery, just as colors are in painting. Anger requires the use of one kind of voice, high and sharp, excited, breaking off repeatedly:

He, my very brother, urges me, o wretch that I am, to mangle

My sons with my own jaws. (= Accius Atreus F 104.1-2 TRF)

Similarly in the lines that you quoted a while ago, Antonius:

Did you dare to let him be parted from you, (= Pacuvius F inc. 226.1 TRF, possibly from Teucer)

and so forth; also in this passage:

Will really no one punish this? Bind him…; (= Accius F inc. 405 TRF)

and thus almost the entire Atreus. Lamentation and grief require another kind of voice, wavering in pitch, sonorous, halting, and tearful: 

Where can I turn now? And what path must I choose to go?

To the house of my father? Or perhaps to the daughters of Pelias? (= Adesp. F 25 TRF, possibly from Ennius’ Medea/ Medea exul)

Likewise in:

O my father, o my country, o palace of Priam!... (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.10 TRF)

as well as in the passage that follows those lines:

All of these I have seen go up in flames, and

Violence tearing Priam’s life away. (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.15-16 TRF)

Fear again has another kind of voice, subdued, hesitating, and downcast:

I’m beset by many troubles: sickness, banishment, and want.

Dread outheartens all my sense from me, I’m totally terrified.

Mother menaces my life with terrible torture and with death.

No one’s spirit is so firm and no one’s confidence so great that

This wouldn’t make his blood flow back in fright and his face turn pale with fear. (= Ennius Alcmeo F 12 TRF)

Energy has yet another kind, intense, vehement, threatening, and with an earnest sort of excitement:

Again Atreus arrives to mount an assault on Atreus,

Again he now attacks me and disturbs my peace.

A greater mass of evil misery I must

Concoct whereby to quench and crush his cruel heart. (= Accius Atreus F 104.3-6 TRF)

[Text: Kumaniecki 19952; transl. May–Wisse 2001, 292-3.]

The lines of Accius’ Atreus corresponding to F 104.1-6 TRF are preserved in a treatise on oratory by Cicero entitled De oratore, often referred to in English as ‘On the Ideal Orator’, which was written in November 55 BCE but is set in 91 BCE. The Accian is quoted in a passage of Book 3 of the treatise devoted to the delivery (actio) of a speech. The speaker is the orator and statesman Lucius Licinius Crassus (140-91 BCE, RE ‘Licinius 55’), who, along with his fellow orator Marcus Antonius (143-87 BCE, RE ‘Antonius 28’), grandfather of the Marcus Antonius attacked by Cicero in the Philippics, is one of the two main speakers in Cicero’s dialogue. Crassus illustrates the various tones which an orator can adopt with his voice to convey a range of different emotions, such as a prolonged state of anger (iracundia), fear (metus), or uis (translated above as ‘energy’), by comparing lines from Republican drama as they were performed by actors on the stage. The Accian fragment is quoted alongside a series of other fragments of Republican drama by Ennius and Pacuvius, as well as, notably a fragment by Accius of uncertain play which has often been attributed to the Atreus (F inc. 405 TRF).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Tusculanae Disputationes 4.77:

Ira uero, [quae] quam diu perturbat animum, dubitationem insaniae non habet. Cuius inpulsi existit etiam inter fratres tale iurgium:

‘Quis homo te exsuperabit usquam gentium inpudentia?

Quis <uir> autem malitia te?’ (= Adesp. F 63 TRF)

Nosti quae secuntur; alternis enim uersibus intorquentur inter fratres grauissimae contumeliae, ut facile appareat Atrei filios esse, eius qui meditatur poenam in fratrem nouam:

‘Maior mihi moles, maius miscendum <est> malum,

qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.5-6)

Quo igitur haec erumpit moles? Audi Thyestem:

‘Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser

mandarem natos.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.1-2)

Eorum uiscera apponit! Quid est enim quo non progrediatur eodem ira quo furor? Itaque iratos proprie dicimus exisse de potestate, id est de consilio, de ratione, de mente; horum enim potestas in totum animum esse debet.

As for anger: while this emotion lasts, there can be no doubt that it is insane. Under its influence even brothers may quarrel like this:

‘Is there anyone in all the world that’s beaten you for insolence?’

‘Or you for spite?’ (= Adesp. F 63 TRF)

You know the rest: verse by verse, the brothers hurl at each other the most serious insults. One can easily believe they are the sons of that Atreus who plots an unheard-of penalty for his own brother:

‘A greater weight, a greater ill I must contrive

to bruise and crush his bitter mind.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.5-6)

When then does this weight of ill burst forth? Hear Thyestes:

‘My very brother bids me bite with my own mouth

alas! my sons.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.1-2)

He serves up their flesh. For anger will go to any length, just as madness will. Thus we are right when we say that angry persons have gone ‘out of control’ – that is, out of their intent, their reason, their conscious thought. For it is these things which ought to control the mind as a whole.

[Text: Giusta 1984; transl. Graver 2002, 67-8, adapted].

These three fragments, which correspond to Adesp. F 63 TRF (which has been connected to Accius’ Atreus), F 104.5-6 (the two last lines of a fragment which is preserved more fully at Cic. De orat. 3.219), and F 104.1-2 (also preserved at Cic. De orat. 3.217), are preserved in a philosophical treatise in five books by Cicero entitled Tusculan Disputations, which was probably written during the summer of 45 BCE (on the dating, see Marinone–Malaspina 20042, 215). In this treatise, which is addressed to Brutus the assassin of Caesar (RE ‘Iunius 53’), Cicero aims to present to a Roman audience Greek philosophy of the Hellenistic period on a range of topics. Book 4, along with Book 3, is devoted to the emotions: Book 4 focuses specifically on perturbationes, ‘disturbances of mind’. The fragment of uncertain author and uncertain play quoted by Cicero in this passage has sometimes been thought also to belong to Accius’ Atreus (see Adesp. F 63 TRF, ‘Fragment reconstruction’). This attribution seems implausible, however, on the grounds that Cicero seems quite clearly to state that the two speakers are sons of Atreus, which makes it virtually certain that they cannot be Atreus and Thyestes, but rather are to be identified with Agamemnon and Menelaus. Therefore, the attribution of this fragment to Ennius’ Iphigenia by scholars such as Ribbeck, Vahlen, and Jocelyn seems at the very least more plausible, though far from certain.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De Natura Deorum 3.68-69:

The Academic speaker Gaius Cotta gives examples drawn from Republican tragedy of instances where reasoning (ratiocinari) is put to use in order to do wicked deeds:

Quid ille funestas epulas fratri conparans nonne versat huc et illuc cogitatione rationem:

‘maior mihi moles, maius miscendumst malum,

qui illius acerbum cor contundam et conprimam’. (= Accius Atreus F 104.5-6 TRF)

Nec tamen ille ipse est praetereundus,

‘Qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum’, (= Accius Atreus F 104.8 TRF)

De quo recte et verissime loquitur Atreus:

‘Quod re in summa summum esse arbitror

Periclum, matres coinquinari regias,

Contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus’. (= Accius Atreus F 104.8-10 TRF)

At id ipsum quam callide, qui regnum adulterio quaereret:

‘Addo’ inquit ‘huc, quod mihi portento caelestum pater

Prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei,

Agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma

Quondam Thyestem clepere ausum esse e regia,

[a] qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi’. (= Accius Atreus F 104.11-15 TRF)

Videturne summa inprobitate usus non sine summa esse ratione? Nec vero scaena solum referta est his sceleribus sed multo vita communis paene maioribus.

Another notorious example is that notorious figure who prepared a feast of death for his brother; does he too not exploit his reason, as his thoughts turn this way and that?

‘More monstrous the misdeed which I must mount

To crush and to constrain his cruel heart.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.5-6 TRF)

And we cannot leave unmentioned Thyestes himself, for whom

‘’Twas not enough to entice a wife to sin’. (= Accius Atreus F 104.8 TRF)

The observations of Atreus on this are just and to the point:

‘I count this matter most profound,

Profound too is the hazard run

When royal matrons are befouled,

Their stock defiled, their lineage stained.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.8-10 TRF)

How craftily that deed was done, for Thyestes sought the kingship by way of adultery! Atreus continues:

‘Note this as well: the Father of the gods

Had sent to me, to fortify my rule,

A prodigy, a lamb whose golden fleece

Shone out conspicuous from out the flock.

Thyestes brazenly once stole that lamb,

Using my wife as accomplice in the deed.’ (= Accius Atreus F 104.11-15 TRF)

This villainy which he employed was clearly buttressed by the utmost use of reason. It is not merely the stage that abounds in such crimes; even more, our daily life is studded with examples almost as outrageous’.

[Text: Plasberg–Ax 19332; transl. Walsh 1997, 134.]

These lines from Accius’ Atreus are preserved in Cicero’s De Natura Deorum (‘On the Nature of the Gods’), a philosophical treatise in three books written in 45 BCE. The passage quoted above falls in a section in Book 3 in which Gaius Aurelius Cotta (RE ‘Aurelius 96’), the character who speaks in support of the philosophical views of the Academy, responds to the account of Stoic theology that had been given by the character of Quintus Lucilius Balbus (RE ‘Lucilius 20’). A substantial part of Cotta’s response to Balbus is lost, including a sizeable portion of text just before our passage begins (though an idea of what it contained can be gleaned from later sources such as Lactantius, Div. Inst. 2.3.2 and Arnobius Adv. Nat. 3.6). In what preceded, Cotta had been arguing against Balbus’ claim that the universe is ruled by providential divine intelligence (Nat. D. 3.64-5). When the full Ciceronian text resumes after the lacuna, Cotta is arguing that mankind’s possession of reason does not constitute proof of the gods’ benevolence towards it because reason can be used to do bad actions. In order to illustrate this, he quotes lines from Republican tragedy which show how reasoning (ratiocinari) can be misapplied in order to prepare and then to enact wicked deeds (the concept of ratiocinari in a rhetorico-legal context is explained at Cic. Inv. 2.5.18 and Quint. Inst. Orat. 7.2.42-44, citing Cic. Mil. 36ff: cf. Lausberg §158). Cicero cites a cluster of fragments of uncertain authorship and uncertain play which have often been thought to belong to Ennius’ Medea (Adesp. F 71 and 73 TRF, attributed by virtually all previous editors to Ennius’ Medea/Medea exul; Adesp. F 72 TRF, attributed to Ennius’ Medea by Warmington and Jocelyn but not by Ribbeck or Vahlen; Adesp. 74 TRF, a fragment clearly from a tragedy about Medea but considered incertum by Ribbeck, Vahlen, and Jocelyn who however tentatively suggests (1969, 348) that it should be attributed to Ennius’ Medea exul). Then Cotta begins to quote from Accius’ Atreus.

doc.metrical_analysis:
104.1-2 ipsus hortatur me frater…: trochaic septenarii.

104.3-15 iterum Thyestes…: iambic senarii. Considering the apparent thematic and metrical unity of these lines, it may well be the case that lines 3 to 15 belong to one and the same speech by Atreus, which is indeed what the TRF editor J. Schultheiß suggests by printing these lines together (cf. IV.174).

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: May he not … the cruel vitality of my fierce nature and soul.
doc.differences:
TrRF: 21 (Naevius' Lycurgus 5/24)

Ribbeck1: 40 Naevius' Lycurgus 12/24)

Ribbeck2: 38 (Naevius' Lycurgus 12/24)

Ribbeck3: 35 (Naevius' Lycurgus 12/24)

Warmington: 49 (Naevius' Lycurgus 26/36)

Marmorale: 12, p.195 (Naevius' Lycurgus 12/24)

ne ille mei feri ingeni atque animi acrem acrimoniam

Ribbeck1, 2: ne ille mei feri ingeni iram atque animi acrem acrimoniam
doc.apparatus:
ne］nae Scriverius coni.

ne ille］nae limet vel elimet Bothius coni.

feri］fert Scriverius, Bongars coni., feret Mueller coni.

feri ingeni］feri igenid Bothe coni.

acrem］agrem L1, sed in codd. γ δ aerem scribuntur, et arrem in H1(Mu.).

The editorial history of this fragment centers largely on the reconstruction of its missing verb, though this task is much complicated by where editors have located the hiatus in the verse.
doc.commentary:
feri]: genitive singular or nominative plural of ferus, adjective for ‘wild (adj.), untamed thing (n.)’. The likelihood of feri corresponding to the verb ferio, ‘I hit, strike’, is not high, though this renders the sentence without a verb. Nonius, however, frequently quotes imperfectly, so that we may safely assume feri to be an adjective. For a discussion of the missing verb, see Spaltenstein’s disapproving analysis of reconstructions: (2014) Commentaires des Fragments Dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels, p. 496 §1368).

ingeni… animi]: ingeni may be parsed as genitive singular of ingenium masc. noun, ‘nature, innate quality’, while animi as a genitive singular of animus, noun for ‘impetus, vital force, soul’. The reappearance of ingenium in fr. 34 (TrRF), in an order, directed at those ‘who disquieten the custodians of the royal body’, to go to leafy places where bushes grow naturally and ‘not sowed’ by hand, explicitly contrasts nature with culture, or Bacchic ritual with social life. This is a comparison which may help us here. If we are to understand ingeni as expressing a natural quality, a type of natural power, the threat enounced in this fragment is thus a warning to beware the natural powers of the speaker, who must be considered Dionysus if this comparadum is legitimate.

acrem]: accusative singular of acer (n.), ‘severe, violent, cruel’.

acrimoniam]: Warmington argues Nonius has defined this word mildly, as ‘vivacity of the soul’. The Oxford Latin Dictionary is more severe and, quoting this very fragment, reads “(of feeling) Harshness, bitterness” (s.v. 3). If we are, however, to seriously consider Nonius’ definition of acrimonia as animi vivacitas, our fragment appears as a Dionysiac injunction to play, to subvert and to destroy. With this in mind, a translation might read ‘May he not … the cruel vitality of my fierce nature and soul’, with our fragment’s language suggestive of the spiritual expansion and social destruction associated with Bacchanalia.

acrem acrimoniam]: The strong alliteration found in the fragments of Naevius indicate it may have been a key feature of his poetry. Compare the symphonic alliteration of fragments 18 ubi bipedes, 20 venatu vitulantis, 24 haec flammis flora fieri and 29 inflexu flectitur of this play alone. Are we to associate this language with a character of the play or with the poet's technique itself?
doc.reconstruction:
The verse may have stood as part of a threat, though its speaker is unclear. It may stand just as much for the legitimate and terrible threat of Dionysiac power as for the boastful and misguided threats made by mortals typical in narratives of hubris in Bacchic myth. The identification of which kind of threat will wholly influence the interpretation of this fragment.

Importantly, the expression of a threat, of a foreboding change of states, of consciousness and of body, fit well with other themes inherited in the fragments of this play. Consider fr. 33, which describes a potential conflict or a showdown, and compare with fr. 19 and 17, which, describing insolence, hint at resistance towards changes and loyalty to one of the belligerents: vos qui astatis obstinati […].

Similarly, our fragment contains a suggestion of a potential change of state against those who resist, of a Dionysus punishing a Lycurgus, or an arrogant Lycurgus punishing his captive prisoner, his divine identity concealed. That even in our few fragments this theme of knowledge appears indicates Naevius to have used dramatic irony to compel his audience’s of his themes’ tragedy, of, in this case, the blindness with which humans injure themselves, and for which gods hate men (cf. fr. 26).

In my commentary, I have argued, with attention to the language of fr. 21 and to its relation with the play’s other fragments, that its speaker is Dionysus. I have thus interpreted it as Dionysus speaking of cautioning Lycurgus to beware his violent nature to an unknown interlocutor. However, this attribution may be questioned when considering the form of the play on the basis of its antecedents. In the Bacchae of Euripides, Dionysus makes no statement comparable in its frankness about his nature, hiding his true identity to Pentheus at every successive episode of the play. Indeed, the play’s tragic sense relies much on the dramatic interplay between human ignorance and divine deceit. Though unparalleled in the Bacchae, earlier literature furnishes us with examples of gods speaking of their powers and nature to mortals (Sophocles, Ajax 1-133, Homer’s Apollo in Iliad 16).

Importantly, however, another fragment of the play speaks openly of the god Liber, undisguised, his identity probably ascertained (fr. 33). Since Dionysus does not seem to disguise his identity in the Lycurgus of Naevius, we may well imagine the speaker of fr. 21 to be Dionysus, perhaps appearing in an epiphany of the god in full splendour, though where this would fit in the play is unclear.

This interpretation, however, is far from consensual. Ribbeck (1875, Die Römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig, p. 60) pursues the comparison with Euripides' Bacchae that I am cautious of, interpreting the fragments through its themes of disguise and revelation. He attributes the fragment to Lycurgus, speaking to the god he has imprisoned, unaware of his true identity, and casts fr. 33 as the god’s oblique but sincere response. As a comparandum for cruel spirit he cites the admittedly compelling adjective ὀξύκολος given to him in Sophocles, Antigone 955. Similarly, the fragment was attributed by Warmington to Lycurgus himself, by which he supposed that ille refers to Dionysus, presumably in the process of instituting his cult in Thrace without the king’s permission, corresponding again to the guileless wrath of Pentheus, who thought Dionysus a stranger he could dominate and rule. Marmorale (1950, Naevius Poeta. Florence, p. 195) broadly follows Ribbeck in interpretation, adding that ne is not an element of a negation, but “una particella asseverativa” like the Greek νή, an interpretation found also in Warmington (1939), Remains of Old Latin vol. II, p. 133. Similarly, Klussmann, in his 1843 Jena edition of Naevius, and Duentzer (1837 ‘De Naevii Lycurgo et Ennii Iphigenia’, Rheinisches Museum für Philologie 5, p. 433-46) argue that ille refers to Dionysus. Additionally, Duentzer (ibid, p.440) adds that Lycurgus’ interlocutor is Silenus, though this is merely speculative. Finally, Spaltenstein (2014, Commentaires des Fragments Dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels, p. 496 §1369) reviews the editorial history, noting that the violent nature with which Lycurgus presumably describes himself is typical of both his being a Thracian and his character as a hubristic king, adding that F.H. Bothe, in his 1834 Poetarum Latii scenicorum fragmenta (Leipzig), is the only editor to attribute it to Dionysus, though I have been unable to locate this work to find his reasoning.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.text:
Vita: T1 (origin: comes from Rudae/Calabria), T73 (spoke Greek, Oscan, Latin), T46 (Rudae as Greek); T55 (one of the elite of Rudiae); T37 (arrived in Rome in 204BC because of Cato); T 41 (Hor. epist. 1 19,6-8 always drunk [see Homer/ wine metaphor, inspiration]); T20 (death date, 169 BC); T63 (Silius, fought in a battle); T83 (Hieronymus confuses Ennius’ and Livius’ city of origin, Ennius as frugal and modest?)

Ennius, who died in 169 BC, shortly after the performance of his Thyestes (T20), according to Cicero at the age of 70 (T32), making his birth year 239 BC, was born in Rudae in Calabria, a Greek colony (T1, T46) where he belonged to the local elite (T55). He was multilingual, speaking Greek, Oscan and Latin, a subject of his apparent pride: According to Gellius (T73) Ennius ‘stated that he had three hearts’ (Quintus Ennius tria corda habere sese dicebat). He arrived in Rome in 204BC (T37), a move due to Cato, who was quaestor at the time (T37). 

While in Rome, Ennius cultivated close ties to members of the patrician elite. These included an enduring relationship with Fulvius Nobilior (T12, T21, T77, T79, T82), with the Scipiones, in particular Scipio Africanus and Publius Cornelius Scipio Nasica (cos. 191BC) (cf. the anecdote told by Cic. de orat. 2, 276 = T16), and with Marcus Porcius Cato. Ennius taught Cato Greek when Cato was praetor in Sardinia (T78). He accompanied Fulvius Nobilior during the war with the Aetolians on campaign to Ambracia (T77), captured in 189BC, and in praise of Fulvius’ victory wrote a celebratory work (T79: victoriam per se magnificam Ennius amicus eius [sc. Fulvii] insigni laude celebravit) which is probably the eponymous praetexta. He was buried in the tomb of the Scipiones (T12, T85) on the Via Appia. Livy recounts that three statues outside the Porta Capena are supposed to depict Ennius with P. Cornelius Scipio Africanus and L. Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus. On the other hand, Ennius was not only associated with the most celebrated men of the era - as we learn from Pliny, he added a further book to the Annales (what is now Book 16) to praise the exploits of an otherwise unknown T. Caecilius Teucer and his brother (T58).

Relationship with the elite: T12 / T21, T77, T79, T82 - relationship with Fulvius Nobilior (who was miserly towards Ennius acc. to T82); T16 (anecdote with Nasica - relationship with elite); T50, T53 (relationship with Scipio Africanus); T 78 (taught Greek to Marcus Porcius Cato); T85 (buried on the Via Appia with Scipio)

Relationship with those outside elite: likely T58 (Ennius added (adiecit 16th book to praise T. Caecilius Teucer and brother; unusual in highlighting a connection with individuals completely unknown to us from any other ancient source - probably belonging to the plebeians and perhaps from humble origins. It is remarkable that Ennius “added” (adiecit) a 16th book to a work that consists of 18 books; maybe this “16th” book has been published separately and was not part of the official publication)

Self-evaluation: T2 (epitaph); T19 (as (merited) self-glorifying); T32 (once fortis equus, now fatigued); 

Ennius’ own estimation of himself was varied. While he was bombastic in comparing his own poetic efforts to those of his predecessors (T19), towards the end of his life he seems to have thought old age had weakened his abilities (T32). His epitaph focussed on how he had glorified the greatest deeds of the Romans (uestrum panxit maxuma facta patrum) but he was concerned that his own passing would be unlamented (nemo me lacrimis decoret nec funera fletu / faxit) (T2).

His reputation flunctuated over time. The epigrammatist Pompilius (2nd cent. BC) praises him as a pupil of the Muses (Ennius [sc. discipulus] Musarum, T 5). Volcacius (early 1st century BC) in his poem De poetis ranks him tenth and last in a list of Roman comic poets where Ennius makes the cut because of his dignity / antiquity (decimum addo causa antiquitatis Ennium) (T7). Cicero has a highly positive view of Ennius, praising him as summus poeta noster (T13) and summus epicus poeta (T25) and poeta egregius (T30) - in contrast with the poetae novi (referred to as cantores Euphorionis) of whom Cicero disapproved. Lucretius, like Cicero (T13), was particularly focused on the idea of Ennius as the quintessential Roman poet, to be compared to the great authors of Greece, hence his repeated references to Ennius as noster (T36).

In the Augustan period, the poet Horace was particularly impressed with Ennius, crediting him as someone his contemporaries could learn from (T39) calling him ‘another Homer’ (alter Homerus, T42), a reference explained by Porphyry, in his commentary on Horace, to Ennius’ claim of metempyschosis, or being Homer reincarnated. Horace also credits him as inspired by the Muses (Calabrae Pierides) (T45). Ovid, on the other hand, concurs with Horace (T39) in describing Ennius as gravis, however, pointing out that Ennius’ genius was counterbalanced by his lack of skill (T49, T52) and earlier in the same poem noting that nothing was more hirsutus than Ennius’ Annales (T51), a sentiment expressed earlier by Propertius, Ennius hirsuta cingat sua dicta corona (4.1.61), both marking Ennius as the quintessential (epic) poet of Rome’s long gone past, and implicitly drawing a contrast with Virgil’s Aeneid as the canonical work of modern Rome. (Propertius presents himself as an enthusiastic supporter of Virgil’s poetic project, in contrast with an epic status quo also at 2.34.61-66, where the targets are Greek poets, rather than Ennius.)

Reputation of Ennius as an author: T6 (Pompilius on E. as pupil of Muses); T7 ()(Volcacius’ ranking of authors, Ennius comicus ranks tenth causa antiquitatis); T10 (Cic. Mur. 30 - ingeniosus poeta); T12 - reputation (see vita); T13 - summus poeta noster (cf. T36 Ennius noster):, T 25 (Cic. opt. Gen. 2 :summus epicus poeta), T 30 (poetam egregium): T55 (author familiar to audience); 

Specifically Augustan evaluation of Ennius’ reputation as an author: T39 (source of inspiration for later authors): T42 (Hor. ep. 2.1.50-2, alter Homerus), T45 (Horace, Calabrae Pierides); T47, T59 (statue at porta Capena); T51 (Ovid, nothing is hirsutius), T 52 (Ov. trist. 2, 423sq: gravis Ennius ore, | Ennius ingenio maximus, arte rudis)

From the first century onwards, it is the Annales that are the centre of the Nachleben of Ennius’ work, although writers are aware of Ennius’ important role as a tragedian. For example, Quintilian praises his iucunditas (sweetness of style) along with other Republican dramatists including Accius and Pacuvius (T61). But they do not display the same knowledge of the tragedies as Cicero, who Quintilian (T61) recognises as incorporating Ennian verses into his forensic speeches (a rhetorical practice endorsed also by Cicero’s contemporary, the author of the Rhetorica ad Herennium, T8, T9, and by Cicero himself, T14, T17) - by contrast, the only Ennian tragic lines Quintilian quotes in his extant works is the opening lines of Medea, a speech that had long formed a commonplace for discussion in rhetorical works (F89j). Over this same contemporary period, the Greekness of Ennius increasingly becomes stressed. Whereas Cicero’s Ennius was noster Ennius, for Suetonius in his De grammaticis Ennius is, along with Livius Andronicus, semigraecus and considered the translator of Greek works (T64). Suetonius also writes in the same passage that some people wrongly attributed to Ennius two theoretical treatises, a De litteris syllabisque and a De metris (on which, see further below), which perhaps reflects the Hellenistic image of Ennius in the Imperial era as well as the symbolic authority Ennius still possessed (idem). Fronto (T68) gives first place along with Plautus to Ennius among the exceptional writers of the middle Republican period. Ennius maintained this authority as the first Roman epic writer of note among later Latin grammarians (Diomedes, T80) and was appreciated as the primus Homerus apud Latinos, “the first Homer among Latin writers” (T86).

Imperial Evaluation of Ennius’ reputation as an author: ; T57 (Sen. ap. Gell. is amazed that people praised Ennius’ ridiculous work); T61 (Quintilian, summa non modo eruditionis modo gratia sed etiam iucunditatis); T62 (Ennius sicut sacros uetustate lucos); T64 (Suetonius, Livius and Ennius semigraeci, Ennius as not the author of Ennian treatises De litteris syllabisque and De metris [arguit L. Cotta non poetae sed posterioris Enni]); T80 (Diomedes, Ennius as first writer of Latin epos to be taken seriously); T85 (Hieronymus, primus Homerus apud Latinos)

Work: T20 (Thyestes, performance date 169BC); T74 (12th book of Annals completed 179BC); T31 & T69 (Euhemerus resp. Fronto Ennius working in many genres); T33 (Andromache); T 40, T81 (saturae)

Ennius worked in a wide variety of genres, such as drama, including crepidata (tragedies), palliata (comedies), and praetexta (drama in a historical setting), epic (Annales), saturae (a collection of texts belonging to different genres), didactic poems (Euhemerus, Epicharmus and the slightly less serious Hedyphagetica devoted to fishy delicacies), and a sotadic poem (Sotas). Fronto calls Ennius multiformis (T69) which could point to the number of different genres in which Ennius worked, but also could be a comment on his style - but perhaps the two are related. Fragments from twenty identified tragedies by Ennius survive, as well as at least sixty incerta fragments (frgg. 142-201 TRF) which could belong to the same Ennian tragedies or other ones. There are fifteen further fragments where the genre or metre is not fully transparent (frgg. 202-216 TRF) which may well belong to the tragedies. There is little information about the performance dates for most of the tragedies - an exception is that we know Thyestes was performed in 169BC, the year of Ennius’ death (T20). Ennius seems to have worked on the Annals and the tragedies concurrently, given that we know he composed the twelfth book of the Annals in 174BC. 

Later authors make much of the source of Ennius’ inspiration, and often do not agree. According to Lucretius (T36), Ennius’ Annales were inspired by a dream of Homer, and this idea seems to be found in Ennius’ own words in the Annales (F5 Warmington): visus Homerus adesse poeta (cf. T11, where Cicero talks of Ennius calling upon sanctos poetas). This idea explains Porphyry’s suggestion of the metempsychosis of Homer by Ennius in his commentary on Horace’s comment Ennius … alter Homerus (T42, see above). 

But against the image of Ennius as Homer reincarnate, or receiving lessons from his ghost, is a tradition which taps into the ancient po(e)tological opposition between poets as either “wine drinkers” and “water drinkers”. Propertius identifies Ennius as a drinker from the waters of Helicon (T48). On the other hand, wine is identified by Horace (T41) as Ennius’ method for inspiration, echoes of which can be found much later in Serenus’ Liber Medicinalis, where the poetic solution of wine is reshaped as medical advice. 

Source of Inspiration: T36 (Annales, dream of Homer, inspiration), T41 (inspiration through wine); T48 (inspiration from Helicon); T87 (drinks wine to become healthy)

Later Roman authors credited Ennius with a major impact on style, metrical practices and even the formal aspects of language. Even if his stylistic practices and use of language were not always favourably received by some critics, Ennius was always recognised as an authority, over an incredibly long period of time, stretching into late antiquity. Ennius’ incorporation of everyday language into non-comic genres (T22) was a feature that placed him among the great epic and tragic authors according to Cicero (T23). Indeed, Plautus, according to the declaration of the prologue of his Poenulus, explicitly employed elements of the opening of some tragedy by Ennius featuring Achilles (either a tragedy Achilles written by a Greek Aristarchus and translated by Ennius or a tragedy entitled Achilles Aristarchi) (T3). That Ennius’ tragic lines could be woven into a comedy perhaps illustrates the interchange in stylistic features between the genres in his tragic work (ref.). Apparently Ennius used established stylistic techniques to create his plays: the comic poet Terence mentions Ennius in one breath with Plautus and Naevius as poetic predecessors who employed contaminatio (the mixing of previous poetic plots and material together to create a new product) in their compositional process - it is unclear whether this only refers to Ennius’ comedies or not. 

Relation Tragedy/Comedy: T3 (Plautus read Ennius translation of Aristarchus’s Achilles)

Pre-Ciceronian evaluation of Ennius’ techniques: T4 (Terence on contaminatio); T5 (Lucilius on Annales as on a single theme) (skipped); T8 & T9 (Rhet. Her. on tragic verses used to practice rhetoric; cf. Cic. de Oratore 1.154 - T14); T11 (Cic. Arch. 18 ars (inspiration) vs. doctrina - Ennius’ dream of Homer

Cicero’s evaluation / reception of Ennian technique: T14 (daily study during Cicero’s rhetorical education); T17 (rhetoric and tragedy as models); T22 (communi more verborum); T23 

Augustan evaluation of Ennian technique: T49 (Ovid, Ennius arte carens); T52 (Ovid, ingenium vs ars, Ennius ingenio maximus arte rudis)

Imperial evaluation of Ennian technique: (T57 (Seneca, inter hircosos...tamen inter unguentatos)

In the Imperial period, Seneca comments ambiguously on Ennius’ style, on the one hand echoing Propertius in placing him among the “shaggy” (inter hircosos), on the other describing him as inter unguentatos - but whether this refers to the effective evocation of pathos or an “embalmed” and old-fashioned style is left unclear (T57). Similarly ambivalent judgements on Ennius prevail in other authors. Suetonius, when describing Virgil discussing ‘collecting gold from Ennius’ muck’ (aurum colligere de stercore Ennii), notes that Ennius’ style included features from various stylistic levels (egregias sententias sub uerbis non multum ornatis, T66, cf. also T39). Ennius, on the one hand, employed an archaic style (T 89-91), yet on the other also created new coinages and shaped the sermo patrius along with Cato (T43). Ennius’ innovation was also stressed by Fronto, who points out his interest in wordplay and his search for words to this end (T68) - Ennius may have cultivated his Roman identity by creating wordplays in order to connect with his audience, for wordplay usually must be constructed within a given language, and cannot be the result of translation (here e.g. from Greek). The playful nature of Ennius’ work is also demonstrated in his inclusion of acrostics in his work (T35). 

Ennius’ pioneering attitude in metrical matters goes hand in hand with the self-confidence he showed in his style. Although famous for adapting the Greek hexameter for use in Latin, he also was as comfortable in home-grown Roman metres according to the poet Silius with reference to an otherwise unknown Hesiodic work (T63). This command over metre shows in the liberty to also break metrical rules (T44), and prosodic conventions such as avoiding hiatus (T24), rather than slavishly following them. Ennius was viewed as such an authority on metre by the subsequent tradition that he was credited, falsely according to Suetonius, with authorship of a treatise De metris (T64). 

Relation Tragedy/Comedy: T3 (Plautus read Ennius translation of Aristarchus’s Achilles)

Pre-Ciceronian evaluation of Ennius’ techniques: T4 (Terence on contaminatio); T5 (Lucilius on Annales as on a single theme); T8 & T9 (Rhet. Her. on tragic verses used to practice rhetoric; cf. Cic. de Oratore 1.154 - T14); T11 (Cic. Arch. 18 ars (inspiration) vs. doctrina - Ennius’ dream of Homer

Cicero’s evaluation / reception of Ennian technique: T14 (daily study during Cicero’s rhetorical education); T17 (rhetoric and tragedy as models); T22 (communi more verborum); T23
