Augustan evaluation of Ennian technique: T49 (Ovid, Ennius arte carens); T52 (Ovid, ingenium vs ars, Ennius ingenio maximus arte rudis)

Imperial evaluation of Ennian technique: (T57 (Seneca, inter hircosos...tamen inter unguentatos)

Ennius’ identity as an innovator extended to being credited with changing practices of spelling (T76), perhaps linked to the adoption of the Annales into the Roman system of education, and the invention of 1100 shorthand signs (T93), more than a century before Cicero’s freedman Tiro.

Impact (a) language: T76 (Ennius changed spelling practices), b) T93 (Isidorus, invention of 1100 shorthand signs)

Style: T23 (register of vocabulary); T24 (employs hiatus); T35 (includes acrostics); T39 (some verses lack dignity); T43 (sermo patrius with Cato, innovative (nova nomina); T56 (antiquated by 1st century AD); T66 (Suetonius, uneven style, Vergil presented himself as collecting aurum… de stercore Ennii); T68 (Fronto, wordplay, verba industriosius quaerendi); T89-T91 (Macrobius & Historia Augusta, Ennius’ archaic style)

Metrum: T64 (Suetonius arguing against a view (nonnulli tradunt) that Ennius was the author of a De metris); T71 (Gellius, pronunciation of Punic names to fit metre)

Comparison with Greek poets: T26; T27 (non verba sed vim Graecorum … poetarum); T29; T94 (nearly all his tragedies translated from Greek, most from Euripides, some from Aristarchus (probably thinking of Ennius’ Achilles)

Cicero is the figure most responsible for our view of Ennius as a translator of Greek tragedy. Cicero frequently touches upon Ennius, treating him as a precedent for the translation of Greek works, when discussing his own project of translating Greek philosophical works into Latin. In the de optimo genere oratorum, Cicero argues that there is an audience for his rhetorical writing, even if it is translated from the work of previous Greek rhetoricians, just as Roman audiences had an appetite for Ennius’ plays (and other tragic and comic poets), even if they could just read the works of Sophocles and Euripides (T26). Cicero’s famous statement in Academica (T27) that the Roman tragic poets Ennius, Pacuvius and Accius translated non uerba sed uim Graecorum … poetarum must also be seen in this light, since it is part of his argument for the necessity that Cicero translate Greek philosophical work into Latin - and a licence for Cicero to treat those works with some degree of liberty. Thus Ennius is an important anchor for Cicero to promote his own innovative activities vis-a-vis Greek rhetoric and philosophy as a time-honoured Roman cultural activity. Cicero could often feel it necessary to be defensive about this project, however, suggesting that there was not total agreement with it among the broader Roman public (T29). Other authors are not nearly so interested in Ennius as a translator - however, Cicero’s statements seem to have shaped the reception, so that in a late antique glossary, this is Ennius’ defining quality (tragoedias autem Ennius fere omnes ex graecis transtulit, plurimas <ex> Euripideis, nonnullas Aristarchiis, T94). 

In Cicero’s day, performances of Ennius’ plays were still appearing in theatres, which Cicero himself was attending: in a letter to Quintus, he gives a glowing review of the actor Antiphon in a production of Ennius’ Andromacha in 54 BC (T18). He mentions the musical accompaniment to the Andromacha as well as Ennius’ Antiopa at Academica 2.20 (T28). And he comments in De officiis on the importance of casting actors appropriate to the leading roles of different plays according to the specific requirements, such as gesture and voice - mentioning Ennius’ Melanippa and Ajax as well as plays by Pacuvius and Accius. All in all, it seems that regular theatrical performances of Ennius were being staged well into the first century BC. After Cicero, we have little evidence whether that trend continued into the Imperial period. Rather evidence for Ennius’ Nachleben in the Imperial period are found for the Annales: Gellius reports a theatrical performance of the Annales by an “Ennianista” in Puteoli in the late 2nd century AD - which seems similar to the rhapsodic performance of Homer in Classical Greece. But the primary encounter with Ennius still seems to be through the page: Fronto recommends Ennius to Marcus Aurelius as holiday reading (T70) - and Gellius comments on the memorable and illustrative style he finds in his reading of Ennius’ Annales (T72). The famous Monnus-mosaic in Trier names Ennius (alongside the Greek and Roman poets Homer, Hesiod, Aratus, Menander and Virgil, and other writers such as Cicero and Livy), showing that he was still well-known (and perhaps read) across the Empire and beyond the Roman intellectuals in late antiquity (T95). 

Nachleben: T18 (performance of Andromache in 54 BC); T28 (Cicero, performance to pipes); T35 Cicero, performances of Ennius’ Melanippa & Ajax & Pacuvius’ Medus & Antiopa, Accius’ Epigoni & Clytemnestra - different leading roles suited to different actors); T75 (Gellius attending a rhapsodic performer of Ennius in a south Italian theatre); T70 (Marcus Aurelius’ holiday reading); T72 (Gellius, memorable and graphic style that commands respect); T95 (inscription from a Trier pavestone, Ennius appreciated in Germany)

The centrality of Ennius’ Annales within the canon down to the time of Virgil and beyond, according to Martial (T60), was perhaps not immediate after its publication. In the mid-second century BC, a new kind of reading and criticism grounded in Hellenistic commentary practices was introduced to Rome by Crates of Mallos, according to Suetonius, who informs us that this approach inspired some members of the contemporary intellectual elite of the latter half of the second century to make the epics of the past better known among their peers. Some of these responses, such as C. Octavius Lampadio’s division of Naevius’ Bellum Punicum into seven books, bear the obvious hallmarks of Alexandrine scholarship . Other responses included Q. Vargunteius’ periodic public recitations of the Annales to large audiences (certis diebus in magna frequentia pronuntiabat) (T65). This effort to raise Ennius’ profile was significantly aided by the efforts to emend the text of Ennius’ work, the importance of which is stressed by Fronto for Ennius’ subsequent reputation (T67).

Canonisation: T65 (Suetonius, Q. Varguneius (of the generation after Ennius) through commentating on and recitation of the text (in imitation of the critic Crates) was responsible for the text’s canonisation; T67 (Fronto, canonised books depend on good redactions, possibly also if a mirrored list Fronto specifically names Staberius (Eros? - freedman of Sulla mentioned by Suet. De Illustribus Grammaticis 13) as responsible for Ennian redaction in 1st century BC) T60 (Ennius as canonical author before publication of Aeneid)

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: cur fugit fratrem? scit ipse.

doc.translation: Why is he fleeing his brother? He himself knows.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. CXV, v. 212:

212: Cur fugit fratrem? scit ipse

Not in Ribbeck1.

Warmington:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. 79:

79: Cur fugit fratrem? Scit ipse.

Why does he flee his brother? He knows.

Franchella:

Fragment 20 (20/22), 385 (certum):

385: cur fugit fratrem? scit ipse – u – u – u –

Perché cerca di sfuggire il fratello? Lo sa lui.

Not in D’Antò.

Not in Dangel.
doc.apparatus:
1: fugit ] fugit Buecheler, iam D: fugis codd. plerique

1: fratrem ] fratrem E: patrem 

Bücheler (apud Ribbeck2, 267) conjectured the third-person form fugit for the transmitted second-person form fugis, on the grounds that the verb scit is in the third person. Ribbeck3, 309 reports that the reading fugit is already found in ‘Brux. m 2’, that is, Brussels, KBR MS 9144, s. XV (Håkanson’s D). The excerpted group of manuscripts of Seneca the Elder, referred to by Håkanson as E, have the reading fratrem, the other branch (Håkanson’s α) has patrem. The readings of E are generally preferred because they are ‘the result, it is probable, of editorial activity in the fifth century or thereabouts’ (Winterbottom apud Reynolds–Marshall–Mynors 19862, 357).
doc.commentary: See 'Fragment reconstruction'.
doc.reconstruction: Bücheler (apud Ribbeck2, 267) attributed this fragment to Varius’ Thyestes (Warmington, II.613, tentatively concurs). Lana (1959, 315), who believes that the play ended with Atreus’ downfall, interprets this fragment as belonging to the final scene: Atreus realises that Thyestes has successfully convinced the chorus of Mycenean citizens to rebel against him, and, as he tries to flee, Thyestes mockingly exclaims: cur fugit fratrem? scit ipse! (‘why is he fleeing his brother? He himself knows!’): see further F inc. 405, ‘Fragment reconstruction’. Franchella (233) attributes as a fragment (certum) of Accius’ Atreus, i.e. F 20 (20/22): ‘Questo frammento, che il Buecheler attribuisce a Vario e il Klotz, p. 349, anche a Ennio (e perché non a Graeco? come osserva il Lana, pag. 369) appartiene al XXVII Inc. Inc. Fab., e potrebbe essere di Accio: 1) per l’iracundia portata al parossismo; 2) per la notorietà (illo uersu tragico) che la tragedia di Accio godeva (cf. Cic. Planc. 54: nostis cetera…); 3) per il contenuto oratorio del parlare in 3e persona, proprio di Accio, come risulta anche da altri frammenti di questa tragedia; 4) perché a questo punto dell’azione drammatica si bolla l’animo vile del tiranno che fugge davanti alla mala parata’.
doc.metrical_analysis: In the ‘Conspectus metrorum’ in his second edition (p. 368), Ribbeck lists this fragment as the beginning of a trochaic verse.

context.author: Seneca the Elder
context.text:
Seneca the Elder Controversiae 1.1.21:

Colorem ex altera parte, quae durior est, Latro aiebat hunc sequendum, ut gravissimarum iniuriarum inexorabilia et ardentia induceremus odia Thyes<teo> more: aiebat patrem non irasci tantum debere sed furere. ipse <in> declamatione usus est summis clamoribus illo versu tragico: cur fugis fratrem? scit ipse. (= Adesp. F 96 TRF)

Latro said that on the other side, which is more difficult, we should follow the colour of representing unremitting and passionate hatred, arising from the gravest injuries, Thyestes-wise. He said the father should not merely be angry: he should rave. He himself, in his declamation, employed the tragic verse: ‘Why do you flee, your brother? He knows,’ (= Adesp. F 96 TRF) which received great applause. 

(Text: Håkanson 1989; transl. Winterbottom 1974, I.53).

This passage falls in Book 1 of Seneca the Elder’s Controversiae, a work originally in ten books of which only five have survived (1, 2, 7, 9, 10). The subject of the first controversia of Book 1 is ‘The Man who Disinherited his Nephew’ (Patruus abdicans). The law is that children must support their parents or be imprisoned, and a son is disinherited by his father for helping his uncle who is in need. After having been adopted by the uncle, the son tries to help his own father after the latter has fallen into need, and for this he is disinherited by his uncle. In this passage, Seneca quotes his friend Marcus Porcius Latro, a rhetor endowed with an extraordinary memory (1 praef. 13), who suggests that the father might employ the rhetorical color of passionate hatred. Seneca reports that in Latro’s declamation, he quoted this fragment from a Thyestes play as an illustration of that emotion.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: You, who stubbornly stand there
doc.differences:
TrRF: 17 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 1/23)

Ribbeck1: 55 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 24/24)

Ribbeck2: 53 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 24/24)

Warmington: 57 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 33/35)

Marmorale: p.198 (Naevius’Lycurgus 24/24)

Vos qui astatis obstinati

Scriverius, Stephanus & Bothe: Vos qui adstatis obstinati

Marmorale, Ribbeck and Warmington place this fragment at the end of the play, for lack of information on where else to put it.
doc.apparatus:
astatis] astati X (Lindsay)

obstinati] opstinati W (Lindsay)
doc.commentary: Astatis] Though astati is attested in an important manuscript (X ed. Lindsay) no editor has chosen to print it, preferring the unfinished line to at least have a verb. Although it is not incompatible with vos , it would make no sense of qui . The modification to adstatis by Stephanus, Scriverius, followed by Bothe, is an unnecessary change, as asto is much more frequent. Obstinati] It is hard to determine exactly how obstinati should be translated, as there aren’t any specific indications for either positive or negative connotations. Much depends on the reconstruction of the play and the place this fragment is granted. Possible variations on stubborn in a negative sense could fit in the context of disobeying an order, or even standing stiffened, stupefied by fear (Klussmann, 1849). When a positive connotation is assumed, translating to unyielding or persistent could be more fitting, in a situation where the addressees are threatened, but persevere. Festus’ synonyms do not clarify unfortunately, because both obfirmatus and perseverans can be used in positive, negative or neutral sense (Bednarek, 2021)
doc.reconstruction:
Not much is known about the context of this fragment, its place in the order of the play, but Festus clearly attributes it to Naevius’ Lycurgus .

Because the speaker(s) and addressees are unknown, a lot of different reconstructions have been suggested, resulting in a positive or negative connotation to obstinati . The only semi-secure fact about vos is that it is most likely a group of men, given the gender of obstinati . Therefore, the chorus (which all scholars assume to be a female body of maenads) is excluded as addressee. Unless, of course, Liber is among the chorus the moment they are addressed.

Schauer (2012) gives an overview of the scholarship concerning this fragment: Bothe (1823) suggested that Liber addresses the Thracians while they attack him, led by Lycurgus. In that case, obstinati would indicate the quite neutral determination of soldiers following orders. Bednarek (2021, 139) also finds the suggestion that Liber addresses people that did not join him ‘tempting’. Duentzer (1837) however, attributes this fragment to Silenus in conversation with the king. He then turns his attention to the bystanders for unknown reasons. This does not give a satisfactory explanation for the inclusion of the fragment in Festus, because it tells us nothing about the meaning of obstinati whatsoever.

Klussmann (1849) also finds these ideas highly improbable, and holds instead that here Lycurgus is speaking to his attendants, who refuse to attack the maenads, struck dumb ‘ Panico terrore ’, by the fear caused by Pan. Though Pan is closely associated with Bacchic cult, this would almost reverse the meaning of obstinati , since the attendants then would hold still for lack of courage, instead of because of their determination. This puts a strain on the meaning of the word obstinatus , further complicated by one of the synonyms given by Festus, namely perseverans . Bednarek (2021) agrees that Lycurgus could be addressing his attendants, but precisely those that did not betray him.

Furthermore, Ribbeck (1875) thinks that the Thracians are called upon to surrender, following the punishment (death?) of their leader. Their obstinacy would then be useless. The same sense of stubbornness would be relevant in and Warmington’s (1936) interpretation, as he guesses this is where Lycurgus personal guards capitulate.

Marmorale (1950) and Traglia (1986) rightly conclude that there are not enough clues to securely place this fragment somewhere in the play.

Marmorale, Traglia and Ribbeck determined the metre a trochaic septenarius.

context.author: Sextus Pompeius Festus
context.text:
Quoted by Festus in De verborum significatione:

Festus quotes Naevius to illustrate the meaning of obstinatus, stubborn, resolute.

obstinato obfirmato perseveranti, ut tenere possit, ut Pacuvius: obstinati exorsus [fr. 276, Schierl], ut Accius in Amphitruone: aut tam obstinato animo confisus tuo [p. 201, fr.1, Dangel] Cato in Q. Thermum de X. hominibus: rumorem fama flocci fecit cutibus stupris obstinatus, insignibus flagitiis. [p. 28, fr. 60, Malcovati] Naevius in Lycurgo: vos qui astatis obstinati (Fest. De verborum significatione p. 208,29-36 ed. Lindsay)

lines.text: Vos qui astatis obstinati […]
Erras erras, nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi
repriment validae legum habenae atque imperii insistent iugo
...Pol mihi fortuna magis nunc defit quam genus.
Namque regnum suppetebat mi, ut scias quanto e loco,
Quantis opibus, quibus de rebus lapsa fortuna occidat.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: like a rapid stream hastily flows, but yet winds with a bending
doc.differences:
TrRF: 29

Warmington: 58

Ribbeck 2: 42

Ribbeck 3: 39
doc.apparatus:
sic] sed codd.[Ri,Wa,Kl,Marm] (L H G) [Mu]; Ri. : sed Ald., Steph., Merc., Scriv., Qui., On., Li., Kl., Marm. : se Wa., Tra.

quasi] quando Urbin.308[Ri]

cita] cis codd.[Ri,Wa,Kl,Marm] (L H G) [Mu] (F H L P G E P7579) [On]; celeris Ri., Wa. : vi Qui. : cis Ald., Steph., Merc., Scriv., Bo., crucibus adiectis Li.

rapit] rapida Merc. adn. ad loc., Klu.; rapit sed tamen] Ri., Wa., Kl. : ruit, sed tamen Mu. : rapit se, tamen Qui., On.

inflexu] codd.[Wa] (L H G) [Mu] (F H L P G E P7579) [On] : inflexa codd. sec. Vossius (1620) 119; inflexa Ald., Steph., Iun., Scriv., Qui. : in fluxu Merc. adn. ad loc., Mu. : in flexus On.

– Crit. in app.: amnis cita rapit] amnis rapida Scriv. i.m.; in flexu Scriv. i.m.; sic quasi amnis res rapit, sed tamen implexa flectitur Bo.
doc.commentary:
The fragment that is transmitted in the manuscripts is sed quasi amnis cis rapit, sed tamen inflexu flectitur. However, Nonius mentions this fragment because amnis is untypically feminine. Another example of this deviant gender is Plaut. Merc. 859: ulla … amnis). Since the femininity is not expressed in the transmitted texts several conjectures have been made:

The reading sed quasi amnis rapida etc. can be found in Merc., Klus. and Ribbeck1 (1852). This reading omits the first foot of the Trochaic Septenar (Spaltenstein 2014). A more modern approach is to replace cis by a feminine adjective.

cita is a conjecture that replaces the cis. The feminine adjective fits the Trochaic Septenar, without making rapit a pyrrhic. A conjecture that does make rapit a pyrrhic is celeris (Ribbeck 2,3, Warmington).

inflexu is an ablative of manner, following the manuscripts. The phrase inflexu flectitur is stylistically comparable with acrem acrimoniam in fragment 21. Conjectures that have been made (see apparatus criticus) are therefore not necessary.

The transmitted sed...sed implies that the second sed revises the first revision, which is unprobable. Therefore, the first sed is revised by sic (Ribbeck 2,3) or se (Warmington).
doc.reconstruction:
Several attempts have been made to fit the fragment in Naevius’ Lycurgus tragedy. The most probable ones are presented. Ribbeck (1875) proposed the idea that the verse can be placed in a confrontation between Bacchus and Lycurgus in which Bacchus warns Lycurgus that he would know how to put an end to his violence.

Since this fragment does not show any signs of a dialogue, this idea would be stronger if we would analyze it as a parable. Marmorale (1950) alternatively suggests that the verse should be placed in a scene in which Bacchus speaks to the bacchants. Anyway, the idea of a warning to a cruel person is supported by rapit, which has a cruel connotation. Lycurgus was considered cruel since he captivated the bacchants. An even more speculative suggestion is to draw a connection between flectitur and dublicat in fragment 38, placing it in a scene in which king Lycurgus needs to bow in fear for his opponent(s).

context.author: Nonius
context.text: amnem masculino genere appellamus … [36] Naevius Lycurgo: sic quasi amnis cita rapit sed tamen inflexu flectitur

lines.text: sic quasi amnis cita rapit sed tamen inflexu flectitur

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Chryses
doc.translation: Ida’s promontory whose tongue stretches out into the sea
doc.differences:
Schierl 62 (1/25)
D’Anna 94 (1/28)
Warmington 284 (5/27)
Ribbeck3 298 (8/21)
doc.apparatus:
Id⟨ae⟩] Idea Vossius, edd. : id Ξ
promunturium] promunturium Holford-Strevens, vid. ThLL x/2 1906.73-1907.8 : promuntorium P : promantorium R : pronunturium V : pronuntiaturum C : promunctorium D : promontorium ς
lingua] lingua de Buxis, edd. : ling⟨u⟩am Ξ

Ξ (= αPRD and ς = lectio vulgata recentiorum) transmit id …. linguam. While linguam doesn’t affect the metrical form of the line (the pronounciation of lingua in is identical with linguam in), id would turn the alleged trochaeic septenarius into an incomplete line. As to the syntax, it is the other way around: While id promunturium (“this promontory”) doesn’t cause any syntactical problems, the accusative linguam can’t be integrated into the syntactical structure of the sentence.
The various readings of the second word of the line can partly be explained by the context in which the line has been transmitted in Gellius (see above, Citation Context), who quotes the line with respect to the pronounciation of proicit. Specifically, the readings of V (pronunturium) and C pronuntiaturum seem to be affected by the scribe’s association with the words pronuntiare and pronuntiatio, which occur several times in the preceding text. Apart from promunctorium (L.-S. s.v. promunctorium: προμυκτήριον, Gloss. Gr. Lat.) none of the spellings transmitted in the codices is otherwise attested.

context.author: Gellius
context.text:
The fragment is quoted by Gellius (Gell. NA 4.17.15). In chapter 4.17, Gellius discusses the pronunciation of composita (nouns and verbs) of iacere, such as subices, obices, subicere, etc. and quotes several examples from Latin poetry (Enn. Achilles frg. 2 TRF; Lucil. 11.394-39 &. 411, 15.509 Marx; Plaut. Epid. 194; Cato orat. frg. 30 Malc.; Verg. Geo. 2.18-19 & 479-480, Aen. 6.365-366; Sall. Hist. 4, frg. 52 Maur.) in order to demonstrate that a short consonantic -i-, when placed between a praefix ending with a consonant (e.g. sub-, ob- etc.) and a consonant (here: c-), will be lenghthened:

Id ipsum autem verbum (sc. iacio) M. Cato sub alia praaepositione dicit in oratione, quam de consulatu suo habuit: ‚ita nos’ inquit ‘fert ventus ad primorem Pyrenaeum, qua proicit in altum.‘ Et Pacuvius in Chryse: Idae promunturium, cuius lingua in altum proicit. (ed. Holford-Strevens. Oxford 2020)

lines.text: Id⟨ae⟩ promunturium quoius lingua in altum proicit

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Stasiastae
doc.translation: mutilated by a horrible wound, befouled by his own tepid blood
doc.differences:
Dangel: Accius Stasiastae 4/4

Warmington: 612-613 (=4/4)

Ribbeck: 3/4

D'Anto: 3/4

The most editors place this line as the last of the four lines from the play. In that case, this line can well depict the punishment of Lycurgus. Ribbeck and D'Anto however place it as third. In that case, this will rather be a treatening warning towards Lycurgus instead of his proper punishment, with the last line of dialogue (fragment 4) also being part of the conversation in which this threat is made.

The Latin is the same in all the editions.
doc.apparatus:
deformatum] deformata Basil.

Suo sibi] Suo sibi om. Bern. b.

Suci sibi Basil.

sanguine tepido] Sanguine tepo Bern. b.

Tepido Sanguine Bothius.

The reading deformata isn't very likely here, because lautum should also be lauta then. It would then have to refer to a neutral or feminime form, which is not plausible in this context (see also fragment commentary). The readings suci sibi and sanguine tepo don't make any sense in Latin.
doc.commentary: This fragment probably depicts the punishment of Lycurgus by Bacchus. This is why deformatum is more plausible than deformata, because it suits the reference to a masculine subject better. The form deformata could refer to animals that are being sacrificed, but then the emphasis on the ''horrible wound'' seems a little strange. The option of the cruel punishment of Lycurgus is thus the most plausible.
doc.reconstruction:
These lines are either an depiction of the actual punishment of Lycurgus by Bacchus or a threat that this will happen. Assuming that the first option is true, these lines are problably spoken by a messenger of some kind, telling the audience (and maybe some characters on stage) what happened with Lycurgus, for it was not common in Roman tragedy to show voilent scenes on stage: they were rather told than shown. If however this is a threat rather than an actual scene, these lines should maybe be contributed to Baccus, warning Lycurgus about what will happen to him if he does not obey, or bragging about his powers to punish to someone else. We cannot say which option is true of course, but they are all very well possible.

Warmington mentions that there are two ways in which Lycurgus could have been punished: he could have been blinded and crucified, or he could have been torn apart by horses. This fragment, with the description of the bathing in blood, suggests that the latter option was used in this play.

Bibliography

Dangel, J. 2002. Accius Oeuvres. Paris.

D’Antò, V. 1980. Accius: I frammenti delle tragedie. Lecce

Ribbeck, O. 1852. Tragicorum Latinorum Reliquiae (1st ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. 1871. Tragicorum Latinorum Reliquiae (2nd ed.). Leipzig.

Warmington, E. 1936. Remains of Old Latin, Volume II: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Pacuvius. Accius. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius Marcellinus
context.text:
'lautum' etiam inquinatum vel maculatum. Vergilius... (19) Accius, Stasiastis vel Tropaeo:

Nonius quotes this line because of the use of the word ''lautum''. He argues that in this case, it means something like ''inquinatum'' (soiled) or ''maculatum'' (stained) rather than ''washed'' or ''bathed''.

lines.text: vulnere taetro deformatum
suo sibi lautum sanguine tepido
[ATREUS]: Natis sepulchro ipse est parens
<13> fulguri praeferuido
Ardor iniectus Iunonis dextera ingenti incidit

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: but the pronouncement confuses me. :: Why? Explain!
doc.differences:
Schierl: 112 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 26/32)

D’Anna: 184 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 28/32)

Ribbeck2: 150 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 26/32)

Warmington: 150 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 26/32)

sed me incertat dictio :: quare? expedi!

D’Anna: sed med incertat dictio: quare expedi!

Ribbeck: set med incertat dictio: quare expedi!

Warmington: set med incertat dictio; quare expedi.

The fragment is difficult to place within the story of the play. Editors place it as one of the last fragments, with only D’Anna putting it even later, as 28/32.
doc.apparatus:
me] med Bothe, pler. edd. : ne codd. : me Iunuis, Strzelecki : me Ribbeck propos. crit. in app. : me Nosarti

quare] quare Σ : quam rem codd. : quin rem conj. Scaliger

About me: codd. read ne instead, which makes little grammatical sense, as no subjunctive or imperative follows. Furthermore, incertat needs an object, therefore the conjecture me or med (which Schierl leaves up in the air), depending on whether the hiatus needs to be solved or not.
doc.commentary:
me] codd. read ne, which makes no grammatical sense as there is no following subjunctive or imperative, and incertat needs an object.

incertat] Nonius cites the fragment to explain the meaning of this verb, namely to make someone unsure.

dictio] generally taken to refer to a prophetic pronouncement of sorts. Could refer to the prediction by Oiax mentioned in fr. 105, or a separate oracle involved in the plot.
doc.reconstruction:
From the word dictio we can surmise that an oracle of is part of the plot. It could also refer to Oiax’s prediction from fr. 105.

Schierl follows Nosarti (1999: 104) in reading the fragment as being spoken by two people in response to each other. Other editors read it as spoken by one person (ex. Warmington translates "But the saying unsures me; wherefore expound!"). Both options are strictly possible.

It is pretty much impossible to meaningfully determine who the speaker is/speakers are. It very much depends on how you read fr. 105. Warmington for example takes the two fragments as being connected and both spoken by Aegisthus, but there is really no way to tell. It could be tied to the trip to Delphi mentioned in fr.87, in which case (one of) the speaker(s) could be Orestes. There could also be another oracle involved, in which case the two speakers could be a messenger returning from the oracle and any other character asking them to explain, or in the case of a single speaker, anyone in reaction to the pronouncement a messenger has just cited at them. What the pronouncement in question entails exactly is also unclear, but most logical is to relate it to Orestes’ revenge on Aegisthus and Clytaemnestra in the climax of the tragedy.

lines.text: sed me⟨d⟩ incertat dictio :: quare? expedi!

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Because I will torment you with hunger, you, locked up in a dark enclosure, and, fatiguing you, I will tear your limbs apart with a rack
doc.differences:
D’Anna: 9 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 9/31)

Ribbeck: 31 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 31/32)

Schierl: 94 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 8/32)

Warmington: 131-132 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 10/34)

D’Anna, Schierl & Warmington: nam te in tenebrica saepe lacerabo fame

clausam et fatigans artus torto distraham

Ribbeck: am te in tenebrica saepe lacerabo fame

clausum et fatigans artus torto distraham
doc.apparatus:
tenebrica] tenebrica Scaliger : tenebricas codd.

clausam] clausam Jahn (1867, 233), pler. edd. : clausum codd., Diehl 53, Mette 97

Most editors agree on this fragment and the two conjectures. The first one is tenebrica, which has been changed from tenebricas by Scaliger, so that it becomes an adjective to saepe, which in turn will then mean ‘prison’ instead of ‘often’, what it would have been it we read saepe as an adverb.

The conjecture of clausam for clausum is not universally accepted. The codices have given us clausum, which has been conjectured by Jahn to clausam, because of the Electra of Sophocles and the Agamemnon of Seneca. In both these tragedies, there is a similar sentence about throwing someone in prison, and in both cases, it is about Electra, which is why Jahn has conjectured this. Diehl, Mette, and Ribbeck are the only editors who have chosen not to accept this conjecture.
doc.commentary:
There are at least two people on stage during this stage: the speaker and an addressee (te). We are not sure who either of them is, although the addressee does tend to be explained as Electra, but there is a slight problem with that explanation. Clausam has been conjectured by Jahn from clausum, which can be found in the codices, and it would change the addressee from a female person into a male. First, the explanation that goes with clausam and, because of that, Electra. This has been deduced from the tragedies about the same story that have survived, mainly the Electra by Sophocles and the Agamemnon by Seneca. The speaker would then be either Aegisthus or Clytemnestra.

It could also very well be that the conjecture is false, and that we have a male person who is imprisoned. This could then be Orestes, Pylades, or some other male character, still under the presumption that either Clytemnestra or Aegisthus is speaking. When we reject the conjecture, we could also take torto as a participle, with perhaps an implied tibi. This would, however, mean that Nonius has misunderstood this fragment, since he quotes this saying that torto is used instead of tormento, and tormento is only a noun, not a form of torqueo.

Unfortunately, Nonius doesn't provide us with enough information to say anything for certain. The only thing we can learn from his work, however, is why he quoted this fragment. This is because he wants to show that torto can be used instead of tormento. As we have seen above, he could be wrong in his comparison, but there is no evidence (that I have found) that indicates this.

lines.text: nam te in tenebrica saepe lacerabo fame
clausam et fatigans artus torto distraham
... in sedes conlocat se regias:
Clytemestra iuxtim, tertias natae occupant.
Matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas
Ioue patre prognatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus
Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus
Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Her peers call on Hymenaios, the air resounds with the blare of music
doc.apparatus:
aura] aula, Lindsay seq. Scaliger, Ribbeck

crepitu] fremitu dub. L. Müller
doc.commentary:
Aura

Though the Lindsay edition and Ribbeck printed aula, Petra Schierl opt for aura, rejecting Scaligers coniecture. Scaliger based his conjecture on a parallel in Euripides’ Iphigeneia in Tauris, suggesting that this passage might be an imitation or translation even of verses 365-368. This line of argumentation is followed by Ribbeck and Klotz. Schierl rejects this on differences in context: In Iphigeneia in Tauris Iphigeneia remembers the fake wedding her father Agamemnon organised, while in this fragment the wedding is real. The other difference is that here only Erigone’s peers are singing, versus the women of Argis and the mother in Euripides. Since we have very little information on the context of the fragment, it is hard to exclude the possibility that this verse in some way imitates those of Euripides, but that of a precise translation can safely be rejected.

Furthermore, Schierl mentions parallels for aura resonit in Verg. Aen. 4,668 and Ov. Met. 3.231. Nonius mentions this passage to illustrate that sonit equals sonat, and resono with a form of air (aether, aer) is well attested.

Fremunt fremere + accusative is rare. Usually it means something like ‘murmur’ ‘growl’ or even ‘rage’. When with accusative it is usually ‘complain about’ though it can also mean ‘demand angrily’. All the options are not very positive, yet Pacuvius uses it here with hymenaeus, wedding song, or Hymenaeus, God of weddings. That makes for two possible translations: either the girls angrily and loudly call for the God, or they murmur a bridal hymn. Both translations do not make a lot of sense, given the lack of context. Therefore both Schierl and Warmington choose to translate more neutrally and opt for ‘shout’ (Warmington) or ‘lassen erklingen’ (Schierl). Though Schierl opts for the girls singing a bridal hymn, she acknowledges that calling upon the god, might do the sentence construction more justice.
doc.reconstruction: This fragment concerns a wedding, and given the metre (iambic senarius) it is most likely from the prologue or, according to Schierl, from a conversation between Orestes, Pylades and indigenous people. The aequales, people of the same age, might refer to the chorus.

lines.text: <10>hymenaeum fremunt
aequales, aura resonit crepitu musico

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: we do not know the road, you know
doc.differences:
TRF: 25 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 9/24)

Ribbeck2: 36 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 10/24)

Ribbeck3: 33 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 10/24)

Warmington: 36 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

Marmorale: 10 (p.194) (Naevius’ Lycurgus 10/24)

Klotz: 35 (p.37) (Naevius’ Lycurgus 10/24)

TRF: ignotae iteris sumus, tute scis

Ribbeck3: ignotae <hic> iteris sumus <si> tute scis

Marmorale: ignoti iteris sumus, tute scis

Among the editors, there is consensus that this fragment belongs to Naevius’ Lycurgus. All editors place this fragment before the middle of the total fragments (this is not necessarily the middle of the tragedy) and they assume that it is about the Bacchants who are asking someone where to go. However, the fragments before and after this fragment differ in the editions, due to differences in reconstruction of the whole plot. See further the reconstruction. Besides, not all editors agree on the metre of this verse. Therefore, the texts printed are sometimes different, see also the reconstruction below. It is interesting that Warmington prints the same text as the TRF, but he adds that ignoti or maybe ignotei is possible when the chorus also consists of Satyrs. I will elaborate further on this in the fragment reconstruction.
doc.apparatus:
ad a Non. 180 Li.

ignotae] Non.[Keil] ed. Lindsay (Non.) Aldus, Mercerus, Klotz: ignoti codd. [Li] Stephanus, Marmorale : ignotae (-te) codd. [Mu, Wa, Marm, Tra] codd. plerique[Ri] : ignote G : ignoteis Mueller

ad b Non. 778 Li.

ignotae] ignotae (-te) codd. Li : ignoti codd. [Ri, Mu, Marm, Wa, On, Tra] Aldus, Onions, Stephanus, Marmorale : igniti Bamb.[Ri, Mu] : igoniti L1 : ignotis Mueller : ignotei Lindsay

ad c Pris. gramm. II 229, 1-5 Keil

ignotae] ignoti codd. [Klu, Ri, Li, Wa, Marm, Tra]

tute scis] tutescis B

tute scis] tute scis? Mueller, Lindsay, Ernout

There is a lot of discussion about the reading of ignotae . As you can see in the apparatus criticus, most manuscripts of the texts in which this fragment occurs, read ignoti, but most editions have ignotae. This has consequences for our interpretation of this fragment in the play. See further the commentary and the reconstruction.

There is one manuscript reading tutescis instead of tute scis. This seems not to be a problem. Besides, some editors place a question mark behind tute scis. On this last point, I will further elaborate in the fragment commentary.
doc.commentary:
ignotae] As already mentioned in the apparatus criticus, there is a lot of discussion about the reading of ignotae. The reading ignotae is based on one Nonius edition. Many other manuscripts have the reading ignoti or other related forms. The consequence of this different readings is the fact that ignotae certainly refers to a group of woman and ignoti and other forms to a group with at least one man. This issue has consequences for our reading of the whole fragment and will therefore be treated further in the reconstruction.

tute] The personal pronoun tu is reinforce here by the suffix te. This maybe says something about the urgency.
doc.reconstruction:
From the fact that sumus (and ignotae or ignoti, according to which reading you prefer) are plural and tu singular, we can deduce that the text of the fragment was spoken by a group to another person. The group does not know where they are and they assume that the other person knows the way.

A metrical analysis can help us to deduce from which part of the tragedy this verse comes. Lindsay has analysed this verse metrically and came to the conclusion that it must be from a choral part (Spaltenstein 2014, 459, 462) However, Spaltenstein does not agree with this. He argues that a jambic analysis of this metre is most probable (also Ribbeck 1897, Traglia 1986). Therefore, this verse is more suitable in a dialogue (Spaltenstein 2014, 459). I agree with him on this point. In my opinion, the contrast between sumus and tu fits more in a dialogue than in a choral song. This question of metre remains difficult. For instance, Marmorale finds the metre obscure. (Marmorale 1950, 194)

Most editors have proposed that the group consists of Bacchants arriving from a foreign country (Spaltenstein 2014, 460). This seems probable to me, because they do not know the way in a foreign land. Although we have no question mark, it is probable that this is a form of a question, as is also proposed by Mueller, Lindsay and Ernout. Namely, we can assume that they ask the way. Also, from the fact that scis is an indicative, we can deduce that it is a direct question (and not an indirect question, for which the subjunctive would have been used). Düntzer (1837) explains this verse by saying that the speaker repeats the question the bacchants has asked him in a direct style, like in a messenger story. However, according to Spaltenstein (2014, 460), it is a question which is directly asked on stage.

Since this verse is probably not from a choral song, it is important to think about who are the group of people and who is the speaker from this group. In a spoken part, namely, it cannot be the whole chorus. Spaltenstein suggests that Dionysus is the speaker. In his opinion, we can then read both ignotae and ignoti. When we read ignotae, Dionysus is speaking on behalf of the Bacchants and when we read ignoti, Dionysus is part of the group himself. But this is not the only reason on which we can base the reading ignoti. When the chorus consists of Bacchants and Satyrs, it is logical that the masculine form is used (Warmington 1936, 126). In Latin, namely, the masculine is already used when there is only one man in a group of women. But, according to Spaltenstein, the choice between ignotae and ignoti is in the end arbitrary, because it is mostly a problem for the editor. It does not matter for the public, because it is always about Bacchants (Spaltenstein 2014, 460-461). Marmorale seems to agree with Spaltenstein on this matter. He chooses for the masculine form ignoti, but refers in his commentary to the Bacchants (Marmorale 1950, 194). Bednarek finds the form ignoti more preferable than ignotae, by taking into consideration the fact that the feminine plural is based on the conjectures of editors (Bednarek 2021, 131). He also argues that the followers of Liber are longing for him (Bednarek 2021, 140).

Besides, it is interesting to think about who is addressed. Because the Bacchants are arriving in a foreign country, it is logical that they ask something to a person from that country. In addition, Ribbeck argues that this verse is aimed at Lycurgus or one of his followers. So the chorus can ask the way further or the way to the king himself. However, he also suggests the option that some of the Bacchants ask the way to Dionysus, but he finds the first suggestion more plausible (Ribbeck 1875, 59; cf. Spaltenstein 2014, 461-462). This last option is also proposed by Bednarek (Bednarek 2021, 140).

Although the interpretation of Bacchus speaking to one of Lycurgus’ followers is the most plausible in my opinion, there are also other interpretations possible. First, some have argued that the Bacchants are complaining to Dionysus, because Dionysus encouraged them to go. However, this seems illogical to Spaltenstein because Dionysus comes to a foreign country and does not know the places (Spaltenstein 2014, 461). Second, Bertini and Bednarek have argued that the chorus consists of Lycurgus’ followers and that the speaker of the verse is one of Lycurgus’ attendants. They could be part of the strategy to imprison Liber. When they were sent by Lycurgus to a place in the mountains, they could pretend that they did not know and ask him to lead the expedition (Bertini 1972; cf. Bednarek 2021, 140). Bednarek admits that this is speculative, but he takes it into consideration. When we take this option, the reading ignoti is easily defendable, because Lycurgus’ followers are men. He also proposes a counterargument against the most common interpretation: the idea that followers of Liber ask Lycurgus for help is weird, especially when they are in prison. This fits in his overall reconstruction of the play, in which he argues that the maenads are imprisoned in the beginning of the play. (Bednarek 2021, 140; 155).

Bibliography

Bednarek, B. 2021. The Myth of Lycurgus in Aeschylus, Naevius, and Beyond. Leiden, Boston.

Bertini, F. 1972. ‘Naev. Lycurg. 33 Ribb. = 35-36 Klotz = 10 Marmorale (ovvero: Un falso problema di tradizione manoscritta’, ALGP 9-10, 111-120.

Klotz, A. 1953. Scaenicorum Romanorum fragmenta: Volumen primus: Tragicorum fragments. Münich.

Marmorale, E. (ed./comm.) 1950. Naevius Poeta. Florence.

Ribbeck, O. 1872. Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (2nd. ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. 1875. Die Römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (ed.) 1897. Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (3rd ed.). Leipzig.

Schauer, M. (ed.) 2012. Tragicorum Romanorum Fragmenta, volume I: Livius Andronicus, Naevius, Tragici Minores, Fragmenta Adespota. Göttingen.

Spaltenstein, F. 2014. Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels.

Traglia, A. 1986. Poeti latini arcaici: Volume primo. Livio Andronicu: Nevio: Ennio. Turin.

Warmington, E.H. (ed./transl.) 1936. Remains of Old Latin, volume II: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Pacuvius. Accius. Cambridge MA/London.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius explains that ignotae is used here instead of insciae.

Ignotae id est insciae. Naevius Lycurgo: ignotae iteris sumus, tute scis (Non. 180 Li.)

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
The word iteris is used here instead of itineris. Nonius quotes the example from Naevius’ Lycurgus first, and then he gives more examples of the same phenomenon.

Iteris positium pro itineris. et quidem recte, ab eo quod iter, iteris facit; itiner, itineris. Naevius Lycurgo: ignotae iteris sumus, tute scis . (Non. 778 Li.)

context.author: Priscian
context.text:
Priscian quotes this line from Naevius’ Lycurgus in a discussion about the genitive. In comparison to other forms, it is logical, according to Priscian, that we found both iter, iteris and itiner, itineris.

Antiqui et hoc iter iteris et hoc itiner itineris dixerunt … Naevius in Lycurgo : ignotae iteris sumus, tute scis. (Prisc. gramm. II 229, 1-5)

lines.text: ignotae iteris sumus, tute scis

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

lines.text: mulier una duum virum

doc.translation: One wife of/for two husbands
doc.differences:
TRF: 407 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 656 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 34 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 657 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 656 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 701 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

Of husbands two, one woman.

Franchella:

mulier una duum uirorum.

Una sola donna di due uomini.

D'Antò:

mulier una duum uirum.

Una donna sola per due uomini.

Dangel:

une seule femme pour deux maris
doc.apparatus:
duum uirum] duum uirum codd.: duum uirorum Ribbeck1-2

duum] duum LAμ.: apud duum BNQ: duom Heerdegen 1884

In his first and second editions, Ribbeck prints his own conjecture, duum uirorum, in which changes the paradosis uirum, archaic form of the genitive plural, to uirorum, the uncontracted form of the genitive plural that was more common in Cicero’s day. Ribbeck presumably thought that a scribe had mistakenly written uirum, the archaic form of the genitive plural of uir, instead of uirorum, the uncontracted genitive form, likely because of the similarity of the form uirum with the immediately preceding word duum. Warmington prints Ribbeck’s uirorum, without however mentioning in his critical apparatus that the reading is a conjecture by Ribbeck. In his third edition, Ribbeck restores the transmitted reading uirum and, further, adopts the emendation of duom (archaic form of duum) proposed by Heerdegen in his edition of the Orator in 1884.

The critical apparatus above is based mainly on that of Westman in his edition of Cicero’s Orator (20022, 50). For a full list of manuscripts and sigla, see Westman 20022, viii-xvii.
doc.commentary: duum uirum: like duorum corporum at F 406 TRF, a genitive of possession (Woodcock §72). Translated either as ‘of two husbands’ (Warmington, Franchella) or ‘for two husbands’ (D’Antò, Dangel).
doc.reconstruction:
Bergk (1884-1886, I.372) combined this fragment with Adesp. F 84 TRF, a fragment of uncertain authorship preserves in Cicero’s Ad familiares (Cic. Fam. 9.22.1, usurpat duplex cubile), so as to produce the following two-line fragment: quae mulier una duum virum | usurpat duplex cubile. Ribbeck (1875, 462) suggests that this line was spoken by Electra to Clytemnestra, the daughter chastising the mother for the latter’s infidelity. On Warmington’s reconstruction, see the 'Fragment reconstruction' section of Accius inc. fab. F 406 TRF.

Dangel 378 considers this fragment, as well as Accius inc. fab. F 406 TRF, to belong to one of the plays of Accius’ so-called ‘Trojan Cycle’ or ‘Atrid (Pelopid?) Cycle’.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
This fragment is quoted, alongside Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (video sepulcra dua duorum corporum), at Cic. Orat. 156, in a section of the treatise in which the phenomena of hiatus, elision, and related sonic effects are discussed (Orat. 149-64: cf. Kaster 2020, 213-23).

At Orat. 155-6, Cicero presents the view of some contemporary critics who believe that forms such as the archaic genitive plural deum, the contracted form of deorum which was more in use in Cicero’s day, should not be used and that the uncontracted form should be preferred. Cicero cites several examples from Republican tragedy in which the contracted form is used, among which notably our two fragments of Accius (see above) and Fragmenta ex certis incertorum tragoediis F 1.1-3 TRF = Pacuvius Chryses F 76 Schierl. The fragments of Accius are quoted to show that the poet uses the genitive plural forms duorum and duum indifferently.

doc.metrical_analysis: As printed by the TRF edition (i.e. quae mulier una | usurpat duplex cubile), the metre would seem to be the trochaic octonarius (Raven §53B) because in this metre the penultimate syllable of the line is heavy (ūna). In his first edition, Ribbeck includes a note about the metre of this fragment (Ribbeck1, 216) in which he suggests that, if the text is read as a single line (quae mulier una usurpat duplex cubile), it could be the final part of an iambic septenarius (Raven §44B). Ribbeck notes, however, that there is no certain attestation of this metre being used in tragedy, comparing the dubious examples at Enn. inc. F 205 TRF = 346 Jocelyn (aliquot somnia uera…sed omnia non necesse est, = Ennius inc. fab. F LVI, v. 401, Ribbeck1) and at Accius inc. fab. F 435 (cum illud oculi⟨s⟩ violave⟨rat⟩ [de Melo: oblivo lavat F] qui invidit [de Melo: incidit F] invidendum = Acc. inc. fab. 697, F XXXV Ribbeck1 = Varro, L.L. 6.80): see De Melo II.880 on the latter fragment, and Moore 2012, 184: ‘the iambic septenarius appears to have had a special association with comedy: its Greek equivalent, the iambic tetrameter catalectic, is never used in extant tragedy’. If Bergk’s combination of this fragment with Accius inc. fab. F 407 (see ‘Fragment reconstruction’) were accepted, i.e. quae mulier una duum uirum | usurpat duplex cubile…, then the metre would be the trochaic septenarius.

lines.text: <15> crispisulcans igneum
Fulmen

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: It is not right to wear your soul down by anxiety in an uncertain situation.
doc.differences:
Schierl: 99 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 13/32)

Ribbeck2: 128 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 9/32)

Ribbeck3: 128 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 9/32)

D’Anna: 162 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 12/31)

Warmington: 125 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 5/34)

All editors accept the conjecture of Scaliger and present the same text as in Schierl’s edition. They also agree that the fragment should be placed around the beginning of the play. In most cases, it is preceded or followed by the fragment 98 (Schierl): nonne officium fungar vulgi atque aegre malefactum feram?, because the editors assume a similar context, perhaps Electra defending her right to be critical and complaining to someone about her arranged marriage with Oeax or about the murder of Agamemnon.
doc.apparatus:
decet] Scaliger et coni. Lachmannus: vetet ω et Lindsay : volet coni. Ribbeck3 in app.

aegritudine] ed. 1476 : aegritudinem ω

In the manuscript tradition as well as Lindsay’s edition, we encounter the word vetet. However, Scaliger reads decet instead which is also proposed by Lachmannus. This conjecture is accepted by most editors and is adopted in Schierl’s text. Ribbeck3 introduces a tentative suggestion with volet in his apparatus criticus. Nevertheless, the idea of what is right/proper for somebody to do and what is not in the context of an uncertain situation is probably described best by decet which seems to be more compatible with the advisory character of the fragment (see further in the reconstruction).
doc.commentary:
aegritudine] aegritudo refers to a sentimental mood, probably sadness or worry. The word is used with similar meaning in Pacuvius’ Atalanta, frg. 43 (75 Warmington): Quae aegritudo insolens mentem attemptat tuam?

re crepera] Nonius quotes the fragment for the rare adjective crepera, which means dark, dusky and is used here for an uncertain, doubtful thing/situation. Schierl points out that the word is first attested in Pacuvius in the specific fragment and then is used with similar meaning in Accius’ Phoenissae (601 Ribbeck3 = 575 Dangel): quae ego cuncta esse fluxa in mea re crepera/ comperi., in Accius’ Telephus (628f. Ribbeck3 = 96f. Dangel): Nunc tu in re crepera tua quid capias consili / vide. and in Lucilius’ Satires (Book V, 198-9 Warmington): Fannius solus mihi in magno maerore / tristitia in summa, crepera re inventus salutis.
doc.reconstruction:
The line is attributed to Pacuvius’ Dulorestes by Nonius who quotes it. It is written in trochaic septenar, a metre that we encounter regularly in Latin comedy and, in particular, in the works of Plautus and Terentius. Trochaic septenar is also commonly used in dialogues, thus we can assume that here there are at least two persons on stage who are involved in a conversation and one of them utters the specific verse. It has been proposed that Orestes is the speaker (cf. D'Anna 205; on Orestes as speaker: Ribbeck 245, Argenio 32, Mette 96) and the addressee is Electra, who is advised to retain her steadfastness in an uncertain situation. Warmington disagrees and places Clytaemnestra as the speaker in a possible discussion that she has with Electra. The line may refer to the dispute between them concerning the marriage of Electra with Oeax. We know that she didn’t want to marry him, so Clytaemnestra’s statement here probably shows her disagreement over this by revealing her distaste for obscuring the psyche with confusion. However, Schierl highlights that we are dealing with a call to persevere, that implies an exhortation (rather than a severe warning), therefore Warmington’s speculation may be wrong. The line could be also uttered by a minor character, for instance the corypheus of the chorus. The addressee seems to be Electra but we cannot be certain.

Bibliography

D’Anna, I. (1967). M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Ribbeck, O. (1871). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (2nd. ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (3rd ed.). Leipzig.

Schierl, P. (2006). Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin, New York.

Warmington, E.H. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Vol. II. Cambridge MA, London.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text:
Nonius quotes this line from Pacuvius’ Dulorestes in his dictionary De compendiosa doctrina while describing the rare adjective crepera as a term used for a doubtful, uncertain matter. He explains that the similar word crepuluscum is used for the dusk, the twilight and decrepiti is used for the elderly, decrepit men, meaning that they belong to the dusk of life. “Creperum” goes with the noun bellum, meaning the doubtful war. Nonius refers to the authors in which we can find this unusual term and among Lucretius, Accius, Lucilius, Plautus and Varo, he mentions Pacuvius that uses it in his tragedy Dulorestes:

'Crepera' res proprie dicitur dubia: unde et 'crepusculum' dicitur lux dubia et senes 'decrepiti' dicti, in dubio vitae constituti; 'creperum' bellum, anceps et dubium. Lucretius ... Accius Phoenissis ... idem Telepho ... Lucilius ... Pacuvius Duloreste: non decet animum aegritudine in re crepera confici. Plautus ... Varro ... (Non. 20 L. = 13,11 M.)

lines.text: non decet animum aegritudine in re crepera confici

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: egone Argivum imperium adtingam aut Pelopis digner domo?
quo me ostendam? quod templum adeam? qu[a]em ore funesto adloquar?

doc.translation: Could I attain the throne of Argos or be worthy of the house of Pelops? Where should I turn? What temple should I approach? Whom should I address with my deadly mouth?
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 17 (17/20), 231-232:

231: Egone Argiuum imperium attingam aut Pelopia digner domo?

232: Quoi me ostendam? quod templum adeam? quem ore funesto alloquar?

Ribbeck1-2:

Fragment 17 (17/20), 231-232:

231: Egone Argiuum imperium attingam aut Pelopia digner domo?

232: Quo me ostendam? quod templum adeam? quem ore funesto alloquar?

Warmington:

Fragment 194-5 (15/17):

[THYESTES]:

194: Egone Argivum imperium attingam aut Pelopia digner domo?

195: Quo me ostendam? Quod templum adeam? Quem ore funesto adloquar?

Could I – could I so much as lay a hand

On sway imperial o’er the Argives, be

Held worthy of the house of Pelops? Where

May I reveal myself? What shrine may I

Approach? Whom may my mournful mouth address?

Franchella:

Fragment 18 (18/22), 382-383:

382: egone Argiuum imperium adtingam? aut Pelopis digner domo?

383: quoi me ostendam? quod templum adeam? quem ore funesto adloquar?

Potrò io toccare il regno di Argo? o essere stimato nella casa di Pelope? a chi mi presenterò? in quale tempio andrò? Con chi parlerò con l’empia bocca?

D’Antò:

Fragment 15 (15/18), 231-232:

231: egone Argivum imperium adtingam: aut Pelopis digner domo?

232: Quo me ostendam? quod templum adeam? quem ore funesto adloquar?

posso io avere il comando degli Argivi, oppure esser reputato degno della casa di Pelope? Dove mi presenterò? Quale tempio avvicinerò? A chi con questa bocca empia rivolgerò la parola?

Dangel:

Fragment 20 (20/21), 65-66:

65: Egone Argiuum imperium adtingam aut Pelopia digner domo ? 

66: Quo me ostendam ? Quod templum adeam ? Quem ore funesto adloquar ?

Moi j’atteindrais à la souveraienté sur les Argiens ou je serais jugé digne de la maison de Pélops ? Mais où me montrer ? Quel temple approcher ? À qui m’adresser avec ma bouche funeste ?
doc.apparatus:
1: Pelopis ] Pelopis codd.: Pelopia Douza Iunior apud Delrium 1593, I.178

2: quo ] quo codd.: cui Welcker 1839-1841, I.364(?)

2: quem ore ] quem ore Agustìn MS uel Lipsius 1585, 120: quaemore uel quamone codd.:  quoi ore Lipsius 1585, 120: quae more Giacomozzi apud Gatti–Mazzacane–Salvadori II.1.97

3: adloquar ] adloquar Mercier, iam Agustìn MS: conloquar uel colloquar codd.

Janus Douza (the Younger’s) conjecture Pelopia introduces an adjectival form of the name of Pelops in the place of the transmitted proper noun in the genitive (Pelopia…domo = ‘the Pelopian house’, literally). Douza seems to have been influenced by the occurrence of the same expression at Sen. Ag. 7 Pelopiae…domus (lit. ‘the Pelopian house’, cf. too 124 Pelopia…sceptra ‘the Pelopian sceptres’, a line omitted in the A branch of the manuscript tradition). So far as I can see, these are the only two occurrences of the adjective Pelopius, and, on that basis, it would be rash to introduce it by means of conjectural emendation here. The reading quem ore (‘whom with my mouth’) was restored on the basis of the corrupt paradosis independently by A. Agustìn (1517-1586) in his unpublished edition of Latin literary fragments (see ‘Apparatus criticus’ of Accius Clytemnestra F 143 TRF) and by Justus Lipsius (1585, 120). The unmetrical colloquar was emended to adloquar, the first vowel of which elides into preceding funesto, by Mercier in his notes (1826 edition, p. 722), having been anticipated by Agustìn. Giacomozzi’s quae more requires construing more with funesto (more funesto ‘in a dismal manner’) and construing quae as object of adloquar with no specified addressee (quae…adloquar ‘what things shall I say?’). While palaeographically plausible, this suggestion has the disadvantage of removing the striking image of the speaker’s ‘deadly mouth’ (ore funesto). There is no reason to go against the manuscript evidence and follow Welcker in reading cui at the beginning of the second line (cui me ostendam = ‘to whom shall I show (myself)’), and Del Rio in reading cui (or quoi) ore (‘to whom with my mouth…’).
doc.commentary:
1-2: Accius is alluding (cf. Lana 1959, 309-10 n. 4) to a fragment traditionally attributed, after Columna (1590, 17; 21), to Ennius’ Medea (F Adesp. 25 TRF = Ennius Medea exul v. 231-232 Ribbeck3 = Ennius Medea v. 217-218 Jocelyn: quo nunc me vortam? quod iter incipiam ingredi? | domum paternamne? anne ad Peliae filias?). The Ennian fragment is modelled on lines of Euripides’ Medea which were extremely famous (Eur. Med. 502-4): νῦν ποῖ τράπωμαι; πότερα πρὸς πατρὸς δόμους, | οὓς σοὶ προδοῦσα καὶ πάτραν ἀφικόμην; | ἢ πρὸς ταλαίνας Πελιάδας;, ‘where am I now to turn? To my father’s house, which like my country I betrayed for your sake when I came here? Or to the wretched daughters of Pelias?’, transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, I.327). Bilinski (1958, 43 n. 80) suggests that the politician and orator C. Sempronius Gracchus (RE ‘Sempronius 47’) alluded to this fragment of Accius in a speech delivered probably during the final days of his life in 121 BCE: quo miser me conferam? quo vertam? in Capitoliumne? at fratris sanguine redundat. an domum? matremne ut miseram lamentantem videam et abiectam? (F 61 ORF4 = Cic. De orat. 3.214), though others argue that C. Gracchus is here imitating the Ennian fragment (Bonnet 1906, 44-5; Zillinger 1911, 66). In any case, C. Gracchus here references the slaughter of his brother Tiberius Sempronius Gracchus (RE ‘Sempronius 540’) on the Capitol amidst discontent at agrarian reforms which he attempted to implement during his tenure tribune of the plebs in 133 BCE (MRR I.433-4). C. Gracchus would soon be murdered himself, after using his special role as triumuir coloniis deducendis, to which he was appointed following two years as tribune of the plebs, to try to grant more rights to the Latins and to Italian allies (MRR I.520-3).

1 adtingam: interpreted by OLD as meaning ‘to attain (a position of distinction, etc.’, s.v. 6c), which seems preferable than Bickel’s interpretation of the word in the physical sense of ‘to arrive’, i.e. peruenire in imperium (apud TLL II.1145.21-4).
doc.reconstruction: Welcker (1839-1841, II.684) suggested that Thyestes is the speaker and that the character displays ‘dasselbe Gefühl’ as in Adesp. F 22 TRF (quidnam est, obsecro, quid te adiri abnutas?, = Ennius Thyestes v. 306 Ribbeck3 = 352 Vahlen2 = 361 Warmington = 290 Jocelyn). Ribbeck (1875, 454) assigns that this fragment to a canticum sung by Thyestes which follows on almost immediately from Atreus’ perverse revelation of the truth (F 103.5 TRF, natis sepulcro ipse es parens), with Thyestes responding that Atreus has broken fides. A horrified and mournful Thyestes begins his canticum, which contains F 104.1-2 TRF (ipsus hortatur me frater, ut meos malis miser | manderem natos), F 109 TRF (egone Argivum imperium adtingam aut Pelopis digner domo? | quo me ostendam? quod templum adeam? qu[a]em ore funesto adloquar?), and Adesp. 83 TRF (ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam). Lana (1959, 309) argues that none of the surviving fragments thought to belong to this scene contains a lamentation by Thyestes for his dead children (cf. F 104.1-2 TRF: ipsus hortatur me frater…, and F 106 TRF, fregisti fidem…). He deduces that Thyestes was depicted by Accius as being more troubled by the means with which Atreus took revenge than by the death of his children. But such an extreme interpretation is surely unwarranted: it cannot be ruled out that the play did contain a lamentation by Thyestes, but that it is not preserved among the fragments that have come down to us. Zwierlein (1983, 122) argues that this fragment makes Lana’s interpretation of the end of the play problematic. According to Lana, Thyestes rallied the citizens of Mycenae against Atreus and had him put to death. But Zwierlein objects that the picture of Thyestes that emerges from this fragment (assuming of course Thyestes is the speaker) is that of a broken man who is excluded from the human community (‘der sich…aus der menschlichen Gemeinschaft ausgeschlossen fühlt’, p. 122). Thyestes’ state of mind makes it implausible, according to Zwierlein, that he could almost immediately lead a rebellion against his brother. Zwierlein sees Thyestes’ broken spirit as similar to the attitude of the speaker of F Adesp. 56 (nolite hospites ad me adire, ilico istic! | ne contagio mea bonis umbrave obsit! | <meo> tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret!, ‘Do not, strangers, come close to me, stay in that spot! Let not my touch or my shadow harm good people. So great is the power of the crime which clings to my body!’), a fragment has often been attributed to Ennius’ Thyestes with the speaker thought to be Thyestes himself (cf. Ennius Thyestes v. 303-305 Ribbeck3 = 293-295 Jocelyn), and also to that of Seneca’s Hercules after killing his wife and children at the end of Hercules furens: 1318 dextra contactus pios | scelerata refugit, ‘My crime-stained shuns contact with one I love’ (transl. Fitch 20182, I.125) and 1321-41, in which Hercules struggles to think of an appropriate place where he can go as an exile. Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 275) goes so far as to suggest that this fragment actually represents the final lines of the play. While most will probably find this idea unconvincing, she does make the good point that it is arbitrary to assume that the play in its ending depicted either the death of Thyestes or the downfall of Atreus simply because references to these events can be found in some ancient literary and mythographical accounts of the story.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius De compendiosa doctrina p. 281.1-13 Mercier = 432-433.1-13 Lindsay:

DIGNATVS significat dignus habitus. Vergilius: ‘coniugio, Anchisa, Veneris dignate superbo.’ (= Aen. 3.475) Accius in Neoptolemo: ‘sed quem mihi iungent? cui, quae cum illo fuerim, dignabor dari?’ (= Accius Neoptolemus F 296 TRF) idem in Atreo: ‘egone Argivum imperium adtingam? Aut Pelopis digner domo? | quoi me ostendam? quod templum adeam? quem ore funesto adloquar?’ (= Accius Atreus F 109 TRF) Cicero in Oratore: ‘quamquam enim omnis locutio oratio est, tamen unius oratoris locutio hoc proprio dignata nomine est.’ (= Cic. Orat. 64) – idem de Oratore lib. III [tamen–oratore lib. III om. (propter homoeotel.)] ‘quae tamen consimili laude dignantur.’ (= Cic. De orat. 3.25)

DIGNATUS means ‘to be considered worthy’. Vergil: ‘you have been deemed worthy, Anchises, of a distinguished marriage with Venus.’ (= Aen. 3.475) Accius in Neoptolemus: ‘But to whom will they wed me? To whom will they deem me worthy of being given, I who was his wife?’ (= Accius Neoptolemus F 296 TRF). The same in Atreus: ‘Could I attain the throne of Argos or be worthy of the house of Pelops? Where should I turn? What temple should I approach? Whom should I address with my deadly mouth?’ (= Accius Atreus F 109 TRF) Cicero in the Orator: ‘for although all utterance is speech, only the utterance of orators is deemed worthy of a label all its own’. The same in De Oratore Book 3: ‘but are nevertheless equally highly valued’.

This fragment is preserved in Book 4 of Nonius’ De compendiosa doctrina, which is entitled ‘De varia significatione verborum’ (‘On the various meanings of words’). It is quoted to illustrate the use of the word dignatus in the sense of ‘having been considered worthy’. The form dignatus serves as the past passive participle of the active verb digno as well as of the deponent verb dignor, which, according to Bögel 1912 (apud TLL V.1.1140.84-1141.44), is attested from Catullus onwards (cf. 64.407 nec talis dignantur uisere coetus [dei]). In all the quotations listed by Nonius contain a form of the passive verb dignor constructed with the ablative, or, in the case of the quotation from Accius’ Neoptolemus, with a verb in the passive infinitive (dignabor dari, lit. ‘considered worthy of being given’). Lindsay (1901, 69) identifies the source from which Nonius takes the quotations listed under the lemma ‘dignatus’ as ‘Accius ii’, which he refers to as source 8 (‘a second volume of Accius, containing (more or less in this order): Epigoni, Meleager, Aeneadae aut Decius, Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi, Athamas, Clytaemestra, Bacchae, Neoptolemus, Erigona, Nyctegresia, Andromeda, Atreus, Phinidae, Agamemnonidae’, p. 8).

doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarius. Soubiran (apud Dangel 1995, 283) observes that the reading Pelopia is preferable to Douza’s Pelopis because with Pelopia there is no hiatus at the point of the diaeresis (i.e. the word-division at the end foot or metron, Raven §18), which would violate Meyer’s law (according to which a spondee at the end of an iambic metron, or a spondee ending the third element of a trochaic metron, is avoided: cf. Meyer 1886 (though Gratwick ‘Meyer’s Law’, CR 41 (1991), 381-4 at 383 suggests the law might more fairly be called Bentley’s or Bothe’s) and Questa 2007, 383-413).

lines.text: Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi !
<10> Nam ut Pergama
accensa et praeda per participes aequiter
partita est,
Tantalo prognatus Pelope natus qui quondam a socru
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctus nuptiis,
Nolite hospites ad me adire, ilico istic!
Ne contagio mea bonis umbrave obsit.
Meo tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret!

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: When radiant white was already melting in the heat of the sun
doc.differences:
TrRF: 35 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 19/24)

Klotz: 48 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 22/24)

Marmorale: p. 198 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 22/24)

Ribbeck2: 51 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 22/24)

Ribbeck3: 38 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 22/24)

Warmington: 59 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 24/24)

iam solis aestu candor cum liquesceret

Marmorale: iam solis aestu candor cui liquesceret

Warmington: iam solis aestu candor quum liquesceret

Most editors print the fragment as one of the last ones. It is difficult to place in the context of the pay as there are no decisive indications of who the speaker may be or the context that necessitates a description like this. Naturally, the TrRF, where the fragments are arranged according to the order in which they are cited by other authors, places this fragment a little earlier than other editions, making it only the nineteenth out of twenty-four Lycurgus fragments. Note also that Warmington counts the fragments in his edition per line instead of per fragment.
doc.apparatus:
solis] solis ed. Lindsay (Non.) : soli α B Gen. H (Non.)

aestu] aestu codd. : axe coni. Bothe (1837)

candor] calor B Basil. : corruptum, fort. caldo Mueller propos. crit. in app.

cum] cum vel quum ed. 1480 : cui codd.

candor cum liquesceret] caro colliquesceret coni. Gerlach et Roth

The most significant textual problems for this fragment are to do with what the TrRF reads as candor and cum.

With regards to candor: the reading calor, as in B and Basil. is difficult to make sense of within the context of the line. candor is the most readily accepted reading, though interpretations of its meaning vary between editors. Gerlach and Roth’s conjecture is worth noting as an attempt to interpret the line as being about a decaying body, and make that more explicit in the text.

With regards to cum: all manuscripts read cui. The conjecture cum (or quum according to some editors, notably Warmington, who prints it as such, and Klotz, who does not) originates in the edition of 1480, and is now the most readily accepted reading. Marmorale however complains that the context of the line is too uncertain to accept this conjecture, and upholds cui.
doc.commentary:
solis] generally taken with the subsequent aestu. Ribbeck (1875, 61) however takes it with candor, so the line refers to the cloudy midday heat being quelled by the bright sun. Spaltenstein (2014, 517) rightfully objects that with this interpretation the meaning of liquesceret would be incompatible with the definition Nonius cites it to support.

candor] designates a bright whiteness, or just a glow or glimmer. This is most often taken to mean either snow/frost or something else pliant and able to melt. Marmorale (1950, 198) for example compares it to wax.

cum] leans towards the line being a scenery descriptor, as opposed to mss. cui.
doc.reconstruction:
Reconstruction of the place of this fragment within the whole of the play is difficult, as there is very little concrete information about its context within the line itself. As such, interpretations of this fragment vary wildly.

First is the interpretation mentioned (and thought less likely) by Marmorale (1950, 198): that the fragment simply describes a bright day. This interpretation of the fragment as a marker of time is supported by Klussman (1843, 110-111), who explains it as describing the afternoon or evening. He proposes that the words are spoken by a messenger, one of the guards sent by Lycurgus to protect the borders, who has returned to report to Lycurgus on the capture of Dionysus and his followers. Spaltenstein (2014, 516) similarly explains that the line could describe the thawing frost in the morning before the guards capture and imprison the Bacchants.

Connected to the interpretation of the fragment as a marker of the time of day is the interpretation taken by Düntzer (1837, 441), that candor refers to snow or ice which is melting away under the heat of the sun. Warmington too understands candor this way, translating the line as “When now the brilliant white of snow was melting in the sun’s scorching heat.” Following this interpretation, the fragment can still be a marker of time, but also one of place, as the snow could refer specifically to the high mountains of Thrace. Marmorale (1950, 198) too thinks that candor most likely refers to a substance that can actually melt. He translates the line as, “whose whiteness liquefied by the heat of the sun” and gives the example of wax. (Note that Marmorale reads cui for TrRF cum, thus translating “whose” instead of “when”.)

The proposed conjecture caro colliquesceret for candor cum liquesceret by Gerlach and Roth (1842, 229), implies the interpretation of the line as being about a decaying corpse, making it explicitly about flesh seemingly melting in the sun’s heat. If the fragment is indeed a description of a decaying corpse, placing it towards the end of the tragedy and close to the climax of the story (that is, Dionysus’ revenge on Lycurgus) would make sense. In that case, the speaker could be a messenger reporting on the death of Lycurgus’s wife and/or daughter, or the gruesome death of Lycurgus himself, who, according to Hyginus (Hyg. Fab. 132), was driven mad by Dionysus and fed to the panthers, and who possibly amputated his own leg after mistaking it for a grapevine.

Bibliography

Bothe, F. H., (1837). Emendationes Nonianae. Rheinisches Museum Für Philologie, 5, 250-300.

Düntzer, H.,(1837). De Naevii Lycurgo et Ennii Iphigenia. Rheinisches Museum Für Philologie, 5, 433-446.

Hyginus & Marshall, P. K. (ed.), (2016). Fabulae. Leipzig.

Klotz, A., Seel, O. & Voit, L., (1953). Tragicorum fragmenta (Scaenicorum Romanorum fragmenta ; vol. 1). Munich.

Klußmann, E., (1843). Cn. Naevii poetae romani vitam descripsit, carminum reliquias collegit, poesis rationem exposuit. Gent.

Naevius & Marmorale, E. V. (ed.), (1950). Naevius Poeta : Introduzione biobibliografica, testo dei frammenti e comment. \ Firenze.

Nonius, Fulgentius & Planciades, Gerlach, F.D., & Roth, K. L., (eds.), (1842). Nonii Marcelli Peripatetici Tuburticencis De compendiosa doctrina per litteras ad filium. Fabii Planciadis Fulgentii Expositio sermonum antiquorum. Basel.

Ribbeck, O., (1871). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 2). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1875). Die Römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O., (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 3). Leipzig.

Schauer, M., Siegl, O., & Hollmann, E. (2012). Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta: Vol. I: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Tragici Minores. Fragmenta Adespota. Göttingen.

Spaltenstein, F. (2014). Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels.

Ennius et al, & Warmington, E. H. (ed.) (2015). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge, MA.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted once by Nonius in relation to the meaning of liquidum, this being “soft and flowing”. He first cites both Plautus’ Milite and Casina and Varro’s Divinarum Rerum, before providing the line from Naevius Lycurgus:

liquidum rursum significat molle et fluxum. . . . Naevius Lycurgo: iam solis aestu candor cum liquesceret. (Non. 334, 24-37)

lines.text: iam solis aestu candor cum liquesceret
Erras erras, nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi
repriment validae legum habenae atque imperii insistent iugo.
Video sepulcra dua duorum corporum.
... ut quae tum absentem rebus dubiis coniugem
tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem
Ipse summis saxis fixus asperis evisceratus
Latere pendens saxa spargens tabo sanie et sanguine atro. | [...]
Neque sepulchrum quo recipiat habeat portum corporis,
Ubi remissa humana uita corpus requiescat malis.
<10> quae ego incipio conata exequar.
Quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus
paretur aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili
poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici ?
perque nostram egregiam unanimitatem quam memoria deiugat
cives, antiqui amici maiorum meum,
consilium socii, augurium atque extum interpretes,
postquam prodigium horriferum portentum pavos
...Pol mihi fortuna magis nunc defit quam genus.
Namque regnum suppetebat mi, ut scias quanto e loco.
Quantis opibus quibus de rebus lapsa fortuna accidat.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Incertum ex comoediis
doc.translation: We shall speak in an open tongue at the time of the games of Liber.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck 112 (Naevius, incert. fab. 5/27)

Warmington 27 (Naevius, ex com.27/32 )

Marmorale 5 (Naevius, incert. fab. frag. 5/30)

The fact that this is from a play which is incertum makes it difficult for editors to place.
doc.apparatus:
loquemur] loquemur edd , loquimur codd.

The manuscripts all indicate loquimur , which has been corrected to loquemur by editors for metrical reasons (the short -qui- does not fit in with the rest of the trochaic septenarius, while the long -que- does).
doc.commentary:
Libera ] The most obvious literary effect in this fragment is the heavy l-alliteration in every word of the line, which is reminiscent of Ennius’ famous O Tite Tati Tuti… . For Marmorale, this is a splendidly elegant line which borders on perfection, and emphasises the loose, liberal and revolutionary attitude of the author (1950, pp.44, 227). Spaltenstein believes this to be an exaggeration (2014, p.606), although one may note the playful tone of the line which is indeed accentuated by the alliteration. Further, the near-chiastic nature of the line, with both the initial and final words starting with Liber- , can be said to emphasise the importance the speaker grants to frank and free speech. The metre is the trochaic septenarius, which was regularly employed in Latin comedy, in particular in the works of Plautus and Terence. The diaresis can be found between the words loquemur and ludis .

ludis ] One question is whether the ludis Liberalibus which Naevius refers to can be entirely conflated with the Dionysia , which Festus appears to be doing in his work. The Roman god Liber would come to be conflated with Dionysus (and therefore Bacchus), though he was initially a god in his own right. As Naevius was known for his adapting Greek models in Roman contexts, it is not a stretch of the imagination to take ludis Liberalibus as meaning the Dionysia .
doc.reconstruction:
It is generally agreed that the plural loquemur is a majestic plural and refers to a single speaker, notably on account of the singular libera lingua .
