The debate, as summarised by Spaltenstein (2014, pp.603-4), centres around the identity of the speaker, which is either that of a slave who is discussing adopting a franker attitude towards their master during the games of Liber, when the latter is inebriated, or that of Naevius himself, who would be in some shape or form informing an audience that he’ll be adopting a candid attitude (for which he was seemingly known, notably in a political sense) at the next games of Liber.

The former explanation roots the fragment in a comic setting, and along with the playful tone of the alliterated passage would seem to further support this being a fragment from a comedy rather than from a tragedy, even though the latter genre was known to sometimes feature comic elements (Slater, 2015), though this is not conclusive.

To Spaltenstein’s mind the slave-master scheme is likelier than the political declaration one, as it was a common trope in comedy that old abusive masters should need some kind of frank challenging, and there are cases in which a slave addresses their drunken master more openly (Spaltenstein 2014, pp.603-4). The more political explanation is seemingly more difficult, with issues surrounding the eventual placement of the line and the simple fact that the games of Liber in Rome did not include theatrical représentations, begging the question as to when/how/in what context Naevius would have been speaking in such a manner. Further, if ludis Liberalibus is taken as meaning the Dionysia , as can reasonably be inferred, it would make the political declaration hypothesis null and void.

Ultimately, much of the political interpretation relies heavily on conjectures concerning Naevius’ own political engagements and difficulties, as Marmorale does when he declares the alliteration to emphasise the author’s championing of free expression (1950, p.44), but encounters many problems setting-wise. This political focus might simply be misplaced if the slave-master interpretation is more credible, and it does not appear to add anything to understanding the specifically dramatic context of the fragment, but rather takes it as an isolated political declaration rather than as a line in a larger work. Roman comedy did contain some political features, but it is perhaps premature to extrapolate so much from this particular line.

context.author: Festus
context.text:
This fragment is from an entry in the De verborum significatu of the grammarian Sextus Pompeius Festus, a lexical work which summarises Verrius Flaccus’ own lexicon. This fragment from Naevius can be found in the entry for the word Liberalia , and reads as follows :

Liberalia Liberi festa, quae apud Graecos dicuntur Διονύσια . Naevius: Libera lingua loquemur ludis Liberalibus.

lines.text: Libera lingua loquemur ludis Liberalibus
[THESPROTUS:] Eho tu di quibus est potestas motus superum atque inferum,
pacem inter sese conciliant conferunt concordiam.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: ‘’Oh, may I now become similar to my mother in respect to my character, so I can revenge my father!’’
doc.commentary:
This line is probably spoken out by Orestes or Elektra: they are the ones who want to revenge their father, Agamemnon. But because this is a dangerous activity, they will need some of the viciousness of their mother Clytaemnestra, who is the person responsible for their father’s death. By becoming like their mother, who is a murderer, they can be a murderer (of their mother) themselves. 
This line seems to be spoken out somewhere in the beginning or the middle of the play; the revenge has not been taken yet. The speaker (most likely Orestes, for he is the one that will kill Clytaemnestra eventually) is persuading himself to take revenge; the wish is presented as real and possible (through the form of the present subjunctive). 
The verb ‘’matrescere’’ is a hapax legomenon, thus the definition is not precisely clear, but the quoting author, Nonius, tells us that it means ‘’becoming similar to the mother’’. The context and the form, with the root mat- and ending on -escere, make this very plausible.

context.author: Nonius
context.text: 'matrescam' matris similis fiam. Pacuvius Duloreste: —.

lines.text: utinam nunc matrescam ingenio, ut meum patrem ulcisci queam!
O Fides alma apta pinnis, et ius iurandum Iovis!
Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beniuoli.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: So that, while rejoicing in the hunt, they send us from their places, decorated with bestial punishments.
doc.differences:
TrRF: 20 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 4/24)

Ribbeck1: 34-35 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 8/24)

Ribbeck2: 32-33 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 8/24)

Warmington: 37-38 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 9/25)

Marmorale: 8 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 8/24)

ut in venatu vitulantis ex suis locis nos mittant poenis decoratas feris

Marmorale: ut in venatu vitulantis ex suis, locis nos mittat poenis decoratas feris

Ribbeck: ut in venatu vitulantis ex suis lucis nos mittat poenis decoratas feris

Warmington: ut in venatu vitulantes ex suis locis nos mittant poenis decoratas feris

Most editors place this fragment in the first half of all fragments of this tragedy. Marmorale places this fragment after the arrest of the maenads. Ribbeck on the other hand, thinks that one woman warns another about the punishments of Lycurgus and places this fragment just before the women are arrested. Warmington places this fragment after Lycurgus has send his soldiers to arrest the women. The maenads then try to get away, complaining about the punishments that Lycurgus is threatening them with.
doc.apparatus:
1 ut in] codd.[Wa, Marm, Kl] : utin coni. Ribbeck3, Diehl, Klotz

1 vitulantis] vitulantes L P[On]

1 suis] codd. [Wa]

2 locis] codd.[Ri, Mu, Wa, Kl] : lucis coni. Mercerus, Ribbeck, Quicherat, Mueller, Klotz

2 mittant] mittat coni. Ribbeck

2 poenis] pennis coni. Mercerus, Scriverius, Quicherat

2 decoratas] decoratus codd. [Ri, Wa, Kl] L H2 G[Mu] F H L P G E [On] : decoratis H1 [Mu, On, Ri.3] : detoratus E [On] : decoratos coni. Stephanus, Merverus, Scriverius, Bothe, Quicherat : laceratos coni. Mueller.

Frg. in Bamb. Deest [Ri]

Decoratas is problematic, since it does not appear in any of the manuscripts. Decoratus is also problematic, because mittant is a plural. Ribbeck has suggested mittat instead, so that the king would be subject. However, this is not what the manuscripts read. Both Decoratas and Decoratos could be possible, since they both refer to a group of people, which would fit with the plural of mittant. Decoratas would then refer to the group of women and decoratos would refer to the soldiers of the king.
doc.commentary:
vitulantis] According to Nonius, the meaning of this word is gaudantes: ‘rejoicing’. Some manuscripts read vitulantes.In both cases it can be an accusative plural according with nos. Most likely it refers to the maenads, which would mean that it is the maenads who are about to be punished. This would mean that decoratas is indeed the right reading of the word, also referring to the maenads. However, different readings for decoratas have also been suggested (see below).

poenis] Instead of poenis pennis has been suggested. Bednarek (2021) points out that decorare ‘seems to have only positive connotations’ which would make the combination of decoratas and poenis a bit strange. pennis would therefore be a good suggestion, also because this would not be the first time that the maenads are compared with birds. On the other hand it could also be possible that decoratas in combination with poenis is used here in an ironic way.

decoratas] Most manuscripts read decoratus which is problematic because of the plural form of mittant. Therefore, two possible suggestions have been made regarding this word. Some editors have chosen the reading of decoratos. In that case the word would probably refer to the soldiers of Lycurgus, who are being hurt by the maenads. It could also be the other way around. In that case the word refers to the maenads, who are threatened with punishments by Lycurgus and his soldiers. It is in any way clear that this fragment is about two groups of multiple persons, since we have two plural forms in the fragment. This makes it likely that it is about the maenads and the soldiers. The word decoratus/os/as goes together with feris, which makes it more likely that it refers to the maenads, since there are more fragments in which the maenads are compared with animals or beasts.

Bednarek, B. 2021. The myth of Lycurgus in Aeschylus, Naevius, and beyond . Leiden.
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment is about a group of people that is going to punish another group. Because it is not sure if the word decoratas is the right reading and refers to a group of women, it is unclear who is speaking here. It could be the maenads speaking about Lycurgus soldiers, but it could also be the other way around. Most editors think that decoratas is the right reading of the word. The fragment would then be about the maenads, who are threatened with punishments. This would make sense, since Lycurgus does intend to punish the maenads. If this word is indeed right, the fragment could be spoken by the maenads against Lycurgus and his soldiers, complaining about the threats that Lycurgus made to punish them. In this case, the fragment should be placed somewhere in the tragedy after the maenads have been arrested. It could also be the case that one maenad is speaking to another about the threats that Lycurgus made, as Ribbeck suggests. This would mean that the lines are spoken by a maenad, before the women are arrested. In any case it is clear that the women are not yet punished, but only threatened to be punished, because of the construction with ut, which shows that Lycurgus is intending to hurt the women.

If decoratos is the right reading of the word, it would refer to the soldiers of Lycurgus. In this case the fragment should be placed later in the tragedy, after the maenads have already been taken captive. After their release, Liber will take revenge on Lycurgus for hurting the women, so that it would be possible that the maenads are intending to punish the soldiers of Lycurgus. However, the first reading seems to be more plausible, because of vitulantis, which is most likely an accusative and it would make more sense if this word would refer to the women who are rejoicing in the hunt rather than to the soldiers of Lycurgus.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted by Nonius in De compendiosa doctrina

The text is quoted by Nonius to point out the meaning of vitulantes, which means ‘rejoicing’:

vitulantes veteres gaudentes dixerunt: dictum a bonae vitae commodo; sicuti qui nunc est in summa Laetitia, vivere eum dicimus. Naevius Lycurgo: ut in venatu vitulantis ex suis locis nos mittant poenis decoratas feris.Non. p. 14, 15-19 Merc. = 21 Li. (Li.)

lines.text: ut in venatu vitulantis ex suis
locis nos mittant poenis decoratas feris

procat, toleratis temploque hanc deducitis?

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Who shall I say you are, who in slow old age…
doc.differences:
Manuwald 133 (2/10)

Vahlen2 348 (7/14)

Ribbeck3 298 (4/11)

Warmington 355 (7/14)

Jocelyn 300 (3/12)

quemnam te esse dicam, qui tarda in senectute

Ribbeck: quemnam te esse dicam, qui tarda in senectute?
doc.apparatus:
quemnam] quemnam codd. : quem nam coni. Levée
qui] qui A U K1 J : quin L K2 : que E
senectute] senectute codd. : senecta coni. Bothe

Levée’s conjecture quem nam is unnecessary and unwarranted by the manuscript evidence.
As regards quin, L represents the consensus of many manuscripts derived from the now lost codex Laudensis (cf. Westman (1980) XVIII). K2 signifies a correction from the L tradition. But quin does not make sense here, so qui should be preferred. That E has the reading que strongly suggests scribal confusion over an abbreviated form for qui.
Bothe thought that the line as it stood was an incomplete trochaic tetrameter. Alternatively he offered the conjecture senecta to turn it into a versus asynartetus (the repetition of two metrical patterns within a single line as if each were self contained) consisting of two ithyphallics. However, the second element of an ithyphallic is usually short. However, it might be a mistake to speak of a strict metrical character to the ithyphallic metre in Latin poetry of the stage: there is one double ithyphallic line in Plautus, Epid. 546, but it is of almost completely different character, with not only five natural shorts but also the three of the four trochaic longs resolved into shorts. But it is unusual to have a complex metre like the ithyphallic in dialogue, which might be why Bothe suggested it uncertainly, and also hypothesised the transmitted reading was a trochaic tetrameter.
If Bothe’s senecta is adopted, it is simpler to think of it as a normal bacchiac quarternarius (so Ribbeck 1875, 200). However, there is no manuscript evidence that the reading is wrong, and the reading senectute that is transmitted can be understood as a bacchiac quaternarius hypermetricus, with the short e at the end of senectute providing an extra element after the four bacchiacs. Since this is an open syllable, it could also be the case that the next line began with a vowel, elided via synaloephea and so there is not an extra element at all (the phenomenon is observed in dactylic hexameter lines in epic (e.g. Ver. G. 1.295, 3.449, Ov. Met. 4.11). There don’t seem to be any examples of hypermetrical bacchiac quaternarii in Plautus, for instance, but that may be precisely for the reason that Cicero quotes TrRF 133, that such lines might not be easily distinguishable as verse rather than prose. And as Jocelyn (1967, 422) highlights, senecta in first century Republican texts was a highly poetic word, whereas senectute was more redolent of prose. A fragment of Varro provides evidence for the use of a Bacchiac system with enjambement (characteristic of synaphea) – the line also seems to be a strong echo of TrRF 133: quaenam te esse dicam, fera qui manu cor- / poris seruidos fortium aperis lacus san- / guinis, teque vita levas ferreo ense?. The upshot is that it is probably better to let the transmitted manuscript reading of senectute stand.
doc.commentary:
dicam] Levée (1823) and Giles (1834) divide this fragment between two speakers, the first part being a direct question, followed by an answer of the addressee. However, given this was a sung line, it is perhaps more likely that we are dealing with one voice in this fragment, who asks an older addressee a question.
senectute] The archaic form senecta is substituted for senectute by Bothe to create a more regular bacchiac tetrameter, instead of the earlier shortened or incomplete trochaic octonarius (tr8 mutil.). Jocelyn (1967, 422) argues convincingly for the reading of senectute (resulting in a quaternarius bacchiacus hypermetricus) because the use of a highly poetic word like senecta would undermine Cicero’s point that the verse would sound like prose without musical accompaniment.
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment deals with an encounter between a speaker who comes into contact with and addresses an unfamiliar (quemnam), older figure (in senectute).
Based on Cicero’s statement – that the verse was originally accompanied by the music of a flute player (tibicen) – it can be argued that the verse was either part of a sung dialogue between multiple characters, or that it belongs to a choral cantica. Both options are valid, but it must be noted that verses in bacchiac metres – if we decide to read this verse as a quaternarius bacchiacus hypermetricus – are closely related to choral songs. However, the metre can also signify the approach of a new character (sc. the older addressee) on stage.
The identification of the speaker and addressee in this verse – as in most fragments of early Roman drama – remains very difficult because the narrative structure and plot of Ennius Thyestes is very uncertain. Different options have been proposed: Welcker (1839, 683) suspects that Atreus converses with an old servant. Ribbeck (1875, 200) agrees with Welcker on the fact that the speaker is probably Atreus, but adds that the addressee might be the chorus, made up of Mycenean elders. Mette (1964, 15) interprets the verse as part of a speech between the chorus and an older messenger, while Webster (1967, 144) is convinced that the chorus is speaking to Thyestes who has just arrived in Mycenae. Jocelyn (1967, 414, 419-421) believes the question posed in this fragment is answered by a reply of Thyestes, who makes himself known to the audience (=TrRF 55 adesp.). The fact that we are dealing with an older addressee might suggest that this verse is not spoken to Thyestes, who is not old, nor to a messenger, because old people are not likely to be retained as couriers. The interpretation of the fragment as a speech between Atreus and an older servant who has come to inform his master of Thyestes’ evil plan, might be the most plausible. If this is the case, the metre would underline the ominous tone of the servant’s arrival, who might visit his master to bring the ominous news about Thyestes’ intentions.

lines.text: quemnam te esse dicam, qui tarda in senectute
tum autem lascivum Nerei simum pecus
ludens ad cantum classem lustratur . . .
.... sed valvae resonunt regiae

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 110.29-33 Mercier = 158.32-38 Lindsay:

fligi adfligi. Livius Aegistho: ipsus se [Bothe: ipse se codd.] in terram saucius fligit cadens. Accius Epinausimache: nec perdolescit fligi socios, morte campos contegi? idem Aegistho: heu! | cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet.

Nonius quotes this fragment to explain the word fligi ‘to be struck’, which, he says, corresponds to adfligi ‘to be struck down’. The line is quoted alongside two other fragments of Roman tragedy: one is from Livius Androncius’ Aegisthus (ipsus se in terram saucius fligit cadens). The other fragment quoted by Nonius is also by Accius, from the tragedy entitled Epinausimache (‘The Battle at the Ships’). In this fragment, the passive infinitive form fligi is also used (nec perdolescit fligi socios, morte campos contegi?, F 185 TRF = v. 317 Ribbeck3 = 303 Warmington = 138 Dangel).

lines.text: cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet?

doc.translation: Alas! From what country is the person who is trying utterly to destroy the family?
doc.differences:
TRF: 5 (Accius' Aegisthus 2/5)

Ribbeck2-3: 21/22 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5)

Ribbeck1: 22 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5)

Warmington: 4-5 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5)

Franchella: 401-402 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5)

D'Antò: 22 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5

Dangel: 306 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

Warmington:

Alas! Of what land is the man who plans / To strike our stock down to its base?

Franchella:

heu!

cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet!

'Ohimé, di quale razza di gente è colui che progetta di distruggere alla base la stirpe?'

D'Antò:

...heu!

cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet?

'Ahimé! Il figlio di chi vuole del tutto rovinare?'

Dangel:

Las ! d'où est celui qui s'acharne à abattre la souche à sa base ?
doc.apparatus:
1 heu] heu codd.: eheu coni. Bothe

2 cuiatis] cuiatis codd.: ciuitatis ‘in aliquibus [sc. libris]’ Vossius: cuiam coni. Iunius: quia tis coni. Palmer: cuiatis ⟨est qui ⟩ stirpem supplevit Nelis

2 funditus] funditus codd.: penitus coni. Delrius(?)

Eheu was conjectured by Bothe (I.160) for metrical reasons (see ‘Metrical issues’). With ciuitatis, a reading recorded by Vossius in Scriverius’ 1620 edition (p. 123) but which can be traced back to the fifteenth century (see Nelis, forthcoming), the line does not scan. Iunius and Palmer tried to remove cuiatis by means of conjectural emendation, proposing cuiam and quia tis respectively, but this is unattractive for palaeographic reasons and because it disobeys the editorial principle known as lectio difficilior, according to which a more difficult is privileged to an easier one. In Delrius' edition, funditus is replaced by penitus (though the change is left unmentioned so it is unclear whether this is his own conjecture or that of an earlier scholar). It is also unclear what advantage penitus has over funditus: perhaps on the grounds that penitus has more attestations? Much debate has turned on the issue of determining whether cuiatis should be construed as a genitive or as a nominative. Those scholars who supply a translation of their text of the fragment translate as follows:

(i) ‘Alas! Of what land is the man who plans to strike our stock down to its base?’ (Warmington).

(ii) ‘Ahi quanta e quale stirpe abbatter vuole | Dai fondamenti!’ (Argenio [1]).

(iii) ‘Oh, di che paese è l’uomo che medita di abbattere dalle fondamenta la nostra stirpa?’ (Argenio [2]).

(iv) ‘Ohimé, di quale razza di gente è colui che progetta di distruggere alla basa la stirpe?’ (Franchella).

(v) ‘Ahimé! Il figlio di chi vuole del tutto rovinare?’ (D’Antò). (vi) ‘Las ! d’où est celui qui s’acharne à abattre la souche à sa base’ (Dangel).

The translations of Warmington, Argenio (2), Franchella, and Dangel show that they take cuiatis as an interrogative pronoun in the nominative. Argenio and D’Antò both write in their commentaries that they take cuiatis as a genitive. In the translation of Argenio (1), which reflects his interpretation of the fragment in so far as it corresponds to the text he prints, cuiatis is rendered with two adjectives (‘quanta e quale’) agreeing with ‘stirpe’ and the sentence is turned into an exclamation. As for D’Antò, his translation suggests that he understands cuiatis as a pronoun in the genitive (‘di chi’) depending on stirpem. While editors agree that the issue of determining the grammatical function of cuiatis is crucial to the correct interpretation of the fragment, both of the interpretations that have been put forward are problematic.

A survey of the usage of cuiatis in extant Latin shows, on the one hand, that in early Latin it always appears in a construction of the type ‘verb esse + relative pronoun’ (i.e. cuiatis est, qui…), and, on the other hand, that it is never used as a genitive without an attribute, and only once in the genitive at all (Gell. NA 15.30.Λ). A further problem is that, as transmitted, the fragment does not scan as a single iambic senarius, since heu is hypermetric and never found at line-end in extant Republican drama (the only two occurrences of heu at line-end are Hor. Epod. 11.21; Carm. 4.6.17, and in both cases heu already appears earlier within the line). A solution to all these problems would be to write it as follows: heu, cuiatis ⟨est qui⟩ stirpem funditus fligi studet? The usage of cuiatis would thus be normalised and the line would scan as a trochaic septenarius. See Nelis, forthcoming.
doc.commentary:
cuiatis: lit. ‘from where, from what country’. Based on the use of the word elsewhere in early Latin, it is doubtful that the word can be understood as a genitive, as some scholars suggest (see 'Fragment apparatus criticus').

fligi: here, the passive infinitive is used in an accusative and infinitive construction, and so, rendered literally, the clause cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet? means: ‘from what country is the person who is striving for the family to be utterly destroyed?’ Although most of the attestations of fligi are mainly found in early Latin, it is nevertheless a rare verb (TLL VI.1.912.50-912.60), and, as such, a direct allusion by Accius to Livius Andronicus, who also used the verb in his Aegisthus (see ‘Citation context’), cannot be ruled out.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker has variously been identified as Cassandra (Ribbeck 1875, Argenio 1966, Franchella 1968), an unspecified speaker (Warmington 1935-40), Strophius (Venini 1954), the chorus (Argenio 1966, Dangel 1995), an unknown Argive citizen (D’Antò 1980), and Aegisthus (Gottfried Hermann apud Ribbeck1, 1852). On the basis of the supposed identity of the speaker, several different dramatic scenarios have been imagined:

(i) Cassandra foreseeing Aegisthus’ murder of Orestes (Ribbeck, Franchella).

(ii) Cassandra foreseeing Orestes’ murder of Clytemnestra (Argenio).

(iii) An unspecified speaker lamenting the plot of Orestes to kill his mother (Warmington).

(iv) An unspecified speaker lamenting the plot of Aegisthus to kill Orestes (Pociña).

(v) Strophius expressing his astonishment to Electra that Aegisthus, having murdered Agamemnon, now plans to kill his son Orestes (Venini).

(vi) The chorus commenting on Aegisthus’ plan to extinguish the house of Atreus (Argenio)

(vii) The chorus lamenting the supposed death of Orestes in the chariot race at the Pythian Games in Delphi (Dangel).

(viii) An unknown Argive citizen reacting with shock to the news that Aegisthus has ordered the execution of Orestes (D’Antò).

(ix) Aegisthus after discovering Clytemnestra’s corpse (Hermann).

(x) Same as (viii), except that Aegisthus, addressing Orestes without recognising him, expresses his fear that he will be the murderer’s next victim (Dangel).

Any of these possibilities is theoretically conceivable. Another scenario can however also be imagined. Our fragment might have been spoken by Orestes, while still in disguise, to his sister Electra during a version of their famous recognition scene. Seeking to elicit sympathy from Electra who still believes that she is speaking with a mere stranger, the disguised Orestes feigns ignorance about her situation and asks her who is responsible for attempting to destroy her family. My reason for making this suggestion is that our fragment can be compared fruitfully with Soph. El. 1191, a line that falls within that play’s recognition scene. At this stage in the scene, Electra has just revealed that she lives with the murders (ἐιμὶ τοῖς φονεῦσι σύντροφος, Soph. El. 1190: on the vagueness of this reference to ‘the murderers’ [φονεῦσι], see Finglass 2007 ad loc.), to which Orestes responds (1191): τοῖς [sc. φονεῦσι] τοῦ; πόθεν τοῦτ ἐξεσήμνησας κακόν; (‘the murderers of whom? From where comes the evil that you have indicated?’). Though the correspondence between the two lines is not total, the theme of murder (in Sophocles, and possibly in Accius too, the murder in question is that of Agamemnon) and the emphasis on the provenance of the evil (πόθεν, cuiatis) make this line of Sophocles is a possible model for our Accian fragment.

It should be noted that Sophocles’ Electra has proven to be a popular play when it comes to identifying potential models for our Accian fragment, with scholars having observed similarities with: (i) El. 764-5, in which the chorus lament the fate of the royal house of Argos after listening to the report, which later turns out to be false, that Orestes died in a chariot race (φεῦ φεῦ·τὸ πᾶν δὴ δεσπόταισι τοῖς πάλαι‎ | πρόρριζον, ὡς ἔοικεν, ἔφθαρται γένος‎ ‘Alas, alas! The whole family of our ancient masters, it seems, is destroyed root and branch’, tr. Lloyd-Jones: Strauss 1887, 44 n. 1; Dangel 1995, 324); (ii) Soph. El. 964-6 οὐ γὰρ ὧδ᾿ ἄβουλός ἐστ᾿ ἀνὴρ | Αἴγισθος ὥστε σόν ποτ᾿ ἢ κἀμὸν γένος | βλαστεῖν ἐᾶσαι, πημονὴν αὑτῷ σαφῆ ‘Aegisthus is not so stupid a man as to allow your children or mine to come into being, bringing obvious trouble for himself’ (cf. too Eur. El. 31-3: ἐκ τῶνδε δὴ τοιόνδ᾿ ἐμηχανήσατο | Αἴγισθος· ὃς μὲν γῆς ἀπηλλάχθη φυγὰς | Ἀγαμέμνονος παῖς, χρυσὸν εἶφ᾿ ὃς ἂν κτάνῃ ‘As a result, Aegisthus formed the following scheme: he announced a reward for whoever should kill the exiled Orestes’, tr. Kovacs), cf. Aricò 2002, 14; and (iii) and El. 1476-7, where Aegisthus has just discovered Clytemnestra’s corpse but does not yet recognise Orestes as the murderer (τίνων ποτ’ ἀνδρῶν ἐν μέσοις ἀρκυστάτοις | πέπτωχ’ ὁ τλήμων; ‘Who are the men into whose trap I have fallen, unluckily?’, tr. Lloyd-Jones: Dangel 1995, 324). Dangel favours the latter interpretation, adducing in support of it Hyginus’ use of the verb studere in Fab. 119 (Orestes…postquam in puberem aetatem uenit, studebat patris sui mortem exsequi ‘having reached the age of a young man, Orestes devoted himself to avenging his father’s death’), which she seems to suggest is modelled on our Accian fragment. The Latin fragment, however, need not necessarily correspond to any Greek model whatsoever.
doc.metrical_analysis:
This fragment is printed by almost all scholars as an iambic senarius preceded by an incomplete line ending with monosyllabic heu. Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 123) pointed out that, for the metre to be trochaic, one would have to posit that the first foot is missing and that if one were to read ciuitatis the metre would be iambic (though the line would scan neither as a senarius, septenarius, or octonarius). Bothe (I.160) replaced heu with eheu, presumably on the grounds that unelided, non-enclitic, monosyllabic interjections such as heu at line-end are almost always avoided in iambo-trochaic verse. Eheu, however, is unappealing because, when quoting lines from Republican drama, the normal practice of Nonius’ source for this fragment, an anonymous glossary known as ‘Alph. Verb.’ (i.e. Lindsay’s source 27), is to quote only single metrical units. The source will deviate from this norm and supply part of another line only of it is required to achieve a full syntactical unit (see Welsh 2012, 829-34). Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xii) suggested that eheu is an extra metrum interjection, in other words, that it does not belong to the metrical sequence of any line in the text, which is not impossible.

With the proposed addition of ⟨est qui⟩ (see Apparatus criticus section), the line would scan as a single complete trochaic septenarius.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation:
Truly, it is Fortune that fails me now more than my birth.
For the throne was available to me, so that you know from which place,
from what power, from which riches my fortune has slipped and fallen.
doc.differences:
TrRF: 157 (frg. inc. )
Jocelyn: 338 (frg. inc.
Warmington: 363-365 (Ennius' Thyestes 11/14)
Vahlen2: 354-356 (Ennius' Thyestes 10/14)
Ribbeck2/3: 369-371 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: ...Pol mihi fortuna magis nunc defit quam genus.
Namque regnum suppetebat mi, ut scias quanto e loco,
Quantis opibus, quibus de rebus lapsa fortuna occidat.
doc.apparatus:
1 namque] neque: K

3 lapsa] lassa: W, M
3 accidat] occidat: P5, Rehd. W, M, Oxon. 5

The variant neque in K (cod. Cameracensis A 943 / 842) seems unlikely if we consider the rest of the fragment, as the speaker of these words is drawing attention to a moment in time where he presumably did have wealth and power. The variant lassa for lapsa is found in W (cod. Vindobonensis (fort. W 222, olim 205)) and M(cod. Ambrosianus T 56 sup. and cod. Mon. 277 (1465)). lassa (‘tired’), an adjective modifying fortuna, would work grammatically and does not significantly alter the content of the verses. Finally, the variant occidat is found in several manuscripts, including as a correction in P (cod. Palatinus 1514), in Rehd (cod. Rhedigeranus 64 (15th century)), W (cod. Vindobonensis 222) and M (cod. Ambrosianus T 56 sup. and cod. Mon. 277 (1465)) and Oxon 5 (cod. Oxoniensis): Delrius, Nobbe and Ribbeck all printed this variant. occidat would work grammatically and does not significantly alter the interpretation of the last line.
doc.commentary:
Fortuna] Although usually Fortuna is a positive force in later texts, in Republican Latin she could easily have negative connotations. See further the discussion on frg. 135.

genus] The speaker seems to be searching for reasons for their currently distressed situation, and finds likelier the actions of Fortune to be the cause than their genus, which refers to parentage or family rather than the actual moment of birth.

scias] The speaker of the words seems to be addressing a single character.
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment clearly presents a speaker who has been deprived of his wealth and power. The speaker points to power that has once been his, but at this point has all vanished. Especially significant seems to be the first verse: the speaker is emphasizing that at this point, it is Fortune that is failing him, instead of his birth, perhaps implying that his lineage has troubled him. This obviously makes a case for either Thyestes or Atreus as speaker of these words, since Myrtilus cursed Pelops, the father of Atreus and Thyestes. Thyestes seems a natural fit: in Seneca’s Thyestes he is depicted as a beggar in his exile, before his return to Mycenae in the course of the play. If the speaker’s point is that he has fallen from power due to a twist of fate, this would be appropriate to Thyestes losing the throne to Atreus and being exiled because of the Sun’s reversal (according to the telling of Apollodorus (Epit. 2.12).

Besides the attribution of this fragment to Ennius, scholars are not totally agreed that the lines are from his Thyestes. Some have attributed it to Ennius’ Telamon (such as Grotius, Hermannn, and Ribbeck in his 1848 edition), others to Ennius’ Hecuba or Andromacha (Scriverius) or to the Hecuba (Bothe), or Ennius’ Telephus (Düntzer). Delrius even considered the possibility that these are Medea’s words. However, Ennius’ Thyestes is favoured by the majority of scholars, including Ribbeck (who revised his opinion in his 1875 monograph) and Paduano (1974: 48). Segura Morena connects this fragment to fragment 135, where there is a strong invocation of Fortuna.

One possible parallel may be found in Accius’ Telephus (Dangel 2002: frg. 88-89 [= Telephus frg. 7]):

x – Nam si a me regnum Fortuna atque opes
eripere quivit, at virtutem nec quiit.
For if Fortuna were to take away my power and wealth, she could not touch my virtue!

But even if Accius modelled that verse on this Ennian line, that does not necessarily mean that it belonged to Ennius’ Telephus, given that both are quite generic statements.

context.author: Cicero
context.text: Cicero, in the third book of his Tusculan Disputations (39-45), quotes this fragment in the context of the description of an Epicurean ideal way of life. He is questioning outright whether the thought of such a life avails to relieve men like Thyestes or Aetes or Telamon. Cicero then quotes three fragments, all depicting characters in severe distress, illustrating that an Epicurean way of thinking about the relief of sorrow would not work for them. A first fragment is about Telamon (in which astonished men ask whether the man they are seeing is really Telamon). Regarding the second fragment, the subject is uncertain: presumably, one of the other characters mentioned as examples earlier (Thyestes or Aetes). Cicero asks how a man is supposed to get rid of his sorrow, if he speaks in the following way – immediately followed by fragment 157. Cicero’s third fragment, ‘from the same poet’, is attributed to Ennius’ Andromacha (frg. 23 in Manuwald). It presents Andromacha’s lament about Hector’s death.

lines.text: pol mihi fortuna magis nunc defit quam genus.
namque regnum suppetebat mi{hi}, ut scias quanto e loco,
quantis opibus, quibus de rebus lapsa fortuna accidat.
cui manus materno sordet sparsa sanguine.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: <20> fregisti fidem.
:: neque dedi, neque do infideli cuiquam.

doc.translation:
You have broken faith.
I did not give it, and I do not give it to anyone unfaithful.

doc.apparatus:
1: fregisti ] fregisti ξ (= BPV): fregistine ζ (= Lc): fregistin’ Heusinger 1783, 772

2: infideli cuiquam ] infideli cuiquam codd.: infideli quoipiam Delrius 1593, I.178

2: neque dedi neque do infideli cuiquam ] neque dedi neque do infideli cuiquam codd.: neque do infideli cuiquam, neque dedi fidem Muret 1580, 362

1 fregisti: on the basis of the manuscript variant fregistine which does not fit in a iambo-trochaic metrical pattern, Heusinger (1783, 772) proposed fregistin’, the ellipsis of final -e of suffix -ne being attested e.g. at Liv. 2.36.4 (satin magnam spreti numinis haberet mercedem?) Dangel prints Heusinger’s conjecture (attributing it to Graevius, who however in his 1710 edition prints fregisti, p. 368) but, rather oddly, prints an exclamation rather than an interrogation mark.

2: on Muret’s conjecture, see ‘Fragment Metrical Analysis’.
doc.commentary:
1-2: Ribbeck (1875, 455) compares Sen. Thy. 1024 hoc foedus? haec est gratia, haec fratris fides?, ‘Is this our agreement? Is this your goodwill, your brotherly promise?’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.319).

1: fidem: what exactly is the sense of this word? A specific pact brokered with regard to the kingship? The hospitality afforded by Atreus to Thyestes on the latter’s return? A universal code between brothers (cf. Seneca’s fratris fides)?

2 neque dedi, neque do: D’Antò (1980, 287) compares Bacchyl. 1.159 Snell–Maehler φαμὶ καὶ φάσω, Pl. Mil. 1058 dixi hoc tibi dudum et nunc dico, Ter. Hec. 722 iamdudum dixi idemque nunc dico, Ennius Telamo F 117.6 TRF: dixi et dicam, Pacuvius Periboea F 184.2 TRF = 217.2 Schierl ut quae egi ago uel axim. He suggests that with such formulas Roman dramatists imitate the language of the magistrates (cf. Fraenkel 2007, 243-4)-
doc.reconstruction: Ribbeck (1875, 454) imagines that this fragment followed on almost immediately from Atreus’ perverse revelation of the truth (F 103.5 TRF, natis sepulcro ipse es parens), with Thyestes responding that Atreus has broken fides. In what follows, a horrified and mournful Thyestes begins a monologue delivered as a canticum which contains F 104.1-2 TRF (ipsus hortatur me frater, ut meos malis miser | manderem natos), F 109 TRF (egone Argivum imperium adtingam aut Pelopis digner domo? | quo me ostendam? quod templum adeam? qu[a]em ore funesto adloquar?), and Adesp. 83 TRF (ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam). L. Müller (1890, 24), however, interprets this fragment very differently to Ribbeck. In Müller’s view, the fragment belongs not to the aftermath of the banquet, but to a scene at an earlier moment in the play, in which the exiled Thyestes implores his brother to allow him to return to the royal court. Müller imagines Thyestes to be disingenuous, and to be feigning desperation in order to allay potential suspicions on Atreus’ part that he may secretly be seeking to usurp Atreus and claim the throne for himself (‘ad suspicionem, qua imperium affectare arguebatur, refellendam’). Lana (1959, 309) agrees with Ribbeck’s analysis, rejecting that of Müller (309 n. 4), pointing out that the expression ore funesto rules out Müller’s interpretation, because Thyestes’ mouth could only meaningfully be describes as ‘fatal’ or ‘mournful’ (funestus) after he has eaten his children. Lana further suggests that the parallel of Seneca’s line lancinat gnatos pater | artusque mandit ore funesto suos (Sen. Thy. 778-9, ‘The father is mangling his sons, gnawing his own limbs with entombing teeth’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.299), which describes Thyestes in the process of eating his children, constitutes further evidence that Müller’s interpretation is erroneous. Lana observes that none of the surviving fragments thought to belong to this scene contains a lamentation by Thyestes for his dead children (cf. F 104.1-2 and F 109). He deduces that Thyestes was depicted by Accius as being more troubled by the means with which Atreus took revenge than by the death of his children, and argues that in this fragment Thyestes expresses self-pity rather than pity for his dead children. But such an extreme interpretation is surely unwarranted: it cannot be ruled out that the play did contain a lamentation by Thyestes, but that it is not preserved among the fragments that have come down to us. Dangel (1995, 281) suggests that the dialogue between the two brothers is stichomythic (i.e. a dialogue in alternate lines).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De Officiis 3.102:

Quid est igitur, dixerit quis, in iure iurando? num iratum timemus Iovem? At hoc quidem commune est omnium philosophorum, non eorum modo, qui deum nihil habere ipsum negotii dicunt, nihil exhibere alteri, sed eorum etiam, qui deum semper agere aliquid et moliri volunt, numquam nec irasci deum nec nocere. quid autem iratus Iuppiter plus nocere potuisset, quam nocuit sibi ipse Regulus? nulla igitur vis fuit religionis, quae tantam utilitatem perverteret. an ne turpiter faceret? Primum minima de malis. Num igitur tantum mali turpitudo ista habebat, quantum ille cruciatus? deinde illud etiam apud Accium: ‘fregistin fidem? neque dedi neque do infideli cuiquam’ (= Accius Atreus F 106 TRF), quamquam ab impio rege dicitur, luculente tamen dicitur.

‘So what point is there’ someone will ask ‘in swearing an oath? We surely do not fear the wrath of Jupiter? All philosophers, not merely those who say that God experiences no trouble himself and imposes it on no other, but also those who have it that God is always busy in activity and toil, share the view that God is never angry and never causes harm. In any case, what greater harm could an angry Jupiter have caused than that which Regulus inflicted on himself? So religious scruple had no impact such as could undermine the effect of what was beneficial to him. Was the oath taken, then, so that he would not act basely? The first objection to this is the proverbial “Take the least of evils”. So did this base behaviour involve as much evil as the torture he suffered? Secondly, there is also the objection as voiced by Accius:

[THYESTES]: Did you break your faith?

[ATREUS]: No faithless man received it then, nor hears it now.

Though this response is spoken by an impious king, the sentiment is splendid.

[Text: Winterbottom 1994; transl. Walsh 2001, 199-120.]

See further the following element in 'Citation context'.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De Officiis 3.106:

itaque nervosius qui ista disserunt, solum audent malum dicere id, quod turpe sit, qui autem remissius, ii tamen non dubitant summum malum dicere. nam illud quidem ‘neque dedi neque do infideli cuiquam’ (= Accius Atreus F 106 TRF) idcirco recte a poeta, quia, cum tractaretur Atreus, personae serviendum fuit. sed si hoc sibi sument, nullam esse fidem, quae infideli data sit, videant, ne quaeratur latebra periurio.

This is why philosophers of more intense views make bold to say that the dishonourable is the only evil, and even those who are less stringent do not hesitate to call it the greatest evil. As for the citation ‘No faithless man received it then, nor hears it now’, the poet was right to use it because in dealing with Atreus, he had to adhere closely to the character. But if critics intend to adopt for their own use the argument that a pledge given to a faithless person counts for nothing, they should be careful that they are not affording perjury a place in which to hide.

[Text: Winterbottom 1994; transl. Walsh 2001, 199-120.]

The main subject of Book 3 of Cicero’s De Officiis is the relation between the honourable (honestum) and the expedient (utilitas), and, in particular, with what one should do when faced with a situation in which what appears to be the honourable course of action is at odds with what appears expedient (si id quod speciem haberet honesti pugnaret cum eo quod utile uideretur, Off. 3.7). Cic. Off. 3.97-115 deal with the legend of M. Atilius Regulus (RE ‘Atilius 51’). Regulus, twice a consul (in 267 and 256), enjoyed some military success during the First Punic War before being captured while proconsul in 255. Although in reality he likely died in captivity, there developed a legend according to which he was sent back to Rome to negotiate an exchange of prisoners but, after convincing the Senate not to cede to the Carthaginians’ requests, returned to Carthage where he was tortured and put to death (C. Sempronius Tuditanus F 8 FRHist = Gell. NA 7.4.1-4, Hor. Carm. 3.5). Cicero praises him as an example of magnitudo animi and fortitudo (Off. 3.100). At Off. 3.101-2, the character of Cicero lists several reasons why Regulus’ actions might be considered as foolish (stulte), only to come up with counter-arguments for each of them in turn. One reason why Regulus might be considered to have behaved improperly is because he broke an oath. The fragment from Accius’ Atreus as a praiseworthy example of respecting an oath, despite the speaker being a ‘wicked king’ (impius rex). The same fragment is cited again some paragraphs later (Off. 3.106), when Cicero discusses a proverb which states one should always choose the lesser of two evils (minima de malis, Off. 3.105). Cicero however sides with the Stoics in holding that there is no evil greater than moral wrong, and rejects the notion, apparently endorsed earlier, that the Accian lines are an example of praiseworthy conduct: while the attitude displayed conforms to notions of poetic decorum in so far as it is appropriate that the tyrannical Atreus should deny having ever sworn an oath, Cicero denounces this attitude as falling short of proper moral standards, describing it as potentially akin to perjury. It is worth noting that, earlier in Book 3 of De Officiis, Cicero had quoted a fragment of Accius (inc. fab. F 413 TRF, multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci benivoli) which has been thought possibly to belong to the Atreus (or to Clytemnestra or to Pelopidae): see inc. fab. F 413 TRF ‘Fragment reconstruction’.

doc.metrical_analysis: Considered by TRF, as well as most modern scholars, as two incomplete trochaic septenarii, the first lacking all but the final feet, and the second lacking the end. Muret (1580, 362) rewrote the second line of fragment as follows so as to turn it into an iambic senarius: neque do infideli cuiquam, neque dedi fidem. Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 141) suggests that the line can scan either as one single iambic octonarius or as the beginning and the end of two iambic senarii. Grotius (apud Peerlkamp 1828, 169) suggested that the fragment consists of two iambic senarii, the second one complete, with hiatus between the words do and infideli. Düntzer (1839, 294) suggested that the metre of the second line (beginning with neque dedi) is the bacchiac quaternarius (ba4), with hiatus between do and infideli. The basic schema of the ba4 is: × - - × - - × - - × - × (cf. Questa 2007, 426)

lines.text: Ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia
Accumbat mensam aut eandem uescatur dapem.
Eheu mea fortuna ut omnia in me conglomeras mala!

lines.text: ariuga

doc.translation: Ram(?)
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Not in Ribbeck3.

Ribbeck1-2:

Fragment inc. fab. XLIII, v. 705:

aruiga:

Not in Warmington.

Not in Franchella.

Not in D’Antò.

Dangel:

Fragment inc. fab. XXXVII:

Varr. L. L. 5.98: aries quod eum dicebant ares (ἀρήν Spengel) ueteres, nostri aruiga, hinc ariugas (a F aruigas codd. aruignus B ariugus Kent). Haec sunt quorum in sacruficiis exta in olla, non in ueru coquuntur, quas et Accius scribit et in Pontificiis libris uidemus.
doc.apparatus: 1: ariuga ] ariuga F: aruiga Ribbeck2(?)
doc.commentary: See 'Fragment reconstruction.
doc.reconstruction: Technically, this fragment is listed in a section of the Accius volume in the TRF series entitled ‘Operum incertorum fragmenta tragoediis fortasse tribuenda’ (IV.592-602). Ribbeck includes this as a fragment of Accius in his first and second editions, but did not do so in his third edition. Warmington (II.599) suggested that it might belong to the Annales. Dangel (1995, 280) and Baldarelli (2004, 239-40) suggest it might belong to the Atreus, connecting the word to the banquet scene and, more specifically, to F 108 TRF (concoquit | partem uapore flammae; tribuit in focos | ueribus lacerta). See F 108 TRF, ‘Fragment reconstruction’. Baldarelli believes that Varro is referring to this exact passage of Accius, and that he makes an implicit distinction between the proper practice of Roman animal sacrifice, and the perverse application of normal sacrificial practice to the cooking of human flesh in Accius’ Atreus.
doc.metrical_analysis: The orthography of auriga is disputed, as other spellings are attested elsewhere, including aruiga, ariga, and hariuga: TLL II.728.77-729.3) The archetype of De Lingua Latina reads ariuga. For discussion, see Kent 1936, 69.

context.author: Varro
context.text:
Varro De Lingua Latina 5.98:

aries, ut quidam dicebant, ab aris; ueteres nostri ariuga, hinc ariugus. Haec sunt quarum in sacruficiis exta in olla, non in ueru coquuntur, quas et Accius scribit et in pontificiis libris uidemus. In hostis eam dicunt ariugam, quae cornua habeat, quoniam si cui oui mari testiculi dempti et ideo ui natura uersa, uerbex declinatum.

The ariēs ‘ram’ got its name from the ārae ‘altars’, as some used to say. Our ancients said feminine āriuga ‘altar-companion’, from which comes masculine āriugus. These are the ones whose entrails are cooked in a pot in the sacrifices, not on a spit, which Accius records and which we can see in the pontifical books. Among the victims they call that one an āriuga which is to have horns, since, if the testicles are removed from a male sheep and consequently its nature has been uī uersa ‘changed by force’, the word uerbēx ‘wether’ is derived.

(Text and translation De Melo 2019, 310-11)

Varro reports that Accius used the archaic feminine form ariuga, a word which apparently denoted a ram whose entrails were boiled during sacrifices.

lines.text: [ATREUS]: Iterum Thyestes Atreum adtrectatum advenit;
iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum suscitat.
Maior mihi moles, maius miscendumst malum,
qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam.
Pervade polum, splendida mundi
sidera binis continuis sex
picti spoliis

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: sprung from Tantalus, the son of Pelops, who once gained Hippodamea from his father-in-law, king Oenomaus, snatching her and marrying her
doc.differences:
Schauer 55 (adesp.)
Jocelyn 291-292 (Ennius' Thyestes, first two lines of 1/12, followed by Schauer 56)
Warmington 356-7 (Ennius' Thyestes, first two lines of 8/14)
Vahlen2 357-9 (Ennius, Thyestes 11/14)
Ribbeck2/3 108-109 (adesp.):

Vahlen: additionally interprets the words Iovis iste quidem pronepos after Cicero's quotation of line 2 (Tusc. 3.26) as also belonging to Ennius' text.

Ribbeck: Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socro
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctust nuptiis.
doc.apparatus:
1 socru] Bentley: socero G K R V socro codd. det.
2 nanctus] G V R1: nactus R2 K: nactus est p: nactu’st coni. Davies: nanctust

The better manuscripts have socero, the worse manuscipts socro. To restore a trochaic verse a bisyllabic word is required, but a second declension noun socrus, socri is totally unattested. On the other hand, the grammarians report that the fourth declension noun socrus, socrūs, always feminine in later Latin, could also be masculine, hence Bentley’s conjecture socru. See further the commentary.
Syntactical difficulties with the participle nanctus/nactus inside the relative clause has prompted the conjectures of the cliticized forms na(n)ctu(‘)st. Again, see further the commentary below.
doc.commentary:
prognatus:] This word appears comparatively rarely in comedy (only 14 occurrences in Plautus), but is more frequent in early Republican epic and tragedy (cf. Fraenkel (1957) 82 n.4). It also is found in the epitaph of Scipio Barbatus, which is written in Saturnian verse: Cornelius Lucius Scipio Barbatus Gnaivod patre prognatus / fortis uir sapiensque… (“Cornelius Lucius Scipio Barbatus, the offspring of his father Gnaeus, was a brave and wise man…”) CIL VI 1285). prognatus can refer to any descendant – in the Scipio Barbatus epitaph it refers to a son, in TrRF adesp. 55 it refers to a ‘grandson’, Thyestes, whose grandfather was Tantalus.

socru]The more common form of this noun was the second declension socer (nom.), soceri (gen.). The fourth declension socrus was almost always feminine, with the meaning ‘mother in law’ (thus in all 19 of Cicero’s uses of socrus). Aside from this verse, the only other two instances of masculine socrus (to mean ‘father-in-law’) are found in Nonius Marcellus and Priscian, both quotations from early Republican texts from the stage. Nonius Marcellus comments of socrus, socrus et masculine genere ueteres dici posse, and illustrates the unusual form by quoting a verse from Naevius’ Pellicus, a fabula togata (a comedy ‘in Roman dress’): desim socru tuo, fratri patrueli meo? . Priscian, after offering an example from Juvenal (Sat. 6.231) of the feminine usage, notes: uetustissimi tamen communiter ‘hic’ et ‘haec socrus’ proferebant. Accius in Atreo: simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta a socru (/…) possedit suo. It is worth observing that, as Miskell (1959, 102-3) points out, no manuscripts actually have any evidence for socru, rather it is the product of inference by Bentley, who has been followed by other editors, based on what the grammarians are saying. (It could be the case, however, that the fourth and fifth century grammarians could themselves be using faulty manuscripts, but given the conjecture solves the metrical difficulty, it seems worth adopting.

nanctus]The better manuscripts are divided between nanctus and nactus, both forms appear in Plautus, although nanctus is rarer, and both forms scan identically. Given the slight trend towards nanctus in the manuscripts (in the Gudianus 294 (G) manuscript, the Cambrai manuscript (cod. Cameracensis, K, and in the first hand of the Parisinus Regius 6332 (R) MS, before it was corrected by the second hand, and the principle of lectio difficilior with nanctus being rarer, it makes sense to lean towards that form. There is also a slight syntactical issue that we have no finite verb inside the relative clause introduced by qui. One possibility is that there was originally a cliticized form of nanctus est. Davies conjected nactu’st in , and Tregder nanctust in 1841, both on the basis of the codex Pithoeanus (p), which had the reading nanctus est. The Pithoeanus is a lost manuscript, but its readings were collated in 1597 in an editio Gryphianus of 1535 (cf. Davies (1730) i n.1, Tregder (1841) xii, Giusta (1984) xxiii). However, its readings do not have great weight and it is descended from R and K anyway. Tregder’s suggestion was adopted by Ribbeck, although other editors of the tragic fragments have not sided with a cliticized form. Cliticized forms are prone to be lost in manuscripts, because scribes “rarely comprehended the spelling -st” (Pezzini (2015) 56). Pezzini (2015, 63) places TrRF adesp. 55 in the category of readings where “the reading of manuscripts is often linguistically acceptable and thus the contracted form is less necessary” (p. 61). (And see further Pezzini (2015) esp. 227-231 on the the cliticization of esse.) But, as Jocelyn points out, another Ennian fragment, TrRF 23, contains such an ellipse of est in a relative clause: uidi, uidere quod me passa aegerrume. Thus conjecturing a contracted form is also unnecessary here, and the evidence of the codex Pithoeanus for doing so is weak.
doc.reconstruction:
The two fragments TrRF adesp. 55 and TrRF adesp. 56 are quoted in the same section of Cicero’s Tusculanae Disputationes (3.26) and Cicero is clearly referring to the same character, i.e. the son of Pelops, Pelope natus.

Those editors venturing attributions (Colonna, Welcker, Ribbeck, Vahlen, Jocelyn, Warmington, Traglia) all further assume that this son of Pelops is Thyestes (not Atreus, although he was also the son of Pelops and Hippodamia), given the uis sceleris of Fr. 56, a corruption due to a hideous crime, which does indeed seem to fit Thyestes’ fate. Cicero’s point is that grief can obliterate the dignity of a man: the plot of the cena Thyestea would fit this trajectory of a fall from grace.

A further assumption is that both fragments are not just about Thyestes but also from the same play, Ennius’ Thyestes. Cicero might possibly be drawing together references to Thyestes from different plays (e.g. Accius’ Atreus), however, the attribution to Ennius’ Thyestes seems the most plausible.

Ribbeck (1875) thought this fragment occurred prior to the Thyestea cena and connected it with TrRF II inc. 157, pol mihi fortuna, providing Thyestes’ motivation for returning to challenge Atreus and restore his faded fortunes (whereas he located adesp. 56 to the end of the play). Vahlen (1904) also couples these two fragments, but relocates them to after the Thyestea cena, and so instead of the misfortune of exile, it is the misfortune of the feast Thyestes laments.

Other critics think adesp. 55 and 56 were juxtaposed or closely connected. For instance, Warmington (1935) suggested coupling them, as part of a speech by Thyestes to the chorus (made up of King Thesprotus’ courtiers). Thyestes has arrived in Thesprotus after fleeing Mycenae in the wake of the cena. Thyestes reveals who he is and a warning of the corruption (contagio) that he brings. Jocelyn agrees with joining the two fragments, but since he does not think the cena featured in Ennius’ Thyestes, he believes that adesp. 55 also functions as the external audience’s introduction to the character of Thyestes.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero quotes this verse in his Tusculanae Disputationes (3, 26) in the middle of a discussion about grief. Cicero argues that the wise man will not feel grief. He uses Thyestes as a counter-example, to show how surrendering to grief is contemptible:

qualis enim tibi ille uidetur:
Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socru
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctus nuptiis.
Iovem iste quidem pronepos. tamne ergo abiectus tamque fractus.

Bentley, followed by Vahlen, thought Iovem iste quidem pronepos were also Ennius’ words, since they could also fit the Bacchiac rhythm. However, most scholars think that they are Cicero’s words and this rhythm is accidental – as Jocelyn (1967, 419) points out, Iovem should precede so that the names are in chronological order, but in Cicero’s wording, the reference to Tantalus as ‘Jupiter’s great-grandson’ comes after the descriptions of him as ‘Tantalus’ grandson, Pelop’s son…’

lines.text: Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socru
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctus nuptiis
Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beniuoli.
Deum regnator nocte caeca caelum e conspectu abstulit

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

lines.text: cui manus materno sordet sparso sanguine

doc.translation: Whose hand is soiled by his mother’s spattered blood.
doc.differences:
TRF: 7 (Accius' Aegisthus 4/5)

Ribbeck: 23 (Accius' Aegisthus 2/5)

Warmington: 12 (Accius' Aegisthus 5/5)

Franchella: 403 (Accius' Aegisthus 2/5)

D'Antò: 23 (Accius' Aegisthus 2/5)


Warmington:

Whose hand is fouled and spattered by his mother’s blood.

Franchella:

cui manu’ materno sordet sparsa sanguine

Le cui mani sono lorde del sangue materno sparso.

D'Antò:

cui manus materno sordet sparsa sanguine

A lui la mano è sporca, spruzzata di sangue materno.

Dangel:

dont la main est souillée par le sang maternel qui l’a aspergée.
doc.apparatus:
cui] cui H2G: cum LFH1E

sparso] sparso codd.: sparsa Bentinus

sordet sparsa] sordet sparsa codd.: sparsa sordet malit Ribbeck

Both cui and cum are found in the manuscripts. What is surely the correct reading, cui, is found in G and as a correction by a later hand in H (British Library, Harleianus 2719: the correction in H derives from the same source as manuscript G [= codex Gudianus, cod. Gud. Lat. 96, Herzog-August-Bibliothek, Wolfenbüttel]: cf. Lindsay 1903, I.xxvii-xvviii). Bentinus, a sixteenth-century scholar who contributed a version of the text of Nonius along with Castigationes which was printed in the new Aldine edition of Niccoló Perotti’s Cornucopiae sive linguae Latinae commentarii (Venice 1526, col. 1289), suggested sparsa instead sparso in order to make it agree with materno sanguine rather than manus. Bentinus’ suggestion, accepted by Ribbeck, Lindsay, Warmington, and others (such as Franchella and D’Antò), is designed to correct a perceived imbalance in the paradosis in which the noun manus is left without an adjective in agreement with it, while sanguine has two. Whether one reads sparsa or sparso is purely a matter of stylistic preference.

Lange (1851, 5) offers two different translations depending on whether we read (i) sparso: ‘dess Hand befleckt ist, vom vergossnen Mutterblut’; or (ii) sparsa: ‘dess Hand befleckt ist, nass vom mütterlichen Blut’.
doc.commentary:
sparso sanguine: a combination attested at Ennius Thyestes F 132.2 TRF = 297 Jocelyn (saxa spargens tabo sanie et sanguine atro ‘sprinkling the rocks with gore, filth, and black blood’) and, in Augustan poetry, at Verg. Aen. 8.645, 11.82, Ov. Met. 7.845, 11.367, 13.532, 14.408, 15.790. For alliterative pattern in a similar context, cf. Adesp. F 259 Hollis = Charisius Instit. Gram. 1, I.13.20 GLK: tune Clytaemestrae foedasti uiscera ferro? ‘was it not you who mutilated Clytemnestra’s entrails with a sword?’

The phrase sordet sparso sanguine need not imply that Orestes was depicted on stage covered in blood: it might rather suggest that Orestes, having incurred pollution for murdering his mother, now finds himself in the longstanding condition of being metaphorically stained by her blood and therefore contaminated.
doc.reconstruction:
The line has been compared with numerous Greek ‘models’: Aesch. Ag. 1280–1 (ἥξει γὰρ ἡμῶν ἄλλος αὖ τιμάορος‎, | μητροκτόνον φίτυμα, ποινάτωρ πατρός ‘There will come yet another to take vengeance for us, an offspring that will kill his mother and exact requital for his father’ [tr. Sommerstein]: cf. Ribbeck1, 298), 1318–20 (ὅταν γυνὴ γυναικὸς ἀντʼ ἐμοῦ θάνηι‎ | ἀνήρ τε δυϲδάμαρτος ἀντʼ ἀνδρὸς πέσηι·‎ | ἐπιξενοῦμαι ταῦτα δʼ ὡς θανουμένη‎ ‘when a woman dies in return for me, a woman, and a man falls in return for a man who had an evil wife. As one about to die, I claim this as my guest-right’ [tr. Sommerstein]), Soph. El. 1422–3 (καὶ μὴν πάρεισιν οἵδε· φοινία δὲ χεὶρ | στάζει θυηλῆς Ἄρεος, οὐδ᾿ ἔχω ψέγειν ‘Look, they are here! And a bloody hand drips with a sacrifice to Ares, nor can I find fault with it!’ [tr. Lloyd-Jones]), Eur. El. 1172 ἀλλʼ οἵδε μητρὸς νεοφόνοις ἐν αἵμασιν [νεοφόνοισιν αἵμασιν‎ Nauck] ‘But here they are: stained with their mother’s newly shed blood’, tr. Kovacs). Similarity to some passages in Senca’s Agamemnon has also been posited: Sen. Ag. 1012 (CLYTEMNESTRA: Furiosa, morere. CASSANDRA. Veniet et uobis furor. ‘CLYT.: Die in your madness. CASS.: Madness will come upon you too’, tr. Fitch) has also been posited. Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xii) compares the line to Aesch. Cho. 886 (Clytemnestra to her servant, entering the stage after Orestes’ murder of Aegisthus: τὸν ζῶντα καίνειν τοὺς τεθνηκότας λέγω ‘What’s the matter? What’s this cry that you’re raising through the house?’, tr. Sommerstein). Hermann suggests that in Accius’ version, Orestes is the speaker, revealing his identity to Aegisthus with these words to Aegisthus (as Hermann notes, such a dramatic moment would be worthy of Accius’ talent, ‘dignissimum hoc foret ingenio Attii’). Indeed, the character referred to in this fragment is almost certainly Orestes. Ribbeck (1875, 465) believes, on the basis of this fragment, that the murder of Agamemnon was a dramatic event in the Aegisthus.

While Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xii) believed the speaker is Orestes, Ribbeck argues (1875, 465) that it is Cassandra, who prophetically foretells Orestes’ future revenge against the murderers (comparing Aesch. Ag. 1280-1). Ribbeck further suggests that this fragment is connected with Acc. inc. fab. F 406 TRF = inc. fab. V, v. 655 Ribbeck3 = inc. 33 Warmington = inc. 700 Dangel (uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum) as Cassandra has a prophetic vision of her own and Agamemnon’s tombstones which signals their impending death. The use of the present tense in F 7 TRF (sordet) may correspond to the present tense in inc. fab. F 406 TRF (uideo), if both fragments are part of Cassandra’s prophetic vision. Venini 1954, 325 accepts Ribbeck’s suggestion. For a different interpretation of this incertum, see Fragment reconstruction under Clytemnestra F 142 TRF = v. 37 Ribbeck3 = 243 Warmington = 296 Dangel.

In line with his belief that the Aegisthus deals with the murder of Agamemnon, Ribbeck places this fragment towards the middle of the play (position 2/5) when Cassandra, still alive at this point, makes the prophetic announcement that retribution for the murder of Agamemnon will come in the future. Those who think that the Aegisthus dealt with the revenge of Orestes place the fragment at the end of the play, after Orestes has murdered Clytemnestra (Dangel and Warmington place it in position 5/5). Another possible solution is that the play dealt with the aftermath of Orestes’ murder, in which this fragment could be placed during the prologue (so D’Antò 196).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 170.5-6 Mercier = 250.4-5 Lindsay:

sordet, sordidata est. Accius Aegistho: cui manu’ materno sordet sparsa sanguine.

Nonius quotes this line to explain the meaning of the verb sordēre (‘to be dirty or soiled’): the third person indicative active sordet is said to be equivalent to sordidata est (sordidatus = ‘meanly or shabbily dressed; wearing mourning clothes’ OLD s.v.). Lindsay (and following him Franchella) prints the word manus with elided ‘s’: this phenomenon is known as 'sigmatic ecthlipsis' and is common in Early Latin poetry. It has no bearing on the metre.

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius (ia6). In her critical apparatus (p. 168), Dangel reports the following: ‘istum locum restituit Ribb.3 in duas partes septenarii trochaici uel in unum senarium iambicum’, but there seems to be no evidence of Ribbeck suggesting, in his third edition or anywhere else, that this fragment could be two truncated lines of trochaic septenarii.

lines.text: quidquid est hoc, omnia animat format alit auget creat
sepelit recipitque in sese omnia omniumque idemst pater
indidemque eadem aeque oriuntur de integro atque eodem occidunt
- × - ut, quae tum absentem rebus dubiis coniugem
tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem. - × - × - ⏑ ⏓
Addo huc quod mihi portento caelestum pater
prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei,
agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma ;
quem clam Thyestem clepere ausum esse e regia !
qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi !
– x – ] x – u x ego incipio ; conata exsequar.

doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: For as we saw them playing joyously amongst themselves along the river, scooping up water from the stream with buckets…

Ribbeck 2 : 44-45(Naevius’ Lycurgus 17/24)

nam ut ludere laetantes inter se vidimus † praetor amnem creterris sumere aquam ex fonte

Ed Li: 2 creterris] terris codd.

Edd. rel.: 2 terris studere fecisset sumere aquam ex fonte codd. [Mu,Wa]

sumere aquam ex fonte] codd. [Ri]

Ed. Li.: 1 namque ut ludere] nam vel ludere P7666

sese is debated to be se, the emphatic sese is indeed not necessary, but could be preferred over a solution by †

doc.reconstruction:
The TRF edition places the fragment earlier in the sequence than the other editions and refers to the Bacchae of Euripides as a possible model Eur. Bacch. 761-768 De voce cf. Aul. Flor. 20,1 prima … creterra ad sitim pertinent.

However, the edition by Warmington looks at Aeschylus as a possible model and places the fragment in the narration of the soldiers to Lycurgus about the capture of the Maenads.

context.location: De Compendiosa Doctrina per Litteras ad Filium
context.text:
Quoted twice by Nonius in De Compendiosa Doctrina per Litteras ad Filium to explain the meaning of creterris .

creterra est quam nunc situlam vocant. Naevius Lycurgo: namque ut ludere laetantis inter sese vidimus praeter amnem aquam creterris sumere ex fonte (Non. 878 Li.).

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere

doc.translation: Pitiless waves hurled them, dashed them against hideous rocks.
doc.differences:
TRF: 143 (Accius' Clytemnestra 6/10)

Ribbeck: 33 (Accius' Clytemnestra 4/10)

Warmington: 248 (Accius' Clytemnestra 4/11)

Franchella: 392 (Accius' Clytemnestra 4/10)

D'Antò: 33 (Accius' Clytemnestra 4/10)

Dangel: 292 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/10)


Warmington:

Merciless billows | Did toss them, clash them on to hideous rocks

Franchella:

flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere.

Flutti spietati percotevano, sbattevano contro scogli orrendi.

D'Antò:

flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere.

I flutti spietati (lo) sballottavano, sugli orridi scogli (lo) scagliavano.

Dangel: Les flots sans merci de jeter, de drosser sur d’affreux rochers.
doc.apparatus:
taetra] taetra Aug. MS in margine uel Mercier: et aetra L1: et aethra L2HPEG: aetera P. 7666: aetera Lugd. (VLQ. 116)

Some notes on the critical apparatus:

(i) I have not checked G (codex Gudianus, Wolfenbüttel 96, saec. X).

(ii) Lindsay reports that P. 7666 (Parisinus Lat. 7666) reads et aetera but I do not see the et.

(iii) The codex Bambergensis listed by Lindsay as ‘M. V. 18, saec. X-XI’ now seems to bear the shelfmark ‘Staatsbibliothek Bamberg, Msc.Class.30 (fol. 86r-128r), saec. XI’.

(iv) Dangel reports the reading of E (= codex Escorialensis M. III, 14, saec. X1) as et aethera: in fact, so far as I can see, it reads et aethra.

The conjecture taetra is universally accepted. The critical apparatus of the TRF (IV.225) suggests that the conjecture is due either to A. Agustìn (1517-1586), archbishop of Tarragona who in the second half of the sixteenth century produced an edition of Accius which was never published in print (cf. Lunelli 1978, Dangel 86-8: the manuscript can be consulted here: http://bdh-rd.bne.es/viewer.vm?id=0000057318&page=1, cf. f. 56r), or to Josias Mercier, whose edition of Nonius was published in 1614, whichever was first. But since Agustìn’s conjecture must necessarily date to before his death in 1586 and Josias Mercier was born in c. 1560 and worked on other projects before turning to Nonius (i.e. an edition of Aristaenetus in 1595, a biography of Pierre Pithou in 1597, and an edition of Tacitus in 1606), it seems highly likely that Agustìn made the conjecture first, although Mercier could well of course have thought of the conjecture independently. On the basis of the extant fragments, in which the word taeter appears five times (374, 553, 556 [abstract noun taetritudo], 606), it is a word which Accius seems to have used frequently.
doc.commentary:
iacere…adlidere: it seems plausible that a direct object such as eos ‘them’, probably referring to the Greeks at sea, should be understood.

ad saxa adlidere: Dangel 321 identifies similar language at Hyg. Fab. 116.1-2: Danai…tempestate et flatibus aduersis ad saxa Capharea naufragium fecerunt. in qua tempestate Aiax Locrus fulmine est a Minerua ictus, quem fluctus ad saxa illiserunt… ‘On account of a storm and adverse winds, the Greeks suffered a shipwreck near the Capharean rocks. During the storm, Locrian Ajax was struck with a thunderbolt by Minerva: the waves caused him to crash into the rocks…’. See further 116.3 militesque plurimi cum ducibus tempestate occisi sunt membraque eorum cum uisceribus ad saxa illisa sunt, ‘several soldiers were killed along with their commanders during the storm, and their limbs and innards were crushed by the rocks’. Though she does not make the point explicitly, Dangel seems to be implying that Hyginus is deliberately alluding to Accius’ language. This is certainly possible (though the expression is not unique, cf. Lucr. 5.1000-1 una dies dabat exitio nec turbida ponti | aequora lidebant nauis ad saxa uirosque, a passage which Munro 18662, II.333 believes alludes to this fragment of Accius) and it might support the argument that Hyginus 116 preserves elements of the outline of the plot of Accius’ Clytemnestra, but nothing can be deduced with any certainty on the basis of this possible verbal echo. Franchella 244 compares Aesch. Ag. 563-6: χειμῶνα δ᾿ εἰ λέγοι τις οἰωνοκτόνον,| οἷον παρεῖχ᾿ ἄφερτον Ἰδαία χιών, | ἢ θάλπος, εὖτε πόντος ἐν μεσημβριναῖς | κοίταις ἀκύμων νήνεμοις εὕδοι πεσών… ‘And if one were to mention the unendurable cold of winter that killed the birds, which the snows of Ida supplied to us, or the sultry heat when the sea used to fall asleep in a waveless, windless noonday siesta’ (tr. Sommerstein), but to my mind the parallel does not seem very close. D’Antò suggests that the liquid consonants imitate the waves crashing against the rocks.
doc.reconstruction: Dangel 321 suggests that the saxa mentioned in this fragment probably refer to the rocks at Cape Caphareus in Euboea, towards which the Greek fleet was tricked into navigating by Nauplius, who shone a beacon near the treacherous rocks, acting in revenge for the murder by the Greeks of his son Palamedes. This was a popular story in what M.L. West (2013, 260) calls the ‘later vulgate’ of Greek nostos narratives and notably features in Seneca’s Agamemnon (557-78). In view of the possible involvement in the dramatic action of the play of Oeax, another son of Nauplius, who, based on the version outlined in Hyginus Fabulae 116, may have manipulated Clytemnestra to take revenge against the returning Agamemnon, it is tempting, though of course wildly speculative, to imagine that an account of Nauplius’ own revenge against the Greeks was given. The play would thus have contained a mention of both the father’s and the brother’s revenge for Palamedes. See further ‘Fragment reconstruction’ for F 146 TRF = v. 32 Ribbeck1-3 = 237 Warmington = 291 Dangel.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 488.11-15 Mercier = 784.10-16 Lindsay:

flucti, pro fluctus. Accius Telepho: flucti cruoris volverentur mihi… idem Clytaemestra: flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere. Pacuvius Chryse: interea loci | flucti flaccescunt, silescunt venti, mollitur mare.

The fragment is quoted by Nonius to demonstrate that the noun fluctus, normally of the fourth declension (fluctus, -us), is attested as a second-declension noun (fluctus, -i).

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: Tell me, in what way has he been captured: by fight or by ambush?
doc.differences:
TrRF: 37 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/24)

Ribbeck1: 25 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 11/24)

Ribbeck2: 37 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 11/24)

Ribbeck1, Ribbeck2, Ribbeck3, Marmorale and Klotz all have the exact same placement, in the middle part at 11/24. Their fragments 10 and 12 are also the exact same, so they assume the same context for this fragment, which is after the Bacchantes ask someone the way (presumably to Thrace) (F25 TrRF) and before Lycurgus’ threat that Liber shouldn’t provoke him (F21 TrRF). pacto eo] P[Qui]

potiti] potiti sitis coni. Ribbeck1, Mueller

pugnan an] pugna an codd., coni. Aldus, Stephanus, Mercerus, Scriverius : pugnan H1[Mu] H[Ri.3] : pugnad an coni. Bothe : pugna, - an coni. Klussmann : pugnane coni. Ribbeck1, Quicherat

Pacto eo instead of pacto eum makes sense, since potior generally is accompanied by an ablative instead of an accusative, but both readings are possible. Nonius however uses this fragment as an example of potior with an accusative. See more on that in Citation Context.

Ribbeck1 and Mueller have proposed to add sitis after potiti in order to achieve the tr7 metre instead of the current ia6. For my view on this matter, see Fragment Commentary.

Pugnan an is the main textual problem of this fragment. pugna an is read in the codices and adopted by a lot editors, which in my opinion is a better fit than pugnan an. An explanation is provided in Fragment Commentary.
doc.commentary:
potiti] Mueller suggests potiti sitis, which he has based on Ri.1, who has proposed this reading in order to achieve the tr7 metre instead of the current ia6. Even though it’s possible to switch between metres, it seems unnecessary to change metre here. Furthermore no one else has adopted Mueller’s suggestion.

pugnan] Bothe conjectured pugnad an in order to avoid a hiatus. A hiatus however is not that surprising within the Lycurgus; there are more instances of this occurring, for example ingeni atque in Fragment 21 of the TrRF. Most editors however prefer pugna an, which I’m also inclined to agree with: it not only preserves the ablative function of the word, it also seems like a logical mistake to write pugnan with -an at the end when the word that follows is an.
doc.reconstruction:
We can conclude for certain that this fragment is part of a dialogue between at least two people on stage and that a male has been captured. These are two indisputable facts. The common view is that the speaker here is Lycurgus and that eum refers to Liber, which does seem like the most logical interpretation. Warmington for example states before quoting the fragment: ‘’Lycurgus asks how Liber was captured’’. Marmorale has the same view as Warmington but also adds that Lycurgus is asking this question to his guards and that this question is asked when he sees Liber. This viewpoint assumes that Liber is on stage when this line was spoken, which is definitely plausible.

Ribbeck (1875) takes his interpretation one step further and combines this fragment with ne ille mei feri ingeni atque animi acrem acrimoniam [F21 TrRF]. He states that Lycurgus most likely didn’t consider his prisoner to be a God, which is why he makes such severe threats in fragment 21. This obviously implies that he places these two fragments next to each other. Spaltenstein also states that Lycurgus isn’t sure at this point that the one being held captive is Liber, contrary to Warmington and Marmorale. By keeping the identity of Liber hidden it allowed Naevius to use dramatic irony of which fragment 21 of the TrRF is yet again a fitting example.

Furthermore Klussmann rightly assumes that it’s very likely that Liber wasn’t captured by fight or by ambush but simply surrendered without resisting. Spaltenstein adds to this argument that it would be very likely that the recipient of Lycurgus’ question responded with ni pugna ni dolis, which in return would be a possible explanation for sine ferro ut pecua manibus ut ad mortem meant [F27 TrRF]. He also states that this could be seen as the start of the confrontation between Lycurgus and Liber, since it’s possible for him to ask this question to his guards even after the prisoners have been introduced to him. However he then refutes his own argument by saying that the direct confrontation between him and Liber happened later with cave sis tuam contendas iram contra cum ira Liberi [F33 TrRf], which he’s basing on the metre usage, being a tr7 instead of ia6.

Bibliography

Marmorale, E. (ed./comm.) (1950). Naevius Poeta. Florence.

Mueller, L. (1885). Livii Andronici et Cn. Naevi Fabularum Reliquiae. Berlin.

Nonius, M., Lindsay, W. M. (ed.) (1903). De compendiosa doctrina libros xx, Onionsianis copiis usus, vol. 3. Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1852). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 1). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1871). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 2). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1875). Die Römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 3). Leipzig.
