context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes this line as an example to demonstrate the usage of potior with an accusative instead of an ablative: Sic potior illam rem, pro illa re potior… Naevius Lycurgo: (Non. p. 481,23-29 Merc. = 772. Li.)

After this statement he follows by giving examples of this occurence in several works, including this fragment of Naevius’ Lycurgus. He quotes: Naevius Lycurgo : Dic quo pacto eum potiti, pugna, an dolis?. He also quotes Terentius, Lucretius, Accius and Pacuvius. In regards to Pacuvius for example he quotes: Pacuvius Atalanta: Regi ut memorabis, nunc regnum potitur transmissu patris [= Schierl: Die Tragodiën des Pacuvius F60]. After presenting these quotes he ends this section with a conclusion stating: Sic et opus est illam rem, pro illa re, indicating once again that potior should be used in conjunction with an accusative, instead of an ablative.

lines.text: Dic, quo pacto eum potiti: pugnan an dolis?
Nec cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia
accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem.
[ATREUS]: qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum;
quod re in summa summum esse arbitror
periclum, matres conquinari regias,
contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus.
Adde huc quod mihi portento caelestum pater
prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei,
agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma
quem clam Thyestem clepere ausum esse e regia;
qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Alas, my fortune, that you pile all these evils upon me
doc.differences:
Manuwald 135 (4/10)

Jocelyn 154 (6/12)

Warmington 362 (10/14)

Ribbeck3 307 (9/11)

Vahlen2 353 (9/14)

⟨e⟩heu mea fortuna ut omnia in me conglomeras mala

Ribbeck: heu heu mea fortune ut omnia in me conglomeras mala
doc.apparatus:
⟨e⟩heu] heu Urbin. 307/308 : eumea P : eu mea rel.; heu heu: coni. Vossius : ⟨e⟩heu coni. Lachmann

mea] mala coni. Pius (1511); mihi coni. Bothe (1837).

1 mala] malas E

The conjecture ⟨e⟩heu, first proposed by Lachmann (1855: 36), is preferred by Manuwald for metrical reasons (see below).

Pius’ conjecture mala for mea at the beginning of the line reacts to a problem with the semantics of the line, the usage of fortuna (see the reconstruction). However, this creates two problems. First, this conjecture introduces an unlikely and unnecessary repetition, a repetition which, from a grammatical point of view, is not even symmetrical since the first mala would be an adjective, the second mala, at the end of the line, a noun. Secondly, mere redundancy that is not part of a witty observation or pun is not a common feature of Ennius’ style. Bothe’s conjecture mihi could work grammatically, but would also produce repetition with in me.

The variant malas, found by Onions in E (the codex Escorialensis M III, 14, from the 10th or 11th century), would not make sense grammatically.
doc.commentary:
conglomeras – in Republican drama, this verb is only found here and in a fragment of Pacuvius; in classical Latin, glomerare more often occurs than conglomerare (Jocelyn 1967: 424).

Fortuna] Fortuna can refer to good luck or bad luck. In this fragment, it appears as misfortune. Fortuna was always ambiguous: there was a temple to Fortuna Mala on the Esquiline from early on in Rome’s history (see Miano (2018) 114-5. In Plautus’ Asinaria, for instance, Philenium complains of his fortune (Pl. As. 515). Cicero mentions Fortuna as a deity but also states that she be capricious and dangerous (Cic. NatD. 3.24.61). Fortuna is mentioned 4 times in Ennius’ fragments (70, 135, 153 and 157). Of these 4 fragments, one belongs to Hectoris Lytra, one to Thyestes, and the other two are incerta, but one of them is likely to belong to Thyestes and the other might belong to Hectoris Lytra, depending on how that play is reconstructed. The prominence and ambiguity of Fortuna in these plays might suggest that Ennius was interested in the volatility of outcomes (similarly to τύχη in Euripides’ late plays or Menander).

There is some discussion among scholars about the metre in which frg. 135 has been written. Manuwald opts for a trochaic septenarius, since she prints eheu (also, for example, Paduano 1974: 48), whereas a iambic senarius hypermetricus is considered possible by Vossius (Vossius 1620: 113).
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment clearly contains an outcry by a character who feels that everything is going wrong. If we are right to adopt either Vossius’ or Lachmann’s emendation of heu heu or eheu respectively, in the process turning the line into a trochaic septenarius, it was likely to have been voiced by a character entering the stage. The lament is strengthened by the repetitive use of words containing an ‘m’. Several scholars interpret this verse as the words of Thyestes (Ribbeck 1875: 203; Mette 1964: 65; Webster 1967: 114). Boyle, for example, proposes that these lines ‘belong to a lament by Thyestes after discovering that he had raped his own daughter’ (Boyle 2006: 81).

Another editor of Ennius’ fragments, Manuel Segura Morena (1984), has compared the invocation of fortuna to the opening line of Enn. inc. frg. 157 (pol mihi fortuna) (a comparison also made by Jocelyn). Morena suggests that the speaker in both fragments could be the same character. In fragment 157, too, fortuna is ‘falling’ on the speaker. In his commentary on this fragment, Jocelyn argues for a linguistic paradox in Ennius’ fragment, since he suggests that calling upon fortuna in republican drama was usually to invoke a positive force. If Jocelyn is right to see this, the theme of a sudden and unexpected change in fortune might have been central to the play. We could draw a comparison with another invocation of fortuna found in republican drama, namely in Terence’s Hecyra (v. 406): O fortuna, ut numquam perpetuo’s data (Jocelyn 1967: 424).

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: As a grammarian, Nonius Marcellus uses the quotation of this verse in order to support his explanation of the world conglomerare, rare in classical Latin, for which he gives two synonyms (involvere and superaddere) before inserting Ennius’ fragment 135.

lines.text: ⟨e⟩heu mea fortuna ut omnia in me conglomeras mala

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Chryses
doc.translation:
There, at the foot of this rock, in the inside, there is a monstrous cave with sand being everywhere on the ground

(Since Festus quotes this line as an example for specus being a femininum, strata should be taken as a participium fem. sg. nom. that goes with specus, not as a fem. sg. abl. that would go with harena)
doc.differences:
Schierl 66 (5/25)
D’Anna 101(6/28)
Warmington 87 (7/27)
Ribbeck3 99 (12/21)
doc.apparatus: There are no different readings nor any conjectures to the text

context.author: Festus
context.text:
The line has been transmitted by Festus in De verborum significatu as an example that specus, usually a masculinum, was also taken as a femininum. Festus quotes three examples, one from Ennius’ Annales (frg. 429 Skutsch [= book 17, frg. 2]), the other from Pacuvius’ Chryses:

Specus feminio genere pronuntiabant antique, ut metus et nepos; tam Hercules, quam masculino stirpis †ut† frons, ut Ennius Tum cau[s]a sub monte alte specus intus patebat; … et Pacuvius in Chryse: Est ibi sub eo saxo penitus strata harena ingens specus.

The fragment from Ennius’ Annales is also quoted in Nonius, see Non. 329 L. = 222.25 M. (Specus genere masculino Varro Rerum Divinarum IV … Ennius Lytris … Feminino Ennius Annalium lib. XVII: … [= frg. 429 Skutsch = book 17, frg. 2]. Pacuvius Niptris [sc. frg. 194 Schierl], cf. also Skutsch ad loc. 1985, 589, who reads Enn. ann. frg. 429 tum cava sub monte late specus intus patebat) and by Priscian in his Institutiones grammaticae 2.260 GLK, who also quotes the passage from Pacuv. Niptra. On the various use of specus as a masculinum, femininum as well as neutrum, see also Serv. ad Verg. Aen. 7.568: specus horrendum] hoc nomen apud maiores trium generum fuit. Ennius feminino posuit.

lines.text: est ibi sub eo saxo penitus strata harena ingens specus
set me Apollo ipse delectat ductat Delphicus.
Pectore incohatum fulmen flammam ostentabat Iovis
ipsus se in terram saucius fligit cadens

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Let him be pieced by the tallest jagged rocks, disembowelled, hanging on his side, sprinkling the rocks with gore, pus and black blood. May he have no tomb to receive him, no harbour for his body, where having let go of human life the body may rest from its evils.[bib-DBAQLYNL-Zimmermann-2009], [bib-ST9MZHRI-Laughton-1951]
doc.differences:
Manuwald 132 (1/10)
Jocelyn 150 (2/12)
Warmington 366-370 (12/14)
Ribbeck3 309-312 (11/11)
Vahlen2 362-365 (14/14)
doc.apparatus:
1-2 evisceratus] †evisceratus†... tabo codd.: asperis / visceratus, latere pendens saxa spargens tabida / sanie coni. Turnebus hoc modo versus dividens

1 evisceratus] evisceratus codd.: visceratis coni. Grotius

2et] et V (Cic. Pis. 43): om. rel. MSS in Pisonem: et ω Cic. Tusc. et Nonius

latere] latere codd. : lacere coni. Gulielmius

sanie] sanie codd. : del. Dusa

3 quo] quo codd. : quod coni. Lambinus

recipiat] recipiat codd. : recipiat se coni. Bothe

4 humana] del. Turnebus

malis] malis codd : a malis coni. Scriverius

Most conjectures are attempts in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries (excepting Grotius’) to wring trochaic septenarii from the lines, but these were made before Bentley in the eighteenth century suggested the lines were two trochaic octanarii followed by two septenarii. There is no textual issue in the manuscript evidence for the first two lines, which are attested by lots of manuscripts for three different texts, and so an attempt to turn all four lines into trochaic septenarii is without warrant.

Both Lambinus’ and Bothe’s conjectures in the third line are attempts to make recipiat have its more usual transitive force, rather than the intransitive usage which arises from the manuscript readings.

Scriverius' conjecture a malis, followed by Levée, Bothe and Giles, is not supported by any manuscript evidence and does not improve the line metrically, but is instead an attempt to bring Ennius' lines into accord with later standards of Latinity.
doc.commentary:
evisceratus] A graphic, poetic word. Grotius’ *visceratis, suggested for metrical reasons, is unattested, and also weakens the crescendo in the lines, as the speaker adds increasingly grim detail to their curse.

tabo, sanie et sanguine atro] “with gore, pus and black blood”. Exactly what fluids tabum and sanies refer to is unclear. The early 1st century AD medical writer Celsus mentions pus in the context of the effects of different kinds of tabes, or wasting diseases, in his De Medicina. Celsus also describes sanies as ‘thinner’ than blood, differing in density, stickiness and colour (sanies est tenuior hoc, uarie crassa et glutinosa et colorata, 5.26.20a.1), while pus is ‘the thickest, whitest substance, stickier than either’ (pus crassissimum albidissimumque, glutinosius et sanguine et sanie, ibid.). Celsus tells us that all three substances, sanguis, sanies and pus, are secreted from wounds, either fresh ones or ones already healing. Virgil refers to sanie taboque fluentis (8.457, ‘as gore and pus flowed’) in the context of Mezentius’ torture of shackling alive prisoners to dead ones, and Virgil’s Acheminides, one of Odysseus’ crewmen, describes to Aeneas and his followers how the Cyclops spilled the fluids (sanies, 3.625) of his crewmates and crushed their limbs dripping with gore (tabum, 3.626) between his teeth. Virgil is also fond of the collocation of sanguis with ater (Georgics 3.221, 3.507-8,Aeneid 3.28, 3.33, 3.622), and later poets such as Ovid and Statius pick up on this.

quo recipiat] An uncommon intransitive usage otherwise attested only in Plautus (Bacch. 294, rursum in portum recipimus; Rud. 880, dum recipis; Merc. 499, ergo actutum face cum praeda recipias) (cf. Eckert (1849) 37). In later Latin, se is coupled with recipiere in this usage of ‘to withdraw to’ or ‘to retreat to’.
doc.reconstruction:
The lines are a curse sworn by Thyestes upon Atreus, that he would die in a shipwreck, as Cicero tells us in the Tusculans. The first two lines present a rising crescendo. It is hard to say whether the third and fourth lines immediately followed, or if there was intervening speech which Cicero has omitted. In comedy octonarii are comparatively uncommon and follow particular patterns of usage – for instance, octonarii often occur in units of one or two lines and represent moments of heightened emotion before the speaker switches into another metre (see Moore (2012) 178-9). Octonarii can often accompany entrances or exits, but do not have to, and often a speech which began with octanarii before a switch to another metre can return to octonarii for one line and then a new metre (cf. Pl. Hec. 516-534). If Ennius’ tragic usage mirrored Plautus’ comic usage of the metre, then we should not necessarily assume that the first two lines of TrRF were opening lines, but were rather punctuating a speech full of angry emotion, which is also reflected in the strong and violent language of the lines.

The criticism that Cicero levels at Thyestes’ sentiments, that Pelops should have better educated his son that the body was insensate upon death, and therefore that Atreus’ body might lack a tomb ought to be of irrelevance to Atreus, in fact rather shows the importance that was placed in Roman society upon a good burial.


Precisely the occasion for this curse is not totally clear. Horace in Ep. 5 mentions Thyesteas preces. The grammarian Porphyrio commented of preces: id est: diras exsecrationes, qualibus in tragoediis Thyestes Atreum exsecratur (Porph. ad Hor. Ep. 5.86). And another scholiast narrowed down the window even more specifically: diras. et tales infossus puer, ait, preces coepit effundere, quales in tragoediis inducitur Thyestes cognitis filii membris in Atreum locutus. ([Acron] ad Hor. Ep. 5.86). In Seneca’s Thyestes, the last act contains the revelation of the truth of the cena and after learning the truth, in a long prayer Thyestes initially calls out to the heavens that either both of them should be killed (1087-8), or if only one, that he, Thyestes, suffer Zeus’ lightning bolt (1088-92). But then Atreus continues taunting him and Thyestes does state briefly that his prayers seek Atreus’ punishment too (1110-1). Seneca’s Thyestes, however, is likely reacting to exactly the kinds of preces uttered by Thyestes in earlier tragedies – and these could well be in Sophocles’ Thyestes as well as Ennius’ and Accius’ versions – where Thyestes invoked simpler curses upon Atreus, the kinds of preces alluded to by Horace. The preces seem well-known enough as an emotional climax in such plays to sustain the Horatian allusion, but it’s still worth noting that Accius’ and Sophocles’ versions would be sufficient to justify the phrase in tragoediis used by the ancient Horatian commentators, if Ennius’ play did not include the feast, as in Jocelyn’s hypothesis.


Both Jocelyn (1967, 415) and Boyle (2006, 81) imagine Thyestes uttering the curses in the presence of some third party, not in Atreus’ presence. Jocelyn suggests that if Thyestes had been cursing Atreus while in exile in Thesprotus, hearing that Atreus was setting out in pursuit from Mycene, there would be added piquancy, since Atreus could be expected to sail across the sea from the Peloponnese to find him. But then again, a death through being dashed upon rocks by the sea would be quite appropriate for a member of the house of Pelops to recall anyway: according to the mythographers Ps.-Apollodorus and Hyginus, Pelops threw the charioteer Mytilus (with whom he had been in league to kill king Oenamaus) from a cliff into the sea (Ps.-Apoll. Epit. 2.7-8, Hyg. Fab. 84, cf. also Eur. Or. 990-991, Μυρτίλου φόνον δικὼν ἐς οἶδμα πόντου. Still, Cicero does make mention of naufragium, which does add an element of plot connection to Jocelyn’s suggestion. Ultimately, the question of when these prayers were uttered depends on other elements in the plot reconstruction.

context.text:
The fragment is quoted by Cicero in In Pisonem 43 and Tusculanae disputationes 1, 107 and by Nonius Marcellus 651 Li. In In Pisonem, Cicero quotes the fragment in the context of a discussion on the definition of punishment (poena); he states that something is only a punishment if the person it is inflicted on is guilty, so not every disaster that happens to someone is a punishment. A good person, according to philosophers, can still be happy even though he or she is being tortured. So Cicero does not simply pray to the gods for disaster to strike his opponents, the way he claims Thyestes does in the fragment; instead, he wishes for them to be punished for their sins, which can also consist in harm to their reputations. (Cicero, In Pisonem 42-44.) Note that Cicero does not attribute the lines to Ennius in this passage, only to Thyestes as its speaker.

The passage in the Tusculanae disputationes is part of a larger discussion about death and why it should not be feared. Cicero quotes the fragment, which he attributes to Ennius this time, and comments that the curse would have been grave if Atreus (whom he names as the target of the curse) would still be able to feel the things Thyestes wished upon him; however, in reality, he would never experience hanging from a rock and being left unburied, because he would already be dead at the time and therefore would not feel anything (Cicero, Tusculanae disputationes 1, 107). The third and fourth lines of the fragment are quoted only in this passage, and separately from the first two. Therefore, we should not assume that lines 3 and 4 directly followed line 2 in Ennius’ text: there could have been lines between lines 2 and 3 of the fragment which Cicero did not quote. For this reason, Ribbeck and others insert a lacuna between the second and third line of the fragment (e.g. Mueller 1884, 121, Ribbeck 1897, 68, Ernout 1966, 191-192).

Nonius, finally, quotes the fragment to explain the word spargere, which he defines as madefacere (“to make wet”). Notably, he does not attribute the fragment to Ennius, but to Lucilius’ 24th book. If Lucilius indeed wrote these same words, it seems likely that he was quoting Ennius and Nonius did not realise this. Ernout 1966, 191 simply considers it an error by Nonius.

lines.text: ipse summis saxis fixus asperis, evisceratus,
latere pendens, saxa spargens tabo, sanie et sanguine atro.
neque sepulchrum quo recipiat habeat, portum corporis,
ubi remissa humana vita corpus requiescat malis.
<23> Te nihil
hominum fortunae, nihil commiserescit meae?
Finge advenam esse; nihil fraterni nominis
sollemne auxilium et nomen Pietatis movet?
Quemnam te esse dicam, qui tarda in senectute
<24> concoquit
Partem uapore flammae, ueribus in foco
Lacerta tribuit.
Video sepulcra duo duorum corporum.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: Iuppiter Dardanum genuit, Dardanus Troum, Trous Assaracum et Ilum Ganymedemque; Capys ex Assaraco satus, qui statuit Pergamum, Laomedon Priamum et Anchisen edidit.

doc.translation: Jupiter begat Dardanus, Dardanus begat Trous, Trous begat Assarcus, and Ilus begat Ganymedes. Capys was fathered by Assaracus, and Laomedon, who founded Pergamum, engendered Priam, (and Capys) Anchises.

6532: Trousque Assaracum et Ilum et Catamitum [creat],

6534: <Alumento ex Ilo> satus qui statuit Pergamum

6535: Alumento Priamum, Capis autem Ancisem edidit.

Jupiter begat Dardanus, Dardanus begat Trous, Trous begat Assaracus and Ilus and Ganymede; Capys was son of Assaracus… Alumento was son of Ilus, who founded Pergamum; Alumento begat Priamus, while Capys was Anchises’ father.

Not in Franchella.

Not in D’Antò.

Dangel:

Fragment Aeneadae siue Decius 1 (1/13), 676 or inc. fab. XIX:

Iuppiter Dardanum genuit, Dardanus Troum, Trous Assaracum et Ilum Ganymedemque; Capys ex Assaraco satus; qui statuit Pergamum, Laomedon Priamum et < Capys > Anchisen edidit.

Jupiter engendra Dardanus, Dardanus Tros, Tros Assaracus et Ilus ainsi que Ganymède ; Capys est issu d’Assaracus ; celui qui fonda Pergame, Laomédon, donna le jour à Priam et < Capys > à Anchise.
doc.apparatus:
quomodo Laomedonte Troiani orti sunt ] quomodo Laomedonte Troiani orti sunt Hagen 1867, 883: quomodo Laomedon te Troiani (Laomedon Troiani C) orti sunt l: quomodo Laomedon te Troia morti sunt M.

Ganymedemque ] Ganymedemque codd.: Catamitumque Ritschl 1857, 110.

lacunam post satus statuit Hagen

This scholion is printed in Suringar’s specimen of the scholia contained in the Leiden manuscript (VLF 79) in Historia critica scholiastarum Latinorum (1834-1835, II.347): like the Bern scholia, these scholia seems to be affiliated with the work of Iunius Philargyrius. In Ribbeck’s first edition (1852), the fragment is printed in prose. But in his edition of the Bern scholia to Vergil’s Eclogues and Georgics published in 1867, Hagen explains (1867, 721) that he suspects that the text as transmitted in the Bern scholion preserves features of the iambic rhythm of the original text of Accius. He even identified a complete senarius: Capis ex Asaraco satus qui statuit Pergamum. Hagen wrote to Ribbeck to inform him of his discovery, and Ribbeck, in his response, suggested that the original text might have been as follows:

Iouis Dardanum <pro>genuit, Troum Dardanus, 

Trousque Asaracum et Ilum et Catamitum <creat>,

Capis ex Asaraco satus qui statuit Pergamum, 

Alumento Priamum et <Capis> Anchisen edidit.

On the basis of Ribbeck’s verse version of the text, Hagen (1867, 721) posits a lacuna of one line between the second and third lines, on the grounds that Accius most likely mentioned that Ilus is the father of Laomedon and, accordingly, that it is Laomedon who founded Troy. He therefore proposes to supplement Ribbeck’s text as follows:

Alumento ex Ilo satus qui statuit Pergamum

Capis ex Asaraco .  .  .  .  .  .  .

In his second and third editions, Ribbeck prints a text which contains a modified version of Hagen’s suggestion:

Iouis Dardanum progenuit, Troum Dardanus

Trousque Assaracum et Ilum et Catamitum [creat],

Capis ex Assaraco .  .  .  .  .  .  .

<Alumento ex Ilo> satus qui statuit Pergamum

Alumento Priamum, Capis autem Ancisem edidit.

Lana (1959, 297-8), while accepting the need to posit a lacuna to account for the lack of reference to Laomedon, the founder of Troy, suggests a less intrusive supplement on the grounds that it is more prudent to print the fragment in prose. He proposes: Capys ex Assaraco satus, <ex Ilio Laomedon> qui statuit Pergamum.
doc.commentary: Capys ex Assaraco satus: Lana (1959, 297 n. 2) compares a line from Book 1 of Ennius’ Annales (28-9 Skutsch = F 23 FRL: Assaraco natus Capys optimus isque pium ex se | Anchisen generat, ‘to Assaracus was born the excellent Capys, and he in turn begat faithful Anchises’, tr. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL I.135), a line which seems to be based on Hom. Il. 20.239 Ἀσσάρακος δὲ Κάπυν, ὁ δ᾿ ἄρ᾿ Ἀγχίσην τέκε παῖδα, ‘and Assaracus begat as a son Capys, and he Anchises’.
doc.reconstruction: Technically, this fragment is listed in a section of the Accius volume in the TRF series entitled ‘Operum incertorum fragmenta tragoediis fortasse tribuenda’ (IV.592-602). In his first edition (p. 319), Ribbeck suggested that this fragment might belong to the prologue of the Antenoridae, which he believes contained an account of the ancestry of Priam. Warmington (II.565) concurs, tentatively. Lana (1959, 297-8) suggested that it might belong to the Atreus, and connected it with Accius T 25 TRF (Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomai Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander), which many scholars believe belongs to the play’s prologue. Dangel attributes this fragment to one of Accius’ fabulae praetextae, the Aeneadae siue Decius, as F 1 (1/13), 676, but she also includes among the fragments of uncertain play (inc. fab. XIX), thus suggesting that it might also belong elsewhere, for example in Atreus.
doc.metrical_analysis: The text of the fragment is printed by TRF in prose. On the attempts that have been made to turn it into verse, see ‘Apparatus criticus’.

context.author: Scholia Bernensia
context.text:
Schol. Bern. Verg. Georg. 1.502, p. 883-884 Hagen: LAOMEDONTEAE. Laomedon pater Priami; quomodo Laomedonte Troiani orti sunt, Accius docet: ‘Iuppiter Dardanum genuit, Dardanus Troum, Trous Assaracum et Ilium Ganymedemque; Capys ex Assaraco satus qui statuit Pergamum, Laomedon Priamum et Anchisem edidit’.

The so-called ‘Bern scholia’ are an anonymous commentary on the Eclogues and on part of the Georgics found primarily in a ninth-century Carolingian manuscript held in Bern (Burgerbibliothek, MS 172). Related, but less complete, material is found in two other Bern manuscripts (MS 165 and 167). The material found in this scholia is believed ultimately to derive from the fifth-century CE Vergilian commentator Iunius Philargyrius. Philargyrius, along with a certain ‘Titus Gallus’ and ‘Gaudentius’, whose names are also mentioned in the scholia, are sometimes thought to have formed part of a putative ‘Milan school’ of Vergilian scholarship, the existence of which is purely conjectural. It is generally agreed that the glosses in the form in which they survive today derive from a compilation made in the seventh century CE by the Irish abbot Adomnán of Iona (Funaioli 1930, 61-2; Ziolkowski–Putnam 2008, 674-6).

lines.text: Video sepulchra duo duorum corporum
Mulier una duum virorum.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Stasiastae
doc.translation: When suddenly out of a crazy pain the soul will have left this body
doc.differences:
Dangel: 2 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 2/4)

Ribbeck: 2 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 2/4)

Warmington: 611(Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 3/4)

D’Anto: 2 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 2/4)


cum subito e deliro dolore hoc anima corpus liquerit

Ribbeck 1: suum sibi de illoc dolore hoc anima corpus liquerit

Ribbeck2: cum sub crudelei dolore hoc anima corpus liquerit

Ribbeck 3: tum subinde levi dolore hoc anima corpus liquerit

Warmington: tum si ibi de dolore hoc anima corpus liquerit

D’Anto: cum subito levi dolore hoc anima corpus liquerit

Although the editors provide different readings of the fragment, they all seem to agree that the fragment is about Lycurgus’ punishment and is placed at the middle or end of the play.
doc.apparatus:
cum] cum Junius Vossius : tum codd.

subito e deliro dolore] subidae (vel sibidae) deloidelore (LE Gen HP3GW) [Ribb 1, Warm, D’Anto, Dangel]: delordelore P2: subidoloe dolore B[Dangel] : subito deliodelore F: tum tibi de T: cum sub (vel tum si) crudeli dolore Buechelerus: tum sub idoloc de lore hac Basil: tum libido deleide lore hoc mss Merceri: cum subito dolore Junius : cum subito ex illo dolore Vossius: tum tibi de dolore Turnebus advers. XXIX28: tum si ibi dolore Onions: si vitae de dolore Quicherat: subito de illo dolore Bothe1: tum ibi dolore Bothe2: coni. suum sibi de illoc dolore Ribbeck1: cum sub crudelei dolore Ribbeck2: tum subinde levi dolore Ribbeck3: tum si ibi de dolore Lindsay, Warmington: subito levi dolore D’Anto

It is not clear how the line began because manuscripts have transmitted different readings that do not make sense, like subidae deloidolore. Many alternative readings have been suggested. What editors do seem to agree upon is that the line contains dolore (pain). However, some specify the pain by adding an adjective (Ribbeck 23, Dangel), others propose to read certain adverbs, like ibi (Lindsay, Warmington) or subito(D’Anto).
doc.commentary:
cum] The manuscripts have transmitted tum. Junius and Vossius have written down cum. Several editors have taken over this change, like Dangel, D’Anto and Ribbeck2. They think the line is part of a dependent clause, which gives more information about the (temporal) circumstances of the main verb or is used in cases where the speaker is involved in the main verb of the independent clause. It is also possible to read cum as a preposition. Some editors stick with tum, for example Ribbeck3 and Warmington. Ribbeck3 combines tum with subinde. The line then becomes an enumeration and follows up on an action mentioned before. Warmington makes a conditional clause out of the line with tum si.

dolore] the word dolor can indicate physical pain as well as emotional pain, like grief, see s.v. dolor in OLD. What kind of pain is meant, should be deducted from the context. Wilson (2013, 130), however, points out that in case of a pain, caused by torture, the Latin word cruciatus would be used. Following this reasoning, the more neutral use of dolor would then not necessarily refer to the torture of Lycurgus. Cf. s.v. cruciatus in OLD. See also Cicero Tusculanis Disputationis II.XV.35-36 for the distinction between dolor and labor.

subito e deliro] Since dolor can refer to different kinds of pain, Wilson (2013, 140) states that it is necessary to use the context to determine the nature of the pain and that the pain is often specified by an adjective, a ‘qualifier’. In this fragment, it is difficult to determine the context due to the textcritical problem. Manuscripts have transmitted subidae/sibidae/subito deloidelore. Editors have made some suggestions for alternative readings. Ribbeck has specified the pain in two ways. In his second edition, he reads sub crudelei dolore. A possible interpretation is then pain deriving from torture. In his third edition, however, he reads levi dolore. D’Anto also refers to the pain als ‘light’. Although the adjective qualifies the strength of the pain, it is still unsure if the body in question will experience physical or emotional/mental pain. The suggestion also seems less likely regarding the mythological stories, as told by Hyginus in Fabulae 132 and Diodorus Siculus in Bibliotheca Historica III.65. Warmington does not read an adjective here. Dangel suggests deliro and interprets the pain as a ‘mental pain’. This would be in line with Diodorus Siculus’ version of the story.

hoc] The hoc can indicate several things, s.v. hoc in the OLD. The speaker might refer to someone or something that is nearby him. This means that there is another person on stage to whom the speaker directs his words. Of course, it might refer to someone who is not present on stage but has been mentioned in the previous lines. However, hoc can also refer to the speaker himself or parts of his body. This means that the speaker would refer with hoc corpus to his own body. Thus, the speaker would be the one to undergo the pain. Yet, it is not clear if hoc should be read with corpore, because hoc could also correspond in case with deliro dolore. If that is the case, the speaker could refer to physical or emotional pain that he described in previous lines and of which he now describes the consequences.

liquerit] can be either 3rd sg. fut. ex. ind. act. or 3rd sg. perf. subj. act. According to Kühner and Stegman (1955, 147), the futurum exactum occurs often in simple indepedent clauses to indicate the determined result of the future action, especially in Roman comedies. Because of the uncertainty about cum/tum it is not possible to say with certainty if the line is part of the dependent or independent clause. Yet, the futurum exactum indicates that the speaker talks about an event that will be completed in the future. In this case, the speaker might predict someone’s death or he might (indirectly) threaten someone. If we read liquerit as a subjunctive, the line indicates circumstances under which or by which the action of the independent clause has happened. The use of the perfect in the dependent clause, indicates that the main verb of the independent clause is either a present indicative, future indicative, or a future perfect indicative. The speaker possibly talks about a certain action, which takes or will take place after someone’s death.
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment has been attributed by Nonius to Accius’ Stasiastae. There are some textcritical problems with the beginning of the line. However, we can conclude that the speaker speaks of or predicts someone’s death. It is possible that the speaker addresses someone on stage, because of the use of hoc, which indicates closeness. Yet, it is also possible that the speaker talks about someone who has been mentioned before or that he speaks of his own death.

It is likely that the line refers to Lycurgus’ punishment and his death. A possible speaker is Dionysus, who predicts or talks about his revenge on Lycurgus. What kind of punishment is ambiguous. It is possible the speaker (Dionysus) refers to Lycurgus who will be tortured and crucified (Diodor. Sic. III.65), which Warmington suggests. It might also refer to Lycurgus’ death after he has gone mad (Hyginus 132). Dangel seems to attribute the line to this context. Moreover, it is possible that the line is part of Dionysus oracle prediction to the Edonians that the barren Thracian land will only become fertile again, when Lycurgus has been killed (Apollodorus III.5.1). Another option, but less likely, is that the speaker is one of the Edonians, who explains Dionysus wish to kill Lycurgus.

The line could also be attributed to Lycurgus. He might plot the death of Dionysus (Diodor. Sic. III.65). The fragment would then belong more to the beginning of the play. It seems unlikely that Lycurgus would have someone else in mind, except for Dionysus. Although he kills in some versions of the story his wife and son Dryas, the words anima and corpus, would indicate that Lycurgus is aware of what is happening and that he knows he kills a human instead of branch of vines. The versions of Apollodorus and Hyginus indicate that Lycurgus was either driven mad or intoxicated when he committed these crimes. There is also the possibility that Lycurgus speaks of his own death. This would, however, refute the reading of Dangel’s e deliro dolore, since Lycurgus seems to foresee what will happen.

It cannot be excluded that the line belongs to a messenger since the speaker mentions someone’s death, a violent act, which was (probably) not performed on stage. Moreover, the verb liquerit can be read as a perfective subjunctive. Although a perfect subjunctive is often preceded by a present indicative in the independent clause, the praesens historicum occurs regularly in a messenger speech to talk about past events. A messenger could thus report on someone’s death, for instance Lycurgus, or possibly Lycurgus’ son or wife.


Glare, P.G.W. 1982. The Oxford Latin Dictionary. Oxford.

Kühner, R. and Stegmann, C. 1955. Ausführliche Grammatik der lateinischen Sprache. Darmstadt.

Wilson, N. 2013. ‘The semantics of pain in the Greco-Roman antiquity.’ Journal of the History of the Neurosciences 22: 129-143.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: […] sweet sounding song
doc.differences:
Ribbeck 2.: 23
Ribbeck 3.: 20
Warmington: 35
Marmorale: p. 192
Klotz: 23
doc.apparatus:
Ed. Li. suavisonum] suave summum codd. 
Edd. rel. suavisonum] suave summum codd.[Ri,Kl,Wa,Marm,Tra] (L H G) [Mu] (F H L P G E P7579) [On]
doc.commentary: Suavisonum is a typical Naevian neologism, comparable to silvicola and thyrsigera. Perhaps, these words are a translation of Euripides' ἀδυβοᾳ (Eur. Bacch. 127). In that case, it is not unlikely that the words in this fragment apply to the song of the Bacchants, as they do in Euripides. This would mean that they apply to the Bacchants at the time that they are still peaceful. The line can be read iambicly, in which case thy would most likely be spoken out by the leader of the choir. If we read them as a trochee, they would probably be spoken out by the choir of Bacchants themselves, which would make the line a commentary on their own song. Another option is that the line is part of a messenger speech. Also, the play might include a Orpheic element, which means that the line may also apply to a song of Orpheus’s song.
doc.reconstruction:
Nonius Marcellus De compendiosa doctrina
The line is cited by Nonius to demonstrate that melos can be a neuter.

lines.text: [...] suavisonum melos

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: quae mulier una
usurpat duplex cubile

doc.translation: ...she who, though one single woman, occupies a double marital bed.
doc.differences:
TRF: 84 (Adespoton)

Ribbeck: 127-128 (Adespoton)

Warmington: 131-132 (Adespoton)

Franchella: 37-38 (Adespoton)

Not in D'Antò's edition.

Not in Dangel's edition.

Warmington:

Who, though one woman, enjoys a two-fold bed.

Franchella:

quae mulier una

usurpat duplex cubile.

La quale donna da sola use due talami.
doc.apparatus:
usurpat] usurpat DH: -ato M: -ar V

For a list of manuscripts and their respective sigla, see Shackleton Bailey 1988, iii-v. On the textual tradition of Cicero's Epistulae ad familiares, see Rouse apud Reynolds 19862, 138-42.
doc.commentary:
quae mulier una: the relative pronoun quae seems to be referring back to a proper name, e.g. Clytemnestra. mulier una would then function as an apposition, probably with an adversative connotation, i.e. ‘she who, though one single woman, …’.

usurpat: possibly echoing Roman legal language (OLD s.v. usurpo 1b ‘(spec.) to take possession of (something possessed, but not owned, by another) so as to prevent his usucapio; (pass. in middle sense, applied to the action of a wife who, to avoid passing into her husband’s manus as a result of one year’s unbroken cohabitation, absents herself for three nights during that period cf. Gaius Inst. 1.111)).

duplex cubile: in poetry, cubile can sometimes have the specific sense of ‘marital bed’ (TLL IV.1270.52-1271.20, cf. e.g. Catull. 61.183, Verg. Aen. 3.324, Ov. Met. 2.592, 10.635). The use of the singular (‘double marital bed’) rather than the plural, e.g. dua cubilia, seems further to underscore the outrage that Clytemnestra (assuming this fragment refers to her) has invited Aegisthus into the marital bed she shared with Agamemnon. When applied to persons, duplex can have the meaning of ‘two-faced’ or ‘treacherous, deceitful’, like Greek διπλοῦς (LSJ IV), though the word is not attested in this sense before the Augustan period (TLL V.1.2259.61-8).
doc.reconstruction: Bergk (1874, 307 = 1884-1886, I.372) combined this fragment with Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF, a fragment from an unknown play preserved in Cicero’s Orator (156, mulier una duum virum), so as to produce the following two-line fragment: quae mulier una duum virum | usurpat duplex cubile. Ribbeck rejects Bergk’s proposition to combine these two independently-transmitted fragments into one single fragment. He instead suggests (1875, 462) that this fragment (quae mulier una | usurpat duplex cubile), along with Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (mulier una duum uirum), might have belonged to a scene in the Clytemnestra in which Electra chastises her mother Clytemnestra for her infidelity. Shackleton Bailey raises a potential objection to the attribution of this fragment to Accius’ Clytemnestra on the grounds that ‘Aegisthus is generally held to have seduced Clytemnestra during her husband’s absence’ (II.331). While it is true that the speaker’s point does seem to be that the woman is sleeping with two different men at the same time, it nevertheless seems feasible to imagine that this fragment could apply to Clytemnestra inviting Aegisthus into her bed. See further the discussion on Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF.

context.author: Cicero
context.text: This fragment is preserved at Cic. Fam. 9.22.1 (= 189 Shackleton Bailey) in a letter from Cicero to his friend L. Papirius Paetus (RE Papirius 69) probably between 46 and 44 BCE, illustrating the Stoic principle, which Cicero attributes to Zeno, the founder of the Stoic school, that it is proper to call a thing by its name (placet Stoicis suo quamque rem nomine appellare, cf. 9.22.4 ὁ σοφὸς εὐθυρρημονήσει = Zeno apud SVF I.77 part.). Prompted by Paetus’ use of the word mentula ‘dick’ in a previous letter, Cicero says that obscenity lies either in the thing or in the word, and to illustrate this quotes several lines from drama, among which one fragment from second-century BCE comic poet Sextus Turpilius’ The Demiurge (45-6 Rychlewska = 43-4 Ribbeck3) and this fragment from an unknown tragedy (Adesp. F 84 TRF).

doc.metrical_analysis: As printed by the TRF edition (i.e. quae mulier una | usurpat duplex cubile), the metre would seem to be the trochaic octonarius (Raven §53B) because in this metre the penultimate syllable of the line is heavy (ūna). In his first edition, Ribbeck includes a note about the metre of this fragment (Ribbeck1, 216) in which he suggests that, if the text is read as a single line (quae mulier una usurpat duplex cubile), it could be the final part of an iambic septenarius (Raven §44B). Ribbeck notes, however, that there is no certain attestation of this metre being used in tragedy, comparing the dubious examples at Enn. inc. F 205 TRF = v. 346 Jocelyn (aliquot somnia uera…sed omnia non necesse est, = Ennius inc. fab. F LVI, v 401, Ribbeck1) and at Accius inc. fab. F 435 (cum illud oculi⟨s⟩ violave⟨rat⟩ [de Melo: oblivo lavat F], qui invidit [de Melo: incidit F] inuidendum) = Acc. inc. fab. F XXXV, v. 697 Ribbeck1 = Varro, L.L. 6.80): see De Melo II.880 on the latter fragment, and Moore 2012, 184: ‘the iambic septenarius appears to have had a special association with comedy: its Greek equivalent, the iambic tetrameter catalectic, is never used in extant tragedy’. If Bergk’s combination of this fragment with Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (see ‘Fragment reconstruction’) were accepted, i.e. quae mulier una duum uirum | usurpat duplex cubile…, then the metre would be trochaic septenarius.

lines.text: Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beniuoli.
<5> – x – ] x – x – x mulier una duum uirum

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: unde estis, nautae, huc hieme delati?

doc.translation: Where are you from, sailors, who have been carried here by the storm?
doc.differences:
TRF: 429 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 677 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 11 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 675 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 677 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 706 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

Whence were you, mariners, | Brought hither to shore by wintriness?

Franchella:

unde ’stis, nautae, huc hieme delati? ⏑-

Donde siete stati sbattuti qui dalla tempesta?

D'Antò: unde estis, nautae, huc hieme delati? ⏑-

Donde, o naviganti, siete stati qui sbattuti dalla tempesta?

Dangel:

D’où, marins, avez-vous été emportés jusqu’ici par la tempête ?
doc.apparatus: No textual issues to report.
doc.commentary:
delati: the sense of the word used here is understood by OLD (s.v. 5) as ‘(of winds, currents, etc.) to cause to travel (landwards) over the sea, carry’: cf. Plaut. Amph. 701 quid si e portu nauis huc nos dormientis detulit?

'What if the ship brought us here from the harbor while we were asleep?' (tr. de Melo).
doc.reconstruction:
Scafoglio (2009, 103) attributes this fragment to Clytemnestra on the grounds that it is a play, he argues, which Vergil seems frequently to imitate in Aeneid 1 and that this resulted in several fragments of this play being quoted in DS’s commentary.

Bothe (Accius inc. fab. 16, I.251) suggested that it belongs to the Philoctetes (cf. too Ribbeck1, 310, comparing Soph. Phil. 220-221: τίνες ποτ᾿ ἐς γῆν τήνδε ναυτίλῳ πλάτῃ | κατέσχετ᾿ οὔτ᾿ εὔορμον οὔτ᾿ οἰκουμένην; ‘Who are you that with the seaman’s oar have put in to this land that lacks anchorages and inhabitants?’). In his 1875 monograph (p. 541-2), Ribbeck further suggests that it could belong to the Phinidae (cf. Warmington II.569). D’Antò 484 suggests that it could also belong to the Medea or to any other drama which contains a voyage at sea. Taking this suggestion further, Dangel 379 compares our fragment to Accius Medea F 247 TRF ut tristis turbinum | toleraret hiemes, mare cum horret fluctibus, though acknowledges that ‘il est également possible de penser à un récit des Nostoi.'

If this fragment does belong to the Clytemnestra, as Scafoglio suggests, then it would seem logical to relate it to the messenger’s account of the storm that destroyed the Greek’s fleet. Admittedly, though, it is not easy to envision a scenario in which the speaker of this line would not recognise the returning Greeks and would have to ask them where they have arrived from (perhaps a citizen of Mycenae?).

context.author: Servius Auctus (DS)
context.text: Servius Auctus ad Verg. Aen. 1.122, I.56.21-24 Thilo–Hagen: Servius explains that the word hiems can (and does, at Verg. Aen. 1.122) denote both the season (‘winter’) and the meteorological event (‘storm’). He quotes Accius’ line as an example of the word used in the latter sense.

doc.metrical_analysis: Probably an iambic senarius lacking the final foot, i.e. …delati ⏑×.

doc.author: Lucus
doc.title: Testing
doc.translation:
And Jesus answered and said to them, 'Give to Caesar the things that are Caesar’s, and to God the things that are God’s.


ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν Τὰ Καίσαρος ἀπόδοτε Καίσαρι καὶ τὰ τοῦ θεοῦ τῷ θεῷ.

lines.text: וַיַּעַן יֵשׁוּעַ וַיֹּאמֶר לָהֶם, 'תָּנוּ לְקֵיסָר אֵת אֲשֶׁר לִקֵּיסָר, וְלֵאלֹהִים אֵת אֲשֶׁר לֵאלֹהִים.
Omnes gaudent facere recte; mali pigrent. × - ⏑ ⏓

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: I have driven my herd for sale to Delphi, from where I have made my way to this accommodation
doc.differences:
D’Anna: 1 (Pacuvius’s Dulorestes 1/31)

Ribbeck: 6 (Pacuvius’s Dulorestes 6/32)

Schierl: 1 (Pacuvius’s Dulorestes 1/31)

Warmington: 133 (Pacuvius’s Dulorestes 11/34)

D’Anna: Delphos venum pecus egi, unde ad stabula haec itiner contuli

Ribbeck: Délfos venum pécus egi, inde ad stábula haec itiner cóntuli

Warmington: Delphos venum pecus egi inde ad stabula haec itiner contuli.
doc.apparatus:
Delphos] Delfos pler. codd.: edelfos L1 teste D’Anna

venum] versum Ranchinus

egi unde] lunius: secunde codd.: egi inde Vossius, Ribbeck2/3 Lindsay

ad] Iunius, codd.: ab codd.

haec] Ribbeck2/3: ac codd.: huc Muller
doc.commentary:
Delphos] Delphi is considered a plurale tantum, meaning that it only has plural forms. This is not uncommon for cities (cf. Athens in both Greek and Latin), so we should take Delphos as a regular accusative of place to which.

venum egi] Venum is another Richtungsakkusativ, compatible with verbs such as ire and dare. The combination with agere is unprecedented. For this reason, Iunius has conjectured these words to secundum, simply meaning ‘following’ or ‘afterwards’.

This fragment implies that there is at least one speaker on the stage. Seeing as the speaker is introducing themselves and their business, it is likely that this fragment fits at the beginning of the play, hence why Schierl has placed this fragment as the first. The speaker can either be a real livestock farmer, like the old man at the beginning of Sophocles’ Electra, who is just a farmer. It can also be Pylades who pretends to be a shepherd, to further the plan of Orestes to avenge the death of his father. In this scenario, Orestes could present himself as Pylades’ assistant (thereby possibly explaining the title Dulorestes). If these two were pretending to be shepherds, this fragment does not necessarily need to be placed at the beginning of the play, for this verse could also be spoken to Clytemnestra and/or Aegisthus, when either of them questions them on their motives for their presence. Schierl thinks that the trip to Delphi of the ‘farmer’ is related to the plot of the rest of the play. Therefore, he suggests the possibility that Orestes and Pylades have come to Clytemnestra and Aegisthus to bring them a prophecy from the oracle of Delphi.

Nonius has cited this verse to show that iter and itiner have the same meaning, and can be used interchangeably. Itiner is more ancient, less used and could be considered archaic in Nonius’ age.

context.author: Nonius
context.text: 'itiner' pro iter. Ennius Telepho ... Pacuvius Atalanta (fr. 39) ... idem Duloreste: —.

lines.text: Delphos venum pecus egi, unde ad stabula haec itiner contuli

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: … at once calling on the faith in Aegisthus, they will incite the people…
doc.differences:
Schierl: 100 (Pacuvius, Dulorestes 14/32)

Ribbeck: 140-141 (Pacuvius Duloreses 19/32)

D’Anna: 163-4 (Pacuvius, Dulorestes 13/31)

extemplo] extemplo F H L P G E P7579: exemplo F H¹

conciebunt] conciebunt G² L: conciebant G¹

nuncupantes] noncupantes G
doc.commentary:
Ennius ] these lines are seemingly ascribed to Ennius rather than to Pacuvius. Given that there are no other attestations of a play by the name of Doulorestes written by the former poet, commentators have seen fit to emend the text to Pacuvius. This is further made easier, to the mind of Schierl (2006, p. 261), by the fact that errors are not uncommon in Nonius’ writings.

conciebunt ] certain manuscripts yield conciebant which would potentially shift the placement of the lines in the Dulorestes
doc.reconstruction: It appears rather difficult to determine who is speaking and what the passage essentially means. Key to trying to understand the fragment is the interpretation of the word fides. If it is taken as meaning “promise” or “protection”, it could be referring to allies of Aegisthus who would then be calling on his help. Alternatively, if it is translated as “allegiance” or “loyalty”, it could be Orestes who is emitting concerns that there will be reprisals from those who support Aegisthus, should he be harmed. The English word “faith” might perhaps retranslate this ambiguity.

context.author: Nonius

conciere cum perturbatione conmovere. Accius Medea ... Afranius ... Pacuvius ( Scaliger : Ennius codd) Duloreste : extemplo Aegisthi fidem nuncupantes conciebunt populum

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: Go on, in Bacchic manner, carrying the thyrsus, in Bacchic apparel
doc.differences:
TrRF: 32 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 16/24)

Ribbeck2: 34-35 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 9/24)

Ribbeck3: 31-32 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 9/24)

Warmington: 33-34 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 6/25)

Marmorale: p. 194 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 9/24)

Klotz: 33-34 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 9/24)

[…] pergite, / thyrsigerae Bacchae modo, Bacchico cum schemate

Ribbeck2: […] pergite, / Tyrsigerae Bacchae, Bacchico cum schemate

Ribbeck3: […] pergite, / Tyrsigerae Bacchae, Bacchico cum scemate

Marmorale: Pergite, thyrsigerae Bacchae modo, Bacchico cum schemate

Klotz: Pergite thyrsigerae Bacchae modo / Bacchico cum schemate

While TrRF arranges the fragments as per dating of citation, the rest of the editions propose a reconstruction of the play by doing so.

The editors, except for Warmington, print the fragment as the 9th of a total of 24 of Naevius’ Lycurgus. It requires a group of Bacchae processioning with their bacchic symbols and garments, thus the positioning towards the late beginning/middle section of the play, possibly before the dispute between Liber and Lycurgus. Warmington prefers an earlier positioning, namely 6th of 25. In most cases it is preceded by fr. 20 and followed by fr. 25, as all of them refer to some sort of movement of a group. Bacchic connotations are to be found in each fragment, and it is supported that in all three it is a chorus of Bacchae who speaks (Warmington) or rather that the chorus speaks in fr. 20, Dionysus answers (fr. 32) and then again the chorus responds with fr. 25 (Klussmann et al.). In Warmington, although his positioning of the fragment is different, placing it between fr. 18 and fr. 31, fragments 25 and 20 are the next to follow in the same scene.
doc.apparatus:
2. Bacchae] brehcae L H G F P E : breche Aldus : Bacchae coni. Bentinus ad ed. 1527, Lindsay Ribbeck2,3 Onions : Baccae Traglia

2. thyrsigerae] thyrsigerae coni. Bentinus ad ed. 1527 : tyrsigere L G F L E : tyrsygere G : tirsigere H : thyrsigere Aldus : Tyrsigerae Ribbeck2,3 Onions : tursigerae Traglia

2. modo] modo L H G F P E : om. Ribbeck2,3 Onions Warmington Traglia

2. modo Bacchico] modo bacchico L H G F P E : commodo Mueller : modo Bacchiaco Bergk

2. schemate] schemate H2 G E : scemate F H1 L Mueller Ribbeck3 Onions

The problematic reading brehcae of the manuscripts was first ameliorated by Aldus Manutius who, in 1527, prints breche and further corrected to Bacchae by Bentinus’ annotation in the same edition. This reading is also preferred by Lindsay, Ribbeck2,3 and Onions, while Traglia prints Baccae (cf. Plaut. Aul. 3.1.4).

thyrsigerae is also Bentinus’ correction of thyrsigere, present in Aldus Manutius’ edition. The manuscripts attest various readings: tyrsigere, tyrsygere, tirsigere. While Ribbeck2,3, grammatically correcting the reading of most manuscripts to Tyrsigerae, and so does Onions, Traglia chooses to print tursigerae. modo, although present in the manuscripts, is omitted by Ribbeck2,3, Onions, Warmington and Traglia, probably assuming it served the purpose of explaining schemate (Spaltenstein, §1236).

The phrase modo Bacchico of the manuscripts is changed to modo Bacchiaco by Bergk, probably to conform to the meter proposed, while Mueller substituted it for commodo, also to help with prosody (ia6).

Lastly, while some manuscripts provide the reading schemate, including the second hand of H, others present scemate, which was initially written in H and adopted by Mueller, Ribbeck3 and Onions.
doc.commentary:
The meter of this fragment is incertum. Different editors and scholars have proposed different metric forms and, in some cases, different arrangement of the verses. Lindsay thinks of a dactylic tetrameter combined with a trochaic tetrameter catalectic, while others interfere more with verse arrangement; Klotz places the change of the verse after modo, supposing a dactylic tetrameter and trochaic tetrameter catalectic verse, while Marmorale believes that all we have is one trochaic septenar verse. Lastly, it is worth noting that Leo, adding a change of verse after modo, proposes dimeter trochaic catalectic verses.

modo] emphasizes the imperative pergite (OLD 1 b). If we choose to omit it (Ribbeck2,3, Onions, Warmington, Traglia), supposing it merely explains schemate, then we could argue that schemate indicates the manner, fashion. Likewise, retaining it, might point to its translation as apparel, clothing.

schemate] < σχῆμα, which has the meaning of habitus. It could mean either manner, fashion or apparel, clothing (cf. above). The second meaning is also supported by similar uses of the greek form σχῆμα by Greek tragedians to indicate clothing (LSJ, s.v. σχῆμα: Soph. Phil. 223, Eur. Med. 1039). However, it could also indicate the posturing, according to Warmington. Naevius, instead of making use of the schema, schemae (f) chooses to follow the greek form σχῆμα, σχήματος (n): schema, schematis (n), which indicates a discourse with greek tragedy and, perhaps, points back to the second given meaning of the word.
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment requires a group of Bacchants, to which it refers to. According to Spaltenstein, it indicates, along with fr. 31, 25, 23 the existence of a chorus in Lycurgus. Accius’ Stasiastae also features a chorus, but one that consists of followers of Lycurgus. On the other hand, Euripides’ Bacchae involves a feminine chorus of Bacchants and, maybe, this is also the case for Naevius’ Lycurgus (Spaltenstein, §1243).

Concerning the speaker, Marmorale, Traglia and Klussmann believe that it is Bacchus who encourages the Bacchants, while for Warmington the chorus speaks these words, encouraging itself. However, accepting the chorus as the speaker here, the meter (ia6) he proposes contradicts the Greek tradition according to Spaltenstein (§1244), except if they are the coryphe’s words, speaking in between chorus’ songs.

Ancient scholars, as well as Ribbeck (1875), have analyzed this fragment as part of the attack to the Bacchants. Similarly, Klussmann thinks that Bacchus addresses these words to the Bacchants, encouraging them to attack Lycurgus (cf Eur. Bacch. 733, 763sqq), not with weapons, but with their thyrsi, Bacchico cum schemate. However, Spaltenstein (§1241) argues that the battle could not be staged, but just narrated, so he considers the previous interpretations improbable. Ribbeck (1875) believes that these words urge the chorus to dance and assumes its entry on the stage (cf. Eur. Bacch. 83). Warmington also suggests that, at this point, the chorus sings as it enters, attributing the verse to the coryphe encouraging it.

Finally, the most persuading speculation seems to be the one of Spaltenstein (§1245); the fragment is part of the orders of the coryphe or the god, encouraging the chorus of Bacchants, while pointing out their particular clothing or behavior (schemate), probably as they enter the stage for the first time.

Bibliography

Klotz, A., Seel, O., Voit, L. (1953). Tragicorum Fragmenta. Monachii.

Marmorale, E. (1950). Naevius’ Poeta. Florence.

Ribbeck, O. (1872). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (2nd ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (3rd ed.). Leipzig.

Schauer, M. (2012). Tragicorum Romanorum Fragmenta. Göttingen.

Spaltenstein, F. (2014). Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels.

Warmington, E. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: So that I shall see these things become brightened by flames through the work of Vulcan
doc.differences:
TrRF: 24 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

Marmorale: 20 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 20/24)

Ribbeck2: 48 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 20/24)

Ribbeck3: 45 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 20/24)

Spaltenstein: 52-53 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 20/25)

Warmington: 52-53 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 20/25)

[...] ut videam Vulcani opera haec flammis flora fieri

Marmorale: - ut videam Volcani opera haec flammis flora fieri

Ribbeck2 & Warmington: … ut videam Volcani opera haec flammis fieri flora

Ribbeck3: . ut videam Volcani opera haec flammis flora fieri

Spaltenstein: Ut videam Vulcani opera haec flammis fieri flora

Every editor that is taken into consideration here, has chosen to place this fragment towards the end of the play. In fact, they all place this fragment as the twentieth of the fragments that remain.
doc.apparatus:
Vulcani] vulgani L1[Mu,On] H1[Mu] (F1 H1 ) [On] Volcani Scriv., Bo., Ri., Mu., Wa., Marm., Tra.

Opera haec] (L H G) [Mu] : haec opere Qui. : opera haece Mu.

Flammis flora fieri] fieri flora codd.[Ri.3,Marm] (L H G) [Mu] : flaminis fieri flora Ald., Steph. : fimbriis fieri florida Iun. non probante Vossio (1620) 120 | fieri flora Steph., Merc., Scriv., Bo., Ri., Li., Wa. : fieri / flora -◡- Kl. : fíeri flora On. ia8 dimetiens.
doc.commentary: flammis flora fieri] These words mostly change order because of metrical considerations. Hadrianus Iunius, who made an edition of Nonius in the sixteenth century, changed this into: fimbriis fieri florida, so the fragment fits with the lemma under which it has been handed down to us. flammis flora is also very interesting, since this is mostly translated with flowers of flame, but this is grammatically impossible, and disregards the use of flora we see here. First of all, flammis is either an ablative, a dative, or a locative, so it cannot be flowers of flame. Secondly, flora isn't the plural of flos, which does mean flower. flora is (in this case) a neutral plural form of florus, which means blond, or bright of colour. This would explain why Nonius has noted this fragment under flora or florus, since florus is mostly used for hair. This is also supported by some other readings of florus, for example in the Aeneid (12.605) and Accius fr. 255 (Ribbeck). In Lucretius’ De rerum natura, the same use of florus as here, so for fire, is attested (4.452).
doc.reconstruction:
Spaltenstein puts all the different options on display: haec most likely refers to the direct environment in which the play takes place, which Spaltenstein takes as the palace of Lycurgus. He continues by saying that this could be said by Bacchus, but that Lycurgus is also a possible candidate. Lycurgus could be talking about the destruction of his own palace in favor of himself surrendering to Bacchus and the Bacchants. It could also be an adynaton, or something similar to say that his surrender is impossible. Spaltenstein continues by saying that Warmington, Ribbeck, and most people nowadays don't actually think that this is said by Lycurgus, but rather by Bacchus.

There are still multiple interpretations possible if Bacchus speaks this verse, according to Spaltenstein. He mostly focuses on the use of ut, and starts off with the opinion of Ribbeck that this ut denotes a result or consequence. Spaltenstein then moves to Warmington, who thinks of the expression of a wish, but translates it more as an expression of purpose (...that I may see). Spaltenstein also says that it could be a hypothesis, and a display of power from Bacchus in discussion with Lycurgus. On top of that, it could also be an order from Bacchus to the Bacchants. He ends by saying that if we ascribe this verse to Bacchus, it is to be situated more at the end of the play, as opposed to when we ascribe it to Lycurgus, since he has no trouble envisioning such horrors at whatever moment in the play.

Spaltenstein also comments on the possibility of a fire in the palace of Lycurgus. He compares it to the palace of Pentheus in the Bacchae of Euripides and that of Creon in the Medea of Seneca, which both also go up in flames. Spaltenstein uses one of the next verses as well to support his idea of a fire. He brings up TRF 39, late longeque trans †nostros fervere, which he reads as transtros nostros, following the readings of Ribbeck and Warmington, but this reading is not undisputed.

The metre is another point of discussion. All editors agree that there is something missing at the beginning of the line, namely the first foot. After this, there are several options for what the metre should be. One option is the trochaic septenarius, and the order of the verse would then be: flammis fieri flora. The other option is either a iambic senarius or septenarius. For the latter option, we have the reading as is used here: flammis flora fieri. The former is only given by Duentzer (1837), so this is not very likely.

context.author: Nonius
context.location: De Compendiosa Doctrina 156 L (Lib. 2, pag 109 M, linea 21)
context.text:
Nonius quotes this line at the word Fimbriae, according to the codices. More likely, however, is that we miss a lemma in Nonius, which Mercerus has filled in as Flora. He has added a missing citation, also from Lycurgus, which makes the missing lemma more likely. This leaves us with:

Fimbriae sunt omnis extremitas. Naevius Lycurgo: ut videam Vulcani opera haec flammis fieri flora

lines.text: [...] ut videam Vulcani opera haec flammis flora fieri
(Eh)eu, mea fortuna, ut omnia in me conglomeras mala
aspice hoc sublime candens quem uocant omnes Iouem
Nulla sancta societas

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: So that the cattle may go to death without iron, by hand.
doc.differences:
TrRF: 27 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 11/23)

Bothe: p.88 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 16/25)

Ribbeck2: 47 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 19/25)

Mueller: p.10 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 13/27)

Ribbeck3: 44 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 19/24)

Warmington: 45 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 15/25)

Marmorale: p.197 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 19/24)

Klotz: 45 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 19/24)

sine ferro pecua manibus ut ad mortem meant

Ribbeck2: ...sine terrore pecua ut ad mortem meant

Ribbeck3: sic sine ferro pecua manibus sueta ut ad mortem meant

sine ferro pecua manibus ut ad mortem meant

Ribbeck2: ...sine terrore pecua ut ad mortem meant

Mueller: sine ferro, manibus, pecua ut, ad mortem meant

Ribbeck3: sic sine ferro pecua manibus sueta ut ad mortem meant

Warmington: sine ferro ut pecua manibus ad mortem meant

Ribbeck, Marmorale, and Klotz place the fragment towards the end of the play, whereas the other editors have it around the middle. Klotz and Marmorale follow the order of Ribbeck’s editions, which place it just before a possible confrontation between Dionysus and Lycurgus (TrRF 24, 23) at the end of the play. It is preceded in all these editions by fragments which describe the maenads (TrRF 22, 40), and which could have been part of a messenger speech describing their capture. Warmington also places the fragment within a messenger speech, but at a less climactic moment, and he implies that Dionysus himself is included in the capture by preceding the fragment with TrRF 37 and an incerta which seems to describe his clothing (Warmington 43). After this, Dionysus and Lycurgus have their agon. Mueller situates it within a similar cluster of fragments referring to a messenger speech, but places it before TrRF 37, 22, and 40 instead of afterwards.
doc.apparatus:
sine ferro] sine ferro codd. : sine terrore coni. Ribbeck2

pecua] pecora codd. : pecua edd.

manibus] manibus codd. : om. Ribbeck2 : mansueta coni. Warmington in app.
doc.commentary:
ferro] ferrum can either simply mean ‘iron’ or some kind of weapon or implement made from it, such as a sword. Bothe thought that it referred to the unarmed maenads, rather than their willingness to die without being driven on by the sword (Bothe 88), as does Marmorale (Marmorale 197). Buecheler thinks the word refers to chains here (see TrRF pp. 103). Ribbeck2 proposed terrore in place of ferro, which makes the line a septenarius.

pecua] The manuscripts read pecora, which cannot be correct since this is what Nonius gives as the later form of the word. Almost all editors amend it to pecua, except for the old editions of Aldus and Stephanus which give pecuda.

manibus] Various editors struggle with making sense of this word in its context. Warmington calls it ‘awkward’, and suggests the rare word mansueta meaning ‘tamed’ instead. Ribbeck3 inserts sueta meaning ‘customary’ or ‘usual’ after manibus, which agrees with pecua. Marmorale rejects the insertion of sueta or the reading mansueta on the grounds that the metre is a senarius.
doc.reconstruction:
Most editors, such as Bothe, Ribbeck, Warmington, and Marmorale, assume that this fragment refers to king Lycurgus’ capture and intended slaughter of Dionysos’ band of maenads. As such, they place it next to or near other fragments which seem to refer to the maenads, such as TrRF 22. There are several fragments from the play which seem to describe the maenads from an outsider’s perspective, including TrRF 28 and 40 as well as 22, and these could well have formed part of a messenger speech describing their capture. The difficulty of including fragment 27 in this group is that the grammatical and semantic sense of the fragment is hard to grasp: Bothe even calls it ‘scarcely Latin’ (Bothe 88). Warmington places pecua after ut, and translates this as ‘as cattle walk to death’, but without this amendment, pecua appears to be the subject of the sentence rather than a simile. Can we be certain that the line refers to humans who are compared to cattle, rather than a real animal sacrifice? TrRF 19 suggests that this comparison may have been made elsewhere in the play. In it, an unnamed collective is ordered to be ‘lead here’ and are compared to quadrupedis – ‘four-footed beasts.’ We know for sure that there was a capture of some kind in the play as fragment 37 alludes to the taking of a single individual, perhaps Dionysus, who is also taken captive in Euripides’ Bacchae. In his commentary, Bothe says that he prefers the reading “ut pecua, manibus ad,” and interprets the fragment as the maenads feigning friendliness and compliance as Lycurgus and his men lead them to death (Bothe 88). With reference to the miraculous escapes from captivity of the maenads and Dionysus in Euripides’ Bacchae, the maenads perhaps go calmly because they know that their god will release them.

There is, however, no more suggestion that pecua (if taken metaphorically) refers to the maenads rather than to Lycurgus’ men, and perhaps the king himself. The same could be said of TrRF 19, and fragment 23 has someone (probably Dionysus) demand that the king be brought to him. TrRF 38 describes the arrival of someone – again probably Dionysus, who is associated with epiphany and arrival throughout Greek and Roman literature – which has the effect of nos dublicat. The psychological effect of this epiphany, presumably on Lycurgus’ men as nos suggests, perhaps corresponds with the traditional story of the king’s madness, to which the comparisons to cattle and the incoherence of the captives in fragment 19 could also refer. There is evidence within the play that the maenads (and perhaps Dionysus as well) were observed, described, and captured, but bearing in mind the theme of madness and Dionysus’ eventual triumph, we cannot say for sure that fragment 27 could not refer to Lycurgus and his men.

Bibliography

Manutius, Aldus / Nicolaus Perottus, 1513, Cornucopiae, Venedig.

Bothe, Fridericus Henricus [Friedrich Heinrich] (ed.), 1823, Poetae scenici Latinorum. Collatis codd. Berolinensibus, Florentino, Friburgensi, Gothano, Guelpherbytanis, Helmstadiensibus, Monacensi, Palatino, Parisio, Ultrajectino, aliisque spectatae fidei libris. Volumen quintum: Fragmenta, Pars prior: Fragmenta tragicorum, Halberstadt.

Klotz, Alfred (ed.), 1953, Scaenicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Volumen prius. Tragicorum fragmenta, adiuvantibus Ottone Seel et Ludovico Voit, München.

Marmorale, Enzo V. (ed.), 1950, Naevius Poeta. Introduzione biobibliografica, testo dei frammenti e commento, Florenz, 187–203.

Mueller, Lucian (ed.), 1885, Livii Andronici et Cn. Naevi fabularum reliquiae, Berlin.

Ribbeck, Otto (ed.),2 1871,[ed. maior], Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta, vol. 1, Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta, Leipzig.

Ribbeck, Otto (ed.),3 1897 [ed. minor], Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta, vol. 1, Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta, Leipzig.

Stephanus, Robertus & Henricus (edd.), 1564, Fragmenta poetarum veterum Latinorum, quorum opera non extant: Ennii, Pacuvii, Accii, Afranii, Lucilii, Naevii, Laberii, Caecilii, aliorumque multorum: Undique à Rob. Stephano summa diligentia olim congesta: nunc autem ab Henrico Stephano eius filio digesta, & priscarû quæ in illis sunt vocum expositione illustrata: additis etiâ alicubi versibus Graecis quos interpretâtur, Genf (apud Huldricum Fuggerum)

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text:
Nonius cites this fragment as an example of pecua and pecuda being older words for pecora , meaning cattle. He quotes the line alongside examples from Accius, Cicero, and the Gymnasticus, another play by Naevius.

PECVA et PECVDA ita ut pecora veteres dixerunt. Naevius Gymnastico: homines, pecua béluasque. idem Lycurgo: sine ferro pecua mánibus ut ad mortem meant. (Non. p. 159, 5–9 Merc. = 234 Li.)

Mercerus [Mercier], Iosias, 1614, Non., Fulg., Paris (apud Hadrianum Perier)]

Lindsay, Wallace M., 1903, Leipzig.

lines.text: sine ferro pecua manibus ut ad mortem meant
[ATREUS]: Proinde ita parent se in uita, ut uinci nesciant.
pol mihi fortuna magis nunc defit quam genus.
Namque regnum suppetebat mi, ut scias, quanto e loco,
Quantis opibus, quibus de rebus lapsa fortuna accidat.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: I may easily get from (my/his/her?) mind that he (/she?) judges vital…
doc.differences:
TrRF: 139 (Ennius’ Thyestes 8/10)

Jocelyn: 306 (Ennius’ Thyestes 10/12)

Warmington: 350 (Ennius’ Thyestes 2/14)

Vahlen2: 346-347 (Ennius’ Thyestes 6/14)

Ribbeck2/3: 299-300) (Ennius’ Thyestes5/11)

Manuwald: impetrem facile ab animo ut cernat vitalem † babium †

Jocelyn: impetrem facile ab animo ut cernat †vitalem babium†

Warmington:

Impetrem facile ab animo meo ut cernat vitale brabium.

Vahlen2: impetrem | Facile ab animo ut cernat vitalem ⟨h⟩abi⟨t⟩um

Ribbeck2/3: impetrem ⟨fac⟩ facile ab animo ut cernat vitalem abigeum (Ribbeck extends the words over two lines but does not specify the linebreak.)
doc.apparatus:
impetrem facile] impetrem facile codd. : impetrem / di immortales facite coni. et tent. Ribbeck1 : impetrem fac ille coni. Ribbeck2 : impetrem fac facile add. Ribbeck3 : impetrem / facile dist. Vahlen2
ab animo] ab animo codd. ab animo meo add. Quicherat
vitalem †babium†] vitalem babium codd. : vitalem abigeum coni. Ri.1 : vitalem habitum Va.2 : vitale brabium coni. Lindsay

Various attempts have been made to wring a reconisable metre from the line, centring on additions to the first half of the line. As found in the manuscripts, vitalem babium is, at least in part, corrupted. Some editors have chosen to adjust babium to, for example, abigeum (‘cattle-stealer’) or habitum (‘appearance’, ‘character’), where others such as Jocelyn have printed babium in cruces, accepting the text as corrupt but also not accepting any of the conjectures. Lindsay suggested uitaleto agree with the hypothesis of a neuter noun brabium. vitalem has also been questioned as part of the textual corruption, for instance by Jocelyn. See further the commentary and the reconstruction
doc.commentary:
impetrem facile ab animo] The obscurity of the second half of the line makes the first half difficult to interpret as well. Warmington adopts Quicherat’s conjectured addition of meo with ab animo and takes it with the second half of the line, translating ‘May I with ease cause him to adjudge the vital prize to my liking’. However, Jocelyn found a parallel for impetrare ab animo ut in Livy (AUC 43.24.8), where it means ‘to prevail upon oneself to...’ and pointed out other uses of impetrare + ab found in Cicero’s letters (Fam.) 8.12.1) and Petronius (Sat. 52.5). If Jocelyn is right, one could translate ‘May I easily prevail upon (my) mind to contest the vital prize’ (if adopting brabium as below). But perhaps in context the animo belonged to a second person who was being addressed, or a third person who was offstage.

uitalem babium] There is no evidence for what uitalem babium might mean, but the suggestion by Lindsay, followed by Warmington, that they might be a corruption of uitale brabium is quite attractive. The word is otherwise unattested in Latin, but it could be a loan-word modelled by Ennius on brabeion, originally used of the prize in athletic contests. The Greek word is found in Menander (Mon. 653) but is only found with relative frequency in inscriptions and New Testament texts of the 1st and 2nd century AD. The word might have been in everyday usage, and so Ennius might have used it precisely because although it had colloquial overtones, but on the other hand the lack of evidence for it even in more colloquial literary genres like comedy perhaps should discount that possibility.
doc.reconstruction:
The reconstruction of fragment 139 within the Thyestes is hard because of the corruption of this line. The lack of information about the context, the speaker but also the metre or the meaning of this line make it impossible to say anything conclusive about this line.

Ribbeck has another reading: impetrem fac facile, ab animo ut cernat vitalem abigeum (‘easily I get, what he from the mind judges a vital cattle-stealer’) and he names Thyestes as the speaker and places the fragment in the beginning of the tragedy as part of a prayer in a monologue (Ribbeck 1875, 200).

If brabium is indeed the word that lies behind the corrupted text, then this prompts some thought over who might discuss a ‘prize’ or view events of the plot in terms of an athletic metaphor. The corruption has led to many suggestions about the precise wording, the speaker and the place within the play. Boyle, for example, suggests vitale brabium (‘vital prize’) as possible correction (Boyle 2006, 82-83). He infers from this version of the fragment that the ‘vital prize’ might be Pelopia. However, he does not name a possible speaker and he concludes his interpretation by stating it is a “complete guess” (Boyle 2006, 82-83). Actually, in Seneca’s Thyestes Atreus does precisely this. At Thy. 1097 he declares himself the winner of the palma, the palm wreath awarded to victorious athletes. And earlier in the play, he declared his intention to be ‘first’ to do harm to his brother (before Thyestes could hurt him), aut perdet aut peribit: in medio est scelus / positum occupanti, “either he will destroy or be destroyed: inbetween us is the crime, for whoever gets to it first” (Thy. 204-5). For Seneca’s Atreus, he is in a race with Thyestes for revenge. Seneca’s Thyestes, on the other hand, shows no cognizance of this situation. Could Ennius’ Atreus also thought of himself in a life-or-death struggle with his brother? This would make some sense of the expression uitale brabium. The line would then be part of Atreus’ self-encouragement to himself towards his cruelty to Thyestes, convincing himself of the necessity of his actions, in a speech similar to those aforementioned lines from the second act of Seneca’s Thyestes or the words of Accius’ Atreus (Atreus 29-32 Dangel).

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: The quoting author is grammarian Nonius Marcellus. He quotes Ennius to illustrate the meaning of cernere as ‘to judge’ in 261.13-17.

lines.text: impetrem facile ab animo ut cernat vitalem † babium †
di monerint meliora atque amentiam averruncassint tuam!
Scibam hanc mihi supremam lucem et serviti finem dari
quemnam te esse dicam qui tarda in senectute
sollemnitusque deo litat laudem et lubens.
[ATREUS]: .  .  .  quod re in summa summum esse arbitror
Periclum, matres conquinari regias,
Contaminari stirpem ac misceri genus

doc.title: Chryses

doc.apparatus:
adiutamini] codd. Non., edd. Don. : adiuta mihi plerique codd. Don. : adiuta me V Don.

(Schierl 2006, 217)

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes the line in the second book of his De compendiosa doctrina, De honestis et nove veterum dictis, in order to gloss the passive adiutamini as the active adiutate:

adiutamini pro adiutate. Pacuvius Chryse: adiutamini et defendite

The line from Pacuvius is the only evidence he gives for this use.

context.author: Donatus
context.text:
Donatus ad Ter. Ad. 16 Donatus, here, explains the word adiutare in Terence’s Adelphoi, l. 16cf. 15-16: nam quod isti dicunt malevoli, homines nobilis | nunc adiutare adsidueque una scribere, in which the speaker of the prologue reacts to the accusation that Terence had committed plagiarism:

eum adiutare legitur et adiuvare. Pacuvius in Chryse: adiutamini

The transmission of Donatus’ text is difficult, specifically the last two words in the quotation of Pacuvius’ line, which have transmitted by most of the codices as adiuta mihi and in V as adiuta me. Wessner’s conjecture to read adiutamini seems to be reasonable, since otherwise the quotation in Nonius wouldn’t make sense.
