doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Alas, do I not know the rashness of tyrants?
doc.differences:
Schierl: 115 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 28/31

D’Anna: 168 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 16/31

Klotz: 149 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 25/33

Ribbeck3: 149 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 25/32

Warmington: 144 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 19/33

Most editors place this fragment near the end of the play. Only D’Anna and Warmington place it more or less halfway. All editors agree that the fragment should be placed before Orestes executes his revenge.
doc.apparatus: heu non] euno codd.: heu non Merc.
doc.commentary:
heu, non] A conjecture by Mercier instead of the transmitted euno.

tyrannum] The archaic form of the genitive plural tyrannorum.

temeritudo] Possibly because of metric reasons this hapax is used instead of temeritas, which is its usual form. The meaning of temeritas (e.g. ‘thoughtlessness’ or ‘rashness’) should be similar to that of temeritudo.
doc.reconstruction: The fragment is an iambic trimeter. Most interpreters consider the fragment to be a reaction by Orestes to a warning against his act of revenge. With the rhetorical question he shows that he is confident in the rashness or thoughtlessness of tyrants, in this case Aegisthus, which will facilitate his plan. If, however, eheu introduces a true complaint, the speaker is more likely to be someone from the retinue of Aegisthus or Clytaemnestra. He or she would then be complaining about the king's or queen's disregard of his or her warning. Lastly, Elektra could be the speaker, accusing Clytaemnestra (or Aegisthus) of ruling recklessly (cf. Sophocles’ Elektra 520). All interpretations, therefore, place the fragment before the murder of Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus.

context.author: Nonius
context.location: De Compendiosa Doctrina per Litteras ad Filium
context.text:
Quoted once by Nonius in De Compendiosa Doctrina per Litteras ad Filium to show the use of temeritudo instead of temeritas:

Temeritudinem pro temeritate. Pacuvius Duloreste heu, non tyrannum novi temeritudinem?. (Non. Li. 266)

lines.text: heu, non tyrannum novi temeritudinem?

The fragment tells about the Bacchants during their mysteries, who carry high crested snakes with them as ornaments. Ribbeck (1871) think that the speaker is probably a herald who tells Lycurgus about the group of Maenads, the followers of Bacchus.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: But Apollo himself leads me, he lures me, the Delphian
doc.differences:
TrRF: 136 (Ennius’ Thyestes 5/10)

Jocelyn: 303 (Ennius’ Thyestes 7/12)

Warmington: 353 (Ennius’ Thyestes 5/14)

Vahlen2: 361 (Ennius’ Thyestes 13/14)

Ribbeck2/3: 295 (Ennius’ Thyestes1/11)

:

Warmington: et me Apollo ipse delectat ductat Delphicus.

Ribbeck2/3: Set me Apollo spe delectat, ductat ⟨dictis⟩ Delphicus.
doc.apparatus:
1 set] set coni. Mercerus : et codd. : sed coni. Scriverius

There is a problem over word division with this fragment, and where the fragment’s context ends and the fragment itself begins. Nonius’ definition (Non. 139 L.) preceding the quotation reads: delectare, inlicere, adtrahere. Ennius Thyeste: but the manuscripts H and P in which Nonius is transmitted carry the variant Thyestes, the first word of Ennius’ quoted line being et. This variant is accepted by Warmington.

The issue is that usually the title quoted by Nonius is given in the ablative, i.e. Ennius Thyeste (as TrRF 140) or Ennius in Thyeste (TrRF 138), not in the nominative. As Manuwald points out, it is possible that the s was written by someone who thought that Thyestes must have been the person speaking without paying attention to the sentence structure (Manuwald 2015, 9). It is very unlikely that the manuscripts that carry the division Thyestes et do actually record the speaker, given that, although speakers are indicated in the papyri which transmit 5th century Greek tragedy and in the medieval manuscripts of Senecan tragedy, it would be peculiar for Nonius to include such an indication, since it is beside his purposes and at odds with the rest of his practice. Hypothetically, even if the line was copied from a papyrus and the speaker indication accidentally included, it would still be very strange for the full name to be written out in full in the papyrus, rather than an abbreviation. If a scribe copied the indication of the speaker name in full, we would assume that the line is the beginning of the speech, and a new character is entering the stage, otherwise we would expect the name to be abbreviated. The language of the line (in this case, et me Apollo, however, does not fit very well for the beginning to a speech. If Thyeste is part of Nonius' introduction, and set the first word of the line, the spelling of set is possible but rather unusual. Of course, the rarity might have confused a scribe. Some editors have followed Scriverius' conjecture sed, with this suggestion the scribe still became confused and made Thyeste a nominative, and then the ed that was left from the changed word division was corrected to et. But it doesn't seem likely that a scribe would change sed, which is highly familiar, and leave ed.
doc.commentary: Delectat ductat Delphicus: note the multiple alliteration of the d and the -ctat. Delectat and ductat are synonymous words in asyndeton. This is one of the few cases where the longer word precedes the shorter in this kind of construction. (Jocelyn 1967, 425).
doc.reconstruction:
Jocelyn (1967) 412-13 suggests that Ennius’ Thyestes wasn’t set in Mycenae in Atreus’ palace (where Atreus, after calling his brother Thyestes back from exile, serves him his own children to eat) but in Epirus. According to the account in Hyginus’ Fabulae, the line could be attributed to Thyestes referring the oracle which said he would have a child with his own daughter (Hyg. Fab. 87) or to Atreus, who received an oracle telling him to invite his brother back to stop the drought in Mycenae while Thyestes was in Epirus (Hyg. Fab.. 88).

The occasion of the performance of Ennius’ play was the Ludi Apollini (Cic. Brut.78 [= Manuwald TrRF T 20]), the festival sacred to Apollo, which might explain why Ennius might have linked Thyestes so closely to the god.

The metre of the line as transmitted in the manuscripts is lyric, which might suggest that it was spoken either by the chorus or, more uniquely, at Delphi by Apollo’s priest, possessed by the god himself (so the fragment would have functioned as the oracle introducing the prophetic words themselves). A staging of the Delphic oracle in Ennius’ play might reflect growing Roman fascination with Delphi - Livy mentions a number of significant embassies from Rome to Delphi in the period contemporary to the likely performance of Ennius’ Thyestes (AUC 41.25, 45.41), which was part of Rome’s involvement in the Greek political sphere. Livy also recounts famous Roman consultations with the Delphic oracle from early in Rome’s history, in the time of the monarchy (1.56) and the fledging Republic (5.15-6) and during the second Punic War. (Furthermore, if the speaker is a Delphic priest, then the relationship expressed between her and the god Apollo would be exceptional. Scenes of divine possession and compulsion and oracular speech by Apollo are common in later Latin literature, but none express the same pleasure that the speaker of this lines takes in their possession. In Virgil’s Aeneid 6.77ff., the contact between the Sibyl and Apollo is depicted as a battle of wills; Lucan’s Delphic priestess Phemonoe (Luc. BC 5.66ff.) is terrified of the god pavidamque… vatem, 124); and Cassandra in Seneca’s Agamemnon is not pleased at being possessed, but in the vision she is shown of Agamemnon’s murder. Given the caveat that the line would be unusual if ascribed to a Delphic priestess, then the arguments for attributing the line to one of the brothers recalling the words of Apollo could be plausible (Welcker thought they were Thyestes’ words, Jocelyn that they were either Thyestes’ words about the oracle about his daughter or the words of Atreus about the oracle telling him to recall Thyestes from exile) - however, the lyric metre is less likely to be appropriate, making the adoption of the conjecture ipsus to render the line a trochaic septenarius more appealing.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: The fragment is quoted by Nonius Marcellus, 97.29-30 (ed. Mercier). Nonius uses the quotation to illustrate how the verb delectare can be understood as a synonym to inlicere and adtrahere (to allure, to draw).

lines.text: set me Apollo ipse delectat, ductat Delphicus

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Hey you there, listen: the gods, who have power over motion in the heavens and the underworld, make peace among themselves and create harmony.
doc.differences:
Manuwald: 143 (frg. inc. )
Jocelyn: 309–310 (frg. inc. )
Warmington: 371-372 (Ennius’ Thyestes 13/14)

Vahlen2: 342–343 (Ennius’ Thyestes 3/14)

Ribbeck2/3: 122–123 (Ennius’ Chrespontes 6/8)

Ribbeck: Eho tu, di, quibus est potestas motus superum atque inferum,
Pacem ⟨enim⟩ inter sese conciliant, conferunt concordiam.

Warmington: [THESPROTUS]: eho tu...
doc.apparatus:
1 tu: di] studii

The form di as well as dii is transmitted in multiple codices of the quoting authors’ individual works. The reading of the word di is adopted here in order to create a trochaic septenarius. The reading studii may be the result of a mishearing of the scribe as the dictator read the passage. The passage, at least as Cicero and the author of Ad Herennium present it, is an example of bad argumentation, where something is taken for granted which should not be (see also Quoting Author section). Within this context, a scribe might easily believe he (mis)hears the word studii in the sounds of tu dii.
doc.commentary: eho] This interjection is more commonly found in early Roman comedy. However, its appearance in this Ennian tragic fragment (regardless of whether Crephontes or Thesprotum is the speaker) underlines the overlap in language between comedy and tragedy on the Roman stage, more than on the fifth century Athenian stage. motus] motus (=mōtūs) is a genitive governed by potestas (=mōtūs). Although the form motum is present in multiple codices, neither an accusative noun or participle form (of moveo) would fit into the syntax of this verse, so this must be interpreted as a genitive plural. Either variant could have easily developed from the other by scribal error. It is difficult to pick one over the other, and it makes no real difference to the interpretation. superum atque inferum] superi usually refers to the gods; inferi to the dead or the inhabitants of the underworld. However, since the di are addressed, superi must either refer to ‘lesser’ deities, or be a polar opposite of inferi, ‘creatures above’ versus ‘creatures below’.
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment starts with a direct exclamation, which orders the addressee to listen carefully to what the speaker(s) has to say. This interjection is followed by a statement about the gods, who have power over everything and are able to reconcile with one another, thereby creating harmony out of conflict. We can conclude that the speaker is directly addressing someone on stage who has been quarrelling with another character, after which the speaker states that even the all-powerful gods are able to reconcile with one another. It seems unlikely that the speaker has a conflict with the addressee, because the verses seem to be spoken as ‘some words of wisdom’ to the latter.

The surrounding context of the passage in the Rhetorica ad Herennium provides us with some possible identifications of the speaker and addressee. As discussed in the passage above, the speaker in this fragment can be identified as Thesprotus or Cresphontes because both names appear in various codices of the Rhetorica ad Herennium.

Based on the reading of the name Thesprotus in this text, Vahlen, Warmington, Zillinger, Traglia and Boyle assign the fragment to Ennius’ Thyestes. Their argument is largely based on the assumption that the Thyestes was set at Thesprotus’ court, which played a fundamental part in the story about the feud between Atreus and Thyestes. If this attribution of the fragment is accepted, we can identify the speaker as Thesprotus, reflecting on the feud between Thyestes and Atreus. This then makes it likely that Thyestes is the addressee, and Thesprotus is advising resolving the feud in the wake of the Thyestean feast. Whether or not Thesprotus (or Thyestes) is aware of the oracle which predicts how he will be revenged, is unclear. It seems that Thesprotus is a naive character, to believe that Thyestes’ and Atreus’ quarrel can be amicably resolved - this seems to be what the author of the Rhetorica ad Herennium and Cicero suggest.

On the other hand, Welcker and Ribbeck have argued that we should read the name Cresphontes in the Rhetorica ad Herennium and ascribe the fragment to Ennius’ Cresphontes. They assume that Cresphontes the Elder is the speaker and this fragment can be placed after he conquered the Peloponnese and gained control of the city Messenia. The statement in the Rhetorica ad Herennium might then refer to Cresphontes, who will later be killed by the usurper Polyphontes. Wecklein furthermore suggests reading Polyphontes instead of Cresphontes as the character attributed the lines. Based on this reading, Mette has argued that Polyphontes is the speaker and the wife of the murdered Cresphontes the Elder, Meropa, is the addressee. However, if the Ennian play used the same plot as Euripides’ play of the same name, as most other scholars have assumed, it would have dealt with the revenge of Cresphontes the Younger, the son of the aforementioned Chresphontes, on Polyphontes and the recovering of his father’s kingdom.

A case can be made for either of the two plays but the fact that the name Thesprotus appears in multiple codices (instead of Cresphontes in just one manuscript) favours an attribution to the Thyestes. However, since the title of the Ennian play isn’t quoted by the authors, Manuwald’s solution to place the fragment under the Incerta is equally plausible, albeit a bit cautious.

context.text: The fragment above is quoted two times, once by the anonymous author of the Rhetorica ad Herennium (Rhet. Her. 2,39) and by Cicero in De Inventione (Cic. Inv. 1.91). In this passage on defective arguments, the author of the Rhetorica ad Herennium and Cicero discuss how the argument presented by an orator can be refuted when the proof he applies to support his reasoning is incorrect. To illustrate their point, both authors cites the two verses quoted above as an example of such a defective argument. The Auct. ad. Her. (in contrast with Cicero) adds that Thesprotus must be the speaker (Threprotum Ennius induxit) and claims that Thesprotus’ words insufficiently proof the point the character is trying to make in the play (sc. that the gods would intervene and settle conflict). Scholars who attribute the frg. to Ennius’ Thyestes , must therefore assume that the play was set at the court of king Thesprotus in Sicyon. Yet, although the name Thesprotus is transmitted in most codices , the name Cresphontes is also present in some manuscripts. (See Manuwald 2012, 277 n. 277, for an overview of the different codices and variants of the name of the character). This makes the attribution of this fragment to Ennius’ Thyestes very difficult and has in turn let to a variety of reconstructions and interpretations (see discussion below).

lines.text: eho tu: di, quibus est potestas motus superum atque inferum,
pacem inter sese conciliant, conferunt concordiam

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: If someone will revile me with this speech, what shall I answer?
doc.differences:
Schierl: 91 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 5/32)

D’Anna: 151 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 6/31)

Warmington: 130 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 9/34)

Ribbeck3: 11 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 11/32)

All editors place the fragment more in the beginning of the tragedy in the context of a dialogue between Electra and Clytemnestra. There is however a difference in the construction of the dialogue. D’Anna and Schierl place it more in the middle of the dialogue between Electra and Clytemnestra. Warmington and Ribbeck seem to place the fragment more at the end of the dialogue.
doc.apparatus: si quis hac] hac si quis An.: siquis hac Li.
doc.commentary:
quis] The aliquis makes it clear that the speaker has not a specific person in mind who will revile or will insult him. The quis, however, might also emphasize a certain irony and might be used to refer to the person who has (just) insulted the speaker.

hac … oratione] oratio can indicate, besides a treatise: the action of speaking, a literary style, a discussion or a certain statement, see s.v. oratio in the OLD. Because of the hac it is likely that the speaker has just discussed a certain statement or speech in which someone tries to rebuke the speaker. Another character might have pointed out what someone will say to rebuke the speaker of the above-mentioned fragment. But it is also possible that the speaker himself has come up with possible insults he might receive. Although it seems more likely that the line is spoken within a dialogue, it cannot be excluded that the line is part of a monologue.

incilet] potential subjunctive in a conditional clause. The verb incilo is not very common, see s.v. incilo in the Thesaurus Linguae Latinae. Other occurences of the word can be found in Non. 180L (124,36M), twice in the glossaria, twice in Accius’ tragedies, namely Clytemnestra 300 (Dangel) and Meleager 508 (Dangel 508), and in Lucil. 1035 M and in Lucr. 3.963. Schierl (2006, 252) points out that, since the word also occurs in Accius’ Clytemnestra, when Electra and Clytemnestra are arguing with each other, it is possible that the word is used in a similar context. According to Schierl, this makes it more likely that the fragment of the Dulorestes is part of a dialogue between Electra and Clytemnestra on Electra’s or Erigone’s wedding to Oiax.

respondeam] Schierl (2006, 252) points out that the subjunctive can be interpreted in two ways: as a subjunctive of indignation or a subjunctive deliberative. The first option would indicate a certain discontent or critical attitude of the speaker towards his discussion partner. Therefore, it is possible that the words are spoken in a dialogue between Electra and Clytemnestra or Aegisthus, see also Warmington frg.130, D’Anna 1967, 204 and Ribbeck 2013, 245. The second option focuses on the doubt or despair of the speaker. The attitude towards the dialogue partner is in comparison with the subjunctive of indignation more neutral. The speaker asks for advice. In that case, Ribbeck (2013, 245) proposes the possibility that the line is part of a dialogue between Orestes and Electra, in which a yet unrecognized Orestes tries to comfort Electra. Schierl (2006, 252) also suggests that the line is part of a dialogue on the revenge plans of Orestes and Electra.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker asks the question what he or she can say, if someone reviles him with a certain insult. It is not clear how the question should be interpreted. Although the subjunctive can be interpreted as a deliberative, it seems more likely that the question here is rhetorical or expresses a certain irony. It is probable that the question is part of dialogue. The speaker has probably received certain insults from the conversation partner and shows with the rhetorical question that he/she is not affected by the insults. The line might have occurred in a dialogue between Electra or Clytemnestra. It cannot be determined which of the two characters might have insulted the other. Schierl connects the fragments to a dialogue between Electra and Clytemnestra on Electra’s or Erigone’s wedding to Oiax, because the word incilare occurs in a similar context in the Accius’ Clytemnestra. Also, D’Anna, Warmington and Ribbeck place the fragment in this context. Warmington also gives the possibility that Aegisthus is part of the dialogue.

If the line should be interpretated as a deliberative, the speaker asks for advice or expresses desparation. Then the line might have occurred in a different context. Schierl and Ribbeck (2013, 245) point out the possibility that the fragment is part of a conversation between Electra and Orestes. Ribbeck thinks the fragment might be part of a dialogue between Electra and Orestes in which Orestes, whose identity is not yet known, comforts Electra. Schierl suggests that the line might be part of a dialogue between the siblings on the revenge plans.

It cannot, however, be excluded that the fragment is part of a monologue in which the speaker explains a certain situation or enumerates insults he has received. The speaker might direct his speech to the audience and/or provide the audience with some background information on the recent developments in the house of Agamemnon. In the monologue, the speaker might shout this line in despair or ask the audience rhetorically what he needs to do. In this situation, it is also likely that the speaker is Electra or Clytemnestra but since it is not clear what the possible insults are, other characters like Orestes are also potential candidates for this line.


Glare, P.G.W. 1982. The Oxford Latin Dictionary. Oxford.

Ribbeck, O. 2013. “Viertes Buch: M. Pacuvius.” In Die Römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Cambridge.

Thesaurus linguae Latinae. 1900–. Berlin.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes the line to explain the meaning of the word incilare, which has the same meaning as increpare or inprobare, 'to reprove'.

’incilare’ est increpare vel inprobare. Accius Meleagro… idem Clytaemnestra… Pacuvius Doloreste si quis hac me oratione incilet, quid respondeam? Lucilius… (180-181Li.; 124-125M)

lines.text: si quis hac me oratione incilet, quid respondeam?

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

lines.text: <15>celebri gradu
gressum adcele[b]rasse decet.

doc.translation: Let us hasten the pace with quickened step.
doc.differences:
TRF: 4 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5)

Ribbeck2-3: 23/24 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

Ribbeck1: 24 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

Warmington: 6-7 (Accius' Aegisthus 2/5)

Franchella: 404 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

D'Antò: 24 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

Dangel: 308-309 (Accius' Aegisthus 4/5)


Warmington:

It is our duty to hasten our steps with redoubled bustling pace.

Franchella:

celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse docet ⏑-⏑⏓

‘Racconta di avere affrettato il passo con veloce andatura’.

D’Antò:

celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse decet ⟨cito!⟩

‘Con celere passo conviene affrettare il cammino, subito!’

Dangel:

Il convient de presser le pas d'un pied preste.
doc.apparatus:
1 celebri] celebri DS codd. PTv: celebrari DS cod. F (= Bernensis 172): celebrique Ribbeck1, 117

1 celebri gradu] celebri gradu codd.:

2 adcelerasse] adcelerasse corr. Iunius: adcelebrasse codd. Non.: adcelerate DS codd. FP: accelerate DS codd. Tv: adcelerauisse Scaliger

2 decet] decet Iunius: docet codd. Non., om. DS: addecet Scaliger: condecet Mähly 1857, 287: dedecet Buecheler (apud Ribbeck2-3, cf. Soph. El. 871-2).

2 <cito> addidit d’Antò


In his 1565 edition of Nonius, Hadrianus Iunius (Adriaen de Jonghe) corrected the Nonian manuscripts’ adcelebrasse docet to adcelerasse decet (p. 117), which is now the reading adopted in modern editions. Scaliger (1577, 57), in a discussion of Catull. 63.26 (quo nos decet citatis, celerare tripudiis), where he argued for the reading celebrare, quoted our Accian fragment as follows: celebri gradu gressum adcelerauisse addecet ‘it is unbecoming to hasten the pace with swift step’. It is worth noting that he changed the tense of the infinitive from present to past infinitive (adcelerauisse) in order to compensate for the syllable lost in the elision of the final -e before addecet. Adopting Scaliger’s addecet, Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 124) suggested changing the word order as follows: gradu celebri gressum adcelerasse addecet (Vossius actually prints gradum, which I suspect is a misprint, and this is seemingly confirmed by the fact that gradu is printed on the previous page as a catchword). Mähly’s (1857, 287) conjecture of condecet (i.e. celebrique gradu gressum adcelerasse condecet ‘it is fitting to hasten the pace with swift step’) is designed to add an extra syllable so as to turn the line into a complete iambic senarius.

In his first edition (1852, 117), Ribbeck prints the following text: ...celebrique gradu / gressum adcelerasse decet, apparently thinking that a word dropped out during the transmission process and supplying trepidum as an example of what Accius might have written. He presents the fragment as ‘Accius 24’, thus implying that the fragment represents a single line, and suggests that the beginning of the line had not been preserved. The line as Ribbeck prints it however does not seem to scan. In his critical apparatus, however, he suggests that the fragment should perhaps be printed as two (presumably iambic) dimeters (‘fortasse satius erat dimetros descripsisse’), i.e. celebrique gradu | gressum adcelerasse decet.

In his second edition (1871, 139), Ribbeck presents the fragment as ‘Accius 23/24’, apparently in order to indicate that the fragment could be presented over two lines as iambic dimeters, as he had suggested in his first edition. Nevertheless, he decides to print the fragment as a single iambic senarius: celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse dedecet (adopting Bücheler’s conjecture of dedecet for transmitted docet, see below). In the ‘Conspectus Metrorum’ at the end of his second edition (p. 366), Ribbeck lists this fragment as a complete iambic senarii, though with a question mark (i.e. ‘Accius 23*?’).

Three years later, in his 1875 monograph (p. 468), Ribbeck has again changed his mind about both the text and the metre of the fragment. He now suggests a reconstruction in anapaestic dimeters, in line with his belief that the speaker is the chorus:

celebri⟨que⟩ gradu

gressum ⟨nunc⟩ adcelerasse decet.

Ribbeck explains in a footnote his reason for rejecting Bücheler’s conjecture dedecet, which he had adopted in his second edition: he feels that the chorus’ heightened expression makes it more likely that it is recommending an increase of pace rather than a decrease of pace. In his third edition (1897-1898, 160), Ribbeck, having changed his mind again, prints the fragment as a single iambic senarius, adopting the following text: celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse †decet (now printing decet between cruces to mark it as a so-called locus desperatus).

D’Antò 196 cites Accian abundantia in support of his suggestion that the word cito be added to the end of the line (on abundantia, see Löfstedt ‘Pleonasmus’ II.173-232).

Note that the text of the fragment is different in each source. In Nonius, the verb adcelebro/accelebro is in the past infinitive, i.e. adcelebrasse/accelebrasse, and is followed by docet (MSS), whereas in Servius Auctus, docet is omitted and the fragment ends with adcelerate, the second-person plural form of accelero. This may suggest that Nonius and Servius Auctus were quoting from the same source.

For a list of the main manuscripts testifying to the commentary known as 'Servius Auctus' or DS, see Murgia–Kaster 2018, xiv-xvi.
doc.commentary:
celebri…adcelerasse: this fragment is problematic for both textual and metrical reasons. First of all, as transmitted by Servius Auctus (or DS), the fragment does not contain the verb celebrare despite the fact that the purpose for the quotation of the fragment is to explain the archaic usage of this verb in the sense of ‘to quicken, speed up’. It therefore seems likely that the correct reading is accelebrasse and that this reading was corrupted to accelerate in the MS tradition of Servius Auctus. This hypothesis is made even more likely by the fact that the final word in Nonius’ quotation of the fragment, docet, does not feature at all in Servius Auctus, which constitutes further evidence of corruption in the DS manuscript tradition.

Reading accelebrasse is itself not unproblematic, however, because celebrare and derivatives are difficult to parallel in the sense of ‘to quicken’. The verb is only attested in this sense in two passages of Plautus (Ps. 168, Cornicula fr. 4 de Melo), both of which are problematic. At Ps. 168, the manuscript reading, preserved solely in the Palatinus branch, is celebrate, but most editors accept Gulielmus’ correction to celerate (Lindsay emends to celebra or celera, changing the metre). At Cornicula fr. 4 de Melo (Non. 134 M. = 196 L.), all the manuscripts of Nonius have celebrassit except for L, which has celerassit. Therefore, no attestation of celebrare in this sense can be identified with absolute certainty (cf. OLD s.v. celebro 5c), as in each case celerare is a variant reading (the two verbs are commonly confused in manuscripts: TLL III.757.84-758.2). Furthermore, celebrare does not seem to be used in this sense elsewhere in Accius (cf. Annales fr. 3.3 D cumque diem celebrant [sc. maxima pars Graium], per agros urbesque per omnes… 'And whenever they [sc. the majority of the Greeks] celebrate this day, throughout the fields and all the cities...') and F 273 TRF = v. 444 Ribbeck3 = 433 Warmington = 505 Dangel gaudent, currunt, celebrant…), although conceivably in the latter fragment celebrare could mean ‘to hasten’ (i.e. ‘they rejoice, they run [to him/her], they rush to throng [around him/her…]’. The difficulty posed by the hapax adcelebrasse in the sense of ‘to quicken’ led the sixteenth-century editor of Nonius, Hadrianus Iunius (1565, 117), to correct the MS reading adcelebrasse to adcelerasse. All editors since have accepted Iunius’ correction of acelebrasse to adcelerasse, but Perutelli (1999, 189) argues for the manuscript reading adcelebrasse, suggesting that in the text preserved by Servius Acutus, adcelerate is a corruption of adcelebrasse and that, since Accius has already included one figura etymologica in this line (gradum/gressus), it is plausible that, using the adjective celeber in the rare sense of ‘occurring often, frequent’ (OLD s.v. 3a), he included another one (celebri…adcelebrasse). A semantic connection between the idea of ‘frequent, repeated action’ and ‘swiftness, speed’ can readily be imagined (cf. W­–H s.v. celeber, ‘vgl. auch die Bed. „angetrieben, schnell“ in Acc. trag. 23’; E–M s.v. celeber, ‘fréquenté […] d’où secondairement celebri gradu dans Accius, Tr. 23, "d’un pas fréquent", i.e. "à pas précipités"’; Imberciadori 2023 traces celeber back to Pre-Proto-Italic *kelh1-es-ro ‘having bustle’), and Accius may be highlighting this connection through verbal play. We might compare the Greek expression πυκνὰ [codd.: πύκα Barnes] ποσσὶν, Hom. Hymn. 19 [to Pan] 20, 23; cf. Ar. Lys. 1310 πυκνὰ ποδοῖν (lit. ‘with close-packed feet’, i.e. quickly). The similarity between Accius’ figura and this Greek expression is reinforced by the fact that celeber in the sense of ‘occurring often, frequent’ (OLD s.v. 3a) is synonymous with the Latin version of Greek πυκνός, creber (TLL III.739.2-739.14).

Bücheler (apud Ribbeck2-3) perceived a parallel with Soph. El. 871-2 (Chrysothemis hurrying to come and tell Electra, who thinks that Orestes is dead, that he in fact is alive and has returned to Argos: ὑφ᾿ ἡδονῆς τοι, φιλτάτη, διώκομαι | τὸ κόσμιον μεθεῖσα σὺν τάχει μολεῖν ‘Delight, dearest one, spurs me to come fast, letting go care for dignity!’, tr. Lloyd-Jones). Ribbeck3, 160 suggests a further parallel: Pl. Poen. 513-14 (Agorastocles to his advocates: gradus succretust cribro pollinario; | nisi cum pedicis condidicistis istoc grassari gradu, ‘That step of yours has been sifted through a flour sieve; unless you learned to walk with shackles at that pace’, tr. de Melo).
doc.reconstruction:
Numerous interpretations have been proposed. Ribbeck (1875, 468) suggested two possible dramatic situations for this fragment: the line is spoken by (1) Clytemnestra or Mycenean citizens as they hasten to greet Agamemnon on his return from Troy (cf. Sen. Ag. 780-1 et festa coniunx obuios illi tulit / gressus reditque iuncta concordi gradu ‘his wife went out to meet him in festive mood, and returns walking in concord at his side’ [tr. Fitch]: Pociña 123 adds that, if Clytemnestra is the speaker, then her purpose is to create a sensation of joy which she in fact does not feel herself in order to conceal her intention to murder Agamemnon) or (2) a member of the chorus wanting to enter the palace to see what is going on inside after hearing Agamemnon utter a cry, cf. Aesch. Ag. 1350-1 ἐμοὶ δ᾿ ὅπως τάχιστά γ᾿ ἐμπεσεῖν δοκεῖ | καὶ πρᾶγμ᾿ ἐλέγχειν σὺν νεορρύτῳ ξίφει ‘I think we should burst in straight away, and get proof of the crime when the blood is flowing freshly from the sword’ (tr. Sommerstein; cf. Ribbeck1, 298); cf. also Warmington II.329, Venini 328. Olivieri 598 suggested Cassandra as the speaker lamenting Agamemnon as she prophesizes his impending death. D’Antò 196 proposed that Electra is instructing her brother Orestes (and Pylades?) to hurry up as she tries to escort them to safety.

Perutelli 192, who believes that the plot of Accius' play was broadly similar to Seneca’s Agamemnon, suggests that the fragment could potentially have been part of a monologue by Clytemnestra (cf. Sen. Ag. 108 quid, segnis anime, tuta consilia expetis? ‘Oh sluggish spirit! Why look for safe strategies?’; 131 maiora cruciant quam moram possim pati ‘My torments are too great for me to endure delays’; 193 pigra, quem exspectas diem? ‘What day are you idly waiting for?’; 198 quid, misera, cessas? ‘Why hesitate, poor woman?’ [tr. Fitch]), but deems the Aeschylean parallel more convincing and concludes that the fragment probably belonged to an anapestic passage spoken by the chorus. Aricò 2002, 14-15 compares, in addition to Soph. El. 971-2, Eur. El. 112-13 = 127-8 (chorus): σύντειν᾿ ᾠδᾷ ποδὸς ὁρμάν· ὤ, | ἔμβα ἔμβα κατακλαίουσα ‘Hasten the tread of your steps with song, O march on, march on in tears!’ (tr. Kovacs).

context.author: Servius Auctus (DS)
context.text:
Serv. Auct. Verg. Aen. 4.641 (I.575.15-18 Thilo–Hagen):

studio anili [...] alii celebrabat legunt, quia antiqui hoc uerbum in uelocitate ponebant. Accius celebri gradu gressum adcelerate.

Servius Auctus quotes this fragment of Accius in a discussion of whether celerare ‘to go quickly, hasten, hurry’ or celebrare ‘to throng, fill; to frequent’ is the correct reading at Verg. Aen. 4.641, a line which describes Dido’s elderly nurse Barce hurrying away from her mistress. While Servius Auctus himself prefers the reading celerare (cf. Aen. 5.609), he mentions that others read celebrare, because in archaic (i.e. pre-Vergilian) Latin this verb could denote speed. Our fragment of Accius is adduced as an example of such a use of celebrare.

The quotation of our fragment as transmitted as Servius Auctus, however, lacks the verb celebrare, despite this word being the very reason why our fragment is quoted (see further ‘Fragment Commentary’). It is worth noting that a text of Vergil which was available to Apuleius seems to have had celebrabat: this can be deduced on the basis of Apuleius’ apparent imitation of Vergil’s line at Apul. Met. 6.14.1 at illa studiose gradum celebrans montis extremum petit tumulum… 'She [Psyche] eagerly and speedily began walking toward the top of that mountain-peak...'. The oldest witness to Apuleius’ text, MS Laurentianus 68.2, reads cele*rans: the letter crossed out by a later hand has been identified as either an r or a b (Perutelli 1999, 195-6, having inspected the manuscript, holds with confidence that it is the latter). In Nonius, the fragment appears under the lemma of the adjective celebre, which Nonius says not only means ‘populous’ (frequens) but also ‘quick’ (uelox).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 89.24-6 Mercier = 127.22-4 Lindsay:

celebre, non solum frequens, sed etiam uelox. Accius Aegistho: celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse docet.

Whereas Servius Auctus quotes our Accian fragment to give an example of the verb celebrare with the sense ‘to quicken’, Nonius quotes it to give an example of the adjective celeber with the sense ‘quick’. Lindsay (1901, 41) suggests that Nonius’ source is Anon. 27 (‘Alph. Verb.’), a glossary of verbs, and that the adjective celebre appeared, in Nonius’ source, in the discussion pertaining to the word celebrascat, quoted some lines above (p. 89.14-15 M = 127.12-13 L).

doc.metrical_analysis:
From the seventeenth to the mid-eighteenth century, several attempts were made to transform the line into a complete iambic senarius:

(i) Scaliger 1577, 57: celebri gradu gressum adcelerauisse addecet.

(ii) Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 124): gradum celebri gressum adcelerasse addecet.

(iii) Mähly 1857, 287: celebrique gradu gressum adcelerasse condecet.

Bothe (fr. 5, V.161), printing celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse decet, suggested that the metre is an ‘iambelegiac’, i.e. an iambic dimeter followed by an hemiepes (– ⏑⏑ – ⏑⏑ –). Ribbeck changed his mind several times regarding the metre of this fragment (see ‘Textual variants’). Over the course of his various publications, he made several different suggestions: an incomplete iambic octonarius; two iambic dimeters; two incomplete anapaestic dimeters; an iambic senarius. A consensus of sorts has been found over the past century. Scholars since Warmington have elected to print the fragment as two incomplete iambic scenarii (celebri gradu / gressum adcelerasse decet). This is the solution adopted by Klotz, Dangel, and the TRF edition. Note however that Barabino 1980, 8 n. 6 lists this Accian fragment among those that she considers metrically unclassifiable); and that, endorsing Scaliger’s addecet and the word nunc proposed by Ribbeck in 1875, Perutelli (1999, 191-3) would print the fragment in anapaestic dimeters:

...celebri gradu

gressum adcelebrasse addecet.

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation: Let none of you remind the woman of this
doc.differences:
TRF (6/8)
Warmington (3/8)
Ribbeck (4/8)
doc.apparatus:
vostrum] voster codd. : vostrum Heinsius
haece] haec codd. : haece coni. Mueller : <-> nemo hoc Duentzer

haece provides an extra short syllable, which gives the line the metre of an iambic senarius, but the last word must be followed by a vowel in order that the -i is elided. It also fixes the problem that in this case ruminetur contains a long -e-, but without an extra syllable added to the transmitted line it scans with a short -e-, a problem Duentzer tried to heal at an earlier date by assuming that the quotation left out the first syllable and that instead of haec (long syllable) there must be read hoc (with a short syllable).
doc.commentary:
haece The final –e is enclitic and indicates emphasis. It refers to the things the mulier should not be reminded of by anyone. The deictic pronoun must refer to something that is present either on stage or in the minds of the people addressed by vostrum.

vostrum The speaker addresses either (a) two (or more) characters or (b) the chorus. Vostrum can be both masculine and feminine.

ruminetur Ruminari is often used to refer to ruminating cows. Here it is certainly used exclusively in a figurative meaning, see further Kruschwitz (2009: 161). By employing the metaphor of a ‘ruminating cow’, the line seems to imply, through its prohibition on further rumination, that haece refers to something ‘that ought to be digested by now (unless one is a silly cow, so to speak)’, see Kruschwitz (2009: 163).

mulieri mulier refers to an adult woman, but unlike uxor, not necessarily to a married one. Therefore, mulier can refer to Clytemnestra as well as Electra, Chrysothemis or Cassandra, see Wessels (2018: 349-350), or the line is a general remark which extends to any woman. mulier seems distancing, and, therefore, presumably is not part of vostrum.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker of this verse advises a group of at least two other people (vostrum) to avoid bringing up something that is present or known to them (haece) to a woman (mulieri). Haece presumably refers to things that are troubling or traumatizing for the mulier, but which she already knows but has forgotten. The speaker is possibly portrayed as quite sensitive towards mulier (cf. Ribbeck 1875: 30), but it is also possible that the line concerns a somewhat disrespectful or shameful business familiar to the speaker and the people referred to by vostrum, not to be brought in front of the mulier.

Agamemnon might be the speaker of the line. Ribbeck suggests that echoes of this line of Livius are found in Seneca’s Agamemnon, in Agamemnon’s care for Cassandra (esp. 800-801 Hanc, fida famuli turba, dum excutiat deum, | retinete, ne quid impotens peccet furor). On Ribbeck’s interpretation of Livius’ line, the mulier is Cassandra and Agamemnon is instructing the Chorus not to remind her of the fate of Troy. There are some problems with Ribbeck’s view: in Seneca, Agamemnon is concerned with bringing Cassandra out of her trance, so that she does not say any more worrying prophecies for his future - his care is not for her wellbeing, but his own state of mind. Seneca’s Agamemnon also does not give any instruction about what the chorus are or are not to say to Cassandra, only instructions such as carrying her off or splashing her with cold water. And his addressees are not one of the two (female) choruses (Argive women and Trojan captives) but some non-speaking male slaves (famuli 787, 800).

A case can be made that Agamenon is the speaker, but the mulier referred to is Clytemnestra. In this case, haece could refer to a number of shameful or problematic prior events, such as the sacrifice of Iphigenia or his adultery with Briseis. As Spaltenstein (2008: 44) remarks, it is impossible that Clytemnestra would have forgotten the sacrifice of Iphigenia. It could be, however, that Agamemnon still tries to prevent Clytemnestra from being reminded of it after his return, and tries to suppress this memory, see Spaltenstein (2008: 44): ‘mais Agamemnon aurait-il, dans le context de son retour triumphal, des raisons de dire à son entourage de ne pas réveiller ce souvenir chez sa femme?’. On the other hand, haece could refer to the sexual relationships by Agamemnon with Chryseis, Briseis and Cassandra, of which Clytemnestra should not be reminded, cf. Spaltenstein (2008: 45). Both of these motives feature in Seneca’s Agamemnon: in Clytemestra’s dialogue with the nurse, 164-191 (revolvit animus… , 164) Clytemestra mentions Iphigenia’s sacrifice, and Chryseis, Briseis and Cassandra (which, from Clytemnestra’s perspective, are love affairs, cf. amore captae captus, 175).

Alternately, Aegisthus or Clytemnestra could be the speakers, which would point to Electra being the mulier. In this case, haece would refer to Agamemnon’s murder, and the instruction would presumably be made to a chorus, or to any servants. If TrRF 2 was interpreted as part of a dialogue between Aegisthus or Clytemnestra and Electra, then this line, on the aforementioned interpretation, could also form part of that scenario, as words perhaps addressed to the chorus after Electra has left the stage.

Spaltenstein suggests an interpretation that Electra is the speaker of this verse, and that she is urging a group of people (presumably of Argive women) not to say anything about Orestes’ escape with Strophius, see Spaltenstein (2008: 45). This would parallel the scene in Seneca’s Agamemnon, where Clytemnestra forces Electra to tell where Orestes went (965-966 citius interea mihi | edissere ubi sit natus, ubi frater tuus; 968 Quo latitat loco?) after he had been saved by Strophius (Sen. Ag. 910-943). However, this impossibly strains the meaning of ruminetur, since Clytemnestra has not heard it and it is not something traumatic.

Although Chrysothemis does not appear in Aeschylus’ or Seneca’s Agamemnon, rather featuring only in Sophocles’ Electra (which dramatises the later events of Orestes’ return to Mycenae to revenge his father). But we do not know how much of the myth Livius included in Aegisthus: he could have included subsequent events in the myth, or intervening material which is not included in other depictions, such as the aftermath of Aegisthus’ and Clytemnestra’s coup. In Sophocles’ Electra Chrysothemis begs the chorus to keep silent about Electra’s intended plans, see Soph. El. 469-471 σιγὴ παρ᾽ὑμῶν πρὸς θεῶν ἔστω, φίλαι· | ὡς εἰ τάδ᾽ἡ τεκοῦσα πεύσεται, πικρὰν | δοκῶ με πεῖραν τήνδε τολμήσειν ἔτι. But the same issue applies in this instance too: ruminari does not seem to fit a secret which should not be divulged, but rather a trauma which should not be brought up again.

Wessels (2018: 350) raises the possibility to read Cassandra as the mulier, since the line ‘suggests that the female character is either particularly vulnerable or energetic [my own translation from Dutch original – WvD]’. She sees this as a possible indication to argue in favour of Cassandra. This would explain the choice for mulieri instead of uxori, which does not necessarily refer to a married woman (see commentary).

Ribbeck (4/8) and Warmington (3/8) all place this fragment at the beginning towards the middle of the tragedy. Both Ribbeck (1875: 30) and Warmington (1936: 5) argue that this fragment refers to this scene with Agamemnon and Cassandra.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
ruminari dictum in memoriam revocare. Varro Tanaquile (1): 'non modo absens quicquam de te sequius cogitabit, sed etiam ruminabitur humanitatem'. -- Livius Aegistho (8 [R.]):
nemo haec vostrorum ruminetur mulieri.

This line is quoted by Nonius (245 L.) in order to demonstrate that ruminari can mean in memoriam revocare.

lines.text: nemo haec⟨e⟩ vostrum ruminetur mulieri
Set me Apollo spe delectat, ductat (dictis) Delphicus.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: What, however, is this kind of cracking noise, that the doors are producing?
doc.differences:
Schierl: 117 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 31/32)

D’Anna: 176 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 23/32)

Ribbeck2: 133 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 13/32)

Ribbeck3: 133 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 13/32)

Warmington: 154 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 23/34)

Schierl: < x - > quidnam autem hoc soniti est, quod stridunt fores?

Ribbeck2/3, Warmington: . . . quidnam autem hoc soniti est, quod stridunt foris?

The placement of this fragment is uncertain. Schierl places this fragment very close to the end, whereas D’Anna, Warmington and especially Ribbeck2/3 place this fragment earlier in the story. Too much is uncertain about the reconstruction of this fragment which is probably the main reason why editors aren’t in agreement as to where this fragment is to be situated within the whole plot.
doc.apparatus:
autem] aut Bambergensis

fores] fores Iunius : fori codd. : foris coni. Ribbeck2/3, Warmington, L. Müller, Lindsay

quod] quid coni. Bothius

Fores is the main word of contention within this fragment with the codices using fori and multiple editors adopting foris. fori seems unlikely here, but foris is plausible here only if its translated as ‘’outdoors’’, which is how Warmington has translated it. For a more in depth explanation, see Fragment Commentary.
doc.commentary:
quidnam] -nam after the interrogative pronoun quid intensifies the question.

fores] the codices read fori whereas Ribbeck2/3, Warmington, Lindsay and Mueller all adopt foris. As mentioned in the Apparatus Criticus, fori seems unlikely here, since foris is a third declination word and can therefore not be a nominative plural, but only a dative singular, which is difficult to fit here. It cannot be translated with ‘’at the door’’, because Latin would use an ablative of place, fore, in that case. foris can not only mean ‘’door(s)’’ but can also be translated adverbially as ‘’outdoors’’, which is how Warmington has translated it: ‘’but what’s this screeching noise they make outdoors?’’

In order to achieve the Iambic Senarius metre, two additional syllables need to be added. There haven't been made any suggestions as to what those could be.
doc.reconstruction:
The reference of the cracking of the doors signifies the announcement of a new appearance. It is unknown who the speaker of this verse is as well as the identity of the person signified by the cracking door. However this person most likely came from the palace to the speaker who is standing on stage. It is possible that Aegisthus or Clytaimnestra is perceiving this sound during the act of revenge. In that case Orestes would have had to step out from inside the house where the first murder had already taken place. It seems more likely however that a resident of the palace is being announced in this way. The verse could also have a connection with the appearance of Electra or Clytaimnestra in the beginning of the tragedy or right before the act of revenge. There are no real textual problems in this verse, but in order to achieve the Iambic Senarius metre, two additional syllables need to be added.

Bibliography

D’Anna, I. (1967). M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Nonius, M., Lindsay, W. M. (ed.), (1903). De compendiosa doctrina libros xx, Onionsianis copiis usus, vol. 3. Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1871). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 2). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 3). Leipzig.

Schierl, P. (2006). Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin; New York.

Warmington, E.H. (ed./transl.) (1936). Remains of Old Latin, vol. 2: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Pacuvius. Accius. Cambridge MA, London.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius has quoted this fragment to show the usage of soniti instead of sonitus and advocate for sonu instead of sono.

’soniti’ et ‘sonu’ pro sonitus et sono. Pacuvius Duloreste: quidnam autem hoc soniti est, quod stridunt fores? (Non. 789 L. (= 491,23 M.))

lines.text: < x - > quidnam autem hoc soniti est, quod stridunt fores?
Egone Argiuum imperium attingam aut Pelopia digner domo?
Quoi me ostendam? quod templum adeam? quem ore funesto alloquar?

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor

doc.translation: Maker of a feast, eraser of a brother’s crimes
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 11 (11/20), 219:

219: Epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor

Warmington:

Fragment 186 (10/17):

186: [NUNTIUS]: Epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor

Maker of a feast,

Outblotter of a brother’s crimes.

Franchella:

Fragment 11 (11/22), 368:

368: epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor.

Ideatore di vivande, lungamente superiore al fratello per scelleratezze.

D’Antò:

Fragment 9 (9/18), 219:

219: epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor

hai escogitato per finta un banchetto, hai offuscato le scelleratezze di tuo fratello!

Dangel:

Fragment 12 (12/21), 50:

50: epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor

fabricateur de festin, effaceur des crimes de son frère
doc.apparatus:
1: delitor ] delitor codd. Prisciani: deditor delitor Thesaurus novus Latinitatis, Mai 1828-1838, VIII.178: perlitor Thesaurus novus Latinitatis, Mai 1828-1838, VIII.301.

The fragment is quoted in garbled form, but with the reading perlitor instead of delitor, on p. 301 of the Thesaurus novus Latinitatis (a work later identified by W. Meyer (1874) as the Panormia or Derivationes of the twelfth-century English Benedictine monk Osbern of Gloucester) as: fictor scelerum, fratris perlitor (cf. Ribbeck’s critical apparatus in the first edition, p. 138). In his second edition, Ribbeck argues (p. 164) that the otherwise-unattested word perlitor is a corruption of perditor, a variant reading which was found in another manuscript of Priscian. He suggests the following line in Accius’ text ended with the words puerorum perditor, and that a scribe mistakenly  wrote perditor instead of delitor in the text of our fragment on account of the similar ending of the two words. Baldarelli (2004, 235) suggests that delitor might be a corruption introduced instead of delutor, from the verb deluere (‘to wash off or away’, OLD s.v. deluo1), which she suggests is an antonym of polluere in the sense of ‘to corrupt someone by seduction’: the contamination of the house of Atreus by Thyestes and Aerope can only be purified by an expiatory sacrifice. This suggestion is not particularly convincing, however, as delutor itself is otherwise attested only in the sixth-century Christian writer Fulgentius (De aetatibus mundi et hominis, p. 172.28 Helm), and the verb deluere is attested for the first time in Gellius (TLL V.1.474.1-17).
doc.commentary:
1: fictor…delitor: Vollmer in his TLL entry s.v. fictor interprets the word in the negative sense of deliberately intending to deceive (TLL VI.1.649.16-17, 64 ‘cum sensu malo, qui falsa fingit’). Baldarelli (2004, 234-5), however, identifies these words as sacral language, comparing Ennius Annales 114-115 Skutsch: mensas constituit idemque ancilia <...> | libaque, fictores, Argeos, et tutulatos (= Varro, LL 7.43-44, ‘he [Numa Pompilius] established the sacrificial tables and likewise the sacred shields and sacrificial cakes, shapers, Argei, and priests with conical caps’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald FRL I.165). Skutsch does not print a lacuna at the end of line 114, but writes in his commentary that ‘the end of 114 is lost beyond recovery’ (1985, 266), suggesting dicta, bis sex, fecit, or Martis as possible restorations. Fictores were attendants of priests and Vestal virgins: Varro suggests that they derive their name from the sacrificial cakes that they made, and Baldarelli thus suggests that Atreus is described as a fictor in the cultic sense of the term. She also compares Cicero’s description of Clodius at De domo sua 139 as ignorant of proper religious practice in so far as he attempted to effect the consecration of Cicero’s house sine fictore, ‘with…none to bake the cakes of sacrifice’ (Watts), ‘with no prompter and no acolyte’ (R.G. Nisbet). Fictor is also attested on an inscription (CIL VI.2125 = Dessau ILS 4942) dedicated to a certain Lucius Manlius, a rex sacrorum from Bovillae who came to Rome and became a fictor. On delitor, see following note.

1: delitor: a hapax legomenon, denominative derived from the verb delino ‘to smear, daub, anoint (with); [...] to obliterate by smudging’ (OLD s.v. delino). The interpretation of this word has given rise to radically different interpretations of the fragment. OLD s.v. delitor understands it as ‘a person who wipes out (i.e. exacts vengeance for)’, an interpretation which seems to draw on that of the Thesaurus novus latinitatis edited by A. Mai (1828-1838, VIII.178: delitor: qui aliquem occidit. Accius: fictor scelerum, fratris deditor delitor: it is unclear from where the word deditor originates). Müller however (1890, 24) glosses it as purgator, in the sense that Thyestes has been ‘purged’ of the crimes he committed against his brother. This would give the fragment the opposite sense, that Atreus is presented not as having avenged himself, but as having pardoned, or rather pretended to pardon, his brother. As Lana (1959, 308) points out, Müller’s explanation is not particularly illuminating. He may, however, be on the right track: might the idea be that of ‘wiping the slate clean’? Franchella (1968, 229) translates scelerum fratris delitor as ‘lungamente superiore al fratello per sceleratezza’ (i.e. ‘making his brother’s crimes pale in comparison to his own’): it is difficult to see how the Latin could mean this. Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 271 n. 17) translates: ‘inventore di banchetti, ha oscurato nell’iniquità il fratello’. Explicitly rejecting the interpretation of delitor as meaning ‘to kill someone’ (qui aliquem occidit), D’Antò (1980, 518) translates: ‘hai offuscato le scelleratezze di tuo fratello!’. Three different interpretations of the line seem thus to have been advanced:

(i) Atreus erases/wipes out the crimes of his brother by committing equivalent crimes.

(ii) Atreus erases/wipes out the crimes of brother by ‘purging’ them (i.e. wiping the slate clean and ‘forgetting’ them). [Müller].

(iii) Atreus ‘erases’ the crimes of his brother by performing much greater crimes himself, which oustrip those of his brother (thus making them seem non-existent?). [Franchella, Di Benedetto Zimbone, D’Antò].

Baldarelli (2004, 235) suggests, following Ribbeck (see below), that delitor might be interpreted in yet another sense: that Atreus has ‘wiped out’ or ‘erased’ the crimes of his brother in so far as he has restored the situation before the adultery and the theft of the lamb (‘So ließe sich das Verbrechen des Atreus als Versuch verstehen, die ursprünglichen Gegebenheiten – die Situation vor dem Ehebruch und dem Diebstahl des Lammes – wieder herzustellen’: cf. Ribbeck 1875, 452: ‘[Atreus], der die Missethaten des Thyestes durch die greuelvolle Mahlzeit auslöscht’). For a similar interpretation of Accius’ revenge in Seneca’s Thyestes, see Schiesaro 2003, 211, with reference to the Freudian notion of Ungeschehenmachen or ‘retroactive annulment of an event’. Baldarelli however also suggests that delitor may be corrupt and that the correct reading could in fact be delutor (see ‘Apparatus criticus’).
doc.reconstruction: According to Ribbeck (1875, 453), this fragment, as well as F 108 TRF (concoquit | partem vapore flammae; tribuit in focos | veribus lacerta) belongs to an eye-witness account of the banquet, which was probably not shown on stage but rather described, probably by a messenger. Lana’s interpretation (1959, 308) is similar. D’Antò (1980, 286), however, believes that in this fragment Thyestes is speaking to Atreus: although he translates scelerum fratris delitor as ‘hai offuscato le scelleratezze di tuo fratello’ (p. 518), in his commentary, he seems to explain delitor rather differently: ‘Delitor ha qui intatto il suo significato etimologico: Atreo col suo misfatto ha come cancellato, annullato, e quindi superato, di gran lunga l’azione del fratello’. Depending on how delitor is interpreted (see ‘Fragment commentary’), the messenger could be presenting Atreus as either: (i) having avenged his brother’s crimes; or (ii) inviting Thyestes to take place at the banquet that he has organised for him and pretending to have pardoned him for his crimes. Atreus’ dishonest and devious behaviour would be reminiscent of F 104.5-6 (maior mihi moles…: cf. Müller 1890, 24; Lana 1959, 308). If (ii) is correct, and it is the one that Lana seems to adopt, then the fragment would likely have been placed towards the beginning of the messenger’s speech: the messenger would have gone on to describe the banquet in horrific detail (cf. F 108 TRF, concoquit…), thus revealing that Atreus’ apparently clement attitude was just a façade.

context.author: Priscian
context.text:
Priscian, Institutio de arte grammatica, II.490.8-16 GLK: a ‘deleo’, cuius simplex [id est ‘leo’] in usu non est, ‘deletum’, a ‘delino delitum’ nascitur, quod Probus et Caper comprobant, usu quoque adiuvante. [...] Accius in Atreo: ‘Epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor’.

This fragment is preserved in Book 9 of Priscian’s Institutes of Grammar, which is entitled ‘De generali verbi declinatione’ (‘On the groups of declension of the verb’). In this section, Priscian discusses the supine form of various different verbs. He cites the authority of Probus and Caper on the correctness of the supine forms deletum and delitum, which belong to the verbs deleo and delino respectively. A version of the fragment is also preserved in the Thesaurus novus Latinitatis edited by A. Mai, in Volume 8 of the series Classicorum auctorum et Vaticanis codicibus editorum (1828-1838, p. 301): ‘Perlino, is quod aliquando dicitur pro occidere; et inde hic perlitor, ris ·i· occisor; unde Accius: fictor scelerum, fratris perlitor’ (see ‘Fragment apparatus criticus’).

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation:
from where does he/she appear? supported by what protection? with what help?
alarmed by what plan? by what power? by whose troops?
doc.commentary:
Nonius cites that consternari means the same as deici and erigi. Warmington (1936, 216-217) says that Nonius seem to take consternatur as 'is excited to sedition'.

exoriri suggest a sudden appearance. quo consilio consternatur refers to the motivation of the deed. vis can be a hostile power. cuius copiis refers to the attacker's resources with which the interviewee is supported. (Schierl 2006, 263)
doc.reconstruction: The speaker in this fragment is probably Aegisthos (Warmington 1936, 216-217), who informs to the resources and intentions of the man that is bringing him in danger. In fragment 104 it is clarified that the danger consists of killing. The man who is bringing him in danger can thus be Orestes, who is his killer in Aegisthus' Oresteia. Seeing the many questions, the speaker is clearly excited. The person who he speaks to must have given him a warning, that all these questions have arisen.

context.author: Nonius
context.text: ‘consternari’ significat deici. ‘consternari’ rursum erigi. Pacuvius in Periboea (fr. 211) … idem Duloreste: under exoritur? quo praesidio fretus? auxiliis quibus? quo consilio consternatur? qua vi? cuius copiis?.

lines.text: unde exoritur? quo praesidio fretus? auxiliis quibus?
quo consilio consternatur? qua vi? cuius copiis?
Adde huc quod mihi portento caelestum pater
Prodigium misit, regni stabilimen mei,
Agnum inter pecudes aurea clarum coma,
Em clam Thyestem clepere ausum esse e regia,
Qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi.
[THESPROTUS:] Sin flaccebunt condiciones repudiato et reddito.
Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: You who guard the royal body, go immediately to the leaf-bearing places where trees grow by nature, not by planting
doc.differences:
Ribbeck2: 24-26 (5/24)

Ribbeck3: 21-22 (5/24)

Warmington: 27-29 (4/25)

Marmorale: p. 192 (5/24)

Klotz: 24-26 (5/24).

TrRF, Ribbeck3, Warmington: Vos qui regalis corporis custodias

agitatis, ite actutum in frundiferos locos,

ingenio arbusta ubi nata sunt, non obsitu.

Ribbeck2: Vos quí regalis córporis custódias

Agitátis, ite actútum in frundiferós locos,

Ingénio arbusta ubi náta sunt, non óbsita.

Marmorale: Vos qui regalis corporis custodias

agitatis, ite actutum in frondiferos locos,

ingenio arbusta ubi nata sunt, non obsitu.

Klotz: Vos qui regali’ corporis custodias

agitatis, ite actutum in frundiferos locos,

ingenio arbusta ubi nata sunt, non obsitu.

All major editors agree this fragment was probably placed near the beginning of the tragedy.
doc.apparatus:
1. regalis] regales Aldus, Stephanus

custodias] custodiam Iunius

2. agitatis, ite] agitatis, ite E α Schauer : agitis, ite L β Aldus, Stephanus, Delrius, Scriverius : agitis, ite, ite Duentzer : agiti’, ite Klotz, Vossius

3. arbusta] arbusto β

ubi nata] vineta codd., Aldus, Stephanus, Mercerus : ubi nata Scriverius, Klussmann : vinata [verisimiliter error typographicus] : innata Delrius, Mercerus in adnotatione ad Nonium p. 14,19, Mette

obsitu] obsitu α, Spengel : obstutas L β : obsita Delrius, Scriverius, Mercerus, Bothe, Klussmann, Quicherat, Ribbeck, Diehl, Traglia, Scaliger, Duentzer : obscuritas Pius, Aldus, Stephanus
doc.commentary:
regalis corporis custodias] corpus can refer both to a living body and to a corpse, but because of the combination with custodias it is almost certainly metonymically used for a living person. Cf. the phrase custodia corporis (protection, guard): quibusdam convenis et feris barbaris corporis custodiam committebat (from Cicero’s Tusculanae Disputationes). It is a frequent phrase in Curtius Rufus (5x) and occurs once in Livy as well. This is, however, the only instance, at least in archaic and classical Latin, where custodia combined with corporis is in the plural. cf. also the phrase custos corporis (body guard): antiqua erilis fida custos corporis (from Ennius’ Medea). It is frequent in the works of historians (2x in Nepos, 14x in Curtius Rufus, 6x in Livy). (cf. also expressions like festivum caput, lepidum caput in Terence, where a body part metonymically denotes a person.)

custodias agitatis] cf. Plautus, Rudens: agitato hic custodiam.

frundiferos] frundiferus=frondiferus

locos] in archaic Latin, loci and loca are not distinguished, as opposed to classical Latin, in which loci is usually used of places and loca of regions (vid. Lewis and Short, locus).

arbusta] originally denotes a place where trees are planted (see Lewis and Short, arbustum), but it can also mean trees, especially in poetry.

obsitu] seems to be an ablative of an otherwise unattested noun obsitus, -us, related to the adjective obsitus, in origin a perfect participle from obsero, ‘to sow’.
doc.reconstruction: The words of this fragment are said to the bodyguards of king Lycurgus, but by who is unclear. Klussmann thought that it is Bacchus, while Ribbeck (1875), Warmington, Marmorale and Traglia think it is Lycurgus himself, who commands them to keep off the invading bacchants. It is maybe a bit strange for the king to address his own body guards as ‘you who guard the royal body’, thus speaking almost in third person, but after all this is poetry, so this interpretation is not all that improbable. The emphatic reference to a place where trees grow by nature must indicate the place where the bacchants stay, because of their association with wild nature.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes the lines from Lycurgus within a definition of ingenio as meaning ‘on its own, by nature’:

ingenio veteres dixerunt et sua sponte vel natura. Naevius Lycurgo: vos qui regalis corporis custodias agitatis, ite actutum in frundiferos locos ingenio arbusta ubi nata sunt, non obsitu (Nonius 506 L)

lines.text: vos qui regalis corporis custodias
agitatis, ite actutum in frundiferos locos
ingenio arbusta ubi nata sunt, non obsitu
Video sepulcra dua duorum corporum.
– x – simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta a socru
possedit suo. x – x – x – x – u x

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

lines.text: <13>melius quam viri
callent mulieres

doc.translation: Women are more adept at trickery than men are.
doc.differences:
TRF: 8 (Accius' Aegisthus 5/5)

Ribbeck: 27 (Accius' Aegisthus 5/5)

Warmington: 10-11 (Accius' Aegisthus 4/5)

Franchella: 407-408 (Accius' Aegisthus 5/5)

D'Antò: 27-28 (Accius' Aegisthus 5/5)


Warmington:

Better than men are women hardened.

Franchella:

melius quam uiri

callent mulieres;

Le donne la sanno piú lunga degli uomini.

D'Antò:

…melius quam uiri

callent mulieres…

Più degli uomini sono astute le donne.

Dangel:

Les femmes s’y entendent mieux que les hommes.
doc.apparatus: No textual issues to report.
doc.commentary: callent: the verb probably means ‘to be cunning’: this is the definition of the word under which Nonius quotes our fragment, and this is clearly the sense the word also has elsewhere in Accius (Neoptolemus F 295 TRF = v. 475 Ribbeck3 = 475 Warmington = 184 Dangel: satin astu et fallendo callet 'Is he/she sufficiently adept at ruse and trickery?'). Lewis–Short see the relation between the two senses of calleo as a pun, with the the figurative sense (‘to be practised, to be wise by experience, to be skilful, versed in’) punning on the literal (‘to be thickskinned’). Such puns are common in Plautus, where slaves often manifest their determination to keep displaying their cleverness in the face of threats of physical violence (e.g. Poen. 574, Ps. 136, Pers. 305 magis calleo quam aprugnum callum callet, ‘I’m not as thick as pig’s skin’ [tr. de Melo]). It is unlikely that Accius is establishing a parallel between women and slaves. We could however imagine that a possible effect of Accius’ stereotype on the cleverness of women is that an audience might identify a pun and think of similar puns found in Plautine comedy on the connection between the cleverness of slaves, the beatings that are often a result of their cleverness, and the hardened skin that ensues. Warmington’s translation (‘are…hardened’) shows that he understands the word in its literal sense, the second sense indicated by Nonius, i.e. ‘to be callous, thickskinned’. The literal sense of ‘to be callous, thickskinned’, however, is rare. Furthermore, it seems unlikely that Accius is depicting a character expressing the idea that women are more ‘hardened’ than men (whether we take this to mean ‘experienced’ or ‘thickskinned, brave’), whereas the notion that women are more devious than men is a commonplace (e.g. Hom. Od. 11.456, Hes. Op. 67, 373-5, Eur. Med. 421-2).
doc.reconstruction:
Scholars are divided as to whether Aegisthus or Clytemnestra is the speaker.

If Aegisthus is the speaker, then he might be defending himself from the chorus’ criticisms by saying that he let Clytemnestra take the initiative in the murder because women are better at deceit and entrapment (cf. Aesch. Ag. 1636, τὸ γὰρ δολῶσαι πρὸς γυναικὸς ἦν σαφῶς ‘Well, the entrapment was obviously a job for a woman’ [tr. Sommerstein]: cf. Ribbeck1, 299 and Ribbeck 1875, 467-8; Warmington II.331; Venini 1954, 325; Franchella 260). Franchella 260 suggests that this fragment might be related to F 6 TRF = v. 25-6 Ribbeck3 = 8-9 Warmington = 302-3 Dangel (neque fera hominum pectora...) and that it forms part of a heated exchange between Aegisthus and the chorus.

If Clytemnestra is the speaker, then she might be saying these words to the chorus in the hope of buying some time (Bothe I.161), discussing her plan to trap Agamemnon (D’Antò 196-7), or speaking about Electra’s ruse to evacuate her brother Orestes from Mycenae (D’Antò 196-7).Aricò 2002, 15 compares Soph. El. 997 (Chrysothemis to Electra): οὐκ εἰσορᾷς; γυνὴ μὲν οὐδ᾿ ἀνὴρ ἔφυς ‘Do you not see? You are a woman, not a man’ (tr. Lloyd-Jones).

It seems difficult to rule out that this line could have been spoken by just about any character in the play (i.e. Aegisthus, Clytemnestra, Cassandra, Electra, chorus,...).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 257.29-31 Mercier = 392.53-393.56 Lindsay:

callet significat scit, hoc est, calliditate, quae est urbana scientia. Accius Aegistho: melius quam uiri | callent mulieres.

Our fragment is the first of eleven examples cited by Nonius of the use of the verb calleo. Nonius first cites passages where the verb callere is used in a sense similar to calliditas, ‘cunning, shrewdness’ (Afranius 3 Ribbeck3, Pacuvius F 36 TRF = 57 Schierl, Septimius Serenus F 14 Courtney, Accius Aeneadae siue Decius F 10 TRF, Accius Neoptolemus F 295 TRF, Sisenna Histories F 31 FRHist. He then writes that this verb is also used in a sense similar to callositas ‘thickness of skin’ and gives three examples (Caecilius Statius Asotus F 2 FRL = 14 Ribbeck3, Pl. Pers. 304, Poen. 578).

doc.metrical_analysis: Most editors print this fragment as two incomplete trochaic septenarii, though Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 123) suggested that the word callent could represent the beginning of an iambic senarius. Bothe (I.161) considers the metre of this line to be ‘asynartetus’ (Gr. ἀσυνάρτητος ‘disconnected, incoherent’): cf. the ‘Conspectus metrorum minus cognitorum’ in his edition of Plautus, I.xv-xxii. He describes the metre as ‘two hypercatalectic trochaics’. Ribbeck (1875, 468 n. 5) suggests that the line could also be the end of an iambic octonarius (…melius quam uiri callent mulieres).

lines.text: Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae
fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent
Natis sepulchro ipse es parens ! [x – x – x – u x]
