doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation: For, as soon as Troy has been set to fire and the booty equally divided among the partakers...
doc.differences:
TRF (8/8)
Warmington (1/8)
Ribbeck (1/8)
doc.apparatus:
1 Pergama] pergam codd. : Pergama emend. Iunius
2 et] et codd. : ac coni. Pius : est coni. Wessels
praeda] praedam codd. : praeda emend. Iunius
participes] codd. : principes vel primores conj. Payne

et is quite awkward in the line, and the ut Pergama accensa subordinate clause would fit better if est was read in place of the manuscript et, a reading which is compatible with either the possibilities of reading Pergama as a neuter plural taking a singular or as a feminine singular or conjecturing Pergamum for Pergama (see further the commentary). Coincidentally this would make it more similar to Seneca’s echo of the line at Sen. Ag. 421-422, ut Pergamum omne Dorica cecidit face, | divisa praeda est .
participes is pleonastic given the verb (‘the booty is shared among the sharers’) and perhaps should be suspected for this reason. More likely would be that the noun specified who the sharing was among. Both principes and primores (‘leaders’) are possibilities, with the former being closer to the transmitted participes and primores scanning better than principes, a word which scans with a short i in the second syllable where, in the line, we would expect a long syllable. What might be possible is that primores, which has quite an archaic flavour, was subsequently glossed as principes and then changed to participes for either metrical reasons or because of a misreading with the following partita est.
doc.commentary: partita est] If the last line follows a iambic senarius metre like the previous lines, partita est would not scan, since it would give three longs ( par-ti-tast), then that might imply that est was not in fact the word Livius wrote: there is, however, some evidence from comedy that syllables produced by aphaeresis which might by nature be scanned long could be scanned short. partio, usually, is constructed with inter, for examples (most of them, however, from later authors and prose texts) cf. L-S. s.v.; as to the transmission of the lines, there is no evidence, that the text is wrong here. Furthermore, per provides a nice alliteration (Pergama … praeda per participes …. partita).
doc.reconstruction: This fragment is placed at the very beginning by Ribbeck (1/8) and Warmington (1/8) and they both ascribe this fragment to the homecoming story of the Greeks.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
aequiter pro aeque ... Livius Aegistho:
nam ut Pergama
accensa et praeda per participes aequiter
partita est.

The lines are quoted by Nonius (824-825 L.) as an example of the use of aequiter in place of aeque.

lines.text: ⟨v-v-v-v-⟩ nam ut Pergama
accensa et praeda{m} per participes aequiter
partita est

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: I beg you to not curve me in prolixity for those things to be said.
doc.commentary:
oro: The use of this word in a discussion, generally signifying ‘I pray, entreat; I beg, plead’, is at first strange. Why should an expression of politeness find itself in an argument? It signifies that the relations of speaker and interlocutor are strained, and that the motive for discussion must be breached, if at all, under the guise of sympathy or insincere cordiality. The repetition of this oro in the preceding fragment thus hints at the necessary exercise of this sociality.

✝mi✝: In principle, there is nothing unusual about the syncope of the dative singular pronoun. It appears in the works of other poems (for instance in Catullus 5, Da mi basia mille, deinde centum). Ribbeck assumed ✝mi✝ ne reflected an earlier minime. For a detailed discussion of this conjecture and of this fragment’s editorial history, see Schierl (2006), 254.

fandi: the appearance here of the participle fandus, ‘which is to be spoken’, is again suggestive of the discretion needed to approach strained an while sustaining an appearance of well-meaning sympathies. The avoidance of implying what exactly ‘must be spoken’ must be of much importance, suggesting, naturally, the murder of Agamemnon and Clytaemnestra’s marriage to Aegisthus. In the Electra of Sophocles, there are prolonged references to the prolixity of the eponymous character and her insistence of speaking of her father’s death (Sophocles, Electra 86-120, 129-36, 213-20; note her ‘countless laments’ at 232f., ἀνάριθμος θρήνων).

prolixitudine: The case and number of this item corresponds to that of vanitudine, located in a verse immediately preceding fr.93, as arranged by Ribbeck3, D’Anna (1955) and Schierl (2006). The similarity of these verses, each initiated by a demand (oro…) and an insult, hints at contrasting views successively spoken in a stichomythia. Prolixity, as Nosarti (1999), 106 argues in his interpretation of the fragment, has pejorative connotations of excess and verbosity. Thus, this word shows us the attitude of one of the speakers of this supposed stichomythia, the need for directness and for the elucidation of ‘those things which must be spoken’, fandi.
doc.reconstruction:
It has been argued in the commentary that the structural similarity of fr.93 and fr.92 suggest they appeared close to one another in a stichomythia. Another factor, though necessarily indirect, is the assumption that Nonius often has recourse to the same passages to elucidate concepts in his lexicon. If this is indeed the case with these fragments, then they are indeed closely related in Pacuvius’ tragedy.

In my view, the speaker of this fragment is Electra, and her interlocutor Clytaemnestra. Electra is challenging her mother, perhaps on her decision to wed her to Oenax (a theme present in other fragments of this play, frgs. 146-8, 179-80 D’Anna = 88-9, 105 Schierl), or on the question of the death of her father Agamemnon.

Alternatively, on the basis of the Electra of Sophocles, may we think of this interchange as a conflict between Electra and the sister Chrysothemis or maybe even the nurse, who as an older figure may counsel Electra on not pursuing the vanities of her youth (fr. 92).

context.author: Nonius
context.text: prolixitudinem. Pacuvius Duloreste:

lines.text: Oro ✝mi✝ ne flectas fandi me prolixitudine

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: they who guard your high boundaries
doc.differences:
TrRF: 36 (Naevius Lycurgus 20/24)

Ribbeck2: 20 (Naevius Lycurgus 1/24)

Ribbeck3: 17 (Naevius Lycurgus 1/24)

Warmington: 24 (Naevius Lycurgus 1/35)

Marmorale: p. 191 (Naevius Lycurgus 1/24)

Klotz: 19 (Naevius Lycurgus ?/24)
doc.apparatus:
Ed. Li.: -

Edd. rel.: tuos qui] evos qui P[Qui]

tuos qui] tuosque Scriv. i.m.

It seems that the Nonius edition of Lindsay does not say anything about this line: Lindsay does not provide an apparatus criticus for this line. It is unclear from which word the word evos in P[Qui] should come. tuosque in the margin in Scriverius’s edition does not give problems. The translation would in this case be ‘and they guard your high boundaries’.

The last part of the fragment is lost, as can be seen: the TRF prints ‘[…]’.
doc.commentary:
tuos] can be read with terminus, ‘your boundaries’, namely Lycurgus’s borders. It can also belong to another male plural word in the accusative, from the line above this line.

qui] this word refers to the men who protect the borders, probably king Lycurgus’s guards.

celsos] ‘high’, ‘lofty’. This can be meant physically and morally (Lewis & Short): it is possible that the borders of the country (Thrace) lie high, but the word can also indicate the status of the boundary-lines (‘lofty’, ‘great’ boundaries). The first option is maybe more likely, because Thrace was very mountainous.

tutant] tutare is the active form of the more frequent verb tutari (‘to watch’, ‘defend’, ‘protect’).
doc.reconstruction: This fragment is probably part of the beginning of the play. It seems that someone is talking to the ruler of the country (‘your boundaries’): presumably king Lycurgus. The person who is talking could be a messenger, who has arrived at – for example – Lycurgus’ palace and tells now what the guards who are protecting the borders have seen. They have maybe seen the bacchants (Bacchae), the female followers of the god Bacchus, in their territories, perhaps hunting, or – as fragment 22 in the TRF says – ‘playing with joy and along a stream taking water with vessels out of the spring’. In the third epeisodion of Euripides’ Bacchae arrives a messenger too and he tells king Pentheus what he has seen: the bacchants are among other things feeding animals and they let come water out of a rock and wine and milk out of the ground (Eur. Bacchae 699-710).

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: Nonius shows some examples of the use of the active form tutare (pp. 763-764 Nonius edition Lindsay, vol. III), instead of the passive form tutari. Nonius’s examples are – besides Naevius Lycurgus – from Plautus Mercator, Pomponius Parci and Pacuvius Periboea.

lines.text: tuos qui celsos terminos tutant […]
[in] pectore *incohatum *fulmen * flammam ostentabat Iovis.

context.text:
Nonius quotes the fragment in his <i>De Compendiosa Doctrina</i>, book 8 “De mutata declinatione” (the same book in which he also quotes frg. 63 Schierl), to explain that aestus is also used in the o-declination. The passage quoted is the only example to demonstrate the issue. There appear to be no other examples for the use of <i>aesti</i> in Latin literature, see ThLL I 1115.73. <br><br>

<i>aesti pro aestus. Pacuvius Chryse: </i><b>aesti forte ex arido.</b>

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: just now in that place fear and anxiety for the approaching one double us
doc.differences:
TrRF: 38 (Naevius Lycurgus22/24

Ribbeck2: 43 (Naevius Lycurgus 16/24)

Ribbeck3: 40 (Naevius Lycurgus 16/24)

Warmington: 40 (Naevius Lycurgus 11/25)

Marmorale: p. 196 (Naevius Lycurgus 16/24)

iam ibi nos dublicat advenientis timos, pavos

Warmington: iam ibi nos dublicat advenientis Liberi timos pavos

iam ibi nos dublicat advenientis timos, pavos

Ribbeck: iam ibi nos dublicat advenientis timos

Marmorale places the fragment more to the end of the tragedy, after Dionysus has been freed from prison. Fear seizes a company not long after they see the Bacchae. Ribbeck puts this fragment at the same place. Only Warmington places it a bit earlier, assuming the one approaching is Dionysus himself.
doc.apparatus:
pavos] om. δ. : del. Ribbeck et Klotz : secl. Quicherat et Mueller

ibi nos] codd.

timos pavos] codd. : lac. stat. post advenientis Ribbeck, Warmington et Klotz: timos, vaposque conj. Manutius, Stephanus et Scriverius: conj. timos vapos Bothe

dublicat] dublicant conj. Manutius et Stephanus

advenientis] advenientes conj. Quicherat

In general, there do not seem to be many textual problems for this fragment. There is an overwhelming consensus in the manuscripts for the reading above. Only editors later have found it a problem that Nonius does not mention pavos, where he does mention timos. Therefore some wanted to replace pavos with vapos,, which Nonius does mention. There is another problem with dublicat, which is a singular and the subject is plural. When we leave advenientis as it is, it can be read as both a genitive (fear for the one approaching) or, with a long -i-, as an accusative, congruent with nos. Someone conjectured it to advenientes to make only the last option possible. This does not seem plausible, however, because it may be preferable to leave both options open. In any case, advenientis is the more difficult reading and thus preferable. In short, these conjectures seem motivated by the citation context (more on that later) or based on grammar. There seems to be, as far as we can infer, no problem with the context within the play.
doc.commentary:
advenientis Ribbeck, Warmington and Klotz have proposed a lacuna after advenientis to make the line fit into a septenarius trochacius. This has inspired Warmington to propose advenientis Liberi timos pavos instead. Ribbeck proposed to delete pavos instead. This word could also be a gloss, which is another reason to delete it according to Mueller. The line as it is however, could be a senarius iambicus without further emendations. This semi-problem has invited a lot of conjectures. Ribbeck proposed in his apparatus advenientis maximus timos and also advenientis anxiae mentis timos. Bothe, also to ensure a senarius iambicus, emendates to iam ibi advenientis duplicat nos timos, vapos, which seems like a rather large change, which cannot be backed up by any of the manuscripts. Iunius, at last, proposed timos Panos to signify a fear of panic. Ribbeck has even got a lot more conjectures to fill this lacuna which he himself had proposed: intimos animos or intimas mentis or mentis turbatos or animi sollicitos, which all seem like a step too far. The manuscripts overwhelmingly agree on the line, with the exception of δ deleting pavos. Ribbeck does not explain how there could have been an error in the manuscript tradition to come to one of the conjectures, which makes them extremely unlikely. A lacuna could indeed exist and pavos could indeed be a gloss, but this is no reason to change the entire line.

timos pavos Some people (Manutius, Stephanus & Bothe) have emendated pavos to vapos, because they think timos pavos is twice the same thing and that pavos could be a gloss. Vapos would then be a good option, because it is metrically convenient and Nonius uses it as an example, as opposed to pavos. The meaning is less clear. It could be that along with fear, the people are also automatically hot. This would assume that people become hot when they are afraid, which goes against the expression in English “frozen with fear”. pavos could then be included later as a gloss for vapos. That seems like a reasonable alternative and also agrees with the principle difficilior lectio potior.
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment is spoken by someone who is telling about an event when he, with others, (nos) came somewhere (ibi) and was immediately overwhelmed by fear (timos, pavos) for someone approaching (advenientis). If we follow Quicherat’s emendation, the people are arriving somewhere and become immediately afraid. This does not change the meaning that much, but leaves out a detail that could probably help us to put this fragment into context. It could be that the someone approaching is Dionysus or some Bacchant of whom people become immediately afraid. Someone from among the people arriving is probably telling a story about what happened to him and others to someone else. A logical conclusion could be that this is a messenger story.

From Nonius we know that the fragment is definitely from Naevius’ Lycurgus. The plot of this play is uncertain, but we know something from Hyginus. Furthermore, the play could be based on the tetralogy by Euripides’ Lycourgia and on the Bacchae by Euripides. From this we learn that the Lycurgus was probably a story about Lycurgus not accepting Dionysus as a god and consequently being punished for this.

Most people assume that this line belongs to a bigger messenger story about people who encounter Bacchi or Bacchae and are immediately struck with fear. This could be a guard who just came back from an encounter with the Bacchi and now tells about this to Lycurgus (Ribbeck, Warmington, Marmorale & Traglia). This seems the most likely and least controversial option. It has also been stipulated that these people were not simply struck by fear, but by fear of panic (Klussmann, Bothe & Iunius). To conclude, it seems that there are a lot of reasons people could be struck by fear in this tragedy and we can never really know where the fragment belongs.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted once in De compendiosa doctrina by Nonius Marcellus

Nonius uses this line to prove that the old form of the nominative ending in -os is the same as the form of the nominative ending in -or for the words vapos, timos, labos and colos. He gives for vapos an example from Lucretius and uses this example from Naevius for timos. Because pavos is also an example for this phenomenon, some editors have assumed it should be deleted or have stipulated vapos should be there instead of pavos.

vapor et vapos et timor et timos et labor et labos ita sunt ut color et colos. […] Naevius Lycurgo: iam ibi nos duplicat advenientis timos, pavos (Non. Marc. De comp. doct. 487.6-10

Qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum.
et me Apollo ipse delectat ductat Delphicus.
qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum
Eho tu, di quibus est potestas motus superum atque inferum,
pacem inter sese conciliant, conferunt concordiam.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: crispisulcans igneum
fulmen

doc.translation: The rough-furrowing, fiery thunderbolt
doc.differences:
TRF: 78 (Adespoton)

Ribbeck: 36 (Adespoton)

Warmington: 239-240 (Adespoton)

Franchella: 3 (Adespoton)

Not in D'Antò's edition.

Not in Dangel's edition.

Warmington:

A crinkle-furrowing fiery lightning-flash.

Franchella:

crispisulcans igneum

fulmen

L’igneo fulmine che solca serpeggiando ⟨il cielo⟩.
doc.apparatus:
crispisulcans igneum fulmen] crispisulcans igneum fulmen: crispisulcans fulmen igneum Klotz﻿

In his critical apparatus, Klotz (1953, 322) suggests that the word order might be inverted so as to read crispisulcans igneum fulmen.
doc.commentary: crispisulcans: a hapax formed of crispus, s.v. OLD 3 ‘curled, twisted’ and sulcare ‘to make a furrow’ (Reinhardt 2003, 329-30). Accius here uses the common metaphor of the sea as tillable land (see Thomas on Verg. Georg. 1.50, 204-30). In his translation (‘L’igneo fulmine che solca serpeggiando ’), Franchella indicates that the direct object of crispisulcans an untransmitted caelum ‘sky’, though this seems to run counter to Cicero’s apparent indication that the tragic fragment describes Ajax’s ship catching fire.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck places this fragment among the plays of uncertain authorship but suggests that it is related to Accius Clytemnestra F 145 TRF = v. 35 Ribbeck3. He goes so far as to print the two fragments together in his 1875 monograph, p. 463: crispisulcans igneum | pectore incohatam fulmen flammam ostentabat Iouis ‘the rough-furrowing, fiery thunderbolt of Jupiter displayed a half-formed flame on his [probably Ajax’s] breast’).

He elsewhere (Ribbeck1, 275) that it could belong to Ennius’ Achilles or to Accius’ Armorum iudicium, in a scene in which Ajax himself speaks of the destruction of the Greek fleet (cf. inc. inc. fab. F XXXII Ribbeck1). Warmington goes so far as to attribute this fragment as a secure fragment (certum) of Accius’ Clytemnestra (239-240 Warmington).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cic. Top. 61: at cum in Aiacis nauim crispisulcans igneum fulmen iniectum est, inflammatur nauis necessario, ‘but when the fiery lightning’s jagged furrow has been hurled onto Ajax’s ship, the ship is necessarily set on fire’ (tr. Reinhardt).

The context for the passage of Cicero is a discussion of sufficient and necessary causes, in which the example of Ajax’s ship taking fire after being struck by a thunderbolt is an example of a sufficient cause and is contrasted with the beams in the woods of Pelion being cut down, which is a necessary cause for the building of the Argo (with quotation from Ennius’ Medea). Reinhardt (2003, 330) notes that ‘Cicero is quoting a phrase from early tragedy’.

doc.metrical_analysis: No metrical information given in the TRF edition. Based on his proposed combination of this fragment with Accius Clytemnestra F 145 TRF pectore incohatum fulmen… (1875, 463), Ribbeck seems to suggest that the metre is the trochaic septenarius.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Stasiastae
doc.translation: Do you not see what tumult, what great martial disorders you spur?
doc.differences:
Dangel: 3 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 3/4)

Ribbeck1: 4 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 4/4)

Ribbeck2: 4 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 4/4)

Warmington: 609 (Accius’ Stasiastae sive Tropaeum Liberi 1/4)

D’Antò: 4 (Accius’ Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi 4/4)

non vides quam turbam, quantos belli fluctus concites?

Ribbeck1: ... non vides quam turbam, quosve fluctus concites?

All the editors attribute the fragment to Accius’ tragedy Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi, although Warmington substitutes vel with sive in the title. Following the order of Nonius’ quotes, Ribbeck1-2 and D’Anto place this fragment (frg. 4 Ribb.1-2 & frg. 4 D’Anto) as last. However, D’Antò argues that it belongs to the first scenes of the tragedy, before Lycurgus’ strong opposition to Liber has really started. Warmington, who arranges the fragments according to their content, places it (frg. 609 Warm.) in the beginning, as first. Dangel (who also uses a content-based arrangement) puts instead the fragment (frg. 3 Dang.) in the middle – more precisely in the third place – when the king has already slaughtered the Maenads.
doc.apparatus:
quantos belli] quantos belli Mueller : quantos vel codd. : quosve Mercerus

The fragment’s text is not very problematic, with the exception of the printed quantos belli. The manuscripts transmitted quantos vel, but this reading is not satisfactory and has been rejected by the majority of the editors. Apart from Ribbeck1 who adopts Mercerus’ conjecture, editors have usually followed Mueller's. The conjecture quantos belli enhances the metaphorical meaning of fluctus and fits nicely with quam turba (see further the commentary).
doc.commentary:
turba] According to the Lewis & Short, turba primarily means ‘turmoil of a crowd of people’, but also – secondarily – ‘disturbance made by a few or a single person’, as in this fragment. In some cases, the term loses this negative value and comes to signify, more neutrally, just ‘multitude’ (of people and things).

belli fluctus] According to D’Antò, belli fluctus is a periphrasis, typical of lofty archaic poetry.
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment contains two verbal forms in the second person singular, meaning that the line belongs to a dialogue where (at least) two people are involved and on stage. The tone is aggressive and the speaker is accusing his addressee using a rhetorical question. The attack is centred on the disorders and turmoil the latter provoked. Considering the tone, it appears that the two people involved are powerful and, therefore, it is likely that they are Lycurgus and Liber.

According to Dangel (2009), the fragment contains a part of the speech delivered by Bacchus to Lycurgus. After the king of Thrace had planned to slaughter the Maenads (see Dang. frg. I), the god threatens him with a deadly punishment (frg. II) – which will become real in Dang. frg. IV. In the fragment under analysis (Dang. frg. III), Liber violently accuses him of turmoils and the murder of the Bacchants. The speaker of this fragment is then, according to Dangel, Bacchus and he is speaking to the murderous king.

Warmington (1936) agrees with Dangel that Liber is speaking to Lycurgus, but he places this fragment not in the middle of the tragedy, but as first. Warmington (1936, 535) moreover argues that Accius probably followed the myth in which the Bacchants are not slaughtered by Lycurgus but set free. These two aspects result in a variation of the meaning attributed to the fragment: the god is not reproaching the king for the massacre, but warning him as Lycurgus threatens to revolt against Bacchus and his followers.

There is another alternative reconstruction which involves again the king and the god, but where Lycurgus is the speaker and Bacchus the addressee. As Dangel (2009, 338) persuasively argued, the conflict between the two speakers can be presented in terms of contrast between political order (embodied by Lycurgus) and Bacchic fury (Liber). With this in mind, we can read the fragment from another perspective. The Thracian king could accuse Bacchus (and the Bacchants) of creating and spurring disorders in his state, alluding to the Dionysiac forces, emblem of wildness. turba and belli fluctus are then to be read metaphorically. Consequently, the fragment could belong to the initial part of the tragedy, where Lycurgus confronts the chaotic Bacchus who has recently arrived in Thrace.

D’Antò (1980) is the only scholar to argue that Bacchus is not directly involved in the scene at this point, but one of Lycurgus’ subalterns is speaking to his king. Compared to the other editors, he gives significant prominence to the role of the Edones – Lycurgus’ people – in the general plot of the tragedy. According to D’Antò (1980, 459), they will eventually revolt against their king (no longer against Bacchus!) and kill him, afraid of the consequences of fighting a god. This fragment, however, is argued to belong to the first part of the tragedy, when the Edones – still loyal to Lycurgus – are admonishing their king for the evil he is attracting against his own subjects. In other words, they are trying to discourage the king from opposing Liber.

Bibliography

Dangel, J. 2002. Accius Oeuvres. Paris.

D’Antò, V. 1980. Accius: I frammenti delle tragedie. Lecce

Ribbeck, O. 1852. Tragicorum Latinorum Reliquiae (1st ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. 1871. Tragicorum Latinorum Reliquiae (2nd ed.). Leipzig.

Warmington, E. 1936. Remains of Old Latin, Volume II: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Pacuvius. Accius. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius discusses the different meanings of ‘turba’ and ‘turbae’, the former signifying ‘crowd’ while the latter ‘troubles’. However, it is possible that in some cases the singular form is used with the meaning of the plural, thus ‘turba’ can indicate ‘troubles’, as in Accius Stasiastae:

‘turbam’ et ‘turbas’ diuersam uolunt habere significationem, ut sit turba populi conuentus, turbae turbationes; nos contra…inuenimus…indiscrete positum et pro ‘turbis’ ‘turbam’: Accius, Athamante…(23) idem Stasiastis uel Tropaeo: Non uides quam turbam, quantos belli fluctus concites? (Non. 524, 18).

lines.text: non vides quam turbam, quantos belli fluctus concites?
Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli
[THYESTES]: Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser
manderem natos!
[ATREUS]: Ecquis hoc animadvortet? Vincite!

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation: Let him/her solemnly praise with pleasure (... ?)
doc.differences:
TRF (7/8)
Warmington (6/8)
Ribbeck (5/8)
doc.apparatus: †adeo ditali† laudet] adconditali laudetL : adeo ditali laudetF3 H G E: adeo attuli laudem et coni. Bothe : adeo litato audit coni. Düntzer : adeo ditat laude illubens coni. Klussmann sollemnitusque deo dicat laudes coni. Mueller : deo litat laudem coni. Ribbeck, def. Spaltenstein : adcantitat laudes coni. etiam dubitanter Ribbeck (Zeitalter..., 1875) : deo litat laudem et coni. Warmington : : adeo inclita dilaudet coni. Quicherat : adeo dyta, li laudes Waszink
doc.commentary:
sollemnitusque sollemnitus used for sollemniter (259 L.) is a hapax, cf. Spaltenstein (2008 : 55). The connotations of sollemnitus suggests a religious setting for this scene. If the conjectures of deo, for transmitted adeo, are right, then perhaps a sacrifice for a specific god, in an act of worship, cf. laudet. The enclitic -que suggests this line is not a single line of dialogue, but is part of a longer speech.

†adeo ditali† These words are incomprehensible due to the textual corruption. The transmitted reading cannot stand, because ditali is not a Latin word. Lots of different suggestions have been made for this verse. Warmington’s conjecture deo litat laudem et (“He made good sacrifice of praise to God”, trans. Warmington) - an expression which could be supported by much later usages from post-Republican literature (e.g. Lucan 1,632: diis sanguinem humanum) - would suggest that a god has to be appeased. But Agamemnon is returning from an undertaking, not beginning one. Conjectures which avoid litat, such as Bothe’s attulil laudem et lubens or Mueller’s deo dicat laudes lubens fit better a context of thanksgiving.

lubens Usually means ‘with pleasure’ or ‘willingly’.
doc.reconstruction:
Little can be concluded from this corrupted verse. What can be said with certainty is that within a religious setting (cf. sollemnitus) an act of praise (cf. laudet is taking place, or to take place, out of a character’s own volition (cf. lubens. It is not clear whether the character is on stage or offstage. With the transmitted reading, then the verse is concurrent with the action described. That suggests that one speaker is describing an action undertaken by another character, which suggests three alternatives: both characters are on stage, or the action is something offstage which the speaker can see but the audience cannot, or the action is seen by neither the speaker nor the audience, but instead the speaker and the audience can hear it. The only conjecture to alter this is Bothe’s attuli laudem, which changes the line to a retrospective first person description. This would ‘solve’’ the problem of a third person description of present onstage action, a practice avoided by Attic tragedy, although there may be evidence of this practice in later Senecan tragedy (cf. Zanobi (2014) on Seneca’s debts to pantomime).

There are several options for speaker and subject. This verse could be about Agamemnon performing sacrifice. Having survived the storm, and victoriously returning to Mycenae after the Trojan War, a sacrifice in thanksgiving to the gods for a safe and successful return would not be unexpected. While Aeschylus’ Agamemnon only refers to his intention to greet the gods (cf. Aesch. Ag. 810-11, 852) and to offer them something in the way of votive offerings (829), he does not offer a sacrifice before he enters the house and meets with death at Clyemnestra’s and Aegisthus’ hands. However, in Seneca’s Agamemnon, when he arrives on stage (Sen. Ag. 782, greeting the lares), after a short dialogue with Cassandra, and Agamemnon invokes Jupiter and Juno and his specific plan to sacrifice to them (cf. colam, 807):

At te, pater, qui saeva torques fulmina
pellisque nubes, sidera et terras regis,
ad quem triumphi spolia victores ferunt,
et te sororem cuncta pollentis viri,
Argolica Iuno, pecore votivo libens
Arabumque donis supplice et fibra colam. Sen. Ag. 802-807

He leaves the stage and again is killed before he can carry the sacrifice out. So in neither Aeschylus’ Agamemnon or Seneca’s Agamemnon does Agamemnon actually perform a sacrifice onstage, although Seneca’s Agamemnon does at least specifically plan to sacrifice. Interestingly, in Seneca, Agamemnon describes himself as libens, the same word used of the character of frg. 8. In this light, this fragment might be understood as ‘a solemn thanksgiving for a happy home-coming’, see Sanford (1923: 282), which Agamemnon performs before entering his palace. There are multiple possibilities for who could be describing the scene, if it is about Agamemnon performing the sacrifice: it could be the chorus, or it could be Clytemnestra, or any other character, e.g. Cassandra or a messenger. There is an another possibility if the line occurs in the immediate aftermath of Agamemnon’s arrival, that Clytemnestra would be the subject, so that the line is not describing Agamemnon’s worship to a god but instead how Clyemnestra praises Agamemnon (cf. Aesch. Ag. 855-913, esp. 908-913, although here her speech is ironic, saying the opposite of what she means). For this possibility, we must trust Nonius that sollemnitus is not a predicative (i.e. grammatically not masculine singular).

The line might also refer to the assassination of Agamemnon, which can be supported by the reading of sollemnitusque deo litat laudem et lubens by Ribb.1,2 and Warmington. The verb litat then refers to a sacrifice. Spaltenstein (2008: 49-50) discusses the possibility to read this fragment as a comment on the murder, which is also presented as a sacrifice in Aes. Ag. 1386-1387 τοῦ κατὰ χθονὸς | Διὸς νεκρῶν σωτῆρος εὐκταίαν χάριν and Sen. Ag. 897-898 qualisque ad aras colla taurorum prius | designat oculis antequam ferro. If we follow this idea, the one who commits the murder and does this willingly (lubens) can be identified with Aegisthus, who would have been happy with the murder, cf. Aes. Ag. 1577 ὦ φέγγος εὖφρον ἡμέρας δικηφόρου. If this fragment does refer to the murder of Agamemnon, the speaker of this verses might perhaps be Cassandra describing the murder, cf. Sen. Ag. 867-909.

If this fragment belongs to the homecoming of Agamemnon, one might presume that the speaker of this line is a messenger, describing Agamemnon’s acts after he comes homes (perhaps, with Seneca’s Agamemnon in mind, even by Eurybates), cf. Spaltenstein (2008: 49). The line might also be sung by the chorus, as suggested by Ribbeck (1875: 29) and Sanford (1923: 282).

Ribbeck (5/8) and Warmington (4/8) place this fragment in the middle of the play.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Should I not perform the task of/ for the people and take the evil deed ill?
doc.differences:
D’Anna: 149 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 4/31)

Ribbeck2,3: 129 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 10/32)

Schierl: 98 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 12/32)

Warmington: 126 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 6/34)



nonne officium fungar vulgi atque aegre malefactum feram?

Ribbeck2,3: nonne officium fungar vulgi, atque aegre male factum feram?

Warmington: nonne officium fungar vulgi atque aegre male factum feram?



All editors implicitly or explicitly ascribe the fragment to a dispute. D’Anna places the fragment at the beginning of the play in what might be the setting of the wedding between Oeax and Electra or Erigone. The fragment is placed directly after the fragment in which the wedding is explicitly mentioned (frg. 89 Schierl). In his edition, the fragment is the first to allude to a dispute, probably between Electra and Clytaemnestra. Warmington also places the fragment at the beginning of the play but after the prologue in which Orestes receives an oracle and tells how his father lost his fleet during his journey home. The fragment is placed after the fragment in which is stated that you should not give in to anxiety (frg. 99 Schierl). Hence, it is the second fragment that is part of the dispute between Clytaemnestra and Electra. Although Ribbeck places the fragment after the same fragment (frg. 99 Schierl), the fragment is assigned a later place. Since it is still placed among other fragments that could refer to a dispute (frg. 92, 89, and 107 Schierl) Ribbeck seems to suggest that the dispute took place somewhat later in the play. In Schierl’s arrangement of the fragments, the fragment is also given a later place. In her edition, the fragment is placed after the fragment in which Electra or Orestes wishes to become similar to her/his mother in character in order to avenge her/his father (frg. 97 Schierl). She also thinks it likely that the fragment is part of a dispute but keeps the option open that it is not a dispute between Electra and Clytaemnestra about the marriage with Oeax but a dispute between Orestes and somebody who warns him.
doc.apparatus:
officium] vitium coni. L. Müller

malefactum] corr. cod. Bambergensis, ed. princ. : male fatum codd. : male factum Ribbeck, Warmington

In the manuscripts the reading male fatum is found. However, based on the codex Bambergensis this reading was corrected to malefactum in the editio princeps. All later editors have adopted this correction. However, Ribbeck and Warmington print male and factum as separate words.
doc.commentary:
nonne] This word is used in ancient Latin for questions to which an affirmative answer is expected. Here the word is used in combination with the deliberative subjunctives (Schierl 2006, 259) or future indicatives fungar and feram. In both cases, the question posed by the speaker probably was a rhetorical question.

officium fungar] In ancient Latin fungi was a transitive verb. In combination with a genitive that designates a group or a person, the word combination officium fungi could mean “to behave like”. The speaker then seems to propose that he or she would perform the same task as the people normally do. However, officium fungi could also be translated more literally as “to perform a task”. When translated as such, this raises the question of whether the genitive vulgi should be translated as a subjective or objective genitive. When vulgi is considered a subjective genitive and is translated as “the task of the people”, the speaker refers to a task that is normally performed by the people. However, Petaccia (2000, 89) and Manuwald (2003, 77) consider it more likely to translate vulgi as a kind of objective genitive: “the task for the people.” The speaker would then consider the task an obligation to the people. Manuwald (2003, 77) interprets the task as the avenge for Agamemnon and thus concludes that the speaker did consider this avenge not only a family affair but also an obligation to the people. The words might then be spoken by Orestes to somebody who warns him that it could be dangerous to avenge Agamemnon. This could be Pylades for example.

officium] This word could refer to several things. Manuwald (2003, 77) interprets officium as the avenge for Agamemnon. Alternatively, officium could refer to the second part of the sentence: aegre malefactum feram. The speaker is then speaking about the way an evil deed is conceived. Müller (1889) also thinks that the word should refer to the second part of the sentence and therefore proposes the conjecture vitium. He probably considers this necessary because he interprets the arbitrary words aegre malefactum feram as “I should bear this injustice reluctantly” (for the arbitrariness, see the note on aegre malefactum feram). As with nonne, an affirmative answer is expected, the speaker would suggest reconciling himself or herself with the evil deed even if it would be with difficulty. This is unlikely since the line is probably spoken by Electra or Orestes (see Fragment Reconstruction). Both are usually depicted as rebellious and revengeful. Hence, it seems unthinkable that they would approve of the compliant behaviour of the people and even take it as an example. By emending officium to vitium Müller makes the speakers’ opinion about the behaviour of the people less positive.

aegre malefactum feram] The interpretation of these words is arbitrary. Schierl (2006, 259) translates them with “die übele Tat ertragen, wenngleich es mir schwerfällt”. According to her, the speaker proposes that he or she would behave like the people and bear the injustice, even when he or she finds this difficult. She identifies the speaker with Electra who discusses her behaviour with Clytaemnestra or Aegisthus. However, this interpretation is problematic since it is not likely that the rebellious and stubborn Electra would propose to comply instead of protest to the malefactum. Müller (1889) recognized this problem and emended officium to >vitium (see the lemma on vitium). However, his emendation solves the problem only partly. The speaker conceives the behaviour of the people more negatively but as long as nonne is retained an affirmative answer is expected. Thus, he or she still proposes to comply and bear the evil dead. This problem does not arise with the interpretation of Warmington (1936, 210) and Manuwald (2003, 77), who consider aegre ferre a fixed combination for “to take it ill” or “to resent”. With this reading, the words indicate that the speaker does not want to reconcile himself or herself with the evil deed. Müller's emendation is then no longer necessary since the speaker could suggest that the injustice should not be accepted by the people either. Whether they have complied with it or not, it would have been their task not to accept it.

malefactum] In the editions of Ribbeck and Warmington this word is printed with a space between male and factum. However, this editorial difference does not lead to a difference in interpretation. In both cases, the word(s) could refer to the murder of Agamemnon. However, the speaker could also refer to something else that is devised by Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus, the marriage between Oeax and Erigone or Electra for example.
doc.reconstruction:
Nonius in his De compendiosa doctrina ascribes this line to the Dulorestes of Pacuvius. None of the editors has doubted that the fragment originates from this play.

The fragment is written in the septenarius trochaicus, which makes it plausible that the line was part of a dialogue. The fact that the speaker uses the first person singular of the verbs fungar and feram to refer to himself or herself makes it also less likely that the line would have been spoken by the chorus. All editors interpret the line as part of a dispute. However, due to the fact that officium fungar vulgi and malefactum could be interpreted in several manners, there are several possibilities for the context and subject of the dispute (see Figure 1).

Warmington (1936, 211) and Schierl (2006, 259) suggest that the words could have been spoken by Electra to Clytaemnestra or Aegisthus. With this line, she could have defended her right to be critical or condemnatory and to disapprove with the malefactum. This dispute could be about the murder of Agamemnon which Electra considers an evil deed. In that case, the fragment was probably part of the beginning of the play in which Electra could have expressed the wish that the murder would be avenged. Schierl (2006, 259) also suggests that Electra could have spoken these words to Orestes when they discuss how she should behave. Electra might have wanted to play a bigger part in the avenge of her father than Orestes had planned. With this interpretation, the fragment would probably have been part of a later scene, as Orestes first has to arrive in Mycene disguised as a slave.

Another possibility is that Electra refers with malefactum to the fact that Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus had given her or her half-sister Erigone to Oeax in marriage. Her marriage with Oeax would certainly be involuntary as Oeax was one of the enemies of Agamemnon. Even if not she herself but her half-sister would be betrothed to him, Electra would probably still disapprove. If a wedding was part of the plot, it probably was one of the first scenes of the play since it requires Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus to be alive and because it provides an additional motive for their murder. The dispute about this wedding could have been part of the scene but could also have been staged just before or after this scene.

Alternatively, the words could have been spoken by Orestes, as was suggested by Petaccia (2000, 89) and Manuwald (2003, 77). This interpretation is based on the fact that vulgi could not only be read as a subjective genitive (the task of the people) but also as an objective genitive (the task for the people). In the latter case, the speaker speaks about an obligation he or she has to the people. A likely interpretation would then be that Orestes is speaking about his obligation to avenge his father. The addressee would probably be somebody who warned him of the potential dangers of murdering Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus. A good candidate would be his comrade Pylades. With this interpretation, the fragment could be part of an opening scene in which Pylades and Orestes undertake their journey to Mycenae and scheme the murder of Clytaemnestra and Aegisthus. However, an interpretation of vulgi as objective genitive does not exclude the possibility that the words are spoken by Electra. officium could also refer to the second part of the sentence (aegre malefactum feram). The speaker is then arguing that he or she has the obligation to function as a spokesman or spokeswoman for the people. This could be both Orestes and Electra.

Finally, it is also possible but perhaps less likely that the fragment is not part of a dialogue but of a monologue. Since questions that start with nonne are often rhetorical, the words are not necessarily directed to other people on stage. The speaker, Electra or Orestes, might have asked the question to herself/ himself while considering what to do or how to behave in reaction to the murder of Agamemnon or the betrothal to Oeax.

Bibliography

Manuwald, G. 2003. Pacuvius summus tragicuspoeta: Zum dramatischen Profil seiner Tragödien. München/ Leipzig.

Ribbeck2, O. 1871. Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Ribbeck3, 1897. Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Warmington, E.H. 1936. Remains of Old Latin. Livius Andronicus: Naevius: Pacuvius: Accius. Cambridge, MA/ London.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: [in] pectore incohatum fulmen flammam ostentabat Iovis

doc.translation:
The thunderbolt of Jupiter began to take shape (or ignite?) within his breast and revealed a flame. 
Or: The thunderbolt began to take shape (or ignite?) within his breast and revealed the flame of Jupiter.
doc.differences:
TRF: 145 (Accius' Clytemnestra 8/10)

Ribbeck3: 34/35 (Accius' Clytemnestra 5/10)

Ribbeck1: 34-35 (Accius' Clytemnestra 5/10)

Warmington: 241-242 (Accius' Clytemnestra 6/11)

Franchella: 393-394 (Accius' Clytemnestra 5/10)

D'Antò: 34-35 (Accius' Clytemnestra 5/10)

Dangel: 293 (Accius' Clytemnestra 3/10)

Warmington:

The half-formed flash of Jupiter revealed | A flame upon his heart.

Franchella:

...in pectore

fulmen incohatum flammam ostentabat Iouis ⏑⏓

Il fulmine che aveva cominciato ⟨a bruciare⟩ nel petto mostrava la fiamma di Giove.

D'Antò:

...in pectore

-⏑ fulmen incohatum flammam ostentabat Iouis

Sul petto egli mostrava la fiamma dal fulmine di Giove accesa.

Dangel: La foudre, formée en sa poitrine, était la manifestation de la flamme de Jupiter.
doc.apparatus:
in] in codd.: delevit Ribbeck2-3

incohatum] incohatum codd.: incohatam Beier: in cauato Frassinetti 1982, 131

incohatum fulmen flammam: incohatum fulmen flammam Ribbeck2-3: fulmen inchoatum flammam codd.: fulmen inchoatam ⟨flammam⟩ flammam Beier: fulmen incohatum flammam Ribbeck1: incohatam fulmen Ribbeck 1875, 463: fulmen incitatum Bothe

Editors before Ribbeck seem to have been more or less content with printing the paradosis, in pectore fulmen inchoatum flammam ostentabat Iouis. That is the text printed by Scriverius–Vossius (1620, 145) and Bothe (I.194). In his critical apparatus, Bothe had suggested incitatum for inchoatum, adducing as parallels a passage of Caesar and a passage of Valerius Flaccus (see ‘Critical apparatus’). D’Antò 202 lends support to Bothe’s conjecture by pointing out that the incitare, like the Greek verb βάλλειν ‘to throw’, can be used of atmospheric phenomena, comparing Lucr. 5.1094-5 multa uidemus enim caelestibus incita flammis | fulgere, cum caeli donauit plaga uapores ('we can see many things catch fire implanted with the flames from on high, when the stroke from heaven has given them its heat', tr. Rouse–Smith), but this parallel is dubious as the manuscript reading is insita, incita being a conjecture found in the 1512 Iuntina edition of Lucretius: cf. Deufert 2019, xix-xx. In his first edition, Ribbeck places the words in pectore in a previous line, making the second line begin with the fulmen, following a suggestion made to him by Hermann (cf. Ribbeck1, xiii). Hermann had also suggested that, in the second line, either inchoatum (incohatum and inchoatum simply being orthographical variants) is corrupt, or there are two syllables missing. Ribbeck solves this problem by adding the word flammans (i.e. fulmen incohatum flammam ostentabat Iouis) presumably on the grounds that it might have dropped out on account of its proximity with the almost identical word flammam. Here Ribbeck was following the lead of Beier (1820-1821, I.244), who had proposed to add flammam. Beier had also changed incohatum to incohatam, so as to make it agree with flammam (i.e. fulmen incohatam ⟨flammam⟩, flammam ostentabat Iouis ‘the thunderbolt revealed the half-formed flame, the flame of Jupiter’), a change which Ribbeck however rejects. In his second and third editions, Ribbeck prints the fragment as a single line. He deletes the preposition in found in the manuscripts of Servius Auctus, thus considering that the Accian fragment proper begins with pectore. He also decides to invert the word order found in the manuscripts, fulmen incohatum, and to write incohatum fulmen; and he removes the word flammans which he had added in his first edition. The text as he prints it constitutes a single trochaic septenarius. He explains in the apparatus of his second edition that he decided not to preserve in because the section of the messenger speech in which this fragment appeared is the same as two other fragments of Accius' Clytemnestra, F 146 (deum regnator…) and F 143 (flucti inmisericordes…), Ribbeck’s fragments III and IV respectively. His thought process is not entirely clear: he seems to think that, because all three fragments came from the same section of the messenger speech, they should each constitute a single line. Warmington prints the fragment over two lines, like Ribbeck in his first edition, but does not include Ribbeck’s flammans. Warmington thus prints the text of the paradosis over two lines, but his text does not seem to scan as a iambo-trochaic verse. In order to make the line scan, subsequent editors posited that a trochaic foot had fallen out of the line, either at the beginning (so Klotz and d’Antò, cf. too Mette 1964, 146) or at the end (Franchella). The two most recent editors, Dangel and Schultheiß, opt to print the same text as Ribbeck in his second and third editions: they thus present the fragment as a single trochaic septenarius.
doc.commentary:
Accius’ version of the death of Ajax son of Oileus seems to have been the one commonly found in Nostoi narratives (West 2013, 245) and in Greek tragedy: having been granted permission by Jupiter to use his lightning bolts, Athena struck down Ajax in punishment for his rape of Cassandra in Athena’s temple. According to other versions, Ajax drowned (Hom. Od. 4.496) or the lightning struck not him, but his ship (Lycophr. Alex. 387, Apollod. Epit. 6.6, Hor. Epod. 10.13-14, Sen. Ag. 537, Prop. 3.7.39, Hygin. Fab. 116.2). Scafoglio (2009, 95-8) however believes that in Accius’ Clytemnestra it was Juno and not Athena who wielded the thunderbolt to kill Ajax son of Oileus (see on Accius inc. fab. F 428 TRF).

The translations proposed by some editors reveal a difference in their understanding of the text. Warmington and D’Antò take fulmen with Iouis (‘the … flash of Jupiter’; ‘dal fulmine di Giove’), whereas Franchella and Dangel take flammam with Iouis (‘la fiamma di Giove’; ‘la flamme de Jupiter’). Moreover, Warmington takes fulmen in hyperbaton with Iouis and flammam in hyperbaton with in pectore: his interpretation is that Jupiter’s lightning bolt causes a flame to appear on Ajax’s breast: (‘The half-formed flash of Jupiter revealed a flame upon his heart’). Franchella and Dangel understand the lightning bolt to have taken shape within Ajax’s breast, taking pectore incohatum fulmen together (‘Il fulmine che aveva cominciato nel petto’; ‘La foudre, formée en sa poitrine, …’). D’Antò interprets the fragment yet differently still: his translation suggests that he believes the subject of the verb ostentabat to be unexpressed and to refer to Ajax (‘egli mostrava…’). The rest of his translation however seems to indicate that the text he is translating contains not fulmen flammam… Iouis, as printed, but rather fulminis flammam… Iouis, because he translates the the expression ‘the thunderbolt of Jupiter’ (fulmen Iouis) as if it were a genitive depending on flammam (i.e. ‘la fiamma dal fulmine di Giove’).

pectore: The textual variant pectore adopted, according to Servius, by those who believe that Vergil is imitating Accius at Aen. 1.44 (see 'Citation context') may suggest that Vergil was following Accius in using a version of the myth in which the thunderbolt struck not Ajax’s ship, but the breast of Ajax himself. Pierini (1980, 18) suggests that this version may have precedent in a Hellenistic gigantomachy or bear the influence of the similar death of Typhoeus (Hes. Th. 859, Aesch. PV 370-1, Ov. Met. 5.353 flammamque ferox uomit ore Typhoeus ‘savage Typhoeus pours forth a flame from his mouth’, cf. Nicander’s version according to Antoninus Liberalis, probably active in the second or third centuries CE (Metamorphoses 28.3): ἔπειτα δὲ Τυφῶνα Ζεὺς βάλλει κεραυνῷ. Καιόμενος δὲ ὁ Τυφὼν ἔκρυψεν ἑαυτὸν καὶ ἠφάνισε τὴν φλόγα τῇ θαλασσῇ, ‘And then Zeus struck Typhoeus with a thunderbolt: and Typhoeus, having been set on fire, hid himself and quenched the flame in the sea’.

pectore incohatum fulmen: Pierini (1980, 18) identifies a linguistic and stylistic affinity with Lucr. 6.391 icti flammas ut fulguris halent | pectore perfixo 'struck and breathe out sulphurous flames from breast pierced through' (of the deserved punishment of those who have committed abominable crimes, tr. Rouse–Smith) and further compares (41 n. 51) Lucr. 6.395 turbine caelesto subito correptus et igni 'suddenly involved in a tornado from heaven and taken off by fire' (of the unjust punishment of an honest man, tr. Rouse–Smith) and 5.1231-2 uiolento turbine saepe | correptus, 'he is often caught up in the furious hurricane' (of an admiral of a fleet in a storm at sea, tr. Rouse–Smith). On the Accian background of this passage of Lucretius, see also Taylor 2016, 144-5.

flammam ostentabat Iovis: Dangel notes that this scene was depicted on a famous fresco in Pergamon (Plin. NH 35.60). Warmington (II.409) suggests that there is a pun and that the mark left on Ajax’s chest by the thunderbolt is flower-shaped because ‘Jupiter’s fire’ was a name given to a certain type of flower (Plin. NH 27.44 ampelos agria uocatur herba foliis duris cineracei coloris, qualem in satis diximus, uiticulis longis, callosis, rubentibus, qualiter flos quem Iouis flammam appellamus, ‘Ampelos agria is a name given to a plant with hard leaves of an ashy colour, as I have described in my account of cultivated trees. It has long, hard-skinned twigs, of a red colour like the blossom we call flame of Jupiter’, tr. Jones–Andrews). This parallel is intriguing to say the least, and it cannot be ruled out that the shape of the flower is what Accius is referring to in this fragment.
doc.reconstruction: Usually printed as a trochaic septenarius, this fragment has often been thought to belong to the same part of the play, a messenger speech describing the storm which destroyed the Greek fleet on its way back from Troy, as two other fragments which are also in trochaic septenarii, F 146 TRF (deum regnator nocte caeca caelum e conspectu tulit, = v. 32 Ribbeck3 = 237 Warmington = 291 Dangel) and F 145 TRF ({in} pectore incohatum fulmen flammam ostentabat Iouis, = v. 34/35 Ribbeck3 = 241-242 Warmington = 293 Dangel). A further fragment of uncertain play that also may have belonged to this messenger speech is Accius inc. fab. F 428 TRF. But based on a different understanding of the metre of this fragment, Warmington instead believed that it belongs not to a messenger speech, but to a dialogue in iambic senarii following the messenger speech (‘the fr. was apparently part of a dialogue following the narrative in a different metre’, II.409). Ribbeck suggested (cf. Ribbeck3, 162; Ribbeck 1875, 463) that this fragment might be put together with an apparent tragic fragment by an unknown dramatist from an unknown play preserved in Cicero’s Topica: Adesp. F 78 TRF (= inc. inc. fab. 36-37, F XXI Ribbeck3 crispisulcans igneum | fulmen = Cic. Top. 61 at cum in Aiacis nauim crispisulcans igneum fulmen iniectum est, inflammatur nauis necessario ‘but when the fiery lightning’s jagged furrow has been hurled onto Ajax’s ship, the ship is necessarily set on fire’). He suggested that this fragment preserved in Cicero preceded our Accian fragment and thus proposed that the two should be combined to read: crispisulcans igneum | pectore incohatam fulmen flammam ostentabat Iouis (cf. Ribbeck 1875, 463).

context.author: Servius Auctus (DS)
context.text:
Servius Auctus in Verg. Aen. 1.44 (p. I.31.11-19 Thilo—Hagen):

expirantem flammas: [...] Probus et ‘tempore’ legit, ut ipse liquefacto tempora plumbo. Sed qui legunt ‘pectore’ de Accio [Actio D’Antò, Dangel] translatum adfirmant, qui ait in Clytemestra de Aiace in pectore fulmen inchoatum flammam ostentabat Iouis.

The full Vergilian text is: illum expirantem transfixo pectore flammas | turbine corripuit (Aen. 1.44-5). The fragment is preserved by Servius Auctus, who reports in his commentary on Verg. Aen. 1.44 that the text of the first-century CE grammarian Marcus Valerius Probus had the reading tempore, and not pectore, i.e. ‘with pierced temple’ and not ‘with pierced breast’. Vergil’s use of tempus at Aen. 9.588-9 (liquefacto tempora plumbo | diffidit ‘he [sc. Mezentius] split his temples in two with moulded lead’) is adduced as a parallel, though it is uncertain whether this parallel was adduced by Probus or by Servius himself. Servius explains that those who choose to read pectore, which is doubtless the correct reading as it makes more sense that Ajax would breathe out (expirantem) flames from his breast than from his temples, support this reading by arguing that Vergil is here alluding to Accius. Servius auctus adds that those who read tempore hold that the line has topical relevance and that it alludes to a painting of Capaneus depicted with a thunderbolt piercing both his temples situated in the temple of Castor and Pollux in Ardea.

doc.metrical_analysis: On the metre of this fragment, see the ‘Apparatus criticus’ section.

doc.title: Chryses
doc.translation: Meanwhile the waves begin to droop, the winds fall quiet, the sea is tamed.

Ribbeck3 76-77 (1/21)

flaccescunt] flaccescunt Mercerus, Lindsay, Klotz, D’Anna : flacciscunt codd., Ribbeck2/3, Warmington

(Schierl 2006, 213)

flacciscunt is a ἅπαξ λεγόμενον

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes the fragment in his De Compendiosa Doctrina, book 8 “De mutata declinatione”, to explain that fluctus is also used in the o-declination:

Flucti, pro fluctus. Accius Telepho: flucti cruoris volverentur mihi … (sc. Acc. Telephus, Frg. 10 D.), idem Clytaemestra: flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere (sc. Acc. Clytaemnestra frg. 2 D.), Pacuvius Chryse: interea loci | flucti flaccescunt, silescunt venti, mollitur mare.

lines.text: ⟨ - × -  × -  × -  × -  ×⟩ interea loci
flucti flaccescunt, silescunt venti, mollitur mare
Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi.
........ Serenas potiuntur plagas.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: <5> ego incipio; conata exequar

doc.translation: I myself am beginning: I shall carry my design to completion.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 4 (4/20), 202:

202: <quae> ego incipio conata exequar.

Warmington:

Fragment 167 (3/17):

[ATREUS]:

167: ego incipio; conata exequar.

I, I am but beginning; the attempts

I make I’ll carry through.

Franchella:

Fragment 4 (4/22), 352:

352: ego incipio; conata exequar.

Io comincio; porterò a termine quanto tenterò.

D’Antò: 

Fragment 3 (3/18), 202:

202: < x – x – > ego incipio; conata exequar.

io comincio; porterò a termine il piano.

Dangel:

Fragment 9 (9/21), 46:

46: – x – ] x – u x ego incipio ; conata exsequar.

Pour ma part, je m’y mets ; je veux mener l’entreprise à son terme.
doc.apparatus:
1: ego ] ego codd.: <quae> ego Ribbeck1, 136

1: incipio ] incipio codd: incipisso L. Müller 1888, I.299

1 ego: Ribbeck proposes to add quae (i.e. ‘I begin these things’), which he prints in all three editions.

1 incipio: L. Müller in his edition of Nonius (1988, I.299) conjectured incipisso. All the possible occurrences of this word are in Plautus (Plaut. Capt. 215, 532, 631-2, Mil. 228, 237 Trin. 833-4) and in each case the manuscript evidence is dubious. Thomas (1935) argues that such forms in -isso are due to a confusion made in Late Antiquity between the desiderative suffix -esso and the inchoative ending -isco, but incipisse at Plaut. Capt. 215 may well be correct and Pacuvius Atalanta F 44 Schierl (= Nonius p. 226.29 M = 336 L) has capissam (modified to capessam by Klotz and D’Anna). Though Thomas’ analysis may not necessarily be correct, there seems to be no reason to go against the manuscript evidence and accept Müller’s incipisso.
doc.commentary: 1: conata: Baldarelli (2004, 223) compares Atreus’ words to the attendant at Sen. Thy. 333 nostra tu coepta occule ‘and you, keep my venture secret’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.261) and Juv. 13.209-210 nam scelus intra se tacitum qui cogitat ullum | facti crimen habet. Cedo si conata peregit?, ‘You see, people who contemplate any secret wickedness in their heads incur the guilt of the deed. Then what if he carries through his efforts?’ (transl. Braund 2004, 451). She also points to Hyginus’ use of the verb exsequi (‘to pursue with vengeance or punishment’, OLD s.v. 3) at Fab. 88.1 (Atreus…cupiens a Thyeste fratre suo iniurias exsequi) as a possible parallel.
doc.reconstruction: Lana (1959, 301) places this fragment towards the beginning of the play, during Atreus’ conversation with his attendant. When the attendant expresses his horror as he begins to realise the nature of the plan for revenge that is taking shape within Atreus’ mind, Atreus responds that he fully intends to put his plan into action (ego incipio; conata exequar, F 104.7). Baldarelli (2004, 223) suggests that it could belong either to the beginning of the conversation (cf. Sen. Thy. 241-243 tandem incipe | animosque sume. Tantalum et Pelopem aspice; | ad haec manus exempla poscuntur meae, ‘Why paralyzed? Start at last, rouse your heart. Look to Tantalus and Pelops: my hands are called to follow their examples’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.251), or at the end (cf. Sen. Thy. 333, quoted above, see ‘Fragment commentary’).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius De compendiosa doctrina p. 202.13-14 Mercier = 297.10-11 Lindsay

CONATVS masculino. Neutro Accius Atreo: ‘ego incipio; conata exequar’ (= Accius Atreus F 107 TRF)

Nonius quotes this fragment in Book 3 of his De compendiosa doctrina, entitled ‘De indiscretis generibus’ (‘On undistinguishable grammatical genders’), in order to show that the word conatus (‘an effort to accomplish a desired end, endeavour, exertion, attempt’, OLD s.v. 1), which is normally a fourth-declension masculine noun, can sometimes be found declined as a neuter noun, as in this fragment of Accius. Lindsay (1901, 60) tentatively identifies Nonius’ source for this fragment as ‘Gloss. iii-v’, that is, one of three anonymous glossaries referred to by Lindsay as sources 26 (= Gloss. iii), 35a-b (= Gloss. iv), and 36a-b (= Gloss. v) respectively.

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambo-trochaic (seemingly the end of a line).

doc.author: Accius
doc.text:
absoluta est [sc. iuridicialis constitutio], cum id ipsum, quod factum est, ut aliud nihil foris adsumatur, recte factum esse dicemus. ea est huiusmodi: 'mimus quidam nominatim Accium
poetam conpellavit in scaena. cum eo Accius iniuriarum agit'. hic nihil aliud defendit nisi licere nominari eum, cuius nomine scripta dentur agenda.

lines.text: nulla sancta societas ne fides regni est,
O Fides alma apta pinnis, et ius iurandum Iovis!
. omnes gaudent facere recte, male pigrent

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Who is he? The one who will put you to death, unless you are ahead of him.
doc.differences:
Schierl: 104 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 18/32)

D’Anna: 173 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 20/31)

Ribbeck 3: 148 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 20/32)

Warmington: 145 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 20/32)

All editors ascribe this fragment to Pacuvius’ Dulorestes. However, there is dispute about who is the speaker and who is the addressee (see further the reconstruction). But the editors agree that this fragment must be placed in the part of the tragedy where Aegisthus and Klytaimnestra have not discovered Orestes’ identity yet. Also, they all adopt the conjectures of Vossius and Scriverius.
doc.apparatus:
te nisi illum] te nisi illum Vossius : tunc si illum pler. codd. : tunc sullum AA

dabit] dabit Scriverius : dabis codd.
doc.commentary:
te nisi illum] As is stated in the apparatus criticus, most codices have tunc si illum. However, the word tunc is difficult to read, because occupas is a present tense and dabit is future. Therefore, the conjecture of Vossius seems logical.

illum] It is probable that this word refers to someone who is not nearby, because otherwise hic or iste must have been used.

occupas] Nonius cites this verse to illustrate that occupare has here roughly the same meaning as praevenire.

leto] dative of direction.

dabit] As is stated in the apparatus criticus, most codices have dabis. If we read this, the subject of the verb will not be ‘he’, but ‘you’. But the premise nisi illum tu occupas already has ‘you’ as subject of the verb occupas. It is not logic that the verb of dare also would have ‘you’ as subject, because te is addressed. Therefore, the conjecture of Scriverius seems logical.

leto dabit] formula of the sacred language. In Republican tragedy we only find this by Ennius. In comedy we find morti in phrases like morti dare or morti mittere. Plautus uses three times letum.
doc.reconstruction:
The line is written in a trochaic septenar. This metre is frequently used in dialogues of Latin plays, which is also the case here. The fragment makes clear that it is a dialogue between two speakers about a third man (illum). The first speaker does not know the third man and this third man is a danger for him. This is probably Orestes who is not recognised by the first speaker due to his disguise. The second speaker seems to be on good account with the first one, because he warns him for the danger.

There are several possibilities for the first speaker, namely Aegisthus or Klytaimnestra. We can assume this from the fact that the other speaker says that he or she is in mortal danger. According to Schierl (2012, 264-265), the answer is given by Oiax. However, Hunink (2007, 176) and D’Anna (1968, 206) assume that this dialogue is between Aegisthus and Orestes (cf. Schierl 2012, 265). This statement is then an example of dramatic irony and may be a comic element in this tragedy, because the public will recognise Orestes while Aegisthus is unknown of his identity. This seems not probable to me, because they speak about a third person. It is more logical that this third person must be Orestes. Warmington (1936, 216-217) does only say that Aegisthus is the speaker of the first part and that the second part is spoken by an unspecified friend of Aegisthus.

Besides, scholars do not agree whether the warning is given when Orestes is already on stage (Warmington 1936, 217; Mette 1964, 97) or not (Ribbeck 1997, 242; Müller 1889, 26; Argenio 1959, 32). But the excitement of the verse, which is expressed in the antilabe, is a good reason for Schierl (2012, 265) to assume that there is an acute danger and thus that Orestes must be there.

However, as quoted upon in the fragment commentary, illum refers to someone who is not nearby. This could mean that Orestes is not on stage and that Aegisthus’ words are based on something he has heard. But it can also strengthen the dramatic irony when Orestes is saying the second part of this verse.

This fragment can be part of a conversation to which also fragment 102 and 103 (ed. Schierl) belong. In fragment 102 the speaker does not know what plan the foreigner has. This plan is specified in fragment 104. Fragment 103 maybe is about Aegisthus referring to Orestes after he has been warned of his plot. According to this interpretation, they belong to the same part of the play, although there are also other interpretations possible.

Bibliography

Argenio, R. (ed.) 1959. M. Pacuvio. I frammenti dei drammi, ricostruiti e tradotti da R.A.. Turin.

D’Anna, I. (ed.) 1968. M. Pacuvii Fragmenti. Rome.

Diehl, E. (ed.) 1911. Poetarum Romanorum Veterum Reliquiae. Bonn.

Hunink, V. 2007. Woeste mensenharten. Nijmegen, Den Bosch.

Lindsay, W.M. (ed.) 1903. Nonius. De Compendiosa Doctrina. Leipzig.

Mette, H.J. 1964. ‘Die Römische Tragödie und die Neufunde zur Griechischen Tragödie (insbesondere fúr die Jahre 1945-1964), Lustrum 9. Göttingen.

Müller, L. (ed.) 1889. De Pacuvii fabulis disputatio. Berlin.

Ribbeck. O. 1897. Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta: Tragicorum Romanorum Fragmenta. Leipzig.

Schierl, P. 2006. Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Ein Kommentar zu den Fragmenten mit Einleitung, Text und Übersetzung. Berlin.

Warmington, E.H. 1936. Remains of Old Latin: Livius Andronicus, Naevius, Pacuvius and Accius II. Cambridge, Mass.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius explains here that occupare has roughly the same meaning as praevenire

occupare est proprie praevenire. … Pacuvius Duloreste: “is quis est?” – “qui te, nisi illum tu occupas, leto dabit.” (Non. 562/563 Li.)

lines.text: is quis est? :: qui te, nisi illum tu occupas, leto dabit
Video sepulcra duo duorum corporum.
Mulier una duum virorum.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Or when you see him troubled by bereavement
doc.differences:
Schierl: 116 (Pacuvius Dulorestes 29/31)

Ribbeck3: 135 (Pacuvius Dulorestes 15/32)

D’Anna: 165 (Pacuvius Dulorestes 14/31)
doc.apparatus: illum] ultim L
doc.commentary:
sollicitum] sollicitus means in principal ‘thoroughly moved’, but here it is used of mental affections (Lewis & Short, II): ‘full of anxiety’, ‘disturbed’, ‘troubled’.

orbitudine] orbitudo (‘bereavement’) is the archaic form of the classic form orbitas (‘bereavement’, ‘childlessness’): bereavement of parents or children, husband or another dear person (Lewis & Short; Schierl 2006, 277).
doc.reconstruction: It is unclear who is speaking this verse and who is meant with the second person singular videas. illum could refer to Aigisthus, who would be troubled and disturbed by the loss of for example Erigone and/or Oiax (see Hunink 2011, 174; Schierl 2006, 277). It is possible that Orestes and Electra, now they are about to avenge their father, are talking here about how Aigisthus will be troubled when they kill his and Clytaemnestra’s daughter Erigone and their son in law Oiax. orbitudine can also refer to Aigisthus’ and Clytaimnestra’s childlessness, before Clytaimnestra bore Erigone (see Schierl 2006, 277). In this case the speaker talks about the mental affections Aigisthus had very long ago, before the plot of the Dulorestes. It is also an option that Aigisthus worries about the fact that he and Clytaimnestra still do not have a male child, a son (see Schierl 2006, 277): they have only their daughter Erigone. Finally it is possible that not Aigisthus is meant with illum sollicitum, but Orestes: he is troubled because he realises that he will bereave himself from his mother and thus be an orphan when he kills Clytaimnestra (see also Schierl 2006, 277).

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: Nonius shows in De Conpendiosa Doctrina the use of the word orbitudo instead of orbitas. See ‘Fragment Commentary’ for more information about these words. orbitudo is also used in Accius’ Amphitryo and Turpilius’ Thrasyleo. See p. 213 of the Nonius edition from Lindsay, vol. I.

lines.text: vel cum illum videas sollicitum orbitudine
Sed valvae resonunt regiae
Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socru
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctus nuptiis,
Iovis iste quidem pronepos
Peruade polum, splendida mundi
Sidera binis continuis sex
Picti spoliis.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Eumenides
doc.translation: I think that staying quiet and being wise according to your strength is the best thing: then you will know how to speak safely
doc.differences:
TRF 54 (3/4)
Jocelyn 65 (3/4)
Warmington 147-8 (1/7)
Ribbeck3 132-3 (1/4)
Vahlen2145-6 (2/7)


TRF: tacere opino esse optumum et pro viribus / sapere, atque fabulari tute noveris
Jocelyn: tacere opino esse optumum et pro viribus / sapere, atque fabulari tute noveris
Warmington: tacere opino esse optumum et pro viribus / sapere atque fabulari tute noveris.
Ribbeck3: ita sapere opino esse optumum, ut pro viribus / tacere, atque fabulari tute noveris
Vahlen2: tacere opino esse optumum et pro viribus / sapere atque fabulari tute noveris
doc.apparatus:
tacere] om. Lugd. Bamb. : tacere rel. : sapere Bothe (tacere Bothe ad initium secundi versi transposuit) : ⟨ita⟩ sapere Ribbeck

opino esse] opinio esse L1, corr. L2 : opino esse rel. : esse opinor Bothe

optumum] optutum G : optumum rel

et] et codd. : ut Scaliger : at Fuchs : aut Lange : del. "vir summus" (apud Vossium)

sapere] ni sapere "vir summus" (apud Vossium) : tacere Bothe (sapere Bothe ad initium prioris versi transposuit)

atque] adque L1, corr. L2 : atque rel. : ac Bothe

fabulari] afabulari H1, corr. H2 : fabulari rel.

tute] tate Lugd. Bamb. : tute rel. : tu te Mercier

noveris] noveris codd. : noverint Scaliger

The omission of tacere in one branch of the family is part of a wider error where the text of this fragment of Ennius' Eumenides has been elided with the entry for Pacuvius' Chryses, this was because both quotations apparently had the sequence opino esse, and the eyes of the scribe skipped forward, missing the rest of the Pacuvian quotation, the citation of Ennius' Eumenides and the first word of the Ennian quotation, tacere. As for tate in the same branch, in Carolingian miniscule u and a have similar shapes. (Mercier's tu te [bib-GA28MGTP-Mercier-1614-475] has nothing to recommend it.)

The awkward logic produced by the coordinating conjunctions et and atque has resulted in suggestions to emend et: Scaliger's ut, which has been accepted by quite a few editors, turns the second half of the line into a result clause. Scaliger suggested [bib-4GWN6MHR-Scaliger-1565-134] separating out the first four words of the first line into a separate colon, and taking pro viribus / sapere with atque fabulari tute noverint (note also Scaliger's emendations of noveris to noverint), which would translate to "I think that staying quiet is best, so that they will know how to be wise according to their strength and to speak safely"): see further the commentary and reconstruction. Fuchs' at [bib-ZSQCXRZE-Fuchs-1935-245] intervenes in the manuscript to supply the contrastive element that some editors have detected between the first half of the fragment and the second half. Still, this still feels somewhat lacking as an explanation of the change in person. Lange's aut [bib-5JT22X3U-Lange-1851-18] is an attempt aiming at something similar. He states that Ennius' words are praising two different kinds of person. However, his suggestion still, in my view, makes even less sense of the change of person.

A "summus vir" is reported in Vossius' commentary [bib-3UVK8KHG-Vossius-1620-24] as suggesting the deletion of et in the first line and inserting ni at the very beginning of the second. ni would be an alternative form of nisi: 'I think the best thing is to keep silent, unless you know how to be wise with your strength and to speak safely'. This makes good sense, but we would expect ni to precede pro viribus, and there is obviously no manuscript support for inserting ni.

Bothe thought [bib-JKFKJ86G-Bothe-1823-41] that the transmitted reading resulted in a 'foolish statement' (sententia inepta), remarking that the statement that wisdom was a feature of the silent person, rather than silence a feature of the wise person, was easily disproved 'when we often observe very stupid people who are quiet' (cum passim videamus stultissimos). His suggestion [bib-JKFKJ86G-Bothe-1823-42] was to switch the positions of tacere and sapere. Since these infinitives, belonging to different conjugations, scan differently, this necessitated further surgery to the line: Bothe suggested emending opino esse to opinor esse, following the "summus vir" of Vossius' commentary in deleting et, and changing atque to ac. While deleting et is not unattractive (see below), changing opino esse into esse opinor seems doubtful when the Nonian lemma in which this quotation appears is devoted to precisely the use of opino for deponent opinor. The scribal slip between the quotation from Pacuvius' Chryses and this quotation from Ennius' Eumenides is also more difficult to explain if the order of opino and esse is inverted. The only situation in which Bothe's hypothesis works is that the line was already corrupted in Nonius' source, but since opino is by far the lectio difficilior, it does not seem hugely likely that the text became corrupted from opinor to opino before Nonius' time.

Ribbeck saw merit in Bothe's efforts and combined them with Scaliger's emendation of ut for et, adding ita so that no change to the transmitted reading opino esse would be necessary. Ribbeck's insertion of ita at the start of the line also serves to signpost the result clause. However, this does produce two resolutions in the first foot, which is unusual. However, Ribbeck's suggestion could well be one of the most attractive from the perspective of making sense of the syntax. However, the inversion of tacere and sapere and the loss of ita are not well-motivated: it is unclear how the paradosis could have changed so dramatically from something with good sense to something making much less sense.

Overall, it is probably the suggested emendations of the early modern scholars Scaliger (...ut...noverint) and Vossius' "vir summus" (...ni...) which are the most commendable in restoring good sense with the least drastic intervention.

.
doc.commentary:
tacere...optumum] The first line of the fragment is dense with elision. The first four words are all elided, but this is not uncommon with Ennius' senarii. For comparison we can take a line from the opening of Ennius' Medea, TRF frg. 89, line 9, where the first three words are elided: Mede(a), anim(o) aegr(o), amore saevo saucia.

optumum] A common form of optimus in pre-Classical Latin, and still common in Classical Latin (cf. TLL bonus).

et...atque] The conjunctions in these lines are a little odd, if we try to match them to a hypothetical sense. et... atque as correlative conjunctions is, according to Pinkster, 'practically unattested' [bib-YEB7RMDT-Pinkster-2015-646]. He cites one example from Varro: ibi tum et pabulum multum, quod in campis aret, ac cultura arborum aptior... (de Re Rustica 1.6.5. Far more likely for this Ennian fragment is that et is a conjunction coordinating the clauses tacere...optumum and pro viribus... noveris, and atque coordinates constituents of the second clause, namely the infinitives sapere and fabulari which precede and follow atque. Thus both et and atque would translate as 'and', but they would be performing syntactically distinct roles. However, editors have offered other interpretations: Jocelyn translates atque as 'but', paraphrasing the two lines as: 'as a rule I think it the best policy to keep one's mouth shut and to show only such verbal cleverness as one's physical strength allows; but (atque) in some circumstances one may know how (noueris) to speak without risk'. Firstly, there is elision between sapere and atque, which would probably deaden the impact of a strong sense break here.
