There is a clear caesura in the fourth foot, which would seem to balance the two halves of the line if atque is coordinating, not adversative: sapere atque fabulari || tute noveris. Moreover, while atque can have an adversative force, I cannot find any good examples of this occurring after et. et... ac/atque and atque/ac... et sequences always seem to be conjunctive: there are three examples of this pattern in Ennius' poetry (Annals fr. 468 Skutsch, et detondit agros laetos atque oppida cepit; TrRF II Telamo fr. 120, nam ita mihi Telamonis patri atque ⟨Ae⟩aci et proavi Iovis; TrRF II inc. fr. 206, ...aerem, qui ventus est et nubes, imber postea, atque ex imbre frigus, ventus post fit, aer denuo...). In these instances, et and atque are used as copulative conjunctives. When atque to be used in a different capacity, et does not seem to be present. Cf. TLL atque II B 2 and III B, where I can see no examples of et appearing when atque is used with an adversative sense. To look at another roughly contemporary author, there are several examples from Lucilius of atque...et: one from his fable of the Lion and the Fox, quid sibi vult, quare fit ut intro vorsus et ad te | spectent atque ferant vestigia se omnia prorsus? (1119-20W = 988-9M); one from his account of the trial of Q. Mucius Scaevola, quae ego nunc Aemilio prae- | canto atque exigo et excanto (78– 9W = 62– 3M). In both instances et and atque seem to have identical meaning and the choice seems metrically and phonically driven (cf. also Ter. Adelph. 590: nam iam abibo atque unum quicquid, quod quidem erit bellissumum, | carpam et cyathos sorbilans paulatim hunc producam diem). But with TrRF frg. 53, some kind of contrastive sense is required for the switch in person from opino to noueris , and an adversative is an obvious way to achieve this. Fuchs suggested reading at for et. But if it is decided that et requires emending, Scaliger's ut seems preferable, providing a stronger logical link between the first and second half of the fragment, explaining the logic of the switch in person and rendering atque in its expected role of binding two members of the same class (the infinitives sapere and fabulari).

noveris] nosco is much more likely to appear as a perfect than as present, and when it does appear as a perfect it usually has a present meaning, analogous to scio [bib-CTITQK8V-Torrego-2019-31-34].
doc.reconstruction:
The chief difficulty with this fragment is how the clauses should be construed together, and who the first person and the second person voice might be. There have also been repeated attempts to connect this fragment with passages of Aeschylus' Eumenides, but that there has been little consensus about this suggests at least some change by Ennius needs to be acknowledged.

Scaliger was the first [bib-4GWN6MHR-Scaliger-1565-134] to offer a parallel for these lines, suggesting Aesc. Eum. 571-3, σιγᾶν ἀρήγει καὶ μαθεῖν θεσμοὺς ἐμοὺς | πόλιν τε πᾶσαν εἰς τὸν αἰανῆ χρόνον | καὶ τούσδ᾽ ὅπως ἂν εὖ καταγνωσθῇ δίκη ('it is good to keep silent and to learn my rules, that goes both for the city for all time, and for these parties, so that the case may be judged well'). While tacere corresponds well with σιγᾶν, so that the polite request for silence is found in both, fabulari tute noveris is quite different in expression from ἂν εὖ καταγνωσθῇ δίκη. Aeschylus' Athena's instructions to both the Furies, the plaintiffs, and Orestes, the defendant would have then, on this account, become instructions directed only at Orestes. Hence Scaliger's emendation of noveris to noverint would absolutely be necessary if this is to be remotely close to Aeschylus' passage. But the middle clause (pro viribus / sapere) is even more of a problem. Scaliger probably saw it as paralleling μαθεῖν θεσμοὺς ἐμοὺς, but since it follows ut, according to Scaliger's emendations, it must be dependent on noverint, like fabulari. 'Being wise in one's strength' seems to be also quite different from 'learning precepts'. It is hard to see how it can be taken with optimum esse like tacere, even though this would be equivalent to the syntax of the Aeschylean passage. The upshot is that even when Scaliger's emendations are both accepted, both the acts of knowing and speaking (sapere and fabulari) in the Ennian passage must be attributed to the legal parties, not to the jury. Besides the parallels with the Aeschylean passage being limited, if the context is the law-court it is difficult to see how someone who was asked to be silent at some points would thereby gain wisdom and be speaking 'safely' at another. One expects an instruction to the jury to be quiet, so that the parties can speak properly, and also an appeal to the jury to be wise, but the Ennian passage refers to 'safe' speaking, and pro viribus remains unclear. Both of these seem to anticipate violence and danger and so are at odds with the civil process of the lawcourt.

Ribbeck, following Bothe, paralleled the Ennian lines with a passage of Aeschylus' Eumenides which occurs earlier in the play [bib-XCEJBREP-Ribbeck-1875-146]. Orestes, pursued by the Furies, enters Athena's sanctuary and calls out to her to come. He begins his speech by saying at 273-6: ἐγὼ διδαχθεὶς ἐν κακοῖς ἐπίσταμαι | πολλοὺς καθαρμούς, καὶ λέγειν ὅπου δίκη | σιγᾶν θ᾽ ὁμοίως: ἐν δὲ τῷδε πράγματι | φωνεῖν ἐτάχθην πρὸς σοφοῦ διδασκάλου ('I, having learned amidst evils, am versed in many purificatory rituals, and where it is right to speak and likewise where to be silent: and in this matter I was instructed to speak by a wise teacher'). The σοφοῦ διδασκάλου ('wise teacher') is Apollo: and Orestes is saying that although the Furies pursuing him might accuse him of polluting Athena's shrine and invoking her improperly, but he had been instructed to supplicate the image in her temple by Apollo himself, events staged earlier in the play at Aesch. Eum. 64-93. There are thematic overlaps here, between the concern about knowing how to 'speak safely' in the Ennian fragment and knowing when it is right to be silent and to speak in Aesch. Eum. 274-5. However, all the lines in the Aeschylean passage relate to the first person: there is no suggestion of a dialogue between a first and second person. Of course, one could emend noveris to noverim, which scans identically, although this never seems to have been suggested. The tone, however, is different between the two passages no matter what emendations are made: the emphasis in the Aeschylean passage is that Orestes has been taught something in the past (by Apollo), wisdom which he draws on in his present situation. In the Ennian passage, the speaker is wanting to impart knowledge, to be the teacher, not to indicate that they have been a student.

Fuchs suggests [bib-ZSQCXRZE-Fuchs-1935-245] that Ennius is elaborating on the Aeschylean Apollo's speech at 64ff., in particular in his instructions to Orestes about how to supplicate the image of Athena in her temple in Athens (line 79-80). There Aeschylus' Apollo tells Orestes merely to sit in the sanctuary and take the image in his arms. When Orestes subsequently does this (273ff.), he is at pains in his speech to pre-empt any concerns by Athena about his impurity, elements that were absent from Apollo's speech. Ennius may well, as a reader of Aeschylus, think that Apollo must have also advised Orestes about this matter. At any rate Ennius may well have thought that the plot would evince more logic if Apollo did advise Orestes about these matters.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
opino, pro opinor. Plautus Bacchidibus: Praenestinum opino esse: ita erat gloriosus. Pacuvius Chryse: inveni, opino, esse optumum Orestes uter esset tamen. Ennius Eumenidibus: tacere opino esse optumum, et pro viribus sapere atque fabulari tute noveris. Caecilius Asoto: nil fore opino inter me atque illum.

Nonius cites opino as an example of an active form used instead of the deponent form standard in Classical Latin.

lines.text: tacere opino esse optumum et pro viribus
sapere, atque fabulari tute noveris

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: (that) the yield of the crops can neither grow nor become ripe
doc.differences:
Schierl: 114 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 27/31

D’Anna: 183 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 27/31

Klotz: 142 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 20/33

Ribbeck3: 142 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 20/32

Warmington: 137 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 15/33

Most editors place this fragment around the middle of the play. Only D’Anna and Schierl place it near the end. D’Anna’s fetus would make an accusative-and-infinitive construction difficult.
doc.apparatus: fetum] fetus coni. D’Anna (1967)
doc.commentary:
grandiri] This verb, which means ‘to grow’, is a preclassical deadjectival verb from grandis. Later, grandescere, an inchoative verb, is used. In its active form it appears in Cato (Agr. 141. 2), and, as Nonius notes, in Varro (frg. Non. p. 115, 1), Plautus (Aul. 49), Lucretius (1. 191, 2. 1160), and Accius (trag. 440 Ribb.3). This is the only attestation of this word in the passive voice.

mitiscere] This inchoative verb is also attested with this spelling in Accius (trag. 684 Ribb.3), but is usually spelled mitescere.
doc.reconstruction: The context of this fragment is uncertain. According to D’Anna (1967, 207), two contexts are possible. The first possible explanation for this fragment is that it is a ruinous image as presented in an oracle. This would then be the latent message of the responsa that is explained in F111. The other option is that these words are spoken by Clytaemnestra as she curses Orestes when he carries out his revenge. However, both claims, Schierl notes, are difficult to substantiate.

lines.text: nec grandiri frugum fetum posse nec mitiscere
Quam mihi maxime hic hodie contigerit malum

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: Iove propagatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus,
ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus
Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

doc.translation: Tantalus, so they say, was sired by Jupiter, and from Tantalus was born Pelops, and then from Pelops sprang Atreus, who continues further our family line.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment LIV, v. 101-103 Ribbeck3:

101: Ioue patre prognatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus

102: Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus 

103: Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

Ribbeck1:

Fragment LIV, v. 101-103 Ribbeck1:

101: Ioue propagatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus

102: Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus 

103: Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

Warmington:

Fragment inc. inc. fab. 47-49 Warmington:

47: Iove propagatus est ut perhibent Tantalus,

48: Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus

49: Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

The story goes that Jupiter was sire

Of Tantalus; from Tantalus sprang Pelops;

From Pelops was begotten Atreus,

Who further is the sire of all our line.
doc.apparatus:
1: propagatus ] propagatus codices plerique (Quint.): propugnatum A ante correctionem (Quint.), ‘ut uidetur’ (Ribbeck2, 249): propugnatus ABM (Diomedes): prognatus ς (Diomedes): propagavit Giffen 1565: patre prognatus Ribbeck2, 249: patre gnatus Buecheler apud Ribbeck2, 249

2: autem ] autem Quint.: omisit ABM (Diomedes)

3: porro propagat genus ] porro propagat genus Diomedes, p* (Quint.): porro pagens G (Quint.): propagat genus A post correctionem (Quint.)

In line 1, the main manuscripts of Quintilian read propagatus (‘having been sired’), whereas the main manuscripts of Diomedes have propugnatus (‘having been fought(?)’), which in the early printed editions (referred to by Keil with the siglum ς) was changed to prognatus (lit. ‘born from’, so ‘son’). According to Ribbeck2, 249, the reading before correction of the A manuscript of Quintilian (Milan, Biblioteca Ambrosiana, E 153 sup., f. 160r) seems to have been propugnatum. The correct reading of line 3 is preserved in the manuscripts of Diomedes, as well as in some fifteenth-century manuscripts of Quintilian (Winterbottom’s siglum p* refers to corrections found in Parisinus lat. 7723 and to the readings of Vaticanus lat. 1766).
doc.commentary:
1-3: the account of Tantalid genealogy in this fragment has been thought to be modelled on the opening of Euripides’ Iphigenia in Tauris (1-4): [IPHIGENIA]: Πέλοψ ὁ Ταντάλειος ἐς Πῖσαν μολὼν | θοαῖσιν ἵπποις Οἰνομάου γαμεῖ κόρην, | ἐξ ἧς Ἀτρεὺς ἔβλαστεν· Ἀτρέως δὲ παῖς | Μενέλαος Ἀγαμέμνων τε· τοῦ δ᾿ ἔφυν ἐγώ…; ‘Pelops the son of Tantalus went to Pisa and with his swift horses won as his bride the daughter of Oenomaus. She gave birth to Atreus, whose sons in turn were Menelaus and Agamemnon. It is from this last that I was begotten… (transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, IV.153).

1 propagatus: cf. OLD s.v. propago1 2: ‘to continue (a family or race) by means of procreation’, cf. Lucr. 1.19-20 (of Venus), omnibus incutiens blandum per pectora amorem, | efficis ut cupide generatim saecla propagent, ‘striking alluring love into the breasts of all creatures, you cause them greedily to beget their generations after their kind’ (transl. Rouse–Smith 19922, 5).
doc.reconstruction: Bothe (I.281) suggests that this fragment might belong to Accius’ Atreus, following the suggestion of Scriverius and Burman. The fragment may be connected with Accius F inc. 413 TRF (multi iniqui…). Ribbeck (1875, 52), though, attributes this fragment to Naevius’ Iphigenia; Warmington suggested that this fragment contains the opening lines of a play entitled Iphigenia, perhaps that by Naevius, which was an adaptation of Euripides’ Iphigenia in Tauris. He rules out Ennius’ Iphigenia as a candidate because he believes that play to have been an adaptation of Euripides’ Iphigenia at Aulis.
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarii. Reading the text printed by Ribbeck in his second and third editions (Ioue patre prognatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus), the line begins with a proceleusmatic (i.e. four light syllables in succession).

context.author: Quintilian
context.text:
Quintilian Institutio oratoriae 9.3.55-7:

Gradatio, quae dicitur κλῖμαξ, apertiorem habet artem et magis adfectatam, ideoque esse rarior debet. Est autem ipsa quoque adiectionis: repetit enim quae dicta sunt, et priusquam ad aliud descendat in prioribus resistit. [...] †Est† inuenitur apud poetas quoque, ut apud Homerum de sceptro, quod a Ioue ad Agamemnon usque deducit, et apud nostrum etiam tragicum:

‘Ioue propagatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus,

Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus

Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus’.

Gradation, called in Greek ‘climax’, has a more obvious and conscious art about it, and accordingly should be used less often. It too is a Figure of Addition, since it repeats what has been already said, and pauses on each earlier step before it proceeds to the next. [...] It is to be found in the poets too, as in Homer’s passage about the sceptre, which he traces from Jupiter down to Agamemnon, and in our own tragic poet:

From Jove, or so they say, came Tantalus,

from Tantalus sprang Pelops, and from Pelops

Atreus, who fathered all our family.

(Text: Winterbottom 1970; transl. Russell 2001, IV.133).

Quintilian quotes this fragment as an example of the rhetorical figure gradatio (Greek κλῖμαξ).

context.author: Diomedes
context.text:
Diomedes Ars grammatica 1.448.25-448.29 GLK:

Invenitur [sc. gradatio] quoque apud poetas, ut apud Homerum in sceptro, quod Iove ad Agamemnon usque deducit; apud nostrum etiam tragicum sic, 

Ioue propagatus est, ut perhibent, Tantalus,

Ex Tantalo ortus Pelops, ex Pelope autem satus

Atreus, qui nostrum porro propagat genus.

Gradation is to be found in the poets too, as in Homer’s passage about the sceptre, which he traces from Jupiter down to Agamemnon, and in our own tragic poet: 

From Jove, or so they say, came Tantalus,

from Tantalus sprang Pelops, and from Pelops

Atreus, who fathered all our family.

In addition to being quoted by Quintilian (Inst. Orat. 9.3.55-7), the fragment is also quoted by the late fourth or early fifth-century grammarian Diomedes.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: I seek him, who helps
doc.differences:
Shierl: 108 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 22/32)

Warmington: 156 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 28/34)

D’ Anna: 174 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 21/31)

Ribbeck2 157 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 30/32)

Ribbeck3: 157 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 30/32)

Schierl and D’ Anna place this fragment a little earlier than Warmington and Ribbeck, but it is safe to assume the fragment should be placed around the act of revenge. Schierl places the fragment after fr. 107 (nunc ne illum exspectes…) as a final search for an ally, after the one who was expected in the previous fragment did not come in help. Warmington and D’ Anna place fr. 107 immediately after 108, for it is perceived as an answer to quaero.
doc.apparatus: adiutatur] adiutetur dub. Müller in app.
doc.commentary: adiutatur] Müller proposed adiutetur. The subjunctive indicates here a final clause.
doc.reconstruction: The only thing sure about this fragment is that somebody needs help. It is speculated that it is one of Orestes’ victims, namely Aegisthus, Cletaemnestra or even Oeax; so either Aegisthus is searching for Oeax, or Clytaemnestra for Aegisthus. But it could also be Orestes who is looking for Pylades’ help, especially if we consider the emendation adjutetur by Μüller, “so that he helps”. The addressee remains unknown in both cases, perhaps a servus, or a member of the chorus (if we assume one.)

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
This line is quoted by Nonius Marcellus in his dictionary De compediosa doctrina to signify that the form adiutatur can be used for the form adiutat.

adiutatur, pro adiutat. Pacuvius Duloreste:

illum quaero qui adiutatur

Non. 477,27 Merc.=766 Li.

lines.text: illum quaero qui adiutatur
Scibam hanc mihi supremam lucem et serviti finem dari

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: <20> ni[hi]lne te
hominum fortunae, ni[hi]l commiserescit meae?
finge advenam esse: ni[hi]l fraterni nominis
sollemne auxilium et nomen pietatis movet?

doc.translation: Does the fortune of (the) men, my own fortune, not make you take pity? Imagine that I am a stranger: does the solemn help of the word ‘brother’ and of the word ‘piety’ not move you?
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment inc. inc. CXLVI, 245-248:

245: nilne te

246: Horum fortunae, nil commiserescit meae?

247: Finge aduenam esse: nil fraterni nominis

248: Sollemne auxilium et numen Pietatis mouet?

N.B.: this fragment does not appear in Ribbeck’s first edition (1852). It is included for the first time in his second edition (1871, 273) as F inc. inc. CXLVI, v. 245-8. It should be noted that in the first edition, the fragment corresponding to ‘F inc. inc. CXLVI, v. 245’ is a different one, i.e. Plaudite! (= Quinst. Inst. Orat. 6.1.52).

Warmington:

Fragment inc. inc. 111-13:

110: Te nihil

111: hominum fortunae, nihil commiserescit meae?

112: Finge advenam esse; nihil fraterni nominis

113: sollemne auxilium et nomen Pietatis movet?

Have you no pity for the fortune of men, have you no pity for mine? Suppose I am a newcomer. Are you not moved at all by aid due in the name of brother (or: does nought of brother’s name…stir wonted help), by the name of Piety?
doc.apparatus:
1: ni[hi]lne ] ni[hi]lne Ribbeck2, 272: nihil codd.

1-2: ni[hi]lne te | hominum ] ni[hi]lne te | hominum TRF I2.295: nilne te | horum Ribbeck2, 272: nilne hominum te Haupt apud GLK I.610: nil omnium | tete L. Mueller 1894, 61

2: fortuna<e> ] fortuna<e> corr. Keil(?): fortuna codd.

2: me<ae> ] me<ae> Keil, GLK 1.280: me codd.

2: commiserescit ] commiserescit codd.: miseret Haupt apud GLK I.610

1-2: ni[hi]lne te | hominum fortuna<e>, ni[hi]lne commiserescit me<ae> ] ni[hi]lne te | hominum fortuna<e>, ni[hi]lne commiserescit me<ae> TRF I2.295: nihil hominum te horum, nil commiserescit mei? Buecheler apud Ribbeck2, 272: nihil hominum te fortuna<e>, nihil commiserescit meae? codd.

2-3: nil commiserescit meae? | Finge aduenam esse: nil fraterni nominis… ] nil commiserescit meae? | Finge aduenam esse: nil fraterni nominis… TRF I2.295: nihil commiserescit meae? finge advenam esse: nihil fraterni nominis Keil, I.280 GLK: commiserescit me fingi aduenam, te nihil codd..

4: sollemne auxilium ] sollemne auxilium Keil, I.280 GLK: solenne auxilium codd., (‘in codice post sollem. nihil deesse uidebatur’, Keil I.280 GLK).

4: nomen ] nomen codd.: numen Haupt apud GLK I.610

The paradosis reads (in Barwick’s text): nihil hominum te fortuna<e>, nihil commiserescit meae? finge advenam esse: nihil fraterni nominis sollemne auxilium et nomen pietatis movet. Barwick’s text is the one adopted by the TRF, though they present it in iambic senarii (ia6), whereas Barwick prints the fragment in prose.

Haupt suggests that the fragment should be printed as follows (see ‘Citation context’):

nil hominum te fortunae, nil miseret meae?

finge advenam esse: nil fraterni nominis 

sollemne auxilium et numen Pietatis movet?

Do you have no pity for the fortune of men, for my own? Imagine that I am a stranger: does the solemn aid of the name of ‘brother’ and of the name of ‘piety’ not move you?

With Bücheler’s conjecture, the text reads:

nihil hominum te horum, nihil commiserescit mei?

finge aduenam esse…

Do you have no pity for these men, for me? Imagine that I am a stranger…

Bücheler’s proposal is supposedly designed to turn the first line of the fragment into a full iambic senarius. It also reads mei instead of meae, which was suggested by Keil instead of the manuscripts’ me on the grounds that commiseresco usually seems to be construed with a direct object in the genitive. Bücheler’s proposal seems, however, to involve the removal of fortunae, and it would be difficult to explain how fortunae made its way into the text of the fragment.

The text of the fragment differs between the first edition of volume 1 of the TRF (published in 2012) and the second edition of that volume (published in 2023). The text in volume 1 reads (p. 281):

1: [...] nilne te

2: hominum fortunae, nil commiserescit meae?

3: finge advenam esse: nil fraterni nominis

4: sollemne auxilium et nomen pietatis movet?

The text in volume 2 reads (p. 295):

1: ni[hi]lne te

2: hominum fortuna<e>, ni[hi]l commiserescit me<ae>?

3: finge advenam esse: ni[hi]l fraterni nominis

4: sollemne auxilium et nomen pietatis movet?
doc.commentary:
2: commiserescit: a verb attested only in pre-classical Latin. Haupt’s suggestion (apud GLK I.610) to remove commiserescit by means of conjectural emendation and to replace it with miseret is unappealing.

4: pietatis: the TRF edition of Accius decides not to capitalise pietas so as to make it a personification, unlike some earlier editions, including Ribbeck’s. The parallel at Sen. Thy. 248-50 is instructive in so far as both non-personified pietas and personified Pietas both seem to feature in the exchange between Atreus and the attendant (satelles): [SATELLES]: nulla te pietas mouet? | [ATREUS]: Excede, Pietas, si modo in nostra domo | umquam fuisti, ‘[SATELLES]: Does no affection move you? [ATREUS]: Begone, Affection, if you ever existed at all in our house!’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.253).
doc.reconstruction: It is attributed to Atreus’ Atreus by Ribbeck 1875, 452, identifying Thyestes as the speaker (and to the ‘Thyestes’ by Ernout 19473, 223, who almost certainly means the Atreus, since Accius, so far as we know, did not write a Thyestes).
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarii.

context.author: Charisius
context.text:
Charisius, Gramm. 4, p. 369.9-12 Barwick = I.280.13-16 GLK: aliis ita placuit zeugma definire, [...]. plures sententiae uno verbo cluduntur. 

‘Some prefer to define the zeugma as follows: [...]. Several sentences are completed with one single verb.’

This adespoton is quoted as an example of what, according to Charisius, some grammarians consider to be an illustration of the stylistic figure of zeugma, in so far as all the questions addressed by the speaker are governed by the verb mouet, in final position. It was first identified as a tragic fragment by M. Haupt in a personal communication to Keil, the editor of Priscian in Grammatici Latini (I.610 GLK). Haupt suggests that the fragment should be printed as follows (see ‘Apparatus criticus’):

nil hominum te fortunae, nil miseret meae?

finge advenam esse: nil fraterni nominis 

sollemne auxilium et numen Pietatis movet?

lines.text: Cur me miseram inridet, magnis compotem et multis malis?

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation:
Why don’t you endure that my majesty intrusively demands

to obey me, why don‘t you lead this woman here from the temple?
doc.differences:
TRF (1/8)
Warmington (7/8)
Ribbeck (8/8)
doc.commentary: ⟨mihi⟩: mihi is added by a large numbers of the editors, given that the verb pareo, according to Non. 34-35 L. with the sense “to obey” here, requires a dative as an object. While Schauer and Lindsay (in his edition of Nonius) place it after vos, the rest of the editors, including Ribbeck and Warmington, place it before vos. Since the metre remains unaffected in both cases, the first choice seems more appropriate; it creates the stylistic effect of the alliteration, which in turn emphasizes the demand of compliance with the authority of the speaker. Bothe and Klussmann suggest maiestas proact, which seems to be less suitable.
doc.reconstruction:
The examination of the text alone can lead us to three conclusions. The male or female character that speaks the words presents him- or herself as a figure of authority (maiestas mea). He or she demands a group of people (vos) to obey and lead a woman, seemingly on stage (hanc), away from a temple. The upset tone of the speaker along with the emphasis on his or her authority indicate that the addressees previously had shown resistance.

Taking into account the title of the play, Aegisthus, and the relevant mythic tradition in Homer, Aeschylus, Sophocles and Seneca, the most reasonable assumption would be to think of Electra or Cassandra as the woman to be drawn away from the temple, and of Clytaemnestra or Aegisthus giving the order. Considering this situation “– it seems pretty clear that the situation is settled somewhere around the death of Agamemnon – be it shortly after Agamemnon has turned home together with his concubine Cassandra, or: after his death and before the murder will be revenged by Electra and/or Orestes.” (Wessels 2019).

Scholars such as Warmington, Spaltenstein and Aricó suggest that Aegisthus is referring to Electra, based on the parallel of Seneca’s Agamemnon (951; 997-1000), where Electra has taken refuge at the altar and Aegisthus orders the slaves (famuli, 997) to carry her away in exile and imprison her. On the other hand, Bothe proposes that these words, referring to Electra, are spoken by Clytaemnestra. It is not that such words per se would not suit the queen because of the more traditional female role that she assumes in both Aeschylus and Seneca, as Spaltenstein (2008, 66) argues. In fact, in Aeschylus she is the active shaper of the play and she is even compared with a man (Aesch. Ag. 1029). Instead, it is the emphasis on the speaker’s authority that renders Aegisthus a more suitable choice. He is the new king who needs, even more as a usurper, to establish his authority, a pattern familiar also from Aeschylus’ Agamemnon, where he also appears as a vindictive tyrant (1434sqq; 1638sqq). The resistance that his addressees had apparently shown might well be explained by this fact, as well as by his order to perform an impious act: the removal of a suppliant from the altar.

Both Electra and Cassandra had found refuge at the altar in Seneca (and Cassandra alone in Aeschylus). Both are to be drawn away, the first to be imprisoned, the second to meet Agamemnon’s fate. Both are willing to leave the altar and die. As Sanford (1923, 284) points out “[…] we get no real help in interpreting Livius’ lines”. Whether hanc refers to Cassandra or Electra is quite unclear, but one indication that we could use in favor of the latter could be the mention of the natae, the daughters in Livius’ fragment 4, which if preceding, could explain Electra’s presence on stage (Spaltenstein 2008, 65).

Bibliography

Aricó, G. 1979. Sull’ Aegisthus di Livio Andronico, in Studi di poesia latina, stud. coll. in hon. A. Traglia, vol. 1, Rom, 3-9.
Blänsdorf, J. (ed). 1995. Fragmenta poetarum latinorum epicorum et lyricorum praeter Ennium. Stuttgardiae.
Bothe, F. H. (ed). 1823. Poetae scenic Latinorum. Halberstadt.
Delrius, M. A. 1953. Opinationes in Tragicorum fragmenta, in Syntagma tragoediae, 161-88. Antwerpen.
Diehl, E. (ed). 1911. Poetarum Romanorum veterum reliquiae selectae. Bonn.
Klussmaan, E. C. Chr. 1848. Zu Livius Andronicus (Aegisth. 2 u. 7. Ed. Duentzer, Varro Bimarc. 14, Tanaquil. 1 ed. Oehler). NJPhP 8, 529-560.
Lindsay, W. M. 1922. Early Latin Verse. Oxford.
Pius, G. B. (ed). 1511. Que hoc libro continentur ... Nonii Marcelli ... Ad filium De verborum proprietate Compendium, Milan.
Ribbeck, O. 1985, Die römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig
Sanford, E. M. 1922/23. The Tragedies of Livius Andronicus. The Classical Journal, Vol. 18, No. 5, 274-285.
Schauer, M. (ed). 2012. Tragicorum Romanorum Fragmenta Vol. 1: Livius Andronicus.Naevius.Tragici Minores. Fragmenta Adespota. Göttingen.
Spaltenstein, F. 2008. Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Livius Andronicus. Bruxelles.
Traglia, A. (ed). 1986. Poeti latini archaici. Volume primo. Livio Andronico, Nevio, Ennio . Turin.
Warmington, E. H. (ed). 1936-1940. Remains of Old Latin, 4 voll. London.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
procacitas a procando vel poscendo: unde et proci dicti sunt matrimoniorum petitores. M. Tullius de Republica lib. IV (6): 'a procando, id est poscendo, procacitas nominata est'. Terentius Hecyra (159):
maligna multo et magi' procax facta ilico est. [p. 35] Livius Aegistho (13 [R.]):
quin, quod parere vos mihi maiestas mea
procat, toleratis temploque hanc deducitis?

lines.text: quin, quod parere vos ⟨mihi⟩ maiestas mea
procat, toleratis temploque hanc deducitis
Flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere
cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet?

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: She betrothed her daughter, she provided this day for the wedding.
doc.differences:
Schierl: 89 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 3/32)

D’anna: 148 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 3/31)

Warmington: 119 (Pacuvius’ Thraldorestes 1/34)

Ribbeck2: 12 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 2/32)

Schierl, Warmington, D’anna and Ribbeck all assign this fragment to the beginning of the play. Furthermore, all authors place it right before or right after fragment 88 (Schierl), which also concerns a wedding. While Schierl, D’anna and Ribbeck all place it after fragment 88, Warmington places it before fragment 88.
doc.apparatus: gratam] gratam Bamb
doc.commentary:
despondit] The verb despondere is used in this particular context to designate someone betrothing someone else to a person, instead of the more general meaning “to promise”. Schierl states that normally the pater familias would betroth his daughter to someone, but since Agamemnon is dead, Clytaemnestra now assumes that role.

nuptiis] This word is a plurale tantum, more literally referring to “nuptials”.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker of this fragment is unclear. Petra Schierl makes no claims about the speaker’s identity, but does give an indication of the placement of this fragment within the play: “Der Sprecher informiert im Prolog oder in einem Gespräch zu Beginn der Tragödie über die für diesen Tag geplannte Hochzeitsfeier.” Warmington, too, places this fragment under the prologue of the play. Electra’s forced marriage is also an important theme in Euripides’ Electra, where her new husband is the speaker in the prologue. Because it would not be illogical for Euripides’ Electra to have influenced Pacuvius’ Doulorestes, the placement of this fragment in the prologue makes sense.

Schierl mentions that this fragment could refer to one of two potential betrothals: Clytaemnestra betrothing Electra to a man (Oeax), or Aigisthos betrothing Erigone, his daughter with Clytaemnestra, to someone. Because Electra is also forced to marry someone in Euripides’ Electra, and because this marriage seems to be important in Pacuvius’ play - there are other fragments about this betrothal as well - while Erigone is not a major character in most versions of the Electra/Orestes story, in my opinion the first option would be the most sensible one, This would mean that Clytaemnestra is the subject of this fragment, and that gnatam refers to Electra.

Bibliography

D’Anna, I. (1967). M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta. Leipzig.

Schierl, P. (2006). Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin; New York.

Warmington, E. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted only once by Nonius in his De Compendiosa Doctrina.

Nonius quotes this fragment in the context of the word diem, which, as Nonius explains, is normally used in the feminine gender as “time”, whereas the masculine gender usually means “day”. However, this is not the case in this fragment from Pacuvius’ Dulorestes.

diem' volunt, cum feminino genere dicimus, tempus significare; masculino diem ipsum. nos contra invenimus. Turpilius ... Pacuvius Duloreste: — Vergilius ... . (Non. 839 L. (522,2 M.))

lines.text: Gnatam despondit, nuptiis hanc dat diem.
sed sonitus auris meas pedum pulsu increpat.
? Oderint dum metuant !
tela, famuli, tela propere ⟨ferte⟩: sequitur me Thoas!

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus
doc.translation: Others report the following: Sterope and Atlas bore as children Oenomaus and Maia; Oenomaus had Hippodamia as a daughter, from whom Atreus was born. But the son of Maia was Mercury, ancestor of the Arcadians, one of whom is Evander. Accius relates this in more detail in the Atreus.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 1 (1/20), no line number attributed: 

Servius Dan. in Aen. VIII 130 ‘alii ita tradunt Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomani Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert.’

Ribbeck2:

Fragment 1a (1/20), no line number attributed:

Schol. ad Ouidii Ibin u. 427 p. 468 Merk. ‘Thiestes cum uxore fratris sui Atrei concubuit et genuit ex ea filios: quos Atreus dedit ipsi Thiesti ad comedendum: quod scelus sol uidens retro fugit. ut dicit Statius in tragoediis et Ouidiis Metamor.’ Pro eo quod est Statius legendum esse Attius conieci, si modo fides habenda nebuloni.

Fragment 1b (1/20), no line number attributed:

Seruius in Aen. VIII 130: ‘alii ita tradunt Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomani Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert’.

Warmington:

Warmington prints the scholion to Ov. Ibis 427 and DS’s scholion on Aen. 8.130 in his brief introduction to Accius’ Atreus but does not present these scholia as fragments.

Franchella:

Fragment 1a (1/22), 344:

Scholia, ad Ouidii Ibin 429, p. 468 Merkel: ‘Thiestes cum uxore fratris sui Atrei concubuit et genuit ex ea filios: quos Atreus dedit ipsi Thiesti ad comedendum: quod scelus sol uidens retro fugit. ut dicit Statius in tragoediis et Ouidiis Metamor.’ Pro eo quod est Statius legendum esse Attius conieci, si modo fides habenda nebuloni. [Ribb. et Warm.].

Fragment 1b (1/22), 345:

Servius, in Aen. 8.130: ‘alii ita tradunt: Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomani Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander; quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert.’

Not in D’Antò.

Dangel:

Fragment 1 (1/21), no line number attributed:

Serv. Auct. ad Verg. Aen., 8,130 : Alii ita tradunt : Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomai Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus ; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert.

Serv. Auct. ad Verg. Aen., 8,130 : Voici ce que d’autres rapportent : Sténopé et Atlas eurent deux enfants, Oenomaus et Maia ; Oenomaus eut une fille, Hippodamie, dont naquit Atrée ; Maia de son côté eut un fils, Mercure, ancêtre des Arcadiens et parmi eux d’Évandre, ce qu’Accius relate assez abondamment dans Atrée.
doc.apparatus:
at Maiae filius ] at Maiae filius D (= Parisinus lat. 7965, Ferrara 1469): aut Maiae filius F (‘Floriacensis’) 

The critical apparatus is based on the edition of Book 8 of Servius’ commentary on the Aeneid by Ramires–Lafond (2022).
doc.commentary:
We owe the preservation of this testimonium or fragment to a scholion by Servius Auctus (DS) on Verg. Aen. 8.130. It is worth quoting extensively from the relevant passage of Vergil in full (Evander’s speech to Aeneas, 8.134-42):

‘Dardanus, Iliacae primus pater urbis et auctor,

Electra, ut Grai perhibent, Atlantide cretus,

aduehitur Teucros; Electram maximus Atlas

edidit, aetherios umero qui sustinet orbes.

uobis Mercurius pater est, quem candida Maia

Cyllenae gelido conceptum uertice fudit;

at Maiam, auditis si quicquam credimus, Atlas,

idem Atlas generat caeli qui sidera tollit.

sic genus amborum scindit se sanguine ab uno.’

Dardanus, the first founder and father of the city of Troy, sailed to our Teucrian land, and that same Electra was the daughter of Atlas, the mighty Atlas who carries the circle of the heavens on his shoulder. On your side you are the son of Mercury and he was the son of Maia, conceived and born on the snow-clad top of Mount Cyellene. But the father of Maia, if we put any trust in what we hear, was Atlas, that same Atlas who supports the starts of the sky. And so we are of one blood, two branches of the same family. (Transl. D. West 20032) According to the genealogy outlined by Vergil’s Evander, Arcadians, Pelopids, and Trojans are all related in so far as they share a common ancestor in the Titan Atlas. According to Servius Auctus, part of this genealogy was found in Accius’ Atreus, though Accius does not seem to have mentioned the Trojans, apparently having focused on the kinship between Pelopids and Arcadians (though Lana 1959, 297 argues that F inc. 437 TRF, which he suggests is related to this testimonium/fragment, suggests that Atreus did in fact mention the genealogy of the Trojans in the genealogical connection between Pelopids and Arcadians). Accius makes Atlas the father of Oenomaus, and not Ares, who according to practically all sources is the father, although Tzetzes (ad Lycoph. Alex. 149, 219) makes Hyperochus the father.
doc.reconstruction:
The TRF edition innovates by considering this a testimonium relating to Accius rather than a fragment of Accius’ Atreus. Earlier editors almost always assigned this testimonium/fragment to the prologue of the play. Dangel seems to go so far as to suggest (1995, 62; 276) that it was an ‘Accian habit’ to begin a play with a genealogy. Dangel writes (1995, 276): ‘Cette généalogie [...] diffère de la tradition courante. Celle-ci donne en effet Arès et non Atlas comme père d’Oenomaus [...]. On notera que cette version rare permet à Accius de relier le mythe à la préhistoire de l’Italie. Rappelons en effet que les Arcadiens et Évandre, affiliés ici au géant Atlas, durent s’exiler d’Arcadie et parvinrent sur la rive gauche du Tibre. Ainsi, avant la fondation de Rome par Romulus, Évandre fonda Pallantée sur le Palatin, tandis que sa mère est honorée à Rome prophétesse sous le nom de Carmenta [...]. Cette reconstruction légendaire conduit alors à relier l’histoire de Rome à celle des Atrides, de sorte qu’avec l’arrivée sur le sol italique des descendants d’Atlas, ont pu s’introduire conjointement l’arrogance, la sauvagerie et la démesure des Géants, qu’illustrent toutes les tragédies acciennes consacrées aux descendants d’Atlas par Hippodamie (cf. supra, Intr., p. 41 et 46)’. The role of Mercury in this genealogy is interesting: according to Accius, as son of Maia and grandson of Atlas, Mercury is the maternal uncle of Atreus and Thyestes, because their mother Hippodamia is also a daughter of Maia. What role, if any, did Mercury have in Accius’ play? According to one version of the myth, Mercury played a role in the conflict between the two brothers (Σ Eur. Or. 812.04 Mastronarde [thom. exeg.]):

Πέλοπος Ἀτρεὺς καὶ Θυέστης ἦσαν υἱοί. Ἀτρεὺς δὲ τὸν ἀμφιμήτριον ἀδελφὸν αὐτῶν Χρύσιππον ἀγαπώμενον ἀμέτρως ὑπὸ τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτῶν φθονήσας εἰς φρέαρ ἀποπνίγει. ὃ μαθὼν Πέλοψ ἐξώρισεν αὐτόν. ὕστερον δὲ μετὰ θάνατον Πέλοπος ἐπανῆλθε, καὶ ἑκάτερος αὐτῶν τῆς βασιλείας ἀντεποιεῖτο, Ἀτρεὺς μὲν, ὡς πρῶτος ὢν κατὰ νόμους, Θυέστης δὲ τοῖς νόμοις ἐναντιούμενος. συνέθεντο οὖν ὡς, εἴ τις αὐτῶν δείξει τι τέρας, κύριος ἔσεσθαι τῆς ἀρχῆς. ἐν τοῖς ποιμνίοις δὲ τοῦ Ἀτρέως εὕρηται χρυσοῦν ἀρνίον μήνιδι Ἑρμοῦ, ὅτι τὸν τούτου υἱὸν Μυρτίλον εἰς Γεραιστὸν ἀκρωτήριον Εὐβοίας κατεθαλάττωσε Πέλοψ. καὶ μέλλοντος Ἀτρέως δεῖξαι τὸ τέρας τοῖς δικασταῖς καὶ λαβεῖν τὴν ἀρχὴν, Ἀερόπη ἡ τούτου γυνὴ, μοιχευομένη Θυέστῃ τῷ ἀνδραδέλφῳ, κλέψασα τοῦτο δέδωκεν αὐτῷ. Θυέστης δὲ λαβὼν τοῦτο καὶ δείξας τοῖς δικασταῖς τῆς ἀρχῆς ἐκράτησε.  μὴ ἀνασχόμενος οὖν Ἀτρεὺς τὴν συμφορὰν καὶ δυσχεραίνων ὅτι ἀδίκως ἐστέρηται τῆς ἀρχῆς, ὁμοῦ τε τὴν γυναῖκα Ἀερόπην τιμωρεῖται κατ’ ἄμφω,  καὶ ὅτι ἐμοιχᾶτο Θυέστῃ καὶ ὅτι κέκλοφε τὸ ἀρνίον καὶ δέδωκεν αὐτῷ, ῥίψας αὐτὴν εἰς θάλατταν, ὥς φησι Σοφοκλῆς [Aj. 1297], καὶ τοὺς τρεῖς υἱοὺς τοῦ Θυέστου, Ἀγλαὸν, Ὀρχομενὸν καὶ Κάλεον κτείνας παρέθηκεν εἰς τράπεζαν τῷ πατρὶ, καὶ αὐτὸν ὕστερον ἀπέκτεινε. δι’ ἃ ἥλιος μὴ στέρξας τὸ παράνομον μίαν ἡμέραν ἐκ δυσμῶν πρὸς ἕω διφρεύει. σὺν αὐτῷ δὲ καὶ αἱ Πλειάδες τὴν ἐναντίαν ὁδὸν ἐβάδισαν.

Atreus and Thyestes were sons of Pelops. Atreus, because of jealousy of their half-brother from a different mother, Chrysippus, who was immoderately doted on by their father, drowns him in a well. When Pelops learned of this, he exiled him. He returned from exile later after the death of Pelops, and each of them laid claim to the kingship, Atreus as being first (in line) according to laws/customs, but Thyestes acting against laws/customs. So they made a compact that if any of them will display some portent, he will be empowered with the rule. In the flocks of Atreus was found a golden lamb because of the wrath of Hermes caused by the fact that Pelops had thrown into the sea at Geraestus, a promontory of Euboea, the son of this (god), Myrtilus. And when Atreus was on the point of displaying the portent to the judges and acquiring the rule, Aerope, his wife, who was engaging in adultery with her brother-in-law Thyestes, stole it and gave it to him. Thyestes, taking this and displaying it to the judges, got control of the rule. Not enduring this misfortune and full of resentment at having been unjustly deprived of the rule, Atreus both avenges himself on Aerope for both things—both because she was committing adultery with Thyestes and because she had stolen the lamb and given it to him—by casting her into the sea, as Sophocles says, and after killing the three sons of Thyestes, Aglaus, Orchomenus, and Kaleus, set them before his father for dinner and later killed Thyestes himself. Because of these things the sun, not tolerating the transgressive act, rides his chariot for one day from the west to the east, and together with him the Pleiades too traveled their course in reverse.

According to this version, Hermes/Mercury, the father of Myrtilus, the charioteer who was killed by Pelops, seeks to avenge the death of his son by punishing the sons of Pelops, Atreus and Thyestes. Atreus and Thyestes having agreed that whichever of them received a portent from the gods would be the one to rule, Mercury causes a golden lamb to appear amidst the flock of Atreus so as to generate strife between the two brothers. Might this version of the myth featured in Accius’ play? Baldarelli (2004, 153-4) further points out that, in the genealogy adopted by Accius, Mercury’s son Myrtilus is an ancestor of Evander and the Arcadians, cf. the words of Aeneas to Evander at Verg. Aen. 8.138 uobis Mercurius pater est (might Accius have emphasised this point in the Atreus?). She speaks of Mercury as having a key position (‘Schüsselstellung’) in Accius’ genealogy in so far as he is an ‘arch-enemy’ (‘Erzfeind’) of the Pelopids on account of Pelops’ murder of Myrtilus. Pelopids and Arcadians are thus divided by this crime, the consequences of which are still felt by the Romans. Baldarelli argues that this genealogy also makes the Trojan War into a civil war between Dardanids and Atrids (Aeneas being related to Atrids through shared ancestry going back to Atlas and Electra).

Cf. too Σ Eur. Or. 812.01 Mastronarde (rec. exeg.), according to which Mercury is sent by Zeus as a messenger to Atreus to inform him that he is favoured for the kingship by Zeus.

Ἀτρεὺς ὁ Πέλοπος βασιλεύων Πελοποννήσου ηὔξατο ποτὲ ὅ τι ἂν κάλλιστον ἐν τοῖς ποιμνίοις αὐτοῦ γεννηθῇ, τοῦτο Ἀρτέμιδι θῦσαι. γεννηηθείσης δὲ αὐτῷ χρυσᾶς ἀρνὸς τῆς εὐχῆς ἠμέλησε καὶ καθείρξας εἰς λάρνακα ἐφύλαττε. μέγα δέ τι φρονήσας ἐπὶ τῷ κτήματι κομπάζει ἐπὶ τῆς ἀγορᾶς. ἠνίασε τοῦτο Θυέστην, καὶ ὑπῆλθεν ὡς ἐρῶν πεῖσαι τὴν Ἀερόπην δοῦναι αὐτῷ τὸ κτῆμα, λαβὼν δὲ ἀντεῖπε τῷ ἀδελφῷ ὡς οὐ δεόντως κομπάζοντι. ἔλεγε δὲ αὐτὸς ἐν τῷ πλήθει δεῖν καὶ τὴν βασιλείαν ἔχειν τὸν τὴν χρυσᾶν ἄρνα ἔχοντα. τοῦτο δὲ ὁμολογοῦντος καὶ τοῦ Ἀτρέως, Ζεὺς Ἑρμῆν πέμπει ὅτι μέλλει ποιεῖσθαι τὴν ἐναντίαν ὁδὸν ὁ ἥλιος. συνθεμένου δὲ περὶ τούτου ὁ ἥλιος τὴν δύσιν εἰς ἀνατολὰς ἐποιήσατο• ὅθεν ἐκμαρτυρήσαντος τοῦ δαιμονίου τὴν πλεονεξίαν Θυέστου, τὴν βασιλείαν Ἀτρεὺς παρέλαβε καὶ Θυέστην ἐφυγάδευσεν.

Atreus the son of Pelops, being king of the Peloponnesus, once made a vow to sacrifice to Artemis whatever finest (animal) may be born in his flocks. When a golden lamb was born for him, he neglected his vow and kept it, having confined it in a chest. Having become really proud of this possession he brags in the marketplace. This pained Thyestes, and he beguiled Aerope as if in love with her, to persuade her to give him the possession. When he got it, he spoke against his brother, saying he was boasting improperly. And he himself said among the populace that the one who has the golden lamb should also have the kingship. When Atreus too agreed with this, Zeus sends Hermes to Atreus, telling him to make an agreement (with Thyestes) concerning the kingship, and he reveals (his plan) about the sunrise, that the sun is going to make the opposite journey. After he (Atreus) made the agreement about this, the sun made its setting to the east. And when divine power had borne witness to the greedy usurpation of Thyestes, Atreus recovered the kingship and send Thyestes into exile. (Transl. Mastronarde)

Franchella (1968, 223), who prints Servius’ scholion as F 1b of his edition of Accius’ Atreus, connects Servius’ testimony to a scholion on Ovid’s Ibis (= F 1a Franchella) which, he alleges, reports that Accius in the Atreus told the story of the Sun’s reversal in horror at the events that occurred at the banquet. The text of the scholion is as follows (Schol. Ov. Ibis 429, II.112 La Penna = p. 468 Merkel): Thyestes cum uxore fratris sui Atrei concubuit et genuit ex ea filios, quos Atreus dedit ipsi Thyesti ad comedendum. Quod scelus Sol videns retro fugit, ut dicit Seneca in tragoediis et Ovidius Metamor. Merkel, in his 1837 edition of the scholia to Ovid’s Ibis, had printed ‘Statius in tragoediis’ instead of ‘Seneca in tragoediis’. La Penna points out that the manuscript reading is in fact ‘Seneca’, not ‘Statius’. The manuscript to which La Penna refers is manuscript D (= Staatsbibliothek zu Berlin, ‘Askewianus’, MS Diez. B Sant. 21), which his critical apparatus suggests is the only witness to this part of the scholion (I conclude this on the basis that manuscript D was not used by Ellis in his edition of the scholia, and his text of the scholion (p. 76) does not include the information that concerns us). Unfortunately, this manuscript, which was collated in full by Merkel in his 1837 edition (cf. p. 408), was destroyed during the Second World War (La Penna 1957-1959, II.xi). La Penna says that he briefly looked at the manuscript himself and concluded that ‘la trascrizione del Merkel, salvo qualche dettaglio insignificante, è esatta’, although clearly was an instance in which Merkel reported the manuscript reading inaccurately, because La Penna (II.112) clearly reports the reading of manuscript D as ‘Seneca’ and not ‘Statius’, noting ‘erravit Merkel’ (cf., for a full explanation, La Penna 1974, 304). Franchella (1968, 223) hesitantly conjectures ‘Accius’, and prints this fragment as ‘F 1a’, connecting it to the information about the account by Accius in the Atreus of Pelopid/Arcadian/Trojan genealogy reported in Servius’ note on Aen. 8.130 (= F 1b Franchella = T 25 TRF). In 1974, La Penna called for this fragment (i.e. F 1a Franchella = schol. Ov. Ibis 429) not to be included in any future edition of Accius (p. 304). See further F 111 TRF, ‘Fragment reconstruction’.
doc.metrical_analysis: No metrical issues to report (the testimonium/fragment is in prose).

context.author: Servius Auctus
context.text:
This testimonium or fragment is preserved in a scholion by Servius Auctus on Verg. Aen. 8.130 (quodque a stirpe fores geminis coniunctus Atridis, '...and that you were linked by blood with the twin sons of Atreus'. I quote the full contents of the scholion:

Servius Auctus (DS) Verg. Aen. 8.130, II.218.21-219.6 Thilo–Hagen: GEMINIS CONIVNCTVS ATRIDIS: geminos hic duos, aliter ‘gemini’ uno partu editi. quaeritur sane unde Euander Atridis genere fuerit coniunctus. et quamvis Hesiodus dicat qualiter coniunctus sit Euander, tamen quidam aiunt Thestii filias Ledam et Hypermestram fuisse, Ledae et Tyndarei filias Clytemestram Helenam et Timandram fuisse, quam duxit uxorem Echemus Arcas, cuius filius Euander: Clytemestram et Helenam notum est Agamemnoni et Menelao iunctas fuisse. alii ita tradunt: Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomai Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert. alii ita narrant: Atlas complures habuit filias, et de Dione (Tantalus) Pelopem genuit, is Atreum, Atreus Agamemnonem et Menelaum; Mercurius deinde est Nicostratae pater, quae fuit mater Euandri; Mercurium Maia filia Atlantis genuit. sic Euander Atridis coniunctus est. Troianis autem sic iungitur: ex Electra, Atlantis filia, et Iove Dardanus nascitur, eius filius Erichtonius, ex eo Assaracus, ex illo Capys, ex illo Anchises, ex illo Aeneas.

GEMINIS CONIVNCTVS ATRIDIS: here, geminos means 'two'; elsewhere, gemini means 'born in the same labour'. One inquires, in any case, in what way Evander was tied in family relations with the Atrids. And although Hesiod does not say in what way, some nevertheless say that Leda and Hypermestra were the daughters of Thestius and Clytemnestra, Helen, and Timandra those of Leda and Tyndarus. Timandra married the Arcadian Echemus, whose son is Evander. It is a known fact that Clytemnestra and Helen were married to Agamemnon and Menelaus. Others say the following: Stenope an Atlas had as children Oenomaus and Maia; Oenomaus had a daughter, Hippodamia, to whom Atreus was born. And Maia had as a son Mercury, from whom are descended the Arcadians among whom is Evander: this is what Accius reports in a more developed way in the Atreus. Other still say this: that Atlas had multiple daughters and, with Dione, Tantalus gave birth to Pelops, and Pelops to Atreus, Agamemnon, and Menelaus. Mercury then is the father of Nicostrata, mother of Evander. Maia, daughter of Atlas, gave birth to Mercury. And so Evander was joined in family ties with the Atrids. Moreover, he is joined with the Trojans in the following way: from Electra, daughter of Atlas, and Jupiter is born Dardanus, whose son is Erichthonius, who engenders Assaracus, who engenders Capys, who engenders Anchises, who engenders Aeneas. (Cf. too the text and translation of Ramires–Lafond 2022, 45-6).

lines.text: quaeritur sane unde Euander Atridis genere fuerit coniunctus… alii ita tradunt: Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomai Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert.
Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser
manderem natos ! x – x – x – x – u x
Ecquis hoc animaduertet ? Vincite ! – x – u x
......... melius quam viri
Callent mulieres

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus
paretur aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili
poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?

doc.translation: What limit can be set to slaughter among the descendants of Tantalus, or what sufficient penalty can ever be imposed so as to pay the price for the death of Myrtilus?
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment inc. fab. VII, v. 657-659:

657: Quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus

658: Paretur? aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili

659: Poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?

Warmington:

Fragment inc. fab. 19-21:

19: Quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus

20: paretur, aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili

21: poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?

What limit, pray, could be devised to stay

Tantalus’ grandsons from a general slaughter?

Or what sufficiency of punishment

Shall e’er pay penalty for Myrtilus’ death?

Franchella:

Fragment inc. fab. VII, v. 658-600:

658: quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus

659: paretur, aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili

660: poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?

Quale limite si potrebbe imporre alla strage dei nepoti di Tantalo oppure quale sufficiente possibilità di supplizio sarà mai data per scontare la pena per la morte di Mirtilo?

D’Antò:

Fragment inc. fab. VII, v. 657-659:

657: quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus

658: paretur aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili

659: poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?

quale limite sarà posto alla strage dei Tantalidi, oppure quale sazietà di supplizi sarà mai data per scontare le pene per la morte di Mirtilo?

Dangel:

Fragment Oenomaus 11 (11/11), v. 20-22 and inc. fab. II.

20: Quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus

21: paretur aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili

22: poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici ?

Quelle limite enfin mettre à la saga meurtrière des Tantalides ? Ou quel supplice sera donc jamais à la mesure du châtiment à payer pour la mort de Myrtile ?
doc.apparatus:
3: satias ] satias A ante correctionem, B:  satietas A post correctionem, ‘Leid. C’, ‘Erl.’

3: supplici ] supplici P (Nat. D.): suplicii AVB (Nat. D.)

The reading of manuscripts A and B (Leiden, VLF 84 and 86 respectively) is satias (corrected in A to satietas). The manuscript referred to by Ribbeck as ‘Leid. C’ is BPL 118 (f. 52r). The manuscript referred to by Ribbeck seems to be ‘Erlangensis 38’, s. XV, Moser’s M, Baiter’s E. Satias (on which see ‘Fragment commentary’) is needed for metrical reasons. The contracted form of the genitive in -ī (on which see Weiss 20202, 240), supplici, is required for metrical reasons.
doc.commentary:
1 quinam: the forms of the interrogative pronoun quinam and quisnam seem to be used interchangeably by Republican poets (cf. e.g. Plaut. Bacch. 234 quinam exit foras? ‘Who is going outside’; Plaut. Epidicus 244 quisnam is est? ‘Who is this?’).

1 internecioni: ‘total destruction of life, extermination, slaughter, massacre’ (OLD s.v. 1), a word first attested either in Accius or in Lucilius (depending on the relative dating). Cf. Accius Meleager F 264 TRF eumpsum uitae finem ac fati internecionem fore | Meleagro, ubi torrus esset interfectus flammeus, ‘that, whenever the flaming firebrand would die, this would be for Meleager the end of his life and the extermination of his fate’; Lucilius 101 Marx: illud ad incita cum redit atque internecionem, ‘whenever it goes back to square one, and to slaughter’.

1: quinam…modus: Baldarelli (2004, 170-1) compares several instances in Seneca’s tragedies, especially the Thyestes, where the limit (modus) of appropriate violence or punishment is said to be surpassed, e.g. Sen. Thy. 26-27 nec sit irarum modus | pudorue ‘may there be no limit to anger nor to shame’; 255 nil quod doloris capiat assueti modum ‘Nothing conforming to the limits of ordinary bitterness’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.253); 1051-3 [THYESTES]: sceleris est aliquis modus! | [ATREUS]: Sceleri modus debetur ubi facias scelus, | non ubi reponas, ‘[THYESTES]: There is some limit to crime. [ATREUS]: Crime is owed some limit when you commit crime, not when you repay it’, (transl. Fitch 20182, II.321). Cf. too Med. 397 [MEDEA]: Si quaeris odio, misera, quem statuas modum, | imitare amorem, ‘(to herself) If you wonder, poor wretch, what limit to put on your hatred, copy your love’ (transl. Fitch 20182, I.351). Cf. too satias, below.

2: mortem Myrtili: the presence of the charioteer Myrtilus, son of Hermes, in the myth of Pelops goes back at least to Pherecydes of Athens (F 37ab BNJ2 = 3 F 37ab FGrHist = Σ Ap. Arg. 1.752-58a, p. 64.11-65.7 Wendel; Σ L+ Soph. El. 504). We do not know what role, if any, he played in the plays entitled Oenomaus by Sophocles (F 471-477, IV.380-385 TrGF) and Euripides (F 571-577, V.591-595 TrGF), but Myrtilus is mentioned in Sophocles’ Electra (505-513, thrown from his chariot headlong into the sea), as well as in Seneca’s Thyestes (139-43, where the story of how Myrtilus gave his name to the Myrtoan Sea is described as notior | nulla est Ioniis fabula nauibus, 142-3, ‘no tale is better known on Ionian ships’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.243).

3 satias: feminine noun satias, -atis ‘an abundance, plenty, sufficiency’ (‘perh. syncopation of satietas’ according to OLD s.v. satias). With the exception of Lucr. 5.39 (satiatem) and 2.1038, 5.1391 (satiate), it is attested  only in the nominative singular: cf. also Accius Astyanax F TRF 86 quorum crudelitatem numquam ulla explet sati[et]as sanguinis, ‘whose cruelty no abundance of blood ever fills up’, where, however, although OLD and TRF both print satias, the manuscript variant satietas might be correct. Like she does with regard to modus (above), Baldarelli (2004, 172) compares several references to the idea of ‘satiety’ (especially of crime and of punishment) in Seneca’s tragedies, e.g. Sen. Thy. 105-6 [FURY]: actum est abunde. gradere ad infernos specus | amnemque notum…, ‘It is done, and amply! Go to the infernal caverns and the river you know’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.241), cf. 889 [ATREUS]: bene est, abunde est, iam sat est etiam mihi. | sed cur satis sit?, ‘This is good, this is ample, this is enough now, even for me. But why should it be enough?’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.307); the chorus’ hopeful wish at Thy. 136-139 tandem lassa feros exuat impetus | sicci progenies impia Tantali. |  peccatum satis est! fas valuit nihil | aut commune nefas, ‘Spent at last, parched Tantalus’ unnatural clan must shed its savage aggression. Enough of iniquity! Right achieved nothing – nor collective wrong’ (transl. Fitch 20182, 243).
doc.reconstruction: Bothe (I.229) attributes this fragment to Accius’ Pelopidae, as F 4. Ribbeck in his third edition (p. 192) suggests that this fragment, which corresponds to Accius inc. fab. VII, v. 657-659 Ribbeck3, might belong to the Atreus. Dangel attributes this fragment to Accius’ Oenomaus (F 11 (11/11), 20-23), presumably on account of the presence of Myrtilus in that play(?), but also includes it among the fragments of uncertain play (incerta) as inc. fab. II, thus acknowledging the possibility that it might also have belonged to Atreus.
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarii.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De Natura Deorum 3.90:

‘Non animadvertunt’ inquit ‘omnia di, ne reges quidem’. Quid est simile; reges enim si scientes praetermittunt, magna culpa est; at deo ne excusatio quidem est inscientiae. Quem vos praeclare defenditis, cum dicitis eam vim deorum esse, ut etiam si quis morte poenas sceleris effugerit expetantur eae poenae a liberis a nepotibus a posteris. O miram aequitatem deorum: ferretne civitas ulla latorem istius modi legis, ut condemnaretur filius aut nepos, si pater aut avus deliquisset?

‘quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus

paretur, aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili

poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?’

Utrum poetae Stoicos depraverint an Stoici poetis dederint auctoritatem non facile dixerim; portenta enim ab utrisque et flagitia dicuntur.

What Balbus claims is that neither gods, nor indeed kings, pay heed to every detail. I fail to see the connection between the two. If kings take no action even in full knowledge, they are seriously culpable, but God cannot even make pretence of ignorance. What a remarkable apology you make for him, when you state that even if someone escapes punishment for a crime by death, the divine power ensures that such punishment is exacted from children, grandchildren, or their descendants! What a signal sense of justice the gods manifest! Would any state allow an individual to promulgate a law condemning a son or grandson, if a father or grandfather had transgressed?

What limit, in the house of Tantalus,

To slaughter can be set? Will penalties

Exacted for the death of Myrtilus

Be ever sated by incurring punishment?

Whether poets have corrupted Stoics, or Stoics have lent their authority to poets, I cannot readily say, for both groups recount marvels and monstrous deeds’.

(Text: Plasberg–Ax 19332; transl. Walsh 1997, 143-144).

This fragment is preserved in Cicero’s De Natura Deorum (‘On the Nature of the Gods’), a philosophical treatise in three books written in 45 BCE. The passage quoted above falls in a section in Book 3 in which Gaius Aurelius Cotta (RE ‘Aurelius 96’), the character who speaks in support of the philosophical views of the Academy, responds to the account of Stoic theology that had been given by the character of Quintus Lucilius Balbus (RE ‘Lucilius 20’). At an earlier stage in Cotta’s speech (3.68-69), he had quoted a cluster of lines from Accius’ Atreus (F 104.5-15) to support his argument against the Stoic view that mankind’s possession of reason constitutes proof of the gods’ benevolence. Cotta argues that this is not the case, because reasoning (ratiocinari) can be used to do bad actions. In this passage, Cotta is arguing at length against the Stoic view that God is wholly benevolent towards humans and is not responsible for the existence of vice, but humans alone are. Here Cotta is responding to a specific claim made by Balbus (cf. 2.167) that neither gods nor kings can take notice of each and every human action (though Balbus does not seem to make this point explicitly). Cotta rejects this as implausible, pointing out the thoroughness of divine justice in such circumstances as when a family is affected by a curse across multiple generations. Our tragic fragment, which concerns the hereditary curse that punishes the line of Tantalus, is quoted to illustrate this point.

context.author: Charisius
context.text:
Charisius Ars grammatica 1, p. 115.4-17 Barwick = I.90.30-91.9 GLK:

Quis cum s littera dici volunt grammatici in quaerendo, ut ‘quis te, nate dea, per tanta pericula casus | insequitur?’ (= Verg. Aen. 1.615) et ‘quis procul ille autem ramis insignis olivae?’ (= Verg. Aen. 6.808) in inferendo autem qui sine s, ut ‘ille, vides, pura iuuenis qui nititur hasta’ (= Verg. Aen. 6.760) et ‘Troiae qui primus ab oris’ (= Verg. Aen. 1.1). est enim praepositivus quis, subiunctivum qui; quod tamen auctores non observaverunt, ut Accius dicens ‘quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus sit?’ et Vergilius ‘qui casus agat res’ (= Verg. Aen. 9.723).

Grammarians want quis with the letter ‘s’ to be used in questions, like in ‘what misfortune pursues you, son of a goddess, amidst such perils?’ (= Verg. Aen. 1.615) and ‘but who is that man there, standing apart, crowned with an olive wreath?’ (= Verg. Aen. 6.808). In assertions, however, one ought to use qui without ‘s’, like in ‘there he is, you see, the young man leaning on an untipped spear’ (= Verg. Aen. 6.760) and ‘who first from the shores of Troy’ (= Verg. Aen. 1.1). Indeed, quis is placed before, while qui is placed after. Some authors, however, do not always observe this rule, like Accius when he says ‘what limit is there to the utter destruction of the Tantalids?’ (= Accius F inc. 412 TRF) and Vergil ‘what chance was governing the situation’ (= Verg. Aen. 9.723).

(Text: Barwick; translation after Uría 2009, 151-2).

Charisius quotes only the first line of the fragment, to illustrate a breach of the general rule that quis is an interrogative pronoun and qui a relative pronoun: in this fragment, Accius writes quinam instead of quisnam. On the lack of distinction between quis and qui in early Latin, see Löfstedt 19562, 79-96, esp. 79-80.

lines.text: sed sonitus auris meas pedum pulsu increpat.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia
accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem

doc.translation: May no-one lie down to eat at the table with the tyrant or eat the same meal.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck2:

Fragment 10 (10/20), 217-218:

217: Ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia

218: Accumbat mensam aut eandem uescatur dapem.

Warmington:

Fragment 181-2 (8/17):

181: Nec cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia

182: accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem.

Let none take seat at table with the king

For feasting’s sake, or eat of the same banquet

As he.

Franchella:

Fragment 10 (10/22), 366-367:

365: ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia

366: accumbat mensam aut eandem uescatur dapem.

Nessuno si assida a banchettare col re o mangi il suo stesso cibo.

D’Antò:

Fragment 8 (8/18), 217-218:

217: ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia

218: accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem.

Nessuno col tiranno a mensa si adagi per bachettar, nè dello stesso cibo si nutra!

Dangel: 

Fragment 11 (11/21), 48-49:

48: Ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia

49: accumbat mensam aut eandem uescatur dapem.

Que personne ne prenne place à la table du roi pour partager son repas ou ne consomme le même festin.
doc.apparatus:
1: ne ] ne codd.: nec Warmington II.386

Warmington prints nec instead of the manuscripts’ ne. In prohibitions addressed to no-one in particular, the conjunction ne with present subjunctive is common (when a specific person is addressed the perfect subjunctive is more commonly used). Further negative commands are usually introduced by neue rather than by nec, although nec is sometimes used to join a negative command to a positive command, cf Livy 23.3.3 clausos omnes in curiam accipite; nec quicquam temere egeritis (Woodcock §126-8). Perhaps Warmington imagines the speaker first to have issued a positive command, and the present fragment to constitute a subsequent negative command.
doc.commentary:
1-2: the treacherous feast prepared by Atreus is mentioned in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon (1590-3):

ξένια δὲ τοῦδε δύσθεος πατὴρ

Ἀτρεύς, προθύμως μᾶλλον ἢ φίλως πατρὶ

τὠμῷ, κρεουργὸν ἦμαρ εὐθύμως ἄγειν

δοκῶν, παρέσχε δαῖτα παιδείων κρεῶν.

But Atreus, this man’s impious father, in an act of hospitality to my father that was more eager than it was friendly, pretending to be holding a cheerful day of butchery, served him with a meal of his children’s flesh. (Transl. Sommerstein 2008, II.193).

Ribbeck (1875, 453) identifies a possible allusion to this fragment of Accius’ Atreus in Ovid’s description of Procne’s revenge against her husband, the tyrant Tereus, who raped and cut out the throat of her sister Philomela (Ov. Met. 6.647 his adhibet coniunx ignarum Terea mensis, ‘This is the feast to which the wife invites Tereus’, transl. Miller–Goold 19773, I.333). Ovid’s passage has been shown also to contain allusions to Accius’ Tereus.

1 tyranno: Bilinski (1958, 39 n. 73) suggests that the Latin word is equivalent to the modern meaning of the term ‘tyrant’ and that a political connotation is active, but Lana (1959, 307-8 n. 3) rejects this idea, arguing that the word simply denotes the person who has royal power. Dangel (1995, 279) proposes that, if Atreus is the speaker, then he here presents Thyestes as of equal status to his own, that of tyrannus (though Atreus need not be sincere: he could in fact be sarcastic).

2 accumbat: D’Antò (1980, 286) explains that the verb accumbere, although normally a transitive verb, is in Early Latin construed as transitive with an object in the accusative, cf. Pl. Bacch. 1189 dimidium auri datur: accipias potesque et scortum accumbas, ‘You’re given half the gold; you should take it, drink, and sleep with a prostitute’ (transl. de Melo 2011, I.495); Men. 476 scortum accubui ‘I lay with a prostitute’, 1142.

2 …dapem: Dangel (1995, 279) interprets daps as denoting a sacrifice in which no blood has been shed (‘des offrandes sans effusion de sang’), or as the part of the feast reserved to Jupiter Dapalis. dapalis is a cult epithet of Jupiter (Cato, Agr. 132) used during the feast held in honour of Jupiter Epulo on the occasion of the Ludi Romani (13 September) and the Ludi Plebei (13 November): see BNJ s.v. ‘Epulo [2]’. TLL, however, does not interpret this Accian use of daps in the cultic sense of the word (TLL V.1.36.41-3, cf. V.1.38.4-54, ‘speciatim, terminus sacralis’). In Seneca’s version, the climactic line in which Atreus, after toying at length with Thyestes with a series of double-entendres, finally reveals the truth also ends with the word daps (Sen. Thy. 1034 epulatus ipse es impia natos dape, ‘You yourself banqueted on your sons – a sacrilegious meal’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.319). By no means an unusual word, especially in this context, but the climactic nature of Seneca’s line makes it attractive to imagine that he might be making his own version of the moment of truth even more emphatic with an allusion to Accius’ version of the banquet scene.
doc.reconstruction: Ribbeck (1875, 453) interprets this fragment as Atreus giving the order to prepare the feast for his brother. Lana (1959, 306) suggests that this fragment took place at the end of a scene which depicted the reconciliation of the two brothers, a scene to which no other extant fragments testify, although there is such a scene in Seneca’s Thyestes (508-545). In Accius’ play, according to Lana, Atreus proposes to Thyestes that they share the throne together and, while Thyestes initially refuses, F 110 TRF (ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia | accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem) shows that he eventually accepts, as Atreus invites Thyestes to take the seat of the tyrannus at the banquet table. The scene is followed by a choral ode (F 111 TRF, sed quid? tonitru…).But, as Mugellesi Christillin (1978, 1045-7) points out, it is not so easy to imagine at what stage in the tragedy Atreus might give such an order. She also believes it implausible that the speaker is Atreus himself, on the one hand because she thinks it unlikely that Atreus would refer to Thyestes as a tyrannus, when in fact Thyestes seems to have been represented, at least in some parts of this tragedy, as a miserable exile seeking hospitality. Ruling out that the line could belong to a messenger’s account of the banquet scene (an idea suggested by La Penna 1972, 364 n. 17) in so far as it would be an odd detail to focus on in such a context, Mugellesi Christillin instead suggests (p. 1047) that Thyestes is the speaker. She connects this fragment with F 103 TRF (vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis. | id, quod multi invideant multique expetant, inscitia est | postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas). After warning his children about the dangers of power, Thyestes instructs them not to sit at the banquet table with the tyrannus, Atreus. Mugellesi Christillin explains Thyestes’ insistence on the banquet in F 110 TRF (epulandi; mensam; vescatur dapem), which would seem rather odd if he were, as Mugellesi Christillin imagines, speaking before it even took place, as a stratagem on Accius’ part to increase suspense and create dramatic irony (p. 1049-50). She also identifies a parallel in Seneca’s Thyestes, where Thyestes, speaking before the banquet, sings the praises of a quiet and simple life, away from elaborate banquets (Sen. Thy. 449-52, o quantum bonum est | obstare nulli, capere securas dapes | humi iacentem! scelera non intrant casas, tutusque mensa capitur angusta scyphus, ‘Oh, what a blessing it is to stand in no one’s way, to take carefree meals lying on the ground! Crimes do not enter huts, and one takes a cup safely at a humble table’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.273). Baldarelli (2004, 265) also identifies Thyestes as the speaker, but suggests that he is speaking after the banquet, issuing a warning to others not to share a table with the tyrant Atreus.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius De compendiosa doctrina p. 415.25-28 Mercier = 670.23-27 Lindsay:

VESCI est edere. Vergilius lib. III: ‘vescitur Aeneas simul et Troiana iuventus’ (= Verg. Aen. 3.182) Accius Atreo: ‘ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia | accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem’ (= Accius Atreus F 110 TRF).

This fragment is preserved in Book 4 of Nonius’ De compendiosa doctrina, which is entitled ‘De varia significatione verborum’ (‘On the various meanings of words’). It is quoted as an example of the deponent verb uescor in the sense of ‘to eat’. Nonius goes on to cite instances where the verb has the meaning ‘to make use of, put to use; enjoy’ (OLD s.v. 1). Incidentally, uescor is used in that latter sense elsewhere in Accius (Armorum iudicium F 77 TRF, sed ita Achilli inclutis armis uesci studet, | ut ea cuncta optima leuia prae illis putet, ‘But he strives to use the famous weapons of Achilles to such an extent that he considers all others, though excellent, to be worthless compared to those’). In this Accian fragment, the verb is constructed with the accusative case, but it can also be followed by the accusative (cf. OLD s.v. 2a). Dangel (1995, 279) suggests that, since this fragment is transmitted in Nonius alongside Verg. Aen. 8.182 (uescitur Aeneas simul et Troiana iuuentus, ‘Aeneas and at the same time the Trojan youth feast…’), the context of the two passages might be similar: as in Vergil a feast is organised to mark the beginning of an alliance between Trojans and Arcadians, so in Accius’ Thyestes the feast might have served the purpose of celebrating the reconciliation between the two brothers. According to Lindsay (1901, 78), however, Nonius’ source for this lemma (uesci) might have been a list containing quotations from Book 5 of Lucretius’ De Rerum Natura (though Lindsay expresses his uncertainty by including an interrogation mark). If Lucretius was indeed the leading source, then it is arguably unlikely that the Vergilian and Accian quotations both come from a collection of passages describing banquets organised for the purpose of celebrating alliances or reconciliations, as Dangel seems to suggest.

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarii.

lines.text: Flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere.

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation: But then the playful snub-nosed flock of Nereus, playing to the song, encircled the fleet
doc.differences:
TRF (5/8)
Warmington (2/8)
Ribbeck (2/8)
doc.apparatus:
1Nerei] rei ω ad Non. 233 L. : nereis ω ad Non. 529 : Nerei simum coni. Bentinus 2 lustratur] lustratur codd : lustratur choro suppl. Ribbeck : lustratur vel suppl. Quicherat

In the manuscript tradition for these two different passages of Nonius, the readings rei (233 L.) and nereis (528L.) are found exclusively: Bentinus recognised that these were both misunderstandings of Nerei. The second reading nereis probably arises from diplography with the ‘s’ beginning the following simum.
doc.commentary:
Nerei … pecus] ‘a playful snub-nosed cattle of Nereus’. It seems clear that pecus must refer to animals. Presumably, the animals do not have four legs, since Nonius uses this verse in order to explain that pecus may also refer to animals with any number of feet (or none at all). Because of the association with Nereus, the speaker presumably means sea animals. Therefore, a cattle of animals from the sea with more or less than four legs, encircles a fleet (classem): one might assume that the speaker has been part of the fleet (classem). Animals that are often associated with Nereus are dolphins, cf. Anthologia Lyrica Graeca (Diehl) p. 5-6 φιλόμουσοι | δελφῖνες, ἔναλα θρέμματα | κουρᾶν Νηρεΐδων θεᾶν. A verbal echo of this line can be found back in Pac. frg. 238 (Schierl) Nerei repandirostrum incurvicervicum pecus, which refers to a group of dolphins (Schierl 2006: 492).

simum] Pliny describes the nose of the dolphin as simus,‘snubnosed’, (a feature that, according to Pliny, gives rise to a popular name for the dolphin, cf. Plin. Nat. 9.7.23 pro voce gemitus humano similis, dorsum repandum, rostrum simum: qua de causa nomen simonis omnes miro modo agnoscunt maluntque ita appellari.)

ludens ad cantum] It cannot be surely said who are actually singing. cantum must refer to people on the ship. It is unlikely to be the captive women, because the dolphins are put in a playful mood by the song (ludens ad cantum), which suggests that the song is cheerful. Therefore, it seems most plausible to argue that the Greek sailors on the ships are the ones that are singing (cf. Spaltenstein 2008: 40, ‘Ad cantum paraît certes, à première vue, impliquer le plaisier des marins’). In the Greco-Roman literary tradition dolphins are known as animals sensitive to music, who are influenced by song, for instance, in the story of the poet Arion who is saved by a dolphin (Hdt. 1.23-25). cf. Ael. Anim. 11. 12 Οἱ δελφῖνες, τὸ μὲν φιλόμουσον αὐτῶν καὶ περὶ τὴν ᾠδὴν σπουδαῖόν τε καὶ φιλόπονον κεκηρυκταί τε καὶ ἐς πολλοὺς ἐξεφοίτησε and Plin. Nat. 9.8.24 Delphinus non homini tantum amicum animal verum et musicae arti. They are also sacred to Apollo, the god of poetry (Apollo himself once changed himself into a dolphin, cf. Hymn to Apollo 399-400: αὐτὰρ ὃ τοῖσι συνήντετο Φοῖβος Ἀπόλλων· | ἐν πόντῳ δ᾽ ἐπόρουσε δέμας δελφῖνι ἐοικὼς | νηὶ θοῇ καὶ κεῖτο πέλωρ μέγα τε δεινόν τε).

classem] The word classis implies multiple ships. The group of dolphins in Seneca’s Agamemnon also swim past the fleet, cf. Sen. Ag. 453 anteire naves laetus et rursus sequi.

lustratur] According to Nonius 528 L. lustrari and circumire are synonyms. The same word is used at Sen. Ag. 455 to describe a group of dolphins encircling a ship: millesimam nunc ambit et lustrat ratem.

< v x> The last foot is missing from Nonius’ citation. Different suggestions have been made to supplement the end of line 2 (see apparatus criticus). Whereas other conjectures, such as Quicherat’s citam (which seems at odds with the relaxed mood of the soldiers) or Traglia’s virum (which would, somewhat unnecessarily, specify who was singing), would shift the focus away from the dolphins, Ribbeck (1875: 29) plausibly conjectures choro. The description of the dolphins as a chorus would correspond to the musicality of the dolphins and their playful character (ludens). Additionally, a group of animals may also be generally referred to as a chorus. There are also textual parallels that argue in favour of the reading choro from both earlier and later Greek and Latin literature, cf. Eur. Hel. 1454-1455 καλλιχόρων | δελφίνων and Verg. Aen. 7.391 te lustrare choro. It would also add to the alliteration of cantum classem.
