Finally, one could find it tempting to supplement choro in order to produce an argument for an echo of Livius Andronicus in the scene with the dolphins in Seneca, where Seneca also describes the group of dolphins as a chorus, cf. Sen. Ag. 454 nunc prima tangens rostra lascivit chorus. Other conjectures based on Seneca echoing Livius’ description is Lenchantin de Gubernatis’ freto or salo, but similar to Quicherat’s conjecture, these are quite colourless and add nothing to the scene.

Spaltenstein (2008: 39) argues that the sentence is complete and that the last two syllables could belong to a new sentence. This might find support from Welsh’s finding (2012: 830-834) that Nonius sometimes quotes partial verses ‘when they form part of a quotation that is longer than one verse’ (831) when the quotation forms a complete sentence.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker of this line draws the attention to lascivum Nerei simum pecus, which must be a group of dolphins that is encircling a fleet. The picture of dolphins encircling a fleet seems to imply serenity on sea. The lines are certainly spoken by one of the characters, possibly a messenger (cf. Spaltenstein 2008: 40). The meter, the iambic senarius, suggests that the chorus can be excluded as speaker. The character may be describing a situation they personally experienced, or news they have heard of the fleet. Hypothetically any of the characters who have been informed about the fleet’s return could speak these lines, but if the speech is reflective of other plays featuring the Greek fleet’s return, and especially if Livius’ play is the model of Seneca’s Agamemnon, it is likely to be delivered by a messenger, perhaps the herald Eurybates as in Seneca’s play.

In Sen. Ag. 449-455 we read:

tunc qui iacente reciprocus ludit salo | tumidumque pando transilit dorso mare
Tyrrhenus omni piscis exultat freto
agitatque gyros et comes lateri adnatat,
anteire naves laetus et rursus sequi;
nunc prima tangens rostra lascivit chorus,
millesimam nunc ambit et lustrat ratem.

Seneca seems to echo Livius Andronicus’ fragment in several ways: it echoes single words, such as lustratur (frg. 6, l. 1) / lustrat (Sen. Ag. 455), ludens (frg. 6, l. 2) / ludit (Sen. Ag.. 449) and lascivum (frg. 6, l. 1) / lascivit (Sen. Ag. 454), as well as structural motifs including the playful character of the dolphins, the encircling movements around the ship (Sen. Ag.. 452 agitatque gyros; 455 ambit et lustrat ratem) and the happy mood of the sailors (428 laetum remigem). The question is how probable it is that Seneca has drawn inspiration from Livius Andronicus. Ribbeck (1875: 29) argues that Seneca ‘in dieser Partie den Text des alten Dichters [sc. Liv. Andr.] vor Augen gehabt hat’, but one could also surmise that Seneca is not specifically referencing Livius Andronicus and rather engaging with (e.g epic or dramatic) intertexts, that are now lost: the accompanying of the dolphins was a common topos within the homecoming story of the Greeks.

There seems to be consensus on the placement of this fragment in the Aegisthus. Ribbeck and Warmington place the fragment prior to most of the other fragments (quoting it as the second fragment of the total eight fragments surviving). If Seneca’s play is modeled after Livius’, and Livius’ lines are spoken by a messenger as in Seneca’s play, it is likely they are spoken prior to Agamemnon’s arrival (cf. Sanford 1923: 281).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
pecus non solum quadrupes animal, verum omnia animalia pecudes dicuntur. Plautus Rudente (942):
non vides referre me uvidum rete sine squamoso pecu?
Livius Aegistho (5 [R.]):
tum autem lascivum Nerei simum pecus.
Vergilius Georgicorum lib. IV de apibus (327):
et pecudum custodia sollers. [p. 234] Lucretius lib. II (342):
praeterea genus humanum mutaeque natantes
squamigerae pecudes.
Lucilius Satyrarum || lib. V (42):
lascivire pecus Nerei rostrique repandum.

These lines or a part of these lines were quoted twice by Nonius. In both cases, he explicitly assigns these lines to Livius Andronicus’ Aegisthus. At 233 L., he quotes the first line in order to show that pecus does not necessarily refer to animals with four feet (quadrupes), i.e. domesticated beasts, but also to animals with a different number of feet or no feet at all (such as dolphins in this case; see commentary).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
lustrare est circumire. Vergilius lib. VIII (231):
lustrat Aventini montem.
[p. 529] Livius Aegistho (5 [R.]):
tum autem lascivum Nerei simum pecus
ludens ad cantum classem lustratur ..

At 528-529 L., Nonius quotes both lines to illustrate that lustrare means ‘going around’ (circumire).

lines.text: tum autem lascivum Nerei{s} simum pecus
ludens ad cantum classem lustratur ⟨v-⟩

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: How greatly has bad luck befallen me here today
doc.differences:
Manuwald: 140 (9/10)

Jocelyn: 307 (11/11)

Warmington: 352 (4/14)

Vahlen2: 360 (12/16)

Ribbeck2/3: 308 (10/11)

Ribbeck: ...e qua mihi maxime hodie hic contigerit malum.
doc.apparatus:
quam mihi maxime] quam maxime etsi hic mi hodie coni. Bothe : maximum hodie hic Ribbeck

Bothe aims to transform the line into a good senarius.
doc.commentary:
contigerit malum] Usually contingere connotes a positive event. Yet Nonius’ examples both focus on misfortunes - his other citation to illustrate the definition of contingere as evenire is from Cicero’s Tusculanae Disputationes, where Cicero was talking about the sudden fall from high status into slavery by not only individuals, but also peoples. If this fragment also was about misfortune, as malum might suggest, the speaker is just talking about a terrible misfortune which has very recently occurred to him.

contigerit: The subjunctive form might mean that we have a portion of indirect speech.

quam maxime] This emphatic expression grammatically qualifies contigerit, but it is ambiguous whether it is emphasising the time and location (hic hodie contigerit), in which case it would imply that the speaker expected some evil, but not so soon and in this location, or whether it is emphasising the magnitude of the evil (contigerit malum).

hic hodie] This has a very strong deictic effect: hic is spatial and limits the event under discussion to the immediate vicinity, while hodie limits the timeframe of the event to the same day.

The different conjectures given are to restore different metrical arrangements to the line. There is no actual issue with the text that the manuscripts transmit, but in order to fit more common metres scholars have altered word order and made insertions. The arrangement offered here is a lyric, but with the first person singular voice, the words might fit a monody better than a chorus. It is also worth noting that as regards the content, a monody sung by Thyestes might be a better fit than ascribing the words to the chorus

.
doc.reconstruction:
Scholars have proposed two different contexts based on what they believe the setting of the play must have been: Ribbeck (1875, 203) interprets the fragment (as with Frg. 135) as a line spoken by Thyestes after finding out that Atreus killed his children and served them to him. Ribbeck’s ascription of the verse is based on his interpretation that Ennius’ version of the play only concerns the Thyestean feast (Ribbeck 1875, 199). On the other hand, Jocelyn suggested that the line refers to the rape of Pelopia, that Thyestes indicates that an evil committed by accident (his incest) will have a positive outcome in the fulfilment of Apollo’s oracle and the downfall of Atreus (413-4). Apart from considerations of whether the plot only covered the Thyestean feast, or also events at Sicyon, then the interpretation also comes down to whether there is an oxymoronic tone or not. One difficulty with Jocelyn’s account is that there seem to be different variations of the Sicyon component of the story - in one tradition, which seems to be traceable in Hyginus 87, Thyestes had received Apollo’s oracle, that a son born to him from Pelopia would carry out the revenge on Atreus, before his incestuous rape of Pelopia, and that was a conscious act (quod cum audisset filiam compressit, Hyg. Fab. 87), whereas in Hyginus 88, Thyestes rapes Pelopia not knowing who she is and without hearing an oracle from Apollo - later in this version, he travels to Delphi but there is no mention of whether he received an oracle even then, or the content of that oracle, before being seized by Agamemnon and Menelaus. There is also no need to think there is an oxymoronic quality to combining Fortuna or contigerit a positive quality that is contrasted with malum: Fortuna was frequently an ambiguous figure, if we consider evidence from Plautus or the contemporary inscriptions addressed to Mala Fortuna. See further the discussions in the commentary at the lemma Fortuna for frg. 135.

In Hyginus 88, there is also much more mention of chance and fortune, which might suggest origins in a tragic plot that might relate to Ennius’ play: Pelopia happens to perform her sacrifices into the grove where Thyestes is hiding (ibi casu nocte, 88.3) which leads to them meeting; Agamemnon and Menelaus happen to be consulting Delphi at the same time as Thyestes is, which leads to them capturing him (casu Thyestes eo venerat, 88.8). These elements of chance may suggest that it has its seeds in a tragic plot. There is also the suggestion of a recognition scene in the encounter between Thyestes and Aegisthus. Finally, the words suggest the tragic device of the events of the play’s plot transpiring in one day.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: Non. De compendiosa doctrina (410 Lindsay): contingere, evenire … Ennius Thyeste: quam mihi ... contigerit malum (Lindsay 1903, 410). Nonius lists the verse as an example for the meaning of the word contingere (to befall or to fall to one's lot). As a further example of the occurrence of this meaning, Nonius refers to Cic. Tusc. 5, 15.

lines.text: quam mihi maxime hic hodie contigerit malum
impetrem (fac) facile ab animo ut cernat vitalem
abigeum
Quis homo te exsuperavit usquam gentium impudentia?
Quis autem malitia te?

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation: ⟨He himself⟩ fell hurt and dashed ⟨him⟩self to earth
doc.differences:
TRF (2/8)
Warmington (6/8)
Ribbeck (7/8)
doc.commentary:
ipsus: it is a generally accepted correction of ipse in the manuscripts, initially made by Bothe. The frequent use of the form ipsus instead of ipse in the Roman drama advocates for such a correction, while the phrasing ipsus se is well attested; according to Lindsay (1922, 177) “ipsus […] is normal in Plautus with se, sibi”.

fligit: Although the compounds of fligo are frequent, its simple form is rather rare and, according to TLL 6.1:912, appears only in Livius here, in Accius 21R and 317R and in Varro Men. 85. It can be considered a poetic term only in the classical period, as it disappeared from classical Latin, the same way that linquere gave its place to relinquere (Spaltenstein 2008, 64).
doc.reconstruction:
The majority of the editors, including Warmington, Waszink, Sanford and Spaltenstein, agree that this verse describes Agamemnon’s death, who falls on the ground mortally wounded. Spaltenstein (2008, 64-65) uses two very compelling arguments to support that Agamemnon is the implicit subject of fligit. Firstly, the Homeric echo of the verse due to the violence of the terms fligit and saucius, that aptly relates to a character such Agamemnon. Secondly, the paradox of ipsus se which seems to imply that Agamemnon is entangled in the lethal cloth given to him by Clytaemnestra, as in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon (1382 sqq) and Seneca’s Agamemnon (887sqq). On the contrary, Terzaghi and Aricó suggest that the dying character is Aegisthus. If, however, we take into account the arguments above along with the fact that both, in Aeschylus’ Choephoroi and in Sophocles’ Electra, the murder of Aegisthus is mentioned only marginally, this suggestion seems rather improbable.

A question remains: who is the speaker of these words? Considering that they most likely refer to Agamemnon’s death, it would seem logical to suppose either Cassandra or Clytaemnestra (Spaltenstein 2008, 64). Indeed, in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon it is the queen who describes the killing and the fall of Agamemnon (1380 sqq). By co-examining, though, this verse with fragment 4, where it is most probable that we encounter the murder setting of Agamemnon, it would seem only natural that this fragment serves as the continuation of his demise. In that case, it would be logical to imagine the same speaker describing the death, and the most suitable candidate would be Cassandra (as also in Seneca’s Agamemnon 901sqq).

Bibliography

Aricó, G. 1979. Sull’ Aegisthus di Livio Andronico, in Studi di poesia latina, stud. coll. in hon. A. Traglia, vol. 1, Rom, 3-9.
Bothe, F. H. (ed). 1823. Poetae scenic Latinorum. Halberstadt.
Kirk, E. 1980. Menippean satire: An annotated catalogue of texts and criticism. New York.
Lindsay, W. M. 1922. Early Latin Verse. Oxford.
Ribbeck, O. 1985, Die römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig
Sanford, E. M. 1922/23. The Tragedies of Livius Andronicus. The Classical Journal, Vol. 18, No. 5, 274-285.
Spaltenstein, F. 2008. Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Livius Andronicus. Bruxelles.
Terzaghi, N. 1925. Studi sull’ antica poesia latina. Due tragedie di Livio Andronico, 1, Equos Troianus, 2, Aegisthus. AAT 60, 660-674.
Warmington, E. H. (ed). 1936-1940. Remains of Old Latin, 4 voll. London.
Waszink, J. H. 1972. Zum Anfangsstadium der römischen Literatur. ANRW I 2, 869-927.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
fligi adfligi. Livius Aegistho:
ipsus se in terram saucius fligit cadens.
Accius Epinausimache:
nec perdolescit fligi socios, morte campos contegi?
idem Aegistho:
heu!
cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet!

lines.text: ipsus se in terra saucius fligit cadens
ipse summis saxis fixus asperis, evisceratus,
latere pendens, saxa spargens tabo sanie et sanguine atro.
neque sepulchrum quo recipiat habeat, portum corporis,
ubi remissa humana uita corpus requiescat malis.
Proin demet abs te regimen Argos, dum est potestas consili.
pol mihi fortuna magis nunc defit quam genus.
namque regnum suppetebat mi, ut scias quanto e loco,
quantis opibus, quibus de rebus lapsa fortuna accidat.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: There, he considers what to do, he readies himself, and he weighs it up
doc.differences:
TrRF: 141 (Ennius’ Thyestes 10/10)

Jocelyn: 308 (Ennius’ Thyestes 12/12)

Warmington: 349 (Ennius’ Thyestes 1/14)

Vahlen2: 340 (Ennius’ Thyestes 1/14)

Ribbeck2/3: 297 (Ennius’ Thyestes 3/11)

Ribbeck: Ibi quid agat secum cogitat pensat putat

Warmington: Ibi quid agat secum cogitat curat putat.
doc.apparatus:
cogitat] cogitat P : cogitet L G H

The subjunctive cogitet is clearly wrong, the indicative cogitat is required: it is probably a scribal slip by analogy with agat, with a failure to realise that quid agat is a dependent clause on the asyndetic colon cogitat, parat, putat (or in any case at least cogitat).
Many scholars have offered conjectures for parat, but there is no textual issue here.
doc.commentary:
ibi] ibi is a strongly deictic marker, and it suggests that the speaker shares space with their addressee and is referring to some other space. ibi could divide the space between the speaker and his addressees, on the one hand, and the character described, on the other. One possibility is that this line comes from a narration of off-stage events, and the character described is not on stage. However, another possibility is that the character and the speaker are both on stage physically, but the stage space is itself divided. This could occur like a Plautine aside, so that the speaker addresses an audience - either the real audience or an on-stage internal audience - while the character is onstage, but the character does not overhear the speaker.

parat] Many scholars have thought there is a problem with parat since it violates the Bentley-Luchs rule. This is in fact two complementary laws about the length of unstressed syllable segments in different parts of the line. Luchs’ Law states that if the first half of the fifth metron cannot be short, but must either be a long or resolved into two shorts.

The manuscripts are all unanimous in reading parat. And even then, we should be cautious about applying a metrical law developed to handle Plautus’ metrical practices to Ennius’ fragments. Yet if for the sake of argument we adopt the hypothesis that Luchs’ Law applies to Ennius too, then there have been some conjectures offered in place of parat to consider. Bothe made the conjecture of the iterative paritat, which does occur twice in Plautus, but it is an incredibly rare word. Others have suggested curat which is palaeographically not too distant from parat given that the scribe could easily have jumbled the letters with putat that follows when reading the line, ur and ar appearing quite similar in many miniscule scripts. Both conjectures solve the problem of the violation of Luch’s law, and curat might be preferred because it is at least a common term that fits well with the sense required, of mental activity, whereas parito, which besides being very rare, is less appropriate to thought, but would rather describe the preparation of action. However, this is not a very strong argument.

In fact, if we keep sight of the text, it’s worth exploring the idea that the effect that Luchs’ law is apparently used to avoid might have been intentionally exploited by Ennius for poetological ends. It is hypothesised that the reason for Luchs’ Law is to avoid an iamb in the fifth metron (instead of a spondee or an anapaest) and the resulting impression that the senarius has ended, in a similar way to the fifth metron of the hexameter being a dactyl, rather than a spondee. However, it might be possible that Ennius deliberately wanted to give the initial impression that the line did end with parat, before then revealing to his audience that the character was still involved in mental considerations. To consider this hypothesis, we should consider how Ennius intertwines sense and metre in the first four metrons of the line. In the first and second metrons the focus is on the course of action, ibi quid agat, se-, and two resolutions are found here, moving the line quickly along. In the third and fourth metrons, -cum cogitat, the line slows down with a preponderance of long syllables, suggesting the slowness of the mental deliberation. The course of action envisaged is decisive and rapid, but the mental deliberations around it are careful and weighty. We could apply this link between sense and metre to the fifth and sixth metrons, to explain the apparent violation of Luchs’ law. parat in the fifth metron might suggest to the audience the beginning of action and would make for an appropriate place to end the line, if we think about sense, not metre. Yet their expectations are confounded by putat in the sixth metron where the character is shown to still be mentally deliberating. There might then be an interesting interaction between metre and sense in this line, and potentially humour derived from the deviation from normal metrical practise in the fifth metron, on this hypothesis. Given Ennius’ predilection for playful experimentation with hexameter, could he not have approached the senarius with a similar attitude? (cf. also frg. 138)
doc.reconstruction:
The context of the fragment is obscure. Considering the argumentation about the plot of Thyestes the interpretations of the fragment vary. Therefore, the speaker is unknown and the verse might refer either to Atreus either to Thyestes. Ribbeck excludes the possibility that it might refer to someone else, since, according to him, there is no trace of the presence of Aerope (Atreus’ wife) in the tragedy of Ennius. Some scholars speculate that the verse alludes to Atreus, who, perhaps, was aware that his brother returned and initiated to plan his revenge against him. On the other hand, Bothe claims with certainty that the verse refers to Thyestes, who returns and estimates how to take revenge from Atreus after the cannibalistic feast. Finally, Boyle suggests that since the context is uncertain, the verse can be attributed to either Atreus or Thyestes.

The fragment reminds us of the passage of Plautus’ Miles Gloriosus in which the character Periplectomenos is describing how the slave Palaestrio, the servus callidus and self-titled “architect” of the play (cf. Mil. 901), is developing his plans. The Plautan passage translates into words the bodily display of the thought processes going on in Palaestrio’s mind, for instance Palaestrio slapping his thigh represents the “light-bulb moment” of his idea.

Additionally, Warmington (1935) suggests doubtfully that the fragment might derive from the prologue of the tragedy when Atreus prepares his evils plans of feeding his brother the limbs of his murdered children. However, later scholars did not take into consideration that possibility.

lines.text: ibi quid agat, secum cogitat, parat, putat

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci benivoli

doc.translation: Many people are hostile and unfaithful to royal authority, few are favourable to it.
doc.differences:
TRF: 413 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 651 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 18 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 647 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 651 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 692 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

Many there are unfriendly and unfaithful Unto a kingdom; few who wish it well.

Franchella:

multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli

Molti sono ingiusti e sleali verso il re, pochi benevoli.

D'Antò:

multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci benivoli.

Molti sono ingiusti e non fiduciosi verso il regno, pochi benevoli.

Dangel: La masse est hostile et infidèle à la royauté, le petit nombre bienveillant.
doc.apparatus:
beniuoli] beniuoli ξ (= BPV): boni ζ (= Mc): beneuoli Stuerenburg

pauci beneuoli] pauci beneuoli: p. b. sunt del. Stuerenburg: boni sunt Stephanus 1564, 41, Delrius 1593, I.151: sunt boni Scriverius 152, Bothe ad Acc. inc. fab. fr. 16, I.186

The TRF edition follows Winterbottom (1994, 144) in rejecting Stuerenburg’s correction beneuoli (18432, 170-1) and in printing the manuscripts’ beniuoli. In a treatise on orthography which was transmitted under the name ‘Albinus’, the name used by Alcuin of York, Alcuin explains (VII.298.14-16 GLK) that although beniuolus derives from the adverb bene, it is written with an -i- and not an -e-. Both variants are attested in epigraphic evidence and in medieval manuscripts (cf. TLL II.1897.34-7).

For an account of the manuscript tradition, see Atzert 19634, v-xlvi; Fedeli 1973; and Shackleton Bailey 1993 (more briefly 1994, v-xiii, with a list of sigla).
doc.commentary: beniuoli: in Republican drama, beniuolus/beneuolus ‘kind, benevolent’ seems most often to be used to describe relationships of a personal nature (cf. Plaut. Asin. 66, of a son’s attitude to his father; Ter. Hec. 761, the senex Laches to the meretrix Bacchis). Cicero describes the behaviour of one of his friends’ freedmen, who has been faithful and kind to him, as beneuolis (Fam. 13.21.2); cf. too Mil. 58 beneuolis…seruis. To use the term to describe a subject’s attitude to a kingdom may have been perceived as somewhat unusual.
doc.reconstruction: Ribbeck compares Aesch. Ag. 807-9 (chorus): γνώσῃ δὲ χρόνῳ διαπευθόμενος | τόν τε δικαίως καὶ τὸν ἀκαίρως | πόλιν οἰκουροῦντα πολιτῶν ‘In time you will know by inquiry which of the citizens has acted honestly when staying at home in the city, and which inappropriately’ (tr. Sommerstein). Ribbeck suspects this fragment might either have belonged to Accius’ Atreus, to Pelopidae, or to Clytemnestra (cf. Warmington II.571). Dangel 378 believes that this fragment was from Atreus and is spoken with reference to the eponymous tyrant. Scafoglio (2010, 174) points out that it cannot be ruled out that this fragment could also belong to Accius’ Aegisthus. He suggests that the speaker could be Aegisthus speaking about Agamemnon or Orestes talking about Aegisthus.

context.author: Cicero
context.text: The Accian fragment is preserved in a passage of Book 3 of Cicero’s De Officiis (3.82) in which Cicero is discussing the ideal of the uir bonus, ‘good man’. He presents Pompey and Caesar, without naming them, as men who fell short of attaining that ideal by their disregard for what is morally right in order to obtain power. Cicero translates into Latin a passage of Euripides’ Phoenissae, 524-5, in which tyranny is justified. It is a passage which Caesar apparently often repeated (cf. Suet. Iul. 30.5, cf. Cic. Ex Euripide conuersi loci fr. II Soubiran). Cicero goes on to make the point (3.83) that although the majority of people might consider being a king to be advantageous, in truth there is nothing more disadvantageous, on account of the constant fear and anxiety a king experiences at potentially being the victim of plotting and conspiracy. The Accian quotation is cited to illustrate this point (3.84), and Cicero contrasts the king referred to in the poetic fragment who, as a descendant of Tantalus and Pelops was the rightful heir to the throne, with Caesar, an illegitimate king who with his army seized control of Rome and thus enslaved a state which had been free and powerful.

doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarius (tr7).

lines.text: Mulier una duum uirorum

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: – x – ut, quae tum absentem rebus dubiis coniugem
tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem

doc.translation: ...so that she who retained her husband then, when he was absent and in a critical situation, now betrays the/an/him as an avenger…
doc.differences:
TRF: 140 (Accius' Clytemnestra 3/10)

Ribbeck: 8-9 (Accius' Clytemnestra 8/10)

Warmington: 235-236 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/11)

Franchella: 397-398 (Accius' Clytemnestra 8/10)

D'Antò: 38-39 (Accius' Clytemnestra 8/10)

Dangel: 298-299 (Accius' Clytemnestra 8/10)


Warmington:

As one who held him as her husband then, | When he was absent and affairs were doubtful, | But now betrays the avenger.

Franchella:

⟨-⏑-⟩ut, quae tum absentem rebus dubiis coniugem

tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem?

Come potrebbe adesso tradire il vendicatore colei che allora, in circostanze incerte, tenne fede al marito assente?

D'Antò:

⟨-⏑-⟩ut, quae tum absentem rebus dubiis coniugem

tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem ⟨-⏑-⏑-⏑⏓⟩

Sicché, quella che un tempo tenne fede al marito assente in momenti difficili, ora la tradisca come vendicatore…

Dangel:

en sorte qu’après avoir, dans une situation incertaine, gardé fidélité à son époux absent, elle trahit maintenant le vengeur.
doc.apparatus:
1 absentem rebus dubiis coniugem] absentem r. d. coniugem codd.: absenti in r. d. coniugi Onions

2 prodat] prodat: probat cod. N. Fabri (teste Lindsay in app. crit.)

2 prodat ultorem] prodat ultorem codd.: u. p. uel prodat uictorem uel prodat auctorem Vossius

2 tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem] t. n. p. u. codd.: t. ⟨ei⟩ n. p. uictori ⟨fidem⟩ Onions

Lindsay in his edition of Nonius testifies to the reading probat (instead of prodat) in the manuscript of Nicolas Faber (cf. Mercier's Preface, xvi-xvii), which is now lost. Vossius proposes uictorem for ultorem to mark a stronger antithesis with absentem, but, while this conjectural emendation won the approval of Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xii), it seems completely to disregard the editorial principle of privileging the more difficult reading (i.e. lectio difficilior). Onions prints the lines as follows:

ut quae tum absenti in rebus dubiis coniugi

tetinerit, ⟨ei⟩ nunc prodat uictori ⟨fidem⟩.

His emendation seems designed to solve what he appears to consider a problematic expression, tenere coniugem in the sense of ‘to hold faith/remain faithful to a spouse’. He changes the text so that both verbs tetinerit and prodat are construed with fidem + dative. Accordingly, absentem coniugem is changed to absenti coniugi, which functions in the second part of the tum... nunc... clause as the antecedent to the pronoun ei which is added to the text. The preposition in is added to what was the ablative absolute clause rebus dubiis to account for what would otherwise be a missing syllable. Vossius’ conjecture of uictor for ultor is also adopted (but not credited in the apparatus criticus). In Onions’ text, the fragment is set as two complete iambic senarii. Though ingenious, Onions’ conjecture does not seem entirely satisfactory. While the expression tenere fidem is attested (Cic. Phil. 12.27 non tenuit omnino colloquium illud fidem ‘faith was not kept at that conference’; Prop. 1.18.4 si modo sola queant saxa tenere fidem ‘provided that the lonely rocks can maintain a faithful silence’; Tib. 1.10.19 tunc melius tenuere fidem… ‘back then, a better faith was kept…’), it is not, so far as I can tell, constructed with the dative (i.e. *tenere fidem alicui), but with a preposition (compare, with seruare, Pl. Capt. 930 quoniam tecum seruaui fidem ‘since I kept my promise toward you’; Cic. Vat. 15 seruarisne in eo fidem? ‘will you keep faith in him?’; with retinere, Tac. Hist. 4.21 pro quo fidem et arma...retenturos ‘for whom we shall keep faith and fight…’). Furthermore, there in fact does not appear to be any need for emendation at all, as the verb tenere is attested in the sense required by the paradosis, that is ‘to hold (by ties of allegiance, or sim.; also, by ties of affection’ OLD 22b: cf. Hor. Carm. 4.11.23 iuuenem puella...tenet ‘the girl holds the young man’.
doc.commentary:
ut…: Most scholars take ut as a conjunction introducing a result clause (‘in clauses expressing result and consequence’, Lewis–Short s.v. ut IIC2aβ: cf. Dangel ‘en sorte que...’; Slater 2002, 295 ‘so that she, who held fast her absent spouse when things were doubtful, now betrays the avenger’; D’Antò 510 ‘sicché, quella che un tempo tenne fede al marito assente in momenti difficili, ora lo tradisca come vendicatore...’). Others take ut to introduce an indignant question (cf. OLD s.v. ut 44, Pinkster I.347-8; Lodge II.931-2 for parallels in Plautus, esp. Mil. 1276 ([Pyrgopolinices, the braggart soldier] egone ad illam quae nupta sit? uir eius me ut prehendat? ‘I should go to the place of a woman who’s married? So that her husband should get hold of me?’, tr. De Melo), also in a context of adultery: this seems to be the interpretation of Ribbeck in so far as he punctuates with an interrogation mark in all three editions, and also that of Argenio 1966, 29 (‘come può quella che allor tenne fede, | In dubbi eventi, al suo consorte assente, | Tradire adesso il suo vendicatore?’) and of Franchella 254 (‘come potrebbe adesso tradire il vendicatore colei che allora, in circonstanze incerte, tenne fede al marito assente?’).

The status of ut in such clauses is debated (Pinkster I.348): those scholars quoted above consider ut to be a conjunction introducing an idiomatic subordinate clause; but others consider ut as an indefinite adverb meaning ‘anyhow’, ‘by any means’ and the expression as an independent sentence (K–S II.510, pointing out that in such cases ut is almost never placed at the beginning of the clause; OLD s.v. ut 37b ‘after impers. pass. of vbs. of happening, ensuing, etc.’, citing Pl. Bac. 1069 euenit ut ouans praeda onustus cederem 'it has become my lot to be marching along rejoicing and weighed down with this booty', Val. Max. 8.14.3 quo effectum est ut ne quis dubitaret... 'As a result no one doubted that...'). Warmington’s translation indicates that he takes ut as a relative adverb followed by pronoun qui (OLD s.v. 21b ‘ut qui [w. generic subj.], as is natural for one who, as being one who [= quippe qui]’; Lewis–Short IB4c). But Warmington’s interpretation is erroneous because ut before relative pronoun followed by a verb in the subjunctive introduces a relative clause which stands in a causal relation to the main clause and, moreover, is found mainly in prose from Cicero onwards (Pinkster II.541: the only two examples in Classical Latin are in Ovid, Her. 1.105, cf. Knox ad loc.; Met. 1.605-6). But, in our fragment, the relation between the two clauses is clearly not one of cause (i.e. ‘seeing that she retained her husband then, when he was absent and in a critical situation, she now betrays the avenger’ makes no sense). Taking ut as a conjunction introducing a consecutive clause seems to be the most plausible option. In that case, one must assume that the sentence originally had a protasis which has not been transmitted. Finally, this cannot be an occurrence of the Old Latin phrase ut qui, where qui is an enclitic in the old instrumental case (cf. e.g. Pl. Capt. 243 with Lindsay ad loc. and Lindsay 1907, 119 ‘in utquî, lit. “as how”, the Rel. is in the Instr. case’), because the form of the relative pronoun in our fragment is feminine singular quae.

ultorem: Agamemnon as avenger of wrongs done by Troy.

rebus dubiis: Accius Eurysaces F 206.3-4 = Ennius Andromache 76 Jocelyn, Pl. Epid. 113, Capt. 406, Most. 1041, Sall. Iug. 14.5, Liv. 7.30, 3.29, 25.3.
doc.reconstruction:
Some editors attribute these lines to Clytemnestra. Others (Hermann apud Ribbeck1, xiii; Ribbeck 1875, 462-3; Warmington II.407) believe that Cassandra is the speaker. Others still (e.g. Ribbeck 1875, 462-3; Mette 1964, 146) suggest that an unidentified speaker (a member of the chorus? a concerned citizen?) might be talking about Clytemnestra, perhaps in the prologue (D’Antò 203 proposes that Accius may have adopted a technique sometimes found in the prologues of Euripides, in which events that are about to happen in the play, particularly events that deviate from the traditional version of the myth, are foretold, though he does not cite any specific parallels).

First of all, let us imagine that the speaker is Clytemnestra (Argenio 1966, 30, Franchella 250, Pociña 120). She might:

(i) be responding to an unidentified character (Oeax? Aegisthus?) who has cast aspersions on her faithfulness to her husband;

(ii) be addressing Aegisthus in the context of a dialogue between the two of them (cf. e.g. Sen. Ag. 226-309).

On the latter interpretation, she is trying to allay his fears that she will lack the courage to carry out their plan to murder Agamemnon and abandon it at the last minute. On the contrary, she argues that the constancy she displayed in remaining faithful to her husband proves that she has the resolve to carry through to its completion their plan to murder Agamemnon. This interpretation involves positing that Clytemnestra is speaking in the third person and understanding ultorem as referring to Aegisthus, who is presented as the ‘avenger’ of Clytemnestra’s honour. Though this is not impossible, it is not straightforward that by engaging in an adulterous affair with a woman one plausibly can be said to be avenging her honour. Moreover, it seems more likely that ultorem refers to Agamemnon, who is commonly referred to as ‘the avenger’, i.e. of Greece against the wrongs committed by Troy (e.g. Sen. Ag. 205 ultor Europae ‘the avenger of Europe’). If the speaker is Cassandra, then she may either be:

(i) prophetically foretelling Clytemnestra’s imminent betrayal of Agamemnon (Ribbeck 1875, 462-3; D’Antò 203);

(ii) protesting to Clytemnestra about the plot to murder Agamemnon (Warmington II.407, who connects this fragment to Accius Clytemnestra F 144 TRF = v. 36 Ribbeck3 = 234 Warmington = 295 Dangel, cur me miseram inridet, magnis compotem et multis malis). The latter interpretation would imply that Clytemnestra reveals to Cassandra her plan to murder Agamemnon, and that Cassandra criticizes her betrayal of her husband. It does not seem very likely, however, that Clytemnestra and Cassandra, on stage together without Agamemnon, would discuss the murder plot together. Moreover, this interpretation seems to attribute to Cassandra a favourable attitude towards Agamemnon that is not found in other sources. If we set aside the issue of the identity of the speaker, what conclusions, if any, can we draw from this fragment? It is mainly on the basis of this fragment that the connection between Accius’ play and Hyg. Fab. 117 has been posited (Ribbeck 1875, 462-3; Venini 325). Indeed, the fragment seems to suggest that Clytemnestra remained faithful to Agamemnon for the duration of his ten-year absence (coniugem tetinerit). If this interpretation is correct, then it would correspond to Hyginus’ version which seems to suggest that she associated herself with Aegisthus and betrayed Agamemnon only after falling prey to Oeax’s deceitful manipulation. The character of Clytemnestra was perhaps portrayed by Accius in a slightly more favourable light in comparison with other versions (e.g. Aeschylus) in which she had already engaged in an adulterous relationship with Aegisthus before learning of her husband’s own infidelity, although it may be going too far to suggest that she was partly exonerated (Dangel 322). It is thus tempting, although of course ultimately unprovable, to believe that the character of Oeax, who seems to have played a greater role as a character in post-classical and Hellenistic tragedy (Aricò 2002, 10-11), was in some way involved in this tragedy.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 178.10-15 Mercier = 261.7-13 Lindsay:

tetinerit pro tenuerit. Pacuvius Medo: cedo, quorsum itiner tetinisse aiunt? Accius Clytaemestra: ... ut, quae tum absentem rebus dubiis coniugem | tetinerit, nunc prodat ultorem. Pacuvius Hermiona: sub iudicio quae omnes Graios tetinerim.

Nonius quotes this fragment because it contains the word tetinerit, a perfect subjunctive form of the verb teneo with reduplication of te-, which he explains is the equivalent of tenuerit, the more common form of the perfect subjunctive. The form tetinerim is attested only in these three examples quoted by Nonius, as well as at Festus, p. 301.7 Lindsay (purime tetinero purissime tenuero).

doc.metrical_analysis: The metre of this fragment is considered by the TRF and most other modern editions to be the trochaic septenarius. In the first of these two lines, the first foot-and-a-half is missing. Some earlier editors, however, have suggested that the lines might originally have been two iambic senarii. Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 145) suggested that if the word order of prodat and ultorem were inverted, i.e. ultorem prodat (though Vossius himself prefers the reading uictorem prodat), then the rhythm would be iambic. Onions took Vossius’ suggestion further, emending the lines quite substantially and printing the fragment as two iambic senarii (see ‘Apparatus criticus’).

lines.text: Quem metuunt oderunt, quem quisque odit periisse expetit.
Ipse summis saxis fixus asperis evisceratus
Latere pendens saxa spargens tabo sanie et sanguine atro | [...]
Neque sepulchrum, quo recipiat, habeat portum corporis,
Ubi remissa humana uita corpus requiescat malis.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: but if they do not weaken the conditions, cast off and return
doc.differences:
Manuwald 137 (6/10)

Jocelyn 156 (8/12)

Warmington 373 (14/14)

Ribbeck3 301 (6/11)

Vahlen2 344 (4/14)

sin flaccebunt condiciones, repudiato et reddito

Ribbeck: sin fracebunt condiciones repudiato et reddito
doc.apparatus:
⟨s⟩in flaccebunt] in flaccebunt codd. : inflaccebunt coni. Stephanus : ⟨s⟩in flaccebunt coni. Gulielmius : ⟨s⟩in fracebunt coni. Bergk
condiciones] conditiones P : condiciones rel.

Similar to frg. 136, there is an issue over the division between the fragment context of Nonius’ introductory words and the fragment itself: ⟨s⟩in is a correction by Gulielmius of the manuscripts, which all transmit ... Ennius Thyeste in flaccebunt… with a space between Thyestes and in. As with frg. 136, it is easy to see how a scribal faced with the unfamiliar form sin (like set) might reanalyse this as the familiar in (like et). In this case, however, the entire manuscript tradition records Thyeste in, and no obvious trace of the s remains.
Many 16th commentators (prior to Guilielmius’ suggested correction of in to ⟨s⟩in) adopted Stephanus' reading inflaccebunt, recognising that in flaccebunt made no grammatical sense. However, Nonius’ lemma introducing the fragment is for flaccet, not inflaccet, and this is unquestioned.
doc.commentary:
sin] sin = si + ne. Usually in prose sin is accompanied by words such as aliter or minus but this is not as customary in comedy. It is often used as a negative contrast to preceding conditional(s) introduced by si: this covering of all possibilities with outcomes would fit with a legalistic or at least a prescriptive tone.

repudiato et reddito] In combination with the noun condiciones, the conditional expression (⟨s⟩in) and the imperatives have the undertones of legalistic language.
doc.reconstruction:
If the language of repudiato et reddito is indeed legalistic (see commentary above, there would seem to be two parties striking an agreement referring to a third party consisting of multiple individuals (cf. the plural third person flaccebunt). The two imperatives require a speaker and an addressee. There is the possibility of the line belonging to a monologue, yet we would expect gerundives of obligation instead of imperatives in that case.

The line suggests that some kind of plan for future action is being formulated. However, the words are vague enough that several suggestions of context have been made which differ quite a lot. For instance, Ribbeck believed that these words were spoken by Thyestes to supporters of a plot to overthrow Atreus. On the other hand, Ladewig argued that the words suggested a betrothal, with the legal language quite appropriate to the formalities of a Roman marriage. Hyginus Fab. 88.6 refers to an engagement between Atreus and Pelopia, the daughter of Thesprotus, but not its realisation. As Jocelyn noted, Ladewig’s hypothesis would necessitate the incorporation of events long after the Thyestean feast, in Thesprotus’ court where Pelopia was found. The words of this fragment might also be interpreted as advisory, in which case, it could even be the chorus giving counsel or a moral statement at the end.

lines.text: ⟨s⟩in flaccebunt condiciones, repudiato et reddito

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis
id, quod multi invideant multique expetant, inscitia est
postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas.
[ATREUS]: oderint, dum metuant
[ATREUS]: <5> natis sepulcro ipse es parens

doc.translation:
(I)

One must always remain vigilant: many traps are laid for good people

It is ignorant to want that which many people envy and many people seek, unless you endure difficulty with the utmost care.

or

To demand for yourself what many envy and many seek is folly, unless you carry out the task to the end with the utmost diligence.

(II)

Let them hate, so long as they fear

(III)

For your sons, you, their parent, are a tomb.
