353: oderint dum metuant.

Odino, purché temano.

Fragment 8 (8/22), 363:

363: uigilandum est semper, multae insidiae sunt bonis

Bisogna stare sempre in guardia: molte insidie sono tese ai buoni.

Fragment 9 (9/22), 364-365:

364: id quod multi inuideant multique expetant inscitias

365: postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas.

È stoltezza cercare quello che molti eventualmente ti invidiano e bramano, se non t’impegni con tutte le forze.

D’Antò:

Fragment 4 (4/18), 203/204:

203/204: …oderint dum metuant…

mi portino pure odio, purché mi temano!

Fragment 6 (6/18), 214:

uigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis

bisogna esser sempre all’erta: molte sono le insidie tese ai buoni.

Fragment 7 (7/18), 215-216:

215: id quod multi invideant multique expetant inscitia est

216: postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas.

ciò che molti potrebbero invidiarti e molti bramare è insipienza voler ottenere; a meno che non ti sobbarchi ad un travaglio con pesanti affanni.

Dangel:

Fragment 10 (10/21), 47:

47: ? Oderint dum metuant !

Qu’ils me haïssent, pourvu qu’ils me craignent !

Fragment 8 (8/21), 45:

45: Vigilandum est semper ; multae insidiae sunt bonis.

Il faut toujours être vigilant : nombreux sont les pièges tendus aux hommes de bien.

Fragment 15 (15/21), 57:

57: Natis sepulcro ipse es parens ! [x – x – x – u x]

Tu es, toi leur père, le tombeau de tes fils !

Fragment 18 (18/21), 62-63:

62: Id quod multi inuideant multique expetant inscitia est

63: postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas.

Ce que beaucoup envient et que beaucoup recherchent, c’est pure ineptie de le postuler, si on ne prodigue pas sa peine avec un soin extrême.
doc.apparatus:
103.1 uigilandum ] uigilandum codd. Cic. Sest. 102 et omnes codd. Cic. Planc. 59 praeter T: iugulandum cod. Cic. Planc. 59 T 

103.1 uigilandum est ] uigilandum est codd.: uigilandumst Merry 1891, 118

103.2 id quod ] id quod codd.: sed te id quod Camerarius 1540, II.423: sed quod Ribbeck1, 138

103.2: invideant ] invideant codd. Cic. Sest. 102: videant codd. Cic. Planc. 59

103.2: expetant ] expetant P: expectant GV

103.2 inscitia est ] inscitia est G: inscitiast P2: incitiast P1: inscitia est G: inscientia est V

103.3 postulare nisi ] postulare nisi (vel ni) Wesenberg: postulare sivi P2B: postularent sivi P1GV

103.3 summa cum ] summa cum P: sūmū (c superscripsit G2)G: suum cum V

103.3 ecferas ] ecferas P: h(a)ec feras GV

103.5: sepulcro ] sepulcro omnes codices praeter Gudd. a c Basil.

103.5: es ] es schol. Gu.: est plerique codd. Cic.: sit ‘Goth., duo Oxon.’, cf. Heusinger 1783, 228.

103.1-3 (apparatus based on Maslowski 1986 for Cic. Sest. 102): the best witnesses to lost archetype A are P [Parisinus lat. 7794], of which B is a copy [Bern, Bürgerbibliothek, MS lat. 136]; G [Brussels, Bibl. royale 5345] and E [Berlin, Deutsche Staatsbibliothek MS lat. fol. 252], both of which derive from a common parent m; and H [London, British Library, MS Harley 4927]. From m descends n, the lost ancestor of three further witnesses, ε, F [lost], and V [Vatican, MS Palat. lat. 1525]. Cf. Maslowski–Rouse 1984.

103.1 uigilandum est: printed as such by virtually all editors. Merry (1891, 118) however prints uigilandumst, with contraction of esse. Brakman (1935, 58) suggests adding certe or sane (presumably at the beginning of the fragment?), but the line already scans as a complete iambic senarius.

103.2 id quod: Camerarius (1540, II.423 ad Cic. Sest. 102) conjectured sed te id quod instead of id quod. He prints the fragment as follows: sed te id quod multi inuideant, multique expetant (a single iambic senarius). It appears from the lack of indentation of the following line that he does not consider what follows (inscitia sit, inquit postulare: si tu [sivi MSS] laborem summa cum cura efferas nullum) as part of the poetic fragment. Ribbeck in his first edition (p. 138) seems to have found Camerarius’ sed appealing and printed it instead of the transmitted id, but later changed his mind and printed the paradosis id quod in his two subsequent editions.

103.2 inscitia est: some editors prefer the uncontracted form inscitia est (e.g. TRF, Warmington, Dangel), others (e.g. Ribbeck) the contracted form inscitiast (which is found in arguably the best witness, P). On contraction of esse, see Pezzini 2015, 42 and 123-6 (and 126 on contracted spelling in early Latin drama, though attested in epic and tragedy, as possibly ‘colloquial’ and low-register).

103.3 postulare nisi: a conjecture by Wesenberg (1837, 4), who in fact thinks that what Accius wrote is the contracted form nī (OLD s.v. ni 5).

103.3 ecferas: surely the correct reading, the corruption h(a)ec feras in GV having arisen likely on account of the rareness of the verb ec-/effero (see ‘Fragment commentary’).

103.1-3 (apparatus based on Olechowska 1981 for Cic. Planc. 59): the variant reading iugulandum is found in manuscript T (= cod. Munich lat. 18787, formerly held in the monastery of Tegernsee). The reading is not mentioned in Olechowska’s apparatus, but in that of Orelli, 1856 edition, II.1120, and in Ribbeck2, 163.

103.4 (apparatus based on Maslowski 1986 for Cic. Sest. 102, Olechowska 1981 for Cic. Plan. 59, Winterbottom 1994 for Cic. Off. 1.97, Fedeli 19862 for Cic. Phil. 1.33-4, Reynolds 1977 for Sen. Dial. 3.20.4 [= Ira 1.20.4], Kaster 2022 for Sen. Clem. 1.12.4 and 2.2.2, Kaster 2016 for Suet. Cal. 30): no textual issues to report. See ‘Metrical issues’.

103.5 (apparatus based on Winterbottom 1994 for Cic. Off. 1.97): Heusinger (1783, 229) reports that the Wolfenbüttel scholiast (referred to by Heusinger as ‘Gu.’, i.e. ‘Guelpherbytanus’) bears witness to the reading ipse es parens, and includes the following note: ‘tu nati sepulcro, i. pro sepulcro, i. in ventre tuo natum tuum sepelisti’. On these scholia, see Heusinger, praef. xxii-xxv. It is worth noting that the TRF chooses to print the reading of the Wolfenbüttel scholiast es, whereas Winterbottom prints est. Es is also preferred by Lana (1959, 308) and by Dangel. Heusinger (1783, 228) notes that ipse est parens is conjectured by Lambinus and Gronovius (and endorsed by Graevius) for the reading ipse sit parens. The conjecture, Heusinger reports, is confirmed by the ‘Goth.’ manuscript (cf. TRF IV.660) as well as the two Oxford manuscripts. Pearce (apud Heusinger 1783, 228) conjectures sepulcro natis ipse sit parens (for metrical reasons).
doc.commentary:
103.1-3 vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis | id, quod multi invideant multique expetant, inscitia est | postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas: Ribbeck (1875, 452) posits a lacuna between the first line (uigilandum…bonis) and the second and third lines (id…ecferas: see ‘Citation context’). He compares Euripides Thyestes F 392 TrGF (εἰ δ’ ἄτερ πόνων | δοκεῖς ἔσεσθαι, μῶρος εἶ, θνητὸς γεγώς, ‘If you expect to be free of troubles you are a fool, seeing that you are mortal’, tr. Collard–Cropp 2008-2009, I.433) and Cretan Women F 461 TrGF: οὐκ ἂν δύναιο μὴ καμὼν εὐδαιμονεῖν, | αἰσχρόν τε μοχθεῖν μὴ θέλειν νεανίαν, ‘You can’t prosper without effort; and it is shameful for a young man to be unwilling to work hard’ (transl. Collard–Cropp 2008-2009, I.523). It is worth pointing out that Ribbeck considers Euripides’ Thyestes and Cretan Women to be one and the same play (1875, 452). 

103.1 vigilandum: a sententia echoed several times throughout Seneca’s Thyestes (Lana 1959, 318): 288 inimica credit cuncta (‘he sees enemies everywhere’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.257: the satelles speaking about Thyestes); 435 nihil timendum uideo, sed timeo tamen (‘I see nothing fearful, but I fear nonetheless’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.269, Thyestes speaking); 473 rogat? timendum est. errat hic aliquis dolus (‘Asking? Fearful! Some trickery is skulking here’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.273, Thyestes’ reaction to hearing that Atreus is asking him to rule); 482-4 ([TANTALUS, son of Thyestes]: Quam tamen fraudem times? | [THYESTES]: Omnem: timori quem meo statuam modum? | tantum potest quantum odit (‘[TANTALUS]: But what kind of deceit do you fear? [THYESTES]: Every kind! What limit can I set on my fear? His capacity is as great as his hatred’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.275); 487 serum est cauendi tempus in mediis malis (‘The time for caution is past in the midst of evil’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.275: Thyestes speaking). 

103.1 bonis: Bilinski (1958, 36) interprets boni as perhaps enclosing a covert political reference to the Roman nobilitas, comparing Lucilius’ use of the term uir bonus at 1252-58 Krenkel = 1228-34 Marx, a description of people doing bad things but pretending to be ‘a good man’ (uir bonus). Franchella (1968, 227 n. 1) disagrees, thinking this too narrow an interpretation of the term, as does D’Antò (1980, 285), but Monteleone (1991, 337) agrees with Bilinski that there is a political reference in this line. Monteleone argues that Accius made a covert distinction between ‘good citizens’ (boni, which Monteleone interprets as referring to political ‘conservatives’) and ‘bad citizens’ (improbi, or ‘revolutionaries’, mentioned by Cicero at Planc. 99, see ‘Citation context’), two commonly-used terms in Republican political discourse. By translating boni as ‘patriots’, Kaster (2006, 85) seems to endorse the idea that the word encloses a political undertone, but without going so far as Bilinski and Monteleone and identifying these individuals with a specific political group.

103.2 multi…multique: Bilinski (1958, 30; 39) interprets multi as equivalent to mali (cf. Nonius p. 519.1-3 M = 835.1-2 L: veterum memorabilis scientia ‘paucorum’ numerum pro bonis ponebat; ‘multos’ contra malos appellabant) and thus identifies political reference to the plebs. Franchella (1968, 228 n. 1) believes this political interpretation is too restrictive.

103.3 ecferas: ‘to carry through (a task); to endure (an evil) to the end’ (OLD s.v. effero1 14). This sense of the verb is listed as ‘ante-class. and very rare’ by Lewis–Short (s.v. D). It is paralleled at Lucr. 1.141 (the reading of the archetype, although Lewis–Short do not consider this a parallel as they think the better reading is sufferre, a marginal correction in the fifteenth-century recentior manuscript T = Flor. Laur. 35.32) and at Cic. Tusc. 4.63, a translation of the first three lines of Euripides’ Orestes ([ELECTRA]: Οὐκ ἔστιν οὐδὲν δεινόν, ὧδ᾿ εἰπεῖν ἔπος, | οὐδὲ πάθος οὐδὲ ξυμφορὰ θεήλατος, | ἧς οὐκ ἂν ἄραιτ᾿ ἄχθος ἀνθρώπου φύσις, ‘There is virtually nothing horrific, no suffering, no god-sent affliction, whose burden man, being what he is, might not shoulder’, transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, V.413). Cicero’s translation reads (Ex Euripide conversi loci F III Soubiran): neque tam terribilis ulla fando oratio est | nec fors nec ira caelitum inuectum malum | quod non natura humana patiendo ecferat, ‘no tale is so terrible to tell, no lot, no ill inflicted by the wrath of the gods, that human nature does not bear its burden’, transl. Graver 2002, 62). Cicero uses ecfero to express Euripides’ ἀείρω ‘to take up and bear, as a burden’ (LSJ s.v. 4).

103.4 oderint, dum metuant: the most often-quoted line of the play: Cic. Sest. 102 (with Schol. Bob. ad loc.), Off. 1.97, Phil. 1.34, Sen. Dial. 3 [= Ira 1] 20.4, Clem. 1.12.4, 2.2.2, Suet. Cal. 30: a version of it was also apparently quoted by Tiberius, as oderint, dum probent ‘let them hate me, so long as they approve my conduct’, Suet. Tib. 59.2, transl. Rolfe, rev. Hurley 1997-19982, I.393. Tacitus’ Agrippina the Younger alludes to the fragment at Ann. 14.9, when, after having been told that Chaldaen astrologers predicted that for her son Nero to rule he would have to kill his mother, she responds: ‘occidat, … dum imperet’, ‘let him slaughter, provided he achieves command’ (transl. Woodman 2004, 279), cf. Monteleone 1975, 302-3. Accius’ line seems to allude to a fragment by Ennius from an uncertain play (F inc. 163 TRF) quoted at Cic. Off. 2.23 which Ribbeck (1875, 202) suggested might belong to the Thyestes: quem metuunt oderunt; quem quisque odit periisse expetit, ‘whom they fear, they hate; whom one hates, one desires his death’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL II.175: the allusion was noticed by Columna 1590, 459). An allusion to the Accian line has also been perceived at Lucr. 3.72-73 (Degl’Innocenti Pierini 1980, 13), and it is ‘paraphrased and developed’ (Lana 1959, 317) at Sen. Thy. 205-10 (quoted below in ‘Fragment reconstruction’).

103.5 natis sepulcro ipse es parens: Marchesi (1908, 87) compares Sen. Thy. 891 implebo patrem funere suorum, ‘I shall fill the father with the death of his sons’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.307-9), but a closer parallel is in Ovid’s version of the Procne, Philomela, and Tereus myth in the Metamorphoses, at the moment when Tereus realises that he has eaten his son Itys: flet modo seque uocat bustum miserabile nati, ‘now he weeps bitterly and calls himself his son’s most wretched tomb’ (Ov. Met. 6.665, transl. Miller–Goold 19773, I.335).
doc.reconstruction:
103.1 vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis: = F 8, v. 45 Dangel. Dangel (1995, 280) interprets this fragment as Thyestes giving advice to his sons, comparing it to Euripides Cretan Women F 461 TrGF (οὐκ ἂν δύναιο μὴ καμὼν εὐδαιμονεῖν, | αἰσχρόν τε μοχθεῖν μὴ θέλειν νεανίαν, ‘You can’t prosper without effort; and it is shameful for a young man to be unwilling to work hard’, transl. Collard–Cropp 2008-2009, I.523). 

103.1-3: vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis | id, quod multi invideant multique expetant, inscitia est | postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas: Most editors believe that these lines, which are quoted in close proximity by Cicero at Planc. 59 and Sest. 102, belong together and form a single fragment (see ‘Citation context’). Dangel, however, argues that the first line, on the one hand, and the two subsequent lines, on the other hand, are in fact two different fragments which belong to two different parts of the play. She places the first fragment (uigilandum est semper ; multae insidiae sunt bonis) in position 8, and the second fragment (id quod multi inuideant multique expetant inscitia est | postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas) in position 18 out of a total of 21 fragments (see below).

Ribbeck, who sees in these lines an allusion to a version by Euripides of the tragedy of Thyestes (1875, 452, see ‘Fragment commentary’), imagines them to belong to a scene in which Thyestes, filled with confidence at the prospect of potentially becoming king, nevertheless warns his children about the dangers of overconfidence and blind trust. The Accian scene would thus be somewhat similar to that involving Thyestes and his sons in Seneca’s Thyestes (404-90): in fact, Ribbeck (p. 453) sees a faint echo of the Accian lines in Sen. Thy. 435 nihil timendum uideo, sed timeo tamen, ‘I see nothing fearful, but I fear nonetheless’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.269) and 487 serum est cauendi tempus in mediis malis, ‘the time for caution is past in the midst of evil’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.275). It is important to bear in mind, however, that in Seneca’s version, Atreus invites Thyestes back to the royal palace and Thyestes ponders whether or not to accept, whereas in Accius’ version, it seems to be the case that Thyestes returns to the Pelopid palace of his own accord, as F 104.3-4 TRF (iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum aduenit; |  iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat) may suggest (but see F 104.3-4 ‘Fragment commentary’).

H.W. Auden (1897, 109), in his commentary on Cicero’s Pro Plancio, suggests that these lines are spoken by Atreus as a warning to his sons Agamemnon and Menelaus, an interpretation which does not seem to have won much approval, but which cannot be ruled out.

In Lana’s view (1959, 304-6), this fragment belongs to a scene which follows (perhaps after an intermediary choral ode?) Atreus’ exchange with the attendant. Thyestes arrives on stage in the company of his young children, warning them about being overly confident and trusting (F 103.1  vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis). Lana’s interpretation of both this fragment and F 104.3-6 (iterum…) is radically different to the traditional view, according to which Thyestes returns to the Pelopid palace of his own accord. Instead, Lana argues (1959, 363-5) that the scenario in Accius’ play was similar to that in Seneca’s Thyestes, and that Atreus actually invited Thyestes back to the palace. The present fragment is central to Lana’s interpretation. He argues that it depicts Thyestes and his children discussing whether or not he should accept Atreus’ invitation. According to Lana, his children suggest that Thyestes should accept, possibly because they themselves hope one day to inherit the throne. Thyestes warns them against such ambitions, saying that being king generates envy from others and that he is not prepared to do anything to stay in power (F 103.2-3 id, quod multi invideant multique expetant, inscitia est | postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas). Lana argues that the position that Thyestes adopts here makes him just as morally corrupt as Atreus: while Atreus invites Thyestes back under the pretense of sharing the throne with him but secretly wishing to exact revenge, Thyestes, though aware that Atreus’ invitation might be a trap, nevertheless accepts it because he himself harbours ambitions to rule alone.

La Penna (1972, 359-361) interprets this fragment differently: he believes these to be the words of Atreus to his sons Agamemnon and Menelaus, as he instructs them to go and fetch Thyestes and warns them to be wary of potential manipulation. He points out that, in Seneca’s Thyestes, Atreus momentarily considers sending his sons Agamemnon and Menelaus to deliver the invitation to Thyestes, but eventually changes his mind (296-330). Endorsed by Messina (1988, 55-56), Monteleone (1991, 335-7), and Petrone (2002, 247), La Penna’s interpretation is considered ‘peu vraisemblable’ and rejected by Dangel (1995, 280), as well as by Baldarelli (2004, 226-8). Baldarelli suggests that, if Atreus were the speaker, the nature of the instructions given to his children here, in which the importance of prudence is emphasised, would be inconsistent with the radically different educational principle stated in F Adesp. 65 TRF (proinde ita parent se in vita, ut vinci nesciant), where Atreus (assuming the Atreus of Accius is meant) insists that his children acquire the mentality of not knowing defeat.

Dangel (1995, 282) argues that these three lines consist of two different fragments (F 8, v. 45 and F 18, v. 62-63 Dangel), both of which are spoken by Thyestes. In F 8 Dangel (uigilandum est semper ; multae insidiae sunt bonis), Thyestes warns his sons that they should be prudent, while they accuse him of being intolerant (p. 280). In F 18 Dangel (id, quod multi invideant multique expetant, inscitia est | postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas), which belongs to a scene after the children’s death, Thyestes is meditating remorsefully on their fate (p. 281-2). Dangel interprets Cicero’s comment, at Pro Plancio 59 (see ‘Citation context’) about Accius’ purpose in writing these lines, ut illos regios pueros qui iam nusquam erant, as meaning not ‘des enfants qui n’ont jamais existé’ (compare Watt’s translation: ‘...those young princes who were merely the figments of his [sc. the poet’s] imagination’), but ‘“qui n’existaient plus nulle part” du fait de leur sépulture inédite’ (i.e. the children no longer exist on account of the unprecedented circumstances of their death and ‘burial’). Dangel then imagines a connection between this fragment and two fragments earlier in the play: Thyestes is here reflecting on his children having returned to the source that gave them life, thinking of the statement he had made in F 105 TRF (= F 7, v. 43-44 Dangel), on Dangel’s interpretation of that fragment, about his children descending from the paternal rather than from the maternal line (probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae | fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent). Dangel further suggests that Thyestes is reminiscing specifically about the advice he gave to his children in F 103.1 TRF (= F 8, v. 45 Dangel, vigilandum est semper ; multae insidiae sunt bonis), and bitterly regretting that his children did not heed his advice. Even if we accept both Dangel’s interpretation of each of these fragments and the connections she identifies between them, however, it is not straightforward to understand Cicero’s phrase at Planc. 59 as meaning what Dangel suggests.

Baldarelli (2004, 224) argues that the proximity with which these three lines are quoted by Cicero suggests that they belong, if not to the same fragment, then at least to the same scene in Accius’ play.

103.4 oderint, dum metuant: Lana (1959, 301) sees this fragment as part of a scene in which Atreus discusses his plans for revenge with his attendant. When the attendant realises the nature and scale of Atreus’ terrible revenge, he urges him to think about the reaction of his subjects when they hear about his terrible revenge, which will have unsettled the stability of the kingdom. Atreus responds with the famous line: oderint, dum metuant (F 103.4 TRF). Lana suggests that Atreus might have continued by listing wrongs committed against him by Thyestes, perhaps in order to justify his revenge, and that this list included F 104.7 (qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum).

Ribbeck (1875, 450) compares the following part of the dialogue between Atreus and the satelles in Seneca’s Thyestes (204-11):

[SATELLES]: fama te populi nihil

aduersa terret? [ATREUS]: maximum hoc regni bonum est,

quod facta domini cogitur populus sui

tam ferre quam laudare. [SATELLES]: Quos cogit metus, 

laudare, eosdem reddit inimicos metus.

[...]

[ATREUS]: Laus uera et humili saepe contingit uiro.

[SATELLES]: You have no fear of hostile talk among the people?  [ATREUS]: This is the greatest value of kingship: that the people are compelled to praise as well as endure their master’s actions. [SATELLES]: When fear compels them to praise, fear also turns them into enemies. [...] [ATREUS]: Sincere praise often comes even to a lowly man (Transl. Fitch 20182, II.247-9).

103.5 natis sepulcro ipse es parens: Ribbeck (1875, 454) imagines this fragment as belonging to a scene in which Thyestes, after the meal, suddenly becomes anxious and asks Atreus where his children are: it is at this moment, Ribbeck suggests, that Atreus reveals the children’s severed heads, hands, and feet: R. considers as non-Accian the detail found in Hyginus that Atreus displays the children’s arms (bracchia et ora, Hyg. Fab. 88.2). Ribbeck thus seems to imply that Accius’ version corresponds to Aesch. Ag. 1594-5: τὰ μὲν ποδήρη καὶ χερῶν ἄκρους κτένας | < κεφαλάς τε σώσας, τἀπίλοιπα τῶν κρεῶν > | ἔθρυπτ᾿…, ‘The foot parts and the combs at the ends of the arms, <and the heads, he set aside; the rest of their flesh> he [sc. Atreus] chopped up’ (transl. Sommerstein 2008, II.193-5, translating the text that Rose, on the basis of Hdt. 1.119, proposes to supply within the lacuna posited by Hermann between lines 1594 and 1595). The corresponding scene in Seneca is at Thy. 1030-1 quidquid e natis tuis | superest habes, quodcumque non superest habes, ‘All that remains of your children you have, all that does not remain you have’ (Atreus’ cruel, riddling words) and 1034 epulatus ipse es impia natos dape ‘You yourself banqueted on your sons – a sacrilegious meal’ (Atreus’ explicit, climactic revelation of the truth; transl. Fitch 20182, II.319). Ribbeck observes that Ovid carries over the motif to his version of the Procne, Philomela, and Tereus myth in the Metamorphoses, at the moment when Tereus realises that he has eaten his son Itys: flet modo seque uocat bustum miserabile nati, ‘now he weeps bitterly and calls himself his son’s most wretched tomb’ (Ov. Met. 6.665, transl. Miller–Goold 19773, I.335: note Ovid’s use of indirect discourse). Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 272) imagines that Thyestes responded to Atreus’ horrific taunt with words along the lines of: ‘“Di’ che non è vero! Di’ che non è vero!”’. This, in turn, prompted Atreus to retort with F inc. 404 TRF (numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi!), and Di Benedetto Zimbone suspects that Atreus also said something like the following: ‘Make no mistake, Thyestes. It is just as you have heard. I killed your children with these very hands. And you, you ate their flesh, with that very mouth!’ (‘“Non ti fare illusioni, Tieste. È proprio come hai udito. I tuoi figli, io li ho uccisi, con queste mani. E tu, tu ne hai mangiato le carni, con codesta bocca!”’). Dangel (1995, 281), building on a suggestion by Lana (1959, 308, also reading es for est), imagines that this line is spoken by Atreus to Thyestes after the latter has asked to see his children, like in Seneca’s Thyestes (997-998, 1028-1031). She compares a fragment of Sophocles not assignable to any play (F inc. 892 TrGF παῖδας γὰρ, οὓς ἔφυσ᾿, ἀναλώσας ἔχει, ‘For he has destroyed the children I begot’, transl. Lloyd-Jones 1994-1996, III.387), without however explaining how this fragment of Sophocles might relate to the Accian scene: are we to imagine a horrified Thyestes speaking a line similar to this? Dangel further compares Ennius Thyestes F 132 TRF (= v. 311-312 Ribbeck3 = v. 298-299 Jocelyn: neque sepulchrum quo recipiat corpus habeat, portum corporis, | ubi remissa humana uita corpus requiescat malis, ‘and he shall not have a tomb where he can withdraw, a haven for the body, where, once human life has been dismissed, the body can rest from troubles’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL II.136): the implication seems to be, although she does not state so explicitly, that Accius is alluding to the Ennian passage. She argues that the Accian fragment is imitated at Lucretius (5.991-3: viva videns vivo sepeliri viscera busto, ‘...as he saw his own living flesh buried in a living tomb’, transl. Rouse–Smith 19922, 455, a description of an early human seeing himself being devoured by a wild beast which is described as a tomb) and by Seneca (Thy. 1030-1, quoted above).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Pro Sestio 102:

Haec imitamini, per deos inmortalis, qui dignitatem, qui laudem, qui gloriam quaeritis! haec ampla sunt, haec divina, haec immortalia; haec fama celebrantur, monumentis annalium mandantur, posteritati propagantur. est labor, non nego; pericula magna, fateor;

multae insidiae sunt bonis 

verissime dictum est; sed te

id quod multi invideant multique expetant inscitiast,

Inquit,

postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas. (= Accius Atreus F 103.1-3 TRF part.)

nollem idem alio loco dixisset, quod exciperent inprobi cives,

oderint, dum metuant; (= Accius Atreus F 103.4 TRF)

praeclara enim illa praecepta dederat iuventi. 

By the immortal gods, these are the models to imitate, those of you who seek worthy standing, who seek honor and glory! These are the models that offer true scope and splendour, godlike and deathless; these are the models that are on everyone’s lips, that are entrusted to the memory of Rome’s annals, that are handed down from generation to generation. It is a toilsome task, I do not deny it; the risks are great, I admit;

many snares are set for patriots,

truer words have never been spoken; but

to demand for yourself what many envy and many seek is folly, 

the poet says,

unless you carry out the toilsome task to the end with keenest intensity. (= Accius Atreus F 103.1-3 TRF part.)

I would prefer that he had not also said what wicked citizens pluck from another context,

let them hate me, so long as they fear me, (= Accius Atreus F 103.4 TRF)

for those others were brilliant precepts to give to the younger generation.

[Text: Maslowski 1986; translation: Kaster 2006, 85-6.]

Scholia Bobiensia Cic. Sest. 102, p. 134.34-135.2 Stangl:

<NOLLEM IDEM> ALIO LOCO DIXISSET: ‘ODERINT DUM METUANT’. Et hoc eiusdem poëtae est: ‘oderint, dum metuant’. Quam sententiam detestatur quasi tyrannicam et inauspicatam liberae civitati.

<I WOULD PREFER THAT HE> HAD NOT ALSO SAID IN ANOTHER CONTEXT: ‘LET THEM HATE ME, SO LONG AS THEY FEAR ME’: This too is from the same poet: ‘let them hate me, so long as they fear me’. This line is despised as tyrannical and inauspicious for a free state.

This fragment is preserved in Cicero’s Pro Sestio, a forensic speech in which Cicero defends Publius Sestius (RE ‘Sestius 6’), tribune of the plebs in 57 BCE (MRR II.201) who, along with his fellow tribune Titus Annius Milo and the consul P. Cornelius Lentulus Spinther, remained faithful to Cicero during his exile and worked actively to secure his return to Rome. Sestius was charged under the lex Plautia de vi, a law governing acts of public violence, on 10 February 56, and, having been successfully defended by Cicero among others, he was acquitted by unanimous vote on 14 March of the same year (TLLR no 271). This fragment is quoted within a section of the speech (§96-135) in which Cicero makes a distinction between two sorts of statesman, the ‘Best Sort’ (optimates) and ‘Men of the People’ (populares): cf. Kaster 2006, 319. Cicero also quotes from tragedy, including from Accius, at §123, although, as Kaster observes (p. 327), the ‘next tragic quotations are in a different vein, offered as evidence of audiences’ and actors’ taking the verses, in performance, to comment on C. and his plight’. 

The issue of determining the identity of the speaker of this fragment in Accius’ play turns on how idem is interpreted in the following sentence: nollem idem alio loco dixisset quod exciperent improbi ciues, ‘oderint dum metuant’... Traditionally, idem had been understood as referring to Accius as the author of the play, but La Penna (1972, 360-1) first suggested that it refers to not to Accius but to Atreus, on the grounds that the famous fragment oderint, dum metuant (F 103.4 TRF), which can with certainty be attributed to Atreus, is quoted shortly afterwards. La Penna’ interpretation is endorsed by some later scholars, but not by all (see ‘Fragment reconstruction’).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Pro Plancio 59: 

Cicero speaks of the advice he gives to his son:

ego vero quamquam illi omnia malo quam mihi, tamen illi honorum aditus numquam faciliores optavi quam mihi fuerunt. Quin etiam, ne forte ille sibi me potius peperisse iam honores quam iter demonstrasse adipiscendorum putet, haec illi soleo praecipere – quamquam ad praecepta aetas non est grandis [Klotz 1865, 803-4: grauis E] – quae rex [rex addidit Klotz] ille a Iove ortus suis praecipit filiis: ‘vigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis’ – nostis cetera; nonne? ‘id quod multi invideant…’ [nonne…invideant del. Madvig 1871-1884, II.231] (= Accius Atreus F 103.1-3 TRF part.) quae scripsit gravis ille et ingeniosus poeta, scripsit non ut illos regios pueros qui iam nusquam erant, sed ut nos et nostros liberos ad laborem et laudem excitaret.

Though I always give him the first and myself the second place in my wishes, I have never hoped that the avenues to honour would be easier for him than they were to myself. Lest he should think that rather than showing him the way to win honours in the future I have already won his honours for him, I am accustomed to give him the advice (though advice is somewhat beyond him at his present years) which that king who was himself sprung from Jupiter gave to his sons: ‘Be wakeful aye; with snares the good are hemmed’ – no doubt you recall the rest of the passage – ‘For jealous hearts are rife…’ These lines were written by an earnest and gifted poet, whose object in writing them was to kindle the spirit of industry and ambition, not in royal youths who did not exist, but in us and in our children. 

[Text: Olechowska 1981; transl. Watt, adapted).

F 103.1-3 TRF is preserved most fully at Pro Sestio 102, less so at Pro Plancio 59. The Pro Plancio is a forensic speech delivered by Cicero in July 54 BCE in defence of his friend Cn. Plancius (MRR II.223), who had been accused on a charge of misconduct during his campaign for the aedileship of 54 by his unsuccessful rival A. Laterensis under the lex Licinia de sodaliciis, which had been carried the previous year by the consul M. Licinius Crassus (TLRR no. 293). Plancius was acquitted. The Accian fragment is quoted in a part of the speech when Cicero turns to address the junior counsel, L. Cassius, who had argued that Laterensis deserved to prevail in the campaign for aedileship on account of his noble birth, and apparently suggested that Cicero’s son would profit from the favourable circumstance of being a consul’s son if he were to pursue a career in politics. Cicero responds that the path to magistrature is open to all, and that, in order to make his son aware that being in office is not plain sailing but involves hardships and risks, gives him advice similar to that given by one of sons of Pelops (it is uncertain whether Atreus or Thyestes is meant) to his sons in the play by Accius.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De Officiis 1.97:

Haec ita intellegi possumus existimare ex eo decoro quod poetae sequuntur, de quo alio loco plura dici solent. Sed tum servare illud poetas quod deceat dicimus cum id quod quaque persona dignum est et fit et dicitur: ut si Aeacus aut Minos diceret ‘oderint dum metuant’ (= Accius Atreus F 103.4 TRF) aut ‘natis sepulcro ipse est parens’ (= Accius Atreus F 103.5 TRF) indecorum videretur, quod eos fuisse iustos accepimus, at Atreo dicente plausus excitantur, est enim digna persona oratio.

We can infer that this is how men regard the question from the concept of the fitting which the poets pursue, a topic often discussed more fully in a context different from this. We maintain that the poets observe what is fitting when what is worthy of each character is reflected in both their actions and their words. For example, if Aeacus or Minos were to say

Yes, let them hate, if only they feel fear as well,

or

The father is himself his offspring’s tomb,

that would seem unfitting, because tradition has it that these characters were just. But when Atreus utters the lines, they evoke applause, because the words are in keeping with the character.

[Text: Winterbottom; translation: Walsh 2001, 34.]

Scholia Guelpherbytana (apud Heusinger 1783, 229): ‘tu nati sepulcro, i. pro sepulcro, i. in ventre tuo natum tuum sepelisti’, ‘You are the tomb of your son, that is, in place of a tomb, that is, you have buried your son within your own very stomach’.

These fragments of Accius (F 104.3 TRF, oderint dum metuant, and F 103.5 TRF, natis sepulcro ipse es parens) are quoted in a passage of Cicero’s De Officiis, written towards the end of 44 BCE, in which Cicero explains the concept of decorum. The concept of decorum as presented by Cicero is problematic, not least because it encloses both ethical and aesthetic components which are not easy to disassociate from one another. Decorum is the Latin word that Cicero chooses to translate the Greek word τὸ πρέπον that was discussed in Book 1 of Περὶ τοῦ καθήκοντος, a treatise by the Stoic philosopher Panaetius (c. 185-109 BCE) on which Cicero is probably drawing here (Dyck 1979, 1996). In fact, the whole section of Book 1 of De Officiis going from 1.93 to 1.101 (...obtemperet) is considered as a fragment of Panaetius by Van Straaten (F 107). Assuming that Cicero is engaging closely with the text of Panaetius here (Dyck 1996, 28 believes that Cicero ‘presented a considerably shortened version of Panaetius’), are we to believe that Panaetius too used quotations from Greek poetry to illustrate certain points in his argument, as was common in the Stoic school? If so, what might have been the Greek poetic citations used by Panaetius to illustrate the concept of τὸ πρέπον corresponding to the Latin ones from Accius introduced by Cicero?

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Philippics 1.33-34:

…illud magis uereor ne ignorans uerum iter gloriae gloriosum putes plus te unum posse quam omnis et metui a ciuibus tuis quam diligi magis. quod si ita putas, totam ignoras uiam gloriae. carum esse ciuem, bene de re publica mereri, laudari, coli, diligi gloriosum est; metui uero et in odio esse inuidiosum, detestabile, imbecillum, caducum. quod uidemus etiam in fabula illi ipsi qui ‘oderint, dum metuant’ dixerit perniciosum fuisse.

But I am particularly afraid that, ignorant of the true path to glory, you may consider that it is more glorious for you to have more power than everyone else together and prefer to be feared rather than respected by your fellow-citizens. Yet, if you think like this, you are entirely ignorant of the road to glory. When a citizen is loved, has done well by the Republic, is praised, honoured and respected, it is glorious; being feared and hateful is repellent, detestable, fleeting and precarious. We even see this in the play where the very man who said ‘Let them hate, as long as they fear’ was ruined by his own maxim. 

(Text: Fedeli 19862; transl. McElduff 2011, 193-4).

The Accian Atreus’ most famous line, oderint dum metuant, is quoted in this passage towards the end of Cicero’s first Philippic, in which Cicero warns Mark Antony that by behaving like a tyrant and incurring the fear of the people, like Atreus does in Accius’ Atreus, he risks getting himself killed. This passage of Cicero has been thought, in particular by Lana 1959, to contain information relevant to the ending of Accius’ Atreus: see ‘Fragment reconstruction’.

context.author: Seneca
context.text:
Seneca Dialogi 3.20.4 = De Ira 1.20.4:

‘Quid ergo? non aliquae uoces ab iratis emittuntur quae magno emissae uideantur animo?’ <Immo> ueram ignorantibus magnitudinem, qualis illa dira et abominanda ‘oderint, dum metuant’. Sullano scias saeculo scriptam. Nescio utrum sibi peius optauerit ut odio esset an ut timori. ‘Oderint’. Occurrit illi futurum ut execrentur insidientur opprimant: quid adiecit? Di illi male faciant, adeo repperit dignum odio remedium. ‘Oderint’ – quid? ‘dum pareant’? Non. ‘dum probent?’ Non. Quid ergo? ‘dum timeant’. Sic ne amari quidem uellem.

‘Do you mean to say that angry people don’t produce some utterances that seem the products of a great spirit?’ No, rather, of spirits that don’t know true greatness, like that loathsome, abominable utterance, ‘Let them hate, so long as they fear’. You could tell that it was written in the age of Sulla. I don’t know which is worse, to be regarded with hatred or with fear. ‘Let them hate’. It dawns on him that they will then curse and plot and seek to crush him: what does he add? May the gods blast him, such a worthy cure for hatred he discovered. ‘Let them hate’ – what, ‘so long as they obey?’ No. ‘So long as they assent’. No. What then? ‘So long as they fear’. I would not want even to be loved on such terms.

(Text: Reynolds 1977; transl. Kaster in Kaster–Nussbaum 2010, 32-33).

In this passage of Seneca’s De Ira, Seneca responds to his fictional interlocutor’s question about the apparent greatness of certain utterances by angry people by marking a distinction between the passion of anger and ‘greatness of spirit’ (magnitudo animi, Gr. μεγαλοψυχία), which the Stoics considered a virtue (e.g. SVF III.264 = Stobaeus Ecl. II.60.9 Wachsmuth). The quotation of Accius serves to illustrate how horrible the passion of anger is, which is one of Seneca’s main objectives in the treatise. Seneca’s claim that the line was written ‘in the age of Sulla’, which plausibly can be taken to refer roughly to the first quarter of the first century BCE (Sulla’s dates are 138-78 BCE), is usually understood to be a mistake (though Baldarelli 2004, 197-205 argues that Seneca is not aiming for historical accuracy, but rather using the name of Sulla as a universal byword for the sort of tyranny represented by Accius’ Atreus: ‘Für Seneca verkörpert Sulla das sinnfälligste Beispiel einer despotischen Entartung des republikanischen Regierungssystems’, p. 204; cf. p. 313: ‘Denn wenn der Philosoph in de ira das saeculum Sullanum als einen terminus zur Datierung des accianischen Atreus heranzieht, handelt es sich ganz gewiss nicht um einen Irrtum oder eine Verallgemeinerung, sondern es liegt die unmissverständliche Absicht zugrunde, den Diktator, der ein halbes Jahrzehnt nach dem Tode des Accius an die Macht gelangt war, mit dem denkwürdigen Bilde zu verbinden, das der Atreus vom Tyrannentum entworfen hatte’). Indeed, Accius’ Atreus was probably in fact composed around 133 BCE, if not earlier (see ‘Short introduction’).

context.author: Seneca
context.text:
Seneca De clementia 1.12.4:

Contrariis in contraria agitur; nam cum inuisus sit quia timetur, timeri uult quia inuisus est, et illo execrabili uersu, qui multos praecipites dedit, utitur – 

oderint, dum metuant –

ignarus quanta rabies oriatur ubi supra modum odia creuerunt.

He [the tyrant] is driven by conflicting courses: since he is hated because he is feared, he wants to be feared because he is hated. He will invoke that accursed line of poetry that has sent many tumbling headlong: ‘They can hate me so long as they fear me’, without realizing the intensity of the frenzy that arises when hatred grows beyond limits.

[Text: Kaster 2022; transl. Braund 2009, 117.]

Cf. note on next entry entitled 'Seneca De clementia'.

context.author: Seneca
context.text:
Seneca De clementia 2.2.2:

Diutius me morari hic [me immorari huic <uoci> Madvig 1871-1874, II.428] patere, non ut *** blandum auribus tuis (nec enim hic mihi mos est; maluerim ueris offendere quam placere adulando). Quid ergo est? Praeter id, quod bene factis dictisque tuis quam familiarissimum esse te cupio, ut quod nunc natura et impetus est fiat iudicium, illud mecum considero multas uoces magnas sed detestabiles in uitam humanam peruenisse celebresque uulgo ferri, ut illam ‘oderint, dum metuant’, quoi Graecus versus <eius> similis est qui se mortuo terram misceri ignibus iubet, et alia huius notae.

Allow me to dwell on this point a little longer [or, translating Madvig’s text, ‘allow me to dwell on this remark (uox) a little longer’, see below], not to charm your ears – after all, that is not my way: I would rather offend you with the truth than please you with flattery. So what’s my point? Besides my desire that you should be as familiar as possible with your own fine actions and words, so that what you now do by natural inclination may become considered judgement, I am reflecting upon the many remarks, notable but loathsome, which have entered human experience and are bandied about, famous ones like this, ‘They can hate me so long as they fear me’, and the Greek line similar to that, which tells the earth to be embroiled with fire when the man himself is dead, and others of this stamp.

[Text: Kaster 2022; transl. Braund 2009, 143.]

Atreus’ famous line oderint dum metuant is quoted twice by Seneca in the De Clementia (as well as in the De Ira, on which see above). The De clementia is a protreptic treatise on kingship addressed to the emperor Nero written between 15 December 55 and 14 December 56 CE when Nero was eighteen years old (cf. Clem. 1.9.1-2, so after Nero’s murder of Britannicus, which occurred early in 55 CE: see Braund 2009, 16 with further bibliography), at a time when Seneca was the preceptor of the young ruler. At Clem. 1.12.4, the quotation of the Accian fragment occurs within a passage in which Seneca explains that clemency is the main difference between a king and a tyrant. It is noteworthy that, in the lines preceding the quotation of the fragment of Accius at Clem. 1.12.4, Seneca had briefly mentioned the tyranny of the Roman dictator Sulla. In the De Ira, the Accian fragment is also quoted in connection with Sulla. The second quotation of the Accian fragment falls at the beginning of Book 2 (Clem. 2.2.2), where Seneca commends the young Nero’s natural tendency to clemency. He refers specifically to an occasion when, having been ordered by the prefect Burrus to write down the names of brigands due to be executed, Nero apparently made the following remark (uox): ‘I wish I didn’t know how to write!’ (uellem litteras nescirem!). While such statements are commendable, Seneca writes, it should also be borne in mind that there are also other statements, such as ‘oderint, dum metuant’, which, though loathsome, have nevertheless become famous. The ‘Graecus uersus’ referred to at Clem. 2.2.2 is a fragment by an anonymous poet, generally consider to be a tragic poet (Adesp. F 513 TrGF), which is preserved in full both by Stobaeus and in the Greek Anthology (AP 7.704). Stobaeus quotes it in a section devoted to the doctrines on ethics of Aristotle and other Peripatetics (Ἀριστοτέλους καὶ τῶν λοιπῶν Περιπατετικῶν περὶ τῶν ἠθικῶν). The fragment reads: ἐμοῦ θανόντος γαῖα μιχθήτω πυρί• | οὐδὲν μέλει μοι• τἀμὰ γὰρ καλῶς ἔχει, ‘When I am dead, may the earth be consumed by fire: I could not care less, for with me all will be well’ (= Stobaeus Eclogae 2.7.13, II.121.12 Wachsmuth–Hense). The first line is quoted by Cassius Dio (58.23.4) and Suetonius (Ner. 38) as being a favourite of Caligula and Nero respectively. Kaster (2022, 253) detects allusions to the line elsewhere in Seneca’s works, at Prov. 5.8, QNat. 6.2.9, and Med. 426-8.

context.author: Suetonius
context.text:
Suetonius De vita Caesarum 4.30 = Caligula 30:

non temere in quemquam nisi crebris et minutis ictibus animaduerti passus est, perpetuo notoque iam praecepto, ‘ita feri ut se mori sentiat’. punito per errorem nominis alio quam quem destinauerat, ipsum quoque paria meruisse dixit. tragicum illud subinde iactabat,

‘oderint, dum metuant’.

He seldom had anyone put to death except by numerous slight wounds, his constant order, which soon became well-known, being: ‘Strike so that he may feel that he is dying.’ When a different man than he had intended had been killed, through a mistake in the names, he said that the victim too had deserved the same fate. He often uttered the familiar line of the tragic poet:

‘Let them hate me, so they but fear me’.

[Text: Kaster 2016; transl. Rolfe 1997-19982, I.465].

The quotation the Accian Atreus’ famous line falls within a section of Suetonius’ biography of the emperor Gaius Caligula (lived 12-41 CE, ruled 37-41) in which Suetonius lists the cruellest acts and sayings of a ruler who was famed for his brutality (Cal. 27-32).

doc.metrical_analysis:
103.1 uigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis: iambic senarii.

103.2-3 id, quod multi inuideant…: trochaic septenarii. If these three lines belonged to one and the same scene, there would have been a transition within the scene from iambic senarii to trochaic septenarii, and thus plausibly from unaccompanied speech to speech (or chant/song) accompanied by a tibicen: see Moore 2016, 346.

103.4 oderint, dum metuant: marked by TRF as ‘?’; ‘il est impossible de déterminer la scansion de ce [fragment]’ (Dangel 1995, 279-280). Several possibilities have been proposed: the beginning of a trochaic septenarius, or an ia4, ia6, ia8 (Soubiran apud Dangel 1995, 280).

103.5 natis sepulcro ipse es parens: marked as either iambic or trochaic by TRF. Soubiran (apud Dangel 1995, 281) suggests that this fragment could be belong to an iambic quaternarius (ia4) or to an iambic octonarius (ia8), but rules out the possibility that the text could be the beginning of an iambic senarius (ia6) on the grounds that the line would lack a caesura and instead would have an archaic diaeresis. He also suggests that the line could be an acalectic iambic dimeter, a lyric metre which would suit this particularly intense moment in Atreus’ vengeful jubilation. The line could belong either to a monody composed in this same metre, or falling within a sequence of lines in a variety of metres, or as the clausula of a sequence of iambic octonarii (ia8).

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: erras erras: nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi
repriment ualidae legum habenae atque imperi insistent iugo

doc.translation: You are mistaken, you are mistaken. You are unrestrained and overly self-confident: the powerful reins of the law will keep you in check and stand you in the yoke of authority.
doc.differences:
TRF: 24 (Adespoton)

Ribbeck: 125-126 (Adespoton)

Warmington: 23-24 (Adespoton)

Franchella: 35 (Adespoton)

Not in D'Antò's edition.

Not in Dangel's edition.

Warmington:

You err, you err; for though you do exult, | And in yourself are overconfident,

The sturdy reins of laws will pull you back, | And stand you firm in yoke of government.

Franchella:

erras erras: nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi

repriment ualidae legum habenae atque imperi inistent iugo

‘Sbagli sbagli: poiché le forti briglie delle leggi ti freneranno e ti peseranno

addosso col giogo del comando, mentre esulti e confidi troppo in te’.
doc.apparatus:
erras, erras] erras, erras L: erras M

nam] nam C: non U

nam exultantem te] nam exultantem te L: nam et uidente te A2: nam et uidente me H: nam et uiolentem Buecheler

praefidentem] praefidentem VOVU: praesidentem OPR: praefidente M

repriment] repriment C(OV): reprimunt O1

ualidae] ualidae MR: ualide VOPUHL: ualid(a)e A2

legum habenae] legum habenae M, om. VV (suppl. Vm)O (suppl. Oal) P1 (suppl. P2) R ‘legum habenae’ non est in veteri RV

atque] atque C: et Iul. Vict.

imperi] imperi MA2ac: -ii LApc

insistent] insistent codd.: sistent Kiessling﻿

The reading of L, the best manuscript of Cicero's De Oratore, was exultantem: Bücheler (apud Ribbeck2-3) conjectured uiolentem, thinking that this is the reading that laid behind the corrupt reading uidente me. The purpose of Kiessling’s conjecture of sistent for insistent (reported in Ribbeck2) seems to be to remove a perceived awkwardness in the use of insisto with iugo (i.e. sistent iugo = ‘stop you with a yoke’), but Kröner (apud TLL VII.1.1923.10-11, 45-6), understanding insisto in our passage as meaning ‘consistere in aliquo loco’, defends the manuscript reading by adducing Caes. Gall. 4.33.3 per temonem percurrere et in iugo insistere (where insistere iugo literally means ‘standing on the yoke’.

The critical apparatus supplied above is essentially that of Kumaniecki (19952, 329). It also contains additions made by Leeman–Pinkster V.xv. For a full list of manuscripts and their respective sigla, see Kumaniecki 19952, v-xxiv and xxxviii-xl, and, more succinctly, Winterbottom–Rouse–Reeve apud Reynolds 19862, 103-109.
doc.commentary:
erras, erras: Gaertner (2015, 439 n. 57), comparing the use of erras to suggest an introduction into a new way of life (‘Einleitung einer Lebensweisheit’) at Pl. Mos. 188-90, Trin. 666, Ter. Ad. 65-7, detects a possible comic undertone in this fragment, although as he himself suggests, the word may also be at home in tragedy: cf. Sen. Thy. 306-7 erras: malorum sensus accrescit die. | leue est miserias ferre, perferre est graue, ‘You are wrong. The sense of hardship accumulates day by day. To bear wretchedness is a light thing; to keep bearing it is heavy’ (tr. Fitch).

exultantem: or exsu-: the primary meaning is ‘to spring up, leap about, dance’ (OLD s.v. 1), and in an extended sense can mean to ‘behave in an unrestrained manner’ (OLD s.v. 2). It is glossed by Nonius as gloriari cum iactantia (300.31 M = 467.38 L), which seems to correspond to the sense here.

legum habenae: conveys the image of the law as a bridle restraining a wild or excited horse (for a similarly bold metaphor, cf. Cic. Cluent. 150 laqueos legum). The image is reminiscent of Aegisthus’ threat to subdue the chorus leader at Aesch. Ag. 1639-40: see further ‘Fragment reconstruction’.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck (Ribbeck2-3; 1875, 462) attributes these lines to Accius’ Clytemnestra, comparing Aesch. Ag. 1639-40 (Aegisthus to the chorus: τὸν δὲ μὴ πειθάνορα | ζεύξω βαρείαις, οὔ τι μὴ σειραφόρον | κριθῶντα πῶλον ‘Anyone who will not obey his master I will yoke with heavy straps—he certainly won’t be a young trace-horse high on barley’, tr. Sommerstein) as well as (cf. Ribbeck3, 293) Sen. Ag. 964-5 (Clytemnestra to Electra, indomita posthac virginis verba impiae | regina frangam ‘These unbridled words from an undutiful virgin – I shall break them later as queen’, tr. Fitch). Warmington (II.605) suggests that these could be the words of Aegisthus to Electra in Clytemnestra.

There seems to be no reason why this fragment could not belong to Accius’ Aegisthus: it could have for example belonged to the same scene as Accius Aegisthus F 6 TRF (neque fera hominum pectora | fragescunt, donec uim persensere imperi), with which it seemingly shares the common themes of tyranny and punishment.
doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarii (tr7).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
This fragment is preserved in Cicero’s De Oratore, a work which contains a large amount of references to or quotations of poetry (eighty-four in total, by Čulík-Baird’s reckoning, p. 238-40). It is cited at De Or. 3.166, a passage which falls within the long technical discussion by L. Licinius Crassus (RE Licinius 55) of ornatus with a focus on individual words (De or. 3.148-70: cf. Leeman–Pinkster V.176-231). More specifically, our fragment is cited in a section devoted to metaphor (translatio) and related devices (De or. 3.155-69), in order to illustrate an example of a metaphor developed over several continuous words whereby one says one thing and means another, a device which in Greek is known as ἀλληγορία ‘allegory’. The full two lines are preserved in Cicero, and the words ‘imperi insistent iugo’ are quoted by the fourth-century CE writer on rhetoric Iulius Victor (Ars rhetorica p. 84.2 Giomini–Celentano = 432.18 Halm).

In Cicero's De Oratore, this fragment is transmitted alongside another, Adesp. F 23 TRF (neque me patiar | iterum ad unum scopulum ut olim classem Achiuom offenderem), which Ribbeck in his first edition (p. 284) compares with two fragments of Pacuvius’ Chryses 58 (adiutamini et defendite) and 57 (est ibi sub eo saxo penitus strata harena ingens specus) TRF = F 68 and 66 Schierl = v. 98-99, F XI and XII Ribbeck1) and considers close to some lines of comedy, e.g. Pl. Most. 677, Merc. 197, Ter. Phorm. 689.

lines.text: ego sum Orestes :: immo enimvero ego sum, inquam, Orestes!
ambo ergo †sunaneganum† precamur

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation:
⟨He?⟩ seats ⟨himself⟩ upon the royal chair,
And Clytaemnestra is next ⟨to him⟩; the thirds their daughters occupy
doc.differences:
TRF (3/8)
Warmington (5/8)
Ribbeck (6/8)
doc.commentary:
Clytaemestra: The name of the famous heroine appears in the manuscripts also with the variations Clytemestra and Clitemestra. The Latin transliterations suggested by the editors can be summarised in the form “Cly/u/it(a)em(n)stra”. As Spaltenstein (2008, 62) states, the absence of –mn– in the vast majority of the corrections should not surprise us because of the form Κλυταιμήστρα that we also find in the corpus of the Greek tragedies. The correct form of Clytaemnestra’s name has concerned both ancient and modern critics, with the two most prominent etymologies being Clytemestra (from κλυτός + μήδομαι, “renowned for her cunning”) and Clytemnestra (from κλυτός + μνηστεύω, “renowned for her suitor/wooing”). In her dissertation on the figure of Clytaemnestra (2020), Simas draws upon passages from the Odyssey to examine etymological evidence for each one of the two forms and reaches a very insightful conclusion. Both forms are equally crucial for her role in the poem by creating connections with Penelope, either to highlight her own ambiguous μῆτις, or to contrast her fidelity (64-65).

Pius punctuates with a colon after Clytaemestra. Such a choice would render her the subject of conlocat. The following tertias, however, illustrates three parties, therefore the commas following regias and iuxtim seem crucial for the distinction between them.

iuxtim: ‘next to’. According to Nonius (185 L.) it is used instead of the later form, iuxta. While on first glance it might seem archaistic, “it must have been nothing notable to Livius, […] as the first adverbs in –tim were accusatives crystallised in this function” (Spaltenstein 2008, 62)[own translation from French].

natae: this word most likely indicates the two other daughters of Agamemnon and Clytaemnestra, Electra and Chrysothemis. Chrysothemis does not appear in Aeschylus’s Agamemnon (as also the rest of the children) nor in Choephoroi or in Seneca’s Agamemnon. Nevertheless, her presence is attested in Homer (Il. 9.145;287), in Euripides’ Phoenician Women and in Sophocles’ Electra, where she has a more active role on her sister’s side. If we adopt the edition’s punctuation we can imagine the royal chair attributed to the missing subject of conlocat, the second one attributed to Clytaemnestra and the third, tertias, being shared by the two sisters.
doc.reconstruction:
Considering the phrase sedes regias as well as the edition’s punctuation placing Clytaemnestra on the second chair, it is logical to assume that the missing subject of conlocat is the king, either Agamemnon or Aegisthus.

Terzaghi (1925, 32) suggests that these verses describe Aegisthus’ ascension to the throne in the form of an official ceremony, which we can imagine at a moment after Aegisthus and Clytaemnestra receive the urn with the (alleged) ashes of Orestes, the final threat against their reign. However, the presence of Electra and Chrysothemis on the third seats seems rather unnatural, even if we take into account their residing in the palace according to Sophocles’ Electra; and especially if we consider Electra’s strong hatred and objection towards the new king and queen. Moreover, Terzaghi connects these verses with frg. 5, iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili, in which Aegisthus asks Clytaemnestra if she is content with the sight of Orestes’ ashes. Such a ceremonial ascension scene appears nowhere in the former literary tradition and even if we examine more closely Sophocles’ Electra instead of Aeschylus, when Clytaemnestra receives the urn, Aegisthus is absent from the palace and returns only after her death. Overall, Terzaghi’s interpretation is strongly based on the assumption that Aegisthus plays a protagonistic role, a suggestion that we should examine with great caution.

A far more plausible and generally accepted interpretation (Warmington. Spaltenstein, Aricó) attributes these words to Cassandra, who is describing the murder of Agamemnon. Apart from the fact that it is much more logical to imagine Agamemnon taking a seat next to Clytaemnestra and his daughters during the welcoming banquet before his murder, Warmington and Spaltenstein identify a strong parallel with the murder scene of Seneca’s Agamemnon, where Cassandra is describing his demise in a state of prophetic trance (872 sqq). The dissimilar murder scene in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon further supports this parallelism, as Agamemnon is murdered in the bathtub. Even if the comparison with a subsequent parallel seems inadequate for the conclusion above, Agamemnon’s murder during a banquet is also familiar from Homer (Od. 4.529-536), where Aegisthus invites him upon his arrival for dinner in the palace and kills him. That way Livius’ introduction of the two daughters in the “murder banquet” would be much more functional by adding to the dramatic effect of the scene. Warmington expresses doubts regarding Cassandra’s trance state. By employing a more rational approach, he suggests that she is describing from a distance what she sees happening inside the palace. Such a proposal would not be improbable and is certainly scenically possible (cf. Sen. HF. 1022 sqq.; Plaut. Bacch. 832sqq.), but the figure of Cassandra herself seems to naturally presuppose a state of divine delirium (Spaltenstein 2008, 58).

Bibliography

Aricó, G. 1979. Sull’ Aegisthus di Livio Andronico, in Studi di poesia latina, stud. coll. in hon. A. Traglia, vol. 1, Rom, 3-9.
Pius, G. B. (ed). 1511. Que hoc libro continentur ... Nonii Marcelli ... Ad filium De verborum proprietate Compendium, Milan.
Simas, A. E. 2020. Killer Queen: Clytemnestra as Goddess, Heroine, and Monster. University of Washington.
Spaltenstein, F. 2008. Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Livius Andronicus. Bruxelles.
Terzaghi, N. 1925. Studi sull’ antica poesia latina. Due tragedie di Livio Andronico, 1, Equos Troianus, 2, Aegisthus. AAT 60, 660-674.
Warmington, E. H. (ed). 1936-1940. Remains of Old Latin, 4 voll. London.
