doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: there is no holy community, no trustworthiness in power
doc.differences:
TrRF: 150 (frg. inc. )
Jocelyn: 320 (frg. inc.
Warmington: 402-403 (frg. inc.)
Vahlen2: 404-405 (frg. inc.)
Ribbeck2/3: 381-382 (frg. inc.)

Warmington, Vahlen, Ribbeck: ......nulla sancta societas | nec fides regni est.
doc.apparatus: The line has been transmitted in two texts, Cicero's De Republica (1.49) and his De Officiis (1.26). The two quotations differ from each other as to the word order. There are textual issues resulting from the different word orders.
doc.commentary:
societas] While this word appears many times in Cicero, it is not found frequently in earlier texts: aside from this fragment, it is only transmitted in the one remaining fragment of a speech by the elder Cato, De Falsis Pugnis, which he made against Q. Minucius Thermus, accusing him of inventing battles in his Ligurian campaign in order to win a triumph. See further in the reconstruction.

Metre: As a fragmentum incertum, no editors have included this fragment with the fragments of Ennius, given that the Ciceronian quotations give no indication of which Ennian play it might belong to. For that matter, the scansion of the line is unclear. If taken as a single line, the words as transmitted in the order Cicero gives them are unmetrical, although various transpositions have been suggested, most focussed on moving regni to avoid elision with est which is the blemish on an the otherwise recognisably (offset) line of an iambic senarius. On the other hand, Bothe suggested a line break after societas, rendering the words the end and beginning of two trochaic septenarii. This would give a double resolution in the final metron, which would be a little unusual, when the four shorts are more easily accommodated within the middle of the line of an iambic senarius. Some kind of iambo-trochaic line is likely, but it could well be that Cicero has jumbled or adapted the word order, or added est.
doc.reconstruction:
Only a few commentators are confident enough to even suggest this fragment belongs to Ennius’ Thyestes, much less to hazard a guess as to the line’s speaker or its context in the play. Such a attribution was cautiously offered by Bothe and followed with similar caution by Ribbeck in all three versions of his edition, though in his 1875 monograph Ribbeck displays considerably more confidence. La Penna thought it refers to the relationship between Atreus and Thyestes (La Penna (1977) 15). As a generalised comment on a political situation, almost any character or the chorus could be imagined voicing the line. With respect to the metrical structure, which allows for both a iambic (Vossius) or trochaeic (Heusinger, Bothe, Ribbeck2) metre (see commentary), it seems to be more probable that the line was spoken by one of the dramatis personae.

The line itself seems echoed in Seneca’s Thyestes. The satelles, Atreus’ servant, opines ubi non est pudor / nec cura iuris sanctitas pietas fides, / instabile regnum est (Sen. Thy. 215-7, ‘where there is no shame or care for law, or reverence or piety or trust, / the throne totters’). But then this parallel has prompted the attribution of frg. 150 in the first place (similarly with frg. 163 inc. and the parallel with Accius’ Atreus), so arguments will inevitably involve an element of circularity. The key question is whether there is a tradition in plays based on the Thyestes myth of critiquing the endurance of values such as societas and fides under a tyrannical figure such as Atreus, or whether we are taking quotations such as frgg. 150 and 163 which could also belong to other plays and contexts in Ennius and in fact constructing such a tradition ourselves. La Penna highlights observations by Skutsch and Timpanaro of anti-tyrannical sentiments within the Annals, but the line in question (Ann. frg. 150 Sk, et qui se sperat Romae regnare Quadratae?, probably concerning one of the would-be tyrants, Sp. Cassius, Sp. Maelius, or M. Manlius Capitolinus, of the early Republic, merely shows that an observation on regnum would not be out of place in a play by Ennius (cf. Timpanaro (1950), Skutch (1967), Skutch (1985) 306-307).

Ennius may possibly be alluding to Cato’s speech De falsis pugnibus. In a fragment of that speech, preserved by Aulus Gellius, Cato attacks Minucius for having the decemvirs whipped, an act that Cato says “no king has dared to do” and which is contrary to the fides maiorum:
quis hanc contumeliam, quis hoc imperium, quis hanc servitutem ferre potest? nemo hoc rex ausus est facere: eane fieri bonis, bono genere gnatis, boni consultis? ubi societas? ubi fides maiorum?. Here we have another instance from early Republican literature where the absence of societas and fides is connected with tyranny and cruelty, which certainly strengthen the attribution of the incertum to Thyestes. Moreover, perhaps the Ennian line might be an allusion specifically to Cato’s speech: according to Nepos (Cato 1.4), Cato was the one to bring Ennius to Rome from Sardinia in 204BC, and according to Cicero (Sen., Cato regarded him as a familiaris. The very late source Aurelius Victor (4th cent) even states that it was Ennius who taught Cato Greek (Vir. Illu. 47.1), but this is likely an invention (see Gruen (1990) 107-9). And de falsis pugnis was a well-known speech in the contemporary period: Plautus in Truculentus alludes to it. The speech was delivered in 190BC, which is not too distant from the performance date of Thyestes in 169BC. Even if not a specific allusion, the connection between societas and fides and good governance was alive in the contemporary discourse.

The line could be a general statement criticising monarchy and either offered by the chorus (since the metre is unclear) or by one of the characters. For instance, it could be made by one of Thyestes’ children or Thyestes himself, while approaching Atreus’ palace (if the plot followed a similar pattern to Seneca’s Thyestes). But the line could be a specific critique or warning to Atreus - in which case it would be more analogous to the satelles’ first warning in Seneca’s Thyestes, quoted above.

lines.text: nulla sancta societas nec fides regni est

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: <22> concoquit
partem vapore flammae; tribuit in focos
veribus lacerta

doc.translation: He cooks one part in the steam of the flame, shares out the arms on spits above the fire.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 12 (12/20), 220-222:

220: concoquit

221: Partem uapore flammae, ueribus in foco

222: Lacerta tribuit.

Ribbeck1:

Fragment 12 (12/19), 220-222:

220: concoquit

221: Partem uapore flammae, tribuit ueribus

222: Lacerta . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . in focos

Warmington:

Fragment 187-189 (11/17):

187: concoquit

188: partem vapore flammae, veribus in focos

189: lacerta tribuit.

With the flame’s heat he boils a part, the arms

He puts about the hearths on spits.

Franchella:

Fragment 12 (12/22), 369-371:

369: concoquit

370: partem uapore flammae, tribuit in focos

371: ueribus lacerta.

Mette a bollire una parte al calore della fiamma, infilza negli spiedi sul fuoco i muscoli.

D’Antò:

Fragment 10 (10/18), 220-222:

220: …concoquit

221: partem vapore flammae, tribuit in focos

222: veribus lacerta

parte della carne cuoce al vapore della fiamma, i lacerti sistema sulla brace con gli spiedi

Dangel:

Fragment 13 (13/21), 51-53:

51: x – x – x – x – x concoquit

52: partem uapore flammae ; tribuit in foco

53: ueribus lacerta. – x – x – u x

Il en fait une partie à la vapeur brûlante ; il destine au foyer les bras qu’il embroche.
doc.apparatus:
2: flammae ] flammae Vossius apud Scriverium 1620, 140: flammam codd.

2: vapore flammae ] vapore flammae: vapore flammeo Vossius apud Scriverium 1620, 140, ‘audacior’

2: tribuit ] tribuit codd.: stridunt [sc. lacerta] Bergk 1835, 82

2: in focos ] in focos codd.: in focum Delrius 1593, I.178: in foco Bergk 1835, 82

2-3: tribuit in focos | ueribus lacerta sic disposuit Onions 1895, 259: tribuit ueribus lacerta in focos codd.: ueribus in foco | lacerta tribuit Ribbeck2, 164: tribuit ueribus | lacerta <...> in focos Ribbeck1, 138

2 flammae: Vossius’ flammae has won virtually unanimous approval among later editors (though Bothe I.184 prints flammam, and I am unsure how he thinks the sentence should be construed).

2 in focos: Del Rio’s in focum seems designed to remove the plural focos, apparently on the assumption that there can only be one hearth, but the plural here can be understood as poetic.

2-3 tribuit in focos | ueribus lacerta: The difficulty in deciding the order of the words as they appear in the manuscripts (ueribus lacerta in focos) is caused by the need for a light syllable at the penultimate position in the line, as dictated by the rules of iambo-trochaic verse. Ribbeck’s solution of placing the word focus (with short o) in final position has been accepted; more recent editors have accepted the word order proposed by Onions, tribuit in focos | ueribus lacerta.
doc.commentary:
1-3: Ribbeck (1875, 453) identify allusions to this Accian fragment in Ovid’s account of the myth of Procne, Philomela, and Tereus in the Metamorphoses (6.412-721, esp. at 6.645 pars inde cauis exsultat aenis, | pars ueribus stridunt, ‘Part [sc. of Procne’s son Itys’ disemembered body] bubbles in brazen kettles, part sputters on spits’, transl. Miller–Goold 19773, I.333) and in Seneca’s version of the banquet in his Thyestes (765-70), which is told by a messenger:

haec ueribus haerent uiscera et lentis data

stillant caminis, illa flammatus latex

candente aeno iactat. impositas dapes

transiluit ignis inque trepidantes focos

bis ter regestus et pati iussus moram

inuitus ardet. stridet in ueribus iecur…

Some of the flesh is stuck on spits, and sits dripping over slow burners; other parts are tossed about by kindled water in a boiling cauldron. The fire leaps past the food placed on it; though forced back again and again onto the trembling hearth and commanded to stay in place, it burns grudgingly. The liver hisses on the spit… (Transl. Fitch 20182, I.299).

The Senecan parallel is noted by Marchesi (1908, 87). Dangel (1995, 280) suggests that this fragment is also imitated by Lucretius, 5.1102-3 inde cibum coquere ac flammae mollire vapore | sol docuit, ‘After that the sun taught them [sc. early humans] to cook food and to soften it by the heat of flames’ (transl. Rouse–Smith 19922, 465).

2: vapore…focos: according to Dangel (1995, 280), these words are chosen by the messenger so as to express that Atreus is preparing the meal in accordance with the customs of a ritual sacrifice. Focus does indeed properly denote the divine fire of the home’s hearth (TLL VI.1.988.1- OLD s.v. 1 ‘a domestic hearth or fireplace, esp. that in the atrium, centre of the worship of the Lar familiaris’). Dangel argues that, since the hearth is sacred to the Lares, the gods of the household, all meals prepared in this space are sacred. Atreus’ actions constitute a severe perversion of normal ritual practice, because he applies to the remains of human victims the ritual practices customarily adopted when cooking the flesh of sacrificial animal: boiling his nephews’ entrails (indeed Cicero mentions the victims’ uiscera explicitly at Tusc 4.77), and grilling their arms on the hearth’s fire. The TLL seems wrong to say that the poetic plural is found only from Vergil onwards (TLL VI.1.987.82-3, cf. Verg. Georg. 1.175 suspensa focis explorat robora fumus, incorrectly referenced as ‘1.75’ in OLD), as Accius’ use does in fact seem to be a poetic plural (Del Rio’s attempt to normalise the expression by emending focos to focum implies that he found the plural odd). Baldarelli (2004, 238) identifies parallels for the two-stage process of boiling some parts of human remains and roasting others in Herodotus’ account of Astyages cooking the remains of the son of Harpagus before serving them to the father (esp. 1.119.3 σφάξας αὐτὸν καὶ κατὰ μέλεα διελὼν τὰ μὲν ὤπτησε τὰ δὲ ἥψησε τῶν κρεῶν ‘[Astyages] cut his [Harpagus’ son’s] throat and tearing him limb from limb roasted some and boiled some of the flesh’, transl. Godley 1920, I.155) and in Odysseus’ account of Polyphemus preparing a feast with the remains of his companions in Euripides’ Cyclops (402-404, καὶ διαρταμῶν | λάβρῳ μαχαίρᾳ σάρκας ἐξώπτα πυρί, | τὰ δ᾿ ἐς λέβητ᾿ ἐφῆκεν ἕψεσθαι μέλη, ‘Then butchering them with a fierce blade he roasted their fleshy parts in the fire and put their arms and legs in the cauldron to boil’, transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, I.105). She also identifies (p. 241) allusions to Accius’ lines in two passages of Ovid, the first in the description of the cannibalistic feast prepared for the gods by Lycaon in Book 1 (Met. 1.228-9 atque ita semineces partim feruentibus artus | mollit aquis, partim subiecto torruit igni, ‘some parts of him [Lycaon’s victim, a Molossian hostage] still warm with life, he [Lycaon] boiled, and others he roasted over the fire’, transl. Miller–Goold 19773, I.19) and in the Procne, Philomela, and Tereus episode in Book 6 (Met. 6.645-6, quoted above).

2 tribuit: The OLD’s explanation (s.v. 1) of this use of the word as ‘to share out, apportion; to divide (into categories, headings, etc.)’ does not seem entirely satisfactory.
doc.reconstruction: According to Ribbeck (1875, 453), this fragment, as well as F 113 TRF (epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor) belong to an eye-witness account of the banquet, which was probably not shown on stage but rather described, probably by a messenger. Dangel (1995, 280) connects this fragment with F inc. 434 TRF (ariuga). That Accian fragment consisting of a single word, denoting a female altar-companion (the masculine form being ariugus), is preserved by Varro, who explains that the entrails (exta) of such sacrificial victims are cooked in a pot rather than on a spit, and notes that Accius uses the word, without however specifying in which work. Cicero mentions specifically that the entrails (uiscera) of Thyestes’ sons are served to their father (Tusc. 4.77), and so, if we hypothesise that the pars of the victims described in F 108 TRF (i.e. concoquit | partem vapore flammae), then it is possible that Atreus used the term ariuga to describe the human victims in this scene of the Atreus. It is unclear, however, why Atreus opted for the feminine noun ariuga, which denotes a female animal, rather than for the masculine noun ariugus, which denotes a male one. Baldarelli (2004, …) argues that Atreus’ preparation of the children’s flesh was depicted by Accius in terms evocative of a ritual sacrifice carried out in accordance with Roman norms. She suggests that this motif goes back to one of the plays about Atreus and Thyestes by Sophocles, which, as Lesky argued (1922-1923, 184-5) included a scene in which Atreus slaughtered the children of Thyestes after they had taken refuge as suppliants at the altar of Zeus (cf. [Apollod.] Epit. 2.13 ἐπὶ τὸν Διὸς βωμὸν καθεσθέντας ἱκέτας ἔσφαξε [sc. Ἀτρεύς], ‘[Atreus] slew them after they had sat down at the altar of Zeus as suppliants’).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius De compendiosa doctrina p. 210.30-32 Mercier = p. 310.37-40 Lindsay

LACERTI generis masculini. Neutri. Accius Atreo: ‘concoquit | partem vapore flammae, tribuit in focos | veribus lacerta’ (= Accius Atreus F 108 TRF)

This fragment, like F 107 TRF (ego incipio…), is preserved in Book 3 of Nonius’ De compendiosa doctrina, entitled ‘De indiscretis generibus’ (‘On undistinguishable grammatical genders’). It is cited in order to show that the word the second-declension masculine noun lacertus (‘the arm from shoulder to elbow, the upper arm’, OLD s.v. lacertus1) is sometimes found declined as a neuter noun, like in this fragment of Accius, in which the neuter accusative plural form lacerta is found. Lindsay (1901, 62) identifies the source from which Nonius takes this quotation as ‘Accius ii’, which he refers to as source 8 (‘a second volume of Accius, containing (more or less in this order): Epigoni, Meleager, Aeneadae aut Decius, Stasiastae vel Tropaeum Liberi, Athamas, Clytaemestra, Bacchae, Neoptolemus, Erigona, Nyctegresia, Andromeda, Atreus, Phinidae, Agamemnonidae’, p. 8).

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius (the first and third lines are incomplete, the second line complete).

lines.text: [NUNTIUS]: Epularum fictor, scelerum fratris delitor.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: – u ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam.

doc.translation: Where I may not hear the deeds or the infamy of the descendants of Pelops.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment inc. inc. 64, v. 119:

119: .  .  ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam.

Ribbeck1: 

Fragment inc. inc. 64, v. 118-119:

118: euolem,

119: Vbi nec Pelopidarum nomen nec facta aut famam audiam.

Ribbeck2:

Fragment inc. inc. 64, v. 119:

119: Vbi nec Pelopidarum nomen nec facta aut famam audiam.

Warmington:

Fragment inc. inc. 73:

73: Ubi nec Pelopidarum nomen nec facta aut famam audiam.

Where I may not hear the name or the deeds or the disrepute of the house of Pelops.
doc.apparatus:
No textual issues to report.

The line is quoted, either in part or in full, on five different occasions by Cicero, on almost all occasions somewhat differently:

(i) Cicero Epistulae ad familiares 7.28.2 (= 200 Shackleton Bailey = a. 46, C30 Marinone–Malaspina): ubi nec Pelopdiarum

(ii) Cicero Epistulae ad Atticum 14.12.2 (= 366 Shackleton Bailey = a. 44, C15 Marinone–Malaspina): ubi nec Pelopidarum

(iii) Cicero Epistulae ad familiares 7.30.1 (= 265 Shackleton Bailey = a. 44, C1 Marinone–Malaspina): ubi nec Pelopidarum nomen nec facta audiam

(iv) Cicero Epistulae ad Atticum 15.11.3 (= 389 Shackleton Bailey = a. 44, C43 Marinone–Malaspina): ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam

(v) Cicero Philippicae 13.49 (= a. 43, B1 Marinone–Malaspina): nec facta nec nomen audiat

(The letters are here presented in chronological order according to the chronology by Marinone–Malaspina 20042).

Scriverius–Vossius (1620, 178) print: ubi nec Pelopidarum nomen, nec facta audiam (in the section entitled ‘Incertorum tragicorum reliquiae’).

In his first edition (p. 215), Ribbeck prints euolem ‘I shall fly’ in a line preceding the fragment: he writes in his notes that he has retrieved this word from the citation context of Cic. Att. 15.11.3 tamen nunc eo minus, evolare hinc idque quam primum, ‘ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam’, ‘I am now all the more determined to fly from here, and as soon as I possibly can, ‘where nevermore of Pelops’ line I’ll hear the deeds or fame’ (transl. Shackleton Bailey). He reports that Bothe had suggested the following text: euolem ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam, but Ribbeck says that he is unsure whether this text abides by the principle of concinnitas (‘nescio an concinnius sit’). In the text of Bothe’s edition, however (I.229), the text is printed as follows: ubi nec Pelopidarum nomen, nec facta aut famam audiam. Like Bothe, Ribbeck also prints the nouns nomen, facta, and famam (nomen nec facta is the text at Fam. 7.30.1, facta neque famam at Att. 15.11.3, and facta nec nomen at Phil. 13.49) together within a single complete trochaic senarius (cf. Ribbeck2, ‘Conspectus metrorum’, p. 367). In his second edition (p. 252), Ribbeck decides to remove euolem from the text of the fragment (‘uerbo num poeta usus sit incertum: olim in antecedentis uersiculi clausula euolem posueram’), but keeps unchanged the text of the second line of the fragment. In his third edition, Ribbeck changes his mind, deciding to remove the word nomen and to posit a lacuna in the first foot of the fragment: …ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam.
doc.commentary: famam: for negative connotations of the word, see OLD s.v. 1b ‘malicious report, slander’ and 6b ‘ill-repute, notoriety’, with examples of each respective sense in Naevius Danae F 8 TRF (desubito famam tollunt, siquam solam uidere in uia, ‘they suddenly raise a scandal if they have seen a woman alone in the street’, transl. Maltby–Slater, FRL VI.267) and in Ennius Achilles (Aristarchi) F 8 TRF (summam tu tibi | pro mala uita famam extolles, pro bona partam gloriam. | male uolentes famam tollunt, bene uolentes gloriam, ‘You will raise the greatest notoriety for yourself in response to a bad life, glory won in response to a good one. Those with bad intentions raise notoriety, those with good intentions glory’, transl. Goldberg–Manuwald, FRL II.11).
doc.reconstruction: Ribbeck (1875, 455) argues that this fragment belongs to a monologue by Thyestes after he has eaten his sons. He argues that these lines were pronounced at the end of the monologue, preceded by, respectively, F 104.1-2 TRF (ipsus hortatur me frater, ut meos malis miser | manderem natos) and F 109 TRF (egone Argivum imperium adtingam aut Pelopis digner domo? | quo me ostendam? quod templum adeam? qu[a]em ore funesto adloquar?). His interpretation is endorsed by Mette (1964, 116). Bothe, however, had attributed it to Accius’ Pelopidae (F 8 Bothe, I.229). Haley (2021b) argues that this fragment is not from a tragedy at all, but rather from an Atellane farce: she suggests that it belongs to an Atellane farce entitled Atreus by Pomponius of Bononia. This is a play to which it has recently been argued that a fragment traditionally believed to belong to the Imperial tragic dramatist Pomponius Secundus’ Atreus (Pomponius Secundus Atreus F 1 TRF) also belongs (Welsh 2013, 276).
doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarius (with a trochee missing at the beginning of the line).

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Epistulae ad Atticum 14.12.2 (= 366 Shackleton Bailey):

Nobiscum hic perhonorifice et peramice Octavius. quem quidem sui Caesarem salutabant, Philippus non, itaque ne nos quidem; quem nego posse <esse> bonum civem. ita multi circumstant, qui quidem nostris mortem minitantur, negant haec ferri posse. quid censes cum Romam puer venerit, ubi nostri liberatores tuti esse non possunt? <qui> quidem semper erunt clari, conscientia vero facti sui etiam beati; sed nos, nisi me fallit, iacebimus. itaque exire aveo ‘ubi nec Pelopidarum’, (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.) inquit. haud amo vel hos designatos, qui etiam declamare me coegunt, ut ne apud aquas quidem acquiescere liceret. sed hoc meae nimiae facilitatis; nam id erat quondam quasi necesse, nunc, quoque modo se res habet, non est item.

Octavius is with me here – most respectful and friendly. His followers call him Caesar, but Philippus does not, so neither do I. My judgement is that he cannot be a good citizen. There are too many around him. They threaten death to our friends and call the present state of things intolerable. What do you think they will say when the boy comes to Rome, where our liberators cannot go safe? They have won eternal glory, and happiness too in the consciousness of what they did; but for us, if I am not mistaken, there is only humiliation ahead. So I long to be away ‘Where nevermore of Pelops’ lines…’, (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.) as the poet says. Nor do I greatly care for these Designates of ours, who furthermore have made me give lessons in oratory, so that I am not allowed to rest even here at the waters. But this comes of my too easy-going nature. Time was when that sort of thing was more or less necessary, but now, stand things as they may, it’s not the same.

(Text and transl. Shackleton Bailey 1965-1970, VI.26-7).

The letter was written at Puteoli and is dated to 22 April 44 BCE.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Epistulae ad Atticum 15.11.3 (= 389 Shackleton Bailey):

Ne multa, nihil me in illo itinere praeter conscientiam meam delectavit. non enim fuit committendum ut ille ex Italia prius quam a me conventus esse discederet. hoc dempto munere amoris atque offici sequebatur ut mecum ipse ‘ἡ δεῦρ’ ὁδός σοι τί δύναται νῦν, θεοπρόπε;’ (= Adesp. F 130 K.-A. = F 1232 Kock) prorsus dissolutum offendi navigium vel potius dissipatum. nihil consilio, nihil ratione, nihil ordine. itaque etsi ne antea quidem dubitavi, tamen nunc eo minus, evolare hinc idque quam primum, ‘ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.).

In short, nothing in my visit gave me any satisfaction except the consciousness of having made it. It would not have been right to let him leave Italy without seeing me. Apart from this obligation of affection and duty I could only say to myself ‘Prophet, what signifies your journey now?’ I found the ship going to pieces, or rather its scattered fragments. No plan, no thought, no method. Hence, though I had no doubts even before, I am now all the more determined to fly from here, and as soon as I possibly can, ‘where nevermore of Pelops’ line I’ll hear the deeds or fame’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.).

(Text and transl. Shackleton Bailey 1965-1970, VI.98-101).

This letter addressed to Atticus was written around 7 June 44 BCE, possibly in Antium. Cicero has recently received the news that he has been nominated as Legate by Dolabella.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Epistulae ad familiares 7.30.1 (= 265 Shackleton Bailey):

Ego vero iam te nec hortor nec rogo ut domum redeas; quin hinc ipse evolare cupio et aliquo pervenire

‘Ubi nec Pelopidarum nomen nec facta audiam’. (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.)

incredibile est quam turpiter mihi facere videar qui his rebus intersim. ne tu videris multo ante providisse quid impenderet tum cum hinc profugisti. quamquam haec etiam auditu acerba sunt, tamen audire tolerabilius est quam videre.

Not so. I no longer urge you or ask you to come home. On the contrary, I am anxious to take wing myself and go to some place ‘where nevermore of Pelops’ line I’ll hear the name or deeds.’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.) You cannot imagine the sense of personal dishonour I feel at living in the Rome of today. Farsighted indeed you turn out to have been when you fled this country. Although the happenings here are painful enough in the report, yet it is more tolerable to hear of them than to see them.

(Text: Shackleton Bailey 2004, II.127; transl. Shackleton Bailey 2001, II.447-9)

The letter is addressed to Curio and was written at Rome towards the beginning of January 44 BCE.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Epistulae ad familiares 7.28.2 (= 200 Shackleton Bailey):

Sed quod tu, cui licebat, pedibus es consecutus, ut ibi esses ‘ubi nec Pelopdiarum’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.) (nosti cetera), nos idem prope modum consequimur alia ratione. cum enim salutationi nos dedimus amicorum, quae fit hoc etiam frequentius quam solebat quod quasi avem albam videntur bene sentientem civem videre, abdo me in bibliothecam. Itaque opera efficio tanta quanta fortasse tu senties; intellexi enim ex tuo sermone quodam, cum meam maestitiam et desperationem accusares domi tuae, dicere te ex meis libris animum meum desiderare.

However, what you have done, and were free to do, by locomotion, i.e. to live ‘where nevermore of Pelops’ line …’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.) (you know how it goes on), I am achieving more or less in a different way. After receiving my friends’ morning calls, which are in even greater numbers than of yore because they seem to see a loyal citizen as a bird of good omen, I bury myself in my library. And so I produce books – of their importance you will perhaps judge; for from a conversation you had with me at your house, when you were taking me to task for my gloom and despondency, I understood you to say that you wondered what had become of the spirit you saw in my writings.

(Text: Shackleton Bailey 2004, II.40; transl. Shackleton Bailey 2001, II.233)

The letter is addressed to Curio and is written at Rome, seemingly in August 46 BCE. Shackleton Bailey (2004, II.357) notes that the work that Cicero refers to in this letter is the Orator, having earlier that year already produced Brutus, Paradoxa Stoicorum, Cato, and perhaps Partitiones oratoriae.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Philippicae 13.49:

Hanc ego epistulam, patres conscripti, non quo illum dignum putarem, recitaui, sed ut confessionibus ipsius omnia patefacta eius parricidia uideretis. Cum hoc pacem M. Lepidus, uir ornatissimus omnibus et uirtutis et fortuane bonis, si haec uideret *** denique aut uellet <aut> fiere posse arbitraretur? ‘Prius unde flamma[m]’, (= Adesp. F 87 TRF) ut ait poeta nescio quis, prius denique omnia quam aut cum Antoniis res publica aut cum re publica Antonii redeant in gratiam. monstra quaedam ista et portenta sunt et prodigia rei publicae. Moueri sedibus huic urbi melius atque in alias, si fieri possit, terras demigrare, unde Antoniorum ‘nec facta nec nomen audiat’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.), quam illos, Caesaris uirtute eiectos, Bruti retentos, intra haec moenia uidere. optatissimum est uincere; secundum est nullum casum pro dignitate et libertate patriae non ferendum putare quod reliquum est, non est tertium, sed postremum omnium, maximam turpitudinem suscipere uitae cupiditate.

I have read out this letter, Members of the Senate, not because I thought the author of it worthy of being heeded, but to let you see all his treasons laid bare by his own confessions. Would Marcus Lepidus, so richly endowed with all the blessings of nature and fortune, wish peace with this man or even think it possible if he were hearing these words of Antonius? ‘Sooner fire with water,’ (= Adesp. F 87 TRF) as some poet or other has expressed it, sooner anything in fact, than the Republic will be reconciled with the Antonii or the Antonii with the Republic. They are, as it were, monsters and portents and prodigies of the Republic. It would be better for this city to be moved from her foundations and to migrate to other lands, if that were possible, where she would ‘hear neither deeds nor name’ (= Adesp. F 83 TRF part.) of the Antonii, than to see once more inside her walls men who were thrown out by Caesar’s prowess and held back from returning by Brutus’. Best of all is victory; second-best is to think no misfortune unbearable, if it be for our native land’s dignity and freedom. That leaves no third-best, only the worst of all, to plumb the lowest depth of dishonor out of a craving for life.

(Text: Fedeli 1986; transl. Shackleton-Bailey, rev. Ramsey–Manuwald 2010, II.285-7).

Philippic 13, an invective speech levelled at Mark Antony, was delivered in the Senate on 20 March 43 BCE (Marinone–Malaspina 20042, 255). This passage falls towards the very end of the speech.

lines.text: sollemnitusque deo litat laudem lubens
Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae
fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent.

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: sed quid? tonitru turbida torvo
concussa repente aequora caeli
sensimus sonere!

doc.translation: But what? We have felt the plains of the sky resound, shaken suddenly, disturbed by fierce thunder!
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 13 (13/20), 223-225:

223: Sed quid tonitru turbida toruo

224: Concussa repente aequora caeli

225: Sensimus sonere?

Warmington:

Fragment 183-5 (9/17):

[CHORUS]: 

183: Sed quid tonitru turbida torvo

184: concussa repente aequora caeli

185: sensimus sonere?

But why perceived we suddenly heaven’s plains

Grown stormy, rocking, roaring with grim thunder?

Franchella:

Fragment 13 (13/22), 372-374:

372: sed quid tonitru turbida toruo

373: concussa repente aequora caeli

374: sensimu’ sonere?

Ma come mai abbiamo sentito all’improvviso rimbombare la volta del cielo scossa dal truce rintronar del tuono?

D’Antò:

Fragment 11 (11/18), 223-225:

223: sed quid tonitru turbida torvo

224: concussa repente aequora caeli

225: sensimus sonere? …

ma come mai che da sordo tuono squassata e rabbuiata abbiama sentito all’improvviso rimbombar la distesa del cielo?

Dangel:

Fragment 14 (14/21), 54-56:

54: Sed quid? tonitru turbida toruo

55: concussa repente aequora caeli

56: sensimus sonere ! (–) u u – x

Mais qu’est-ce ? Troublées par un tonnerre terrible, dans un séisme soudain, nous avons entendu résonner les étendues célestes !
doc.apparatus:
1: torvo ] torvo ed. 1476: torbo uel turbo codd.

2: sonere ] sonere codd. plerique: sonare P CA (= fons codicum Paris. 7666, Lugd., Bamb., Turic.)

The manuscripts’ turbo, which usually denotes a tornado or whirlpool but can also be used in a metaphorical sense of mental disturbance (OLD s.v. turbo2), was modified to toruo in the 1476 edition of Nonius (printed in Venice by Nicolaus Jenson), because a nominative turbo would be difficult to construe given that the main verb (sonere) is in the plural and depends on aequora. The adjective toruo (‘grim, fierce’), more commonly used of persons or facial expressions (OLD s.v. toruus 1-2), qualifies tonitru (‘fierce thunder’).
doc.commentary:
1: tonitru turbida torvo: Lana (1959, 307 n. 1) suggests that the alliteration in t, as well as the alliteration in s in the third line (sensimus sonore), creates ‘un’armonia imitante il fenomeno che viene descritto’. On toruo, see ‘Fragment pparatus criticus’.

2: aequora caeli: aequor denotes properly of extended plains of land (TLL I.1022.84-1023-60) or of the sea (TLL I.1023.71-1026.28). This Accian usage of the term to denote the ‘plains’ of the sky seems fairly exceptional (cf. TLL I.1023.61-70, ‘de variis rebus’), although cf. Pl. Trin. 834 caeruleos per campos, ‘throughout the azure plains’ (transl. De Melo 2011, V.205). On sky imagery in Republican verse, see Timpanaro 1996b.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck (1875, 454) attributes these lines to the chorus, which he suspects may be composed of Mycenean citizens like in Seneca’s Thyestes. He argues that the thunder described in these lines probably occurs as a result of the sun changing its course in horror at the events that have occurred. He compares in particular Sen. Thy. 992-5, a passage which falls during the banquet when Thyestes, after drinking a cup full of his children’s blood which he believes to be wine, suddenly feels an earthquake and the world descending into darkness:

quid hoc? magis magisque concussi labant

convexa caeli; spissior densis coit

caligo tenebris noxque se in noctem abdidit:

fugit omne sidus.

What is this? More and more heaven’s vault is shaking and lurching. Darkness gathers more thickly amid dense shadows, and night buries itself in night; every star is in flight. (Transl. Fitch 20182, II.315).

And also the fourth choral ode at Sen. Thy. 789-884 in which the chorus respond to the earth suddenly descending into total darkness by addressing a prayer to Jupiter, expressing their fear that the darkness may have been caused by gods resuming their conflict with the Giants. For the argument that this one single event is described at a simultaneous moment in dramatic time by the messenger (Sen. Thy. 776-88), the chorus (789-884), Atreus (892-5), and finally Thyestes (992-5), see Shelton 1975, 267 n. 9. Lana (1959, 306-7) argues that this fragment belongs to a choral ode which follows the scene depicting the reconciliation between the two brothers (cf. F 110 TRF, ne cum tyranno quisquam epulandi gratia | accumbat mensam aut eandem vescatur dapem). In this ode, the chorus expresses its joy at the reconciliation of the two brothers and its terror at a series of mysterial celestial phenomena. Dangel (1995, 280) rejects Ribbeck’s argument that the thunder accompanies the sun’s reversal of its course, arguing that there is no evidence that the Accius included the Sun’s reversal in the play. She thus rejects the theory, notably adopted by Franchella, that a scholion to a line of Ovid’s Ibis 429 reports that Accius tells the story of the Sun’s reversal on account of his horror at the events that occurred at the banquet (see on T 25 TRF). Baldarelli (2004, 244), however, argues that, had Accius adopted the version of the myth according to which the reversal of the sun occurred following the theft of the golden lamb, then Atreus would have mentioned this celestial event in the passage in which he describes the adultery of Aerope and theft of the lamb (F 104.11-15 TRF). She therefore suggests that Accius followed the version according to which the sun reversed its course in reaction to the horrors of the banquet, although she herself acknowledges that this is her argument is made from silence.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius De compendiosa doctrina p. 505.4-11 = 811-812.4-11 Lindsay:

SONERE inde tractum est. Lucretius lib. III: ‘caligare oculos, sonere auris, succidere artus.’ (= Lucr. 3.156) Accius Neoptolemo: ‘atque adeo valvas sonere sensi regias’ (= Accius Neoptolemus F 301 TRF) idem Atreo: ‘sed quid tonitru turbida torvo | concussa repente aequora caeli | sensimu’ sonere?’ (= Accius Atreus F 111 TRF)

This fragment is preserved in Book 10 of Nonius’ De compendiosa doctrina, entitled ‘De mutatis coniugationibus’ (‘On changes in conjugation’). It is quoted as an example of sonere (‘to make a noise (of any kind), sound’, OLD s.v. 1) as a third-conjugation verb, which is mostly attested in Early Latin, the first-conjugation form sonare being more common in Classical Latin.

doc.metrical_analysis: The TRF scans the first two of these lines as anapaestic quaternarii (an4), the third as anapaestic quaternarius catalectic, or paroemiac (an4c), which is attested in nine other lines of the surviving Accian corpus (see TRF IV.760). Indeed, Soubiran (apud Dangel 1995, 281), noted that the third line of the fragment, sensimus sonere, can be scanned as a catalectic anapaestic dimeter, cf. Questa 2007, 446) or as an anapaestic dipody: both of these metres are suitable for the final line of a strophe.

lines.text: . . . in sedes conlocat se regias;
Clytaemestra iuxtim, tertias natae occupant

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: 1: ecquis hoc animadvortet? vincite

doc.translation:
Is there anyone who will punish this? Bind him (or: subdue him)

Or

Is there anyone who will take notice of this? Bind him (or: subdue him)
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 18 (18/20), 233 (certum):

233: Ecquis hoc animaduortet? uincite!

Ribbeck1:

Fragment 18 (18/19), 233 (certum):

233: Ecquis hoc animaduertit? uincite!

Warmington:

Fragment 196-8 (16/17) (certum):

[THYESTES]:

196: Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser

197: manderem natos!

[ATREUS]:

198: Ecquis hoc animadvortet? Vincite!

[THYESTES]:

Yes, my own brother – he exhorted me

To chew my children with my jaws – poor me!

[ATREUS]:

Will some one pay heed to this? Bind ye him!

Franchella:

Fragment 19 (19/22), 384 (certum):

394: ecquis hoc animaduortet? uincite [ – u – u

Che mai baderà a ciò? Legatelo!

D’Antò:

Fragment 16 (16/18), 233 (certum):

233: Ecquis hoc animaduortit? uincite < (–) x – x – u x >

nessuno punisce questo misfatto? Legatelo!

Dangel:

Fragment 19 (19/21), 64 (certum):

64: Ecquis hoc animaduertet ? Vincite ! – x – u x

Quelqu’un va-t-il punir ce méfait ? Enchaînez-le !
doc.apparatus:
1: ecquis ] ecquis L (De orat.): e quis AH (De orat.): et quis X (= GKRV) (Tusc.): ecqui Geppert 1858, 114

1: animadvortet ] animadvortet AH (De orat.): animadvertet E (De orat.): animum advertit VOPR (De orat.): animadvertit (= GKRV) (Tusc.)

1: vincite ] vincite codd.: vindicet Baldarelli 2004, 262

The critical apparatus is based on Kumaniecki 19952 on Cic. De orat. 3.217-219 and Giusta 1984 on Cic. Tusc. 4.55.

The L manuscript of De oratore had the reading ecquis (‘is there anyone who?’, OLD s.v.) which at some stage in the M branch of the tradition was corrupted to e quis. The corruption to et quis found in the manuscrupts of the Tusculan Disputations is a particularly common one (TLL V.2.53.14-63). Geppert’s proposal, ecqui, is attested elsewhere in Republican drama (e.g. Plaut. Stich. 222 ecqui poscit prandio? ‘Does anyone want to buy them for the price of a lunch?’, the hanger-on Gelasimus trying to sell miscellaneous Greek goods, transl. De Melo 2011, V.41). The orthography of the verb animaduerto/-uorto, a compound formed animum/animos aduertere, is variable (see Winterbottom 1983, 37 on De orat. 1.121: ‘it is striking that animum advertere is transmitted as often as it is, and it would be quite reasonable to read that rather than animadvertere everywhere, whatever the manuscripts say’). What concerns us here is whether the verb is in the present or in the future tense, both being attested in the manuscripts. The future tense animaduortet is printed by Ribbeck (second and third editions), Warmington, Franchella, Dangel, and TRF; the present tense animaduortit is printed by Ribbeck (first edition) and D’Antò. D’Antò (1980, 289) defends the present tense by arguing that it suits the urgency of the command better than the future does; La Penna (1972, 362-3 n. 11) suggests that the question is a rhetorical one, and implies that future suits this better. On the meaning of the verb, see ‘Fragment commentary’. Baldarelli suggests changing uincite to uindicet ‘Let him take revenge…’ (‘Rachen soll er üben…’): This suggestion is based on the assumption that the end of Accius’ play was essentially similar to the end of Seneca’s Thyestes (see ‘Fragment reconstruction’).
doc.commentary:
1 ecquis: on ecquis and similar interrogative pronouns in early Latin, which introduce questions which can be answered either by a ‘yes’ or a ‘no’, see Bodelot 2023.


1 animadvortet: originally written, in early Latin, in two words, animum aduortere (cf. too animum aduorsaui at Pl. Rud. 306): Abraham (1885, 219) explains animum not as a true direct object in the accusative, but as an accusative of motion (sometimes called ‘accusative of goal’, Woodcock §5). Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 274) interprets the verb in a legalistic sense, cf. OLD s.v. animaduerto 8 (‘to deal with (actions) punitively, visit with punishment, proceed against; to inflict (capital) punishment on (persons)’), translating ‘chi mai giudicherà di questo eccidio? Legatelo!’ D’Antò (1980, 289) supports this interpretation of the verb, adducing parallels at Ter. An. 767 o facinus animaduortendum! ‘oh, crime that must be punished!’ (cf. too Ter. An. 156, ea primum ab illo animum advortenda iniuriast, ‘that will be a clear act of disobedience on his part which deserves to be punished’, transl. Barsby 1999, I.63); Cic. Rosc. Am. 116 atque ea sunt animaduertenda peccata maxime, quae difficillime praecauentur, ‘Besides, the offences which it is hardest to guard against are the ones which merit the strictest punishment’ (transl. Berry 2000, 45); Tac. Germ. 7 (on the customs of the people of Germania) ceterum neque animaduertere neque uincire, ne uerberare quidem nisi sacerdotibus permissum, ‘But anything beyond this – capital punishment, imprisonment, even flogging – is permitted only to the priests’, transl. Hutton, rev. Warmington 19702, 141). As Cipriani (1978, 218-19) points out, however, elsewhere in the surviving fragments of Accius, as well as in those of Ennius, the verb animadvertere/animadvortere is used in the sense of ‘to pay attention’: Accius Nyctegresia F 308 TRF, Ennius Achilles v. 14 Ribbeck3 (= Pl. Amph. 3, not in TRF) (although in all three cases the reading is animum aduortere), F Adesp. 32 TRF (= inc. inc. fab. v. 156/157 Ribbeck3, which Scaliger 1565, 110 had suggested belongs to Euripides’ Medea, cf. Medea exul v. 224-225 Ribbeck3 = F Adesp. 34 TRF). And, indeed, the Oxford Latin Dictionary understands the meaning of the word in this fragment of Accius as ‘to pay attention to, attend to, head (esp. w. implication of consequential action’ (s.v. 2).

1 vincite: Cipriani (1978, 219-21) interprets this as meaning not ‘to bind with chains’ but ‘to coronate’ (cf. uincla at Sen. Thy. 544, where Atreus puts a crown on Thyestes’ head before the banquet: imposita capiti uincla uenerando gere, ‘Wear this bond set on your venerable head’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.281) and uincire at Sen. Thy. 945-6, a drunken Thyestes relishing the feeling of being treated like a king: quis me prohibet flore decenti | uincire comam, prohibet, prohibet?, ‘Who prevents me from binding my hair with comely flowers, prevents me, prevents me?’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.311), but Zwierlein (1983, 123) argues that the context in which the fragment is quoted at Cic. De orat. 3.216-17 (to illustrate the role of anger in the play) makes this interpretation implausible. It should be pointed out that, if the word were to be scanned with a short -i- (i.e. uincĭte), which cannot be ruled out given the uncertainty of the metre of the line(s), then it would parse as a form of uinco ‘to vanquish, subdue’ and not uincio (‘to fasten with bonds, tie up’).
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck1-3 prints this fragment as a fragment of Atreus, i.e. fr. 18 (18/20), v. 233. He suggests (1875, 455) that it points to one final act of violence in the play, after all the horrors of the banquet: after Thyestes has finished singing a canticum in which he expresses his pain and grief (F 104.1-2 TRF, ipsus hortatur…; F 109 TRF, egone Argivum…; Adesp. F 83 TRF, ubi nec Pelopidarum…), he possibly utters the famous curse against Atreus (cf. Aesch. Ag. 1602 οὕτως ὀλέσθαι πᾶν τὸ Πλεισθένους γένος, ‘So perish all the race of Pleisthenes!’, transl. Sommerstein 2008, II.195) and perhaps also encouraged the chorus to rebel against Atreus (‘vielleicht sogar eine Aufforderung an den Chor, sich gegen ihn [Atreus] zu erheben’, p. 455). Atreus reacts with an order, possibly addressed to a chorus of Mycenean citizens, to bind the ‘open enemy’ (‘offene[r] Feind’) Thyestes in chains. D’Antò (1980, 280) considers this point by Ribbeck highly plausible. But Ribbeck believes that the fragment, as well as Accius inc. F 404 TRF (numquam istam imminuam…) and 413 TRF (multi iniqui...) and could also belong to Accius’ Pelopidae (1875, 456).

Warmington (II.390-1) interprets the fragment differently. He too prints this fragment as a fragment of the Atreus: he connects it with F 104.1-2 TRF (ipsus hortatur me frater, ut meos malis miser | manderem natos) so as to construct a single fragment, F 196-8 (16/17):

[THYESTES]:

Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser

manderem natos!

[ATREUS]:

Ecquis hic animadvortet? Vincite!

[THYESTES]:

Yes, my own brother – he exhorted me

To chew my children with my jaws – poor me!

[ATREUS]:

Will some one pay heed to this? Bind ye him!

This proposed reconstruction by Warmington seems to envision a scene in which Thyestes and Atreus are engaging with other characters (perhaps other attendees of the banquet? Citizens of Mycenae? Police guards?): Thyestes is trying to reveal to them the truth of the horrific crime that Atreus has committed, while Atreus disingenuously denies the accusation and orders a group of characters to seize and bind Thyestes. In his summary and critique of Ribbeck’s analysis, Lana (1959, 310-12) makes some new suggestions. He puts forward a new argument in favour of the theory (cf. Ribbeck 1875, 455) that Atreus is the speaker in F inc. 405 TRF (ecquis hoc animadvortet? vincite). Lana deduces this on the basis of two testimonies. The first is that of Cicero, who quotes the fragment at Tusc. 4.55, and notes: num aut egisse umquam iratum Aesopum aut scripsisse existimas iratum Accium? (‘Do you think that Accius was angry when he wrote this line, or that Aesopus was angry when he delivered it?’, transl. Graver 2002, 58). The second testimony is that of Plutarch, according to whom once, when the actor Aesopus was playing the role of Atreus, he knocked down with his sceptre a slave who happened to be running across the stage and killed him (Plut. Vit. Cic. 5.5). Lana argues that Aesopus must have been playing the main role, that of Atreus, and therefore that this incident occurred during a representation of Accius’ Atreus, and not that of another play, for instance Ennius’ Thyestes. It thus follows, on Lana’s account, that it was Atreus, played by Aesopus, who said the line preserved by Cicero at Tusc. 4.55 (= F inc. 405 TRF, ecquis hoc animadvortet? vincite). The overall conclusion that Atreus was indeed speaker of this line seems plausible enough, but there is a flaw in Lana’s argument. Lana seems to assume Atreus was the main character, but this assumption is not necessarily correct: indeed, if we compare Seneca’s Thyestes, although the play bears the title ‘Thyestes’, Atreus is the main character of that play. It thus seems hard to prove with any certainty that the play performed in the anecdote reported by Plutarch was indeed Accius’ Atreus. 

Lana’s own interpretation of the ending of the play is however entirely different. In Lana’s view (1959, 312-15), a key piece of information about how the play ended is to be found in a passage of Cicero’s first Philippic (1.33-4). In this passage, Cicero warns Mark Antony that if he behaves like a tyrant he will incur the wrath of the people and risk being assassinated. Quoting Atreus’ famous line from Accius’ play (oderint, dum metuant, F 103.4), Cicero seems to suggest that Mark Antony could face the same end as the character ‘in that play’ (in fabula), whom Lana takes to be Atreus. Thus, on Lana’s account, Accius’ Atreus ended with the death of the tyrant Atreus. This fragment, F inc. 405 TRF, plays a central role in Lana’s reconstruction of the end of the play. Lana builds on Ribbeck’s suggestion (1875, 455) that Thyestes urged the chorus to rebel against Atreus: whereas Ribbeck thinks that Thyestes’ attempt to foment political upheaval was unsuccessful and that Atreus swiftly had him bound in chains, Lana, on the contrary, thinks that Thyestes managed to convince the chorus to side with him and to arrest Atreus. In a desperate attempt to save himself, Lana imagines Atreus to have asked the chorus: ecquis hoc animadvortet?, ‘who will ever pay attention to that?’ (F inc. 405 TRF), before turning to another group of citizens whom he believes to be faithful to him and ordering them to bind Thyestes in chains (vincite!, F inc. 405 TRF). Lana also interprets two other fragments as belonging to this final scene depicting the downfall and punishment of the tyrant Atreus (F Adesp. 96 cur fugit fratrem? scit ipse!; and F inc. 404 TRF numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi!). Zwierlein 123 rejects Lana’s reconstruction, arguing that the fragment does not belong to the end of the play, and tentatively suggests another interpretation: the fragment could correspond to the part of the myth described at Hyg. Fab. 88.8, when Atreus has Thyestes thrown in prison after the latter’s return to Mycenae (quem Atreus in custodiam conici iussit).Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 274) suggests that the speaker is not Atreus, but Thyestes: having just recognised Atreus’ crime, Thyestes, who believes that no court of law, neither human nor divine, is competent to judge such a monstrous crime, orders that the murderer be seized immediately (‘pur non scorgendo un tribunale, né umano né divino, competente a giudicare un delitto sì efferato, ordina che l’assassino sia legato’). She translates the fragment: ‘chi mai giudicherà di questo eccidio? Legatelo!’ Reading uindicet rather than uincite (see ‘Apparatus criticus’), Baldarelli (2004, 261-2) argues that this fragment constitutes Thyestes’ call for Atreus to be punished (by a deity? or a descendant?) and falls at the end of the play. She suggests that Accius’ play might have ended in the same way as Seneca’s, and that, at the end of his play, Seneca perhaps alluded to the end of Accius’ play: Sen. Thy. 1110-1111 [THYESTES]: Vindices aderunt dei: | his puniendum vota te tradunt mea, ‘The gods of vengeance will come: my prayers consign you to them for punishment’, transl. Fitch 20182, II.327.
doc.metrical_analysis:
Iambo-trochaic (TRF). Mette (1964, 116) imagines this fragment as the surviving part of two truncated trochaic septenarii: 

<– x – x – x – x – x – x> [ATREUS]: ecquis hoc

animadvortit? vincite <(–) x – x – x – u x>

Soubiran (apud Dangel 1995, 282-3) identifies this fragment as a trochaic septenarius of uncertain rhythm on account of the word ecquis, which scans either as a trochee (– u) or as a pyrrhic (u u). If the latter, then an elision (uincit(e)) is likely.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero De oratore 3.217-219:

nullum est enim horum [sc. sonorum] generum, quod non arte ac moderatione tractetur. hi sunt actori, ut pictori, expositi ad variandum colores. aliud enim vocis genus iracundia sibi sumat, acutum, incitatum, crebro incidens:

Ipsus hortatur me frater ut meos malis miser

Manderem natos . . . . . . . . . . . . . (= Accius Atreus F 104.1-2 TRF)

et ea, quae tu dudum, Antoni, protulisti:

Segregare abs te ausus . . . . . (= Pacuvius F inc. 226.1 TRF, possibly from Teucer)

et:

Ecquis hoc animadvortet? Vincite . . . . . (= Accius F inc. 405 TRF)

et Atreus fere totus. aliud miseratio ac maeror, flexibile, plenum, interruptum, flebili voce:

Quo nunc me vortam? quod iter incipiam ingredi?

domum paternamne? anne ad Peliae filias? (= Adesp. F 25 TRF, possibly from Ennius’ Medea/ Medea exul)

et illa:

O pater, o patria, o Priami domus! (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.10 TRF)

et quae sequuntur:

Haec omnia videi inflammarei,

Priamo vi vitam evitarei. (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.15-16 TRF)

aliud metus, demissum et haesitans et abiectum:

Multis sum modis circumventus, morbo, exilio atque inopia

tum pavor sapientiam omnem mi exanimato expectorat;

mater terribilem minatur vitae cruciatum et necem,

quae nemo est tam firmo ingenio et tanta confidentia

quin refugiat timido sanguen atque exalbescat metu. (= Ennius Alcmeo F 12 TRF)

aliud vis, contentum, vehemens, imminens quadam incitatione gravitatis:

Iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum advenit

iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat.

maior mihi moles, maius miscendumst malum

qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam. (= Accius Atreus F 104.3-6 TRF)

The employment of each of these kinds [of sounds] falls under the regulation of art. They are at our disposal to be varied at will in delivery, just as colors are in painting. Anger requires the use of one kind of voice, high and sharp, excited, breaking off repeatedly:

He, my very brother, urges me, o wretch that I am, to mangle

My sons with my own jaws. (= Accius Atreus F 104.1-2 TRF)

Similarly in the lines that you quoted a while ago, Antonius:

Did you dare to let him be parted from you, (= Pacuvius F inc. 226.1 TRF, possibly from Teucer)

and so forth; also in this passage:

Will really no one punish this? Bind him…; (= Accius F inc. 405 TRF)

and thus almost the entire Atreus. Lamentation and grief require another kind of voice, wavering in pitch, sonorous, halting, and tearful: 

Where can I turn now? And what path must I choose to go?

To the house of my father? Or perhaps to the daughters of Pelias? (= Adesp. F 25 TRF, possibly from Ennius’ Medea/ Medea exul)

Likewise in:

O my father, o my country, o palace of Priam!... (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.10 TRF)

as well as in the passage that follows those lines:

All of these I have seen go up in flames, and

Violence tearing Priam’s life away. (= Ennius Andromacha aechmalotis F 23.15-16 TRF)

Fear again has another kind of voice, subdued, hesitating, and downcast:

I’m beset by many troubles: sickness, banishment, and want.

Dread outheartens all my sense from me, I’m totally terrified.

Mother menaces my life with terrible torture and with death.

No one’s spirit is so firm and no one’s confidence so great that

This wouldn’t make his blood flow back in fright and his face turn pale with fear. (= Ennius Alcmeo F 12 TRF)

Energy has yet another kind, intense, vehement, threatening, and with an earnest sort of excitement:

Again Atreus arrives to mount an assault on Atreus,

Again he now attacks me and disturbs my peace.

A greater mass of evil misery I must

Concoct whereby to quench and crush his cruel heart. (= Accius Atreus F 104.3-6 TRF)

[Text: Kumaniecki 19952; transl. May–Wisse 2001, 292-3.]

The lines of Accius’ Atreus corresponding to F 104.1-6 TRF are preserved in a treatise on oratory by Cicero entitled De oratore, often referred to in English as ‘On the Ideal Orator’, which was written in November 55 BCE but is set in 91 BCE. The Accian is quoted in a passage of Book 3 of the treatise devoted to the delivery (actio) of a speech. The speaker is the orator and statesman Lucius Licinius Crassus (140-91 BCE, RE ‘Licinius 55’), who, along with his fellow orator Marcus Antonius (143-87 BCE, RE ‘Antonius 28’), grandfather of the Marcus Antonius attacked by Cicero in the Philippics, is one of the two main speakers in Cicero’s dialogue. Crassus illustrates the various tones which an orator can adopt with his voice to convey a range of different emotions, such as a prolonged state of anger (iracundia), fear (metus), or uis (translated above as ‘energy’), by comparing lines from Republican drama as they were performed by actors on the stage. The Accian fragment is quoted alongside a series of other fragments of Republican drama by Ennius and Pacuvius, as well as, notably a fragment by Accius of uncertain play which has often been attributed to the Atreus (F inc. 405 TRF). It is noteworthy that Zwierlein (1983, 123) takes Crassus’ words Atreus fere totus to mean not ‘throughout the whole play entitled Atreus’ but to the role of Atreus himself (‘throughout the whole representation of Atreus’). Iracundia would thus describe specifically the character of Atreus, and not the tone of the play in general.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Tusculanae Disputationes 4.55: 

Oratorem uero irasci minime decet, simulare non dedecet. An tibi irasci tum uidemur, cum quid in causis acrius et uehementius dicimus? Quid? Cum iam rebus transactis et praeteritis orationes scribimus, num irati scribimus? ‘Ecquis hoc animaduortit? Vincite!...’ (= Accius Atreus F inc. 405 TRF) num aut egisse umquam iratum Aesopum aut scripsisse existimas iratum Accium? Aguntur ista praeclare (et ab oratore quidem melius, si modo est orator, quam ab ullo histrione), si aguntur leniter et mente tranquilla.

It is most improper for an orator to become angry, although there is nothing wrong with his putting on a show of anger. You sometimes see me arguing my cases more vehemently than usual: do you think I am always angry on those occasions? What about when the case is over and done, and I write the speech down? Surely you don’t think I’m angry then!

Does no one punish this deed? Bind him! (= Accius Atreus F inc. 405 TRF)

Do you think that Accius was angry when he wrote this line or that Aesopus was angry when he delivered it? Such things are excellent to perform (and the orator, if he is really an orator, performs them better than any actor), but they are performed calmly and with a tranquil mind.

(Text: Giusta 1984; transl.: Graver 2002, 58-9).

This fragment is preserved in a philosophical treatise in five books by Cicero entitled Tusculan Disputations, which was probably written during the summer of 45 BCE (on the dating, see Marinone–Malaspina 20042, 215). In this treatise, which is addressed to Brutus the assassin of Caesar (RE ‘Iunius 53’), Cicero aims to present to a Roman audience Greek philosophy of the Hellenistic period on a range of topics. Book 4, along with Book 3, is devoted to the emotions: Book 4 focuses specifically on perturbationes, ‘disturbances of mind’. The fragment is quoted in a passage (4.47-57) in which the Ciceronian persona outlines the Stoic view of emotions in response to the Peripatetic view. The Peripatetics hold that moderate emotion can occasionally be useful, whereas the Stoics deny that emotion can ever be useful. The Stoics do however suggest that it in certain specific contexts can be expedient for individuals such as orators and actors to simulate emotions (Sen. Ira 2.17.1, for the notion see too Quint. Inst. 9.2.26 and 39).Three other fragments either belonging to or connected with Accius’ Atreus are also preserved in Cicero’s Tusculan Disputations. These are Adesp. F 63 TRF (which has been connected to Accius’ Atreus), F 104.5-6 (the two last lines of a fragment which is preserved more fully at Cic. De orat. 3.219), and F 104.1-2 (also preserved at Cic. De orat. 3.217).

lines.text: Sed sonitus auris meas pedum pulsu increpat
..... seras potiuntur plagas.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation:
Gods do hate unjust men. Is it me or him acting 
unjustly?
doc.differences:
TrRF: 26 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 10/24)

Ribbeck2: 40-41 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 14/24)

Ribbeck3: 38-39 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 14/24)

Warmington 50-51 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 19/25)

Marmorale: p.195 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 14/24)

Klotz: 37-38 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 14/24)

oderunt di homines iniuros egone an ille iniurie facimus?

Bothe: oderunt di homines iniuros egone an ille iniurie facimus?

Mercerus & Scriverius & Klussmann: oderunt dii homines iniurios egone an ille iniurie facimus?

Stephanus: oderunt dii homines. Iniuriose egone an ille iniurie facimus.

Lindsay: oderunt di homines †iniuriose egone an ille iniurie facimus?

Mueller & Traglia: oderunt di homines iniuros egone an ille iniure facimus?

Ribbeck:1: oderunt di homines: egone an illi iniurie facimus […]

Warmington places this fragment towards the end of the play, just before the destruction of the royal palace and Lycurgus’s final punishment, which is not described in any of the remaining fragments, but it is implied in several passages. On the other hand, Ribbeck, Marmorale and Klotz agree that the fragment is to be placed in the middle of the play and, in particular, as the 14th of a total of 24 fragments of Naevius’ tragedy. In both cases, the fragment is associated with frgg. 21 and 33 (TrRF), which are placed exactly before in the editions, and the frg. 24 (TrRF) that can be placed afterwards because of the similar context. The capture of the Maenads (and of Dionysus (?)), the dialogue between Lycurgus and his followers/bodyguards, a confrontation between him and the god and the following punishment of the king is assumed to be the general context.
doc.apparatus:
1.di] i dii L F H1 : dii L G H

1.oderunti L2 H1 : oderunte fort. L1

1.iniuros] iniuriose ω Ri. et Wa. et Kl. rel. (Mu.) F H L P G E P7579 (On.) plerique codd. (Marm.) : iniuriosae H1 L1: iniuniosae fort. L1 (Mu.): iniurios coni. Mercerus et Scriverius et Klussmann : dii homines. Iniuriose coni. Stephanus : iniustos coni. Havet (1886α): odérunt di homines: égone an illi iniúrie / facimus […] propos. Ribbeck | homines iniuriose dictum pro iniuriose egone P

1.Bothe primus distinguens personas et corr. di homines iniuros nunc vulgo.

1.iniurie] iniuriae L1 G1 : iniure coni. Mueller et Traglia

In the manuscript tradition we have various readings for what it is transmitted in the text as iniuros. The main problem is that these readings do not fit with the metre, which is trochaic septenar as Ribbeck, Lindsay, Marmorale, Klotz and Traglia suggest. To solve this problem, Bothe emends which is accepted by all modern editors mainly because it is compatible with the metre. With an extra syllable added to the transmitted iniuros, the problem of the metre arises.
doc.commentary:
1.di homines iniuros] In the manuscripts we find the word iniuriose or iniuriosae, but it is probably an inspiration from the lemma of Nonius and a misreading of the original text of Naevius than a confirmed conjecture. Nonius quotes the line for the adverb iniurie and not for iniuriose, according to Traglia and Spaltenstein. The ancient editors (Mercerus, Scriverius) read iniurios, but this does not serve the metre (trochaic septenar). Lindsay places an obelus next to iniuriose as the word is plainly corrupt and he cannot see how to emend. Bothe’s correction iniuros is almost unanimously accepted and it is also adopted in the text as it complies with the metre. However, it is not confirmed either. The same holds for the emendation of Havet (1886α) iniustos which is mentioned in the editions of Lindsay and Warmington.

Klussmann has objections for iniuros and read iniurios instead, following the ancient editors, although he recognizes its use in Plautus’ Persa 408: impure, inhoneste, iniure, inlex, labes popli (insults to a leno). Marmorale admits that the adjective iniuros is unusual but also refers to the same passage from Plautus’ play. As Spaltenstein suggests, although this adjective appears only in these two texts, its formation reveals that it is not an unexpected or rare term. Ribbeck1 proposes to remove the word iniuros completely and proposes oderunt di homines: egone an illi iniure / facimus (7tro incomplete) but, according to Spaltenstein, in that way oderunt would be a later prosody, used like a poetic licence of hexametric poets when dealing with a cretic metrical foot and by imitation of the perfect tense in -ere, where the first e is long. The conjecture illi is also not a satisfactory solution as it does not correspond to the meaning of iniurie facimus.

Mueller argued that the catalectic trochaic tetrameter can be served only if we read: oderunt di homines iniustos. –egone an ille iniure facimus?, but as the manuscripts have homines iniuriose and taking into account Plautus’ passage from Persa, maybe we should accept what Bothe corrected as iniuros. Apart from that, iniure facimus creates trochaic octanar. We should rather prefer iniurie (and not iniure as Mueller and Traglia suggested) and then change of verse, mainly because this is the word that Nonius has in his lemma. Furthermore, as Spaltenstein mentions, trochaic octonar is not suitable for dialogue sections like the one we assume is happening here and it rather suits a canticum, a chorus lyric section. One of the reasons that we cannot exclude theoretically this metre in Naevius’ tragedies is that in his fragments we encounter other unexpected metres as well (cf. frg. 32 TrRF).

Nonetheless, if we accept the octonar, we must assume that the verse belongs to the chorus (the followers of Lycurgus or the Bacchants). On the other hand, trochaic septenar is used in dialogues (rather than monologues). According to Dangel, this metre is suitable for a verse with strong emotional content (cf. frg. 33 TrRF). Spaltenstein disagrees with this assumption, reminding us that we are dealing with fragments and not the whole tragedy, so we cannot reach certain conclusions based on the rhythmic character of a verse and therefore we cannot follow general rules. For instance, although Daviault argues that the last scene (of comedies) is always written in trochaic septenar, we cannot apply this rule in this fragment because of its context and the fact that the final scenes of a play are usually very short and our fragments have little chance of belonging to them. In addition, trochaic septenar does not appear exclusively in the final scenes, but also in other parts of a play.

Ribbeck compared homines iniuros to genitorem iniustum in Accius’ Clytaemnestra 245 (Wa.) where Clytaemnestra disputes with Electra about Agamemnom’s injustice: matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas.
doc.reconstruction:
The eternal confrontation between gods and humans is the main theme in the specific fragment. Here, the first interlocutor of the assumed dialogue (and probably by extension Naevius) seems concerned about the tyrannical abuse of power on behalf of human beings and the consequent wrath of the god. According to Boyle, the warning against opposition to religions from the East along with the idea of justice and the need for appropriate treatment of the gods is apparent here. Furthermore, the influence of Euripides’ Bacchae and the comparison with Pentheus’ madness and blindness of mind is obvious. We have to bear in mind that Naevius’ Lycurgus was displayed during an era in which the Roman elite’s main concern had to do with the spread of foreign religions and cults at Rome and, therefore, the need to define clearly what was Roman and what was not. In this tragedy, the Roman god Liber is identified with the Greek god of passionate ecstasy Bacchus, whose worship would have later been violently suppressed by the Roman Senate. This may have shocked the conservative and suspicious Roman audience as it may indicate Naevius’ suggestion for inclusiveness in Roman religion as well as tolerance of different gods and their worships. According to Manuwald, Lycurgus’s story (rather than Pentheus’ story) emphasizes the direct opposition of a god and a human with dire repercussions for the latter.

Bothe was the first who made the distinction between the two persons speaking in the fragment. This is accepted by all modern editors. Before that, it was assumed that the lines were uttered by the same person as a sort of self-reflection about a moral injustice. It may seem inconceivable, but we can never be sure. A dialogue, however, is more proper indeed, even if we imagine a scene with three characters on stage and even if we do not exclude the possibility of a separate self-reflection in this context.

The editors (Ribbeck, Warmington, Marmorale & Traglia) agree that we have a dialogue between two persons, as in frgg. 48 and 49 (33 and 21, TrRF) which are placed exactly before. Ribbeck and Traglia assume that the first one can be identified as Dionysus/Liber who after the capture of the Maenads has also been caught and brought in front of the king. The second person is Lycurgus who is now interrogating the god. Marmorale admits that the first speaker may not be Dionysus, but someone else, perhaps a follower of Lycurgus. Warmington also assumes a dialogue between Liber and Lycurgus, with the first one being captive and the second being mad, but he seems a little hesitant about the choice of Bacchus as the first speaker. Klotz does not give us any information about the interlocutors.

It is rather certain that Lycurgus can be identified as speaker B. As for speaker A, we cannot be sure. Düntzer suggests the following: after the chorus has left the stage, the king remains alone and then Silenus (the companion of god Dionysus), who is restrained, approaches and vehemently criticizes Lycurgus. Then we have this dialogue as a possible reconstruction: (Silenus) Pessimorum pessime, audax, ganeo, lurco, aleo/ Endocoepta per te temeriter face missa. Tu cave,/ Sis, tuam contendas iram contra ira Liberi./ Oderunt di homines iniuros. (Lycurgus) Egone an ille iniurie/ Facimus?. Merula conjectures hemones iniuriosos. According to Klussmann, Düntzer wrongly associates the fragment with incertum 20 (Wa., Unassigned fragments), where there is no reference of the name of Lycurgus and it is rather a fragment of comedy than tragedy. Klussmann suggests that the first interlocutor might be a Thrace who has tried before (cf. frg. 30 TrRF) to reason Lycurgus and deter him from prosecuting Bacchus. Now, he tries again in vain to admonish his king for his wicked endeavours.

Mette also attributes the line to a follower of Lycurgus (maybe a member of the chorus) who wants to discourage him from exposing himself to god’s fury. Pastorino argues that the first speaker is a member of Dionysus’ chorus which we assume to be present on stage and maybe represented here by the coryphaeus. This actually might be the case, if we take into account the severe tone of the warning, which seems to be uttered by someone who does not necessarily care about Lycurgus but rather wants to make a moral accusation and preludes king’s fall and punishment. Above all, the warning does not seem to come from a friend, a follower or a bodyguard of Lycurgus who would possibly have a milder and more respectful way of expression towards his king.

Oderunt di if uttered by Bacchus himself adds a tragic note, parallel to the one we find in Euripides’ Bacchae. The comparison with Pentheus is indicated in Traglia’s edition regarding fragment 26 (TrRF). Both Lycurgus and Pentheus have acted unjustly towards the god, have provoked his ira and have been eventually punished cruelly by being lured out of the city, driven mad and being killed. Lycurgus, like Pentheus, does not consider his prisoner as the god himself, so he threatens him (cf. frg. 21 TrRF) and wants for him to feel his anger. Although the prisoner replies warningly (cf. frg. 33 TrRF), Lycurgus claim that the god was the one that acted unjustly. In the god has not yet been revealed to Pentheus, so Dionysus might also be incognito in fragment 26, as Ribbeck suggested in his reconstruction. This does not mean, however, that we can exclude the possibility of a discussion between three people (Spaltenstein). Furthermore, in the context of a dialogue, the word ille, when uttered by the second speaker, cannot be referred to the first one. To be more specific, if we do have a dialogue between Lycurgus and Liber, why would Lycurgus refer to the god as he and not just as you directly? Therefore, this ille makes sense if we imagine Lycurgus on stage speaking to someone else and pointing to Liber who was also present, or rather referring to him and his injustice while thinking he was not there at all. In this hypothesis, the god has not yet been revealed and this intensifies the king’s madness. In any case, because of ille we cannot expect Liber to be Lycurgus’ interlocutor, but rather somebody else who tries to dissuade Lycurgus from acting improperly, an assumption that seems to find confirmation in Platus’ Meneaechmi 835-875 that features a woman and an old man who mistakenly believes that he has Apollo in front of him.

The injustice to which speaker A is referring is probably the arrest of the foreign harmless Maenads, the followers of Bacchus, by the king’s soldiers. Lycurgus, on the other hand, claim his right to defend his territory and his people after the invasion of the god and his Bacchants in the city whose foreign licentiousness and delirium had provoked disgust. Therefore, as Pastorino and Mueller suggest, the king tries to justify himself by accusing Liber of being unjust and unrespectful. He believes that the hubris had begun by the god and not by him. According to Traglia, Lycurgus asseverate that he defended the moral common good in opposition to the orgiastic culture of foreign divinity. In Bacchae, we encounter a similar justification in Pentheus’ speech and behaviour. Naevius might have an ideological reason to talk about Bacchus in this way in front of the Roman audience. However, as Spaltenstein points out, even if Traglia is right, the context in frg. 26 (TrRF) is too general to support such analysis. But, vice versa, exactly because of its general character, we cannot deny the assumption either. Moreover, the arrest of the harmless foreigners by Lycurgus may be an implication about royal arbitrariness, to which Latins might have been more sensitive.

Oderunt placed at the beginning of the first line, probably for emphasis, highlights the fact that gods loathe unjust humans who act wrongfully as a result of blindness of their mind, so they punish them in order to restore the natural order of cosmos. This is a common topos in ancient Greek tragedy. In Lycurgus’ case, the cause of his imminent punishment is the non-recognition of the divinity of Bacchus and, also, the arrogant demand to compete against a god, which clearly indicates an act of hubris. This hubris is intensified by his response to the warning which he totally ignores and, even, goes further by questioning god’s justice. We may also sense an irony in Lycurgus’s rhetorical (?) question. Keeping in mind the general context of Lycurgus’ fury, his attitude should not surprise us, although he would eventually come to understand his error. His victory is ephemeral. Liber soon sets himself free and wreaks revenge (Pastorino).

A similar warning against human opposition to a god’s will can be traced in the lost tragedy Bacchae of Aeschylus that was part of a tetralogy about the legend of god Dionysus. Bacchae is maybe an alternative title for Bassarai or Pentheus. For sure, the remaining fragment we have belongs to the context of Bacchae: τό τοι κακόν ποδῶκες ἔρχεται βροτοῖς καί τ᾿ ἀμπλάκημαͺτῷ περῶντι τὴν θέμιν (frg. 22 R.) Despite the scarcity of information, we can assume that the fault (ἀμπλάκημα) of those who turn against the justice of gods may be a reference to Pentheus’ hubris who has exceeded the human limits and violated the established divine order of things, exactly like Lycurgus in Naevius’ tragedy.

Another useful parallel is found again in the lost tetralogy of Aeschylus and, specifically, in the tragedy Hedonoi in which we see Lycurgus confronting his captive, Bacchus, in a way that reveals arrogance against the god: ποδαπός ὁ γύννις ; τίς πάτρα ; τίς ἡ στολή; (frg. 61 TGrF III).

Τhe whole dialogue in fragment 26 may be part of a narration of the events that took place outside the city, in the mountains, where Lycurgus was lured to or chased away. A messenger, probably an eyewitness, is to be envisaged as standing in front of the royal palace and quoting these. As Bednarek suggests and despite what all scholars assume, there is no clear evidence of Liber’s captivity, interrogation and liberation in the remaining fragments of Naevius’ tragedy. This annotation supports the argument that the first speaker in the fragment is not Liber, but someone else.

Bibliography

Bothe, F.H. (1834). Poetae scenici latinorum. Leipzig.

Radt, S. (1985). Tragicorum Graecorum Fragmenta. Vol. 3: Aeschylus. Göttingen.
