context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes these lines from Naevius’ Lycurgus in his dictionary De compendiosa doctrina within a definition of iniurie as a term synonym for iniuriose, meaning ‘unjustly’:

iniurie dictum pro iniuriose. Naevius Lycurgo: oderunt di homines iniuros. : : egone an ille iniurie / facimus? (Non. p. 124, 33-35 Merc. = 180 Li.).

lines.text: oderunt di homines iniuros. : : egone an ille iniurie
facimus?

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation:
[…] Lure them with wings high up the other mountains,
Where two-footed birds may leave behind the light in flax.
doc.differences:
TRF: 18 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 2/25)

Ribbeck2: 29-31 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

Ribbeck3: 26-28 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

Warmington: 30-32 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 5/25)

Marmorale: p. 193 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

Klotz: 29-30 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

.

[…] alis sublime alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino liquant lumina

Klotz: […] alis sublimen alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

[…] alis sublime alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

Warmington: alii sublime in altos saltus inlicite lacuna, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

[…] alis sublime alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

Ribbeck2: alias alis sublime in altos saltus inlicite invios, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

[…] alis sublime alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

Ribbeck3: alias alis sublimen altos saltus inlicite invios, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

[…] alis sublime alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

Marmorale: […] alis sublimes saltus alites inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina.

Ribbeck2, Ribbeck3, Marmorale, Warmington and Klotz all place this fragment at the beginning of the play. However, while Marmorale, Klotz, Ribbeck2 and Ribbeck3 all place it after TRF: 19, both the TRF and Warmington place it before TRF: 19.
doc.apparatus:
1 sublime] subumem L1: sublimen rell.

1 alis] aliis F1 F3.

1 saltus] saltos F1.

2 inlicite] inlicitae L1 E : illicitae L2 B.
doc.commentary:
alis ] Warmington conjectures alii, which completely alters the meaning of the word, but might not be unreasonable within the context of the fragment. If we adopt Spaltenstein’s interpretation, namely that Lycurgus is commanding his guards to catch the bacchants in this fragment, he might simply give the command to “some of them”, and give others a different task. However, alii is attested in none of the extant manuscripts. Furthermore, alis is not incomprehensible either, specifically as a form of ala within the context of the volucres in the second line.

alios ] Warmington, Ribbeck2 and Ribbeck3 all conjecture altos, even though it is not attested in any of the extant manuscripts. However, it is true that alios poses some issues, as Spaltenstein explains: if alios should be taken with saltus and the speaker is referring to “other places”, to what places should these be compared? In contrast, altos saltus is a generic way of describing mountains, which would go well with sublime.

sublime ] Most manuscripts have sublimen. However, Spaltenstein mentions that this is a rare archaic form, and that it would make more sense to print sublime, as this form is also attested in Ennius’ works. Also interesting is Marmorale’s conjecture, who prints […] alis sublimes saltus alites. According to Spaltenstein, however, this conjecture derives from an uncertain manuscript, and should therefore not receive too much attention.
doc.reconstruction:
Warmington, Ribbeck2, Ribbeck3, Klotz and Marmorale all assign this fragment to Naevius’ Lycurgus, probably on the basis of the context: this fragment derives from Nonius’ De Compendiosa Doctrina, where Nonius explicitly states that it is part of Naevius’ Lycurgus.

The main verb in these two lines is a second person plural, probably an imperative. This indicates that the speaker is addressing multiple people, and possibly giving them a command. The fragment does not explicitly state who the speaker is. Warmington does not specify who the speaker is, but Marmorale and Spaltenstein are convinced that it is Lycurgus, which indeed seems to be the most logical option: he is the most likely character to issue a command, given his authority.

Düntzer thinks bipedes volucres refers to actual birds, but this view gets no support from Marmorale, Spaltenstein and Warmington, who regard bipedes volucres as a metaphor for the bacchants. In this case, the bacchants are being compared to two-footed birds. This does seem to be the most sensible option, especially when we take Euripides’ Bacchae, vv. 748 into account, where the Bacchants are also compared to birds. Furthermore, the epithet bipedes does not make much sense when the speaker is referring to actual birds, as all birds are two-footed. According to Spaltenstein, we should rather think of bipedes in the sense of “humanlike”, as humans are sometimes (jokingly) called two-footers. This could imply that bipedes volucres should be understood as some kind of derogatory joke of Lycurgus, who reduces the bacchants to “humanlike birds” by calling them bipedes volucres. Then again, it is also very well possible that the epithet bipedes is merely used in a poetic sense and does not carry any further implications.

The context of the passage suggests that inlicite should be interpreted in a similar way as the verb inlaequeare, which means “to ensnare”. This could help make sense of lino, which, combined with the image of birds that the speaker sketches, most likely refers to a net used to catch birds.

Keeping all of the above in the back of our minds, we may now possibly reconstruct the addressees of this fragment. According to Spaltenstein, Lycurgus could very well be speaking to his guards: he commands them to catch the bacchants, so that they linquant lumina, a euphemism for death. This seems like a probable interpretation of the fragment, especially because it resonates with Euripides’ Bacchae, which was probably an important source of Naevius’ Lycurgus.

Bibliography

Klotz, A., Seel, O., Voit, L. (1953). Tragicorum Fragmenta. Monachii.

Marmorale, E. (1950). Naevius’ Poeta. Florence.

Ribbeck, O. (1872). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (2nd ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (3rd ed.). Leipzig.

Schauer, M. (2012). Tragicorum Romanorum Fragmenta. Göttingen.

Spaltenstein, F. (2014). Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels.

Warmington, E. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted only once by Nonius in De Compendiosa Doctrina.

Nonius quotes these lines in the context of an explanation of the verb inlicire, which according to him has a similar meaning to the verb inlaqueare

.

inlicire est proprie inlaqueare. Naevius Lycurgo: […] alis sublime alios saltus inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino liquant lumina. (Non. 18)

lines.text: […] alis sublime alios saltus
inlicite, ubi bipedes volucres lino linquant lumina

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: [THYESTES]: numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi!

doc.translation:
I shall never reduce your worries by denying them to you. 

Or

I shall never reduce your worries for you by denying.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 19 (19/20), 234 (certum):

234: Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi.

Warmington:

Fragment 191 (13/17) (certum):

191: Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi.

Never will I abate that care within you

By making you denial.

Franchella:

Fragment 21 (21/22), 386 (certum):

386: Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi.

Mai diminuirò codesto affanno, negando <sott. di volerti far morire>.

D’Antò:

Fragment 17 (17/18), 234 (certum):

234: < – x – > Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi.

Giammai diminuirò codesta tua ansia col mentirti.

Dangel:

Fragment 6 (6/21), 43 (certum):

42: Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi !

Jamais je n’amoindrirai le tourment qui est tien en niant ma culpabilité !
doc.apparatus: No textual issues to report. Scriverius (1620, 104) prints quamquam quod ad me attinet, which seem to be the words with which Cicero introduces the fragment at Pis. 82, as part of the fragment: Quamquam quod ad me attinet | Nunquam istam imminuam curam inficiando tibi.
doc.commentary: 1 …imminuam curam infitiando tibi: there seems to be disagreement between scholars regarding what exactly this means. The basic meaning is infitior is ‘to deny’ (TLL VII.1.1450.69-83, OLD s.v. 1 ‘to refuse to acknowledge as true, to deny’). The difficulty identifying the meaning of the verb is not made easier by the uncertainty regarding the context in which the fragment is quoted (see ‘Citation context’), but ‘deny’ does indeed seems to be the sense here, even though D’Antò translates it as ‘to lie’ (‘mentirti’). The main difficulty resides in whether the dative pronoun tibi is to be construed with infitiando (‘deny to you’) or with imminuam (‘diminish for you’, ethic dative). The latter is paralleled at Pl. Cist. 87-88: [SELENIUM, meretrix], asked whether she has ever been intimate with a man: nisi quidem cum Alcesimarcho, nemine, | nec pudicitiam meam mi alius quisquam imminuit, ‘No, with no one, except for Alcesimarchus, and no one else has done damage to my chastity’ (transl. De Melo 2011, II.145). Most scholars construe it in this way, taking tibi with imminuam: ‘never will I abate that care within you’ (Warmington); ‘never shall I ease your pang by weak disclaiming’ (Watts, Loeb, XIV.127); ‘giammai diminuirò codesta tua ansia…’ (D’Antò); ‘jamais n’amoindrirai le tourment qui est tien…’ (Dangel). Franchella, however, seems to construe tibi with imminuam (‘...by denying your request to die’ (‘negando…[di volerti far morire]’). For more on these different interpretations, see ‘Fragment reconstruction’. The first-century CE commentator on Cicero’s speeches, Asconius, identified an allusion to this Accian fragment in Cicero’s Post reditum in senatu (‘The speech delivered before the senate after his return from exile’) 33: duae partes esse in re publica cum putarentur, altera me deposcere propter inimicitias, altera timide defendere propter suspitionem caedis putabatur. Qui autem me deposcere videbantur, ii hoc auxerunt dimicationis metum, quod numquam infitiando suspitionem hominum curamque minuerunt, ‘When two factions were believed to be in the Republic, one was believed to demand me for punishment because of enmity, the other to defend me timidly because of a presentiment of slaughter. But those who seemed to demand me increased the fear of fighting by the fact that they never reduced the presentiment and concern of people by denying’ (transl. Manuwald 2021, 29). Nisbet (1961, 150) also identifies a possible allusion at Cic. Sest. 8, numquam illum [Antonium] illo summo timore ac periculo ciuitatis neque communem metum omnium nec propriam non nullorum de ipso [Catilina] suspicionem aut infitiando tollere aut dissimulando sedare uoluisse, ‘at no point in our community’s time of fear and peril did he [Gaius Antonius, Cicero’s colleague as consul in 63] have the will either to remove (by denial) or to palliate (by dissimulation) the general terror of us all and the suspicion that some entertained about him [Catiline] in particular’ (transl. Kaster 2006, 45).
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck2-3 prints this fragment as a fragment of Atreus, i.e. F 19 (19/20), v. 234. He suggests (1875, 456) that these could be the defiant words of Thyestes, having been put in chains (F 405 TRF) and under interrogation about his words about the curse he threw on Atreus. Ribbeck however believes that this fragment, as well as Accius inc. F  405 TRF (ecquis hoc animadvortet…) and 413 TRF (multi iniqui...), and  could also belong to Accius’ Pelopidae (1875, 456). If it belongs to Pelopidae, then Ribbeck suggests that Thyestes says this while being seized by Atreus’ men in Delphi, where he had come to gain information about how to take revenge on his brother (cf. Hyg. Fab. 88.8).

Warmington also prints this fragment as a fragment of the Atreus (fr. 191), identifying the speaker as Thyestes (‘bitter words between Thyestes and Atreus’, II.389), and noting: ‘Speaking to Atreus in a play of Accius, according to Asconius on this passage (see opposite). The context is unknown. Some [i.e. Düntzer 1839, 293, see ‘Citation context’] would alter the text of Asconius to dici ab Atreo Thyesti, The fr. may belong to Pelopidae (pp. 500ff.).’ 

Lana (1959, 315), who believes that the play ended with Atreus’ downfall, interprets this fragment as belonging to the final scene. While Atreus is being bound in chains after Thyestes has successfully convinced the chorus of Mycenean citizens to rebel against him, he begins fearfully to anticipate the fate that awaits him, and Thyestes mocks him by saying: numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi! (‘codesto tuo affanno non mai diminuirò negando’, i.e. ‘I shall never diminish your pain by refusing [i.e. to put you to death]’). See further F inc. 405, ‘Fragment reconstruction’.

Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 272) argues that this fragment is spoken by Atreus after revealing to Thyestes that his children are dead and that he, Thyestes, has eaten their remains (F 103.5 natis sepulcro ipse es parens). After Thyestes begs Atreus to say that this is just a lie, Di Benedetto Zimbone suspects that Atreus responded with this fragment (F inc. 404 TRF, numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi!) and also with something along the lines of: ‘Make no mistake, Thyestes. It is just as you have heard. I killed your children with these very hands. And you, you ate their flesh, with that very mouth!’ (‘“Non ti fare illusioni, Tieste. È proprio come hai udito. I tuoi figli, io li ho uccisi, con queste mani. E tu, tu ne hai mangiato le carni, con codesta bocca!”’). She thus considers that Asconius made a mistake in attributing these lines to Thyestes (see ‘Citation context’), and that in fact they were spoken by Atreus (as Düntzer 1839, col. 293 had already suggested).

Cf. too Dangel 117 (F 6 (6/21), v. 42). Dangel’s interpretation (1995, 278) of the fragment is interesting: accepting Asconius’ identification of Thyestes as the speaker, she points out that Cicero quotes the fragment in a juridical context (Cicero Against Piso 82, see ‘Citation context’). In order to shed light on the possible context of the Accian fragment within the Atreus, Dangel thus looks to the account of status theory given by Antonius in Book 2 of De oratore (99-113), a passage which falls at the very beginning of Antonius’ explanation of the process of finding arguments for a case, known as inuentio. The first thing an orator must do when preparing a case is to get to know the case by identifying its status (OLD s.v. 4, ‘(rhet.) the point on which two opposing parties come to grips or where the defendant makes a stand ’). At De orat. 2.105, Antonius remarks that in criminal cases, it is usual practice for the defendant to deny allegations which are made (Antonius cites the examples of extortion, election bribery, murder, poisoning, and embezzlement of public property). In such cases, the status turns on a controversy which happened in the past (id est igitur genus…causarum in iudiciis ex controuersia facti). Dangel suggests that Antonius’ explanation of how to organise a defence in a criminal law case in Cicero’s De oratore might illuminate this Accian fragment, which she argues belongs to a scene in which Atreus questions Thyestes and lists the offences that his brother committed against him in the past (cf. F 104.7-15 TRF). Such a scene would be based on a scene in Euripides’ Thyestes as imagined by Welcker (1839-1841, II.681-2), on the basis of Euripides Thyestes F 396 TrGF (= Euripides Cretan Women F 15 Dindorf: ἀλλ᾿ εἴπερ ἐστὶν ἐν βροτοῖς ψευδῆ, γέρον, | πιθανά, νομίζειν χρή σε καὶ τοὐναντίον, | ἄπιστ᾿ ἀληθῆ πολλὰ συμβαίνειν βροτοῖς, ‘But if, old man, there are falsehoods amongst men that are persuasive, you should recognize the opposite – that many truths turn out to be unpersuasive to them’, transl. Collard–Cropp 2008-2009, I.435) and F 554a TrGF (attributed by TrGF to Euripides’ Oedipus = Euripides inc. F 24 Dindorf: ἐγὼ γὰρ ὅστις μὴ δίκαιος ὢν ἀνὴρ | βωμὸν προσίζει, τὸν νόμον χαίρειν ἐῶν | πρὸς τὴν δίκην ἄγοιμ᾿ ἂν οὐ τρέσας θεούς· | κακὸν γὰρ ἄνδρα χρὴ κακῶς πάσχειν ἀεί, ‘I’d myself take away to face justice any man who sits in sanctuary at an altar without just cause, and would leave the law aside; and I’d not fear the gods: for a bad man should always suffer badly’, transl. Collard–Cropp 2008-2009, II.25). In Accius’ scene, as Dangel imagines it, Thyestes deliberately does not deny the allegations made against him, but instead fully admits that he is guilty of having committed them, thus displaying an attitude of ‘provocative cynicism’ (Dangel 1995, 278). Dangel suggests that F 105 TRF (probae etsi in segetem…) constitutes a further provocation against Atreus on Thyestes’ part (see further F 105 TRF ‘Fragment reconstruction’). But Baldarelli (2004, 263 n. 4) objects that such a response by Thyestes presupposes that Atreus questioned him about the issue of kingship, and that this would be implausible, because, assuming Atreus invited Thyestes back in a feigned spirit of reconciliation, he would not risk revealing his concerns about the succession by bringing up the topic of the kingship, as this might alert Thyestes to the true nature of Atreus’ intentions.

Baldarelli herself (2004, 263-4) proposes that these words are spoken by Thyestes to Atreus at the end of the tragedy. Thyestes predicts that Atreus will be the victim of a violent revolt by the people of Mycenae, and, whenever Atreus commands him to retract his statement, Thyestes tells him that he will not diminish his worry by retracting (infitiando) what he has just said.
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius. Mette (1964, 116) supplemented a cretic element (– x –) at the beginning of the line which would make the line a trochaic septenarius.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero In Pisonem 82:

Perfecit ille [Gaius Iulius Caesar] ut si montes resedissent, amnes exaruissent, non naturae praesidio sed victoria sua rebusque gestis Italiam munitam haberemus. Sed cum me expetat diligat omni laude dignum putet, tu me [a] tuis inimicitiis ad simultatem revocabis, sic tuis sceleribus rei publicae praeterita fata refricabis? Quod quidem tu, qui bene nosses coniunctionem meam et Caesaris, eludebas, cum a me trementibuss omnino labris, sed tamen cur tibi nomen non deferrem requirebas, quamquam, quod ad me attinet, ‘numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi’, tamen est mihi considerandum quantum illi tantis rei publicae negotiis tantoque bello impedito ego homo amicissimus sollicitudinis atque oneris imponam.

The result of his [Gaius Julius Caesar’s] efforts is that if the mountains sank down and the rivers dried up, we would still have Italy fortified not by nature’s defenses but by Caesar’s victory and military accomplishments. But since he seeks me out, shows affection for me, thinks me worthy of all kinds of praise, are you going to bring me back to an old quarrel through your hostility, will you pick at scabs of past public troubles through your crimes? Even though you knew perfectly well how close I am to Caesar, you scoffed at me – although your lips were trembling – in asking why I didn’t prosecute you. As far as I’m concerned, ‘I will never reduce your worries by denying them to you,’ but I must take into account the worry and burden I will be placing on a good friend who is occupied by such important public business and by so great a war.

(Text: Nisbet 1961; transl. Zetzel 2009, 268-269).

Cicero quotes this fragment in a passage of his speech against L. Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus. Father-in-law to Julius Caesar and the patron of the Epicurean philosopher Philodemus, Piso was consul in 58 BCE and later became proconsul of Macedonia in 57-55 (RE ‘Calpurnius 90’). He became an enemy of Cicero when, during his tenure as consul, he did nothing to prevent Clodius’ plans to send Cicero into exile. When Piso, after his return from Macedonia in 55, defended himself from attacks made by Cicero with a speech during a meeting of the senate, Cicero in turn responded with this invective. Cicero caricatured Piso in other speeches written during this period, too (In Sen. 13-16, Sest. 19-24). The fragment is quoted in the course of a passage in which Cicero says that the he would like to prosecute Piso for provincial extortion during his time as proconsul of Macedonia (though Cicero may be overstating his case, the grounds for prosecution being in fact seemingly rather thin: see Nisbet 1961, ‘Appendix I: Piso’s Proconsulship of Macedonia 57-55 B.C.’, 172-180 for an overview of the evidence). Quoting Accius, Cicero tells Piso that he would never reduce Piso’s worries ‘infitiando tibi’. The only thing stopping him from going through with the prosecution is the burden that this would place on Piso’s son-in-law Julius Caesar, a man whom Cicero commends for his praiseworthy achievements and his benevolent attitude towards Cicero himself. What exactly is meant by the words infitiando tibi is not entirely clear (see ‘Fragment commentary’ and ‘Fragment reconstruction’).

The attribution of this fragment to Accius’ Atreus is virtually certain thanks to a scholion on this passage of the speech by Asconius, the first-century CE commentator on Cicero’s speeches (see 'Citation context').

context.author: Asconius
context.text:
Asconius ad Cicero, In Pisonem 82, p. 21.4 Stangl:

prope notius est quam ut indicandum sit hunc versum esse L. Accii poetae et dici a Thyeste Atreo.

It is so well known that it almost need not be mentioned that this is a line by the poet Lucius Accius which is said by Thyestes to Atreus.

Were it not for Asconius’ testimony, we would have no way of knowing that this fragment belongs to Accius’ Atreus (and perhaps even that it is a quotation from tragedy). Lana (1959, 344) interprets this testimony as revealing that Accius’ Atreus was still extremely well-known and popular in the first century CE, given that Asconius seems to take it for granted that his readership is familiar with the play. Clearly believing that Atreus is the speaker, Düntzer (1839, 293) proposed to emend Asconius’ text to ab Atreo Thyesti.

lines.text: Quis homo te exsuperavit usquam gentium impudentia?
:: quis autem malitia te?

doc.translation:
What man in all the world has surpassed you in impudence?
And who you in spite?

doc.apparatus:
1: quis homo ] quis homo codd.: qui homo Bentley 1709, 74

1: usquam ] usquam Bentley 1709, 74: umquam codd.

1: exsuperavit ] exsuperavit codd.: exsuperabit Giusta

2: quis autem malitia te ] quis autem malitia te codd.: qui autem m. t. Bentley 1709, 74: quis te autem m. Bouhier: <ec>quis autem m. t. Bergk 1874, 290: quis tete autem m. Ribbeck1, 34: quis item m. t. ‘Fleckeisen 1864’: quis ted autem m. Ribbeck2, 41: quis <uir> autem m. t. Giusta

﻿Bentley’s conjectures qui homo and qui [sc. homo] autem are designed to remove the use of quis as an interrogative adjective: quis is usually the pronoun (‘who?’), qui the adjective (‘what [e.g. man]’). But the use of quis as an interrogative adjective is paralleled elsewhere in Republican Latin (e.g. Plaut. Curc. 412 quis to homo es? ‘Who [lit. what man] are you?’; Ter. Eun. 804 quis to homo’s? ‘Who do you think you are?’ transl. Barsby 2001, I.407). The occurrence at Accius Philoctetes F 334.1 TRF falls out if one takes mortalis as a vocative: quis tu es, mortalis, qui in deserta et tesca te apportes loca?, adopting TRF’s text with Dangel’s punctuation (‘who are you, mortal [or, reading quis tu es mortalis: what mortal man are you], to be carrying yourself around this deserted and sacred place?’).

Bentley also proposed usquam for umquam, observing that the spatial sense of usquam (i.e. lit. ‘what man anywhere on earth…’), and not the temporal sense of umquam, is what is wanted (see ‘Fragment commentary’). Ribbeck reports that the reading usquam is found ‘in some Oxford manuscript’ (‘Oxon. aliq.’: there are nine manuscripts of Tusculan Disputations in Oxford according to the Bodleian Library’s online catalogue), but Bentley can be credited with coming up with the conjecture independently. 

Bouhier’s modification of the word order (1737, 438) aims at giving the second line a clearer trochaic rhythm by making it start with two heavy syllables, quis te (quis thus becoming long by position). Building on Bouhier’s suggestion, Ribbeck suggests changing te to the reduplicated form tete (in his first edition) or to the archaic ted (in his second edition), the latter ‘freq. used by Plautus to avoid hiatus’ (OLD s.v. te). Bergk (1874, 290) proposed ecquis for quis, adding a syllable so as to give the line a trochaic base (see ‘Metrical issues’). Fleckeisen’s conjecture item (‘too, as well’) seems to aim at removing a possible adversative force of autem (‘but, on the contrary’), but autem can be explained as OLD 4b, ‘and in fact, and indeed, and (repeating a word from the previous clause)’. Giusta suggests that uir dropped out on account of the word’s similarity to the final letters of quis.

Giusta (1984, 259) conjectures the future form exsuperabit for the transmitted perfect form exsuperauit. The future would give the sense: ‘what man in all the world will (ever) surpass you…?’.
doc.commentary: 1 usquam gentium: ‘anywhere on earth’: see Plaut. Mil. 685-6 nam bona uxor suaue ductu est, si sit usquam gentium | ubi ea possit inueniri, ‘Yes, a good wife is sweet to marry, if there were any place on earth where one could be found’, transl. De Melo 2011, III.213
doc.reconstruction:
This adespoton is attributed as a fragment of Accius’ Atreus by Scriverius–Vossius (1620, 105) and by Bothe (I.187), F 17 (17/19). Bothe credits Columna with having first made the suggestion that this fragment belongs to Accius’ Atreus. In fact, Columna (1590, 262) only suggests that Accius is the author of the fragment: he does not seem to suggest that the fragment belongs to the Atreus specifically. 

Bothe imagines the speakers to be first Atreus, and then Thyestes. Scriverius–Vossius go further, suggesting that the subject of the argument is the sons of Atreus: presumably doubts were raised as to their legitimacy? Scriverius–Vossius envision that Atreus then begins to think about his revenge against Thyestes in F 104.5-6 TRF (maior mihi moles, maius miscendum est malum, | qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam), and later that Thyestes complains about the very moles that Atreus has in the meantime planned and enacted (F 104.1-2 TRF: ipsus hortatur me frater, ut meos malis miser | manderem natos).

Some later editors (e.g. Vahlen, Ribbeck, Warmington) attribute it to Ennius’ Iphigenia, but G. Manuwald in her TRF edition and Manuwald–Goldberg in their Loeb edition of Ennius’ tragedies do not include it among the fragments of Ennius’ Iphigenia. Hartung (1843-1844, I.202) attributes this fragment to Ennius’ Telephus (cf. discussion at Jocelyn 405). The TRF edition (I.234-5) does not seem to have picked up on this attribution.
doc.metrical_analysis: Bentley (1709, 74) considered both of these lines to be trochaic septenarii. But Bücheler (apud Ribbeck2, xxvii-xviii) imagined that, in this scene full of passion and quick exchanges, the metre could alternate rapidly between trochaics and iambics. He suggested that the first line is a trochaic septenarius, and that the second line is an iambic octonarius (thus defending the manuscript reading quis autem so that the line begins with a light syllable). Bergk however (1874, 290) pointed out that in successive lines in which there is a change of speaker there is often no change of metre. Bergk therefore believes that both lines are trochaic septenarii, the second of which is truncated. Most later editors, including the TRF, concur.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Ira uero, [quae] quam diu perturbat animum, dubitationem insaniae non habet. Cuius inpulsi existit etiam inter fratres tale iurgium:

‘Quis homo te exsuperabit usquam gentium inpudentia? Quis autem malitia te?’ (= Adesp. F 63 TRF)

Nosti quae secuntur; alternis enim uersibus intorquentur inter fratres grauissimae contumeliae, ut facile appareat Atrei filios esse, eius qui meditatur poenam in fratrem nouam… (Accius Atreus F 104.5-6 maior mihi moles… etc.)

As for anger: while this emotion lasts, there can be no doubt that it is insane. Under its influence even brothers may quarrel like this:

‘Is there anyone in all the world that’s beaten you for insolence?’ ‘Or you for spite?’ (= Adesp. F 63 TRF)

You know the rest: verse by verse, the brothers hurl at each other the most serious insults. One can easily believe they are the sons of that Atreus who plots an unheard-of penalty for his own brother…(Accius Atreus F 104.5-6 maior mihi moles… etc.)

[Text: Giusta 1984; transl. Graver 2002, 67, adapted.]


This fragment of uncertain author and uncertain play is quoted by Cicero in a philosophical treatise in five books by Cicero entitled Tusculan Disputations, which was probably written during the summer of 45 BCE (on the dating, see Marinone–Malaspina 20042, 215). In this treatise, which is addressed to Brutus the assassin of Caesar (RE ‘Iunius 53’), Cicero aims to present to a Roman audience Greek philosophy of the Hellenistic period on a range of topics. Book 4, along with Book 3, is devoted to the emotions: Book 4 focuses specifically on perturbationes, ‘disturbances of mind’. The fragment has sometimes been thought also to belong to Accius’ Atreus. This attribution seems implausible, however, on the grounds that Cicero seems quite clearly to state that the two speakers are sons of Atreus, which makes it virtually certain that they cannot be Atreus and Thyestes, but rather are to be identified with Agamemnon and Menelaus. Therefore, the attribution of this fragment to Ennius’ Iphigenia by scholars such as Ribbeck, Vahlen, and Jocelyn seems at the very least more plausible, though far from certain.

lines.text: Iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili?
<25> concoquit
partem vapore flammae, veribus in focos
lacerta tribuit.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: Take them away to that place! Those sharp-tongued, stupid as animals.
doc.differences:
TRF: 19 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 3/24)

RIBBECK1: 6 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 6/24)

RIBBECK3: 6 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 6/24)

WARMINGTON: 46-47 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 16/25)

MARMORALE: 6 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 6/24)

Klotz: 6 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 6/24)

Ducite eo cum argutis linguis mutas quadrupedis.

Ribbeck: Ducito eo tum argutis linguis mutas quadrupedis.

Warmington, Marmorale: Ducite eo tum argutis linguis mutas quadrupedis.

Klotz: Ducite eo tum argutis linguis mutas quadrupedes.

It seems that Warmington is the only one who deviates from the standard placement of this fragment. Most editions place this fragment as the sixth one. TRF has it as the third fragment. A bit earlier than most but still at the beginning of the play. Warmington on the other hand places this fragment about a little over halfway as the sixteenth of twenty-five fragments. Warmington places the fragment after the capture of the maenads when they have been presented to the king. Lycurgus decides their fate and orders for them to be taken away.
doc.apparatus:
1.ducite] Bamb

2.cum] eo tum F H L P G E Lugd

2.argutis linguis] argutus lingis Lugd
doc.commentary:
cum] in following the Aldine edition, most editors substitute cum for tum. Marm. on the other hand preserves tum. Lattanzi argues that substituting cum for tum is unnecessary since cum without an ablative would defend itself. Tum, according to Spaltenstein, could have the meaning of referring to the future. This would also support the idea that ducite is meant to be ducito. The editors using cum use it as an instrumental. This however does not hold up since cum as an instrumental often refers to physical or moral characteristics, which is not the case here since linguis references not to an actual, physical tongue.

ducite] the codex Bambergensis transmits ducito instead of ducite. This alternation would mean that the meaning of the sentence slightly changes. The imperative would first of all be aimed towards a single person instead of multiple people. This means that Lycurgus (who is probably the speaker) addresses one person, maybe the captain of the guard, instead of multiple people, meaning all the guards. Second of all the imperative would no longer be a present but a future. This would support the idea of preserving tum instead of cum since that word also refers to the future.

quadrupedis] Spaltenstein argues that quadrupedis is ‘parasite’ and quadrupedis should therefore be corrected to quadrupedes.
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment starts with a plural imperative. The addressees are commanded to take away a certain group of people to a certain place. The people who are meant to be taken away are said to be sharp-tounged and stupid like four footers. From these statements you can conclude that the speaker has an animosity against those people. From this fragment we can also deduce that there is a hierarchy of power. The speaker is of the highest power. They command the addressees to perform a task which indicates that the speaker has authority over them. At their turn, the addressees must hold power over the group of people since they have the power to take them away.

It is assumed by most editors that the feminine mutas are the maenads. Ribbeck and Deichgräber believe that quadrupedis refers to the way that the maenads’ hands and feet are bound together. Deichgräber refers to Terence (Andria 865) in which this bound-up position is mentioned as quadrupedis constringito. This theory however is not foolproof since the maenads are supposed to be guided somewhere and that is hardly possible when a person is tied up at hands and feet. Marmorale suggest that the person speaking must have seen the maenads in their frenzy walking on all fours. This in reference to fragment 30-32. Some also suggested that Lycurgus, who is most likely to be the speaker, orders his guards to fetch some real animals for sacrifice, but this does not add up with the statement that the mutas are cum argutis linguis.

There are some difficulties with the statement that the maenads are mutas. Klussmann suggests that the maenads are using instruments that produce animal-like sounds like in fragment 57 of the Edonoi. A plausible suggestion but one with a flaw. In this fragment the instruments are played by the male followers of Dionysus. Klussmann also adds that the maenads may have really mooed themselves. In the Bacchae 691 there is also a reference to mooing of animals but the maenads hear these moos and don’t produce these sounds themselves. Spaltenstein adds that the maenads producing Bacchic cries can be making incomprehensible sound all the while still having a sharp tongue. Another suggestion is that the maenads may have not only been tied up but also gagged, which would explain their incomprehensible sounds.

Eo probably refers to a prison. There is a suggestion that one should add ad mortem to ducere, but that would not really go well with eo. Ducere should therefore be taken as meaning something like ‘to take’ or ‘to lead’.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted once by Nonius in De Compediosa Doctrina:

The fragment here is quoted in connection with the word mutus as meaning stupid:

Mutus onomatopoeia est incertae vocis, quasi mugitus. Nam mutus sonus est proprie, qui intellectum non habet. Naevius Lycurgo: ducite eo cum argutis linguis mutas quadrupedis. (Non. 19)

lines.text: Ducite
eo cum argutis linguis mutas quadrupedis
Ecquis hoc animaduortet? uincite!
eheu mea fortuna ut omnia in me conglomeras mala.
..... neque fera hominum pectora
Fragescunt, donec vim imperi persenserint.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Now surely do not wait for him.  When did you do a friendly act for him, your friend ?
doc.differences:
1. Schierl: 107 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 21/32)

2. Warmington: 157–8 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 29/34)

nunc ne illum exspectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti

Warmington: nunc ne illum expectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti

3. D’ Anna: 175 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 22/31)

4. Ribbeck2: 131-132 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 12/32)

nunc ne illum exspectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti

Ribbeck2: nunc ne illum expectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti

5. Ribbeck3: 131-132 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 12/32)

nunc ne illum exspectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti

Ribbeck3: nunc ne illum expectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti

Schierl, Warmington, and D'Anna place this fragment towards the end of the play, in the broader context of the quest for an ally in order to execute the act of revenge. Warmington and D'Anna place fr. 107 after fr.108 so as to give the impression that the one who was expected for help did not show up. Schierl places fr. 107 before fr. 108, maybe to show that the speaker still had expectations for help, despite the absence of reciprocal friendly acts. In both cases, they assume that illum of fr. 107 and fr. 108 refers to the same person. On the other hand, Ribbeck2&3 places fr. 107 earlier on the play, when the discussion about the wedding between Klytaemnestra and Electra takes place. According to Ribbeck2&3, perhaps Klytaemnestra warns Electra not to expect help from Orestes in order to defend herself and escape the wedding.
doc.apparatus:
1. illum] illum codd. : hilum Scriverius, L. Müller

1. quando] quam CA : qua Bamb
doc.commentary:
amiciter] The adverb amiciter is found only in Pacuvius and Plautus, in both of them in the phrase amico amiciter which emphasizes the reciprocal feelings of friendship. (cf. Plautus' Persa 255 quia meo amico amiciter hanc commoditatis copiam , ed. Woytek 1982, 254f; also ThLL I 1914, 63f). A possible sexual connotation can also be noted here (D'Anna, 1967, 206f; also ThLL I 1908, 3f).

ne [...] exspectes] The present active subjunctive is very often used in order to express prohibition.

meter: It has been suggested that an iambic heptameter can be applied in this fragment (cf. Strzelecki 1952, 50f.; D'Anna 207, 283), but this meter is mostly preferred in comedy and it is not witnessed elsewhere in the corpus of Pacuvius. The iambic hexameter is a more possible meter for this fragment and in this case, the verse must end after the word amiciter ( Ribbeck, 1897), (Warmington, 1936), (Klotz, 1953), (Mette, 1964).
doc.reconstruction:
Klytaemnestra's complaint, expressed in fr.106, could be followed by a dialogue in which Orestes asks her not to wait for Aegisthus and gives reasons for his absence (cf. D'Anna, 1967, 206f.). In a similar context, the addressee is possibly Aegisthus who asks for information about Oiax (Hofmann-Szantyr,1972, 607; Bennett II,1910-1914, 132f). This fragment can also refer to a dialogue between Orestes and Pylades, with the assumption that Orestes sends Pylades away before the act of revenge ( Mette, 1964, 97). Ribbeck (1897, 247) also supports that Pylades is the addressee. Warmington (1936, 220) supports that the question introduced with quando implies the neglection of a friend in the past and speculates that this friend can possibly be Oiax or another minor character.

D’Anna, I. (1967). M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta. Leipzig.

Schierl, P. (2006). Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin; New York.

Warmington, E. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.location: De compendiosa doctrina, vol III, Lindsay, ed.Teubner
context.text:
Nonius Marcellus presents two examples of the use of the adverb amiciter instead of the use of the adverb amice, one from Plautus and one from Pacuvius.

NON. 821 L. (510,20 M.) AMICITER, pro amice. Plautus in Persa (255): quia meo amico amiciter hanc commoditatis copiam danunt. Pacuvius Duloreste (131): nunc ne illum expectes, quando amico amiciter fecisti.

lines.text: nunc ne illum exspectes, quando amico amiciter
fecisti
Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Chryses
doc.translation: Yet, I think, I have discovered, who of the two is Orestes
doc.differences:
Schierl 70 (9/25)
D’Anna 122 (14/28)
Warmington 100 (17/27)
Ribbeck 3 101 (14/21)
