opino, Orestes] opino Orestes Ribbeck2/3 qui esse optumum ex seq. loc. Enn. Eum. huc illata intellexit : opino esse optumum Orestes codd.
uter esset] uter esset Roth, pler. edd. : ut heres sed codd.

(Schierl 2006, 220)

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes the line in book 8 of his De compendiosa doctrina, De contrariis generibus verborum, in which he had also quoted Pacuv. frg. 65 Schierl (Non. 748 L.: aucupavi, activum positum pro passivo):

opino pro opinor. …. Pacuvius Chryse: inveni [esse optumum] Orestes uter esset tamen.

The other passages Nonius quotes here are from tragedy (Ennius Eumenides frg. 54 TRF, and comedy: Plaut. Bacch. frg. 10, and Caecilius Astotus, frg. 17 Ribb. = frg. 7).

lines.text: inveni, opino, Orestes uter esset tamen
Flucti inmisericordes iacere, taetra ad saxa adlidere

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: They fear whom they hate, and anyone who hates a man, also wishes him dead
doc.differences:
TrRF: 163 (frg. inc. )
Jocelyn: 348 (frg. inc. )
Warmington: 410 (frg. inc. )
Vahlen2: 402 (frg. inc. )
Ribbeck2/3: 379 (frg. inc.)
doc.apparatus:
oderunt] codd. pler. : odere Gothanus

periisse] Bibl. Brit. Add. 11935, Guelpherbytanus XIX.43 : perisse codd. pler.

expetit] codd. pler. : cupit Bernensis 104

perisse for periisse is a simple haplographic mistake. expetit for cupit could be the intrusion of a scribal gloss.
doc.commentary:
quem metuunt oderunt] There is such a verbal affinity with the Accian line oderint dum metuant (Acc. Atreus, frg. 204 R = frg. 10 Dangel) that it is hard to argue that Accius’ Atreus (ca. 140BC) does not engage with this line, whether it belongs to Ennius’ Thyestes (supposedly written shortly before his death in 169BC, cf. Cic. Brut. 78) or another of his plays. However, even if the Accian line is obviously the words of a tyrant – all our sources indicate that – the Ennian line is more ambiguous. It could be a word of warning about or to a tyrant, or it could be the tyrant’s own paranoid assessment.

Metre
The line is written in the metre of trochaic septenarii. The line’s rhythm is full of spondees and resolutions: the anceps syllables produce longs and a couple of the long syllables are resolved into two shorts. None of the anceps syllables consists of a single short. There is an elision at the diaresis after the second foot as well. All this extends the feel of the length of the line and perhaps adds to its sententiousness. The second relative pronoun quem becomes the antecedent in the accusative-infinite construction (eum) periisse expetit, ‘he wishes him dead’.
doc.reconstruction:
The similarity with the Accian line oderint dum metuant was noticed by Ribbeck. Accius’ line is one of the most famous verses from Roman Republican tragedy and has been quoted at least 10 times in ancient literature. According to e.g. Cic. Off. 1.97 the words, in Accius’ play, were spoken by Atreus. On the basis of their similiarity with Accius’ line, Ribbeck suggested Ennius’ verse were the words of Atreus to his satelles. However, both of the apparent receptions of Ennius’ line – in Ovid’s Amores and Seneca’s Thyestes (see the commentary below) – are assessments by a second party of powerful person’s position, which might suggest that it is spoken by Atreus or to Atreus, or even shared as part of a hemistichal dialogue, such as appears as a famous feature of Seneca’s tragedy.

La Penna (La Penna (1972) 15) was less confident that Seneca based his plot on Ennius’ Thyestes, citing Jocelyn’s arguments (Jocelyn (1967) 415-9) that Ennius’ script was actually set in the court of Thesprotus, after the events of the feast, but La Penna still thought that the fragment belonged in a Thyestes. Nevertheless, the only character who displays actual cruelty, even if the play included events at Thesprotus, is Atreus. It has to be about a figure who exerts the kind of power that a group of people hate. It is a general statement, so it could be spoken by almost anyone, including the chorus (except the metre would make this unlikely), or some outsider.

It lacks the dramatic power of the Accian line, which perhaps offers some explanation why Accius’ line was quoted so often, and Ennius’ line only once by Cicero in a philosophical work (not one of his law-court speeches).

The words are echoed in Ovid’s Amores 2.2 and Seneca’s tragedy Thyestes. In Ovid’s poem, the words are addressed to a slave Bagoe (a pseudonym based on the name of two Persian eunuch courtiers) the chaperone of the girl Ovid wants to seduce. Ovid addresses him as o custos – Bagoe is thus in a position of power over the success of the poet’s love-scheming, but the poet is trying to convince Bagoe that he is vulnerable and has plenty to lose at the poet’s hands. As Booth notes in her commentary ad loc., there is a “note of controlled menace” in Ovid’s words (si sapis, o custos, odium, mihi crede, mereri / desine; quem metuit quisque, perisse cupit, Am. 2.2.9-10) which contrasts with the tone of the rest of the poem. It would perhaps be going too far to impute a similar sense of menace to Ennius’ words, since the sentiment seems to be expressed as a more neutral warning in Seneca’s Thyestes.

In Seneca’s tragedy, the sentiment is part of a dialogue, in which the satelles, Atreus’ attendant, tries to keep his master from taking revenge on his brother Thyestes. The dialogue starts in the style of a domina-nutrix-scene: the satelles cites plenty of general statements in order to help Atreus change his mind. In this context, he argues that fear may force men to praise, yet, at the same time, fear will drive these men to hate (quos cogit metus | laudare, eosdem reddit inimicos metus, Thy. 207-208). Yet, in the course of the dialogue, the satelles’ attitude seems to change. After several attempts to change his master’s mind, he swears loyalty to him, helps to work out the details of his plans, and promises that he won’t reveal these plans to anyone else (see Thy. 334-335). The satelles is clearly afraid of Atreus. That said, the line, ex post, can also be read as a warning, which addresses the satelles’ potential to build up hatred toward Atreus: if the satelles’ words suggest loyalty, his alleged loyalty will be based on fear and all the more demonstrate his hate.

lines.text: quem metuunt oderunt; quem quisque odit periisse expetit

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: cur me miseram irridet, magnis conpotem et multis malis?

doc.translation: Why is he/she mocking me, who am wretched and have a share in great and numerous misfortunes?
doc.differences:
TRF: 144 (Accius' Clytemnestra 7/10)

Ribbeck: 36 (Accius' Clytemnestra 6/10)

Warmington: 234 (Accius' Clytemnestra 1/11)

Franchella: 395 (Accius' Clytemnestra 6/10)

D'Antò: 36 (Accius' Clytemnestra 6/10)

Dangel: 295 (Accius' Clytemnestra 5/10)


Warmington:

Why does she jeer at me, unhappy me, | Partaker in many great misfortunes?

Franchella:

cur me miseram inridet magnis conpotem et multis malis?

Perché si fa gioco di me misera, afflitta da molti grandi mali?

D'Antò:

cur me miseram inridet, magnis compotem et multis malis?

Perché deride me misera, afflitta da grandi e molti mali?

Dangel:

Pourquoi dans mon malheur se rit-elle de moi qui ai en partage et à profusion de grands maux?
doc.apparatus:
magis] me AA: meae LBACA

magnis] magnis Lipsius: magis codd.

A reminder of Lindsay’s ‘index siglorum’ (1903, I.vi):

AA = source of manuscripts H et PE

BA = source of manuscript G et of the following correcting hands: H2 L2 P2 E2

CA = source of manuscripts Paris. 7666, Lugd., Bamb., Turic.

Lindsay misreports Lipsius’ conjecture. He reports that Lipsius (1875, 175) conjectured malis for magis, which implies that the manuscript reading of the final word of the line is magis, but in fact what Lipsius suggested was that the manuscripts’ reading magis compotem be replaced with magnis compotem. His mistake seems to have arisen owing to the similarity between magis in the middle of the line and malis at the end of the line, and he seems to have confused the two. In fact, the manuscripts read malis at line-end. Ribbeck3 suggests that Accius wrote mee or mehe (so Klotz’s apparatus) in accordance with his habit of reduplicating letters (Quint. Inst. 1.5.21: nam ‘mehe’ quoque pro ‘me’ apud antiquos tragoediarum praecipua scriptores in ueteribus libris inuenimus, 'we actually find mehe for me in old texts, especially of the early tragic poets', tr. Russell).
doc.commentary: me miseram…magnis compotem et multis malis: The alliterative sequence of the letter m is striking and is found elsewhere in the Accian corpus, e.g. Atreus F 104.5 TRF (maior mihi moles, maius miscendum est malum). For reference to both magnitude and multitude of misfortune, cf. Pl. Pseud. 770 malam rem magnam multasque aerumnas danunt 'they allot a big thrashing and many toils to him' (tr. De Melo). Magna multitudo is common (e.g. Caes. B.G. 2.11.4, Cic. Verr. 2.4.54, Sall. Cat. 30.1). For multi and magnus together, cf. Hor. Epist. 1.16.42 multae magnaeque; Liv. 42.11.9 multis magnisque). D’Antò 203 believes this could be a case of disiunctio. On this phenomenon, see Marouzeau 1935, 333, who cites as an example Cicero De consulatu suo fr. II.1 Soubiran: aetherio flammatus Iuppiter igni (= Div. 1.11).
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker of this fragment has been argued to be:

(i) Electra during her exchange with Clytemnestra (Bothe I.195, Venini 1954, 325-6);

(ii) Cassandra responding either to the chorus (Hermann apud Ribbeck1, xiii) or to Clytemnestra, in which case the subject of mockery is perhaps Cassandra’s relationship with Agamemnon (Ribbeck 1875, 463, Warmington II.407, Argenio 1966, 31, Franchella 250 n. 3, D’Antò 203, Pociña 120);

(iii) Clytemnestra (Dangel 322): filled with jealousy, Clytmnestra makes a sarcastic hint to the relationship between Agamemnon and Clytemnestra (cf. Ribbeck 1875, 463).

All of these options are plausible. By placing this fragment in the first position of the play and identifying the speaker as Cassandra, Warmington suggests that an encounter between Cassandra and Clytemnestra occurred towards the beginning of the play: on this account, the plot of Accius’ Clytemnestra would be radically different to that of, for instance, Aeschylus’ Agamemnon, in which Clytemnestra’s first encounter with Cassandra occurs at line 1035 (out of a total of 1673).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 521.25-27 Mercier = 839.24-26 Lindsay:

compotem bonae rei solum dici existimatur, cum etiam mala †re positum sit. Accius Clytaemestra: cur me miseram inridet magnis conpotem et multis malis.

The fragment is preserved by Nonius in his lemma on compotem (nom. compos ‘having the mastery of or control over a thing; partaking of, possessing, participating or sharing in’). A lemma for compotem had already occurred earlier (456.20-5 Mercier = 731.20-5 Lindsay) with a slightly different formulation (in bonam partem solum accipi putatur, cum et in malam positum sit) and Pl. Epidicus 559 (accipe aerumnarum et miseriarum compotem [sc. mulierem] 'you're holding a woman distressed and afflicted by sorrows', tr. De Melo) and Naevius Danae F 10 FRL (eam nunc esse inuentam probris | compotem scis 'and now you know she has been found to be a partaker in unchastity', tr. Maltby–Slater) are quoted. In both instances, Nonius reports that the word is usually believed only to have a positive sense and the examples he quotes serve to show that this is misguided as the word is also attested in a negative sense. The negative sense of compos which Nonius mentions is found only in pre-Classical and post-Augustan writers and refers to misfortune or guilt (cf. Pl. Truc. 835, Quint. Inst. Orat. 12.1.7).

doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarius (tr7).

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: sed valvae resonunt regiae

doc.translation: But the doors of the palace resound.
doc.differences:
TRF: 147 (Accius' Clytemnestra 10/10)

Ribbeck: 29 (Accius' Clytemnestra 1/10)

Warmington: 244 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/11)

Franchella: 389 (Accius' Clytemnestra 1/10)

D'Antò: 30 (Accius' Clytemnestra 1/10)

Dangel: 294 (Accius' Clytemnestra 4/10)

Warmington: But the doors of the palace resound.

Franchella:

⏑-⏑- sed ualuae resonunt regiae

or

sed ualuae resonunt regiae ⏑-⏑-

Ma le porte della reggia risuonano.

D'Antò: …sed ualuae resonunt regiae

...ma i battenti regali cigolano.

Dangel:

Mais les portes du palais résonnent.
doc.apparatus:
ualuae] ualuae Aug. MS in margine uel Delrius: ualde codd. Priscianus

regiae] regiae ⟨domus⟩ Vespolatus(?)

Contrary to what H. Keil and M. Hertz, who co-edited Priscian's Institutiones Grammaticarum in Volumes 2 and 3 of Grammatici Latini, report in their apparatus, the manuscript evidence seems unanimously to bear witness to the reading sed ualde. The L manuscript (Leiden Universiteitsbibliothek BPL 67 = 270 Passalacqua) does not seem to read ual*de as reported by Keil–Hertz. While there is some space between the l and the d, that space is by no means significant and there is no clear sign of the erasure of a letter between the l and the d. As for the K reading, it has been misreported by Keil–Hertz as sualde whereas in fact the reading is sed (abbreviated) ualde (f. 72r).

The reading ualuae is a conjecture made either by A. Agustìn, archbishop of Tarragone who produced an edition of Accius at some point in the second half of the sixteenth century (the terminus ante quem is 1586, year of his death: on this edition, see on F 143 TRF, 'Fragment apparatus criticus'), or by Del Rio, whose Syntagma tragoediae Latinae was first published in 1593 (cf. I.179), though it is of course perfectly possible that each of them thought of the conjecture independently. Del Rio notes that some early printed editions have an additional word, domus. He is referring to the sed ualde resonunt regiae domus which is attested in MS Leipzig UnivB. 1232 (f. 96r), reported by Ribbeck2, 139, and e.g. in 1476 Milan edition of Priscian, ISTC No ip00963000: the typographist is identified by Ganda 1985 as Domenico Giliberti da Vespolate. It is unlikely that domus is to be understood as a conjectural emendation, i.e. an attempt to improve the text: a more plausible explanation is that domus is a gloss on regiae (i.e. to clarify that the form regiae is the genitive singular of the substantive regia (‘palace’ = domus) and not the adjective regius, -a, -um) that was later copied into the text of the Leipzig manuscript.
doc.commentary:
resonunt: the third-conjugation verb resonere is occasionally found in Early Latin (cf. Enn. Ann. 355 Skutsch, Acc. Aeneadae siue Decius F 12 TRF); resonare, the Classical form, is also found (e.g. Pacuvius F 156 TRF = 170 Schierl clamore et sonitu colles resonantes bount).

ualuae…regiae: Del Rio’s conjecture ualuae has much to commend it, as Accius uses the expression ualuae…regiae elsewhere in his tragedies (Neoptolemus F 301 TRF atque adeo ualuas sonere sensi regias), and it is also found at Adesp. F 152 TRF (ibo ad eam, ut sciscam quid uelint. <...> ualuae sonunt ‘I shall go to her in order to find out what they want… the doors are sounding’, a fragment for a long time thought to belong to Pacuvius’ Iliona). Moreover, the expression is found in Vitruvius where it refers to the middle door of the three doors on a Roman stage whether the stage was configured for a tragedy, a comedy, or a satyric play (De arch. 5.6.3 reliqui [sc. cunei] quinque scaenae designabunt compositionem: et unus medius contra se ualuas regias habere debet, et qui erunt sinistra, hospitaliorum designabunt compositionem ‘The remaining five [cunei, i.e. the blocks of seats in a theatre] will indicate the arrangement of the stage. One in the middle should have the palace doors opposite to it. Those which are to the right and left, will indicate the apartments provided for strangers’; 5.6.8 ipsae autem scaenae suas habent rationes explicitias ita, uti mediae ualuae ornatus habeant aulae regiae, dextra ac sinistra hospitalia ‘The scenery itself is so arranged that the middle doors are figured like a royal palace, the doors on the right and left are for strangers’, tr. Granger): cf. Beare 19603, 247. If ualuae regiae was already in Accius’ time the technical term for the doors of a palace on the tragic stage, it would be attractive to see in this fragment a comment put in the mouth of a character on a reality of stagecraft which was possibly designed by the dramatist to create a metatheatrical effect. We know that Accius dealt ‘with the Realien of the stage’ in his scholarly works on the theatre (Slater 1992, 99 with refs. to Accius Didascalica 11 Dangel and Varro L.L. 7.64).
doc.reconstruction:
Most scholars take an agnostic stance when it comes to trying to situate this fragment in the plot of the play, usually saying that it probably indicated the arrival of a character on stage. Ribbeck (1875, 464) suggests that the speaker could be Oeax or Clytemnestra.

But some rather adventurous possibilities have been proposed. Argenio 1966, 31 suggests that the line could have been spoken by Electra as she sees the palace doors opening while preparing Orestes’ escape; Franchella (248 n. 1, 250 with n. 1) proposes that this fragment belonged to the prologue in which Agamemnon’s triumphant return to Argos was celebrated. Franchella interprets the sed as marking an important moment in the prologue and imagines that the scene shifted from the preparation in secret of a plot to murder Agamemnon, to the beginning of the celebration of his return as the doors of the palace open. He suggests that the fragment depicts the entrance on stage of a new character based on the line’s similarity with Pac. Dulorestes F 97 TRF = 117 Schierl (...quidnam autem hoc soniti est, quod stridunt fores?) and Acc. Neoptolemus F 301 TRF (atque adeo ualuas sonere sensi regias). Dangel 322 understands that this fragment relates to the return of Agamemnon at Argos with the spoils of war and with Cassandra, whom Dangel suggests might actually have arrived before Agamemnon on a different ship.

context.author: Priscian
context.text:
Priscian p. II.473.22–474.5 GLK:

haec tamen [i.e. uerba uelut sonare, sonui, sonitum and tonare, tonui, tonitum] ipsa et secundum tertiam uetustissimi protulisse inueniuntur coniugationem, ut Ennius in XI annali: tunc clipei resonunt et ferri stridit acument. idem in XIIII: litora lata sonunt. Accius in Clytaemnestra: sed valvae resonunt regiae.

Priscian explains that archaic authors (uetustissimi) use verbs such as sonare and tonare in the third conjugation rather than in the first, adducing this fragment of Accius along with Enn. Ann. 355 Skutsch (tum clipei resonunt et ferri stridit acumen) as examples.

doc.metrical_analysis: The TRF edition lists the metre of this fragment as unidentifiable, indicating this with a question mark, as does Dangel. Most previous editors have identified the metre as the iambic senarius. Ribbeck posits a lacuna at the beginning of the line. Franchella 243 (v. 389 Franchella) points out that the sed ualuae resonunt regiae can go either at the end of an iambic senarius (⏑-⏑- sed ualuae resonunt regiae) or at the beginning (sed ualuae resonunt regiae ⏑-⏑-).

lines.text: Mulier una duum uirorum

doc.author: Livius Andronicus
doc.title: Aegisthus
doc.translation: And have you done with gladdening your eyes upon a sight desirable?
doc.differences:
TRF (4/8)
Warmington (8/8)
Ribbeck (3/8)
doc.commentary:
laetavisti: the form laetavisti, as preserved in the majority of the manuscripts, seems to be the most satisfying and the one that most aptly illustrates the meaning (in the sense “do you make your eyes happy [at a sight of something]?”). Nevertheless, it is not a common verb (there is no lemma on TLL) and it might be possible that Nonius himself, whose sources were also glossaries that might have contained mistakes, has falsely transcribed it. The alternative writing in the manuscripts, laetaviri, does not generate any meaning and must be a misspelling. Interestingly, from a psychological point of view such a mistake could give us a window in the scribe’s mind, who might have been thinking of a vir mentioned in one of the previous verses (Wessels 2020). As much useful as it would be to use this consideration as an argument that the sight being described is a male character, we must be aware that we are not provided with any actual information regarding the original wording.

specie: sight, appearance, as if facies or effigies (Non. 617 L.).
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment poses a difficult interpretation and can be read in a large variety of ways. The only information that we can deduce with certainty is that the question refers to the viewing of a very desirable sight. Bothe places the fragment first as a question regarding the long desired view of the homeland, addressed to the messenger, who interrupts the main action, the same way as in Aeschylus’ and Seneca’s Agamemnon (503 sqq; 392sqq respectively). It could be also possible to perceive this question being about the booty of Troy addressed either to the messenger (Ribbeck 1875, 30), or even to Agamemnon himself, not unlikely by Clytemnestra while inviting her husband to enter the palace (Spaltenstein 2008, 69). This question could also be placed at a moment after Agamemnon’s death, in which case we could image Electra ironically addressing her mother on the sight of Agamemnon’s corpse (Ribbeck 1875, 31). Regarding the sight of Agamemnon’s body Waszink and Antonelli have suggested a verbal dispute between Aegisthus and Clytemnestra. It would not, finally, be implausible to imagine an argument between Electra and Chrysothemis at the sight of Aegisthus’ corpse (Sanford, Aricó and Spaltenstein).

Bibliography

Antonelli Rinaldi, M. R. 1982. Intorno all’ Aegistus di Livio Andronico. Saggio di lettera dei frammenti. RCCM 24, 3-14.
Aricó, G. 1979. Sull’ Aegisthus di Livio Andronico, in Studi di poesia latina, stud. coll. in hon. A. Traglia, vol. 1, Rom, 3-9.
Ribbeck, O. 1985, Die römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig
Sanford, E. M. 1922/23. The Tragedies of Livius Andronicus. The Classical Journal, Vol. 18, No. 5, 274-285.
Spaltenstein, F. 2008. Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Livius Andronicus. Bruxelles.
Warmington, E. H. (ed). 1936-1940. Remains of Old Latin, 4 voll. London.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
laetare, et laetiscere, laetificare. Livius Aegistho (7 [R.]):
iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili?
Accius Pelopidis (513 [R.]):
et te ut triplici || bono.
Sisenna lib. IV (123): 'utrumne divi cultu erga se mortalium laetiscant an superna agentes humana neglegant.

context.author: Nonius
context.text: species dicitur effigies, facies. Livius Aegistho (5 [R.]): iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili?

lines.text: iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili?

doc.author: Lucus
doc.title: Testing
doc.translation: Go, eat your bread in gladness, and drink your wine in joy; for your action was long ago approved by God.

lines.text: לֵ֣ךְ אֱכֹ֤ל בְּשִׂמְחָה֙ לַחְמֶ֔ךָ וּֽשְׁתֵ֥ה בְלֶב־ט֖וֹב יֵינֶ֑ךָ כִּ֣י כְבָ֔ר רָצָ֥ה הָאֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶֽת־מַעֲשֶֽׂיךָ׃

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Eumenides
doc.translation: I shall explain and express that what I did was fair and just.
doc.differences:
TRF 55 (4/4)

Jocelyn 66 (4/4)

Warmington 154 (4/7)

Ribbeck3 135 (3/4)

Vahlen2 148 (4/7)


Warmington: - ⏑ ego aecum ac ius fecisse expedibo atque eloquar

Vahlen2: id ego ius atque aecum fecisse expedibo atque eloquar

Ribbeck3: id ego aecum fecisse expedibo atque eloquar
doc.apparatus:
ego aecum] ego aequum Parisinus 7667 : ego aecum cett. : (a)equum ego. ed. princ. 1471

accius fecisse] accius fecisse codd. : Accius fecisse ed. princ. 1471 : aptius fecisse Jenson : ac ius fecisse Mercier : fecisse me Delrius : ac ius fuisse Vossius : aecum fecisse [accius del.] Ribbeck1 : aecum me ac ius fecisse Quicherat : atque aecum fecisse Vahlen [ius et aecum transp.] : atque ius fecisse Klotz : ac iustum fecisse Jocelyn

eloquar] aeloquar Parisinus 7667 : eloquar cett.

The central problem in this line is that the manuscripts transmit accius, which is impossible, as given, to make any sense of as anything but a citation of the playwright Accius. The editio princeps did indeed see fit to speculate that only part of the Ennian line was transmitted and that there followed another citation from Accius. However, the other citations in this lemma are accompanied by the title of their work. It seems obvious instead to emend accius (given that Accius' name appears earlier in the lemma, it is not hard to see how the scribe might have made a mistake). While Lindsay thought the head quotation might belong to the Accius 2 list Nonius was working from, he thought the secondary quotations might come from List 27, which is a list belonging to the anonymous glossarian (whose habits have been discussed by Welsh) who tended to quote in complete metrical units, preserving sense. (In fact, all of the quotations in this lemma may be drawn from the anonymous glossarian). This strengthens the likelihood that we have a complete trochaic tetrameter here.

If we have a trochaic tetrameter, then Mercier's emendation of accius to ac ius from the transmitted reading of the manuscripts will produce a line that scans, so long as we accept two hiatuses: one at the diaresis, which is unproblematic, and another between aecum and ac, which is a locus Jacobsonianus and therefore also unproblematic. Jocelyn finds them suspicious in company [bib-KDEMX3WU-Jocelyn-1967-289], but double hiatus occurs often enough in Plautus (e.g. Poenulus 474, Amphitryo 498) that it seems wrong to rule it impossible in Ennius, particularly if aecum ac ius is seen as a phrase that is being particularly emphasised in the line. A second issue, also highlighted by Jocelyn, involves the difficulty of taking facere with both id and aecum ac ius, given that the latter are nouns, so that we end up with facere seemingly having two objects. The main editorial response has been to this has been to turn aecum ac iusinto adjectives modifying id. It is not really clear how to take this - there do not seem to be any good parallels for the line if they are treated as 'adverbial accusatives', 'I shall explain and express that I did that according to fairness and justice'. A third issue Jocelyn points out is that we might expect the word order aecum ac iustum for the adjectives, but ius atque aecum for the nouns. While there is an excellent parallel for the order ius atque aecum in a fragment from Ennius' Hectoris Lytra (TrRF 62), the other instances of the set phrasing fall apart on closer examination (see commentary), and this does not seem to be clinching evidence for a problem in the word order.

An early suggestion, and the most popular amongst sixteenth century editions, was aptius ('more suitably') for the MSS accius. aecum can then be understood as an adjective modifying id, rather than a noun. The line then translates 'I shall explain and express that I more suitably (or 'rather suitably') did this just act'. This is however makes the line seem more boring and also the comparative seems quite unmotivated.

Among other conjectures, Ribbeck simply deleted accius entirely, to create a senarius, which seems unnecessarily drastic. Vossius' fuisse [bib-3UVK8KHG-Vossius-1620-24] similarly aims to create a senarius, and alters the syntax so that id now becomes the subject of the accusative infinitive clause, but there is no good reason to suspect fecisse, and we would still expect adjectives as arguments for fuisse, rather than nouns. Quicherat's insertion [bib-PPK7ZZ5G-Quicherat-1872-590] of me removes the hiatus but does not change the syntax. Vahlen rearranges to read ius atque aecum, which also removes the first hiatus, but this also requires changing ac to atque in addition to the transposition. Finally, Jocelyn suggests reading iustum in place of ius, making it an adjective that is syntactically simple: 'I shall explain and express that what I did was fair and just', which also removes the need for the first hiatus. However, the loss of -tum is not particularly explicable, and should surely also have made a scribal diplography of ac ius to accius far less likely, if ac iustum was written in the antegraph.

It is worth noting that the syntax is not awkward if we understand facio in the sense of 'I make X into Y, I render X as Y' (OLD 17a / TLL 6.1.111.77-112.62). facere would not then refer to the carrying out of whatever id referred to (i.e. 'I did X') but a statement about establishing its legality. So the translation would be something like 'I shall explain and declare that I have made that thing the law and justice'. See further the reconstruction.
doc.commentary:
aecum ac ius⟨tum⟩] Jocelyn argues that 'the normal order of the doublet seems to have been ius atque aecum', citing: a line from Ennius' Hectoris Lytra, TrRF F62, ius atque aecum se a malis spernit procul, which is indeed a good parallel; Pl. Stich. 423, et ius et aequom postulas; Ter. Heaut. 642, qui neque ius neque bonum atque aequom sciunt; Cic. Brut. 145, quid in iure aut in aequo; and one instance each from Tacitus (Annals 14.18), Fronto (Epistulae ad M. Caesarem 5.51), and Claudian (Epithalamium de Nuptiis Honorii Augusti 313). It is true that ius does appear in these instances before aequum, although none except for the Ennian line use the preposition atque. Sallust places aequum before ius: fit reus magis ex aequo bonoque quam ex iure gentium Bomilcar, Jug. 35.7. Here aequum (coupled with bonum, as at Ter. Heaut. 642) is being opposed to ius. It does not seem impossible that Ennius was inverting the usual order of the words (followed at TrRF 62), in order to lay stress on aecum, a stress compounded by the hiatus between aecum and ac. Jocelyn argues that for the adjectives the order was inverted, i.e. aequus atque iustus, citing four instances (Pl. Amph. 16, Cic. Phil. 2.72, Cic. Fin. 3.71, and Sen. Ep. 95.52) but arguing that two counter-examples (Cic. Fin. 3.71, at the end of the paragraph, and Hor. Sat. 1.3.98) are dictated by rhythm (this cannot be the case for the Cicero passage since the scansion ends up being identical) - but why should that objection on metrical grounds not be possible for aecum ac ius in Ennius TrRF 55?

In TrRF 62, the speaker seems to be reducing ius atque aequum solely to ius: it is given a singular verb and in the preceding line the speaker only speaks of ius. aequum precedes ius in Sallust precisely where Sallust draws a distinction between ius and aequum, and Cicero often places aequitas (albeit not aequum) before ius (e.g. De Oratore 1.173, Pro Rabirio 14). In TrRF 55, the speaker may intentionally be placing a greater stress on aequum than on ius, and a different relationship between the two concepts, and this would be heightened by the unusual word order and by the hiatus.

expedibo] This seems to be used here of revelatory speech: the speaker promises to reveal or explain something that is hidden or unclear. That seems to be the also the case in its usage in Pacuvius' Atalanta, another quote from the Nonian lemma, and perhaps also for the line from Accius' Nyctegresia, where the speaker may be promising to reveal something once their addressee has sworn an oath.

eloquor] In early Latin this can mean simply 'to speak out' or 'to declare'.
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment seems to introduce an extended speech: that would also fit well with a line in trochaic tetrameters, which are a typical metre for a speech by a character. The speech must be a self-justification (ego... expedibo atque eloquar for something that has been done by the speaker themselves (id fecisse). One obvious candidate is Orestes, which was suggested by Scaliger, who paralleled [bib-4GWN6MHR-Scaliger-1565-134] Aesch. Eum. 468-9, σὺ δ’ εἰ δικαίως εἴτε μὴ κρῖνον δίκην· πράξας γὰρ ἐν σοὶ πανταχῆι τάδ’ αἰνέσω ('And you, judge if I killed justly or not; however you treat me in your judgement, I will acquiesce'). In his first edition, Ribbeck thought they were the words of Apollo (an opinion that has been shared by many subsequent editors, including Vahlen and Warmington), and he compared both Apollo's opening words defending Orestes before the Athenian jury, Aesch. Eum. 614-21, and his words later on advocating the rights of the father over the mother, Aesc. Eum. 657-666. In his monograph [bib-XCEJBREP-Ribbeck-1875-147], Ribbeck specifically noted the opening words of line 657, καὶ τοῦτο λέξω, καὶ μάθ’ ὡς ὀρθῶς ἐρῶ ('And I will explain this, and note how I speak correctly'), which do seem to have more than a passing resemblance to the second half of the Ennian line. However, in this work Ribbeck also suggested understanding me not eum in the Ennian line, i.e. that Orestes himself gave the speech for the defence, not Apollo. Finally, Vossius suggested that Athena could have spoken the lines (but without suggesting a parallel in Aeschylus' Eumenides), but this was on the basis of accepting the reading fuisse for fecisse (see further the apparatus), so that id would become the subject of fuisse, not the object of fecisse: Athena would be explaining how Orestes' act was just and fair. Since this conjecture has not found favour, editors have not been inclined to see these as Athena's words.

As Jocelyn notes, '[t]he substance of the Ennian verse, which is plain whatever the details of its restoration, departs a long way from the spirit of Aeschylus' [Eumenides]' [bib-KDEMX3WU-Jocelyn-1967-289]. It is interesting that both Scaliger's and Ribbeck's parallels can each only correspond to one half of the Ennian line. Scaliger could find a decent parallel for the first half of the line in the Aeschylean Orestes' words, but not the second: in the passage Scaliger cites, Aeschylus' Orestes clearly poses it as a question for the jury to answer, whereas the Ennian verse suggests that the speaker is going to answer such a question himself. Ribbeck's parallel from the words of the Aeschylean Apollo correspond well with the second half of the Ennian line, but he offers nothing for the first half of the line. Therefore, Jocelyn seems to be correct in saying that Ennius must be departing somewhat from Aeschylus' script. Scaliger's and Ribbeck's parallels may be suggestive but they are not convincing. Several options present themselves:

1. Understanding me with id... fecisse and postulating an Ennian Orestes who offers a much more vigorous self-defence of his actions than the Aeschylean Euripides.

2. Understanding eum with id... fecisse and postulating an Ennian Apollo who defends Orestes in an arguably more aggressive and straightforward manner than the Aeschylean Apollo.

3. Understanding id... fecisse with aecum ac ius as 'I made that act right and fair' and postulating an Ennian Athena as a law-maker, establishing precedent from the verdict.


Accepting (1) is quite hard with the syntax of the transmitted reading, so some conjecture must be accepted (see further the apparatus criticus). The same applies to (2), with the further issue that an understood eum is tricky to understand, whereas an understood me is unproblematic. However, with slight alteration of the transmitted reading, (1) has real force. Still, whatever reading for (1) or (2) is accepted, the Ennian line would have no exact analogue in the Aeschylean text and at best would be a conglomeration of ideas expressed at different points. (3) would enable the transmitted reading (with the obvious correction of accius to ac ius) to be retained, but it would be an even greater departure, making Athena not merely the presiding juror of the Areopagus (her vote may be the casting one, but this seems to be only because the jurors number 601 with Athena included), but more like a magistrate. Perhaps this is not so ridiculous when one considers Ennius was writing for a Roman audience who might expect a iudex in a trial - jury courts were only introduced in Rome twenty years after Ennius' death. Perhaps Ennius may have anticipated that the lack of a figure resembling a magistrate would have been too alien to his Roman audience.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Expedibo, pro expediam. Pacuvius Atalanta: nam quod conabar, cum interventum est, dicere, nunc expedibo. Accius Nyctegresia: cuncta expedibo: id modo iusiurandum date. Pomponius Vernionibus: sed qui utrosque error vos agitat, expedibo. Ennius Eumenidibus: id ego aecum ac ius fecisse expedibo atque eloquar.

Found in Nonius' 11th book on verbs with changed conjugations. A form, expedibo, with an ending in -bo resembling a first or second conjugation future, is found in some texts in place of the expected future form expediam. All the instances in Nonius are of the first person singular future.

lines.text: id ego aecum ac ius⟨tum⟩ fecisse expedibo atque eloquar
ut naufragio pereat Atreus!
ipse summis saxis fixus asperis evisceratus,
latere pendens saxa spargens tabo sanie et sanguine atro,
Neque sepulchrum quo recipiat habeat portum corporis
ubi remissa humana uita corpus requiescat malis.
Nemo haec vostrorum ruminetur mulieri
eho tu: di, quibus est potestas motus superum atque inferum
pacem inter sese conciliant, conferunt concordiam.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Eumenides
doc.translation: I say that Orestes has triumphed; all of you, leave this man alone!
doc.differences:
TRF 53 (2/4)
Jocelyn 64 (2/4)
Warmington 155 (5/7)
Ribbeck3 136 (4/4)
Vahlen2149 (5/7)


TRF: dico vicisse Orestem; vos ab hoc facessite
Jocelyn: dico vicisse Orestem; vos ab hoc facessite
Warmington: edico vicisse Orestem—Vos ab hoc facessite.
Ribbeck3: Dico vicisse Orestem: Vos facessite
Vahlen2: Dico vicisse Orestem; vos ab hoc facessite.


Warmington adopts Mueller's edico, to make the line a trochaic septenarius. Ribbeck in all his editions follows Bothe in deleting ab hoc to create an iambic senarius.
doc.apparatus:
dico] dico codd. : dico ego Popma [bib-IAFKJMEB-Popma--551] : iudico Spengel [bib-3NC8G5E4-Spengel-1899-395] : edico Mueller [bib-LJ8Z9TCR-Müller-1888-485]

Orestem: vos ab] horestē uos ab ed. princ. : horestē uos ab hoc Urbin. 307 : oresten uos ab L : orestennuos sab Gen. H.1 (corr. H2 Oresten uos ab) : Oresten: vos (ab hoc del.) Bothe

facessite] facessite Urbin. 307 : facessit rel.

The fragment as transmitted, if it is meant to be a complete line, fits neither an iambic senarius nor a trochaic septenarius. Bothe, followed by Ribbeck in all his editions and Vahlen in his first, suggested deleting ab hoc to make the line a iambic senarius: this seems an extremely dramatic cut to make on the paradosis. Rather better are the attempts other scholars have made to add an extra syllable to create a trochaic septenarius. While Popma's dico ego creates a strong contrast with the vos in the other half of the line, the elision seems awkward and the conjecture does nothing to increase a trochaic rhythm. Spengel's iudico does create a stronger trochaic rhythm (scanning as - ⏑ - )and is entirely appropriate, although there is no good palaeographic explanation for the two lost letters iu. Mueller's edico is also appropriate to a legal sentence, but scans - - - . While this scansion is unproblematic, it provides less of a recognisable trochaic rhythm compared to Spengel's conjecture. Both iudico and edico also result in a metrical pause corresponding to the sense pause between Orestem and vos. Jocelyn points out that dico without any prefix could be used in a formal sense of 'declare', although the example he provides, from Terence's Eunuchus, is not very good since dico is used in combination with edico: dico edico vobis nostrum esse illum erilem filium ('I tell you, I declare to you, that that man is the son of our master'). dico appears in the formula expressing the praetor's powers do, dico, addico, showing that dico could appear on its own without ius to express the passing of judgement. There is perhaps further motivation for finding a trochaic septenarius here: Welsh [bib-UE92SVX5-Welsh-2013-271-273] ascribes other quotations from this lemma to a glossary with a preference for full metrical lines, rather than the glossary Lindsay [bib-2ML88S9W-Lindsay-1901-71] suggested which has no such preference.

As for horestem and facessite, the readings attested in the MS Urbin. 307 (codex Vaticanus Urbinas latinus 307), since it is a fifteenth century manuscript, these almost certainly should be considered as resulting from scholarly correction, corrections also found in the editio princeps (on fifteenth century MSS of Nonius, see Onions [bib-ZWST9MWR-Onions-1895-xxiv-xxvi]. The insertion of a superfluous is common enough in fifteenth century Italian manuscripts: there is another example of horestes for orestes in a fifteenth century Italian manuscript of Ovid's Heroides [bib-9EB63UB9-Owen-1936-155-156]. An accusative Oresten is regular in Classical texts: Housman argues chiefly on metrical grounds that from Lucretius onwards a form such as Orestem would never be encountered [bib-9H4PPGPQ-Housman-1910-245-247]. However, a few -em endings for loaned Greek proper nouns can be proved by metre for Republican comedy (e.g. Ter. Hec. 432, Callidimidem hospitem) and between -em and -en, the former occurs more commonly in the manuscripts of Plautus and Terence. Jocelyn's generalisation that "Greek terminations of proper names did not appear on the stage until the time of Accius" [bib-KDEMX3WU-Jocelyn-1969-287] seems to go too far as regards Greek -en, given the MSS spread and the absence of any metrical data for authors aside from Plautus and Terence. In this fragment, metrically speaking either -em or -en is acceptable, since the syllable makes position when followed by uos. Palaeographically, the presence of the corruption Orestenn in one family of manuscripts might perhaps point to an original Orestem that was subsequently corrupted, and corrected to Oresten in the other two traditions, although Orestenn could also be produced by simple diplography as with the diplography of s seen later in the line, uos sab for uos ab. What one reads here, Orestes or Orestem, will be shaped by one's preconceptions about Ennius' relationship to his contemporary Roman poets and with the Greek dramatic traditions he also engaged with. One could either see Ennius as following his comic contemporary Plautus and sticking to Latin endings for borrowed Greek proper nouns, or as doing something different from the early comic authors and emulating Greek endings: either way one will be more influenced by one's own image of Ennius and one's own biases than any evidence (whether from the metre or from the manuscripts or anything else) that can be provided.
doc.commentary:
vicisse] The obvious presumption is that this refers to victory in the courtroom. vincere is used in this sense by Terence (cf. e.g. Phormio 132 quom tu horum nil refelles vincam scilicet, 135persuasit homini: factumst: ventumst: vincimur). The comparison with the examples from Terence shows that if the legal context was clear, vincere could be used without any clarifying qualifiers like causa or iudicium.

facessite] The example from Cicero's Laws, also quoted by Nonius (see Citation Context) where it is suggested that the Epicureans withdraw from involvement in the activities of the state, ab omni societate rei publicae... paulisper facessant rogemus seems quite similar to the construction in this fragment of Eumenides. While other examples do occur with hinc, there doesn't seem a good reason to follow Vossius in substituting hinc for ab hoc, and deleting these words leaves facessite looking rather bare.
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment is typically understood as the words of Athena at the conclusion of the trial: it is taken as a description of the acquittal of Orestes and a command to the Furies to leave Orestes be.

The parallels offered with Aeschylus' Eumenides include 752-3 by both Ribbeck [bib-XCEJBREP-Ribbeck-1875-148] and Warmington: Αθ. ἁνὴρ ὅδ’ ἐκπέφευγεν αἵματος δίκην· ἴσον γάρ ἐστι τἀρίθμημα τῶν πάλων. (Athena: 'This man has been acquitted on the charge of murder: for the number of votes is equal'). Ribbeck connects this fragment also with a fragment of the Ennian incerta (TRF II F172) Areopagitae quid dedere †quam pudam† (Ribbeck reads aequam pilam in place of †quam pudam†), a fragment which Warmington also sees as belonging to the scene of Orestes' acquittal. Colonna seems to connect the fragment with two lines spoken by Athena very slightly earlier (Aesch. Eum. 741-2), since he includes this passage in the parallels from Aeschylus' Eumenides that precede the Ennian fragments he prints: νικᾶι δ’ Ὀρέστης κἂν ἰσόψηφος κριθῆι. / ἐκβάλλεθ’ ὡς τάχιστα τευχέων πάλους ('But Orestes will win if the votes are tied. Empty the votes from the urns as quickly as possible'). Colonna observes that Ennius' language is more concentrated than Aeschylus', and also that Ennius has Athena spelling out the consequences of the judgement for the Furies (noster autem Graeco breuius, et significantius. Hic enim non tantum vicisse Orestem decernit Dea, sed addit, ut quod decreuerat, exsequatur). One difference that the different parallels offered by Warmington and Ribbeck, on the one hand, and Colonna, on the other, are that Ennius brings the idea of Orestes 'winning' the case into Athena's judgement itself. While Athena mentions the possibility of Orestes prevailing if the votes are tied at Aesch. Eum. 741, it is absent from the sentence pronounced by Athena at Aesch. Eum. 752-3, where Athena speaks not of Orestes' victory but his acquittal. Athena's vote in Aeschylus' version is likely the vote that produces the tie, rather than breaking it [bib-8VABEIJ2-Gagarin-1975]. In Aeschylus' play, Athena goes on to explicitly say that the Furies have not been defeated (Aesch. Eum. 794-5): οὐ γὰρ νενίκησθ’, ἀλλ’ ἰσόψηφος δίκη / ἐξῆλθ’ ἀληθῶς οὐκ ἀτιμίαι σέθεν ('for you were not defeated, but the trial turned out in a genuine tie of votes, without any dishonour for you'). This comes as Athena tries to assuage the Furies' anger, so they will not be inclined to destroy Athens, Athena's city, as Traina points out [bib-S67TMEFJ-Traina-1970-124]. Traina suggests that Ennius was more interested in the dramatic tensions of the moment of Athena's judgement, than how it tied in to the rest of the play ('Tant' è vero che, non molti versi dopo, tenterà di placare il corrucio delle Erinni con parole blande e promesse. Ennio ha guardato più alla tension drammatica del momento che ai futuri sviluppi dell' azione'). But given the Ennian fragment seems to come from Athena's sentence, it may also suggest that Ennius may have had a different treatment of the remainder of the play. We may speculate that Ennius may have judged that his Roman audience would have little interest in an aetiology of the worship of the Eumenides in Attica, and so a dramatic need for Athena to be diplomatic towards the Furies for plot purposes was absent. Still, Ennius titled his play Eumenides, and so the transformation of the Furies into those gods presumably did feature in the latter portion of the play, just perhaps not in the same way as it does in Aeschylus.

As Traina [bib-S67TMEFJ-Traina-1970-124] points out ('il tono piu ufficiale e nello stesso tempo piu soggettivo', 'a tone both more official and at the same time more subjective'), Ennius' Athena seems more involved in the judgement than Aeschylus' Athena: she explicitly ties the sentence to her first person voice (dico), as well as issuing the instruction to the Furies to leave Orestes alone. Traina is not the only one to suggest that Ennius' Athena seems to take more of a personal role in the pronouncement of the sentence: Jocelyn argues that the words of the Ennian Athena 'imply that the decision is Athena's own and not the automatic result of the deadlock brought about by her equalising vote' [bib-KDEMX3WU-Jocelyn-1967-286]. Jocelyn ties this to what might have constituted the contemporary legal practice, since 'it is possible that in Ennius day the decision in some cases of homicide was purely that of a magistrate exercising jurisdiction' [bib-KDEMX3WU-Jocelyn-1967-286-7]. Jocelyn contrasts this with what we know was the practice in the late Roman Republic that a tie of votes automatically meant acquittal: 'the president of the court had nothing to do except announce the juror's decision' [bib-KDEMX3WU-Jocelyn-1967-286]. Incidentally, the practice that tied votes resulted in acquittal was the same in 5th century Athens [bib-8VABEIJ2-Gagarin-1975-126]. Still, it is not necessarily the case that Athena's first person voice precludes her making a statement in her capacity as president of the court. However, Jocelyn's point about contemporary Roman legal practice is worth considering. It would mean a dramatic change from the plot of Aeschylus' play. Jury trials were introduced later in the Republic's history: as far as we can tell, prior to 149 BC, there were no jury trials. If Ennius had based his play on what was familiar to the Romans from the contemporary practice in their city, then Athena would be the sole iudex. There is nothing in the fragments which are securely attested for the Eumenides to make this impossible. However, if the incertum TRF II F172 does belong to Ennius' Eumenides and does read aequam pilam rather than the transmitted †quam pudam†, then this conclusion would not be very likely.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text:
facessere significat recedere. Ennius Eumenidibus: dico vicisse Orestem: vos ab hoc facessite

Nonius gives two different meanings for facessere, that it is synonymous with facere and with recedere. For the former Nonius offers examples from Ennius' Annales, from Afranius' Inimicis and from the fourth book of Virgil's Aeneid. It is clear that when saying that facessere is synonymous with facere, it is in the sense of 'to fulfil', particularly used in connection with commands. For facessere as synonymous with recedere, Nonius also gives examples, one from Titinius' Gemina, two from Pacuvius' Teucer, one from Cicero's De legibus, one from Afranius' Libertus, one from Plautus' Rudens and finally one from Cicero's Hortensius.

lines.text: dico vicisse Orestem; vos ab hoc facessite

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

lines.text: <10> neque fera hominum pectora
fragescunt, donec vim persensere imperi[i].

doc.translation: ...and men’s savage hearts are not broken until they have experienced fully the force of authority.
doc.differences:
TRF: 6 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

Ribbeck: 25-26 (Accius' Aegisthus 4/5)

Warmington: 8-9 (Accius' Aegisthus 3/5)

Franchella: 405-406 (Accius' Aegisthus 4/5)

D'Antò: 25-26 (Accius' Aegisthus 4/5)

Dangel: 302-303 (Accius' Aegisthus 1/5)


Warmington:

…nor break men’s savage souls until they’ve felt the might of government.

Franchella:

⏑-⏑-⏑ neque fera hominum pectora

fragescunt, donec uim persensere imperi

‘Gli animi degli uomini crudeli non si domano, finché sentono le redini del comando’.

D'Antò:

⏑-⏑-⏑ neque fera hominum pectora

fragescunt, donec uim persensere imperi

‘...né gli animi crudeli degli uomini si piegano, finché non sentano la forza del potere’.

Dangel:

et les coeurs sauvages des hommes sont indomptables, jusqu’à ce qu’ils aient profondément ressenti la violence du pouvoir.
doc.apparatus:
1 neque] neque codd.: nec Warmington, sic(?)

2 persensere imperi] persensere imperi Hermannus, iam Agustìn: persenserim imperii LFH1EGP: -rint imperii H2: persensint imperi Hermannus: imperi persenserint Bothe

N.B.: In TLL X.1.1685.57-8 the line is cited ‘hominum pectora non fragescunt donec vim persensere imperi’.

The paradosis persenserim imperii is unanimous in all manuscripts except for H (= British Library, Harleianus 2719), in which a second hand has corrected persensirim to persenserint, doubtless because a third-person verb was felt to be needed. The corrector’s line of thinking has been followed by modern editors who have continued to conjecture third-person verbs (indeed Ribbeck, apparently not noticing the correction in the Harley manuscript, attributed the reading persenserint to Bentinus as a conjecture). But the paradosis does not scan either in its original or in its corrected form (the line would have a foot too many), and so most editors have adopted Hermann’s conjecture persensere, which is the contracted form of the third person perfect indicative persenserunt (this conjecture had been anticipated, unbeknownst to Hermann, by A. Agustìn in the sixteenth century: on Agustìn, see Accius Clytemnestra F 147 TRF, ‘Fragment apparatus criticus’). Hermann also suggested, as an alternative, persensint, the contracted form of the third person perfect subjunctive persenserint. Ribbeck adopted persensint in his second edition in spite of Hermann’s doubt as to whether the subjunctive mood is really desirable (cf. Hermann apud Ribbeck1, xii).

Indeed, since donec can be followed either by a verb in the subjunctive or in the indicative, one might think that the indicative is better suited to a tyrant’s expression of his perspective that the people will feel the full might of his authority not as a matter of possibility, but as a matter of fact (see Commentary section under donec). Furthermore, persensint would need to be parsed as a unique, syncopated form of the perfect subjunctive persenserint, analogous to serpsit (= serpserit), a form considered archaic by Festus (p. 473.15 Lindsay: cf. K–S II.790). The manuscripts have imperii at line-end but this word cannot stand in that position iambo-trochaic verse as the metrical pattern -⏑⏑⏓ is not allowed. Two solutions have been envisioned: either to conjecture the contracted form of the genitive imperi (sometimes marked by means of a circumflex over the final i, i.e. imperî: so Bentinus, Hermann, Ribbeck2), or to invert the word order to imperi persenserint, in which case either imperii or imperi would be possible (so Bothe, Ribbeck1+3).

It is unclear why Warmington prints nec. He does not report this modification in his critical apparatus. Both forms are technically allowed (cf. OLD s.v. neque).
doc.commentary:
fragescunt: fragescere is not attested beyond this lemma of Nonius and two entries in the glossaries (see TLL VI.1.1226.69-74) but the meaning is ‘to become broken; to be conquered, subdued’ (Lewis–Short); ‘to become subdued or tractable’ (OLD); W–H I.541 gloss it as ‘frangor’. The relation of fragescere to frango is unclear. On the one hand, frangesco seems like a deverbative derivation (‘Abweizung’) into an inchoative form of the verb frango (LHS I.§415D). The morpheme -sc- conveys the sense of the progressive development of a process (Mignot 1969, 213-14), and the phenomenon is attested with especial frequency in Republican tragic poets (Mignot 1969, 191-2 with examples, e.g. celebrēscere Acc., fatīscī Pac. and Acc., mītēscere Pac. and Acc.). On the other hand, the lack of nasal infix in frangesco may suggest that the verb was formed not from the verbal root than frang-, but rather from frag- (indeed fragesco is not mentioned by Mignot).

donec: in the sense of ‘until’, donec can be followed either by a verb in the indicative or in the subjunctive (OLD s.v.). Pinkster I.638: ‘the indicative is used when the state of affairs referred to is presented as a factual time indication; the subjunctive when it is presented as either “intended” or “designed”’.

persensere: the earliest attested use of the verb persentio (TLL X.1.1685.49-50). It appears only three times in Classical literature, once in Lucretius (4.25, possibly interpolated, cf. Deufert’s critical apparatus, though Lucretius does at 3.249 use the verb persentisco, attested from Plautus onwards, e.g. Amph. 527) and twice in Vergil, both times in Book 4 of the Aeneid (Aen. 4.90 Iuno Didonem tali persensit peste teneri 'Juno felt that Dido was suffering from such a disease...'; Aen. 4.448 Aeneas magno persensit [persentit Serv. ad 443] pectore curas 'Aeneas felt cares in his great heart').
doc.reconstruction:
Almost all scholars attribute this line to Aegisthus as the speaker (Bothe I.160, Ribbeck passim, Venini 326-7, Warmington II.329, Mette 1964, 142, Argenio 1966, 27, Aricò 15).

Several Greek models have been proposed: Aesch. Ag. 1619-20 (Aegisthus to the chorus): γνώσῃ γέρων ὢν ὡς διδάσκεσθαι βαρὺ | τῷ τηλικούτῳ, σωφρονεῖν εἰρημένον (‘You’ll learn, old as you are, that education is tough for someone of your age who’s been ordered to behave himself’, tr. Sommerstein); Aesch. Ag. 1632 (Aegisthus to the chorus): κρατηθεὶς δ᾿ ἡμερώτερος φανῇ (‘once under control, you’ll show yourself a bit tamer’); Aesch. Ag. 1639-41 (Aegisthus to the chorus): τὸν δὲ μὴ πειθάνορα | ζεύξω βαρείαις, οὔ τι μὴ σειραφόρον | κριθῶντα πῶλον, ἀλλ᾿ ὁ δυσφιλὴς σκότῳ (‘Anyone who will not obey his master I will yoke with heavy straps’): cf. Ribbeck1, 298; Aesch. Ag. 1632 (Aegisthus to the chorus): κρατηθεὶς δ᾿ ἡμερώτερος φανῇ (‘once under control, you’ll show yourself a bit tamer’): cf. Ribbeck 1875, 468; Hom. Od. 3.304 δέδμητο δὲ λαὸς ὑπ’ αὐτᾠ ‘and the people were subdued under him [sc. Aegisthus]', cf. Argenio 1966, 27 n. 22; Soph. El. 1458-63 (Aegisthus): οἴγειν πύλας ἄνωγα κἀναδεικνύναι | πᾶσιν Μυκηναίοισιν Ἀργείοις θ᾿ ὁρᾶν, | ὡς εἴ τις αὐτῶν ἐλπίσιν κεναῖς πάρος | ἐξῄρετ᾿ ἀνδρὸς τοῦδε, νῦν ὁρῶν νεκρὸν | στόμια δέχηται τἀμά, μηδὲ πρὸς βίαν | ἐμοῦ κολαστοῦ προστυχὼν φύσῃ φρένας ‘I tell you to open the doors and to reveal the sight to all the Mycenaeans and the Argives, so that if anyone was previously buoyed up by vain hopes centered on this man, he may now see him a corpse and accept my bridle, and not need violent chastisement from me to teach him sense’, tr. Lloyd-Jones: cf. Aricò 2002, 15.

Ribbeck1, 298 suggests that this fragment could be related to the anonymous trochaic septenarii preserved by Cicero at De or. 3.166 (= Adesp. F 24 TRF): erras, erras; nam exsultantem te et praefidentem tibi / repriment ualidae legum habenae atque imperi insistent iugo, ‘Wrong, you’re wrong! You’re overconfident and prancing, but the strong reins of law shall curb you, halt you under the yoke of discipline', tr. Wisse–May.

It is noteworthy that Ribbeck imagines this fragment to have occurred towards the end of the play (position 4/5), Dangel imagines it to have occurred towards the beginning (position 1/5). The play might thus either have begun or ended with a scene displaying Aegisthus' lawless tyranny.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Non. 111.1-3 Mercier = 158.1-3 Lindsay:

fragescere, frangi. Accius Aegistho: neque fera hominum pectora / fragescunt, donec uim persensere imperi.

The fragment is transmitted by Nonius to give an example of the use of the verb fragescere, which is said to be synonymous with frango 'to break'. This line is quoted alongside another Accian fragment, from Eurysaces, where the verb fragescere also appears (F 213 TRF = v. 337-338 Ribbeck3 = 331-332 Warmington = 325-326 Dangel).

In the L manuscript, the lemma originally read fligistcere, flangi, and was corrected to fragiscere, frangi by a later hand.

doc.metrical_analysis:
This fragment is considered by virtually all editors to be two trochaic septenarii, the first line being incomplete. Vossius (apud Scriverius 1620, 123), however, prints the text as follows:

…neque fera hominum

pectora fragescunt, donec uim persenserint

imperii…

It is unclear to me what this metre is supposed to be.

lines.text: aspice hoc candens quem inuocant omnes Iouem
solisque exortu capessit candorem, occasu nigret
Vigilandum est semper ; multae insidiae sunt bonis.
(S)in flaccebunt condiciones, repudiato et reddito

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae
fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent

doc.translation: Even if good seeds are sown in bad soil, they nevertheless crop splendidly thanks to their own nature.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck3:

Fragment 20 (20/20), 2341-2: 

2341: Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae

2342: Fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent

N.B.: This fragment is not assigned line numbers among the fragments of Accius’ play because it is technically an incertum printed by Ribbeck in his first edition (1852, 190) as Accius inc. fab. fr. XVIII, v. 672-673. In his second and third editions, Ribbeck attributes the fragment to the Atreus, removing it from the Accian incerta. It had previously been assigned by Bothe (I.49) to Ennius’ Hecuba (F 17) on the basis of a similarity with Eur. Hec. 592-598 (see ‘Fragment reconstruction’).

Warmington: 

Fragment 199-200 (17/17):

199: Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae

200: fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent

Good grain, though to a field of poorer kind

‘Tis given, yet it grows of its own nature

Into a gleaming crop.

Franchella: 

Fragment 22 (22/22), 387-388:

387: probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae

388: fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent

I semi buoni, anche se gettari in terreno poco buono, si riconoscono ugualmente per virtú propria.

D’Antò:

Fragment 18 (18/18), 2341-2:

2341: probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae

2342: fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent

Una buona semenza, anche se affidata ad un terreno alquanto inadatto, tuttavia per sua stessa natura lussureggia.

Dangel:

Fragment 7 (7/21), 43-44:

43: Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae

44: fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent.

Même si de bonnes semences ont été confiées à un assez mauvais terrain, celles-ci néanmoins prospèrent spontanément, du fait de leur nature propre.
doc.apparatus:
1: probae ] probae Muret 1600, 56-7: ac inprobe uel improbe codd.

1 probae: the reading probae was brilliantly conjectured by Marc-Antoine Muret in 1600 (p. 56-7), who at the same time proposed the attribution of this fragment to Accius. He noticed that the manuscript paradosis falsumque [falsoque, a manuscript variant noted in the margin of Lambinus’ 1579 edition, p. 419] illud ac improbe etsi in segetem… was corrupt. Muret observed that while this sententia might well be described as false, but not as improper or wanton (improbe). He therefore suggested that at improbe is a corruption of Atti probae, that is, the name of Accius in the genitive case, followed by the first word of the poetic quotation. That Muret’s attribution of the fragment to Accius is correct is confirmed by the testimony of the Laurentine scholion (see ‘Citation context’).
doc.commentary:
1-2: several scholars (e.g. Bothe I.49, Ribbeck3, 192) compare this fragment with a passage of Euripides’ Hecuba (592-598):

[HECUBA]: οὔκουν δεινόν, εἰ γῆ μὲν κακὴ

τυχοῦσα καιροῦ θεόθεν εὖ στάχυν φέρει,

χρηστὴ δ᾿ ἁμαρτοῦσ᾿ ὧν χρεὼν αὐτὴν τυχεῖν

κακὸν δίδωσι καρπόν, ἄνθρωποι δ᾿ ἀεὶ

ὁ μὲν πονηρὸς οὐδὲν ἄλλο πλὴν κακός,

ὁ δ᾿ ἐσθλὸς ἐσθλός, οὐδὲ συμφορᾶς ὕπο

φύσιν διέφθειρ᾿ ἀλλὰ χρηστός ἐστ᾿ ἀεί;

Is it not passing strange? A poor plot of land that gets its due rain from above bears a good harvest and good land that does not bears a poor one, but where mankind is concerned, the base man continues ever base and the noble is ever noble, never changing his nature under the blows of misfortune but always remaining good. (Transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, II.451).

In the Euripidean passage, the idea expressed by Hecuba is that aristocrats are virtuous by nature (see Battezzato 2018, 152, adducing Hom. Il. 6.146-9, Pind. Nem. 6.8-11, Eur. F 415 TrGF, all containing comparisons between humans and plants). Lana (1959, 362-3), however, points out that the thoughts expressed in both passages are in fact not very similar at all. Baldarelli (2004, 222) compares another Euripidean passage, which explicitly compares the relative merits of legitimate and illegitimate offspring using a vegetation metaphor (Andr. 636-638):

πολλάκις δέ τοι

ξηρὰ βαθεῖαν γῆν ἐνίκησε σπορᾷ,

νόθοι τε πολλοὶ γνησίων ἀμείνονες.

For just as stony ground often surpasses deep soil in its produce, so bastards are often better men than legitimate sons. (Transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, II.333).

1-2 probae…fruges: Baldarelli (2004, 222 n. 4) compares the common Lucretian expression nitidae fruges (1.250, 2.187, 594, 991, 1157).

1 segetem: the word seges is also used by Accius in the Oenomaus (F 319.1-2 TRF): ferte ante auroram, radiorum ardentem indicem, | cum somno in segetem agrestis cornutos cient…, ‘Go, before dawn, blazing sign of the rays, at the time when farmers move bullocks from sleep to the field…’. Here, seges is explained by the OLD (s.v. 2) as meaning ‘arable land’, and it is listed as a use of the word ‘in figurative phrasing’.

2 suapte: the suffix -pte is described by OLD s.v. as ‘an emphatic particle…in OLat. added to pers. pronouns’; ‘dissyllabic by synizesis’ (Dougan–Henry 1905-1934, I.173).
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment is not included as a fragment of Accius’ Atreus in Ribbeck’s first edition (the play as presented in Ribbeck’s first edition thus has only nineteen fragments, and not twenty like in the two subsequent editions).

It was assigned by Bothe (I.49) to Ennius’ Hecuba (F 17) on the basis of a similarity with Eur. Hec. 592-598:

οὔκουν δεινόν, εἰ γῆ μὲν κακὴ

τυχοῦσα καιροῦ θεόθεν εὖ στάχυν φέρει,

χρηστὴ δ᾿ ἁμαρτοῦσ᾿ ὧν χρεὼν αὐτὴν τυχεῖν

κακὸν δίδωσι καρπόν, ἄνθρωποι δ᾿ ἀεὶ

ὁ μὲν πονηρὸς οὐδὲν ἄλλο πλὴν κακός,

ὁ δ᾿ ἐσθλὸς ἐσθλός, οὐδὲ συμφορᾶς ὕπο

φύσιν διέφθειρ᾿ ἀλλὰ χρηστός ἐστ᾿ ἀεί;

Is it not passing strange? A poor plot of land that gets its due rain from above bears a good harvest and good land that does not bears a poor one, but where mankind is concerned, the base man continues ever base and the noble is ever noble, never changing his nature under the blows of misfortune but always remaining good. (Transl. Kovacs 1994-2002, II.451).

Ribbeck (1875, 450-1) connects this fragment with those that seem to pertain to Atreus’ complaints about Thyestes’ affair with his wife Aerope and, more generally, about the dangers that the unfaithfulness of wives in royal families pose to due succession of kingship, that is: F 104.7 TRF (qui non sat habuit coniugem inlexe in stuprum) and F 104.8-10 (quod re in summa summum esse arbitror | periclum matres conquinari regias, | contaminari stirpem, admisceri genus); cf. too F 104.15 TRF (qua in re adiutricem coniugem cepit sibi!). Ribbeck 451 glosses the meaning as follows: ‘so mochte Atreus erklären, ein gemeines Saatfeld könne durch Pflege edele Früchte hervorbringen, ebenso leicht aber pflege eine edele Mutter von einem niedriggesinnten Manne des Stammes unwürdige Nachkommen zu gebären’. Lana 1959, 298 however suggests that this fragment belonged to the play’s prologue, along with T 25 TRF, F 112, and F inc. 437 TRF, on the grounds that it seems to deal, albeit in a metaphorical way, with matters of genealogy, like those other fragments do. He believes that the ‘worse crop’ (seges deterior) refers to the offspring of Atlas the Giant, mentioned in T 25 TRF as the father of Oenomaus (Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse), as well as of Maia, Electra, and Pluto. From this ‘bad crop’, Jupiter begets a ‘good crop’ consisting, respectively, of Mercury, Dardanus, and Tantalus. Pluto as the mother of Tantalus: Paus. 2.22.3, Σ Od. 11.582, Σ Or. 4, Hyg. Fab. 82, 155, Nonnos 7.119. Thus, the descendants of Maia and Electra, the Arcadians and Trojans respectively, remained good, whereas the descendants of Pluto, the Tantalids, lapsed back into badness. Lana supports his argument by adducing the argument attributed by Aristotle to Anaxagoras (cf. Arist. Gen. an. 763b30-764a1), and expounded by Apollo in Aeschylus’ Eumenides (658-66), that the mother’s role in procreation is purely receptive and passive (cf. Accius’ seges), and that the father is supplying the seed (cf. Accius’ fruges). Franchella (1966, 223) disagrees, arguing that the moralising tone of the fragment makes it rather more suited to the end of the play (an idea endorsed by D’Antò 1980, 289). Franchella argues (p. 237) that, while Atreus is being led away to be executed, the chorus makes this point to illustrate that, in the case of the sons of Pelopids, the opposite holds true: that bad seeds such as the Pelopids will become degenerate. Di Benedetto Zimbone (1973, 269) argues that this fragment belongs to the scene in which Atreus says his famous line, oderint, dum metuant (F 103.4). She interprets the fragment as a statement by Atreus on the importance of ‘political virtue’ (‘virtù politica’, probae fruges) for governing a state: Atreus would thus be justifying his tyranny by marking himself out as a virtuous politician, in contrast with his debased subjects (etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae).

﻿ Dangel (1995, 278) suggests that, in this fragment, Thyestes continues his provocative admission of guilt to the offences he committed against his brother in the past, and especially to the fact that he committed adultery with Atreus’ wife Aerope. Endorsing Lana’s suggestion that in the background of the fragment lie ideas about the respectively active and passive roles of the father and mother in human procreation, Dangel imagines Thyestes making the point that his children by Aerope will be good because he fathered them, despite their mother being a ‘bad field’. Thus, on Dangel’s interpretation, Thyestes’ shameless profession of guilt and provocation of his brother make him seem like a thoroughly despicable and unappealing character. In fact, Dangel goes so far as to suggest (p. 279) that it is at the precise moment that Thyestes utters these words that the idea of taking his revenge by killing his nephews forms in Atreus’ mind. Baldarelli (2004, 222) argues that this fragment belongs to the scene involving Atreus and his attendant. She argues that these lines are spoken by the attendant after Atreus has expressed concerns about the legitimacy of the children he initially believed to be his owing to his wife Aerope’s sexual relations with Thyestes. If they are in fact the children of Thyestes, then they pose a threat to the proper line of succession. Baldarelli believes that the attendant responds by pointing out that, just as good seeds always flourish even if they are planted in bad soil, so the excellence of Atreus’ sons will become apparent even if their mother is such a bad woman as Aerope. But her suggestion seems unconvincing. Presumably, what Atreus fears is the possibility that children fathered by Thyestes will access the throne. In that case, arguments that emphasise the intrinsic excellence of his own sons would be irrelevant. Furthermore, it is difficult to see how the passage from Euripides’ Andromache (636-638, quoted above, see ‘Fragment commentary’) is relevant, as the point made there is that illegitimate sons are often better than legitimate ones: it is very difficult to imagine an attendant saying something along these lines to the tyrannical Atreus.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Cicero Tusculanae Disputationes 2.12-13:

– Quod est enim maius argumentum nihil eam [philosophiam] prodesse quam quosdam perfectos philosophos turpiter uiuere? 

– Nullum uero id quidem argumentum est. Nam ut agri non omnes frugiferi sunt qui coluntur, falsumque illud Acci: ‘Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae | fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent’ (= Accius Atreus F 105 TRF) sic animi non omnes culti fructum ferunt. Atque, ut in eodem simili uerser, ut ager quamuis fertilis sine cultura fructuosus esse non potest, sic sine doctrina animus; ita utraque res sine altera debilis. Cultura autem animi philosophia est; haec extrahit uitia radicitus et praeparat animos ad satus accipiendos, eaque mandat is et, ut ita dicam, serit quae adulta fructus uberrimos ferant.

A [= an anonymous interlocutor]: What stronger proof is there that it [philosophy] is of no benefit than that some fully trained philosophers lead disgraceful lives?

M [= Marcus Tullius Cicero, the character in the dialogue]: But that is no proof. Just as not all cultivated fields are fertile, and Accius was wrong to say: ‘Even if good seeds are sown in a poor soil, still by their own nature they crop splendidly’ (= Accius Atreus F 105 TRF) – so not all cultivated minds bear fruit. And, to keep up the same simile, just as a field however fertile cannot be fruitful without cultivation, neither can the soul without instruction. Each is powerless without the other. Now the cultivation of the soul is philosophy. It tears out vices by the roots and prepares the soul to receive the sowings, and commits to them and so to speak sows those seeds which will bear the most abundant crops on coming to maturity.

[Text: Giusta 1984; transl. Douglas 1985-1990, II.23.]

This fragment, like some other fragments of or possibly related to Accius’ Atreus (Adesp. F 63 TRF, which has been connected to Accius’ Atreus; F 104.5-6, the two last lines of a fragment which is preserved more fully at Cic. De orat. 3.219; and F 104.1-2, also preserved at Cic. De orat. 3.217) is preserved in a philosophical treatise in five books by Cicero entitled Tusculan Disputations, which was probably written during the summer of 45 BCE (on the dating, see Marinone–Malaspina 20042, 215). In this treatise, which is addressed to Brutus the assassin of Caesar (RE ‘Iunius 53’), Cicero aims to present to a Roman audience Greek philosophy of the Hellenistic period on a range of topics. This fragment falls towards the end of the introductory section to Book 2, which as a whole is largely devoted to the topic of pain. In the introductory section to Book 2, ‘Marcus’ and his anonymous interlocutor debate the usefulness of studying philosophy. When the interlocutor remarks that the shameful life lead by some philosophers proves that studying philosophy is not useful, ‘Marcus’ rejects this line of reasoning, and, in order to show that it is false, has recourse to an analogy in which he compares the cultivation of the animus (‘soul’, ‘mind’) with philosophy with the cultivation of soil. He cites this fragment from Accius’ Atreus in order to reject the point made in it, that good seeds will bear fruit irrespective of the quality of the soil in which they are sown. Instead, ‘Marcus’ argues, just as not all fields that receive cultivation bear fruit, so not all animi that receive philosophical training achieve goodness. The role of philosophy is first to rid the animus of vice so as to make it as receptive as possible to the ‘seeds’ (satus) of philosophical training that might lead to goodness. Cicero here uses the word cultura, which properly denotes the cultivation of land (Cato, Agr. 61.2, Varro, Rust. 1.1.6), in an extended sense of ‘training or improvement (of the faculties)’ (OLD s.v. 2): this passage is apparently the first recorded use of cultura in this extended sense (TLL IV.1323.20-22).

context.author: Scholia Laurentiana
context.text:
Schol. Laur. Verg. Georg. 1.2, III.2.198.3-5 Hagen: ‘Accius in Atreo: si in segetem deteriorem datae sunt fruges’.

A garbled version of the fragment is found in a scholion on the first line of Vergil’s Georgics which is printed by H. Hagen as part of the anonymous Breuis expositio on Vergil’s Georgics (III.2.191-320), which draws heavily on material attributed to the Vergilian commentator conventionally known as Philargyrius. The Breuis expositio is often preserved alongside two texts entitled Explanatio in Bucolica Vergilii which are thought to derive in some way from the work of Philargyrius. These texts are transmitted in two different redactions, usually referred to as a or b (Funaioli 1930) or I and II (Ziolkowski–Putnam 2008, 674). The scholia of the a or I redaction are sometimes referred to as the ‘Laurentine scholia’ in order to differentiate them from the scholia of the second redaction (sometimes referred to as ‘Bern scholia’ after MSS Bern 172, 167, and 165) because one important manuscript in which they is preserved is housed in the Biblioteca Medicea Laurenziana in Florence, under the shelfmark Laur. Plut. 45.14 (f. 23r, cf. Thilo 1859-1860, Mommsen 1861, 442). It belongs to the first redaction of the Explanatio in Bucolica Vergilii, the other witnesses to which are Hagen’s manuscripts N (= Paris BNF, MS Lat. 7960, f. 19r); P (= Paris BNF, MS Lat. 11308, f. 43r-43v); and G (= Leiden Universiteitsbibliotheek, MS BPL 135, which however omits our fragment, lines III.2.198.18-199.9 Thilo–Hagen being absent from this manuscript, f. 96r): cf. Funaioli 1930, 8-11 for a description of these four manuscripts.
