doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Atreus

lines.text: video sepulcra dua duorum corporum

doc.translation: I see two tombs of (or for?) two corpses.
doc.differences:
Ribbeck1-3:

Accius inc. fab. 655, F V Ribbeck1-3:

Video sepulcra duo duorum corporum.

Warmington:

Accius inc. fab. 33 Warmington:

Video sepulchra duo duorum corporum

I see, of corpses two, two sepulchres.

Franchella:

Accius inc. fab. 656 Franchella:

uideo sepulchra duo duorum corporum

Vedo due sepolcri di due cadaveri.

D’Antò:

Accius inc. fab. 655 D’Antò:

uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum

Vedo due sepolcri per due cadaveri.

Dangel:

Accius inc. fab. 700 Dangel:

Video sepulcra dua duorum corporum.

Je vois deux tombeaux pour deux corps.
doc.apparatus:
sepulcra: sepulcrum AJ: sepulchra FUBSKRE

dua LP2: (al.) ε: a AΣΞ: ad ΔΘΦ: om. P1JV: duo f, cf. Ribbeck1-2 (Quint. Inst. Orat. 1.5.15)

Ribbeck prints duo on the authority of Westerman’s manuscript f (= Heerdegen’s Vitebergensis), a manuscript which I have not looked at, adducing Quintilian’s statement at Inst. Orat. 1.5.15 that dua is a barbarism. The critical apparatus above is based mainly on that of Westman in his edition of Cicero’s Orator (20022, 50).
doc.commentary: duorum corporum: a genitive of possession (Woodcock §72) depending on sepulcra, ‘tombs’. Translated either as ‘for two corpses’ (D’Antò, Dangel) or ‘of two corpses’ (Warmington, Franchella).
doc.reconstruction: In his first edition (1852), Ribbeck proposes that this fragment might belong to Accius’ Aegisthus (Ribbeck1, 334: either spoken by Atreus gleefully showing Thyestes two tombs prepared for the latter’s sons, or spoken by Thyestes, reacting in horror). But in his 1875 monograph (Die römische Tragödie), he suggests that it might also have belonged to the Aegisthus: he imagines the speaker as Cassandra experiencing a prophetic vision of two tombs, one for Agamemnon and the other for herself. Warmington (II.575) that this fragment belonged either to the Aegisthus or to Clytemnestra. But this attribution hinges on his assumption that the two fragments of Accius preserved at Cic. Orat. 156 (i.e. both this fragment, inc. fab. F 406 TRF, and inc. fab. F 407 TRF, mulier una duum uirum) belonged to the same Accian play. Indeed, Warmington prints the two lines together as a single fragment (Accius inc. fab. 33-4). Nothing in Cicero’s wording need however imply that these two lines of Accius belonged to the same play. Apparently believing that these two lines did belong to the same play, Warmington supports his suggestion that they might be attributed either to the Aegisthus or to the Clytemnestra by citing Ribbeck. Ribbeck had suggested in his 1875 monograph (p. 462, 467) that the Accian line under discussion (inc. fab. F 406 TRF, uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum) belonged to the Aegisthus, and the other to the Clytemnestra (inc. fab. F 407 TRF, mulier una duum uirum). So Ribbeck’s suggestion in fact was that the former line might have belonged to the Aegisthus and the latter to the Clytemnestra (in a scene in which Electra chastises Clytemnestra for her infidelity). Moreover, Ribbeck clearly distinguished between the two fragments. So Warmington misrepresents Ribbeck’s view. Dangel 378 considers this fragment, as well as Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (mulier una duum uirum), to belong to one of the plays of Accius’ so-called ‘Trojan Cycle’ or ‘Atrid (Pelopid?) Cycle’.
doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius.

context.author: Cicero
context.text: This fragment is quoted, alongside Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (video sepulcra dua duorum corporum), at Cic. Orat. 156, in a section of the treatise in which the phenomena of hiatus, elision, and related sonic effects are discussed (Orat. 149-64: cf. Kaster 2020, 213-23). At Orat. 155-6, Cicero presents the view of some contemporary critics who believe that forms such as the archaic genitive plural deum, the contracted form of deorum which was more in use in Cicero’s day, should not be used and that the uncontracted form should be preferred. Cicero cites several examples from Republican tragedy in which the contracted form is used, among which notably our two fragments of Accius (see above) and Fragmenta ex certis incertorum tragoediis F 1.1-3 TRF = Pacuvius Chryses F 76 Schierl. The fragments of Accius are quoted to show that the poet uses the genitive plural forms duorum and duum indifferently.

lines.text: Sed quid tonitru turbida toruo
Concussa repente aequora caeli
Sensimus sonere?
[THYESTES]: Numquam istam imminuam curam infitiando tibi.
Iterum Thyestes Atreum adtractatum aduenit ;
iterum iam adgreditur me et quietum exsuscitat.
Maior mihi moles, maius miscendum est malum
qui illius acerbum cor contundam et comprimam.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Look upon this that shines aloft, who all men call Jupiter.
doc.differences:
TrRF: 134 (Ennius’ Thyestes 3/10)

Jocelyn: 301 (Ennius’ Thyestes 12/12)

Warmington: 351 (Ennius’ Thyestes 3/14)

Vahlen2: 345 (Ennius’ Thyestes 5/14)

Ribbeck2/3: 302 (Ennius’ Thyestes 7/11)

aspice hoc sublime candens quem uocant omnes Iouem

Ribbeck & Warmington: aspice hoc sublimen candens, quem inuocant omnes Iouem

Warmington places this fragment (frg. 351 Warm.) at the beginning of the play during the sun’s eclipse, when Thyestes gets his own children served for dinner. He based his interpretation on Hyginus’ Fabula 88: ob id scelus etiam sol currum avertit (‘due to this crime, even the sun turned his chariot’). Vahlen2 puts this fragment (Thy. frg. 5) in the beginning of the tragedy as well. Ribbeck3 puts this fragment (p. 66, 302 Ribb.3) in the middle of the tragedy. Jocelyn excludes the possibility that the fragment (frg. 153 Joc.) belongs to the cena Thyestea, and he places the fragment in the beginning of the tragedy.
doc.apparatus:
sublime] sublime F B R (Apul.) : sub lumine P L N U (Apul.) : sublimen cod. Benedictinus (Apul.): sublime ω Cic. et Prob. et Fest.
cadens V (Cic. nat. deor. 3.40) : candens rel. Cic. : candens ω Prob. et Fest. : cadens P L F N U (Apul.): candens B R (Apul.)
uocant] uocant ω Prob. et Fest. : inuocant ω Cic. et Apul.

There is majority consensus across the manuscripts of each of the quoting texts for sublime, although Ritschl argued for the reading sublimen. See further the commentary. sub lumine in some manuscripts of Apuleius has likely arisen in combination with the corruption of cadens from candens.
We see a neat split between the manuscripts of Cicero and Apuleius for the reading inuocant, on the one hand, and Probus and Festus for the reading uocant, on the other. Here the issue is not with the manuscript tradition of any of the texts but the fidelity of the original quotations to Ennius' words. In this instance the text quoted by these authors seem to have been motivated by what they thought Ennius was trying to express. See further the reconstruction.
doc.commentary:
sublime] Ritschl conjectured sublimen, a hypothesised archaic form of the adverb sublime. There are three texts that cause us to pause and consider this suggestion. In Festus’ lexicon, we have an issue with Festus’ lemma, since although Festus quotes the line exactly as Cicero and Probus do, he introduces his quotation of the Ennian line with sublimem. In Paulus’ excerpta of Festus’ lexicon, in a good manuscript, L, the reading sublimen is found instead of sublimem as found in most manuscripts, apart from T and I which have sublime . Finally, some manuscripts of Apuleius, an author who quotes the Ennian line without any introductory note or comment in de Mundo, contain the reading aspice hoc sublimen candens....

It is strange that Festus uses sublimem in his lemma that introduces this fragment, an accusative masculine adjectival form, while in the Ennian line, we read sublime, an adverbial form. We would expect sublime, the neuter singular adjectival form, in Festus’ lemma, in order for it to agree with elatum in his definition.

In the codex Leidensis of Paulus the reading sublimen is found instead of sublimem in Paulus’ definition: sublimen est in altitudinem elatum; id autem dicitur a limine superiore, quia supra nos est.. However, this could quite probably be a scribal slip: the scribe may have been inspired by Paulus’ etymological explanation of sublime from a limine.

Separately, a few generally inferior manuscripts of Apuleius record the form sublimen for the Ennian fragment itself.

Considering the evidence of Festus, Paulus and Apuleius together, the possibility cannot be excluded that sublimen was the form originally used by Ennius. This would still be an adjectival form, but an archaic one. If this hypothesis is adopted, then the resulting predominant readings across the different manuscript traditions have occurred because this form has subsequently either caused confusion for some scribes, or been changed to the contemporary adverbial form sublime. If it was used adverbially, it would not alter the interpretation of the line. However, and probably likelier, it could also be that all of these things (sublimem in Festus’ lemma, sublimen in the L manuscript of Paulus, and sublimen in some Apuleian manuscripts are due to independent scribal errors, rather than some artefacts of sublimen being the form Ennius used. The fact that the other quotation Festus cites is from Virgil’s Georgics 242, where the form is certainly sublimis probably supports the argument that that sublime, not sublimen was Festus’ lemma. See further the notes on Festus’ text and Apuleius’ text in the Context Author section.

Iovem] Thyestes and Atreus are ultimately descended from Zeus/Jupiter by Tantalus and Pelops, so this might be part of a genealogical commentary, rather than merely an astronomical observation.
doc.reconstruction:
Cicero in De natura deorum and Probus in his commentary on Virgil’s Eclogues attribute this fragment to Ennius, and Festus attributes it specifically to his Thyestes.

Interestingly enough, the two quoting authors who have an interest in religion, Cicero and Apuleius, read invocant (rather than vocant), ascribing a religious dimension to the line: people call upon Jupiter, the god of lightning. Meanwhile, the grammatici Probus and Festus, who have a partiality to etymologies and naming, transmit the reading vocant, not invocant, which turns the line into an explanation of hoc sublime candens. Since the readings scan the same, due to invocant eliding with quem, both are possible. If Paulus, in his statement sublimen est in altitudinem elatum; id autem dicitur a limine superiore, quia supra nos estaccurately reflects the etymological interest Festus took in the first half of the line, in hoc sublime candens, then Festus saw in Ennius’ description an explanation of Jupiter’s position as beyond some kind of threshold, between mortals (nos) and gods. Perhaps this tells us more about the quoting authors than what Ennius himself wrote. Or it could allow us to draw conclusions about the Ennian line: that the original context of Ennius’ line suggested that there was a limen, a threshold between mortal and divine activites. To go one step further, we could speculate that the line might critically reflect on Atreus’ attitude towards divinity - in the Senecan reception of the myth, Atreus is presented as a character who wants to supersede the gods. Alternately, there could be an ironic, or even sarcastic, dimension to the line: that the speaker is pointing out that everyone is calling (vocant) or invoking (invocant) Jupiter, and yet all he does is shine uselessly and without a care, but failing to intercede in the mortal world - a quite Epicurean vision of divine inactivity. An argument for Ennian interest in the philosophy of religion could be supported by Ennian fragments such as TrRF frg. 82 and famously the fragments of Pacuvius’ Chryses (Schierl frgg. 76-81, and esp. 77)

The line is in the metre of trochaic septenarii. As a consequence, a preferred reading may come down to whether one imagines Ennius to himself be more interested in reflecting religious practice or a contemporary competitive discourse between religious thinking and a rationalising philosophy.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
Quoted four times by Cicero in De natura deorum.:

The first time is in the context of the connection often drawn in religion between the sky and the gods:

quid enim potest esse tam apertum tamque perspicuum, cum caelum suspeximus caelestiaque contemplati sumus, quam esse aliquod numen praestantissimae mentis quo haec regantur? quod ni ita esset, qui potuisset adsensu omnium dicere Ennius aspice hoc sublime candens, quem invocant omnes Iovem—illum vero et Iovem et dominatorem rerum et omnia motu regentem et, ut idem Ennius, patrem divumque hominumque [=TrRF F159] et praesentem ac praepotentem deum? quod qui dubitet, haud sane intellego cur non idem sol sit an nullus sit dubitare possit; qui enim est hoc illo evidentius? (Cic. Nat. D. 2.4)

The second time Cicero quotes the Ennian line he speaks more specifically about Jupiter as the sky god:

sed ipse Iuppiter, id est iuuans pater, quem conversis casibus appellamus a iuuando Iovem, a poetis pater diuomque hominumque [=TrRF F159] dicitur, a maioribus autem nostris optumus maxumus, et quidem ante optimus id est beneficentissimus quam maximus, quia maius est certeque gratius prodesse omnibus quam opes magnas habere—hunc igitur Ennius, ut supra dixi, nuncupat ita dicens aspice hoc sublime candens, quem invocant omnes Iovem planius quam alio loco idem cui quod in me est exsecrabor hoc quod lucet quicquid est hunc etiam augures nostri cum dicunt “Ioue fulgente tonante”: dicunt enim "caelo fulgente et tonante". Euripides autem ut multa praeclare sic hoc breuiter: "uides sublime fusum immoderatum aethera / qui terram tenero circumiectu amplectitur: / hunc summum habeto diuum, hunc perhibeto Iouem". (Cic. Nat. D. 2.64-65)

The third occasion is a recapitulation of the connection between the sky and divinity:

primum fuit, cum caelum suspexissemus statim nos intellegere esse aliquod numen quo haec regantur. ex hoc illud etiam aspice hoc sublime candens, quem inuocant omnes Iouem: quasi uero quisquam nostrum istum potius quam Capitolinum Iovem appellet, aut hoc perspicuum sit constetque inter omnes, eos esse deos quos tibi Velleius multique praeterea ne animantis quidem esse concedant. (Cic. Nat. D. 3.10)

The fourth Ciceronian context for the Ennian quotation is within a discussion of the different pantheons cultures develop and religious syncretism:

nec uero uolgi atque imperitorum inscitiam despicere possum, cum ea considero quae dicuntur a Stoicis. sunt enim illa imperitorum: piscem Syri uenerantur, omne fere genus bestiarum Aegyptii consecrauerunt; iam uero in Graecia multos habent ex hominibus deos, Alabandum Alabandis, Tenedi Tenen, Leucotheam quae fuit Ino et eius Palaemonem filium cuncta Graecia—Herculem Aesculapium Tyndaridas Romulum nostrum aliosque compluris, quos quasi nouos et adscripticios ciues in caelum receptos putant. Haec igitur indocti; quid uos philosophi, qui meliora? omitto illa, sunt enim praeclara: sit sane deus ipse mundus. hoc credo illud esse sublime candens, quem inuocant omnes Iouem. Quare igitur pluris adiungimus deos? quanta autem est eorum multitudo: mihi quidem sane multi uidentur; singulas enim stellas numeras deos eosque aut beluarum nomine appellas, ut Capram ut Nepam ut Taurum ut Leonem, aut rerum inanimarum, ut Argo ut Aram ut Coronam. (Cic. Nat. D. 3.39-40)

context.author: Festus
context.text:
Festus quotes the line from Thyestes within a definition of sublimis as meaning ‘raised on high’:

sublimem est in altitudinem elatum, ut Ennius in Thyeste aspice hoc sublime candens quem uocant omnes Iouem Vergilius in georgicis lib. 1: hic uertex nobis semper sublimis (Fest. 306M)

context.author: Apuleius
context.text:
Apuleius quotes the line within a discussion of the tendency for people to address prayers to the sky:

Namque habitus orantium sic est ut manibus extensis caelum precemur. Romanus etiam poeta sic sensit: aspice hoc sublime candens, quem inuocant omnes Iouem. unde illa, quae uidentur suntque omnibus praestantiora, easdem sublimitates regionum tenent, astra caelestia et mundi lumina; ac merito illis ordine licet perpetuo frui nec diuersis spatiis et temporibus obseruantissimam legem suorum aliquando itinerum mentiuntur. (Apul. Mund. 33)

context.author: Probus
context.text:
Probus quotes Ennius while explaining the theory of the four elements:

principem habuerunt Empedoclem Agrigentinum qui de his (quattor elementis) ita scripsit, τέσσαρα δὴ πάντων ῥιζώματα πρῶτον ἔασιν / Ζεὺς ἀργὴς, ut accipiamus Ζεὺς ἀργὴς ignem qui sit ζέων et candens, quod ignis est proprium, de quo Euripides: ὁρᾷς τὸν ὑψοῦ τόνδ' ἄπειρον αἰθέρα καὶ γῆν πέριξ ἔχονθ' ὑργαῖς ἐν ἀγκάλαις; τοῦτον νόμιζε Ζῆνα, τόνδ' ἡγοῦ θεόν. et Ennius: aspice hoc sublime candens quem vocant omnes Iouem (Prob. in Verg. Ecl. 6.31)

This line is quoted four times by Cicero in De Natura Deorum (2.4, 2.64-65, 3.10 and 3.40) once by Probus in his commentary on Vergil’s Eclogues (6.31), once by Apuleius in De Mundo (33) and once by Festus (p. 400, 17-20 Lindsay). Cicero quotes this line in relation to the existence of the gods. Probus uses this line in his commentary to Vergil’s Eclogue 6 to demonstrate that Jupiter is candens. Apuleius cites this line when he elaborates on the custom to stretch out hands while praying to the heavens. Ultimately, Festus uses this line to prove the meaning of sublimem, which is related to altitude, as he tells us.

lines.text: aspice hoc sublime candens quem uocant omnes Iouem
Quam mihi maxime hodie hic contigerit malum.
......... crispisulcans igneum
fulmen

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: Whenever they march, they crash down all the fields
doc.differences:
TRF:30 (Naevius' Lycurgus 14/24)

1. Ribbeck3: 19 (Naevius' Lycurgus 3/24)

[...] quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

Ribbeck3: Liberi ⟨sunt⟩: quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

2. Ribbeck2: 22 (Naevius' Lycurgus 3/24)

[...] quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

Ribbeck2 : Liberi ⟨sunt⟩: quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

3. Klotz: 21 (Naevius' Lycurgus 3/24)

[...] quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

Klotz: Liberi / hi quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

4. Marmorale: p.192 (Naevius' Lycurgus 3/24)

[...] quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

Marmorale: I quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt

5. Warmington: 26 (Naevius' Lycurgus 3/25)

Warmington denotes the metrical difficulty and spots the lacuna but retains the text without any emendation. Schauer's and Warmington's texts coincide.

In general, all editors ( Ribbeck, 1871 & 1897 ), ( Marmorale,1950 p.192), (Warmington, 1936), (Klotz, 1953) place this fragment in the third position of Naevius' Lycurgus fragments. They agree that this fragment belongs to messenger's narration, which is placed at the beginning of the play as it introduces its main focal point; the rivalry between Lycurgus and the followers of Liber that threaten to destabilize social normality.

Spaltenstein(1190) suggests that if the editorial variations propose trochaic heptameter instead of iambic hexameter for this fragment, its position in the core part of messenger's narration is questioned. A possible answer to that can be its placement in the introduction of the messenger's speech, in which a variety of metrical choices can be justified. RibbeckRT also notes this specific metrical difficulty, saying this fragment should be placed at the beginning of the messenger's narration.
doc.apparatus:
Ed. Li.

no notable remarks for Lindsay edition

Edd. rel.:

[...] quaque] lib. II quaque codd.[Ri,Wa]

[...] quaque] lib. II codd.[Marm,Kl,Tra] (L H G)[Mu]

[...] quaque] lyc. libro II (F H L P G E P7579)[On]

[...] quaque] quaque (L H G)[Mu] : quaquae H1 [Mu] (F H1 E)[On]

[...] quaque] [...] quaque Wa. : lib. ii quaeque Ald. : Liberi quaqua Merc., Scriv. , Klu. : Liberi, quaquad Bo. : ii quaqua Qui. : Liberi sunt, quaque Ri.2, Ri.3, Tra. : Liberi / i quaque Ri.3 : ⟨famulae⟩ Liberi, / quacumque Ri.1 : liberi, / quaquomque Mu. : i quaque On., Li. : i quaque Marm. : hi quaque Kl. : Liberi, quacunque i. Vossius (1620) : Liberi, quaqua i. Duentzer (1837)

incedunt] incaedunt F1 [On]

opterunt] obterunt Vossius (1620)

omnis arvas opterunt] omnes arvas obterunt Ald., Bo., Qui., Klu., Merc.
doc.commentary:
As far as the meter is concerned, Schauer, Warmington, Lindsay, Klotz, Marmorale, and Traglia maintain the iambic hexameter. Ribbeck2-3 with his emendation Liberi ⟨ sunt ⟩ : quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt introduces trochaic heptameter. Similarly, Vossius (1620), Duentzer (1837) and Mercier (1583) with their emendations also introduce a trochaic meter.

arvas] Originally an adjective arvus-a-um. Etymologically connected with the verb arare, which signifies land cultivated by the human factor ( Spaltenstein 1199 & 1200). It can be said that the civilised world that is symbolically represented here by arvas is violently destroyed by the primitive and ecstatic power of the Bacchae (Klussmann 1843) & ( RibbeckRT). Another possible opinion expressed by Schauer is that arvas is related to vineyards cultivated by the followers of Liber and destroyed by Lycurgus' command.

[...]]Based on the assumption that an iambic hexameter is applied here, a lacuna is spotted. Ribbeck (1875) proposes Liberi ⟨ sunt⟩ as a probable text that properly fills the metric gap. Liberi can have a double interpretation. On the one hand, it can signify the Bacchae, the followers of God Liber. On the other hand, it can also be written with a lower-case letter, as an adjective introducing the idea of freedom. Notably, its Greek correspondent word is the proper noun Βάκχοι. Mueller also perceives liberi as an adjective (Mueller, 1885). Strzelecki (1947) prefers the capital letter, writing Libero. The above two interpretations can be interconnected in the sense that the Bacchae represent the deliberating savagery that makes the civilized norms deconstruct. The ambivalence between Liberi and liberi can also be interpreted as a wordplay (Spaltenstein, 1194). Moreover, Spaltenstein rejects the opinion that it was anticipated by the audience to decode the wordplay, as this mostly seems to be a modern literary interpretation (Spaltenstein, 1195).

quaque] Used as an indefinite relative pronoun. Most recent editors do not accept quaqua or quacumque of ancient editions and retain quaque, even if there are no parallel literary uses of quaque in this sense attested ( Spaltenstein 1197).

opterunt] From the verb obtero, with the meaning to destroy, to condemn, to break into pieces. Reinforces the context of destructive, damaging action ( Spaltenstein 1199).
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment probably belongs to the messenger's narration. This narration is placed at the beginning of the play as it constitutes the initial trigger point of the tragic plot, introducing the rivalry between the conflicting poles of civilized secular order and divine uncivilized ecstatic savagery. The speaker is the messenger, the addressee is possibly Lycurgus or the citizens of Edones and the content refers to the invasion of the Bacchae and Satyrs in their land and their series of destructive actions, particularly, the pillage of the cultivated land ( Ribbeck, 1871), (Marmorale,1950 p.192), (Warmington, 1936), (Spaltenstein 1190). Marmorale (1950) has noted that it might have been impossible for the messenger to understand the identity of the Bacchae from the very first time he encountered them. Nevertheless, a counter-argument is that the messenger is probably familiar with the identity of the Bacchae, as he might have heard them calling the name of their God Liber. A parallel text can be found in EUR., IT 249. Furthermore, another counter-argument to Marmorale can be that the verse is attributed to a follower of Liber who notices the arrival of Lycurgus. But, in this case, the placement of the fragment in the tragedy corpus has to be altered (Spaltenstein, 1196).

Another less possible interpretation by Mercier (1583), Vossius (1620), Duntzer (1847) and Bothe (1837) is that the people who are committing the destruction are Lycurgus' people, the army and the citizens of Edones. In particular, they are destroying the vineyards that have been created by the Bacchae and that represent their inclination to sensual, ephemeral pleasure. In this case, the messenger is addressing his narration to the Bacchae and Satyrs, Liber's followers. As Klussmann supports, it is doubtful how possible it was for the Bacchae to have already cultivated vineyards by the time of their arrival in the city (Klussmann 1843), but helpful divine intervention from Liber is always a possible answer (Spaltenstein, 1192). In any case, the scenario in which the Bacchae are devoting themselves to the cultivation of the land is contradictory to their savagery and it undermines the substantial conflict between civilization and destructive ecstatic pleasure that is tragedy's epicentre.

Klotz, A., Seel, O., Voit, L. (1953). Tragicorum Fragmenta. Monachii.

Marmorale, E. (1950). Naevius’ Poeta. Florence.

Ribbeck, O. (1872). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (2nd ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (3rd ed.). Leipzig.

Schauer, M. (2012). Tragicorum Romanorum Fragmenta. Göttingen.

Spaltenstein, F. (2014). Commentaire des fragments dramatiques de Naevius. Brussels.

Warmington, E. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.location: De compendiosa doctrina,vol. 1, Lindsay, Ed. Teubner
context.text:
Quoted once by Nonius in De compendiosa doctrina:

Nonius' intention is to show an example in which the neutral noun arvum-i is used in feminine grammatical gender. In particular, in the feminine plural accusative arvas instead of the neutral accusative arva:

Arva neutri sunt generis. Vergilius (Aen.X,78): arva aliena iugo premere. Feminino Naevius Lycurgo (19): i quaque incedunt,omnis arvas opterunt. Pacuvius (396): postquam calamitas plures annos arvas calvitur. (Non. De compendiosa doctrina, p.192,29–32 Merc.=283Li.(Li.) )

lines.text: [...] quaque incedunt, omnis arvas opterunt
Video sepulcra duo duorum corporum.
[ATREUS]: <10> natis sepulchro ipse est parens
En impero Argis, sceptra mihi liquit Pelops,
Qua ponto ab Helles atque ab Ionio mari
Vrgetur Isthmus.
......nulla sancta societas
Nec fides regni est.
Deum regnator nocte caeca caelum e conspectu abstulit.
o Fides alma apta pinnis et ius iurandum Iovis.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: The father has ruined most of the Achaeans on the rocks of Caphereus.
doc.differences:
Schierl: 90 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 4/32)

D’Anna: 150 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 5/31)

Ribbeck: 16 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 16/32)

Warmington: 124 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 4/34)

pater Achaeos in Capherei saxis pleros perdidit

D’Anna: pater Achaeos in Capherei saxis pleros perdidit.

Ribbeck: Pater Achiuos in Caperei saxis pleros perdidit.

Warmington: Pater Achivos in Capharei saxis pleros perdidit.

The fragment is placed in different parts of the tragedy by different editors. Schierl and D’Anna both place this fragment somewhere in the beginning of the tragedy. Ribbeck and Warmington however, place this fragment more in the middle of the tragedy. The fragment most likely contains complaints of Electra about the marriage of Oeax. If the fragment is placed in the beginning of the tragedy, then the wedding would also have to take place somewhere in the beginning. If however the fragment is placed in the middle, then that would probably also count for the wedding.
doc.apparatus:
Achaeos] acheos B A D : acheus R : achaeios H : Achivos coni. Hertz : Acivos coni. Ribbeck2

Capherei] caperei R, Ribbeck 2,3, Klotz : Caphareis Hertz : Capharei Warmington

perdidit] prodidit G corr. G1

Instead of perdidit the reading prodidit has been found. This would give a different meaning to the fragment, since the subject of the fragment would have destroyed something in the case of perdidit. In the case of prodidit however, the subject would be the one who have lost something. The reading of this word is therefore very important for the reconstruction of this fragment.
doc.commentary:
pater] This refers most likely to Oeax’ father, Nauplius. When his son Palamedes died during the Trojan War, Nauplius wanted to take revenge on the Greeks who were responsible for his son’s death. He therefore caused some of the Greek ships to sink, when they tried to go home after the war.

perdidit] As Schierl points out, perdere could also be used in the meaning of ‘losing something’. In that case Agamemnon could be subject and pater would refer to him. These words could in that case be spoken by Orestes. If this would be the case the fragment could still refer to the same happening, namely Agamemnon who lost many of the Greeks, because of Nauplius. However, if the reading of prodidit is accepted, it is not certain that Nauplius is blamed for the loss of so many Greek soldiers. It would therefore be more likely that pater refers to Nauplius, since Oeax probably plays an important role in this tragedy and so does his enmity with Agamemnon and his family. It would therefore make sense that this act of vengeance, the sinking of the Greek ships by Nauplius, is once again reminded, to emphasize this enmity.
doc.reconstruction:
The speaker of this fragment could be Electra. She does not want Oeax and Erigone to marry, and by bringing up this story about Oeax’ father, she can give extra weight to her objections against the marriage by reminding the others about the crimes of Oeax’ father. Electra could also use these words to object against her own marriage with Oeax, since it is not certain with whom Oeax will marry in this tragedy.

As stated above, the words could also be spoken by Orestes, referring to his father Agamemnon, if the reading prodidit would be accepted. The speaker of the fragment could also be Oeax, referring to his own father and his issues with Agamemnon. However, there is no certainty that Oeax is one of the characters playing a role in this play. It could also be the case that he is just referred to rather than playing an actual role.

Since the fragment probably shows the objections against the marriage of Oeax, it would make sense to place the fragment at the beginning of the tragedy, since the wedding would most likely be at the beginning of the tragedy as well. The plot mainly deals with Orestes’ act of vengeance, so that the wedding would probably take place before Orestes has fulfilled his revenge.

context.author: Priscianus
context.text:
Quoted by Priscianus, referring to pleros, to show that forms of plerus without que added to it, were previously used:

vetustissimi tamen et ‘altera utra’ et ‘alterum utrum’ et ‘alterius utrius’ solebant proferre et ‘plerus plera plerum’ absque ‘que’ additione. Pacuvius in Duloreste: Pater Achaeos in Capherei saxis pleros perdidit. Prisc. gramm. II 182, 1.

lines.text: pater Achaeos in Capherei saxis pleros perdidit
Erras erras: nam exultantem te et praefidentem tibi
Repriment ualidae legum habenae atque imperi insistent iugo
Aspice hoc sublimen candens, quem inuocant omnes Iouem
Quidnam est obsecro quod te adiri abnutas?

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: great, wild with a red color and a bold look
doc.differences:
Schierl: 101 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 15/32)

D’Anna: 169 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 17/31)

Klotz: 147 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 23/33)

Warmington: 135 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 16/34)

Ribbeck: 147 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 23/32)

amplus, rubicundo colore et spectu protervo ferox

Klotz: amplus, rubicundo colore et spectu proptervo ferox

All editors agree with the conjecture of rubricundo, which is found in the sole surviving manuscript, to rubicundo. Almost all editors agree as well that proptervo in that manuscript should be changed to protervo, but Klotz does maintain proptervo (see commentary).
doc.apparatus:
rubicundo] rubicundo edd. rubricundo F

protervo] protervo pler. edd. proptervo F
doc.commentary:
amplus] according to Schierl, Pacuvius is the first to use this word in this sense in the description of a person, a kind of usage that would remain rare. Cf. Thesaurus Linguae Latinae I 2007, 53. However, since the words preceding amplus are not preserved, we cannot rule out the possibility that amplus could have referred to something else than a description of a person, and could as well have been used in a more common way in a sense of measurement.

rubicundo] a verbal adjective, which expresses the intensity of the red coloring. Cf. Plaut. Pseud. 1219, Plaut. Rud. 315, Ter. Hec. 440.

spectu] Festus quotes this line to show that spectus is used by Pacuvius without a preposition in the same sense as aspectus or conspectus.

protervo] This adjective expresses boldness or imprudence. One manuscript however has preserved the reading: proptervus. This could be an old spelling of the word, but this cannot be decided for certain because the etymology of the word is unclear.
doc.reconstruction:
This line contains a description of a person. Probably someone describes this individual while he is approaching. This could very well be Orestes entering the stage for the first time. The red color of his skin could be explained by the fact that Orestes has been wandering outside through the country in the preceding period. The wild look in Orestes’ eyes can be explained by the intentions he wants to accomplish after arriving.

D’Anna however has argued that this description could be about Aegisthus, which is a possibility that cannot be fully ruled out. Just as well, it could also be another male character in the play, like Oeax. Reconstructing the identity of the character speaking this line is even harder. Based on how the other character is described, it could be someone who is afraid of or angry with that person.

context.author: Festus
context.text:
This line is quoted once by Festus in De Verborum Significatione to show how Pacuvius uses spectu without a preposition (in contrast with for example aspectus):

Spectu sine praepositione Pacuvius in Duloreste usus est, cum ait:amplus, rubicundo colore, et spectu protervo ferox.

lines.text: amplus, rubicundo colore et spectu protervo ferox
Cur me miseram irridet, magnis compotem et multis malis?

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: video sepulcra dua duorum corporum

doc.translation: I see two tombs of/for two corpses.
doc.differences:
TRF: 406 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 655 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 33 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 656 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 655 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 700 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

I see, of corpses two, two sepulchres.

Franchella: uideo sepulchra duo duorum corporum.

Vedo due sepolcri di due cadaveri.

D'Antò:

uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum.

Vedo due sepolcri per due cadaveri.

Dangel: Je vois deux tombeaux pour deux corps.
doc.apparatus:
sepulcra] sepulcra: sepulcrum AJ: sepulchra FUBSKRE

dua] dua LP2: (al.) ε: a AΣΞ: ad ΔΘΦ: om. P1JV: duo f, cf. Ribbeck1-2 (Quint. Inst. Orat. 1.5.15)

Ribbeck prints duo on the authority of Westman’s manuscript f (= Heerdegen’s Vitebergensis), a manuscript which I have not looked at, adducing Quintilian’s statement at Inst. Orat. 1.5.15 that dua is a barbarism.

The critical apparatus above is based mainly on that of Westman in his edition of Cicero’s Orator (20022, 50). For a full list of manuscripts and their respective sigla, see Westman 20022, viii-xvii. For a succinct account of the textual tradition of Cicero's Orator, see Winterbottom–Rouse–Reeve apud Reynolds 19862, 103-109.
doc.commentary: duorum corporum: a genitive of possession (Woodcock §72) depending on sepulcra, ‘tombs’. Translated either as ‘for two corpses’ (D’Antò, Dangel) or ‘of two corpses’ (Warmington, Franchella).
doc.reconstruction:
In his first edition (1852), Ribbeck proposes that this fragment might belong to Accius’ Aegisthus (Ribbeck1, 334: either spoken by Atreus gleefully showing Thyestes two tombs prepared for the latter’s sons, or spoken by Thyestes, reacting in horror). But in his 1875 monograph (Die römische Tragödie), he suggests that it might also have belonged to the Aegisthus: he imagines the speaker as Cassandra experiencing a prophetic vision of two tombs, one for Agamemnon and the other for herself.

Warmington (II.575) that this fragment belonged either to the Aegisthus or to Clytemnestra. But this attribution hinges on his assumption that the two fragments of Accius preserved at Cic. Orat. 156 (i.e. both this fragment, inc. fab. F 406 TRF, and inc. fab. F 407 TRF, mulier una duum uirum) belonged to the same Accian play. Indeed, Warmington prints the two lines together as a single fragment (Accius inc. fab. 33-4). Nothing in Cicero’s wording need however imply that these two lines of Accius belonged to the same play. Apparently believing that these two lines did belong to the same play, Warmington supports his suggestion that they might be attributed either to the Aegisthus or to the Clytemnestra by citing Ribbeck. Ribbeck had suggested in his 1875 monograph (p. 462, 467) that the Accian line under discussion (inc. fab. F 406 TRF, uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum) belonged to the Aegisthus, and the other to the Clytemnestra (inc. fab. F 407 TRF, mulier una duum uirum). So Ribbeck’s suggestion in fact was that the former line might have belonged to the Aegisthus and the latter to the Clytemnestra (in a scene in which Electra chastises Clytemnestra for her infidelity). Moreover, Ribbeck clearly distinguished between the two fragments. So Warmington misrepresents Ribbeck’s view.

Dangel 378 considers this fragment, as well as Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (mulier una duum uirum), to belong to one of the plays of Accius’ so-called ‘Trojan Cycle’ or ‘Atrid (Pelopid?) Cycle’.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
This fragment is quoted, alongside Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (video sepulcra dua duorum corporum), at Cic. Orat. 156, in a section of the treatise in which the phenomena of hiatus, elision, and related sonic effects are discussed (Orat. 149-64: cf. Kaster 2020, 213-23).

At Orat. 155-6, Cicero presents the view of some contemporary critics who believe that forms such as the archaic genitive plural deum, the contracted form of deorum which was more in use in Cicero’s day, should not be used and that the uncontracted form should be preferred. Cicero cites several examples from Republican tragedy in which the contracted form is used, among which notably our two fragments of Accius (see above) and Fragmenta ex certis incertorum tragoediis F 1.1-3 TRF = Pacuvius Chryses F 76 Schierl. The fragments of Accius are quoted to show that the poet uses the genitive plural forms duorum and duum indifferently.

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius (ia6).

lines.text: propemodum animus coniectura de errore eius augurat
[Thyestes] Nolite... hospites, ad me adire: ilico isti:
Ne contagie mea bonis umbrave obsit.
Tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret.
[ALIUS:] Quidnam est, obsecro? quid te adiri abnutas?
Heu heu, mea Fortuna, ut omnia in me conglomeras mala!
Iuppiter Dardanum genuit, Dardanus Troum, Trous Assaracum et Ilum Catamitumque
<10> Capis ex Assaraco satus .  .  .
Alumento ex Ilo satus qui statuit Pergamum;
Alumento Priamum, Capis et Anchisem edidit.
iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili?
Tum autem lascivum Nerei simum pecus
Ludens ad cantum classem lustratur ...

alii ita tradunt Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomani Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert.
Quin quod parere [mihi] vos maiestas mea
Procat, toleratis temploque hanc deducitis?

doc.author: Accius
doc.text: quare pro facie, pro statura Accius

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: Is this he whom the talk of Greece makes known before all other men?
doc.differences:
Schierl: 103 (Pacuvius’ Doulorestes 9/32)

D’Anna: 170 (Pacuvius’ Doulorestes 18/31)

Ribbeck3: 119 (Pacuvius’ Doulorestes 5/32)

Warmington: 134 (Pacuvius’ Doulorestes 15/34)
doc.apparatus:
hicine] hecine BA

quem] quam L1 :

fama Graia] fama gratia codd. : formae gratia Scaliger, Ciacconius : fama Graii L. Mülller

nobilitat] nobilita AA : nobilitant L. Mülller
doc.commentary:
hicine] The combination of the deictic particle -ce and the interrogative particle -ne modifies the sound of the pronoun (Schiel 2012 264).

fama] an ambivalent word which can refer either to good or bad reputation or to either fame or rumour. Without the context of this statement, we cannot tell which implication is intended here. Schierl notes that fama is found as a topic in Latin comedy, citing Plaut. Persa 384 and Ter. Phorm. 724 (Schierl 2012 264).

nobilitat] Nonius quotes Pacuvius as an example of nobilito being used with the same meaning as notefacio. The same meaning is found in Accius’ Telephus, which Nonius also cites.
doc.reconstruction:
Schierl notes that the deictic sense of hic requires that the person mentioned must be one of the characters in the play. Her question is whether fama has positive or negative connotations in this context, and whether the sentence is ironic. If fama is positive, it could describe Orestes (Cf. L. Müller 25), but if it is negative or ironic it could be used mockingly of Aegisthus (Schierl 2012 264). Since Orestes is disguised as a slave in this tragedy, and Aegisthus is more infamous than well-renowned, I find the proposal that the statement is ironic and perhaps mocking to be convincing. Pacuvius may be deliberately utilising the ambivalence of fama to insinuate that the character addressed has either no reputation or a bad one, giving the statement a cruel and sarcastic irony. The hyperbole of ante omnis… viros could also indicate mockery.

In the Loeb edition, Warminton reconstructs this as Orestes talking about Aegisthus, with no mention of his tone. It is followed by Fr. 101, presumably also thought to be referring to the king. This takes place when Orestes and Pylades have first entered in disguise, and before Aegisthus is warned of their plot. D’Anna (1967) places the fragment just before Fr. 102 and 104, suggesting instead that Aegisthus is referring to Orestes after he has been warned of his plot. However, the extra emphasis placed on the particle hic (to hic-ce) might suggest that Warmington and d’Anna are wrong in situating the statement in a private conversation, pointing to the likelihood that it occurs during a confrontation between the speaker and the man referred to.

context.author: Nonius Marcellus
context.text: Nonius 588 L. (352, 5 M): ‘nobilitarent’ notefacerent dictum est. Titinius… Accius Telepho… Pacuvius Douloreste: hicine is est quem fama Graia ante omnis nobilitat viros?

lines.text: hicine is est quem fama Graia ante omnis nobilitat viros?
Multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli
Quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus
Paretur? aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili
Poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?
mater est terra, ea parit corpus, animam | aether adiugat
Iamne oculos specie laetavisti optabili?
Cur me miseram inridet, magnis compotem et multis malis?
quidnam est obsecro quod te adiri abnutas?
Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socru
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctus nuptiis.
nolite hospites ad me adire. ilico istic.
ne contagio mea bonis umbrave obsit.
tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret.
Matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas!

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation:
Then hither the king born from his father Dryas,

bring Lycurgus!
doc.differences:
Klotz: 46 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/24)

Marmorale: 21 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/24)

Ribbeck1: 21 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/24)

Ribbeck2: 49-50 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/24)

Ribbeck3: 46-47 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/24)

TrRF: 23 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 7/24)

Warmington: 55-56 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 22/25)



proinde huc Dryante regem prognatum patre, Lycurgum cette

Ribbeck1,2,3: proinde huc Dryante regem prognatum patre, Lycurgum cette!

Klotz: proinde huc Triante regem prognatum patre Lycurgum cette.



The editorial differences concern only the spelling of the word Dryante and the interpunction. Therefore, these differences have no implications for the interpretation of the fragment. Except for the TrRF edition, which orders the fragments chronologically, this fragment is placed third to last in all editions. The editors also seem to ascribe the lines to the same moment in the play. Ribbeck attributes the fragment to Liber, who would have spoken these lines after the burning of Lycurgus’s palace. He places this fragment after the fragment in which Vulcanus is mentioned (frg. 24 TrRF). Marmorale and Klotz agree with this positioning. In Warmington’s edition, the fragment follows fragment 39 (TrRF). However, this fragment could also be related to the burning of Lycurgus’ palace and is preceded by fragment 24 (TrRF). Therefore, Warmington also seems to place the fragment after the destruction of Lycurgus’s palace.
doc.apparatus:
1. Dryante] Triante coni. Diehl, Klotz, Leo (1910, 22), Grilli (1965, 148) : Druante coni. Traglia

1. proinde huc Dryante] proindustriantte vel proin dustriant te codd.[Li, Wa, Marm, Kl] (L H)[Mu] (F H L P G E P7579)[On] : pro industriam te G[Mu] : pro industria ne te coni. Aldus, Stephanus : proin Dryante Iunius prob. Vossius (1620, 119), Mercerus, Scriverius, Bothe, Klussmann, Quicherat : proín Dryante Duentzer (1837, 435) : proin Dysdryante (vel Dusdriante) Bergk (1874, 281) (= Id. 1884, 348) : proin dis Dryante Spengel (1899, 392) : proinde huc Dryante coni. Ribbeck.

1. patre] patrem G[Mu, Ri] G1[On]

The manuscript reading proindustriantte does not make sense. Many alternative readings have been suggested but in the more recent editions Ribbeck’s coniecture proinde huc Dryante is adopted. More information about this is provided in the commentary. As is also discussed in the commentary, alternative spellings for Dryante were proposed by Leo (1920, 22) and Traglia. For a discussion of those spellings, see the commentary. G is the only manuscript that reads patre instead of patrem. However, the accusative patrem does not make much sense in combination with regem and prognatum. Therefore, the accusative patrem probably is a scribal error. This error could easily be made since the the two preceding words are accusatives.
doc.commentary:
proinde huc Dryante] The manuscripts read proindustriantte, which does not make sense. As pro is usually followed by an ablative and according to Lewis and Short (1966, 1447) the combination of pro with accusative only occurs in late Latin, the reading pro industriam te found by Mueller in G seems to be corrupt. The coniecture pro industria ne te made by Aldus and Stephanus solves the problem with the accusative after pro. However, by retaining te the speaker would refer to somebody who is already present. This is problematic as te then corresponds with regem prognatum. These words likely refer to king Lycurgus, whose name is mentioned in the following line. From Lycurgum cette, we can deduce that Lycurgus is not present yet and thus could not be addressed with te. Reading Dryante for the part could be a sensible alternative since Lycurgus was considered to be the son of Dryas. Nevertheless, Iunius’ emendation proin Dryante is problematic. proin should be read disyllabic to fit the metre (senarius iambicus) but according to Lewis and Short (1966, 1462) proin usually is monosyllabic. Therefore, the reading proinde huc Dryante with disyllabic proinde as proposed by Ribbeck should be preferred. Alternatively, the part proindus could be read as pro in dus with dus corrupted dis from diis. According to Spengel (1988, 392) this would perfectly make sense, since cette is often followed by the dative mihi. In this case mihi would then be replaced by dis to designate that Lycurgus should be handed over to the gods.

Dryante] Diehl and Klotz in their editions adopt the suggestion by Leo (1910, 22) to read Triante. He prefers this reading since in Latin the onset /dr/ usually is avoided. This onset did not occur in ancient Latin words and the combination /dr/ in Greek loanwords was probably pronounced /tr/ (eg. drachma pronounced as /trachma/). As the manuscripts read triantte instead of driantte, the reading Triante might be preferred. The choice for <y> or <i> is arbitrary since both graphemes render the Greek upsilon.

cette] This word is the reason Nonius quotes these lines. The word originates from the imperative cedo which plural cedate is contracted to cette. As Nonius states, the word has the same meaning as dicite (tell!) or date (bring!). Since the context of the fragment is lost, it is impossible to decide which meaning is used here.
doc.reconstruction:
Nonius in his De compendiosa doctrina (Non. p. 84, 31-34 Merc. = 120 Li.) attributes this fragment to Naevius’ Lycurgus. This is plausible since Lycurgus himself is mentioned in the second line (Lycurgum cette). Hence, all editors ascribe the fragment to the Lycurgus of Naevius.

From the fragment itself we can deduce that at least two people are on stage. The adverb cette is the plural of the imperative cedo and should therefore refer to more than one person. The number of persons present on stage is partly dependent on the interpretation of cette. Nonius states that this word could have the same meaning as both dicite and date. If the word is interpreted as dicite, the speakers were probably exhorting themselves to shout. This would require that there were at least two persons on stage. Duentzer is the only editor who seems to interpret cette in this manner. He thinks the words are spoken by a chorus consisting of the followers of Lycurgus when the king comes back from hunting.

If the word is interpreted as date there should at least be three persons on stage since somebody is giving an order to at least two other persons. From the fact that those persons are ordered to bring Lycurgus, it can be deduced that Lycurgus was not yet on stage. Spengel (1899, 392), who proposes the reading proin dis Dryante, suggests that the speaker of these lines requests that Lycurgus is handed over to the gods to be punished. He does not specify who is speaking to whom but his argument suggests that the lines were spoken after Lycurgus had imprisoned Bacchus and killed the Bacchants. Following this interpretation, the speaker should sympathize with the Bacchants or at least disapprove of the deeds of Lycurgus. Therefore, the speaker most likely was a bacchant or even a chorus of Bacchants. If the latter would be true, the chorus was probably not addressing specific people but uttering a more general wish that Lycurgus may be punished by the gods. However, it is not very plausible that the lines were spoken by the chorus, as the senarius iambicus normally was not used for choral songs.

Out of the editors that read proinde huc Dryante with or without a variation in the spelling of Dryante, Bothe and Klussmann think that the words are spoken by Bacchus to the attendants of Lycurgus after they have left their king. Bacchus would have requested king Lycurgus in exchange for peace. This interpretation fits partly with the story told by Apollodorus (3.5), as in this story the Edonians indeed handed over Lycurgus to Bacchus by bounding him on mount Parnassus. However, in Apollodorus’ version of the story, not Bacchus himself but an oracle declared that the Edonians should extradite Lycurgus. Furthermore, Diodorus Siculus (3.65) tells in his version of the story that Lycurgus was not handed over but was captured by Bacchus after he and his army had destroyed Thrace. In their editions, Ribbeck, Wacholder, Marmorale, and Traglia also ascribe the lines to Bacchus but they think that these are victoriously spoken by Bacchus after he has burnt down Lycurgus palace. He would triumphantly have asked for the king so that he could show his revenge to him.

Dryante regem prognatum is a quite respectful designation for Lycurgus. These words could be spoken by Bacchus ironically. However, if they are actually meant to be respectful, it would be unlikely that the lines are spoken by Bacchus or somebody else who wants to punish Lycurgus. Furthermore, it suggests that the speaker has a lower standing than the king. As this speaker is giving orders to other people, the addressees were probably from the same or an even lower standing. This corresponds with the interpretation Ribbeck gives in one of his articles (Ribbeck 1875, 85). There, he suggests that the fragment should be placed more at the beginning of the play. A messenger would have spoken the lines of the fragment to ask for king Lycurgus since he wanted to announce that the Bacchants had arrived. The addressees could then have been the satellites of Lycurgus, who might or might not have constituted the chorus.

Bibliography

Oldfather, C.H. 1933. Diodorus of Sicily. The Library of History. Books I-II.34. Cambridge, MA/ London.

Ribbeck, O. 1875. Die römische Tragödie im Zeitalter der Republik. Leipzig.

Ribbeck1, O. 1852. Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Ribbeck2, O. 1871. Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Ribbeck3, 1897. Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta: vol. 1: Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta. Leipzig.

Schauer, M. 2012. Tragicorum Romanorum fragmenta: vol. 1: Livius Andronicus: Naevius: Tragici Minores: Fragmenta Adespota. Göttingen.

cette significat dicite vel date ab eo quod cedo. Naevius Lycurgo proinde huc Dryante regem prognatum patre, Lycurgum cette. (Non. p. 84, 31-34 Merc. = 120 Li.)

context.text: autumare est sperare. Pacuvius Duloreste:-

lines.text: aut hic est aut hic adfore actutum autumo

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: First I ask this of you: do not make me relentless, do not defile your age with lying talk.
doc.differences:
Schierl: 92 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 5/32)

D’Anna: 153-154 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 8/31)

Warmington: 127-128 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 7/34)

Ribbeck2 14 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 7/31)

primum hoc abs te oro: ni me inexorabilem faxis, ni turpassis vanitudine aetatem tuam

D'Anna: primum hoc abs ted oro: ni me nimis inexorabilem faxis, ni turpassis vanitudine aetatem tuam

Warmington: primum hoc abs te oro: minus inexorabilem faxis, ni turpassis vanitudine aetatem tuam

Ribbeck2: primum hoc abs te oro: nimis inexorabilem faxis, ni turpassis vanitudine aetatem tuam

All authors place the fragment at the beginning of the play. D’Anna and Ribbeck2 put it together with the oro, nive plectas fandi mi prolixitudinem fragment. Schierl and Warmington don’t make the fragments into one fragment, but they do place one after the other.
doc.apparatus:
te] codd. : ted D'Anna

ni me nimis] nimis Lachmann, Ribbeck2: nimis codd. : minus Ribbeck3, Lindsay, Warmington, Klotz, Mette : ni me in te ante nimis add. Onions : ni me ante nimis add. D'Anna.
doc.commentary:
Nimis] The codices hand down nimis, which is problematic. Nimis could be corrected to ni me or ni me could be added to the sentence. The added nimis would mean that the speaker asks to not be made exceedingly relentless. Ribbeck in his third edition conjugates minus since he suspects that there must have been a corresponding accusative at the beginning of this verse. He also believes that there is a change in speaker after faxis. The second speaker rebuked the first speaker’s threat. There are also editors (Warmington, Lindsay, Klotz, Mette) who accept the minus without the change in speaker.

Faxis] is mostly found as a prohibitive and is therefore a logical counterpart to ni turpassis. Faxis and turpassis as well are forms of the sigmatic subjunctive.
doc.reconstruction:
Some editors combine this fragment wit fragment 93 because of the oro nive that Lachmann suggests.

A suppliant is scolded for lying or doing something the speaker doesn’t want them to do. This scene might portray Klytaemnestra rejecting Electra’s reproaches. Another possibility is that the speaker is Orestes who refuses someone’s plea for mercy. The remark about aetatem tuam indicates that there must be a age difference significant enough to touch upon it. Whether the statement refers to someone older or younger than the speaker is unknown. It could mean that such a young person shouldn’t defile their pure, young age with lying or it could mean that an old person who should be wise with age shouldn’t defile that with lying. Most scholars believe that this heated argument is between Klytaemnestra and Electra. Which of the two is the speaker and, if there is indeed a change in speaker as Ribbeck suggests, which person speaks which parts, is unknown. Regardless of who the speaker is, this scene is probably placed at the beginning of the play during Erigone’s and Oeax’s wedding where Klytaemnestra and Electra come in to contact and start a fight. Fragment 92, if it is spoken by Electra, could mean that she makes a snappy comment towards Klytaemnestra for the killing of Agamemnon. Or, if it is Klytaemnestra who is speaking, fragment 92 may be an answer to Electra’s snappy comment.

@book{D'Anna1967, author = {D'Anna, I}, title = {M. Pacuvii Fragmenta}, date = {1967}, location = {Rome}, }

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted by Nonius in his De Compendiosa Doctrina.

Nonius quotes these lines to let us know that vanitudo is used here for vanitate.

'vanitudo' pro vanitate. Plautus ... Pacuvius Duloreste: —. NON. 270 L. (184,3 M.)

lines.text: — x —) primum hoc abs te oro: ni me inexorabilem
faxis, ni turpassis vanitudine aetatem tuam
Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socro
Oenomao rege Hippodameam raptis nanctust nuptiis.
[THYESTES]: .  .  . Vigilandum est semper; multae insidiae sunt bonis.
Nolite, hospites, ad me adire, ilico istic,
Ne contagio mea bonis umbrave obsit.
Meo tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret
Quidnam est obsecro quod te adiri abnutas?
quam mihi maxime hodie hic contigerit malum.
...e qua mihi maxime hodie hic contigerit malum.
... sed valvae resonunt regiae.
Probae etsi in segetem sunt deteriorem datae
Fruges, tamen ipsae suapte natura enitent.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: the feeling, which
doc.differences:
Schierl: 118 (Pacuvius Dulorestes 31/31)

Ribbeck3: 160 (Pacuvius Dulorestes 31/31)

D’Anna: 188 (Pacuvius Dulorestes 31/31)

animum quae

D’Anna: animumque

Ribbeck3: animum quae … moenis

All editors place this fragment at the end of the play, probably because there almost no information to be gained from this fragment, so therefore they placed it at the end.
doc.apparatus:
animum quae] animumque H1 : animum quae rel. : animumque (munem) conj. Stieglitz : animumque conj. Nosarti : animum quae … moenis conj. Ribbeck 2/3 : animum quae … moenes conj. Klotz : animum quae conj. Lindsay : animum quae … (moenes) conj. Warmington

This whole line, or the two words (or possibly one) that remain, is one giant textual problem. Therefore, almost all editors have tried to conjecture the line to something more than it is, mostly based on the context in Nonius, who comments on the word moenes so editors have tried to place the word in one case or another somewhere in the text. Because there is not much to work, they placed the word after animum quae mostly in the accusative plural. The most important notion we are left with is the fact we should place moenes somewhere and that we do not have enough information to figure out the context.
doc.commentary: This fragment is very short, because Nonius’ text, which cites this fragment, is corrupt. Nonius cites this fragment to explain the meaning of “moenes”, which comes from munus, meaning “duty”. However, this word is not in the fragment and therefore, people have made conjectures with moenes. It is not clear in what case the word should be or if it should be singular or plural. Ribbeck proposed moenis and Klotz and Warmington proposed moenes, which they all add after this fragment with a lacuna in between (i.e. animum quae […] moenis/moenes). It seems like munus has to mean that someone is doing his duty and treating others well.
doc.reconstruction:
To even think about beginning a reconstruction of this fragment within the play seems practically impossible. The most information we have seems to come from the word moenes, which is not even transmitted in the fragment itself. Someone could be saying to someone else that they should fulfil their duty, but that could be almost anyone in the story of Orestes. Orestes himself thinks he his fulfilling a duty by revenging his father and killing his mother. Following Euripides’ Elektra it could be that Elektra is telling Orestes here that he should fulfil his duty. Indeed, the very name of the play Dulorestes seems to suggest that Orestes had no choice but to fulfil his duty. There are of course lots of other options. For example, earlier in the story, it could also be Aegisthus telling Clytaemnestra that she should fulfil her duty by killing her husband Agamemnon.

D’Anna, I. 1967. M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Klotz, A., O. Seel & L. Voit. 1953. Tragicorum fragmenta (Scaenicorum Romanorum fragmenta; vol. 1), Munich.

Nosarti, L.: “Coniectanea II (Pacuvio, Accio, Corippo)”, MusPat 5,1987, 311-313.

Ribbeck, O., 1897. Scaenicae Romanorum poesis fragmenta (ed. 3). Leipzig.

Schierl, P. 2006. Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin; New York.

Stieglitz, H.W.A., 1826, De M. Pacuvii Duloreste, Leipzig.
