Quoted once in De mutata declinatione by Nonius Marcellus

Nonius presumably uses this line to prove that moenes can be ascribed to someone who is doing what his friends want.

’moenes’ apud veteres dicebantur non a largitia (suppl. ed. a. 1476) ignota erat, sed consentientes ad id quod amici velint. Pacuvius Duloreste: animum quae (Non. Marc. De comp. doct. 23,9 M.

lines.text: animum quae
si qua potestur investigari via

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: What now (alas me!), it pains me to utter the name of my father, it embarrasses me to utter the name of my mother.
doc.differences:
Schierl: 96 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes: 10/32)

D’Anna: 158-160 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 10/31)

Ribbeck2: 143-145 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 21/32)

Ribbeck3: 143-145 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 21/32)

Warmington: 138-140 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 16/32)

quid quod iam (ei mihi!) piget paternum nomen, maternum pudet profari

Ribbeck2: quid? quod iam et mehe piget paternum nomen, maternum pudet profari

Ribbeck3: quid? quod iam et mihi piget paternum nomen, maternum pudet profari

All the editors put the fragment around the middle of the tragedy, when Electra and Orestes probably meet for the first time after years. However they disagree on the exact position. Schierl and D’Anna place it in the first half, whereas Ribbeck places it in the second half. See further the commentary for the implications for the reconstruction.
doc.apparatus:
1. quod iam] quod iam L. Müller (quod coni. Bergk) : quondam codd. : quoniam Iunius

1. ei mihi] ei mihi Umpfenbach : et mihi codd. : et mehe Ribbeck2 (cf. Quint. 1,5,21)

There are some problematic elements in the first line. Firstly, the transmitted quondam, meaning ‘once’, did not make much sense, therefore it has been modified by Iunius in quoniam and by Müller in quod iam (the latter following Bergk’s conjecture). The second issue concerns the printed ei mihi, conjectured by Umpfenbach. The manuscripts’ reading et mihi is unsatisfactory, because the verbs piget and pudet usually govern the accusative of the person, not the dative. For similar reasons, Ribbeck2’s mehe (based on Quintilian), ‘for me’, has been rejected. As in the case of quid quod, ei mihi can be explained as an instance of Pacuvius’ well-known interest in comic elements (see further the commentary).
doc.commentary:
quid quod] It introduces a question (for this use see e.g. Plaut. Poen. 678).

ei mihi!] This interjection, used to express distress, is often found in comedy, but can appear also in tragedy. Interestingly, it is not so odd, in the case of Pacuvius, to find comic elements within a tragedy.

piget] This impersonal verb is usually used to refer to repentance, as Nonius argues, or annoyance. However, piget can also denote mourning, as it is probably the case in this fragment. Thus, uttering the name of the father provokes pain in the speaker.
doc.reconstruction:
One person, on stage, is speaking in the first person (ei mihi), complaining about his/her own familiar misfortunes. Given the mention of the parents, it is highly likely that the speaker is either Orestes or Electra. The mention of both mother and father also points towards frg. 97, where the evil nature of the former is evoked together with the murder of the latter. According to Schierl (2006), these lines are pronounced in the moment of the anagnorisis (as in D’Anna 1967) or in the context of the siblings’ plot against Clytemnestra (as in Warmington 1936, who argues that the lines belong to Orestes). According to D’Anna (1967, 205) they can also belong to Electra’s outburst against her mother.

The meaning revolves around the contraposition of piget and pudet, which form a chiasm (piget paternum…maternum pudet) and an alliteration (also with profari). piget, referring to Agamemnon, may allude to the grief for his death (see commentary), whereas pudet may signify the sense of shame provoked by the murderous mother. This interpretation is substantiated by the connection with frg. 97.

Bibliography

D’Anna, I. 1967. M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Ribbeck, O. 1871. Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (2nd ed.). Leipzig.

Ribbeck, O. 1897. Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta (3rd ed.). Leipzig.

Schierl, P. 2006. Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin, New York 2006.

Warmington, E. 1936. Remains of Old Latin, Volume II: Livius Andronicus. Naevius. Pacuvius. Accius. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius quotes these lines as he is discussing the different meaning of piget (signifying ‘repentance’) and pudet (signifying ‘feeling shameful’):

‘pudet’ et ‘piget’. Hoc distat: ‘pudet’ enim verecundiae est, ‘pigere’ paenitentiae. Plautus…idem…Accius Neoptolemo…Turpilius…Pacuvius Duloreste: quid quod iam (ei mihi!) piget paternum nomen, maternum pudet profari — Sallustius…Varro… (Non. 685 L.)

lines.text: quid quod iam (ei mihi!)
piget paternum nomen, maternum pudet
profari

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Aegisthus

lines.text: video sepulcra dua duorum corporum

doc.translation: I see two tombs of/for two corpses.
doc.differences:
TRF: 406 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 655 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 33 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 656 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 655 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 700 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

I see, of corpses two, two sepulchres.

Franchella: uideo sepulchra duo duorum corporum.

Vedo due sepolcri di due cadaveri.

D'Antò:

uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum.

Vedo due sepolcri per due cadaveri.

Dangel: Je vois deux tombeaux pour deux corps.
doc.apparatus:
sepulcra] sepulcra: sepulcrum AJ: sepulchra FUBSKRE

dua] dua LP2: (al.) ε: a AΣΞ: ad ΔΘΦ: om. P1JV: duo f, cf. Ribbeck1-2 (Quint. Inst. Orat. 1.5.15)

Ribbeck prints duo on the authority of Westman’s manuscript f (= Heerdegen’s Vitebergensis), a manuscript which I have not looked at, adducing Quintilian’s statement at Inst. Orat. 1.5.15 that dua is a barbarism.

The critical apparatus above is based mainly on that of Westman in his edition of Cicero’s Orator (20022, 50). For a full list of manuscripts and sigla, see Westman 20022, viii-xvii.
doc.commentary: duorum corporum: a genitive of possession (Woodcock §72) depending on sepulcra, ‘tombs’. Translated either as ‘for two corpses’ (D’Antò, Dangel) or ‘of two corpses’ (Warmington, Franchella).
doc.reconstruction:
In his first edition (1852), Ribbeck proposes that this fragment might belong to Accius’ Aegisthus (Ribbeck1, 334: either spoken by Atreus gleefully showing Thyestes two tombs prepared for the latter’s sons, or spoken by Thyestes, reacting in horror). But in his 1875 monograph (Die römische Tragödie), he suggests that it might also have belonged to the Aegisthus: he imagines the speaker as Cassandra experiencing a prophetic vision of two tombs, one for Agamemnon and the other for herself.

Warmington (II.575) that this fragment belonged either to the Aegisthus or to Clytemnestra. But this attribution hinges on his assumption that the two fragments of Accius preserved at Cic. Orat. 156 (i.e. both this fragment, inc. fab. F 406 TRF, and inc. fab. F 407 TRF, mulier una duum uirum) belonged to the same Accian play. Indeed, Warmington prints the two lines together as a single fragment (Accius inc. fab. 33-4). Nothing in Cicero’s wording need however imply that these two lines of Accius belonged to the same play. Apparently believing that these two lines did belong to the same play, Warmington supports his suggestion that they might be attributed either to the Aegisthus or to the Clytemnestra by citing Ribbeck. Ribbeck had suggested in his 1875 monograph (p. 462, 467) that the Accian line under discussion (inc. fab. F 406 TRF, uideo sepulcra duo duorum corporum) belonged to the Aegisthus, and the other to the Clytemnestra (inc. fab. F 407 TRF, mulier una duum uirum). So Ribbeck’s suggestion in fact was that the former line might have belonged to the Aegisthus and the latter to the Clytemnestra (in a scene in which Electra chastises Clytemnestra for her infidelity). Moreover, Ribbeck clearly distinguished between the two fragments. So Warmington misrepresents Ribbeck’s view.

Dangel 378 considers this fragment, as well as Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (mulier una duum uirum), to belong to one of the plays of Accius’ so-called ‘Trojan Cycle’ or ‘Atrid (Pelopid?) Cycle’.

context.author: Cicero
context.text:
This fragment is quoted, alongside Accius inc. fab. F 407 TRF (video sepulcra dua duorum corporum), at Cic. Orat. 156, in a section of the treatise in which the phenomena of hiatus, elision, and related sonic effects are discussed (Orat. 149-64: cf. Kaster 2020, 213-23).

At Orat. 155-6, Cicero presents the view of some contemporary critics who believe that forms such as the archaic genitive plural deum, the contracted form of deorum which was more in use in Cicero’s day, should not be used and that the uncontracted form should be preferred. Cicero cites several examples from Republican tragedy in which the contracted form is used, among which notably our two fragments of Accius (see above) and Fragmenta ex certis incertorum tragoediis F 1.1-3 TRF = Pacuvius Chryses F 76 Schierl. The fragments of Accius are quoted to show that the poet uses the genitive plural forms duorum and duum indifferently.

doc.metrical_analysis: Iambic senarius (ia6).

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: but a sound strikes my ears with the beat of feet
doc.differences:
TrRF: 138 (Ennius’ Thyestes 7/10)

Jocelyn: 305 (Ennius’ Thyestes 9/12)

Warmington: 354 (Ennius’ Thyestes 6/14)

Vahlen2: 341 (Ennius’ Thyestes 2/14)

Ribbeck 2/3: 296 (Ennius’ Thyestes2/11)

No textual differences between editors.
doc.apparatus:
pedum pulsu] transp. pulsu pedum in uet. Gloss. Bibl. imp. 7580 : pedum pulsu rel.

Quicherat found this transposed word order in a couple of codices deteriores: this transposition turns a smooth senarius into rather an ungainly one.
doc.commentary:
increpat] Jocelyn assembles a large number of linguistic parallels for the image of sound physically affecting the ears, whether that be by ‘rattling’, ‘ringing’ or ‘striking’ them, in Roman and Greek texts, including many from Plautus, as well as Homer, Sophocles, Accius, Virgil and Seneca’s Medea. The most famous of these was from the Iliad, ἵππων μ‘ ὠκυπόδων ἀμφὶ κτύπος οὔατα βάλλει (Hom. Il. 10, 535, ‘the sound of quickfooted horses strikes my ears’) and comes from Nestor’s speech announcing the return of Odysseus and Diomedes. Perhaps Ennius’ line also has the diegetic role of announcing an arrival on stage. Boyle, who in general is interested in the lesser generic differences (compared to Greek drama) between comedy and tragedy on the Roman stage, notes that the words would certainly not be out of place in comedy.

pedum pulsu] Note the alliteration in pedum pulsu (further examples in Jocelyn ad loc.), which is characteristic for Ennius’ style and links the verse directly to his Annals, frg. 1 Sk (see reconstruction). pulsu could also suggest something more sinister than either running or dancing, such as a march or a pursuit.

In Seneca’s version of the play, before Atreus’ coup de théâtre, the actor playing Thyestes delivers a monody after the feast, a melancholy song that quickly moves from festive jubilation to mournful lament (on this monody, see particularly Meltzer (1988) 312-315). It is possible that such a monody – perhaps not quite so bittersweet and ironic – was found in Ennius’ play, and frg. 138 refers to its accompanying dance (a contrast with Seneca, where, to judge from Thyestes’ words, the Senecan Thyestes seems to lie near-comatose, although there are no explicit stage directions). Frg. 136 is also a line which might belong to such a monody, which the majority of commentators attribute to Thyestes (although Jocelyn suggests it could be sung by either Thyestes or Atreus, and Boyle favours Atreus).
doc.reconstruction:
This line must belong to a single speaker (meas) or a group of speakers (the chorus), who refers to themselves in the first person singular, who perceive/s a sound of ‘feet’, i.e. someone(?) approaching. Only a few editors and commentators have hazarded guesses about a hypothetical context for this fragment, and even these are vague. Ribbeck, seeing in them a certain dramatic excitement, suggests that they could be spoken by Atreus or another character to announce Thyestes’ arrival on stage after the feast off-stage. Boyle suggests that it might be Atreus’ arrival. Segura Morena suggested they were the words of Thyestes himself, without explanation. Warmington, as part of his assumption that Ennius’ Thyestes also included an episode at the court of King Thesprotus in Sicyon, thought that this line was delivered by the chorus as Thyestes rushed in, having fled from Atreus and the palace at Mycenae in the wake of the macabre dinner. So, where some have been brave enough to make suggestions, these have been along the lines that an entry is being marked, although no commentator is particularly sure about when, where or whose entry this might be and who is speaking. The line could be announcing someone arriving on the stage with an urgent message, or in hot pursuit, or marching in. If, as in Accius’ Atreus, Ennius’ Thyestes also made an aggressive move on Atreus, it could be the chorus announcing the messenger who warns Atreus, or Thyestes coming himself.

The parallel mentioned in the commentary with the first line of Ennius’ Annals (Musae, quae pedibus magnum pulsatis Olympum, frg. 1 Sk) with its dancing muses highlight that the entering figure might be dancing onto the stage, rather than running or walking. Frg. 1 Sk plays with the association of rhythmic dance with the striding rhythm of the poetic hexameter line, as the Annals was the first Latin epic composed with this meter as opposed to the relatively free Saturnian (see further Hinds (1998) 56-57). There is nothing unusual about the scansion of frg. 138, a fairly standard senarius, with the resolution of the second element of the first metron very common, in the same way that Sk. Ann. 1 is a typical hexameter. The parallel between TrRF frg. 138 M and Ann.1 Sk, with its alliterative pattern, points to an onomatopoeic effect that seems to have interested Ennius, the opportunity to mirror the (rhythmic) movement of feet in the beat of a metrical line, even if TrRF 138 is an iambic senarius not a dactylic hexameter. Previous hypotheses of context have suggested the entry of a character who is running, but the parallel with the Annals might make the arrival of a dancing character or characters – whether that is the chorus or e.g. the drunken Thyestes (see reconstruction) – a possibility worth consideration. A drunken Thyestes might announce the entrance of a dancing chorus, or, vice versa, the chorus might announce the entrance of a drunken Thyestes, who dances and perhaps sings a monody, in the wake of the feast but before Atreus’ revelation. If the line does refer to dancing, perhaps it is a description of Thyestes or the chorus dancing onto the stage, the likeliest characters to be involved in dancing.

lines.text: sed sonitus auris meas pedum pulsu increpat
Nemo haece vostrum ruminetur mulieri.

doc.author: Naevius
doc.title: Lycurgus
doc.translation: that [they] rage over our [...] far and wide
doc.differences:
TRF: 39 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 23/24)

Klotz: 49 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 23/24)

Marmorale: p. 196 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 23/24)

Ribbeck2: 53 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 23/24)

Ribbeck3: 49 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 23/24)

Warmington: 54 (Naevius’ Lycurgus 21/25)

late longeque trans nostros fervere

Klotz: late longeque Traces nostros fervere

Marmorale: longe lateque Thraces nostros fervere

Ribbeck2: late longeque transtros fervere

Ribbeck3: late longeque transtros nostros fervere

Warmington: late longeque transtros nostros fervere

All modern editors place this fragment near the end of the play. Ribbeck and Warmington place the fragment during the palace miracle when Liber punishes Lycurgus for having apprehended him and his retinue. According to Ribbeck, the fragment shows the palace being destroyed in a manner similar to Euripides’ Bacchae 594. Marmorale, however, places the fragment after the death or punishment of Lycurgus. He argues that the Thracians are in uproar because of the death of their king and/or for having accepted the Bacchic rites. In his interpretation fervere has the sense the word has in Vergil’s Aeneid IV 409-10: cum litora fervere late / prospiceres arce ex summa (when you saw from the top of the citadel that the beach was in an uproar everywhere).
doc.apparatus:
late longeque] late longeque codd. potiores: α Bamb. β γ δ E G H L Lugd. Montepess. Oxon. P P7665 P7666 : longe lateque Scriv., Bo., Klu., Marm.

trans] trans codd. potiores: α Bamb. β γ δ E G H L Lugd. Montepess. Oxon. P P7665 P7666 : transtros Ri., Wa. : Thracia nostros Grotius : Thraces Bo., Mu., Li., Marm. : Traces Kl., Tra. : transtra nostris Qui.

nostros] nostros codd. potiores: α Bamb. β γ δ E G H L Lugd. Montepess. Oxon. P7665 P7666 : nostras P

Editors such as Bothe, Klussmann and Marmorale printed the more common longe lateque instead of the transmitted late longeque. Most manuscripts contain trans nostros which is contested by almost all editors except Klussmann. Ribbeck first conjectured transtros nostros, which was taken up by Warmington. Bothe suggested Thraces nostros from an earlier suggestion by Grotius, which was taken up by, among others, Müller and Marmorale, but reprinted by Klotz as Traces.
doc.commentary:
Late longeque] The manuscripts contain late longeque, even though longe lateque is more common. Resolving the order of the first two words does not seem to change the overall meaning of the fragment.

trans nostros] Klussmann assumed that Nonius simply neglected the word in agreement with nostros (e.g. fines or terminos), since he was mainly interested in the form of fervere. Ribbeck conjectured transtros, and later transtros nostros, the latter of which was also accepted by Warmington. The doubling of -stros could very well explain a scribal slip in the transmission of the original manuscript. Ribbeck’s conjecture is clever, even though transter, the supposed masculine form of transtrum, is not attested. Furthermore, Klotz rejects transtros, because it does not agree with the general sense of late longeque. Bothe suggested Thraces nostros (printed by Klotz as Traces nostros), which is accepted by most editors. It should be noted that all conjectures keep nostros as part of the subject accusative in the ACI construction, perhaps in virtue of keeping the fragmented phrase grammatically contained. In the citing style of Nonius, however, this is not a necessity (cf. TrRF 21, where the main verb is missing).
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment is an iambic trimeter, if the conjectures of Ribbeck or Bothe are correct. Klussmann’s adherence to the original manuscripts would make the fragment a cretic, which should therefore be attributed to, for example, a sung part of the choir. Instead, he incorrectly placed the cretic in a messenger’s speech to Lycurgus about the Bacchants being unleashed on his lands.

Transtros in the conjecture of Ribbeck and Warmington would refer to the crossbeams or transoms of Lycurgus’ palace, taking on a metonymical sense. Ribbeck explicitly refers to Euripides’ Bacchae 594-5 as a predecessor to this fragment: ἅπτε κεραύνιον αἴθοπα λαμπάδα, / σύμφλεγε σύμφλεγε δώματα Πενθέος (kindle the flashing light of a thunderbolt, / burn up, burn up the palace of Pentheus). Nostros implies that the line would have been uttered by Lycurgus, one of his attendants or some other Thracian character announcing the destruction of the palace. The particularity of transtros fits awkwardly with the general sense of late longeque (cf. Klotz), especially if “crossbeams” is to be taken as a pars pro toto for just one building. Spaltenstein (2014, p. 505) suggests that the general idea conferred by late longeque makes the destruction of the palace final and total, which would make sense from a dramatic perspective.

There are, however, two other reasons to believe that transtros is not the correct conjecture. Firstly, no masculine form of transtrum is attested, about which Ribbeck himself does not make any comment. Secondly, according to Bednarek (2021, p.138-9), since there is no need to postulate a palace miracle (i.e. the miraculous destruction of the palace of the king) in the Aeschylean version of the play, there is no need to think that it necessarily featured in Naevius’ tragedy. Aeschylus probably described the palace as rejoicing in Bacchic ecstasy rather than being destroyed.

Most editors accept Bothe’s Thraces instead of transtros, later reprinted by Klotz as Traces. Early Latin did not know the aspirations in words originally taken from Greek. Bothe interpreted the line as an observation from Lycurgus, that his people are agitated by the devastation caused by the Bacchants. This would make the meaning of the fragment akin to TrRF 30 in which a messenger describes the Bacchants destroying the land. Müller suggested that it was indeed Lycurgus uttering these words, but that he was instructing his people to go out and apprehend the Bacchants. Marmorale, on the other hand, argues that these words, perhaps spoken by a messenger, describe the Thracians being agitated by the death of their king or having been converted to the ecstatic rites of Bacchus. Finally, Spaltenstein (2014, p. 506) considers the option that Bacchus or a Bacchant is speaking. Nostros would then signify that the Thracians are in some way under the influence of Bacchus (e.g. conquered or converted).

The fragment as transmitted in the manuscripts confers a sense of movement of people across something to fervere. This might be another reason to favour Bothe’s conjecture, which is essentially about the frantic movement of Thracians or recently converted Bacchants. Like Ribbeck’s conjecture, it has a precedent in Euripides’ Bacchae, namely lines 217-9 spoken by Pentheus: γυναῖκας ἡμῖν δώματ᾿ ἐκλελοιπέναι / πλασταῖσι βακχείαισιν, ἐν δὲ δασκίοις / ὄρεσι θοάζειν (that our women have left their homes / in fabricated Bacchic rites, and that they move quickly in the bushy mountains). The fragment could be part of a monologue of Lycurgus worrying about the suspicious activities of his people, similar to what Pentheus does in the Bacchae. Contrary to all modern editions, this would place the fragment at the beginning of the play, for example nearer to fragment TrRF 30. If Euripides is to be taken as a guide for the reconstruction of Naevius' Lycurgus, then this placing of the fragment provides a solid alternative to those mentioned above.

context.author: Nonius
context.location: De Compendiosa Doctrina per Litteras ad Filium
context.text:
Quoted once by Nonius in De Compendiosa Doctrina per Litteras ad Filium to show the use of fervere with a short vowel:

Ab eo quod est fervit breviato accentu fervere facit, ut spernit spernere… Naevius Lycurgo Late longeque trans nostros fervere. (Non. Li. 808)

lines.text: Late longeque trans nostros fervere

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: deum regnator nocte caeca caelum e conspectu abstulit

doc.translation:
The ruler of the gods, on a blind night, removed the sky from sight.
Or: The ruler of the gods removed the sky from sight in blind night.
doc.differences:
TRF: 146 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/10)

Ribbeck: 32 (Accius' Clytemnestra 3/10)

Warmington: 237 (Accius' Clytemnestra 3/11)

Franchella: 391 (Accius' Clytemnestra 3/10)

D'Antò: 32 (Accius' Clytemnestra 3/10)

Dangel: 291 (Accius' Clytemnestra 1/10)

Warmington:

The monarch of the gods withdrew from view | The sky in blinding night.

Franchella:

Deum regnator nocte caeca caelum e conspectu asbtulit.

Il re degli dèi sottrasse allo sguardo, nella cieca notte, il cielo.

D'Antò:

Deum regnator nocte caeca caelum e conspectu abstulit.

Degli dèi il sovranno nella notte cieca il cielo sottrasse al nostro sguardo.

Dangel: Le souverain des dieux, par une nuit obscure, a dérobé le ciel à nos regards.
doc.apparatus: No textual issues to report.
doc.commentary:
deum regnator: the title has an epic flavour: cf. Naevius Bellum Punicim 66, F 45 FRL, where Neptune is referred to as regnatorem marum, and, of Jupiter, at Verg. Aen. 4.269-70.

nocte caeca: probably to be understood as an ablative of time (‘a period of time within which something happens’, Woodcock § 54: cf. the translations of Franchella, D’Antò, and Dangel), but could also be understood as an ablative of description or quality (cf. Warmington’s ‘in blinding night’). Caeca is used of nox at Ennius Phoenix F 109.4 = 257 Jocelyn, Lucr. 1.1108, Verg. Aen. 2.397, Cic. Mil. 19.
doc.reconstruction:
This fragment is universally attributed to a messenger’s account of the storm that destroyed the Greek fleet on its return from Troy. This is an episode which would probably have been familiar to Accius from poems in the Epic Cycle such as the Sack of Troy (Ilioupersis, cf. the Argument preserved at Procl. Chrest. 237–74 Severyns = I.88-9 Bernabé, PEG = p. 142-7 West, GEF) and Nostoi 'The Homecomings' (cf. Procl. Chrest. 275–303 Severyns = I.94-5 Bernabé, PEG = p. 154-7 West, GEF) and certainly from tragedy: cf. the herald’s account of this storm in Aeschylus’ Agamemnon (Aesch. Ag. 636-80, cf. Warmington II.407). Scholars have also often compared the speech of Eurybates in Seneca’s Agamemnon (Sen. Ag. 421-578, cf. Bothe I.194), and D’Antò 202 further compares Hyg. Fab. 116 (though he does not say whether or not he thinks Accius’ version may have influenced Hyginus’).

At Aesch. Ag. 653, the messenger makes no mention of Zeus, simply stating that the storm began during the night (ἐν νυκτὶ). We do not know whether Accius innovated in having the messenger attribute the responsibility for the storm to Zeus: the responsibility usually lies with Athena (e.g. Od. 3.135-6). This line in Accius may represent the beginning of the storm: if so, then the storm began with total darkness. Aeschylus’ herald does not mention explicitly how the storm began, but points out that it is mainly fire and water that destroyed the fleet (Aesch. Ag. 651-2). In Seneca, the storm begins with a threatening murmur (Ag. 466) before building up to a full catastrophe. In both Aeschylus and Seneca the violent force of the winds is emphasized (Aesch. Ag. 654-7, Sen. Ag. 474-89): in Seneca, this is followed by a spell of total darkness (Ag. 491-6).
doc.metrical_analysis: The TRF edition lists this fragment as a trochaic septenarius. It could however also be an iambic octonarius depending on whether deum forms an iamb or is pronounced in synizesis (Dangel 321, cf. Soubiran 1988, 92). The fact that F 143 TRF (flucti inmisericordes iacere…), which is also from the storm scene, is a trochaic septenarius arguably makes it more likely that this fragment is also a trochaic septenarius.

lines.text: Ubi nec Pelopidarum nomen nec facta aut famam audiam.
... in sedes conlocat se regias;
Clutaemestra iuxtim, tertias natae occupant.
Simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta a socru possedit suo
………..quae mulier una
Vsurpat duplex cubile

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: <13> praeferuidoque fulgore
ardor iniectus Iunonis dextra ingenti incidit

doc.translation: ...and, with a burning flash of lightning, the fire hurled by the powerful right hand of Juno strikes/struck.
doc.differences:
TRF: 428 (frg. inc.)

Ribbeck: 652-653 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 16-17 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 648-649 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 652-653 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 702-703 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

And fire from burning lightning cast by Juno’s great right hand fell upon him [sc. Ajax, son of Oileus?].

Franchella:

...praeferuido fulgure

ardor iniectus Iunonis dextera ingenti incidit.

Il fuoco scagliato dalla potente mano di Giunone cadde con infuocato lampo.

D'Antò:

...fulguri praeferuido

ardor iniectus Iunonis dextera ingenti incidit.

Con folgore ardentissima, il fulmina vibrato dalla potente mano di Giunone s’abbattè’.

Dangel:

Le feu, lancé dans un éclair brûlant par la main toute-puissante de Junon, frappe.
doc.apparatus:
fulguri praeferuido] fulguri praeferuido Ribbeck2-3: praeferuido fulgore codd.: praeferuido | fulgore Bergk: fulgore…praeferuido Ribbeck1: praeferuidoque fulgure Buecheler apud Ribbeck2-3

fulgore] fulgore codd.: fulgure Warmington(?)

dextra] dextra codd.: dextera Buecheler ut hiatus tollatur, iam Scriverius, Bothe

ardor iniectus Iunonis dextra ingenti] ardor iniectus Iunonis dextra ingenti codd.: ardor Iunonis dextra iniectus ingenti Bergk

Iunonis dextra] Iunonis dextra codd.: Iunonis e dextra Ribbeck1: Iunonis dextera Ribbeck2-3

Based on the critical apparatuses of Thilo–Hagen and of the Harvard Servius (I have not inspected the manuscripts of DS myself), all witnesses seem to be in agreement. The paradosis is: praeferuido fulgore ardor iniectus Iunonis dextra ingenti incidit. In his 1620 edition, Scriverius (152), identifying the metre as trochaic and noticing that fulgore cannot stand at line-end because a heavy syllable (-ō-) is not allowed in penultimate position in iambo-trochaic metre, seems to have posited a lacuna between fulgore and ardor so as remove fulgore the final position in the verse. He further seems to have corrected dextra to dextera in order to avoid an unappealing hiatus between ingenti and incidit. Bothe (Accius inc. fab. F 15, I.251) prints the same text as Scriverius, specifying in the margin that he takes the first line as a ‘t.t.’ (i.e. ‘trochaic tetrameter’, cf. Raven §30A) and the second as a ‘t.t.c.’ (i.e. ‘trochaic tetrameter catalectic’, cf. Raven §47A). It is unclear whether Bothe believed the two lines were in the same metre or not.

The text printed by Bergk (1844, 19 n. 5) is praeferuido | fulgore ardor Iunonis dextra iniectus ingenti incidit. The second line, however, does not seem to scan. Ribbeck’s solution in his first edition (Ribbeck1, 188) was to invert the order of praeferuido and fulgore in order to make praeferuĭdo the final word of the line. He does not seem to have known the reading dextera adopted by Scriverius and Bothe, instead printing the manuscripts’ dextra: in order to avoid the hiatus, his own solution is to conjecture e dextra. In his second and third editions (Ribbeck2, 220; Ribbeck3, 254), however, Ribbeck prints his own conjecture fulguri praeferuido, with the dative fulguri presumably to be taken as a dative of ‘end aimed at’ or ‘result achieved’ (Woodcock §67: cf. Baratin apud Dangel 379). fulguri praeferuido translates to ‘le feu lancé pour former un éclair brûlant’ (Baratin) or, as I have rendered it in English, ‘in order to form a burning flash of lightning’. Reading fulgur instead of fulgor seems first to have been suggested by H. Grotius (cf. Peerlkamp 1828, 175).

In his second and third editions, Ribbeck also adopts the reading dextera, a reading which he credits Buecheler with having conjectured, again apparently not realizing that dextera was already the reading found in Scriverius’ and in Bothe’s texts. Ribbeck mentions in the critical apparatus of his second and third editions another conjecture by Buecheler, praeferuidoque fulgure. The reading fulgure, with short -ŭ-, instead of fulgōre is metrically acceptable. praeferuidoque is however less appealing because it would remove the caesura. The TRF edition prints a text similar to that of Buecheler, praeferuidoque fulgore, presenting it as the text found in Klotz and in Franchella, though it misreports the text printed by Franchella, which is actually praeferuidoque fulgŭre, cf. Franchella 459. The reading printed by TRF however does not scan, as they have printed the manuscripts’ fulgōre rather than Buecheler’s metrically acceptable conjecture fulgŭre. The text suggested by Scafoglio (2009, 94) seems much preferable:

…fulgore praeferuido

ardor iniectus Iunonis dextera ingenti incidit.

The only differences between this text and the paradosis is the inversion of the words praeferuido and fulgore and the addition of an -e- in dextera. This text has the merit of keeping the manuscripts fulgōre. Scafoglio proposes two different translations: ‘la fiamma lanciata dalla forte mano di Giunone, con bagliore brucante’ or ‘la saetta lanciata da Giunone con la sua forte mano, in una vampa di luce ardente’. Both of these translations seem to leave incidit untranslated.
doc.commentary:
fulgore: derives, according to Varro (LL 5.70), from the verb fulgēre: cf. the discussion by De Melo, II.708-9. Its literal meaning is ‘brightness, brilliance, radiance’ (OLD s.v. 1), but it is also used of a flame or flash or, more specifically, of a flash of lightning (OLD s.v. 3). Fulgōre is in manuscripts often confused with fulgŭre (cf. N–W I.271), the latter seemingly first having been conjectured in this Accian fragment by Grotius.

ardor: a word apparently first attested in Accius, cf. also his Phoenissae F 358.2 TRF, with language close to that of our fragment: Sol qui micantem candido curru atque equis | flammam citatis feruido ardore explicas…, an adaption of the opening lines of Euripides’ Phoenissae (1-6, esp. 1-3 [Ὦ τὴν ἐν ἄστροις οὐρανοῦ τέμνων ὁδὸν | καὶ χρυσοκολλήτοισιν ἐμβεβὼς δίφροις] | Ἥλιε, θοαῖς ἵπποισιν εἱλίσσων φλόγα… ‘[O you that cut your heavenly path through the stars and ride in a chariot inlaid with gold,] Sun, who on swift steeds whirl your blaze in an arc…’, tr. Kovacs) – Accius seems to have known a text of Euripides containing the first two lines that are now believed to have been interpolated – with ardore seemingly corresponding to φλόγα (cf. TLL II.489.43-5). And OLD s.v. 1, ‘a burning, conflagration, fire’ (with our fragment cited as an example).
doc.reconstruction:
The fragment was first ascribed to Accius’ Clytemnestra by Bergk (1844, III.19 n. 5: ‘nam ad Clytemnestram est referendus [Attii versus], ubi tempestatem illam a Junone concitatam poeta octonariis iambicis descripsit’), who connected it with the three fragments of that play which describe the storm which destroyed the Greek fleet (F 143, 145, 146 TRF).

Bergk’s suggestion is tentatively endorsed by Warmington, who further suggests that the fragment could have been about the death of Ajax son of Oileus, and later by D’Antò 479. Ribbeck however (1875, 463 n. 4) rejects Bergk’s suggestion that this fragment belongs to the Clytemnestra, arguing that it could have belonged to any of the plays from Accius’ Trojan Cycle, comparing Hom. Il. 1.45 and Accius Epinausimache F 197 TRF = 331/332, F XVII Ribbeck3 (lucifera lampade abietem exurat Iouis). Dangel 378 suggests that the fragment belongs to Accius’ Bacchae, believing that it describes the death of Semele at the hands of Juno, an attribution rejected by Scafoglio (2009, 93). Attributing this fragment to the Clytemnestra and pointing out that two other fragments are ascribed explicitly to Accius’ Clytemnestra in Servius Auctus’ commentary on Aeneid 1, Scafoglio (2009, 95-8) believes that in Accius’ play it was not Athena but Juno who wielded a thunderbolt to kill Ajax son of Oileus. On this account, Accius made a significant innovation with respect to the version of the myth found in the Epic Cycle.

context.author: Servius Auctus (DS)
context.text: The fragment is preserved in Servius Auctus’ note on Aeneid 1.42 (I.30.13-14 Thilo–Hagen). Servius Auctus raises the question why Minerva is said by Vergil to use the thunderbolt of Jupiter when, according to Varro in Book 5 of the Divine Antiquities, four different gods, including Minerva, have their own thunderbolts. Servius Auctus further reports that the ancients (antiqui) thought, on the basis of information found in Etruscan books on the interpretation of lightnings (De fulguratura), that the there existed twelve different types of thunderbolts, including those belonging to deities such as Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva (I.30.8-19 Thilo–Hagen). A passage of Accius pertaining to Juno’s thunderbolt is then quoted: nam de Iunonis fulmine Accius ait praeferuido fulgore ardor iniectus Iunonis dextra ingenti incidit.

doc.metrical_analysis: On the metre of this fragment, see the 'Apparatus criticus' section.

lines.text: Egone Argiuum imperium adtingam aut Pelopia digner domo ?
Quo me ostendam ? Quod templum adeam ? Quem ore funesto adloquar ?
Matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Eumenides
doc.translation: unless I avenged my father by shedding my mother's blood
doc.differences:
TrRF 52 (1/4)

Jocelyn 53 (1/4)

Warmington 149 (2/5)

Ribbeck3 134 (2/4)

Vahlen2 147 (3/7)
doc.apparatus:
ulciscerem] ulciscerer Montepess. : ulciscerem cett.

The active form ulcisco is very rare; in the Montepessulanus codex, the fragment appears with the more familiar deponent form ulciscor, which is presumably the result of scribal emendation.
doc.commentary:
sanguine] The image seems to be one of ritual sacrifice. Exsanguination was a key element of Roman animal sacrifice [bib-TYQIGD68-Rüpke-2001-143].

exanclando] exanclare appears in Quintilian's list of archaic words which should be avoided in speeches. When used here with sanguine it must refer to the draining of blood through a mortal wound. exanclare is used at Plaut. Stich. 273 (also cited by Nonius, but with the gloss exhaurire in a rather less grisly context when the parasite Gelasimus describes how the slave boy Pinacium has drained the wine of his master Epignomus to fill wine-cups for Gelasimus.

ulciscerem] A use of an active form ulcisco in place of the usual deponent ulciscor, otherwise unattested. However, there are passive usages in later Latin texts (Sall. Iug. 31.8, Livy 5.49.3) which is probably the vestiges of the non-deponent form, as a deponent cannot have a passive voice.
doc.reconstruction:
Columna [bib-BR8NX8EE-Columna-1590-388] observed a parallel with Aeschylus' Eumenides 463-6:


ἔκτεινα τὴν τεκοῦσαν, οὐκ ἀρνήσομαι,

ἀντικτόνοις ποιναῖσι φιλτάτου πατρός.

καὶ τῶνδε κοινῆι Λοξίας ἐπαίτιος

ἄλγη προφωνῶν ἀντίκεντρα καρδίαι


'I killed my mother, I will not deny it, the penalty in recompense for my beloved father. And Apollo shares in the responsibility for this, telling me beforehand of the pains that would goad my heart'


Many later commentators have pointed to this parallel. Ribbeck [bib-XCEJBREP-Ribbeck-1875-146] points out that Ennius also blends in the line after those Columna quotes, Aesch. Eum. 467: εἰ μή τι τῶνδ’ ἔρξαιμι τοὺς ἐπαιτίους ('unless I exacted this vengeance on those responsible'). The εἰ μή and the nisi are indeed linguistically close. Ennius seems to condense several lines of Aeschylus into a more pointed thought.Columna also offered the words of Electra in the opening of Euripides' Orestes as another parallel to consider:

Φοίβου δ’ ἀδικίαν μὲν τί δεῖ κατηγορεῖν;

πείθει δ’ Ὀρέστην μητέρ’ ἥ σφ’ ἐγείνατο

κτεῖναι, πρὸς οὐχ ἅπαντας εὔκλειαν φέρον·

ὅμως δ’ ἀπέκτειν’ οὐκ ἀπειθήσας θεῶι,


'And must I charge Apollo with injustice? But he persuaded Orestes to kill the mother who bore him, which hardly brought him a good reputation anywhere: yet nevertheless he did not disobey the god and he killed her.'


Given its first person speaker, the fragment clearly refers to Orestes' explanation of his revenge for his father upon his mother, and the nisi suggests Apollo's threats to find ways to compel Orestes.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
exanclare, effundere. Ennius Eumenidibus: nisi patrem materno sanguine exanclando ulciscerem

This citation from Ennius' Eumenides is the only example Nonius gives for his gloss of exanclare with effundere. He also glosses exanclare as exhaurire, perfici and perpeti.

lines.text: nisi patrem materno sanguine exanclando ulciscerem
Quinam Tantalidarum internecioni modus
paretur, aut quaenam umquam ob mortem Myrtili
poenis luendis dabitur satias supplici?
Ibi quid agat secum cogitat pensat putat

Alii ita tradunt : Steropes et Atlantis filios Oenomaum et Maiam fuisse, Oenomai Hippodamiam filiam, unde Atreus natus ; at Maiae filius Mercurius, ex quo Arcades, de quibus Euander, quod Accius in Atreo plenius refert.
Quemnam te esse dicam, qui tarda in senectute ?

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Why is it, I beg, that you forbid [us] to approach you?
doc.differences:
Schauer 22 (adesp.)
Jocelyn 290 (Ennius, Thyestes line 1 of 1/12)
Warmington 361 (Ennius, Thyestes 9/14)
Vahlen2 352 (Ennius, Thyestes line 4 of 8/14)
Ribbeck2/3 306 (Ennius' Thyestes line 4 of 8/11, preceded by Schauer 56)

Schauer:

quidnam est, obsecro, quid te adiri abnutas?

Ribbeck: [ALIUS]: Quidnam est, obsecro? quid te adiri abnutas? (printed as a final line of 4 line dialogue between Thyestes and another, with fragments taken from elsewhere in Cicero [= TrRF adesp. 55 & 56]

Jocelyn: quidnam est obsecro quod te adiri abnutas?

Warmington: Quidnam est obsecro quod te adiri abnutas? (printed as a single line)

Vahlen: [UNUS HOSPITUM]: Quidnam est obsecro quod te adiri abnutas? (printed as final line of 4 line dialogue between Thyestes and a stranger, with fragments taken from elsewhere in Cicero) [= TrRF adesp. 55 & 56]
doc.apparatus:
adiri] abire A2 (see Schauer 2012, 200 for reference) : adirier Vahlen 1854, 143, Ribbeck 1871, 59
Codex A2's abire means "go away", instead of "to be approached" (adiri). Ribbeck prints adirier in his second edition (though not in his third) instead of adiri, following a suggestion by Vahlen (Vahlen 1854, 143; Ribbeck 1871, 59).
doc.commentary: 1 adiri – Vahlen has suggested adirier in his first edition (Vahlen 1854, 143), an archaic passive infinitive. Jocelyn writes that the purpose of Vahlen’s emendation is to “procure a complete bacchiac tetrameter”, but that it is not problematic to retain adiri since “such ‘contracted’ tetrameters commonly occur in the company of full tetrameters” (Jocelyn 1967, 419).
doc.reconstruction: In Ribbeck’s edition, the lines quoted as adesp. 22 and adesp. 56 Schauer form one fragment, which Ribbeck attributes to Ennius’ Thyestes. He considers Thyestes the speaker of adesp. 56, after which someone else responds with adesp. 22. Thyestes would then be telling his interlocutor not to approach him because he considers himself unclean, after which the latter asks why he or she should not approach (Ribbeck 1897, 67). Vahlen prints the fragment in the same way as Ribbeck in both his editions; furthermore, he considers the interlocutor to be one of Thyestes’ guests (Vahlen 1854, 143; Vahlen 1903, 186).

context.author: Cicero
context.text: The fragment is quoted without attribution to a specific play or author in Cicero’s De oratore (3, 164) as an example in a discussion about metaphors. The speaker says that a word used as a metaphor should not be angustius, “narrower”, than the word it refers to. Adesp. 22 is given as an example where this is not the case; in the speaker’s view, its author should have used a stronger term than adiri abnutas. (Cicero, De oratore 3, 164.)

lines.text: quidnam est, obsecro, quid te adiri abnutas?
- × - × - × - × - × melius quam viri
callent mulieres. × - × - × - × - ⏑ ⏓
Video sepulchra duo duorum corporum
Mulier una duum virorum.
atque ut promeruit! pater mihi patriam populavit meam
Celebri gradu gressum adcelerasse †decet

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra
doc.translation: they are suffering long-delayed blows
doc.differences:
TRF: 138 (Accius' Clytemnestra 1/10)

Ribbeck: 40 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/10)

Warmington: 247 (Accius' Clytemnestra 'Unplaced fragments' 2/2)

Franchella: 399 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/10)

D'Antò: 40 (Accius' Clytemnestra 9/10)

Dangel: 297 (Accius' Clytemnestra 7/10)


Warmington:

Regions calm and bright they do possess.

Franchella:

seras potiuntur plagas

Tardi ricevono i colpi.

D'Antò:

…seras potiuntur plagas

...occupano luoghi esposti ad occidente.

Dangel:

ils reçoivent avec retard les coups mortels.
doc.apparatus:
seras] seras Klotz ed. Ven. 1485 falso attribuit(?): siras codex Cuiacianus: syras K: feras V, om. C: serenas Buecheler (uel superas uel astríferas): saeuas Ribbeck

Manuscripts of Donatus’ commentary on Terence which preserve this part of the Adelphoe read either siras (syras K) or feras. The reading feras, omitted by the C manuscript (Oxford, Bodleian Library, Canon. Class. Lat. 95, saec. XV), is found in V (Vaticanus Reginensis lat. 1496, saec. XV, f. 181r). K (Vat.Chig.H.VII.240, saec. XV, f. 60v) and the lost codex Cuiacianus, which represent another branch of the manuscript tradition, have the readings syras and siras respectively. K (the codex Chigianus) was rediscovered by Warren and described in an article published in 1906 and therefore too late for inclusion in Wessner’s Teubner edition, not to mention for Ribbeck’s third edition of the Accian fragment 1897-1898. Its testimony goes unreported in subsequent editions of this Accian fragment, including the most recent TRF edition.

Although the codex Cuiacianus (on which more below) is now lost, some of its readings survive in sixteenth and seventeenth-century printed editions (Wessner I.xv-xvii, Reeve 1979, 324-6). The reading seras (with an e) which is printed by the majority of editors seems solely to derive from R. Klotz’s edition of Donatus’ commentary on Terence. Though Klotz himself prints feras, he reports in his apparatus criticus (1838-40, II.559) that seras is found in the ‘Venice edition’ (it is clear from p. I.v that he means the 1485 Venice edition, ISTC No. it00081600). This seems to be a mistake, as the Venetian 1485 edition, as well as the Calphurnian edition of 1476 (printed in Venice) on which it depends and the Calphurnian edition of 1477 (printed in Treviso), all read not seras but feras. Regardless of where the reading seras originates from, however, it seems clear that it is an orthographical correction of siras, the reading of the codex Cuiacianus (since K was at that time still undiscovered). That seras is the correct reading, a notion seemingly rejected by Zwierlein (1970, 132) who lists siras and syras as corruptions shared by K and codex Cuiacianus, finds support on both palaeographical and literary grounds. On the one hand, seras has the advantage of being the more difficult reading, because the notion that a blow is suffered after a long delay (seras...plagas, i.e. as the fulfilment of a revenge) is more difficult, and also has more point, than the rather colourless description of a blow as ‘savage’ (i.e. feras...plagas). On the other hand, the letters s and f are easily confused in minuscule hand. These two points contribute to explaining why seras was corrupted to feras. In a conjecture reported by Ribbeck, Bücheler suggested emending seras to serenas (see Commentary).

The codex Cuiacianus is so named because it belonged to Jacques Cujas (1522-1590) known as codex Cuiacianus (Wessner I.xv-xvii, Reeve 1979, 324-6). It was identified by Wessner as a very important manuscript: it is a descendant of another lost, but very important, manuscript known as the codex Carnotensis because it was discovered at Chartres. The codex Carnotensis (or Γ) was one of the two known hyparchetypes of Donatus’ commentary and its text was better than any of the surviving witnesses (Wessner 1902, xvi; Reeve 1979, 324-326: cf. Cioffi 2018, 36). This manuscript was collated by one of the Pithoeus brothers (François Pithou or one of his brothers) from Stephanus’ 1541 edition but this collation itself is now lost. Two extant sources preserve readings of the lost codex Cuiacianus, however, because Lindenbrog (1573-1648) used the Pitthoeus brother’s collation for his edition (Paris 1602, Frankfurt 1623) and Gronovius (1611-1671) transcribed either the Pithoeus brother’s collation directly or, more likely according to Reeve, Lindenbrog’s own copy of Stephanus’ 1541 edition which contained the Pithoeus brother’s collation of the codex Cuiacianus. In the margins of his copy of Lindenbrog’s 1623 edition (now in Leiden University Library, shelfmark 759 C16), Gronovius made corrections to the printed text on the basis of the readings he found in the collation of the codex Cuiacianus to which he had access. In the lemma that concerns us (i.e. Ad. 871, p. 324), the printed reading feras is corrected by Gronovius to siras (who also corrects ablatiuo to septimo).

It is worth noting that Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xiii) seems to have suggested printing the fragment over two lines: ...feras | potiuntur plagas.
doc.commentary:
seras…plagas: seras is the reading of the codex Cuiacianus with the orthography which Klotz reports to be that of the Venice edition of 1485 (see 'Fragment apparatus criticus'). It is adopted by the majority of editors. Bücheler’s conjecture serenas (or superas or astriferas) rests on the assumptions (i) that seras, the reading of the codex Cuiacianus, is closer to the truth than feras but is corrupt and (ii) that plagas should in fact be understood as plăga ‘zone, region; the ethereal regions, the air’ and not as plāga ‘blow, stroke’ (see below). It is perhaps noteworthy that at Don. in Ter. Ad. 386 (III.86.9-10 Wessner), Donatus quotes Ennius Iphigenia F 82 TRF caeli scrutantur plagas, where plăgas = ‘zones, regions of the sky’.

Dangel, noting that the emphasis on delay ‘insiste sur l’idée d’une vendetta’ (Dangel 322), compares Aesch. Cho. 583-4 (τὰ δ’ἄλλα τούτῳ δεῦρ’ ἐποπτεῦσαι λέγω | ξιφηφόρους ἀγῶνας ὀρθώσαντί μοι ‘and I charge this one here with looking over the rest and see that all goes right in the contest into which I take my sword’, tr. Sommerstein), where Orestes charges the pillar of Hermes with the task of overseeing his revenge (though the exact nature of the relation between these two lines is to my mind unclear) and 935 (the chorus states that justice came eventually to the house of Priam, ἔμολε μὲν δίκα Πριαμίδαις χρόνῳ).

Stressing the delay with which a vengeance is finally fulfilled is common, though it is often associated with punishment delivered by the gods, the subject of a treatise by Plutarch (De sera numinis uindicta). See Nisbet–Rudd on Hor. Carm. 3.2.31-2, with refs. to Aeschin. 1.190, Tib. 1.9.4 (cf. Aesch. Cho. 383), Eur. F 979.3-4 TrGF, Liv. 3.56.7.

potiuntur: potiri, or poti (Pacuvius F 142 TRF = 158 Schierl) is found with the accusative (as here and usually elsewhere in early Latin, cf. Naev. F 37 TRF, Turp. 146 Ribbeck3, Ter. Adelph. 876, Acc. F 370 TRF), the ablative (the usual construction from Cicero onwards), and the genitive (e.g. Pl. Rud. 1337, Lucr. 2.13, and the expression potior rerum ‘to seize power’). All three constructions are attested in Early Latin onwards (Pinkster I.115; cf. Wackernagel I.68-9). The deponent verb potior usually has the active sense of ‘to obtain’. On some rare occasions, though, it can be used in a passive sense in circumstances where something bad occurs, either followed by an accusative, as here (and at Pl. Rud. 192 de Melo nam hoc mi haud labori est laborem hunc potiri ‘meeting with this suffering is no suffering to me’, Most. 415 ne quid potiatur quam ob rem pigeat uiuere ‘so that he doesn’t come in for anything to make him sorry he was born’, Gell. NA 13.1.5 quae [Elissa] mortem per uim potita est ‘[Dido], who inflicted a violent death upon herself’), or by a genitive (Ter. Phorm. 469 ne quid propter tuam fidem decepta poteretur mali ‘to save her from coming to some harm through her misplaced trust in you’ [cited as a parallel by Venini 1954, 326 n. 1] with Dziatzko–Häuler ad loc.); cf. TLL 329.19-329.40. Dismissing the possibility that potiri is here used in the passive sense of ‘to receive blows’, D’Antò 204 proposes that the meaning of seras potiuntur plagas could be ‘occupano le plaghe occidentali’ (or ‘occupano luoghi esposti ad occidente’, 510), but none of the parallels he adduces is convincing. He compares the meaning of serus in the title of a Menippean Satire by Varro, nescis quid Vesper serus uehat (fr. 333-41 Krenkel), at Sen. Tro. 527 petere…seras domos (where serus is in fact used predicatively [‘long-overdue homecoming’, tr. Fitch], see Löfstedt II.368-70: this actually rather provides an adequate parallel for seras…plagas = ‘delayed blows’), and at Verg. Georg. 1.251 sera rubens accendit lumina Vesper (where sera...lumina actually means ‘evening light’). The title of Varro’s work is preserved at Gell. NA 13.11.1 (where serus is deleted by Holford-Strevens; cf. 1.22.4 where the word does not feature in the quotation of the title of Varro’s work) and at Macr. Sat. 1.7.12 and 2.8.2 (where serus does not feature in the title, which is given as nescis quid uesper uehat). Holford-Strevens (2018, 124) suggests that the addition of serus in Gellius’ text might actually be a ‘scribal memory’ of the Vergilian passage (i.e. Georg. 1.251).
doc.reconstruction:
A number of theories for reconstructing this fragment have been proposed:

(i) Electra commenting on the deserved fate of Clytemnestra and Aegisthus as Orestes and Pylades are in the process of murdering them inside (the palace?) (Bothe I.195).

(ii) A messenger reporting on the disaster suffered by the Greeks at sea, perhaps specifically at Cape Caphareus, on their return from Troy (Hermann apud Ribbeck1, xiii).

(iii) A line spoken by Oeax (Ribbeck 1875, 463).

(iv) Clytemnestra commenting on the fate of Agamemnon and Cassandra (Ribbeck1 299, Argenio 31, Franchella 251, Pociña 120 [although he believes that the speaker could also be Aegisthus or a messenger]).

Ribbeck proposes that this fragment is related to Aesch. Ag. 1374-6 (πῶς γάρ τις ἐχθροῖς ἐχθρὰ πορσύνων, φίλοις | δοκοῦσιν εἶναι, πημονῆς ἀρκύστατ᾿ ἂν | φάρξειεν ὕψος κρεῖσσον ἐκπηδήματος; (‘How else could anyone, pursuing hostilities against enemies who think they are friends, set up their hunting-nets to a height too great to overleap?’, tr. Sommerstein). Franchella suggests that it formed part of the same dialogue as F 139 TRF = v. 41 Ribbeck3 = 247 Warmington = 300 Dangel (matrem ob iure factum incilas, genitorem iniustum adprobas).

(v) Clytemnestra and Aegisthus commenting on how Agamemnon has deserved his punishment, both for sacrificing Iphigenia and on account of his role in the feud between Thyestes and Atreus, the son of Thyestes, Aegisthus, having punished the son of Atreus, Agamemnon (Olivieri 1897, 598).

(vi) A prophetic announcement by Cassandra who foretells that Clytemnestra and Aegisthus will one day be punished for murdering Agamemnon (Venini 1954, 326, although one might remark that in this case a future tense would be more appropriate, cf. e.g. Sen. Ag. 1012 [Cassandra] ueniet et uobis furor). Venini thinks this fragment belonged to the same speech as F 7 TRF = v. 23 Ribbeck3 = 12 Warmington = 310 Dangel cui manus materno sordet sparso sanguine and compares Ennius Incerta F 151.12-13 = Alexander v. 43-4 Jocelyn (iamque mari magno classis cita | texitur. Exitium examen rapit 'Already a swift fleet is being built for the great sea. It hurries along a swarm of deaths'). (vii) Cassandra describing the murder of Agamemnon as it is being carried out within the palace, in a scene similar to Livius Andronicus Aegisthus F 3 and 4 TRF and Sen. Ag. 867-909 (Stackmann 218 n. 60). Stackmann’s hypothesis is rejected by Franchella (251 n. 5) on the grounds that it is inconsistent with F 142 TRF = v. 37 Ribbeck3 = 243 Warmington = 296 Dangel (scibam...), where Cassandra foretells her own death, and with the plural verb in the present fragment (potiuntur), which implies that there at least two victims.

None of these theories can be rejected, although (iii), (vi), and (vii) arguably seem the least plausible. The brevity of the fragment as well as the considerable uncertainty of the text makes it particularly difficult to reconstruct this fragment.

context.author: Donatus
context.text:
Donat. in Ter. Adelph. 871 (p. II.166.25-167.2 Wessner):

patria potitur commoda accusatiuo casu extulit, quod nos septimo casu dicimus, id est patrio potitur commodo. Accius in Clytemestra: ‘seras potiuntur plagas’.

Donatus comments on the fact that Terence uses potior ‘take possession of’ with the accusative (‘mostly ante- and post-class.’ Lewis–Short s.v. Iβ; see Commentary), whereas Donatus himself would have used the ablative. Donatus cites our fragment of Accius’ Clytemnestra because it contains another example of potior with accusative.

lines.text: seras potiuntur plagas

doc.metrical_analysis: Too little survives for a specific metre to be distinguished with any certainty, but it is certainly iambo-trochaic. It could for example be an iambic senarius (reading plāgas) or the final part of an iambic senarius or a trochaic septenarius (reading plăgas). If this line was a trochaic septenarius, then it might have belonged to a messenger speech about the storm, since the metre of the other lines that seem to be about the storm is also the trochaic septenarius (F 146, F 143, F 145 TRF).

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: How much, if your merits are like that, I fear that I am not able to equal them, unless I never forsake doing the good I am able to!
doc.differences:
Schierl: 109 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 23/32)

Ribbeck3: 153-154 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 28/32)

d’Anna: 181-182 (Pacuvius’ Dulorestes 26/31)

ut, si ita sunt promerita vestra, aequiperare ut queam,

vereor, nisi numquam fatiscar facere quod quibo boni.

Ribbeck3: ut ista sunt promerita vestra, aequiperare ut queam,

vereor, nisi numquam fatiscar facere quod quibo boni.
doc.apparatus:
ut si ita] ut si ita codd., Vahlen : at si tanta D’Anna, Lindsay, Warmington : ista si ita Buecheler, Ribbeck2, Klotz : nunc si ita Scioppius : ut ista Ribbeck3 (cf. ut ista Grote)

si numquam] si numquam pler. codd. Non. 478 : Non. LAA 478 : si numquam codd. Non. 768 : sin umquam Müller in Non. 768

quod quibo boni] quod qui boni Non. 478 : quodquod boni codd. Non. 768 : quod quibo boni Iunius.
doc.commentary:
ut, si ita sunt promerita vestra, aequiperare ut queam, vereor] Not being able to return a favour was a topos already in antiquity, vid. e.g. Plautus, Captivi 931-3: fecisti ut tibi / Philocrates, numquam referre gratiam possim satis, / proinde ut tu meritu’s de me et filio, and in Mostellaria 214: numquam ego illi possum gratiam referre ut meritust de me.

ut… queam ] This is interpreted by Schierl as an exclamation, which seems to be the only logical interpretation if we accept the reading of ut, although it seems somewhat strange to separate the two words by means of a subordinate clause. Amphitruo 1103 is the only locus mentioned by Lewis and Short or Forcellini which shares some resemblance with this one: Sed puer ille, quem ego lavi, ut magnu’st et multum valet! But nowhere else to my knowledge are an ut of exclamation and the verb separated by a conditional clause like here. Some editors therefore have suggested emendations, for which see the apparatus criticus.

aequiperare ] In Trinummus 1125-6, Plautus even uses the word aequiperare in this same context: Neque fuit neque erit neque esse quemquam hominem in terra arbitror, / quoi fides fidelitasque amicum erga aequiperet tuam.

vereor ] Usually, vereor ut means the same as vereor ne (unlike the difference between timere/metuere ne, ‘to fear that’, and timere/metuere ut/ne non, ‘to fear that not’), namely, ‘to fear that’, but here of course it must mean ‘to fear that not’, like in Terentius’ Andria 705-6: dies mi ut sati’ sit vereor / ad agendum, and Hecyra 101: habet, sed firmae haec vereor ut sint nuptiae.

quibo ] The form quibo occurs only four times in Latin literature before 200 AD; the other three loci are Plautus’ Miles Gloriosus vs. 1240 and two in De litteris, de syllabis, de metris by Terentianus Maurus.
doc.reconstruction: These lines could have been uttered by Orestes, as Warmington, D’Anna and Mette think, or perhaps by Electra, as Ribbeck and Argenio argue. However, it seems clear that they were spoken to people who had helped carry out the plan to kill Aegisthus and Clytaemnestra, so if it is Orestes speaking, the people addressed must be Electra and Pylades, if it is Electra speaking, Orestes and Pylades are addressed.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Quoted twice by Nonius:

First to explain the meaning of the verb fatiscere:

fatiscere est aperire. Rursum deficere. Accius Epinausimache: tamen haud fatiscar quin tuam implorem fidem. Pacuvius Duloreste: ut, si ita sunt promerita vestra, aequiperare ut queam, vereor, nisi numquam fatiscar facere quod quibo boni. (Non. 478 L. (307,9 M.))

Then to show that fatiscere is sometimes used as a deponent:

fatiscuntur pro fatiscunt. Accius Epinausimache: tamen haud fatiscar quin tuam implorem fidem. Varro, Antiquitate humana lib. XIV: altera ita, altera vulneribus fatiscuntur. Pacuvius Duloreste: vereor, nisi numquam fatiscar facere quod quibo boni. (Non. 768 L. (479,10 M.))

lines.text: ⟨ -×⟩ ut, si ita sunt promerita vestra, aequiperare ut queam,
vereor, nisi numquam fatiscar facere quod quibo boni

doc.author: Accius
doc.title: Clytemnestra

lines.text: omnes gaudent facere recte, mali pigrent

doc.translation: Everyone is happy to act rightly: bad people are lazy/reluctant to do so.
doc.differences:
TRF: 141 (Accius' Clytemnestra 4/10)

Ribbeck2-3: 30/31 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/10)

Ribbeck1: 30-31 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/10)

Warmington: 246 (Accius' Clytemnestra 'Unplaced fragments' 1/2)

Franchella: 390 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/10)

D'Antó: 31 (Accius' Clytemnestra 2/10)

Dangel: 301 (Accius' Clytemnestra 10/10)

Warmington:

All men take joy in doing right, the base | Are slack in doing it.

Franchella: omnes gaudent facere recte, male pigrent

Tutti amano agire bene, indugiano ⟨ad agire⟩ malo.

D'Antò: omnes gaudent facere recte, mali pigrent ⏑-⏑⏓

Tutti godono nell’agire rettamente, i malvagi sono restii.

Dangel: Tous se réjouissent d’agir droitement ; mais les méchants rechignent.
doc.apparatus:
omnes] omnes codd.: ⟨boni⟩ uel ⟨strenui⟩ omnes Vossius: omnes ⟨boni⟩ Ribbeck1: omnes ⟨se⟩ Ribbeck2

facere recte] facere recte codd.: ualere recte Ribbeck1: facere recte recti Grotius

omnes gaudent facere recte] o. g. f. r. codd.: ⟨boni⟩ o. g. f. r. ⟨se⟩ Onions

mali] mali corr. ed. princ.: male codd.: ⟨sed⟩ mali Ribbeck1: nam mali Grotius

mali pigrent] mali pigrent codd.: male pigrant Bothe: ⟨non⟩ mali, ⟨mali⟩ pigrent Hermann


All our manuscripts of Nonius bear witness to the same text: omnes gaudent facere recte male pigrent. Male was corrected to mali in the editio princeps of Book 3 of Nonius (Paris, J. Marchant, 1511: on this edition, see Lunelli 1986). The paradosis does not scan as a single line, which has prompted attempts by numerous editors to turn the fragment into a full line.

In his first edition, Ribbeck printed the fragment over two lines, as iambic senarii thus:

omnes ⟨boni⟩

Gaudent ualere recte ⟨set⟩ mali pigrent.

His proposition won no approval and he later changed his mind.

Two not unattractive suggestions are that of Hermann omnes gaudent facere recte non mali, mali pigrent (apud Ribbeck1, xiii) and that of Onions boni omnes gaudent facere recte se, mali pigrent, which draws on earlier suggestions by Vossius (who suggested supplying boni before omnes) and Ribbeck (who suggested supplying se, but who placed it after omnes rather than after recte, like Onions does).

Most editors now print the paradosis as an incomplete trochaic septenarius, lacking either a foot-and-a-half at the beginning of the line (Warmington) or at the end (TRF, Dangel).
doc.commentary: omnes...mali: in this sententia, a curious distinction is made between the behaviour of ‘everyone’ (omnes) and that of a minority of ‘bad people’ (mali). What distinguishes the latter group from the rest seems to be that, although they have the intention to act well just like everyone else, they are too lazy to carry through on their good intention. The point of marking an antithesis between ‘everyone’ and ‘bad people’ is however not entirely clear (Ribbeck’s conjecture of ⟨non⟩ omnes in preface to second edition, p. l, is designed to solve this issue, as is Onions’ boni: in the former case, the point would then be that not everyone is happy to act rightly since there are bad people who are too lazy to do so; in the latter, a neat contrast would be marked between the behaviour of ‘good people’ and that of ‘bad people’).
doc.reconstruction:
Hermann (apud Ribbeck1, xiii) suggests that the speaker might be Electra criticizing her mother for her crime during their heated encounter. Franchella 248 imagines that this line was pronounced during the prologue which contained a banquet scene. Ribbeck1, 299 suggests numerous Greek parallels: Aesch. Ag. 501-2 (Chorus): χὤστις τάδ᾿ ἄλλως τῇδ᾿ ἐπεύχεται πόλει, | αὐτὸς φρενῶν καρποῖτο τὴν ἁμαρτίαν (‘and whoever expresses his prayer for this city differently, may he himself reap the fruit of his mind’s perversity!’, tr. Sommerstein); 580 (Herald): τοιαῦτα χρὴ κλυόντας εὐλογεῖν πόλιν (‘Having heard such a tale as I have told, you should praise the city…’, tr. Sommerstein); and 807-9 (Chorus to Agamemnon): γνώσῃ δὲ χρόνῳ διαπευθόμενος | τόν τε δικαίως καὶ τὸν ἀκαίρως | πόλιν οἰκουροῦντα πολιτῶν (‘In time you will know by inquiry which of the citizens has acted honestly when staying at home in the city, and which inappropriately’, tr. Sommerstein) a passage which is vaguely similar to the fragment of Accius preserved at Cic. Off. 3.84 = Accius inc. fab. F 413 TRF: multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli, though the Accian fragment is of gnomic value and the Aeschylean passage is not. Scafoglio (2010, 171) speculates that the speaker could be Clytemnestra, presenting Aegisthus with the argument that murdering Agamemnon is a rightful deed (recte factum), or Aegisthus trying to convince Clytemnestra to go through with the murder by arguing that it is legitimate to take revenge, but generally scholars have found it impossible to attribute this fragment to any particular speaker or to suggest where it might be placed within the drama (‘sfugge ad ogni tentativo di collocazione’, Venini 1954, 328; ‘...est une sentence impossible à situer’, Dangel 323).

It might however be possible to say a little more by trying to establish the position of this fragment in the Clytemnestra in relation to some of the other fragments. This fragment is transmitted in Book III of Nonius, De indistinctis generibus (‘On nouns with indistinct genders’), under the lemma pigret, which stands alongside pigritia and pigror. As a verb, however, pigret is out of place in a book on nouns and therefore it is believed that Nonius included it in Book III by mistake. Quicherat (1872, 10-11) notes that the lemma in fact belongs to one of the first two books and suggests that it might have been intended to stand alongside pigrare (p. 153.31-154.1 M = 225.33-7/1 L) in Book II. If this suggestion is correct, then, by applying the lex Lindsay, we can deduce that this fragment appeared in the play before the two other fragments of Accius’ Clytemnestra transmitted in Book II of Nonius, i.e. F 139 TRF = v. 41 Ribbeck3 = 247 Warmington = 300 Dangel (matrem ob iure factum...) and F 140 TRF = v. 38-9 Ribbeck3 = 235-6 Warmington = 298-9 Dangel (ut quae tum absentem...). We might thus tentatively suggest that this sententia came towards the beginning of the play: if not in the prologue, then at least before or possibly during an altercation between Electra and Clytemnestra (F 139).

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius, p. 219.12-14 Mercier = 324.14-17 Lindsay:

pigret. Ennius lib. XVI: post aetate pigret subferre laborem. Accius Clytaemestra: omnes gaudent facere recte, male pigrent.

Nonius quotes this line in his entrance under pigret, third person singular of the apparently impersonal verb pigreo, attested only here and at Enn. Ann. 401 Skutsch (TLL X.1.2128.7–2128.33). The verb is usually first conjugation, as attested in Accius himself (pigrasse, Chrysippus F 135 TRF; pigrem, Epigoni F 170 TRF). As a deponent, cf. Cic. Att. 14.1.2 scribere ne pigrer and Lucr. 1.411 quod si pigraris, paulumue recesseris ab re, ‘should you be sluggish or draw back a little from the task’, tr. Rouse–Smith), for pigraueris.

doc.metrical_analysis: The TRF lists this fragment as a trochaic septenarius, as does Dangel, who further specifies that the final part of the line is missing (×-⏑×; cf. also Grotius (cf. Peerlkamp 1828, 170), Bothe I.194, D’Antò 69). Franchella 243 suggests that if a monosyllabic word were placed before omnes (e.g. sed) the line would form an iambic senarius: ⟨sed⟩ omnes gaudent facere recte, male pigrent: Ribbeck’s conjecture ⟨non⟩ would achieve the same effect. With Onions’ conjecture (⟨boni⟩ omnes gaudent facere recte ⟨se⟩, mali pigrent), I am not sure that the line scans.

doc.author: Pacuvius
doc.title: Dulorestes
doc.translation: If my suspicion does not deceive me, this is what Oeax covertly foretold that will happen
doc.differences:
Schierl: 105 (Pacuvius' Dulorestes 18/32)

D' Anna: 179-180 (Pacuvius' Dulorestes 25/31)

Ribbeck2 137-138 (Pacuvius' Dulorestes 17/32)

Ribbeck3 137-138 (Pacuvius' Dulorestes 17/32)

Warmington: 148-149 (Pacuvius' Dulorestes 22/34)

Schierl: 

me calvitur suspicio,

hoc est illud quod fore occulte Oeax praedixit

Ribbeck2: 

[nisi] me calvitur suspicio,

Hoc est illud quod fore occulte Oeax praedixit

Warmington: 

me calvitur suspicio?

Hoc est illud quod fore occulte Oeax praedixit

All the editors place this fragment around the middle, towards the end of the play. Schierl believes that it belongs to the part of the play where the plan of Orestes is being executed. D' Anna, Ribbeck and Warmington seem to be of the same opinion.
doc.apparatus:
1. ni me] L, Muller, Onions, Klotz : me codd. : ni Hermann : nisi me Bothe

2. quod] codd. : om. V : corr. V1
doc.commentary:
ni] has probably the meaning of nisi, or nisi itself could be added at the beginning of the verse.

occulte] indicates the manner in which the prediction (praedixit) was given.
doc.reconstruction:
Oeax seems to have predicted that something would happen. The prediction is likely to be about Orestes. Since Oeax is usually presented as favorably disposed towards Clytemnestra and Aegisthus, scholars suppose that one of them should have been the speaker.

This fragment might be connected with fr. 111. The prediction of Oeax could be an interpretation of the oracle possibly indicated by the word responsa of that fragment.

Warmington attributes these words to Aegisthus, who starts to realize that the oracle about Orestes, which Oeax interpreted, is being confirmed.

Bibliography

D’Anna, I. (1967). M. Pacuvii Fragmenta. Rome.

Ribbeck, O. (1897). Scaenicae Romanorum Poesis Fragmenta. Leipzig.

Schierl, P. (2006). Die Tragödien des Pacuvius. Berlin; New York.

Warmington, E. (1936). Remains of Old Latin. Cambridge.

context.author: Nonius
context.text:
Nonius Marcellus quotes these verses from Pacuvius' Dulorestes discussing the meaning of the word calvitur.

Calvitur dictum est frustratur: tractum a calvis mimicis, quod sint omnibus frustratui.

Plautus...Pacuvius Medo...et...Accius...Lucilius...Pacuvius Duloreste:

me calvitur suspicio,

hoc est illud quod fore occulte Oeax praedixit

Salustius...

lines.text: ⟨ni⟩ me calvitur suspicio,
hoc est illud quod fore occulte Oeax praedixit
<10>nam ut Pergama
Accensa et praeda per participes aequiter
Partita est
Heu!
Cuiatis stirpem funditus fligi studet?
.  .  ubi nec Pelopidarum facta neque famam audiam.
Vnde estis, nautae, huc hieme delati? x –

Varr. L. L. 5.98: aries quod eum dicebant ares (ἀρήν Spengel) ueteres, nostri aruiga, hinc ariugas (a F aruigas codd. aruignus B ariugus Kent). Haec sunt quorum in sacruficiis exta in olla, non in ueru coquuntur, quas et Accius scribit et in Pontificiis libris uidemus.

lines.text: multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci benivoli

doc.translation: Many people are hostile and unfaithful to royal authority, few are favourable to it.
doc.differences:

Ribbeck: 651 (frg. inc.)

Warmington: 18 (frg. inc.)

Franchella: 647 (frg. inc.)

D'Antò: 651 (frg. inc.)

Dangel: 692 (frg. inc.)

Warmington:

Many there are unfriendly and unfaithful Unto a kingdom; few who wish it well.

Franchella:

multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci beneuoli

Molti sono ingiusti e sleali verso il re, pochi benevoli.

D'Antò:

multi iniqui atque infideles regno, pauci benivoli.

Molti sono ingiusti e non fiduciosi verso il regno, pochi benevoli.

doc.apparatus:
beniuoli] beniuoli ξ (= BPV): boni ζ (= Mc): beneuoli Stuerenburg

pauci beneuoli] pauci beneuoli: p. b. sunt del. Stuerenburg: boni sunt Stephanus 1564, 41, Delrius 1593, I.151: sunt boni Scriverius 152, Bothe ad Acc. inc. fab. fr. 16, I.186

The TRF edition follows Winterbottom (1994, 144) in rejecting Stuerenburg’s correction beneuoli (18432, 170-1) and in printing the manuscripts’ beniuoli. In a treatise on orthography which was transmitted under the name ‘Albinus’, the name used by Alcuin of York, Alcuin explains (VII.298.14-16 GLK) that although beniuolus derives from the adverb bene, it is written with an -i- and not an -e-. Both variants are attested in epigraphic evidence and in medieval manuscripts (cf. TLL II.1897.34-7).

For an account of the manuscript tradition of Cicero's De Officiis, see Atzert 19634, v-xlvi; Fedeli 1973; and Shackleton Bailey 1993 (and more briefly, with a list of sigla, 1994, v-xiii,).
doc.commentary: beniuoli: in Republican drama, beniuolus/beneuolus ‘kind, benevolent’ seems most often to be used to describe relationships of a personal nature (cf. Plaut. Asin. 66, of a son’s attitude to his father; Ter. Hec. 761, the senex Laches to the meretrix Bacchis). Cicero describes the behaviour of one of his friends’ freedmen, who has been faithful and kind to him, as beneuolis (Fam. 13.21.2); cf. too Mil. 58 beneuolis…seruis. To use the term to describe a subject’s attitude to a kingdom may have been perceived as somewhat unusual.
doc.reconstruction: Ribbeck compares Aesch. Ag. 807-9 (chorus): γνώσῃ δὲ χρόνῳ διαπευθόμενος | τόν τε δικαίως καὶ τὸν ἀκαίρως | πόλιν οἰκουροῦντα πολιτῶν ‘In time you will know by inquiry which of the citizens has acted honestly when staying at home in the city, and which inappropriately’ (tr. Sommerstein). Ribbeck suspects this fragment might either have belonged to Accius’ Atreus, to Pelopidae, or to Clytemnestra (cf. Warmington II.571). Dangel 378 believes that this fragment was from Atreus and is spoken with reference to the eponymous tyrant. Scafoglio (2010, 174) points out that it cannot be ruled out that this fragment could also belong to Accius’ Aegisthus. He suggests that the speaker could be Aegisthus speaking about Agamemnon or Orestes talking about Aegisthus.

context.author: Cicero
context.text: The Accian fragment is preserved in a passage of Book 3 of Cicero’s De Officiis (3.82) in which Cicero is discussing the ideal of the uir bonus, ‘good man’. He presents Pompey and Caesar, without naming them, as men who fell short of attaining that ideal by their disregard for what is morally right in order to obtain power. Cicero translates into Latin a passage of Euripides’ Phoenissae, 524-5, in which tyranny is justified. It is a passage which Caesar apparently often repeated (cf. Suet. Iul. 30.5, cf. Cic. Ex Euripide conuersi loci fr. II Soubiran). Cicero goes on to make the point (3.83) that although the majority of people might consider being a king to be advantageous, in truth there is nothing more disadvantageous, on account of the constant fear and anxiety a king experiences at potentially being the victim of plotting and conspiracy. The Accian quotation is cited to illustrate this point (3.84), and Cicero contrasts the king referred to in the poetic fragment who, as a descendant of Tantalus and Pelops was the rightful heir to the throne, with Caesar, an illegitimate king who with his army seized control of Rome and thus enslaved a state which had been free and powerful.

doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarius (tr7).

lines.text: <7> simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta a socru
possedit suo

doc.translation: At the very moment he seized the prize of Pisa, snatched from his own father-in-law.
