doc.apparatus:
1: simul et ] simul et codd.: simul ut Müller 1890, 24 dubitanter

1: arrepta ] arrepta in mg. r: erepta RDGLK

1: socru ] socru H2: socro G: uel u superscr. super socro G2: socro BLK: socr*(o?) H: soceru Müller 1890, 24

1-2: simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta… ] simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta… codd.: simul et praemia | Pisaea arrepta… Vossius (apud Scriverium 1620, 141)

Welcker (1839-1841, I.363) prints the fragment as follows: simul et Pisaea praemia erepta arrepta a socro possedit suo. L. Müller (1890, 24) prints it as: simul et praemia | Pisaea arrepta a soceru possedit suo, replacing socru with soceru on the grounds that he deems it unlikely that Accius wrote a pyrrhic. Klotz prints the better-attested manuscript variant erepta, a reading defended by Lana (1959, 361). The reading arrepta is included by another hand in the margin of the manuscript R (= Paris, Bibliothèque nationale de France, Lat. 7496, f. 54r). Lana argues that if arrepta were to be read, then a…socru would be the agent of the participle and the sense would be ‘snatched, grabbed, by her father-in-law’, which is not what is wanted. He also points out that, while arripio is unparalleled in Accius, eripio is a verb he also uses at Deiphobus F 152 TRF (nos continuo ferrum eripimus, [omnibus] manibus manicas neximus), Nyctegresia F 307 TRF (aut ego illum eripiam aut illi poenas sufferam), and Telephus F 389 TRF (<u –> nam si a me regnum Fortuna atque opes | eripere quivit, at virtutem nec quiit).
doc.commentary:
1: simul: Pelops and Hippodamia, the newly-wed couple (Lana 1959, 295).

1: Pisaea praemia: commonly understood as referring to Hippodamia. Baldarelli (2004, 128-9), however, argues that, if we read arrepta and not erepta, then a socru can refer to Oenomaus as ablative of agent, and that Pisaea praemia functions as a direct object of arrepta, denoting the power and territory that Pelops took over by force from Oenomaus, who himself had previously acquired it by force. She defends this interpretation by adducing Prop. 1.8.33-6 illa uel angusto mecum requiescere lecto | et quocumque modo maluit esse mea, | quam sibi dotatae regnum uetus Hippodamiae | et quas Elis opes apta pararat equis, ‘she has chosen to sleep with me even on a narrow bed, and to be mine in any way at all, rather than to have the ancient kingdom that was Hippodamia’s dowry and the wealth that Elis, fit for horses, long ago acquired’ (transl. Heyworth 2007b, 522), presenting Oenomaus as an originally poor king who supplied his daughter with a rich dowry thanks to the spoils won in chariot races. The language is compared by Baldarelli (2004, 127-8) to Sen. Thy. 122-4 Argos si superis si quis Achaicum | Pisaeasque domos curribus inclitas, | Isthmi si quis amat regna Corinthii…, ‘If any god loves Achaean Argos, or the Pisan district famed for chariots; or loves the realm of Corinth’s Isthmus…’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.242-3). For more on this parallel, see ‘Fragment reconstruction’. Baldarelli also identifies another possible allusion to this fragment in Seneca’s Thyestes, in the messenger’s description of the Atrid palace: praemia arrepta ~ Sen. Thy. 659-654 affixa inhaerent dona: uocales tubae | fractique currus, spolia Myrtoi maris, | uictaeque falsis axibus pendent rotae | et omne gentis facinus; hoc Phrygius loco | fixus tiaras Pelopis, hic praeda hostium | et de triumpho picta barbarico chlamys, ‘Here votive gifts are fastened: hanging up are bruiting trumpets and wrecked chariots, spoils from the Myrtoan Sea, wheels defeated because of rigged axles, and all the exploits of the clan. In this place is pinned the Phrygian cap of Pelops, here are spoils from his enemies and an embroidered cape from his triumph over barbarians’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.289).

1-2: a socru…suo: Oenomaus, Pelops’ father-in-law and rival in the chariot race. One of only two occurrences of the fourth-declension socrus, -us ‘father/mother-in-law’, rarer than the second-declension socer, in Republican drama, the other being, probably not by accident, also referring to Oenomaus, F Adesp. 55 TRF (Tantalo prognatus, Pelope natus, qui quondam a socru | Oenomao rege Hippodamiam raptis nanctus nuptiis = Cic. Tusc. 3.26). While it should be noted that socru is a conjecture for transmitted socero by Bentley (1709, 48-9), socru is the more attractive reading, both for metrical reasons (with socru the lines scan as two trochaic septenarii) and because, in accordance with the principle of lectio facilior, it is more plausible that the rarer form socru became corrupted to socero than the other way around.

2 possedit: cf. perhaps Eur. IT 825 ἐκτήσαθ᾿ Ἱπποδάμειαν (Pelops seizing Hippodameia). The primary sense of possidere relates to the possession of land (TLL X.2.113.12-115.3 ‘praevalente respectu iuris’; OLD s.v. 1 ‘to have (land) in one’s control’,), and Dangel suggests that a legalistic sense of the verb may be active, and perhaps that abusive appropriation might be implied: compare, for example, Cic. Dom. 107 tamen illo ipso in furore suo non est ausus meam domum, cuius cupiditate inflammatus erat, possidere (‘he [P. Clodius Pulcher] did not, however, despite in his state of madness, dare to take possession of my house, which he ardently desired’), which seems to imply illicit appropriation of property. For praemia possidere, cf. Sall. Cat. 52.22 omnia uirtutis praemia ambitio possidet, ‘ambition appropriates all the prizes of virtue’, although praemia there denotes an abstract entity.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck (1875, 448) believes that these lines about how Pelops won his victory against King Oenomaus in the chariot race at Pisa were sung in a canticum. He conjectures that this canticum directly followed the prologue, in which the genealogy of the Pelopids was expounded (= F I Ribbeck = T 25 TRF), and thus lists it as F II. It is noteworthy that the chariot race between Pelops and Oenomaus was also mentioned in the first choral ode of Seneca’s Thyestes (139-43): see ‘Fragment commentary’ and further below in this section. Ribbeck further suggests that these lines may enclose a reference to Accius’ own version of the race in his tragedy Oenomaus. Lana (1959, 294-5) interprets these lines as belonging to the prologue, which consisted of an account of the origin of the conflict between Atreus and Thyestes (‘l’antefatto dei rapporti tra i fratelli Atreo e Tieste, causa immediata delle sceleratezze che sono argomento della tragedia’).

﻿ Dangel (1995, 283) argues that this fragment should be assigned not the prologue, but rather to the end of the tragedy. She detects allusions to several accounts of Pelops’ chariot race and subsequent act of violence in Greek tragedy: Soph. El. 507-515 [chorus] εὖτε γὰρ ὁ ποντισθεὶς | Μυρτίλος ἐκοιμάθη, | παγχρύσων δίφρων | δυστάνοις αἰκείαις | πρόρριζος ἐκριφθείς, | οὔ τί πω | ἔλιπεν ἐκ τοῦδ᾿ οἴκου | πολύπονος αἰκεία, ‘For since Myrtilus fell asleep, plunged into the sea, hurled headlong from the golden chariot with cruel torment, never yet has the torment of many troubles departed from this house’ (transl. Lloyd-Jones 1994-1996, I.213); Eur. IT 795-799 (this reference seems to be erroneous: perhaps she means 823-6, where the spear of Pelops serves as a token thanks to which Electra recognises her brother Orestes?); Eur. Or. 979-1001 (Electra presents Pelops’ murder of Myrtilus as the act because of which her family is cursed); El. 723-749 (though this passage is not about Myrtilus, but rather about Zeus changing the sun’s course after Atreus’ proclamation that he has discovered a golden lamb among his flock). There seems however to be no evidence to support the claim that these lines are connected, in Accius’ play, to an account of the curse of the house of Pelops.

Baldarelli (2004, 127-8) perceives a parallel between this Accian fragment and the first choral ode of Seneca’s Thyestes (see ‘Fragment commentary’; esp. Pisaea praemia ~ Sen. Thy. 123 Pisaeasque domos; a socru | possedit suo ~ Sen. Thy. 139-40 proditus occidit | deceptor domini Myrtilus, et fide | uectus qua tulerat nobile reddidit | mutato pelagus nomine, ‘Myrtilus fell betrayed, his master’s betrayer; conveyed as disloyally as he conveyed others, he made the sea renowned through its changed name’ (transl. Fitch 20182, II.243)). In particular, she identifies a similarity in verbal tense between Accius’ possedit and Seneca’s occidit and reddidit; notes the absence of the name of Oenomaus in both passages (though he could of course have been mentioned in the surrounding lines of the Accian passage); and between the description of Myrtilus as dominus in Seneca and of Pelops as socer in Accius. On this basis, she argues that Seneca is imitating Accius so closely as to allude, in the first choral ode of his Thyestes, to the first choral ode of Accius’ Atreus, in which the chorus focuses specifically on the crime of Pelops following the account of Pelopid genealogy in the prologue. She thus believes, like Lana (see above), that this fragment belonged to the prologue. She further argues that Accius might have presented Pelops as a Phrygian-born usurper of the throne of the Greek ‘shepherd-king’ (Hirtenkönig) Oenomaus (p. 132-3). Pelops thus commits a double offence against the god Mercury: first causing the death of Mercury’s uncle Oenomaus (according to the genealogy adopted by Accius, cf. T 25 TRF), and then killing his son Myrtilus.

context.author: Priscian
context.text:
Priscian, Institutio de arte grammatica 6.44, II.233.9-10 GLK:

Praeterea notandum ‘socer soceri’, cuius femininum cum secundum analogiam ‘haec socera’ esse deberet, ‘haec socrus’ in usu est. Iuvenalis in II:

Desperanda tibi salva concordia socru (= Juv. 6.231)

vetustissimi tamen communiter ‘hic’ et ‘haec socrus’ proferebant. Accius in Atreo:

Simul et Pisaea praemia arrepta a socru

…possedit suo (= Accius Atreus F 112 TRF)

This Accian fragment is quoted in Book 6 of Priscian’s Institutes of Grammar, which is entitled ‘De nominativo et genetivo casu’ (‘On the nominative and the genitive case’). In this section, Priscian discusses the conjugation of the second-declension noun socer (‘father-in-law’). He explains that the word for ‘mother-in-law’ should, according to the principle of analogy, be the first-declension feminine noun socera (which is attested on inscriptions, e.g. CIL 11.405, 6410), but the word in common use is in fact the fourth-declension form socrus. He quotes a line from Juvenal’s sixth Satire as an example of the form socrus used as a feminine noun to denote a mother-in-law. Priscian goes on to note that in very ancient literature (uetustissimi), the form socrus could be found both as a masculine and as a feminine noun, quoting as an example this fragment from Accius’ Atreus which contains the the fourth-declension ablative singular form socru. The more widespread manuscript reading socro was almost certainly introduced by mistake on account of it being the more common form (see ‘Apparatus criticus’).

doc.metrical_analysis: The fragment is printed by Ribbeck in his first and third editions as two truncated iambic senarii, but in his second edition he presented it as a single iambic octonarius (cf. ‘Conspectus metrorum’, Ribbeck2, p. 367, where the line number is given simply as ‘196’, cf. the apparatus criticus on p. 161, which implies that the line number 197 has disappeared in the second edition). But, as L. Müller (1890, 24) pointed out, the fragment as printed in Ribbeck’s second edition does not scan, because socru cannot scan as a pyrrhic, i.e. two light syllables, because the final u of socru is normally long (‘ita Ribbeckius numeris nullis, nam socru ab Accio pyrrichiace elatum nego’). Müller’s explanation seems to have been what influenced Ribbeck’s decision, in his third edition, to revert to the text of the fragment he had printed in his first edition. Klotz, however, defended the interpretation of the fragment as an iambic octonarius, arguing that the first syllable of erepta could be scanned as light on account of iambic shortening (‘in correptione iambica hoc loco versus nil offensionis’, p. 222), which would in turn allow the fifth foot to be scanned as a spondee (sōcrū). Lana (1959, 361), however, argues that the interpretation of the fragment as two iambic senarii should be preferred for two reasons: (i) it is plausible that the fragment belongs to the prologue, for which the iambic senarius would be a natural type of metre; and (ii) understanding the metre as the iambic senarius has the advantage of not needing to posit iambic shorting in erepta. Dangel (1995, 283) suggests that the fragment could scan as a trochaic septenarius, with a cretic (- ⏑ -) missing at the beginning of the line. Baldarelli (2004, 128), in line with her argument that this fragment belongs to the first choral ode, argues that, whether the fragment is reconstructed as a trochaic septenarius (Dangel) or as an iambic octonarius (Ribbeck2, Klotz), both of these metres are acceptable in the cantica of Roman Republican drama.

lines.text: Eho tu, di, quibus est potestas motus superum atque inferum,
Pacem enim inter sese conciliant, conferunt concordiam. (attributed to Enn. Cresphontes.)
Sin fracebunt condiciones, repudiato et reddito.
[THYESTES]: .  .  .  uigilandum est semper: multae insidiae sunt bonis.
Id quod multi inuideant multique expetant inscitiast
Postulare, nisi laborem summa cum cura ecferas.
Tum autem lascivum Nerei simum pecus
ludens ad cantum classem lustratur

beniuoli ξ (= BPV): boni ζ (= Mc): beneuoli Stuerenburg 18432, 170-1

pauci beneuoli: p. b. sunt del. Stuerenburg 18432, 171: boni sunt Stephanus 41, Delrius I.151: sunt boni Scriverius 152, Bothe ad Acc. inc. fab. fr. 16, I.186

The TRF edition follows Winterbottom (1994, 144) in rejecting Stuerenburg’s correction beneuoli (18432, 170-1) and in printing the manuscripts’ beniuoli. In a treatise on orthography which was transmitted under the name ‘Albinus’, the name used by Alcuin of York, Alcuin explains (VII.298.14-16 GLK) that although beniuolus derives from the adverb bene, it is written with an -i- and not an -e-. Both variants are attested in epigraphic evidence and in medieval manuscripts (cf. TLL II.1897.34-7).
doc.commentary: beniuoli: in Republican drama, beniuolus/beneuolus ‘kind, benevolent’ seems most often to be used to describe relationships of a personal nature (cf. Plaut. Asin. 66, of a son’s attitude to his father; Ter. Hec. 761, the senex Laches to the meretrix Bacchus). Cicero describes the behaviour of one of his friends’ freedmen, who has been faithful and kind to him, as beneuolis (Fam. 13.21.2); cf. too Mil. 58 beneuolis…seruis. To use the term to describe a subject’s attitude to a kingdom may have been perceived as somewhat unusual.
doc.reconstruction:
Ribbeck compares Aesch. Ag. 807-9 (chorus): γνώσῃ δὲ χρόνῳ διαπευθόμενος | τόν τε δικαίως καὶ τὸν ἀκαίρως | πόλιν οἰκουροῦντα πολιτῶν ‘In time you will know by inquiry which of the citizens has acted honestly when staying at home in the city, and which inappropriately’ (tr. Sommerstein). Ribbeck suspects this fragment might either have belonged to Accius’ Atreus, to Pelopidae, or to Clytemnestra (cf. Warmington II.571). Dangel 378 believes that this fragment was from Atreus and is spoken with reference to the eponymous tyrant. Scafoglio (2010, 174) points out that it cannot be ruled out that this fragment could also belong to Accius’ Aegisthus. He suggests that the speaker could be Aegisthus speaking about Agamemnon or Orestes talking about Aegisthus.

On the assumption that this fragment belongs to the Atreus: this fragment was attributed to Accius’ Atreus by Scriverius–Vossius (1620, 105), with the comment: ‘at cui regno? quod a Tantalo et Pelope proditum iure obtinebatur’. Ribbeck suggests (1875, 455) that these are the words of Atreus, who has just ordered that Thyestes be bound in chains following a canticum in which, on Ribbeck’s account, Thyestes, may have uttered a curse against Atreus similar to the one that he utters in Aeschylus’ Agammemnon (1602 οὕτως ὀλέσθαι πᾶν τὸ Πλεισθένους γένος, ‘So perish all the race of Pleisthenes!’, transl. Sommerstein 2008, II.195) and may perhaps also have encouraged the chorus to rebel against Atreus (‘vielleicht sogar eine Aufforderung an den Chor, sich gegen ihn [Atreus] zu erheben’, p. 455). But Ribbeck believes that the fragment, as well as Accius inc. F  404 TRF (numquam istam imminuam…) and 405 TRF (ecquis hoc animadvortet?...) and  could also belong to Accius’ Pelopidae (1875, 456).
doc.metrical_analysis: Trochaic septenarius.

context.author: Cicero
context.text: This Accian fragment is preserved in a passage of Book 3 of Cicero’s De Officiis (3.82) in which Cicero is discussing the ideal of the uir bonus, or ‘good man’. He presents Pompey and Caesar, without naming them, as men who fell short of attaining that ideal by their disregard for what is morally right in order to obtain power (Cicero translates into Latin a passage of Euripides’ Phoenissae, 524-5, in which tyranny is justified and which Caesar apparently often repeated [cf. Suet. Iul. 30.5], Cic. Ex Euripide conuersi loci fr. II Soubiran). Cicero goes on to make the point (3.83) that although the majority of people might consider being a king to be advantageous, in truth there is nothing more disadvantageous, on account of the constant fear and anxiety a king experiences at potentially being the victim of plotting and conspiracy. The Accian quotation is cited to illustrate this point (3.84), and Cicero contrasts the king referred to in the poetic fragment who, as a descendant of Tantalus and Pelops was the rightful heir to the throne, with Caesar, an illegitimate king who with his army seized control of Rome and thus enslaved a state which had been free and had powerful.

doc.author: Ennius
doc.title: Thyestes
doc.translation: Do not, strangers, come close to me, stay in that spot! Let not my touch or my shadow harm good people. So great is the power of the crime which clings to my body!
doc.differences:
Schauer 56 (adesp.)
Jocelyn 293-295 (Ennius' Thyestes last three lines of 1/12, preceded by Schauer 55)
Warmington 358-360 (Ennius' Thyestes, final three lines of 8/14, preceded by Schauer 55)
Vahlen2 349-351 (Ennius, Thyestes first three lines of 8/14, followed by Schauer 22)
Ribbeck2/3 lines 303-305 (Ennius, Thyestes first three lines of 8/11, followed by Schauer 22)

Jocelyn: does not rule out the possibility of a lacuna between nolite and hospites.
Jocelyn, Vahlen and Ribbeck do not adopt Bentley's conjecture meo: Tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret.
Ribbeck: Ne contagie mea bonis umbrave obsit. [Presumably a typo].
doc.apparatus:
1 istic] istic A2 H V O P U R (de orat.), F G K R V (Tusc.) : isti M p (Tusc.) ista vel. istinc Oxonn. (Tusc.)
2 contagio mea] contagio meaA2 H P U R (de orat.), F G K R V (Tusc.) : contagio me a V OV : contagio ne a O1
3 meo] meo coni. Bentley : vobis coni. Seyffert : coni. Lach.

The five MSS Oxonienses record two different variants for istic, ista and istinc. Both variants seem to be scribal overcorrections: the scribes recognise istic as impossible to take with adire and respond with alternatives. ista is presumably to be taken with contagio mea in the following line, while istinc adds the element of motion required by adire. The reading isti in the codex Ambrosianus and the codex Pithoeanus (see commentary ad adesp. 55), to qualify hospites, seems to have arisen in the same way. The unanimity of readings from the de orat. MSS, where the quote is shorter and offers less scope for scribal confusion (lacking nolite hospites ad me adire, there is less opportunity for head-scratching about how ilico istic fits in, as well as the weight of all the codices potiores of the Tusculanae, suggests the majority reading should not be doubted.
The variant readings contagio me a and contagio ne a, found in the codex Ottobonianus and the codex Vaticanus of the de oratore should not be given much weight.
doc.commentary:
hospites] The word can be used for ‘hosts’ or ‘guests’ or ‘friends’ or ‘strangers’ or ‘foreigners’, all of this usages evolving out of the concept of hospitality. And each of these meanings might be possible, depending on the reconstruction developed: ‘guests’ or ‘friends’ if the addressees are visitors to the palace in Mycenae or some refuge Thyestes has fled to, or ‘hosts’ if Thyestes has fled to his addressees, seeking sanctuary, or ‘foreigners’ or ‘strangers’ if Thyestes and his addressees do not know each other yet.

ilico istic] The phrase (lit. “on that spot there”) is highly emphatic but seems to lack a imperative like state or manete. The collocation is very common in early comedy, as Jocelyn (1967, 420) points out, but never without a verb (cf. Pl. Merc. 912, atque istic sta ilico; Rud. 836 illic astate ilico). Jocelyn suggests that “a gesture by the actor would have taken the place of the verb”, but perhaps a suitable imperative was also found in the preceding line which Cicero does not include.

⟨meo⟩] Bentley conjectured ⟨meo⟩ in order to turn the final line into a complete bacchiac tetrameter. Others have argued for leaving the line as a cretic tetrameter, although as Jocelyn notes, a switch from bacchiacs to cretics without any motivation (e.g. a change in the speaker’s tone) would be highly unusual. Jocelyn offers up the idea that a conjecture may not be required since truncated bacchiacs can occur, but in the examples he cites, none have a cretic opening: instead they have a bacchius, a molossus or a choliamb. Bothe’s conjecture is the most persuasive offering – but if adopted, would its omission be a lapse on Cicero’s part or scribes in the tradition? Perhaps the former, given the cretic rhythm would still be recognisably lyric but Cicero might not be totally sensitive to the usage of bacchiacs in early Roman choral lyrics.
doc.reconstruction:
As an adespoton, we cannot be entirely sure which work or which author TrRF 55 adesp. belonged to, since Cicero makes no reference to a specific work or author on either of the occasions he quotes the line, only in the Tusculans mentioning it is about Thyestes. However, many editors have thought that the line belongs to Ennius’ Thyestes.

While Stephanus (1564) placed it in his adespota, Columna (1590) attributed the fragment to Thyestes, on the basis of the context of the citation in the Tusculanae disputationes. For Warmington (1935), the line belonged to a scene set in the wake of the feast, as Thyestes arrives at the court of King Thesprotus. That is, the scene belongs to the second half of the play, given Warmington’s reconstruction of the Thyestes includes both the feast and the aftermath. For Jocelyn, since the hospites are presumably the chorus, then they need background information on who Thyestes is midway through the play, and so Jocelyn thought that Frr. 55 and 56 adesp. must occur near the beginning of the play. Of course, this neglects the possibility of fitting two choruses into Warmington’s hypothesis: a chorus of Myceneans in Mycene for the first part of the play, and a chorus of Thesprotians at Thesprotia for the second half of the play.

While the majority of commentators have thought the scelus which Thyestes refers to is the cena Thyestea, Segura Morena (who splices Fr. 55 and 56 adesp. together) suggested that this fragment is actually Thyestes’ confession of his adultery with Aerope. This would necessarily place the fragment early in a Thyestes play solely about the feast, and it does address the issue of background information usually coming sooner rather than later. It would represent a major divergence from Seneca’s Thyestes to have Thyestes’ adultery confessed by him (he makes no reference to it in Seneca’s play). However, as Littlewood highlights, Aerope may have had a much more prominent role in one of Sophocles’ lost plays (see Littlewood (1997) 62-63).

lines.text: nolite hospites ad me adire, ilico istic!
ne contagio mea bonis umbrave obsit!
⟨meo⟩ tanta vis sceleris in corpore haeret!
